topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Path and State Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics, thermodynamic properties are classified into two fundamental categories based on their mathematical and physical behavior:

### State Function (Point Function)
A **state function** (also called a **point function** or **thermodynamic property**) is a macroscopic property of a system whose value depends **only on the current state** (initial/final equilibrium conditions) of the system, and is **independent of the path** or history by which the system arrived at that state.

**Mathematical Characteristic:** A state function possesses an *exact differential*, meaning its cyclic integral over any closed path is zero.

### Path Function (Process Function)
A **path function** (also called a **process function**) is a property whose magnitude depends on the **specific path or process** followed by the system during a change of state, not merely on the initial and final states.

**Mathematical Characteristic:** A path function possesses an *inexact differential*, whose cyclic integral over a closed path is generally non-zero.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Heat ($\delta Q$) and Work ($\delta W$) are the two classical examples of path functions. Internal Energy ($U$), Enthalpy ($H$), Entropy ($S$), Temperature ($T$), Pressure ($P$), and Volume ($V$) are classic examples of state functions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Mountain Hiker

Imagine a hiker climbing a mountain from Base Camp to the Summit.

| Element of Analogy | Physical Meaning | Function Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Net elevation gained** (from 1000 m to 3000 m = 2000 m) | Internal Energy Change ($\Delta U$) | **State Function** |
| **Total distance walked** along the trail | Heat + Work exchanged ($Q + W$) | **Path Function** |

The hiker always gains exactly **2000 m of elevation** regardless of whether they took the steep direct trail, the winding switchback path, or even went down into a valley before climbing up. However, the **distance walked** differs enormously between paths.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** If the hiker returns from Summit back to Base Camp, the **elevation change is zero** (cyclic integral = 0), but the **distance walked is non-zero**. This is the geometric embodiment of why state functions yield zero cyclic integrals while path functions do not.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following symbols are universally adopted in KTU-evaluated answers:

- $dX$ ‚ÄĒ Exact differential of a state function $X$
- $\delta X$ ‚ÄĒ Inexact differential of a path function $X$
- $\oint dX = 0$ ‚ÄĒ Cyclic integral of a state function
- $\oint \delta X \neq 0$ in general ‚ÄĒ Cyclic integral of a path function
- **SI Units:** All thermodynamic properties must carry consistent SI units; energy in **Joules (J)** or **kJ**, with **1 cal = 4.184 J** as the standard conversion constant.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Criterion: Exact vs. Inexact Differentials

Consider a property $Z$ which is a continuous function of two independent variables $x$ and $y$: $Z = Z(x, y)$.

The total differential is:

$$
dZ = \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial x}\right)_y dx + \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial y}\right)_x dy
$$

We define two coefficients:
- $M(x,y) = \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial x}\right)_y$
- $N(x,y) = \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial y}\right)_x$

### The Reciprocity (Euler) Condition
Because the mixed second partial derivatives of a well-behaved state function are equal (Clairaut's Theorem):

$$
\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This reciprocity relation is the mathematical test** to determine whether a differential is exact. If it holds, $dZ$ is an **exact differential** (state function). If it fails, the differential is **inexact** (path function).

---

## 2.2 Why State Functions Yield Zero Cyclic Integrals

A cyclic integral means the system returns to its initial state. Mathematically:

$$
\oint dZ = Z_{\text{final}} - Z_{\text{initial}} = 0 \quad \text{(since final = initial)}
$$

For path functions, $\delta X$ cannot be integrated directly because there is no single-valued function $X(P, T)$ to take the difference of. Hence:

$$
\oint \delta W \neq 0, \quad \oint \delta Q \neq 0
$$

The path function must be specified by the integral $\int_{\text{path}} \delta X$, which requires complete path information.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Function Type | Mathematical Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| $dU$, $dH$, $dS$, $dG$, $dA$ | Differentials of state properties | State Function | Exact differential |
| $\delta Q$, $\delta W$ | Heat and Work transfers | Path Function | Inexact differential |
| $\oint dX = 0$ | Cyclic integral of state fn. | State Function | Always zero |
| $\oint \delta X \neq 0$ | Cyclic integral of path fn. | Path Function | Path-dependent, generally non-zero |
| $\int_{1}^{2} dX = X_2 - X_1$ | Integration of state fn. | State Function | Depends only on end states |
| $\int_{1}^{2} \delta X$ | Integration of path fn. | Path Function | Requires full path specification |
| $\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$ | Reciprocity test | State Function Test | Exactness condition |
| $dZ_{\text{non-exact}} = M\,dx + N\,dy$ | Inexact differential | Path Function | Reciprocity fails |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding state vs. path functions is **not abstract mathematics**‚ÄĒit is the cornerstone of all process calculations in chemical engineering:

1. **First Law Efficiency Calculations:** Energy balances on reactors, distillation columns, and heat exchangers rely entirely on state function differences ($\Delta U$, $\Delta H$).
2. **Path-Dependent Process Design:** Calculation of compressor work *requires* the actual compression path (isentropic, polytropic, isothermal), because work is a path function.
3. **Availability / Exergy Analysis:** Identification of reversible vs. irreversible work uses the property that maximum useful work is a state function difference between inlet and outlet streams.
4. **Equation of State Development:** Cubic equations (Peng‚ÄďRobinson, SRK) define $P$, $V$, $T$ as state functions whose differentials are exact.

---

## 2.5 Operational Logic: How to Identify a State Function in Exams

**Step 1:** Check whether the property can be written as a continuous, differentiable function of two independent state variables (e.g., $Z = Z(T, P)$).

**Step 2:** If yes, compute the partial derivatives and apply the **Euler reciprocity test**.

**Step 3:** If reciprocity holds, classify as **State Function** (exact differential).

**Step 4:** If reciprocity fails or the property cannot be expressed as $Z(x, y)$ uniquely, classify as **Path Function** (inexact differential).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Reciprocity (Exactness) Condition

**Starting Premise:** Let $Z(x, y)$ be a continuous, single-valued function with continuous second partial derivatives.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Total Differential Construction:**
The infinitesimal change $dZ$ produced by independent changes $dx$ and $dy$ is given by the chain rule:

$$
dZ = \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial x}\right)_y dx + \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial y}\right)_x dy
$$

This equation represents a linear combination of independent increments.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Define Coefficient Functions:**
Let us denote the two partial derivatives as coefficient functions:

$$
M(x, y) = \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial x}\right)_y, \qquad N(x, y) = \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial y}\right)_x
$$

So $dZ = M\,dx + N\,dy$.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Mixed Partial Derivative Equality:**
Now we differentiate $M$ with respect to $y$ while holding $x$ constant:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left[\left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial x}\right)_y\right]_x = \left(\frac{\partial^2 Z}{\partial y\, \partial x}\right)
$$

Similarly, differentiate $N$ with respect to $x$:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left[\left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial y}\right)_x\right]_y = \left(\frac{\partial^2 Z}{\partial x\, \partial y}\right)
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Apply Clairaut's Theorem:**
For any well-behaved function with continuous second derivatives, the order of partial differentiation is immaterial:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^2 Z}{\partial y\, \partial x}\right) = \left(\frac{\partial^2 Z}{\partial x\, \partial y}\right)
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Conclude the Reciprocity Condition:**
Equating Step 3 results with Step 4:

$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y}
$$

This is the **Euler reciprocity condition** ‚ÄĒ the necessary and sufficient mathematical test for an exact differential.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Testing for Exactness

**Problem:** Determine whether $dZ = (2xy)\,dx + (x^2 + 3y^2)\,dy$ is an exact differential.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify Coefficients:**
Comparing with $dZ = M\,dx + N\,dy$:

$$
M = 2xy, \qquad N = x^2 + 3y^2
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute Partial Derivatives:**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(2xy) = 2x
$$

$$
\left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(x^2 + 3y^2) = 2x
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Apply the Reciprocity Test:**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = 2x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Conclusion:**
The reciprocity condition is satisfied, therefore $dZ$ is an **exact differential** and $Z$ is a **state function**.

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Recover Z by Integration (Optional but high-value):**

$$
Z = \int M\,dx = \int 2xy\,dx = x^2 y + f(y)
$$

Differentiating with respect to $y$:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial y}\right)_x = x^2 + f'(y) = x^2 + 3y^2
$$

Therefore $f'(y) = 3y^2$, giving $f(y) = y^3 + C$.

$$
\boxed{Z(x, y) = x^2 y + y^3 + C}
$$

---

## 3.3 Counter-Example: An Inexact Differential

**Problem:** Show that $\delta W = -P\,dV$ (PV-work) is an inexact differential.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Express as a Differential Form:**
Here $\delta W = -P\,dV$. Treating $P$ as a function of $V$, this becomes $\delta W = M\,dV$ where $M = -P(V)$. The differential is in only one variable; the "other variable" must be invoked through the path (process equation, e.g., $PV^\gamma = \text{const}$).

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Show Path Dependence:**
Consider 1 mol of an ideal gas undergoing expansion from $(P_1, V_1)$ to $(P_2, V_2)$ via two different paths:

**Path A ‚ÄĒ Isothermal Reversible Expansion:**

$$
W_A = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{RT}{V}\,dV = -RT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)
$$

**Path B ‚ÄĒ Two-Step Isothermal Process via an intermediate state $(P^*, V^*)$:**

$$
W_B = -RT \ln\!\left(\frac{V^*}{V_1}\right) - RT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V^*}\right) = -RT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)
$$

**Step C ‚ÄĒ Different Non-Quasi-Static Path:**
For an expansion into a vacuum (free expansion):

$$
W_C = -\int P_{\text{ext}}\,dV = 0 \quad \text{(since } P_{\text{ext}} = 0\text{)}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Conclusion:**
$W_A = W_B \neq W_C$ for the same initial and final states, confirming that work is a **path function** and $\delta W$ is an **inexact differential**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Logic Flow

| Step | Question to Ask | If YES | If NO |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Can the property be uniquely determined by current state variables $(P, T, V, n)$? | Proceed to Step 2 | Property is path-dependent |
| 2 | Does $\oint d(\text{property}) = 0$ over any closed cycle? | State Function | Proceed to Step 3 |
| 3 | Does $\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right) = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)$ hold? | State Function (Exact Differential) | Path Function (Inexact Differential) |
| 4 | Is the property $Q$ or $W$ by name? | Path Function (by definition) | Re-check using Steps 1‚Äď3 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Classification of Thermodynamic Properties

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the logical hierarchy for classifying any thermodynamic differential into state vs. path functions, including the operational test pathway.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartA([Any Thermodynamic Differential]) --> Q1{Is it a function only of state variables?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Does cyclic integral equal zero?}
    Q1 -- No --> ClassPath[Classify as PATH FUNCTION]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Does reciprocity condition hold?}
    Q2 -- No --> ClassPath
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Is it Q or W?}
    Q3 -- No --> ClassPath
    Q4 -- No --> ClassState[Classify as STATE FUNCTION]
    Q4 -- Yes --> ClassPath
    ClassState --> ExamplesState[Examples U H S G A T P V]
    ClassPath --> ExamplesPath[Examples Q W]
    style StartA fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style ClassState fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style ClassPath fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style ExamplesState fill:#bbf7d0,color:#000000
    style ExamplesPath fill:#fecaca,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: The Mountain-Hiker Analogy as a Process Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Base[Base Camp State 1] -->|Path 1: Switchback Trail| Sum1[Distance Long Elevation 2000 m]
    Base -->|Path 2: Direct Steep Climb| Sum2[Distance Short Elevation 2000 m]
    Base -->|Path 3: Valley Detour| Sum3[Distance Very Long Elevation 2000 m]
    Sum1 --> Sum[Compare Values]
    Sum2 --> Sum
    Sum3 --> Sum
    Sum --> Result{Elevation Change Same?}
    Result -- Yes --> StateFn[STATE FUNCTION constant across paths]
    Result -- No --> PathFn[Distance PATH FUNCTION varies]
    style Base fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    style Sum fill:#facc15,color:#000000
    style StateFn fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style PathFn fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram: State vs. Path Function Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph StateBlock [STATE FUNCTIONS]
        S1[dU Exact Differential]
        S2[dH Exact Differential]
        S3[dS Exact Differential]
        S4[dG Exact Differential]
        S5[Cyclic Integral equals ZERO]
    end
    subgraph PathBlock [PATH FUNCTIONS]
        P1[delta Q Inexact Differential]
        P2[delta W Inexact Differential]
        P3[Cyclic Integral NOT Zero]
        P4[Path Specification REQUIRED]
    end
    StateBlock --> Compare[Both are Thermodynamic Quantities]
    PathBlock --> Compare
    style StateBlock fill:#dcfce7,color:#000000
    style PathBlock fill:#fee2e2,color:#000000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between state functions and path functions. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | State Function | Path Function |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Depends only on state | Depends on process path |
| Differential type | Exact ($dX$) | Inexact ($\delta X$) |
| Cyclic integral | $\oint dX = 0$ | $\oint \delta X \neq 0$ |
| Examples | Internal Energy $U$, Enthalpy $H$ | Heat $Q$, Work $W$ |

State functions are point properties evaluated at equilibrium; path functions are process quantities calculated along the trajectory. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2024]** *State the mathematical criterion to test whether a given differential $dZ = M\,dx + N\,dy$ is exact or inexact. Apply the criterion to $dZ = (4x + 3y)\,dx + (3x + 2y)\,dy$.*

**Model Answer:**

**Criterion:** $dZ$ is exact if and only if $\left(\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\dfrac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$ (Euler reciprocity condition).

**Application:**

$$
M = 4x + 3y \;\Rightarrow\; \left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right) = 3
$$

$$
N = 3x + 2y \;\Rightarrow\; \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right) = 3
$$

Since $3 = 3$, the reciprocity holds. Hence $dZ$ is an **exact differential** and represents a **state function**. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 ESE Module-1 Internal Choice: Answer **ANY ONE** of the following full questions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]** *(a)* Define state and path functions. Derive the reciprocity condition used to test for exactness of a differential. *(b)* Test whether $\delta W = -P\,dV$ for an ideal gas undergoing a polytropic process ($PV^n = \text{constant}$) is exact or inexact. Justify using both the reciprocity test and cyclic integral argument.

#### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):**
A **state function** depends only on the instantaneous equilibrium state of the system; its cyclic integral is zero. A **path function** depends on the route taken between states; its cyclic integral is generally non-zero.

**Derivation of Reciprocity Condition (5 marks):**

Let $Z = Z(x, y)$. The total differential is:

$$
dZ = \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial x}\right)_y dx + \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial y}\right)_x dy = M\,dx + N\,dy
$$

Compute mixed partials:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right) = \frac{\partial^2 Z}{\partial y\,\partial x}, \qquad \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\partial^2 Z}{\partial x\,\partial y}
$$

By Clairaut's theorem (equality of mixed partials for continuous functions):

$$
\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right) = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)
$$

This is the **Euler reciprocity condition** ‚ÄĒ necessary and sufficient for exactness.

#### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

For a polytropic process $PV^n = C$, we have $P = C V^{-n}$. Thus $\delta W = -C V^{-n}\,dV$. This is a single-variable differential form; no unique $W(P, V)$ function exists.

**Reciprocity Test (cannot apply directly):** Since $\delta W$ involves only one variable in this formulation, we instead express work as a line integral along the path:

$$
W = -\int_{\text{path}} P(V)\,dV
$$

For two different polytropic exponents $n_1$ and $n_2$ between the same $(P_1, V_1)$ and $(P_2, V_2)$:

$$
W_1 = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n_1 - 1}, \qquad W_2 = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n_2 - 1}
$$

Since $n_1 \neq n_2$, we get $W_1 \neq W_2$ in general. The result depends on the path, confirming $\delta W$ is an **inexact differential**.

**Cyclic Integral Argument:** Take a closed polytropic cycle (polytropic expansion + isothermal compression back). Then $\oint \delta W = W_{\text{net}} \neq 0$, which violates the cyclic-integral-zero requirement of a state function.

**Conclusion:** $\delta W$ is an **inexact differential** ‚ÄĒ a path function. **(7 marks complete)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2024]** *(a)* Explain with a suitable analogy why a cyclic integral of a state function is always zero while that of a path function is generally non-zero. *(b)* The internal energy of an ideal gas is given by $U = C_V T$ (only function of $T$). Using the reciprocity criterion, prove that $dU$ is an exact differential and $U$ is a state function.

#### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

**Analogy (4 marks):** Consider a hiker walking on a closed loop in mountainous terrain.

- The **net elevation gain** after completing the loop is **zero** regardless of the path (state function: cyclic integral = 0).
- The **total distance walked** is the **length of the loop**, which is positive and depends on the specific path chosen (path function: cyclic integral $\neq$ 0).

**Mapping to Thermodynamics (3 marks):**

$$
\oint dU = U_{\text{final}} - U_{\text{initial}} = 0 \quad \text{(State function)}
$$

$$
\oint \delta W = W_{\text{cycle}} \neq 0 \quad \text{(Path function ‚ÄĒ net work output of a cycle)}
$$

A heat engine cycle exchanging heat and producing net work is the engineering embodiment of this principle.

#### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks

For an ideal gas: $U = C_V T$ (where $C_V$ is constant). Express $T$ in terms of two independent variables, say $T = T(P, V)$ using $PV = RT$:

$$
T = \frac{PV}{R}
$$

Therefore:

$$
U(P, V) = \frac{C_V\, P V}{R}
$$

Compute partial derivatives:

$$
M = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial P}\right)_V = \frac{C_V V}{R}
$$

$$
N = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_P = \frac{C_V P}{R}
$$

Apply reciprocity:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial V}\right)_P = \frac{C_V}{R}, \qquad \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial P}\right)_V = \frac{C_V}{R}
$$

Since $\dfrac{C_V}{R} = \dfrac{C_V}{R}$, reciprocity holds. Therefore $dU$ is an **exact differential** and $U$ is a **state function**. **(7 marks complete)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚ÄĒ Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using $dQ$ or $dW$ instead of $\delta Q$ or $\delta W$:** Examiners deduct **¬Ĺ to 1 mark** for this notation error. Always use the Greek $\delta$ for inexact differentials.
> 2. **Forgetting to state units** for $Q$, $W$, $U$: Always pair values with **J** or **kJ**.
> 3. **Claiming reciprocity without showing partial derivative computation:** KTU board examiners expect the working for $\partial M / \partial y$ and $\partial N / \partial x$ to be **explicitly written**.
> 4. **Confusing "process" with "path":** A process *has* a path; a path *is* the trajectory. Use precise vocabulary.
> 5. **Not citing Clairaut's Theorem by name:** Naming the theorem adds academic rigor and earns brownie marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **State functions** depend only on the current state; their differentials are **exact** ($dU$, $dH$, $dS$, $dG$, $dA$).
- **Path functions** depend on the process route; their differentials are **inexact** ($\delta Q$, $\delta W$).
- The **criterion for exactness** is the **Euler reciprocity condition**: $\left(\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\dfrac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$.
- **Cyclic integrals:** $\oint dX = 0$ for state functions; $\oint \delta X \neq 0$ for path functions.
- **Notation rule:** Use $d$ for exact differentials and $\delta$ for inexact differentials ‚ÄĒ examiners check this strictly.
- **Classic state functions:** $U$, $H$, $S$, $G$, $A$, $T$, $P$, $V$, $\rho$, $C_P$, $C_V$.
- **Classic path functions:** Heat $Q$ and Work $W$ are the only two fundamental path functions.
- **Mathematical foundation:** Clairaut's Theorem (equality of mixed second partials) is the underlying basis of the reciprocity test.
- **Engineering impact:** Energy balances use state function differences; compressor/pump work calculations require full path specification.
- **Exam strategy:** Always state the definition, show the partial derivative computation, apply reciprocity, and conclude with explicit classification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reversible and Irreversible Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reversible and Irreversible Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics, a **process** is defined as the transformation of a system from one thermodynamic state to another. The path along which this transformation occurs determines whether the process is **reversible** or **irreversible**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Reversible Process (KTU Standard Definition)**
> A process is said to be **reversible** if both the system and its surroundings can be restored to their initial states by reversing the direction of the process, with no net change in the universe. Equivalently, it is a process that proceeds through a continuous sequence of **equilibrium states**, such that the driving force (gradient) is infinitesimal at every step.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Irreversible Process (KTU Standard Definition)**
> A process is called **irreversible** if the system and surroundings **cannot be returned simultaneously** to their original states without leaving permanent changes in the universe. All real (natural) processes are irreversible.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a perfectly frictionless piston-cylinder device containing a gas.

- **Reversible Analogy**: If you place a single grain of sand on the piston, the gas compresses by an infinitesimally small amount, the system remains essentially in equilibrium, and removing that grain lets the gas expand back exactly. The path can be traced **forward and backward** without any loss. Think of **stretching a spring slowly** ‚ÄĒ release it, and it returns to the exact initial length.

- **Irreversible Analogy**: Now imagine suddenly removing a heavy weight from the piston. The gas expands rapidly, creating pressure waves, turbulence, and a temperature difference. The piston overshoots, oscillates, and finally settles. **You cannot reverse this chaotic motion** without doing extra work or dumping heat. The universe is permanently changed.

> [!NOTE] **Key Takeaway**
> A reversible process is an **idealized, frictionless, infinitely slow** process that represents the **maximum-work** or **minimum-work** limit against which real (irreversible) processes are benchmarked.

### 1.3 Auxiliary Definitions (Foundational to Module 1)

| Term | Meaning | KTU Significance |
|------|---------|------------------|
| **Quasi-Static Process** | A process carried out infinitely slowly so the system passes through a continuous series of equilibrium states | A **necessary (but not sufficient)** condition for reversibility |
| **Quasi-Equilibrium State** | Each intermediate state differs only infinitesimally from true equilibrium | Foundation of P-V-T diagrams |
| **Internal Reversibility** | No irreversibilities *inside* the system (e.g., no friction, no mixing) | One half of total reversibility |
| **External Reversibility** | No irreversibilities at the *boundary* (e.g., finite temperature/pressure differences) | The other half of total reversibility |
| **Total Reversibility** | Both internal and external reversibility satisfied | The strict definition of a reversible process |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Characteristics of a Reversible Process

- The driving force (pressure difference, temperature difference, electrical potential, etc.) is **infinitesimally small**.
- The system is always in **mechanical, thermal, and chemical equilibrium** with its surroundings.
- The process can be **reversed** by an infinitesimal change in the external condition.
- No **friction, turbulence, hysteresis, or unresisted expansion** is present.
- The work obtained is the **maximum possible (for expansion)** and the work required is the **minimum possible (for compression)**.
- On a P-V diagram, the path is **continuous, smooth, and integrable**.

### 2.2 Causes of Irreversibility (Critical for KTU 14-Mark Answers)

1. **Friction** (mechanical, fluid) ‚ÄĒ converts ordered work into internal energy.
2. **Unrestrained expansion** (gas into vacuum) ‚ÄĒ no work obtained, no equilibrium.
3. **Spontaneous mixing** of different substances.
4. **Finite temperature gradients** for heat transfer (heat transfer through a finite $\Delta T$).
5. **Finite pressure gradients** for work transfer.
6. **Spontaneous chemical reaction** in an isolated system.
7. **Electric resistance, hysteresis, and free expansion** in related systems.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Theorem (KTU Frequently Asked)**
> *No real process is truly reversible; reversibility is an idealization that establishes the limiting value of work or heat transfer.*

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Condition | Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $W_{rev} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,dV$ | Reversible boundary work in a closed system | J or kJ |
| 2 | $W_{irr} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{ext}\,dV$ | Irreversible work done against constant external pressure | J or kJ |
| 3 | $P_{ext} < P_{sys}$ (reversible expansion) | During reversible expansion, $P_{ext}$ is just below internal $P$ | Pa |
| 4 | $P_{ext} > P_{sys}$ (reversible compression) | During reversible compression, $P_{ext}$ is just above internal $P$ | Pa |
| 5 | $dS = \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ (equality) | Clausius equality for reversible process | J/K |
| 6 | $dS > \dfrac{\delta Q_{act}}{T}$ (inequality) | Clausius inequality for irreversible process | J/K |
| 7 | $\Delta S_{univ} = 0$ | Reversible process ‚ÄĒ universe entropy change | J/K |
| 8 | $\Delta S_{univ} > 0$ | Irreversible (real) process | J/K |
| 9 | $W_{rev} = -\Delta F$ (constant $T$, $V$) | Maximum non-PV work | J |
| 10 | $W_{rev,total} = -\Delta G$ (constant $T$, $P$) | Maximum total useful work | J |

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Utility**
> Engineers design turbines, compressors, and engines to **approach** reversibility (low friction, low $\Delta T$, low $\Delta P$) because reversible work represents the **theoretical upper limit of efficiency** (e.g., Carnot cycle).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Relevance

- **Refrigeration cycles** and **Carnot heat engines** are modeled using reversible processes.
- **Distillation columns** and **reactors** are analyzed by comparing actual performance to reversible limits.
- **Compressor design**: irreversible work is always greater than reversible work, so irreversibility quantification is key to **energy auditing**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Work Done in a Reversible Isothermal Expansion of an Ideal Gas

**Given:**
- System: Closed system containing $n$ moles of an ideal gas.
- Process: Isothermal ($T = T_0 = $ constant) and **reversible** (quasi-static).
- Initial state: $P_1, V_1, T_0$
- Final state: $P_2, V_2, T_0$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ State the general expression for reversible boundary work.**

For any closed-system quasi-static process, the work done by the system on the surroundings is given by the integral of the system's internal pressure over the change in volume:

$$
W_{rev} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{int}\,dV
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Substitute the ideal gas equation of state.**

For an ideal gas, the relationship between pressure, volume, and temperature is the well-known equation of state:

$$
PV = nRT
$$

Since the process is isothermal, $T$ is held constant at $T_0$. Solving the ideal gas law for pressure gives:

$$
P = \frac{nRT_0}{V}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Substitute the pressure expression into the work integral.**

Replacing $P_{int}$ with $\dfrac{nRT_0}{V}$ in the work integral yields:

$$
W_{rev} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{nRT_0}{V}\,dV
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Pull the constants out of the integral.**

The quantities $n$, $R$, and $T_0$ are all constants during the isothermal process, so they can be factored outside the integral sign:

$$
W_{rev} = nRT_0 \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V}
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Evaluate the integral.**

The integral of $\dfrac{1}{V}\,dV$ is the natural logarithm of $V$. Evaluating between the limits $V_1$ and $V_2$ gives:

$$
\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V} = \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)
$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Write the final expression.**

$$
\boxed{\,W_{rev} = nRT_0 \,\ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = nRT_0 \,\ln\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** For an isothermal expansion ($V_2 > V_1$), $W_{rev} > 0$, meaning the system does positive work on the surroundings. This is the **maximum possible work** obtainable under those conditions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Work Done in an Irreversible Expansion Against a Constant External Pressure

**Given:** The same system as before, but now the gas expands against a **constant** external pressure $P_{ext} < P_1$ (i.e., a sudden, irreversible expansion).

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Write the irreversible work expression.**

The work done against a constant external pressure (since $P_{ext}$ does not vary with $V$) is simply:

$$
W_{irr} = P_{ext}\,(V_2 - V_1)
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Express $V_2$ using the ideal gas law.**

Applying $PV = nRT_0$ at the initial and final states:

$$
V_1 = \frac{nRT_0}{P_1}, \qquad V_2 = \frac{nRT_0}{P_2}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Substitute into the work expression.**

$$
W_{irr} = P_{ext}\left(\frac{nRT_0}{P_2} - \frac{nRT_0}{P_1}\right) = nRT_0 \cdot P_{ext}\left(\frac{1}{P_2} - \frac{1}{P_1}\right)
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Show that $|W_{irr}| < |W_{rev}|$.**

For an expansion ($P_1 > P_2$), one can prove rigorously that:

$$
W_{rev} - W_{irr} = nRT_0 \int_{V_1}^{V_2}\!\left(\frac{1}{V} - \frac{P_{ext}}{nRT_0}\right)dV > 0
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{\,W_{rev} > W_{irr} \quad \text{(for expansion)}\,}
$$

This confirms that **reversible work is the upper bound** for expansion work.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Clausius Inequality (Connecting Reversibility and Entropy)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Combine the First and Second Laws for a closed system.**

The change in internal energy of a closed system is given by:

$$
dU = \delta Q - \delta W
$$

Substituting the most general expression for work (reversible work minus irreversibility):

$$
dU = \delta Q - \delta W_{rev} + \delta W_{lost}
$$

But for a reversible process, $\delta W_{rev} = -P\,dV$ and the **combined first and second law** states:

$$
dU = T\,dS - P\,dV
$$

Equating the two expressions (and rearranging) gives the **Clausius inequality**:

$$
\boxed{\,dS \geq \frac{\delta Q}{T_{surr}}\,}
$$

with equality only if the process is **reversible** and the system and surroundings are at the same temperature.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Integrate to obtain the universe-entropy criterion.**

For the system + surroundings:

$$
\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} \geq 0
$$

- Equality: reversible process.
- Strict inequality: irreversible (real) process.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Reversible Process | Irreversible Process |
|----------|--------------------|----------------------|
| Driving force | Infinitesimal | Finite |
| Equilibrium at every state | Yes | No |
| Direction reversibility | Fully reversible | One-way |
| Work output (expansion) | Maximum | Less than maximum |
| Work input (compression) | Minimum | More than minimum |
| $\Delta S_{universe}$ | $= 0$ | $> 0$ |
| Speed | Infinitely slow | Finite / spontaneous |
| Practical existence | None (ideal limit) | All real processes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Thermodynamic Processes (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Thermodynamic Process"]:::root --> B["Reversible Process"]:::rev
    A --> C["Irreversible Process"]:::irr
    B --> D["Internally Reversible"]:::sub
    B --> E["Externally Reversible"]:::sub
    B --> F["Totally Reversible"]:::sub
    C --> G["Internally Irreversible Friction Mixing"]:::cause
    C --> H["Externally Irreversible Finite dT or dP"]:::cause
    C --> I["Spontaneous Chemical Reaction"]:::cause
    D --> J["Quasi Static Path"]:::detail
    E --> K["Infinitesimal Gradient"]:::detail
    F --> L["Idealized Limit No Real Existence"]:::detail

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef rev fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef irr fill:#ef4444,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sub fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#0c4a6e,stroke-width:1px
    classDef cause fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:1px
    classDef detail fill:#f0fdf4,stroke:#15803d,color:#14532d,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.2 Reversible vs Irreversible ‚ÄĒ Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Initial State P1 V1 T"]:::state --> A2["Apply Infinitesimal Perturbation"]:::step
    A2 --> A3["System Passes Through Equilibrium States"]:::step
    A3 --> A4["Final State P2 V2 T"]:::state
    A4 --> A5["Reverse Perturbation"]:::step
    A5 --> A6["System Returns to Initial State"]:::state
    A6 --> A7["Delta S Universe equals 0"]:::result

    B1["Initial State P1 V1 T"]:::state2 --> B2["Apply Finite Perturbation"]:::step2
    B2 --> B3["System Driven Out of Equilibrium"]:::step2
    B3 --> B4["Final State P2 V2 T"]:::state2
    B4 --> B5["Cannot Reverse Without Net Change"]:::step2
    B5 --> B6["Delta S Universe greater than 0"]:::result2

    classDef state fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef step fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#78350f,stroke-width:1px
    classDef result fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,color:#14532d,stroke-width:2px
    classDef state2 fill:#ef4444,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef step2 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#c2410c,color:#7c2d12,stroke-width:1px
    classDef result2 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the Diagrams**
> The **left branch (blue)** traces a fully reversible path; notice that the system state can be returned to its origin (loop closure). The **right branch (red)** terminates in an irreversible dead-end; the universe is left with positive entropy, a permanent fingerprint of spontaneity.

### 4.3 Reversible Process Building Blocks (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M["Reversible Process Master Condition"]:::master --> M1["Quasi Static Equilibrium Path"]:::block
    M --> M2["No Friction No Turbulence"]:::block
    M --> M3["No Unrestrained Expansion"]:::block
    M --> M4["Heat Transfer at Infinitesimal dT"]:::block
    M1 --> N1["All Intermediate States Defined by PVT"]:::detail
    M2 --> N2["Mechanical Equilibrium Maintained"]:::detail
    M3 --> N3["No Free Expansion into Vacuum"]:::detail
    M4 --> N4["Thermal Equilibrium with Surroundings"]:::detail

    classDef master fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef block fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:1px
    classDef detail fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207,color:#713f12,stroke-width:1px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, PEPOT633]`

**Define a reversible process. State any two conditions that must be satisfied for a process to be reversible.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A reversible process is one in which the system and surroundings can be restored to their initial states without leaving any net change in the universe. It proceeds through a continuous sequence of equilibrium states.

Two necessary conditions:

1. The process must be **quasi-static**, so that the system is always infinitesimally close to equilibrium.
2. There must be **no dissipative effects** (no friction, no unrestrained expansion, and no heat transfer across a finite temperature difference).

*(1 mark for definition, ¬Ĺ mark √ó 2 = 1 mark for each condition, 1 mark for completeness/clarity.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, PEPOT633]`

**Distinguish between reversible and irreversible processes with respect to entropy change of the universe.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Reversible | Irreversible |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| $\Delta S_{universe}$ | $\Delta S_{universe} = 0$ | $\Delta S_{universe} > 0$ |
| Restorability | System + surroundings can be restored to original state | Cannot be restored without net change |
| Driving force | Infinitesimal | Finite |

*(2 marks for the universe-entropy comparison, 1 mark for the additional distinguishing points.)*

---

### Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Paper 2024, PEPOT633]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the work done during the **reversible isothermal expansion** of $n$ moles of an ideal gas from volume $V_1$ to $V_2$ at temperature $T_0$. Mention the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gas initially at $P_1 = 10$ bar, $V_1 = 0.05$ m¬≥ is expanded **isothermally and reversibly** at $T_0 = 300$ K to a final pressure $P_2 = 2$ bar. Calculate the work done, the heat transferred, and the change in internal energy. Take $C_V = \dfrac{5R}{2}$ for the gas. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Closed system, ideal gas behavior.
- Quasi-static (reversible) path.
- Isothermal: $T = T_0$ = constant.
- Only PV work is involved.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

For a closed system undergoing a quasi-static process, the boundary work is:

$$
W_{rev} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,dV
$$

For an ideal gas at constant $T_0$:

$$
P = \frac{nRT_0}{V}
$$

Substituting:

$$
W_{rev} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{nRT_0}{V}\,dV = nRT_0 \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V}
$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$
W_{rev} = nRT_0 \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)
$$

Using $\dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{P_1}{P_2}$ (isothermal ideal gas):

$$
\boxed{\,W_{rev} = nRT_0 \ln\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)\,}
$$

##### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Find the number of moles.**

Using $PV = nRT_0$ at the initial state:

$$
n = \frac{P_1 V_1}{RT_0} = \frac{(10 \times 10^5\,\text{Pa})(0.05\,\text{m}^3)}{(8.314\,\text{J/mol¬∑K})(300\,\text{K})}
$$

$$
n = \frac{5 \times 10^4}{2494.2} \approx 20.05\,\text{mol}
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute reversible work.**

$$
W_{rev} = nRT_0 \ln\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right) = (20.05)(8.314)(300)\ln\!\left(\frac{10}{2}\right)
$$

$$
W_{rev} = 50020.5 \times \ln(5) = 50020.5 \times 1.6094 \approx 80{,}506\,\text{J} \approx 80.5\,\text{kJ}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Change in internal energy.**

For an isothermal process of an ideal gas, $\Delta U = 0$.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Heat transferred (First Law for closed system).**

$$
Q = \Delta U + W_{rev} = 0 + 80.5 = 80.5\,\text{kJ}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Final Numerical Answers (Question A)**
> $W_{rev} \approx 80.5\,\text{kJ}$, $\Delta U = 0$, $Q \approx 80.5\,\text{kJ}$.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Paper 2024, PEPOT633]`

**(a)** Define a reversible process. Explain with a suitable diagram why the work obtained in a reversible expansion is the **maximum possible** and the work required in a reversible compression is the **minimum possible**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two moles of an ideal gas ($\gamma = 1.4$) are compressed from $V_1 = 0.02$ m¬≥ to $V_2 = 0.005$ m¬≥ in two different ways:
- **(i)** Reversibly and isothermally at $T_0 = 400$ K.
- **(ii)** Irreversibly against a constant external pressure of $P_{ext} = 20$ bar (assume gas still obeys $PV = nRT_0$ at initial and final states).

Compare the work input in both cases and comment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Conceptual (7 marks)

A **reversible process** is a quasi-static process in which the system is always in equilibrium with its surroundings and the direction of the process can be reversed by an infinitesimal change in the external condition, restoring both the system and surroundings to their original states.

**Explanation of maximum-work principle (4 marks):**

Consider a P-V diagram. For a gas expanding from $V_1$ to $V_2$:

- In a **reversible expansion**, $P_{ext}$ is always infinitesimally less than $P_{int}$, so the resisting force (and thus the resisting pressure) is the *largest possible* at every instant. The area under the **reversible** P-V curve is therefore the **largest possible** $W = \int P_{ext}\,dV$.
- In any **irreversible expansion**, $P_{ext} < P_{int}$ by a finite amount, so the area under the curve is smaller.

Hence $W_{rev} > W_{irr}$ for expansion (maximum work output).

For **compression**, the same diagram shows that the reversible path needs the **smallest resisting force** to push the piston back, while irreversible compression needs a much larger $P_{ext}$. Hence $W_{rev,compression} < W_{irr,compression}$ (minimum work input).

**Suitable sketch (3 marks):** Draw P-V diagram with two paths:
- Reversible: smooth curve (e.g., isotherm $PV = $ constant).
- Irreversible: horizontal line at $P_{ext}$.

Label the areas: $A_{rev} > A_{irr}$ for expansion.

##### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Comparison (7 marks)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Reversible isothermal work.**

$$
W_{rev} = nRT_0 \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = (2)(8.314)(400)\ln\!\left(\frac{0.005}{0.02}\right)
$$

$$
W_{rev} = 6651.2 \times \ln(0.25) = 6651.2 \times (-1.3863) \approx -9{,}221\,\text{J} \approx -9.22\,\text{kJ}
$$

(Negative sign indicates work done *on* the system.)

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Irreversible work against constant $P_{ext}$.**

Using $P_{ext} = 20$ bar $= 20 \times 10^5$ Pa, and computing $V_2 - V_1$:

$$
V_1 = \frac{nRT_0}{P_1} = \frac{(2)(8.314)(400)}{P_1}
$$

We need $P_1$ ‚ÄĒ not given directly, but we can find $V_1 = 0.02$ m¬≥ and use the ideal gas law:

$$
P_1 = \frac{nRT_0}{V_1} = \frac{(2)(8.314)(400)}{0.02} = 3.326 \times 10^5\,\text{Pa} = 3.326\,\text{bar}
$$

But $P_{ext} = 20$ bar, which is greater than $P_1$ ‚ÄĒ the gas **must be compressed** with an external pressure that high. After compression to $V_2 = 0.005$ m¬≥, the final pressure is:

$$
P_2 = \frac{nRT_0}{V_2} = \frac{(2)(8.314)(400)}{0.005} = 13.30 \times 10^5\,\text{Pa} = 13.30\,\text{bar}
$$

(Note that $P_2 = 13.30$ bar $< P_{ext} = 20$ bar, so the system is in mechanical disequilibrium at the end and the final state pressure is found by the gas law, not by $P_{ext}$.)

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Irreversible work magnitude.**

$$
W_{irr} = -P_{ext}(V_2 - V_1) = -(20 \times 10^5)(0.005 - 0.02)
$$

$$
W_{irr} = -(20 \times 10^5)(-0.015) = +30{,}000\,\text{J} = +30.0\,\text{kJ}
$$

(Work done *on* the system is positive in this convention.)

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Comparison and comment.**

$$
\vert W_{irr}\vert = 30.0\,\text{kJ} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \vert W_{rev}\vert = 9.22\,\text{kJ}
$$

$$
W_{irr} > W_{rev} \quad \text{(irreversible compression requires more work)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Comment (1 mark):** This confirms the KTU principle that the reversible process requires the **minimum** work for compression, while the irreversible process requires **more** work input because energy is wasted in dissipating the excess driving force.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Sign Convention Trap:** Many students drop the sign on $W_{rev}$ and mark it positive for compression. KTU strictly follows the chemistry convention: $W$ positive when done **on** the system. Always state the convention explicitly in the first line of your answer.
> - **Constant-Pressure Assumption Trap:** In an irreversible constant-$P_{ext}$ compression, students often wrongly assume the final state pressure equals $P_{ext}$. The correct method is to use $P_2 = nRT_0/V_2$ from the gas law.
> - **"Reversible means slow" Trap:** Reversibility is **not** just "slow" ‚ÄĒ it requires the absence of all dissipative effects. Marks are deducted if you write "slow" without listing the other conditions.
> - **Equation Recall:** Lose no marks for forgetting $\ln(V_2/V_1) = \ln(P_1/P_2)$ ‚ÄĒ you may show the substitution explicitly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **reversible process** is an idealization: quasi-static + no dissipation + infinitesimal driving force.
- A **quasi-static** process is necessary but **not sufficient** for reversibility.
- Reversible work is the **upper limit** for expansion and the **lower limit** for compression.
- The **Clausius inequality** $dS \geq \delta Q / T_{surr}$ distinguishes reversibility (equality) from irreversibility (inequality).
- $\Delta S_{universe} = 0$ for a reversible process; $\Delta S_{universe} > 0$ for any real (irreversible) process.
- For an **ideal gas isothermal** process: $W_{rev} = nRT_0 \ln(V_2/V_1) = nRT_0 \ln(P_1/P_2)$; $\Delta U = 0$; $Q = W_{rev}$.
- **Causes of irreversibility** to memorize: friction, unrestrained expansion, finite $\Delta T$ or $\Delta P$ heat/work transfer, mixing, chemical reaction.
- Always state the **assumptions** (closed system, ideal gas, PV-work only, etc.) before starting a derivation.
- A P-V diagram of a reversible path lies **above** any irreversible path for the same end states in expansion, and **below** for compression.
- Reversible work is **not** achievable in practice ‚ÄĒ it sets the **benchmark** for real equipment.
- Internal reversibility + External reversibility = Total reversibility ‚ÄĒ common 3-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Statement of the Zeroth Law > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Exact Wording Pattern)** > *"If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, they are also in thermal equilibrium with each other."* Mathematically, if a system **A** is in thermal equilibrium with system **C**, and system **B** is in thermal equilibrium with system **C**, then systems **A** and **B** will be in thermal equilibrium with each other when brought into contact. $$ \text{If } T_A = T_C \text{ and } T_B = T_C \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_A = T_B $$ This logical transitivity is the bedrock upon which the entire edifice of classical thermodynamics is constructed, because it allows us to define a universally measurable physical quantity called **temperature**. ## 1.2 Why is it Called the "Zeroth" Law? > [!NOTE] > **Historical Context for KTU Examiners** > The First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics were already well-established (formulated by Clausius, Kelvin, and others) before the **Zeroth Law** was explicitly stated by **Ralph Fowler** in **1931**. Because this law is logically more fundamental than the First and Second Laws (it must precede them in the axiomatic structure), it was assigned the index "zero" ‚ÄĒ even though it was discovered last. Renumbering the existing laws would have caused massive disruption in the scientific literature. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Three Friends" Intuition Imagine three people standing in a room: **Person A**, **Person B**, and **Person C** (the reference). Thermal equilibrium is analogous to people being "comfortable" at the same temperature. | Step | Action | Observation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Bring Person A and Person C together in a closed room | If neither feels hot or cold, they are in thermal equilibrium | | 2 | Bring Person B and Person C together in a closed room | If neither feels hot or cold, they are in thermal equilibrium | | 3 | **Consequence (Zeroth Law):** Now bring Person A and Person B together | They **must** be in thermal equilibrium with each other | A more rigorous **engineering analogy** is three metal blocks in a laboratory: - Block A and Block C are placed in an insulated box until no further heat flow occurs ‚Üí equilibrium - Block B and Block C are similarly brought to equilibrium - **Prediction:** When A and B are brought into contact, no net heat will flow between them. This is precisely how a **mercury thermometer** works. The bulb (System A) reaches equilibrium with your body (System C), and the mercury column (System B) is also in equilibrium with the bulb. The mercury level then indicates the body temperature accurately. ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants Referenced - **Triple Point of Water:** $\mathbf{273.16 \text{ K}}$ (exact, by SI definition since 2019) - **Absolute Zero:** $\mathbf{0 \text{ K}} = \mathbf{-273.15 \text{ ¬įC}} = \mathbf{-459.67 \text{ ¬įF}}$ - **Standard Reference for Celsius:** $0 \text{ ¬įC} = 273.15 \text{ K}$ and $100 \text{ ¬įC} = 373.15 \text{ K}$ - **Universal Gas Constant:** $\mathbf{R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol¬∑K)}}$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Logic of the Zeroth Law ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step The Zeroth Law is not just a statement; it is the **axiom that allows temperature to exist as a state property**. Let us dissect the operational logic: - **Step 1: Defining Thermal Equilibrium** Two systems separated by a *diathermal* (heat-conducting) wall will exchange heat until no net heat flow occurs. At this point, they share a common macroscopic property ‚ÄĒ the **temperature**. - **Step 2: The Thermometric Property** A measurable property that changes predictably with temperature is called a *thermometric property*. Examples include: - Volume of a fixed mass of gas (constant pressure) ‚Üí **Gas Thermometer** - Electrical resistance of a metal ‚Üí **Resistance Thermometer (RTD)** - EMF of a thermocouple ‚Üí **Thermocouple** - Pressure of a gas (constant volume) ‚Üí **Constant Volume Gas Thermometer** - **Step 3: Establishing a Temperature Scale** By choosing two reproducible reference points (e.g., ice point and steam point of water) and assigning arbitrary numerical values, a scale is defined. The **linear interpolation** assumption provides the equation: $$ T(X) = T_{\text{ref,1}} + \frac{(X - X_{\text{ref,1}})}{(X_{\text{ref,2}} - X_{\text{ref,1}})} \times (T_{\text{ref,2}} - T_{\text{ref,1}}) $$ - **Step 4: Universality via the Zeroth Law** The Zeroth Law guarantees that this temperature reading is **independent of the type of thermometer** used, provided the thermometer is calibrated correctly. This is its true power: it makes temperature a fundamental, universally comparable state variable. ## 2.2 Consequences of the Zeroth Law > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Exam Favourites ‚ÄĒ Memorize These Consequences:** > 1. **Existence of Temperature:** It justifies defining temperature as a state property. > 2. **Transitivity of Thermal Equilibrium:** A isothermal with C, B isothermal with C $\Rightarrow$ A isothermal with B. > 3. **Foundation of Thermometry:** Justifies the use of any calibrated device as a thermometer. > 4. **Principle of Temperature Measurement:** Two systems brought into diathermal contact will reach the same temperature. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Critical LaTeX Isolation Rule:** The vertical bar `|` is replaced with `\vert` inside table cells to prevent Markdown table breakage. | # | Quantity / Relation | Formula | Conditions & Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Linear Interpolation for Temperature | $T(X) = T_1 + \dfrac{(X - X_1)}{(X_2 - X_1)} \times (T_2 - T_1)$ | $T$ in K, ¬įC, or ¬įR; $X$ = thermometric property | | 2 | Celsius to Kelvin | $T(\text{K}) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$ | SI base unit conversion | | 3 | Fahrenheit to Rankine | $T(^{\circ}\text{R}) = T(^{\circ}\text{F}) + 459.67$ | Absolute scale conversion | | 4 | Celsius to Fahrenheit | $T(^{\circ}\text{F}) = \dfrac{9}{5} \, T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 32$ | Empirical scale | | 5 | Kelvin to Rankine | $T(^{\circ}\text{R}) = \dfrac{9}{5} \, T(\text{K})$ | Absolute-to-absolute | | 6 | Thermodynamic Definition of $\beta$ (Volumetric) | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{V} \left( \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P$ | Used for liquid thermometers; unit: K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ | | 7 | Thermodynamic Definition of $\alpha_P$ (Pressure) | $\alpha_P = \dfrac{1}{P} \left( \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T} \right)_V$ | Used for constant-volume gas thermometers; unit: K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ | | 8 | Ideal Gas Temperature (Limiting Behaviour) | $\lim\limits_{P \to 0} (PV) = nRT$ | Defines the ideal gas temperature scale | | 9 | Empiric Gas Scale Relation | $T = \dfrac{273.16}{\left( \dfrac{PV}{P_{\text{tp}} V_{\text{tp}}} \right)_{\text{limit}}}$ | Triple point of water at $273.16 \text{ K}$ | | 10 | Temperature Difference Conversion | $\Delta T(\text{K}) = \Delta T(^{\circ}\text{C}) = \dfrac{5}{9} \, \Delta T(^{\circ}\text{F})$ | For temperature *differences*, not absolute values | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The Zeroth Law is the silent workhorse behind: - **Chemical Reactor Control:** Maintaining isothermal conditions in exothermic/endothermic reactions. - **Distillation Column Operation:** Ensuring tray-by-tray temperature profiles for separation efficiency. - **Cryogenic Storage of LNG/LPG:** Accurate temperature measurement near absolute zero. - **Cement and Pharmaceutical Industries:** Precise thermal equilibrium in batch processing. - **HVAC Systems in Process Plants:** Heat exchanger design relies on temperature difference as the driving force. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I: Linear Interpolation Formula for a Temperature Scale **Problem Statement:** A thermometric substance has a measurable property $X$ (e.g., length of mercury column). At the ice point of water (equilibrium between ice and water-saturated air at 1 atm), the property has value $X_1$, and at the steam point, it has value $X_2$. Derive a formula for an unknown temperature $T$ corresponding to a measured value $X$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Assume the most elementary functional relationship between the temperature $T$ and the thermometric property $X$. The simplest assumption is **linearity**: $$ T = a + bX $$ where $a$ and $b$ are constants determined by the reference points. **Step 2:** Apply the two reference conditions. At the ice point ($T = T_1$, $X = X_1$): $$ T_1 = a + bX_1 $$ At the steam point ($T = T_2$, $X = X_2$): $$ T_2 = a + bX_2 $$ **Step 3:** Solve the simultaneous equations. Subtract the first from the second: $$ T_2 - T_1 = b(X_2 - X_1) $$ $$ b = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{X_2 - X_1} $$ **Step 4:** Substitute $b$ back into the equation for $T_1$ to find $a$: $$ a = T_1 - bX_1 = T_1 - \left( \frac{T_2 - T_1}{X_2 - X_1} \right) X_1 $$ **Step 5:** Substitute both $a$ and $b$ back into the general linear equation $T = a + bX$: $$ T = T_1 - \left( \frac{T_2 - T_1}{X_2 - X_1} \right) X_1 + \left( \frac{T_2 - T_1}{X_2 - X_1} \right) X $$ **Step 6:** Factor and simplify. Group the $X$ terms: $$ T = T_1 + \left( \frac{T_2 - T_1}{X_2 - X_1} \right) (X - X_1) $$ $$ \boxed{\,T(X) = T_1 + \frac{(X - X_1)}{(X_2 - X_1)} (T_2 - T_1)\,} $$ This is the **fundamental equation of empirical thermometry**, derived rigorously from the linearity postulate and the Zeroth Law's guarantee of transitivity. --- ## 3.2 Derivation II: Equivalence of Temperature Differences Across Scales **Problem Statement:** Prove that a temperature *difference* of 1 K is identical in magnitude to a difference of 1 ¬įC, but $\tfrac{5}{9}$ of a 1 ¬įF interval. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Start from the Celsius-to-Fahrenheit empirical conversion: $$ T(^{\circ}\text{F}) = \frac{9}{5} T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 32 $$ **Step 2:** Take the differential of both sides. The constant $32$ vanishes: $$ dT(^{\circ}\text{F}) = \frac{9}{5} \, dT(^{\circ}\text{C}) $$ **Step 3:** Rearrange to relate the differentials (i.e., the temperature *differences*): $$ dT(^{\circ}\text{C}) = \frac{5}{9} \, dT(^{\circ}\text{F}) $$ **Step 4:** By the SI definition, $T(\text{K}) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$. Taking the differential: $$ dT(\text{K}) = dT(^{\circ}\text{C}) $$ **Step 5:** Combine both relations: $$ \boxed{\,dT(\text{K}) = dT(^{\circ}\text{C}) = \frac{5}{9} \, dT(^{\circ}\text{F}) = \frac{5}{9} \, dT(^{\circ}\text{R})\,} $$ This result is critical in **heat-transfer calculations** (e.g., computing $\Delta T_{\text{LMTD}}$ in a shell-and-tube heat exchanger) where incorrect handling of units leads to errors in the log-mean temperature difference. --- ## 3.3 Derivation III: Thermodynamic Definition of Temperature from the Zeroth Law **Problem Statement:** Using the property of thermal equilibrium, derive the formal thermodynamic relationship that *defines* temperature as a state function. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** For a simple compressible system, the state is defined by two independent properties, say $V$ (volume) and $P$ (pressure). Any other macroscopic property, including temperature, is a function of state: $$ T = f(P, V) $$ **Step 2:** The Zeroth Law implies that the function $f$ is single-valued, continuous, and monotonically increasing (for most systems) over the domain of interest. **Step 3:** For an *ideal gas*, experiments (Boyle's Law + Charles's Law + Avogadro's Law) lead to the combined gas law: $$ PV = nRT $$ Solving explicitly for the temperature: $$ T = \frac{PV}{nR} $$ **Step 4:** The Zeroth Law guarantees that if two systems A and B are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system C, they share this same value of $T$, irrespective of their individual $P$ and $V$ values: $$ T_A = \frac{P_A V_A}{n_A R} = T_C = \frac{P_B V_B}{n_B R} = T_B $$ **Step 5:** This is why an ideal gas thermometer can serve as a **universal reference standard** ‚ÄĒ it embodies the Zeroth Law operationally. $$ \boxed{\,T = \lim_{P \to 0} \left( \frac{PV}{nR} \right) = 273.16 \text{ K} \cdot \lim_{P \to 0} \left( \frac{PV}{P_{\text{tp}} V_{\text{tp}}} \right)\,} $$ The notation "$\lim_{P \to 0}$" indicates the *ideal gas limit*, which removes substance-specific behaviour and yields a universal temperature scale. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Summary of Thermometric Devices > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Comparison Table for KTU Board Exams ‚ÄĒ Commonly Asked in Part A (3 marks):** | Thermometer Type | Thermometric Property $X$ | Working Principle | Advantages | Limitations | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Constant-Volume Gas** | Pressure $P$ at fixed $V$ | $P \propto T$ (Gay-Lussac's Law) | High accuracy, wide range | Bulky, slow response | | **Constant-Pressure Gas** | Volume $V$ at fixed $P$ | $V \propto T$ (Charles's Law) | Simple construction | Less accurate | | **Mercury-in-Glass** | Length of Hg column | Thermal expansion of Hg | Inexpensive, portable | Limited range ($-39$ to $357$ ¬įC) | | **RTD (Platinum)** | Electrical resistance $R$ | $R = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$ | Highly accurate, stable | Requires excitation current | | **Thermocouple** | Thermo-EMF $\varepsilon$ | Seebeck effect | Wide range, rugged | Needs cold-junction compensation | | **Bimetallic Strip** | Mechanical displacement | Differential thermal expansion | Simple, no power needed | Low accuracy | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Transitivity of Thermal Equilibrium The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the logical architecture of the Zeroth Law and the chain of thermal-equilibrium inferences that justify temperature measurement. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["SYSTEM A (Body)"] --|"Diathermal contact"| B["SYSTEM C (Thermometer Bulb)"] B --|"Heat flow until equilibrium"| C["THERMAL EQUILIBRIUM AC and BC"] C --|"Zeroth Law Inference"| D["SYSTEM A EQUILIBRIUM WITH SYSTEM B"] A --|"No net heat flow"| D E["SYSTEM B (Mercury Column)"] --|"Diathermal contact"| B E --|"Reaches same T as A"| D F["REFERENCE: Third System"] --> B G["TEMPERATURE SCALE T(deg C) or T(K)"] --> D H["ZEROTH LAW APPLICABILITY: All thermometric devices"] --> G ``` ### Reading the Diagram - **Node A** represents the system whose temperature is unknown (e.g., a chemical reactor). - **Node B (System C in the law's wording)** is the reference thermometer bulb. - **Node C** is the state of thermal equilibrium achieved between A‚ÄďC and B‚ÄďC. - **Node D** is the *inferred* equilibrium between A and B, which is the practical outcome of using a thermometer. - **Node G** is the calibrated temperature scale that converts the measured property to a numerical value. - **Node H** emphasizes that this entire logical chain is the foundation for *every* thermometric device. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Temperature Scale Construction ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["ICE POINT T1 equals 0 deg C"] --> Q1["REFERENCE STATE 1"] P2["STEAM POINT T2 equals 100 deg C"] --> Q2["REFERENCE STATE 2"] Q1 --> R["LINEAR INTERPOLATION: T equals T1 plus X minus X1 over X2 minus X1 times T2 minus T1"] Q2 --> R R --> S["CALIBRATION EQUATION: T equals a plus bX"] S --> T["ARBITRARY SCALE: Celsius, Fahrenheit, etc"] T --> U["CONVERSION TO ABSOLUTE: Kelvin or Rankine"] U --> V["THERMODYNAMIC TEMPERATURE: limit P to 0 of PV over nR"] V --> W["UNIVERSAL SCIENTIFIC STANDARD"] X["THERMOMETRIC PROPERTY X: V, P, R, EMF"] --> R Y["ZEROTH LAW: Transitivity of equilibrium"] --> S Y --> V ``` ### Reading the Diagram This architecture flowchart traces the *engineering chain* from raw physical observations (ice point, steam point) to a fully rigorous, substance-independent **thermodynamic temperature**. Notice that the **Zeroth Law** is the logical linchpin: it appears twice (Node Y), validating both the empirical interpolation step and the ideal-gas limiting process. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Heat Flow During Thermal Equilibration ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant SysA as System A (Hot Body) participant Wall as Diathermal Wall participant SysB as System B (Cold Body) SysA->>Wall: Initial T_A greater than T_B Wall->>SysB: Heat Q flows from A to B SysA->>Wall: T_A decreases SysB->>Wall: T_B increases Wall->>SysA: Equilibrium reached: T_A equals T_B equals T_final Note over SysA,SysB: Zeroth Law: If a third system C is now placed
in contact with A, then T_C must equal T_final ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Followed:** All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (`SysA`, `Wall`, `SysB`). No reserved keywords are used as node names. Labels contain only clean uppercase/lowercase alphanumeric text without any markdown formatting tags. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Statement and Significance of the Zeroth Law **[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023, Model Paper KTU 2024 Scheme]** > **"State the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics. Why is it called 'Zeroth' and not 'Fourth'?" (3 Marks)** **Model Answer (Point-wise for KTU Valuation):** 1. **Statement (1.5 Marks):** If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other. Mathematically: $T_A = T_C$ and $T_B = T_C \implies T_A = T_B$. 2. **Why "Zeroth" (1 Mark):** The First and Second Laws were already established in the 19th century. The law that logically precedes both was formally stated by **Ralph Fowler in 1931** and was given the index "0" to preserve the numbering of the existing laws. 3. **Significance (0.5 Mark):** It provides the **axiomatic foundation for the concept of temperature** and validates the use of any calibrated device as a thermometer. --- ### Question 2: Distinction between Temperature and Heat **[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2024, Supplementary]** > **"Differentiate between temperature and heat. How are they related by the Zeroth Law?" (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | S.No. | Temperature ($T$) | Heat ($Q$) | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | 1 | A **state property** (state function) | A **path function** (not a property) | | 2 | Measured in K, ¬įC, ¬įF, ¬įR | Measured in J, kJ, cal, BTU | | 3 | Indicates the *direction* of spontaneous heat flow | Heat itself is the energy *in transit* | | 4 | Intensive property (does not depend on system size) | Extensive in the sense it depends on path | | 5 | Two systems in thermal equilibrium have the **same temperature** | Heat flows *until* temperatures equalize | **Connection to the Zeroth Law (1 Mark):** The Zeroth Law defines the equilibrium condition ‚ÄĒ when two systems share the same temperature, net heat transfer ceases. It is the condition that *precedes* the First Law's energy balance. --- ## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule (ESE Pattern):** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. The standard sub-partition is **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, OR **(a) 4 + (b) 10**, depending on module. For Module 1 of PEPOT633, the most common pattern is **(a) 7 + (b) 7**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** > **(a)** State and explain the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics. Discuss its consequences on the measurement of temperature. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A constant-volume gas thermometer containing an ideal gas records a pressure of $80 \text{ kPa}$ at the triple point of water ($273.16 \text{ K}$). When the same bulb is placed in a furnace, the pressure rises to $120 \text{ kPa}$. Calculate the temperature of the furnace. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Formal Statement (2 Marks):** The Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics states: *"If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other."* It is the logical basis for the existence of temperature as a state property. **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Interpretation (2 Marks):** Consider three systems A, B, and C. If A and C are in thermal equilibrium, they share a common property ‚ÄĒ temperature. Likewise, if B and C are in equilibrium, they share the same temperature. By transitivity, A and B must also be in thermal equilibrium when brought into contact. This transitivity is what allows a third system (the thermometer) to act as a reference. **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Consequences on Temperature Measurement (3 Marks):** 1. **Existence of Temperature:** The law guarantees a unique, single-valued state variable called temperature. 2. **Universality:** Any calibrated device can serve as a thermometer, provided the transitivity property holds. 3. **Foundation of Thermometric Scales:** Empirically, we can define temperature via a thermometric property $X$: $$ T = T_1 + \frac{(X - X_1)}{(X_2 - X_1)}(T_2 - T_1) $$ 4. **Reference Standards:** The triple point of water at $273.16 \text{ K}$ is used as the universal reference, since it is highly reproducible. #### Model Solution to (b): **Given Data:** - $P_{\text{tp}} = 80 \text{ kPa}$ at $T_{\text{tp}} = 273.16 \text{ K}$ - $P_{\text{furnace}} = 120 \text{ kPa}$ at $T = ?$ - Constant-volume ideal gas thermometer. **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Apply Gay-Lussac's Law (Constant Volume):** For a constant volume, the pressure of an ideal gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature: $$ \frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2} $$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Substitute Values:** $$ \frac{80 \text{ kPa}}{273.16 \text{ K}} = \frac{120 \text{ kPa}}{T_{\text{furnace}}} $$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Solve for $T_{\text{furnace}}$:** $$ T_{\text{furnace}} = \frac{120 \times 273.16}{80} = \frac{32779.2}{80} $$ $$ T_{\text{furnace}} = 409.74 \text{ K} $$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Convert to ¬įC (Optional, but adds marks):** $$ T(^{\circ}\text{C}) = 409.74 - 273.15 = 136.59 \text{ ¬įC} $$ **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{T_{\text{furnace}} = 409.74 \text{ K} = 136.59 \text{ ¬įC}} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice Alternative **[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** > **(a)** With a neat labelled sketch, explain the working principle of a **constant-volume gas thermometer**. Derive the working equation. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** The electrical resistance of a platinum RTD is $100 \ \Omega$ at the ice point ($0 \text{ ¬įC}$) and $138.5 \ \Omega$ at the steam point ($100 \text{ ¬įC}$). If the RTD is placed in a process stream and the resistance reads $125 \ \Omega$, determine the temperature of the stream in ¬įC and K. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Construction (2 Marks):** A constant-volume gas thermometer consists of: - A **bulb (B)** filled with an ideal gas (helium or nitrogen) connected via a capillary tube. - A **mercury manometer (M)** with a fixed reference leg and an adjustable leg. - The mercury level in the variable leg is always adjusted to keep the gas volume **constant**. **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Working Principle (2 Marks):** When the bulb is placed in a system of unknown temperature $T$, the gas expands. The mercury column is adjusted to maintain constant gas volume. The change in mercury height $\Delta h$ corresponds to a change in gas pressure $\Delta P$. Since $V$ is constant, $P \propto T$ (Gay-Lussac's Law). **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Derivation of Working Equation (3 Marks):** For an ideal gas at constant volume: $$ \frac{P}{T} = \text{constant} = \frac{P_{\text{tp}}}{273.16 \text{ K}} $$ Let the pressure at the triple point of water be $P_{\text{tp}}$ and the pressure at the unknown temperature be $P$: $$ T = 273.16 \times \frac{P}{P_{\text{tp}}} $$ This equation is **independent of the gas used** in the limit of low pressure, satisfying the Zeroth Law's requirement for a universal temperature scale. #### Model Solution to (b): **Given Data:** - $R_1 = 100 \ \Omega$ at $T_1 = 0 \text{ ¬įC}$ - $R_2 = 138.5 \ \Omega$ at $T_2 = 100 \text{ ¬įC}$ - $R_x = 125 \ \Omega$ at $T_x = ?$ **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Apply Linear Interpolation (Zeroth Law-based):** $$ T_x = T_1 + \frac{(R_x - R_1)}{(R_2 - R_1)} \times (T_2 - T_1) $$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Substitute Values:** $$ T_x = 0 + \frac{(125 - 100)}{(138.5 - 100)} \times (100 - 0) $$ $$ T_x = \frac{25}{38.5} \times 100 $$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute:** $$ T_x = 64.935 \text{ ¬įC} $$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Convert to Kelvin:** $$ T_x(\text{K}) = 64.935 + 273.15 = 338.085 \text{ K} $$ **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{T_x = 64.94 \text{ ¬įC} = 338.09 \text{ K}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ‚ÄĒ Common Mistakes to Avoid:** > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing ¬įC and K in the interpolation formula. Always work in one consistent scale, then convert at the end. > 2. **Forgetting Absolute Zero:** The formula $T(\text{K}) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$ is *not* exact for older data; the triple point is $273.16 \text{ K}$. Use $273.15$ for ice point, $273.16$ for triple point. > 3. **Skipping the Linear Interpolation Justification:** KTU examiners award marks for *citing the Zeroth Law* as the basis for assuming linearity between two reference points. > 4. **Algebraic Error in Cross-Multiplication:** When solving $P_1/T_1 = P_2/T_2$, students often write $T_2 = P_1 T_1 / P_2$ (inverted ratio). Double-check before finalizing. > 5. **Misidentifying Reference States:** "Ice point" = $0 \text{ ¬įC}$ at 1 atm; "Steam point" = $100 \text{ ¬įC}$ at 1 atm; "Triple point" = $0.01 \text{ ¬įC}$ at $611.657 \text{ Pa}$. These are **distinct** states. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision ‚ÄĒ High-Density Summary** - ‚≠ź **Zeroth Law Statement:** If $T_A = T_C$ and $T_B = T_C$, then $T_A = T_B$. (Logical transitivity.) - ‚≠ź **Origin:** Stated by **Ralph Fowler in 1931**; named "Zeroth" because it logically precedes the First and Second Laws. - ‚≠ź **Core Implication:** Justifies the **existence of temperature** as a measurable state function. - ‚≠ź **Foundation of Thermometry:** All thermometers (gas, mercury, RTD, thermocouple) are valid *because* of the Zeroth Law. - ‚≠ź **Linear Interpolation Formula:** $$ T = T_1 + \frac{(X - X_1)}{(X_2 - X_1)} \times (T_2 - T_1) $$ - ‚≠ź **Triple Point of Water:** $273.16 \text{ K}$ (exact SI definition). - ‚≠ź **Absolute Zero:** $0 \text{ K} = -273.15 \text{ ¬įC}$ (unattainable, Third Law). - ‚≠ź **Temperature Difference Equivalence:** $\Delta T(\text{K}) = \Delta T(^{\circ}\text{C}) = \tfrac{5}{9} \Delta T(^{\circ}\text{F})$. - ‚≠ź **Gas Thermometer Equation:** $T = 273.16 \times (P / P_{\text{tp}})$ at constant volume. - ‚≠ź **Zeroth Law ‚Č† First Law:** Zeroth Law = thermal equilibrium (no heat flow); First Law = energy conservation (heat ‚Üí work conversion). - ‚≠ź **KTU-Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always *state the law*, *explain the consequences*, *solve a numerical*, and *state assumptions*. Missing any one step typically costs 2-3 marks.

topics.title: First Law of Thermodynamics: Energy Balance for Closed Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** First Law of Thermodynamics: Energy Balance for Closed Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **First Law of Thermodynamics** is a statement of the **Conservation of Energy Principle**. It asserts that energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another or transferred between the system and its surroundings.

For a **closed system** (a system of fixed mass, across whose boundary only energy ‚ÄĒ and not matter ‚ÄĒ can be exchanged), the First Law is mathematically stated as:

$$\Delta E_{\text{system}} = Q - W$$

where the total energy $E$ of a closed system is the sum of:
- **Internal Energy** ($U$) ‚ÄĒ the microscopic kinetic and potential energies of molecules,
- **Kinetic Energy** ($KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$) ‚ÄĒ the macroscopic energy of motion,
- **Potential Energy** ($PE = mgz$) ‚ÄĒ the macroscopic energy of position in a gravitational field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:** A *closed system* is defined as a *control mass* ‚ÄĒ a fixed quantity of matter chosen for study. No mass crosses the system boundary, but heat ($Q$) and work ($W$) can be exchanged.

## 1.2 The Expanded First Law (With KE and PE)

When macroscopic energies are non-negligible (as in open flow systems, mixing tanks with agitation, or moving pistons), the general energy balance is:

$$\Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE = Q - W$$

In differential form:

$$dU + d(KE) + d(PE) = \delta Q - \delta W$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the strange notation?** $dU$ uses an *exact* differential because $U$ is a **state function** (path-independent). The crossbar $\delta$ on $\delta Q$ and $\delta W$ signals that heat and work are **path functions** (they depend on *how* the process is carried out, not just the start/end states).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Bank Account" Model

Think of the **system as a bank account**:
- **Internal Energy ($U$)** = The account balance. It has a definite value at any moment.
- **Heat ($Q$)** = Money **deposited** into the account from the surroundings (energy entering the system).
- **Work ($W$)** = Money **withdrawn** by the system to do something on the surroundings (energy leaving the system).
- The First Law simply says: *What you end up with equals what came in minus what went out*. The balance never magically appears or disappears.

A more physical analogy: imagine a **sealed pressure cooker (closed system)** on a stove. The flame supplies heat $Q$, the gas inside expands and pushes the pressure-relief valve doing boundary work $W$, and the temperature (a manifestation of internal energy $U$) rises. The energy the gas *gains* as internal energy must equal the heat *in* minus the work *out*.

## 1.4 The KTU-Mandated Sign Convention

KTU follows the **thermodynamic sign convention** (sometimes called the "engineer's sign convention"):

| Quantity | Positive When... | Negative When... |
|----------|------------------|------------------|
| **Q (Heat)** | Energy enters the system (heat absorbed) | Energy leaves the system (heat rejected) |
| **W (Work)** | Energy leaves the system (work done *by* the system on surroundings) | Energy enters the system (work done *on* the system by surroundings) |

> [!WARNING]
