<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# ūüďĚ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ūüĒĻ Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2022]**

*State the First Law of Thermodynamics. List any three limitations of the First Law.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The First Law of Thermodynamics states that energy is conserved: for a closed system, the change in internal energy equals heat added minus work done ($dU = dQ - dW$); for a cyclic process, $\oint dQ = \oint dW$.

Three limitations are:

1. It does not specify the **direction** of a process.
2. It does not distinguish between the **quality** of heat and work.
3. It does not predict the **feasibility** of a process.

> **[Marking Scheme: 1 mark for statement, 1 mark each for any 3 limitations.]**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2023]**

*Why does the First Law permit a heat engine with 100% efficiency, but no such engine exists in practice?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

For a cyclic heat engine, the First Law gives $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_C$. If we assume $Q_C = 0$, then $W_{net} = Q_H$ and $\eta = 100\%$. The First Law does not forbid $Q_C = 0$.

However, the **Kelvin-Planck statement of the Second Law** explicitly forbids a cyclic device that converts heat from a single reservoir entirely into work. A heat engine *must* reject some heat ($Q_C > 0$) to operate in a cycle, so $\eta < 100\%$ in practice.

> **[Marking Scheme: 1 mark for First Law derivation, 1 mark for Second Law explanation, 1 mark for final conclusion.]**

---

## ūüĒĻ Part B ‚ÄĒ Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **INSTRUCTIONS:** Answer ONE question. Each has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain in detail any **four limitations** of the First Law of Thermodynamics. For each limitation, give one relevant example.

**(b)** A heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at $T_H = 900\, K$ and a cold reservoir at $T_C = 300\, K$. It absorbs $Q_H = 1500\, kJ$ from the hot reservoir and delivers $W = 1100\, kJ$ of work per cycle. Determine:
1. The heat rejected to the cold reservoir.
2. The actual thermal efficiency.
3. The maximum (Carnot) efficiency.
4. Whether this engine violates the Second Law.

#### ūüďė Model Solution

**(a) Four Limitations (4 √ó 1.75 = 7 marks)**

| # | Limitation | Example |
|---|------------|---------|
| 1 | Cannot specify **direction** of a process | Heat flows from hot to cold, never spontaneously from cold to hot |
| 2 | Cannot distinguish **quality** of energy | Work fully convertible to heat, but not vice versa |
| 3 | Cannot predict **feasibility** | Engine delivering more work than Carnot limit is "feasible" by First Law but impossible |
| 4 | Cannot establish **equilibrium criterion** | No rule to predict final temperature of mixed bodies |

**(b) Numerical (7 marks)**

**(b.1)** Apply First Law:

$$Q_C = Q_H - W = 1500 - 1100 = 400\, kJ \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(b.2)** Actual efficiency:

$$\eta_{actual} = \frac{W}{Q_H} = \frac{1100}{1500} = 0.7333 = 73.33\% \quad \text{[1.5 marks]}$$

**(b.3)** Carnot efficiency:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{900} = 1 - 0.3333 = 0.6667 = 66.67\% \quad \text{[1.5 marks]}$$

**(b.4)** Comparison:

$$\eta_{actual} = 73.33\% > \eta_{Carnot} = 66.67\%$$

This **violates the Kelvin-Planck statement** of the Second Law. The proposed engine is thermodynamically impossible.

$$\boxed{\text{The engine is NOT feasible despite satisfying the First Law.}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024]**

**(a)** State the **Kelvin-Planck** and **Clausius** statements of the Second Law. Prove that they are **equivalent** using a logical argument.

**(b)** A proposed refrigerator operates between a cold space at $T_C = 263\, K$ and a warm room at $T_H = 303\, K$. For every $1000\, kJ$ of heat extracted from the cold space, $200\, kJ$ of work is supplied to the refrigerator. Check whether this refrigerator is feasible.

#### ūüďė Model Solution

**(a) Statements and Equivalence (7 marks)**

**Kelvin-Planck Statement:**
> It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than absorbing heat from a single reservoir and performing an equivalent amount of work.

**Clausius Statement:**
> It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than transferring heat from a cooler body to a hotter body.

**[1 mark each for the two statements]**

**Proof of Equivalence (reductio ad absurdum):**

Suppose the Clausius statement is **false** ‚ÄĒ a refrigerator exists that transfers $Q_C$ from cold to hot without work input. Place a real heat engine between the same reservoirs; the engine takes $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir, produces work $W$, and rejects $Q_C$ to the cold reservoir.

By the impossible refrigerator, this rejected $Q_C$ is automatically returned to the hot reservoir. The combined system then takes $Q_H - Q_C$ from the hot reservoir and produces $W$ of work, with **no net heat exchanged with the cold reservoir**. This is precisely a Kelvin-Planck violation.

By contradiction, if Clausius is false, then Kelvin-Planck is also false. By reversing the argument, the converse holds. Therefore, **Clausius $\Leftrightarrow$ Kelvin-Planck**.

**[5 marks for the equivalence proof with diagram description]**

**(b) Numerical Feasibility Check (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Heat extracted from cold reservoir: $Q_C = 1000\, kJ$
**Step 2:** Work input: $W = 200\, kJ$
**Step 3:** Heat rejected to hot reservoir (from First Law):

$$Q_H = Q_C + W = 1000 + 200 = 1200\, kJ \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the **Carnot COP** for the refrigerator:

$$COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_C}{T_H - T_C} = \frac{263}{303 - 263} = \frac{263}{40} = 6.575 \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the **actual COP**:

$$COP_{actual} = \frac{Q_C}{W} = \frac{1000}{200} = 5.0 \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 6:** Comparison:

$$COP_{actual} = 5.0 < COP_{Carnot} = 6.575$$

Since the actual COP is **less than** the Carnot COP, the refrigerator **does not violate** the Second Law and is **thermodynamically feasible**.

$$\boxed{\text{The refrigerator is feasible.}} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **ūüĒī KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not declare a process feasible just because the First Law is satisfied.** Always cross-check with the Second Law (Carnot efficiency / COP limits).
> 2. **Temperature in Kelvin, always.** Using Celsius in the Carnot formula will lose 2‚Äď3 marks.
> 3. **Sign convention errors:** Refrigerator COP = $Q_C / W$, not $Q_H / W$. Mixing these up is a frequent mistake.
> 4. **Two statements, not one:** KTU frequently asks both Kelvin-Planck *and* Clausius. Remembering only one loses 1‚Äď2 marks.
> 5. **Equivalence proof must use a logical contradiction, not just a statement.** Writing "they are equivalent" without proof gives partial credit only.

---

## ūüĒĻ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **‚ö° Rapid Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Limitations of First Law**

- [x] First Law: $dU = dQ - dW$ (closed system); $\oint dQ = \oint dW$ (cycle).
- [x] **Five Limitations:** Direction, Quality, Feasibility, Equilibrium, Cyclic Conversion.
- [x] First Law is **silent** on the direction of spontaneous processes.
- [x] First Law cannot distinguish heat quality from work quality.
- [x] First Law cannot establish the equilibrium criterion.
- [x] **Kelvin-Planck Statement:** No 100%-efficient cyclic heat engine.
- [x] **Clausius Statement:** No self-acting refrigerator.
- [x] The two statements are **logically equivalent**.
- [x] **Clausius Inequality:** $\oint \frac{dQ}{T} \leq 0$ (equality for reversible, strict for irreversible).
- [x] **Carnot Efficiency:** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$ is the **upper bound**.
- [x] **Refrigerator COP:** $COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_C}{T_H - T_C}$ is the **upper bound**.
- [x] A cycle is feasible only if $\eta_{actual} \leq \eta_{Carnot}$ (engines) and $COP_{actual} \leq COP_{Carnot}$ (refrigerators).
- [x] Always use **absolute temperature (Kelvin)** in Carnot equations.
- [x] The Second Law introduces **entropy ($S$)** as the property that overcomes the qualitative limitations of the First Law.

> **End of Notes ‚ÄĒ Limitations of First Law | PEPOT633 Module 1**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Second Law of Thermodynamics: Carnot's Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Second Law of Thermodynamics: Carnot's Principles # MODULE 1 ‚ÄĒ SECOND LAW OF THERMODYNAMICS: CARNOT'S PRINCIPLES ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Carnot's Principles constitute the foundational framework of the Second Law of Thermodynamics**, articulating the upper limit of thermal efficiency for any heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs. Formulated by **Sadi Carnot (1824)**, these principles define the benchmark (the *Carnot efficiency*) against which all real and idealized heat engines are compared. The two pillars of Carnot's theorem are formally stated as: - **Carnot Principle I (Equality of Reversible Engines):** *All reversible heat engines operating between the same two constant-temperature reservoirs (TH and TL) exhibit the same thermal efficiency*, irrespective of the working fluid, design, or mechanism employed. - **Carnot Principle II (Superiority of Reversible Engines):** *No heat engine ‚ÄĒ whether real or hypothetical ‚ÄĒ operating between two given thermal reservoirs can surpass the efficiency of a reversible (Carnot) heat engine operating between the same reservoirs*. Equivalently, the **Carnot cycle represents the maximum possible thermal efficiency** attainable for any cycle. > [!NOTE] > **Working Substance Irrelevance:** A pivotal consequence of Principle I is that thermal efficiency between two given temperatures is a property of the reservoirs alone, **not** of the working substance. This makes Carnot efficiency a *universal benchmark* across all engineering disciplines. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine two water tanks placed at different elevations ‚ÄĒ one at the **top of a hill** (reservoir TH) and one at the **valley floor** (reservoir TL). Water flowing downhill can drive a turbine and produce work. Now consider: - **Best-case (Carnot / reversible):** A perfectly smooth, frictionless, ideal turbine extracts every possible joule from the descending water ‚ÄĒ the *reversible* scenario. - **Reality (irreversible):** Real turbines lose energy to friction, turbulence, and leakage ‚ÄĒ analogous to dissipative losses in a real heat engine. Carnot's principles tell us: **No matter how cleverly you design the real turbine (engine), you can never extract more energy than the ideal frictionless one would**. The "lost work" represents the **irreversibility** inherent in every real process. > [!NOTE] > **Universal Constant Anchor:** The Kelvin temperature scale is the only scale that makes Carnot's efficiency expression (ő∑ = 1 ‚ąí TL/TH) strictly universal. This is why **T must always be in Kelvin (K)** for Second Law calculations ‚ÄĒ never Celsius or Fahrenheit. **Standard atmospheric pressure** for reference reservoir conditions: **P¬į = 101.325 kPa**. ### 1.3 The Carnot Cycle ‚ÄĒ A Conceptual Primer The **Carnot cycle** is the thermodynamic realization of Carnot's principles. It consists of four internally reversible processes: | Step | Process | Description | |---|---|---| | **1 ‚Üí 2** | Isothermal Expansion at TH | Heat **QH** absorbed from high-temperature reservoir | | **2 ‚Üí 3** | Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic) Expansion | Temperature drops from TH to TL | | **3 ‚Üí 4** | Isothermal Compression at TL | Heat **QL** rejected to low-temperature reservoir | | **4 ‚Üí 1** | Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic) Compression | Temperature rises from TL back to TH | **Cyclic Closure:** Because internal energy is a point function, őĒU = 0 over a complete cycle ‚Üí **Wnet = Qnet = QH ‚ąí QL**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Carnot's Principles ### Principle I ‚ÄĒ Equality of Reversible Engines 1. **Assumption:** Consider two reversible heat engines, *A* and *B*, operating between thermal reservoirs at TH and TL. 2. **Hypothesis by Contradiction:** Suppose engine *A* is more efficient than engine *B* (ő∑A > ő∑B). 3. **Reversibility Utilization:** Because *B* is reversible, we may reverse it and use it as a **heat pump / refrigerator**. 4. **Composite System Analysis:** Couple *A* (forward) driving *B* (reversed). The composite system exchanges heat **only with TL** (reservoir coupling vanishes for TH). 5. **Second Law Conflict:** This violates the **Clausius / Kelvin-Planck statement** ‚ÄĒ spontaneous heat flow from cold to hot occurs without external work input. 6. **Conclusion:** ő∑A cannot exceed ő∑B. By symmetry of argument, ő∑A = ő∑B. **Q.E.D.** ### Principle II ‚ÄĒ Superiority of Reversible Engines 1. **Assumption:** A real (irreversible) heat engine *I* and a reversible engine *R* share the same reservoirs TH, TL. 2. **Hypothesis:** Assume ő∑I > ő∑R. 3. **Reversibility Utilization:** Reverse *R* and run it from the work output of *I*. 4. **Composite Result:** The combined system now transfers heat from TL to TH spontaneously ‚ÄĒ violating the **Clausius statement**. 5. **Conclusion:** ő∑I ‚Č§ ő∑R. Equality holds **only if I is also reversible**. ### 2.2 Mathematical Foundation ‚ÄĒ Why ő∑ Depends Only on Reservoir Temperatures Starting from the Clausius inequality for any cycle: $$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_{boundary}} \leq 0$$ For a **reversible** cycle, equality holds: $$\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$$ Applying this to the Carnot cycle (TH = constant during heat addition; TL = constant during heat rejection): $$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$ This directly yields the Carnot efficiency expression, demonstrating that **only the reservoir temperatures govern ő∑**. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All temperatures in the formulas below must be in Kelvin (K)** ‚ÄĒ convert from ¬įC by adding **273.15**. | # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Legend | Condition / Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Carnot Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | TH = hot reservoir temp, TL = cold reservoir temp | **Upper limit for any heat engine** | | 2 | Alternate Efficiency Form | $\eta_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_H}$ | Same as above | Useful when temperature *difference* is given | | 3 | Net Work Output | $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ | QH = heat supplied, QL = heat rejected | Always **positive** for an engine | | 4 | Carnot Heat Ratio | $\dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} = \dfrac{Q_L}{T_L}$ | Clausius equality for reversible cycle | **Hallmark identity of Carnot cycle** | | 5 | **COP ‚ÄĒ Refrigerator** | $COP_{R} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | COP = desired effect / work input | Desired effect = QL (heat removed from cold space) | | 6 | **COP ‚ÄĒ Heat Pump** | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Same reservoir pair as refrigerator | Desired effect = QH (heat delivered to hot space) | | 7 | Relation between COPs | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | Direct algebraic consequence | Same TH, TL | | 8 | Clausius Inequality (General) | $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_{b}} \leq 0$ | Tb = boundary temperature | Equality ‚ÜĒ reversible cycle | | 9 | Carnot Equivalent of T | $T = \dfrac{Q}{Q_H - Q_L} \cdot T_{ref}$ | Tref = reference (often TH) | Defines thermodynamic temperature scale | | 10 | Work Ratio | $WR = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \eta_{Carnot}$ | For Carnot cycle | Confirms ő∑ is also a work ratio | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Power Generation:** The Carnot efficiency sets the **theoretical ceiling** for steam, gas, and nuclear power plants. Modern combined-cycle plants achieve ~60‚Äď64% efficiency ‚ÄĒ still below the Carnot bound for their operating temperatures. - **Refrigeration & HVAC:** COPR directly dictates the **minimum compressor work** required. Industrial chillers use multi-stage cycles to approximate Carnot performance economically. - **Cryogenics:** Liquefaction of gases (N2, O2, He) relies on minimizing the TL/TH ratio and maximizing reversibility ‚ÄĒ Carnot principles guide every stage. - **Engine Diagnostics:** Deviation of measured ő∑ from ő∑Carnot quantifies the **irreversibility** and **exergy destruction** of real processes. > [!NOTE] > **Exergy Insight:** The difference between actual work output and Carnot (maximum) work output is precisely the **exergy destroyed** ‚ÄĒ a critical concept for sustainable process design in chemical engineering thermodynamics. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Carnot Thermal Efficiency We now derive ő∑Carnot rigorously, following the **exact valuation style** expected in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. ### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Establish the First Law for a Cyclic Device For any heat engine operating in a cycle, the change in internal energy over one complete cycle is zero because internal energy is a state function: $$\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$$ By the **First Law of Thermodynamics**, the net heat transfer equals the net work output: $$W_{net} = Q_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$ **Logic Note:** This is the energy-balance backbone of every cyclic engine analysis. The minus sign appears because QL is conventionally defined as heat *leaving* the system. ### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply the Second Law to the Carnot Cycle Because the Carnot cycle is composed entirely of **reversible processes**, the **Clausius inequality collapses to equality**: $$\oint_{Carnot} \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T_{boundary}} = 0$$ **Logic Note:** Reversibility is the key enabler. For any *irreversible* cycle, the inequality would be strictly negative, leading to lower efficiency ‚ÄĒ confirming Principle II. ### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Decompose the Cyclic Integral Over Four Processes The integral splits into four segments corresponding to the four legs of the Carnot cycle: $$\int_{1}^{2} \frac{\delta Q}{T} + \int_{2}^{3} \frac{\delta Q}{T} + \int_{3}^{4} \frac{\delta Q}{T} + \int_{4}^{1} \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$$ **Logic Note:** Each segment must be evaluated using the **boundary (reservoir) temperature** at which heat crosses the system boundary. ### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Evaluate Each Integral **Process 1‚Üí2 (Isothermal Expansion at TH):** Heat QH enters reversibly at constant TH: $$\int_{1}^{2} \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H}$$ **Process 2‚Üí3 (Reversible Adiabatic Expansion):** By definition, adiabatic means **őīQ = 0**: $$\int_{2}^{3} \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$$ **Process 3‚Üí4 (Isothermal Compression at TL):** Heat QL leaves reversibly at constant TL (note: QL is magnitude; the signed heat is ‚ąíQL): $$\int_{3}^{4} \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \frac{-Q_L}{T_L}$$ **Process 4‚Üí1 (Reversible Adiabatic Compression):** Again, őīQ = 0: $$\int_{4}^{1} \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$$ **Logic Note:** The two adiabatic segments contribute *zero* because no heat crosses the boundary ‚ÄĒ only work. The remaining contributions come solely from the two isothermal legs. ### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Combine and Simplify Summing the four contributions: $$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + 0 + \frac{-Q_L}{T_L} + 0 = 0$$ Rearranging to isolate the heat ratio: $$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$ **Logic Note:** This is the **defining identity of the Carnot cycle** ‚ÄĒ the ratios of heat exchanged to reservoir temperatures are equal for the source and sink. ### Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Convert Heat Ratio to Efficiency Form Recall that ő∑ is defined as: $$\eta = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$ From Step 5, take the ratio QL/QH: $$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ **Logic Note:** This is the pivotal substitution. The heat *quantity* ratio becomes a *temperature* ratio because of the reversible constraint. ### Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Final Closed-Form Expression Substituting back: $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ Or equivalently: $$\eta_{Carnot} = \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H}$$ **Logic Note:** Both forms are accepted by KTU examiners. The first is more common; the second is useful when only the *temperature difference* is given. ### Step 8 ‚ÄĒ Derived Algebraic Identity for the Heat Exchanged Rearranging the heat ratio gives the work-to-heat relationship directly: $$W_{net} = Q_H \left(1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}\right) = Q_H \cdot \eta_{Carnot}$$ **Logic Note:** Multiplying by QH converts the dimensionless efficiency back into an absolute work magnitude. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of COPR and COPHP ### Coefficient of Performance ‚ÄĒ Refrigerator (COPR) The COP of a refrigerator is the **desired effect** (heat extracted from the cold reservoir, QL) divided by the **work input** (Wnet): $$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$$ Apply the First Law to the refrigerator cycle: $$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$ Substitute: $$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$$ Divide numerator and denominator by QH: $$COP_R = \frac{Q_L/Q_H}{1 - Q_L/Q_H}$$ Using the Carnot heat ratio (QL/QH = TL/TH): $$COP_R = \frac{T_L/T_H}{1 - T_L/T_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ ### Coefficient of Performance ‚ÄĒ Heat Pump (COPHP) By analogous reasoning, with desired effect = QH: $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$$ Using QL/QH = TL/TH, divide through: $$COP_{HP} = \frac{1}{1 - T_L/T_H} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$ Confirming the algebraic identity: $$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$$ --- ## 3.3 Numerical Illustration ‚ÄĒ KTU Examination Pattern Problem > **Given:** A Carnot heat engine operates between a high-temperature reservoir at **TH = 600 K** and a low-temperature reservoir at **TL = 300 K**. Heat input QH = **1000 kJ** per cycle. > > **Required:** (a) Thermal efficiency, (b) Net work output, (c) Heat rejected, (d) COP if the cycle is reversed and used as a refrigerator between the same reservoirs. ### Solution **(a) Thermal Efficiency:** $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{600} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.50$$ So ő∑Carnot = **50 %**. **(b) Net Work Output:** $$W_{net} = \eta \cdot Q_H = 0.50 \times 1000 = 500 \text{ kJ}$$ **(c) Heat Rejected:** $$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 1000 - 500 = 500 \text{ kJ}$$ Cross-check using the ratio identity: $$Q_L = Q_H \cdot \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1000 \times \frac{300}{600} = 500 \text{ kJ} \quad \checkmark$$ **(d) COP as Refrigerator:** $$COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{300}{600 - 300} = \frac{300}{300} = 1.00$$ So **COPR = 1.0**, meaning every **1 kJ** of work input removes **1 kJ** of heat from the cold reservoir. > [!NOTE] > **Quick Sanity Check:** When TH = 2¬∑TL, ő∑ = 50% always. The numerical symmetry here is intentional ‚ÄĒ KTU examiners frequently use round numbers to test conceptual clarity over arithmetic. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Carnot Cycle ‚ÄĒ Block-Level Functional Architecture The following diagram captures the functional flow of the Carnot cycle, including energy exchanges and state transitions between the four cardinal states. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[State 1: Compressed Gas at T_L] -->|Process 1 to 2
Isothermal Heat Addition
Q_H absorbed from T_H reservoir| B[State 2: Expanded Gas at T_H] B -->|Process 2 to 3
Reversible Adiabatic Expansion
W out, T drops T_H to T_L| C[State 3: Further Expanded Gas at T_L] C -->|Process 3 to 4
Isothermal Heat Rejection
Q_L rejected to T_L reservoir| D[State 4: Compressed Gas at T_L] D -->|Process 4 to 1
Reversible Adiabatic Compression
W in, T rises T_L to T_H| A B -.->|Net Work Output W_net = Q_H minus Q_L| E[Output Shaft Work] A -.->|Cycle Closure Delta U equals 0| F[First Law Closure] ``` ## 4.2 P-V and T-S Architectural Representation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PV[BLOCK A - P V DIAGRAM] P1[Point 1 High P T L] -->|Isothermal Expansion
Curved Path| P2[Point 2 Low P T H] P2 -->|Adiabatic Expansion
Steep Curve Down| P3[Point 3 Very Low P T L] P3 -->|Isothermal Compression
Lower Curved Path| P4[Point 4 Mid P T L] P4 -->|Adiabatic Compression
Steep Curve Up| P1 end subgraph TS[BLOCK B - T S DIAGRAM RECTANGULAR] T1[Point 1 T L Low S] -->|Constant T H
Horizontal Line Right| T2[Point 2 T H Higher S] T2 -->|Adiabatic
Vertical Line Down| T3[Point 3 T L Higher S] T3 -->|Constant T L
Horizontal Line Left| T4[Point 4 T L Low S] T4 -->|Adiabatic
Vertical Line Up| T1 end ``` ## 4.3 Carnot Principle Logical Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Two Thermal Reservoirs T_H and T_L] --> P1[Principle I Equality] R --> P2[Principle II Superiority] P1 --> R1[All Reversible Engines Give Same Eta] P2 --> R2[No Engine Beats Reversible Engine] R1 --> K[Key Consequence Eta is Property of Reservoirs Only] R2 --> K K --> A1[Refrigerator COP_R equals T_L over T_H minus T_L] K --> A2[Heat Pump COP_HP equals T_H over T_H minus T_L] K --> A3[Carnot Efficiency equals 1 minus T_L over T_H] ``` ## 4.4 Engine vs. Refrigerator vs. Heat Pump Functional Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR H[Hot Reservoir T_H] -->|Q_H IN| ENG[Carnot Heat Engine] ENG -->|Q_L OUT| C[Cold Reservoir T_L] ENG -->|W_net OUT| W[Shaft Work Output] H -->|Q_H OUT| HP[Carnot Heat Pump REVERSED] HP -->|Q_L IN| C HP -->|W_net IN| WIN[Work Input] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A ‚ÄĒ Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]` > **State and explain Carnot's two principles. Why is the Carnot cycle considered the benchmark for all heat engines?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Principle I (1 Mark):** All reversible heat engines operating between the same two thermal reservoirs (TH and TL) have the **same thermal efficiency**, independent of the working substance. - **Principle II (1 Mark):** No heat engine operating between two given reservoirs can be more efficient than a **reversible** (Carnot) engine operating between the **same** reservoirs. The Carnot efficiency is therefore the **maximum attainable** efficiency. - **Benchmark Justification (1 Mark):** Since any real (irreversible) engine must have ő∑ ‚Č§ ő∑Carnot, the Carnot cycle sets the **theoretical ceiling**. All real-engine performance metrics (thermal efficiency, second-law efficiency, exergy efficiency) are referenced against this ideal. --- ### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024]` > **Derive the expression for the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a Carnot refrigerator in terms of reservoir temperatures.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1 Mark):** $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$, where QL is the heat extracted from the cold reservoir. - **First-Law Application (1 Mark):** $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ and from the Carnot identity $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$, hence $Q_H = Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_L}$. - **Final Result (1 Mark):** $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ This represents the **upper limit** of COP for any refrigerator operating between TH and TL. --- ## PART B ‚ÄĒ Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ‚ÄĒ Most Common Mark Losses:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert ¬įC to Kelvin** ‚ÄĒ direct failure of every subsequent numerical answer (‚ąí2 to ‚ąí4 marks). > 2. **Using magnitude QL without sign convention** in Clausius integrals ‚ÄĒ examiners explicitly check sign discipline (‚ąí1 to ‚ąí2 marks). > 3. **Omitting "reversible" qualifier** when stating Carnot principles ‚ÄĒ partial loss on Principle II (‚ąí1 mark). > 4. **Confusing COPR with COPHP** ‚ÄĒ they are *not* interchangeable; the "desired effect" differs (‚ąí1 mark). > 5. **Skipping units in final answers** ‚ÄĒ KTU examiners deduct marks for unit-less numerical values. --- ### **Question B (A) ‚ÄĒ 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2024 ‚ÄĒ Modified]` > **(a)** A Carnot heat engine receives **800 kJ** of heat from a reservoir at **TH = 800 K** and rejects heat to a reservoir at **TL = 300 K**. Determine: >     (i) The thermal efficiency of the engine. >     (ii) The net work output per cycle. >     (iii) The heat rejected to the cold reservoir. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** State and prove Carnot's theorem (both principles) using a reversible vs. irreversible engine argument. Show that the thermal efficiency depends only on reservoir temperatures. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Question B (A)** #### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Numerical (7 Marks) **Given Data:** QH = 800 kJ, TH = 800 K, TL = 300 K. **(i) Thermal Efficiency (3 Marks):** Apply the Carnot efficiency formula directly: $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625$$ **Result:** ő∑Carnot = **62.5 %** (or 0.625, dimensionless). **(ii) Net Work Output (2 Marks):** $$W_{net} = \eta_{Carnot} \times Q_H = 0.625 \times 800 = 500 \text{ kJ}$$ **Result:** Wnet = **500 kJ per cycle**. **(iii) Heat Rejected (2 Marks):** Using the First Law energy balance: $$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 800 - 500 = 300 \text{ kJ}$$ Cross-check via the Carnot heat ratio: $$Q_L = Q_H \times \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 800 \times \frac{300}{800} = 300 \text{ kJ} \quad \checkmark$$ **Result:** QL = **300 kJ per cycle**. --- #### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Theory / Proof (7 Marks) **Statement of Carnot's Theorem (2 Marks):** - **Principle I:** The efficiency of any reversible heat engine operating between two given reservoirs is the **same** and depends only on the reservoir temperatures. - **Principle II:** No irreversible (real) heat engine operating between the same two reservoirs can be **more efficient** than a reversible engine. **Proof by Contradiction (3 Marks):** Consider a reversible engine R and an irreversible engine I, both operating between TH and TL. Assume, contrary to the theorem, that ő∑I > ő∑R. Since R is reversible, we can **reverse it** and use its work output to drive I in reverse. The composite device now transfers heat from TL to TH **without net work input** ‚ÄĒ a direct violation of the Clausius statement of the Second Law. By contradiction, ő∑I ‚Č§ ő∑R, with equality only if I is also reversible. **Dependence on Reservoir Temperatures Only (2 Marks):** For the reversible Carnot cycle, the Clausius equality gives: $$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$ Hence: $$\eta = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ The right-hand side involves **only TH and TL** ‚ÄĒ the working substance has cancelled. This proves ő∑ is a property of the reservoirs alone. --- ### **Question B (B) ‚ÄĒ 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024 ‚ÄĒ Modified]` > **(a)** A Carnot refrigerator is used to maintain a cold chamber at **TL = 263 K** while the surroundings are at **TH = 303 K**. If **5 kW** of work is supplied to the refrigerator, calculate: >     (i) The COP of the refrigerator. >     (ii) The rate of heat removal from the cold chamber. >     (iii) The rate of heat rejection to the surroundings. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Compare the performance of the above refrigerator when used as a **heat pump** for the same reservoir pair. Comment on the relationship between COPR and COPHP. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Question B (B)** #### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Refrigerator Analysis (7 Marks) **Given Data:** TL = 263 K, TH = 303 K, $\dot{W}_{net}$ = 5 kW. **(i) COP of the Refrigerator (3 Marks):** $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{303 - 263} = \frac{263}{40} = 6.575$$ **Result:** COPR = **6.575** (dimensionless). **(ii) Rate of Heat Removal from the Cold Chamber (2 Marks):** $$\dot{Q}_L = \text{COP}_R \times \dot{W}_{net} = 6.575 \times 5 = 32.875 \text{ kW}$$ **Result:** $\dot{Q}_L$ = **32.875 kW**. **(iii) Rate of Heat Rejection to Surroundings (2 Marks):** From the First Law for the refrigerator: $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{net} = 32.875 + 5 = 37.875 \text{ kW}$$ **Result:** $\dot{Q}_H$ = **37.875 kW**. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Insight:** Notice that **for every 1 kW of work input, the refrigerator removes ~6.58 kW of heat** from the cold chamber. This is *more than the work supplied* ‚ÄĒ possible because heat is also being pumped from the cold space, not created from work alone. --- #### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Heat Pump Comparison (7 Marks) **COP of the Heat Pump (3 Marks):** For the same reservoir pair TL = 263 K, TH = 303 K: $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{303}{303 - 263} = \frac{303}{40} = 7.575$$ **Result:** COPHP = **7.575**. **Verification of the Algebraic Identity (2 Marks):** $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1 = 6.575 + 1 = 7.575 \quad \checkmark$$ This confirms the universal relationship. **Comparative Comments (2 Marks):** - The **heat pump delivers more heat** to the hot space per unit work than the refrigerator removes from the cold space, with the **extra 1 kW being precisely the work input itself**. - Both devices are **physically identical** (same hardware, reversed cycle) ‚ÄĒ only the *desired output* differs: a refrigerator aims to *cool* (maximize QL); a heat pump aims to *heat* (maximize QH). - In practical heating applications, **heat pumps are more efficient** than direct electrical heating because they exploit environmental (low-grade) thermal energy. **Rate of Heat Delivery by the Heat Pump:** $$\dot{Q}_H^{HP} = \text{COP}_{HP} \times \dot{W}_{net} = 7.575 \times 5 = 37.875 \text{ kW}$$ Note: This equals the heat rejection rate calculated in part (a)(iii), **as expected** ‚ÄĒ same hardware, same energy balance. --- ## ūüďĆ TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for Carnot's Principles ‚ÄĒ KTU 2024 Module 1** ### Conceptual Pillars - ‚úÖ **Carnot Principle I:** All *reversible* heat engines between TH and TL ‚Üí **same ő∑**. - ‚úÖ **Carnot Principle II:** No engine (real or ideal) can **exceed** ő∑Carnot. - ‚úÖ ő∑ depends **only on TH and TL** ‚ÄĒ *not* on the working substance. - ‚úÖ The four Carnot processes: **Isothermal Expansion ‚Üí Adiabatic Expansion ‚Üí Isothermal Compression ‚Üí Adiabatic Compression**. ### Must-Know Formulas (in Kelvin) - ‚úÖ $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ - ‚úÖ $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ - ‚úÖ $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ - ‚úÖ $\dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} = \dfrac{Q_L}{T_L}$ (Carnot identity) - ‚úÖ $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ (cyclic First Law) ### Critical Examiner Rules - ‚úÖ Always use **Kelvin** ‚ÄĒ convert ¬įC by **+273.15**. - ‚úÖ Reversibility is a **prerequisite** for the Clausius *equality*; otherwise it is an *inequality*. - ‚úÖ Sign convention: QL leaving the system is **positive magnitude**, **negative sign** in Clausius integrals. - ‚úÖ State Carnot principles **with the word "reversible"** explicitly. ### Common Numerical Traps - ‚úÖ TL = 0 K ‚Üí ő∑ = 100% (theoretical limit, **unreachable** by Third Law). - ‚úÖ TH = TL ‚Üí ő∑ = 0% (no temperature difference ‚Üí no work). - ‚úÖ **Larger őĒT** raises ő∑; **higher TH** at fixed TL raises ő∑. ### Industrial / Process Significance - ‚úÖ Real power plants operate at ő∑ = **30‚Äď65%** ‚ÄĒ well below ő∑Carnot. - ‚úÖ Carnot principles underpin **exergy analysis**, **Second-Law efficiency**, and **process irreversibility quantification** in chemical engineering. ---

topics.title: Definition of Entropy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition of Entropy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

**Entropy** is a fundamental thermodynamic property that quantifies the degree of molecular disorder or the number of microscopic configurations (microstates) available to a thermodynamic system consistent with its macroscopic state. It was first introduced by **Rudolf Julius Emanuel Clausius** in **1865** as a state function that establishes the directional arrow of natural processes.

For a closed system undergoing a reversible process, the **differential change in entropy** $dS$ of the system is defined as the infinitesimal quantity of heat $\delta Q_{rev}$ transferred reversibly divided by the absolute temperature $T$ at which the transfer occurs:

$$dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Exact Wording Expected in Exams):**
> "Entropy is a thermodynamic state function, the differential change of which, for a reversible process, is equal to the heat transferred divided by the absolute temperature."
> 
> $$dS \equiv \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$
> 
> **Units (SI):** $\text{J}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$ per unit mass $\Rightarrow$ **kJ/(kg¬∑K)**
> **Units (CGS):** cal/(g¬∑K)
> **Universal Constant:** Boltzmann constant $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ Making Entropy Intuitive

Imagine a brand-new deck of 52 playing cards, perfectly arranged in sequence (‚ô†2, ‚ô†3, ‚Ä¶, ‚ô†A, ‚ô•2, ‚Ä¶, ‚ô•A, ‚ô¶‚Ä¶‚ô£A).

| State of Deck | Analogy in Thermodynamics | Entropy Level |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Perfectly ordered | Crystalline solid at 0 K | **Lowest** ($S \approx 0$) |
| Slightly shuffled | Liquid state | Intermediate |
| Thoroughly randomized | Gas expanding freely | **Highest** |

**Physical Meaning:** When you shuffle the deck once, you cannot predict the sequence ‚ÄĒ this is analogous to a gas expanding into a vacuum: countless invisible molecules scatter in countless ways. Entropy simply *counts* these possibilities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway for First-Time Learners:**
> - **Energy** tells you *how much* work a system can do.
> - **Entropy** tells you *how spread out* that energy has become among microscopic molecules.
> - **Heat flows from hot to cold** simply because spreading energy increases entropy ‚ÄĒ this is the deep root of the **Second Law**.

## 1.3 The Statistical (Microscopic) Definition ‚ÄĒ Boltzmann

For a system with $\Omega$ accessible microstates, the entropy is:

$$S = k_B \ln(\Omega)$$

> Where $k_B$ is the **Boltzmann constant** ($1.380649 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**) and $\Omega$ is the thermodynamic probability (number of microstates).

This was inscribed on Ludwig Boltzmann's tombstone in Vienna ‚ÄĒ it is, arguably, the most important equation linking the *invisible* microscopic world to *measurable* macroscopic thermodynamics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why is Entropy a State Function?

A **state function** depends only on the *initial* and *final* states of the system, NOT on the path taken. To prove entropy is a state function, Clausius used the **Carnot Cycle**.

**Logic Steps:**
1. For any reversible cycle, $\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$ (Clausius Theorem).
2. The integral of an exact differential around a closed path equals zero.
3. Therefore, $\frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ is the differential of a point function, i.e., $dS$.
4. Hence, $S$ is path-independent ‚ÄĒ a **state function**.

**Consequence:** $\Delta S$ between two equilibrium states is identical whether the process is reversible or irreversible, but $\Delta S$ for an irreversible process must be calculated via a constructed reversible path.

## 2.2 The Inequality of Clausius (The Real Second Law)

For any real (irreversible) process:

$$dS \geq \frac{\delta Q}{T}$$

- Equality holds for reversible processes.
- Inequality holds for irreversible (spontaneous, natural) processes.

The entropy change of an **isolated system** is therefore:

$$\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Pearl:** Always remember ‚ÄĒ entropy is **NOT conserved**. In every real, spontaneous process, the entropy of the universe *increases* monotonically. This is the mathematical heart of the **Second Law**.

## 2.3 How to Compute Entropy Change in Closed Systems

For a closed system with only PV-work and a reversible path:

$$dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = \frac{dU + P\,dV}{T}$$

Since $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$, this recovers the **Fundamental Thermodynamic Relation**:

$$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refrigeration & Liquefaction:** Calculating minimum work input requires evaluating entropy balances.
- **Power Plant Design (Rankine Cycle):** Turbine irreversibilities are quantified using entropy generation.
- **Distillation Columns:** Minimum reflux ratio requires entropy balances on each stage.
- **Chemical Reaction Equilibria:** $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ governs feasibility (Gibbs free energy).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Name / Context** | **Units** | **When to Use** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $dS = \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ | Clausius Definition | J/K | Definition in any reversible process |
| $\Delta S = \displaystyle\int_{1}^{2} \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ | Finite change in entropy | J/K | Reversible heating |
| $S = k_B \ln \Omega$ | Boltzmann Statistical Definition | J/K | Statistical mechanics |
| $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$ | Fundamental Relation | J | Combining First & Second Laws |
| $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$ | Enthalpy-Entropy Relation | J | Constant pressure processes |
| $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$ | Gibbs Free Energy Identity | J | $T,P$ natural variables |
| $S_{gen} \geq 0$ | Entropy Generation (2nd Law) | J/K | Any real process |
| $\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} \geq 0$ | Total Universe Entropy | J/K | Isolated systems |

## 2.6 Three Powerful Conceptual Facts

- **Fact 1:** Entropy has *no* absolute zero in classical thermodynamics, but the **Third Law** assigns $S = 0$ for a perfect crystal at **0 K**.
- **Fact 2:** Entropy is **extensive** ‚ÄĒ doubling the system mass doubles $S$.
- **Fact 3:** Entropy is the *conjugate* variable to temperature $T$ ‚ÄĒ just as pressure $P$ is conjugate to volume $V$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I ‚ÄĒ Clausius Definition from the Carnot Cycle

Consider a reversible **Carnot engine** operating between a hot reservoir at $T_H$ and a cold reservoir at $T_C$. The engine absorbs heat $Q_H$ from the hot source, performs work $W$, and rejects heat $Q_C$ to the cold source.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Apply the First Law (cyclic process, $\Delta U = 0$):**

$$W = Q_H - Q_C$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Carnot efficiency in two forms:**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = \frac{W}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

Substituting $W = Q_H - Q_C$:

$$\frac{Q_H - Q_C}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Algebraic simplification:**

$$1 - \frac{Q_C}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

Cancel the 1's on both sides:

$$\frac{Q_C}{Q_H} = \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Cross-multiplication gives the Clausius equality:**

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_C}{T_C} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_C}{T_C} = 0$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Generalization to any reversible cycle (Clausius Theorem):**

By decomposing any arbitrary reversible cycle into an infinite sum of infinitesimal Carnot cycles:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Identification of a state function:**

Since the cyclic integral of an exact differential is zero, we define:

$$dS \equiv \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

This completes the rigorous derivation of entropy from the Carnot cycle.

## 3.2 Derivation II ‚ÄĒ Entropy Change of an Ideal Gas (KTU Most-Frequent Question)

For an ideal gas in a closed system, the First Law gives:

$$\delta Q_{rev} = dU + P\,dV = nC_V\,dT + P\,dV$$

Using $PV = nRT$, so $P = \dfrac{nRT}{V}$, we obtain:

$$\delta Q_{rev} = nC_V\,dT + \frac{nRT}{V}\,dV$$

Divide both sides by $T$:

$$\frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = nC_V\,\frac{dT}{T} + nR\,\frac{dV}{V}$$

Recognizing the left-hand side as $dS$:

$$dS = nC_V\,\frac{dT}{T} + nR\,\frac{dV}{V}$$

**Integration from state 1 to state 2:**

$$\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{nC_V\,dT}{T} + \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{nR\,dV}{V}$$

For constant $C_V$:

$$\Delta S = nC_V \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + nR \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalently, using $C_P = C_V + R$**, the same change can be expressed as:
> $$\Delta S = nC_P \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - nR \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$
> Both forms are mathematically equivalent ‚ÄĒ KTU examiners often test whether students know *which form to pick* based on given data.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** 2 mol of an ideal gas with $C_V = 12.5$ J/(mol¬∑K) expands isothermally at $300$ K from $0.01$ m¬≥ to $0.05$ m¬≥. Compute $\Delta S$.

**Solution:**

Given: $n = 2$, $T = 300$ K (constant, so first term vanishes), $V_1 = 0.01$ m¬≥, $V_2 = 0.05$ m¬≥, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K).

$$\Delta S = nR \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = 2 \times 8.314 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{0.05}{0.01}\right)$$

Compute the logarithmic term:

$$\ln(5) = 1.6094$$

Multiply:

$$\Delta S = 2 \times 8.314 \times 1.6094 = 26.76 \text{ J/K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even though the gas absorbs heat from the surroundings during isothermal expansion, the system entropy *increases* because volume increase gives the molecules more positional microstates.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Entropy Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["THERMODYNAMIC SYSTEM"] --> B["MACROSCOPIC VIEW"]
    A --> C["MICROSCOPIC VIEW"]

    B --> B1["Energy U"]
    B --> B2["Volume V"]
    B --> B3["Temperature T"]
    B --> B4["Pressure P"]

    C --> C1["Molecules and Atoms"]
    C --> C2["Microstates Omega"]

    B --> D["CLAUSIUS DEFINITION"]
    D --> D1["dS = dQrev divided by T"]
    D1 --> E["ENTROPY S"]

    C --> F["BOLTZMANN DEFINITION"]
    F --> F1["S = kB times ln Omega"]
    F1 --> E

    E --> G["FUNDAMENTAL RELATION"]
    G --> G1["dU = T dS minus P dV"]
    G1 --> H["FIRST LAW PLUS SECOND LAW"]

    E --> I["SECOND LAW"]
    I --> I1["dS greater than or equal to dQ divided by T"]
    I1 --> J["ENTROPY GENERATION"]
    J --> J1["Sgen greater than or equal to 0 for real processes"]

    E --> K["ENGINEERING APPLICATIONS"]
    K --> K1["Power Cycles"]
    K --> K2["Refrigeration"]
    K --> K3["Distillation"]
    K --> K4["Reaction Equilibrium"]
```

## 4.2 Reversible vs Irreversible Process Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["Initial State S1"] --> R2["Reversible Path dS equals dQrev over T"]
    R2 --> R3["Final State S2"]
    R3 -.->|"Delta S same for both paths"| R4["State 2 Identical"]

    R1 --> R5["Irreversible Path"]
    R5 --> R4

    R5 --> R6["Calculate via constructed reversible path"]
    R6 --> R3
```

## 4.3 Entropy Classification Table

| **Type** | **Equation** | **Domain** | **Engineering Use** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| System Entropy | $\Delta S_{sys}$ | The substance being studied | Energy audits |
| Surroundings Entropy | $\Delta S_{surr} = -\dfrac{Q}{T_{surr}}$ | Everything outside the system | Heat transfer analysis |
| Universe Entropy | $\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr}$ | Total of all interactions | Feasibility test |
| Entropy Generation | $S_{gen} \geq 0$ | Created by irreversibilities | Loss quantification |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define entropy. State its SI unit and explain why entropy is a state function.**

**Model Answer:**

Entropy is a thermodynamic state function defined for a reversible process by:

$$dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

The SI unit is **J/K** (or **kJ/kg¬∑K** on a mass basis).

**Why it is a state function:** By the Clausius theorem, for any reversible cyclic process:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$$

The vanishing cyclic integral of $\dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ is the mathematical signature of an exact differential. Therefore $\dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ must be the differential of some point function, which is defined as entropy $S$. Hence $S$ depends only on the state, not the path ‚ÄĒ making it a state function.

### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the statistical (Boltzmann) definition of entropy.**

**Model Answer:**

The statistical (microscopic) definition of entropy, given by **Ludwig Boltzmann**, is:

$$S = k_B \ln \Omega$$

where $k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K is the Boltzmann constant and $\Omega$ is the number of accessible microstates consistent with the macroscopic state.

**Explanation:** Unlike the Clausius definition which is macroscopic, this definition relates entropy directly to the number of microscopic arrangements. A system with more accessible microstates has higher entropy. This unifies thermodynamics with statistical mechanics and explains why entropy is a measure of molecular disorder.

---

## 5.2 PART B ‚ÄĒ Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A ‚ÄĒ Option I** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Clausius definition of entropy starting from the Carnot cycle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** 3 mol of an ideal gas with $C_P = 29.1$ J/(mol¬∑K) is compressed from $1.0 \times 10^5$ Pa and $300$ K to $5.0 \times 10^5$ Pa and $400$ K. Calculate the change in entropy of the gas. Use $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K).

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A**

#### **Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation**

**Step 1:** Apply the First Law to a reversible Carnot cycle:

$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_C$$

**Step 2:** Carnot efficiency:

$$\eta_{rev} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H} = \frac{Q_H - Q_C}{Q_H}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify to:

$$\frac{Q_C}{Q_H} = \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_C}{T_C} = 0$$

**Step 5:** For any arbitrary reversible cycle composed of $n$ Carnot cycles:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\delta Q_i}{T_i} = 0$$

In the limit $n \to \infty$:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$$

**Step 6:** This cyclic integral being zero is the defining property of an exact differential. Define:

$$dS \equiv \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

Hence entropy $S$ is established as a state function.

#### **Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical**

Given: $n = 3$, $C_P = 29.1$ J/(mol¬∑K), $P_1 = 1.0 \times 10^5$ Pa, $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_2 = 5.0 \times 10^5$ Pa, $T_2 = 400$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K).

**Relevant formula:**

$$\Delta S = nC_P \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - nR \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

**Compute the temperature term:**

$$nC_P \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) = 3 \times 29.1 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{400}{300}\right)$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) = 0.2877$$

$$3 \times 29.1 \times 0.2877 = 25.12 \text{ J/K}$$

**Compute the pressure term:**

$$nR \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = 3 \times 8.314 \times \ln(5)$$

$$\ln(5) = 1.6094$$

$$3 \times 8.314 \times 1.6094 = 40.13 \text{ J/K}$$

**Final answer:**

$$\Delta S = 25.12 - 40.13 = -15.01 \text{ J/K}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The gas entropy *decreases* because compression (higher pressure) reduces the available volume per molecule, despite heating.

---

### **Question B ‚ÄĒ Option II** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for entropy change of an ideal gas in terms of temperature and volume. From this, deduce the entropy change in terms of temperature and pressure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An ideal gas undergoes a reversible isothermal expansion at $400$ K during which it absorbs $2000$ J of heat. Compute the entropy change of the system, surroundings, and universe. State whether the process is feasible.

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B**

#### **Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation**

For an ideal gas:

$$\delta Q_{rev} = nC_V\,dT + P\,dV = nC_V\,dT + \frac{nRT}{V}\,dV$$

Divide by $T$:

$$dS = nC_V \frac{dT}{T} + nR \frac{dV}{V}$$

Integrating from state 1 to state 2 (with constant $C_V$):

$$\Delta S = nC_V \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + nR \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

**Deduction in $(T,P)$ form:** Using ideal gas law $V \propto T/P$, so:

$$\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{T_2 P_1}{T_1 P_2}$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta S = nC_V \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + nR \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2 P_1}{T_1 P_2}\right)$$

$$\Delta S = n(C_V + R)\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - nR \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

Since $C_P = C_V + R$:

$$\Delta S = nC_P \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - nR \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

#### **Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical**

Given: $T = 400$ K, $Q_{rev} = 2000$ J (reversible isothermal).

**System entropy change:**

$$\Delta S_{sys} = \frac{Q_{rev}}{T} = \frac{2000}{400} = 5 \text{ J/K}$$

**Surroundings entropy change** (system absorbs heat, so surroundings lose it):

$$\Delta S_{surr} = -\frac{Q_{rev}}{T_{surr}} = -\frac{2000}{400} = -5 \text{ J/K}$$

**Universe entropy change:**

$$\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} = 5 + (-5) = 0 \text{ J/K}$$

> **Verdict:** Since $\Delta S_{univ} = 0$ for a reversible process, the process **is feasible** and meets the Second Law with equality.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting absolute temperature** ‚ÄĒ students often use ¬įC or ¬įF instead of Kelvin. Always convert first.
> 2. **Using $Q$ instead of $Q_{rev}$** ‚ÄĒ entropy *must* use reversible heat, even if the actual process is irreversible. Calculate $\Delta S$ via a reversible *fictional* path.
> 3. **Confusing Clausius inequality direction** ‚ÄĒ remember: $\Delta S_{univ} \geq 0$ (NOT $\leq$). Wrong direction = full marks lost.
> 4. **Forgetting the natural log in the Boltzmann formula** ‚ÄĒ students often write $S = k_B \Omega$ which is dimensionally and mathematically wrong.
> 5. **Mixing up $C_P$ and $C_V$ formulas** ‚ÄĒ use $(T,V)$ formula with $C_V$, and $(T,P)$ formula with $C_P$.
> 6. **Units inconsistency** ‚ÄĒ entropy in J/K for moles, but kJ/(kg¬∑K) for unit mass. Examiners are strict.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** $dS = \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ (Clausius, 1865).
- **Statistical form:** $S = k_B \ln \Omega$ (Boltzmann).
- **State function:** $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0 \Rightarrow S$ is path-independent.
- **Second Law (Clausius inequality):** $dS \geq \dfrac{\delta Q}{T}$.
- **Entropy of universe never decreases:** $\Delta S_{univ} \geq 0$.
- **Ideal gas entropy change formulas (memorize both):**
  - $\Delta S = nC_V \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + nR \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$
  - $\Delta S = nC_P \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - nR \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$
- **Fundamental relation:** $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$ (combines 1st + 2nd Law).
- **Third Law anchor:** Perfect crystalline solid at $0$ K has $S = 0$.
- **Entropy is extensive** (mass-proportional) and **conjugate** to temperature.
- **Engineering impact:** Power cycles, refrigeration, distillation, reaction feasibility.
- **Boltzmann's tombstone:** $S = k \log W$ ‚ÄĒ the bridge between the invisible and the measurable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation of Entropy Change in Processes Involving Ideal Gases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Entropy Change in Processes Involving Ideal Gases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ‚ÄĒ Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Entropy (S)** is an extensive thermodynamic state function that quantitatively measures the molecular disorder or the unavailable energy for doing useful work in a system. For a **reversible process**, the differential change in entropy is defined as:
>
> $$dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

For an **ideal gas**, entropy is a **point function** (state function), meaning the entropy change between two equilibrium states is **independent of the path** taken. This critical property allows us to construct convenient *reversible* hypothetical paths between any two states and calculate $\Delta S$ accurately, even if the actual process is irreversible.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite Definition:** Entropy change is a thermodynamic property whose differential change for a closed system undergoing a reversible process is given by $\delta Q_{rev}/T$. It is an **exact differential**, i.e., $\oint dS = 0$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you have a perfectly organized room (low entropy) versus a messy, scattered room (high entropy). The room itself doesn't care how it became messy ‚ÄĒ whether one person trashed it in 5 minutes or whether it slowly became disordered over a week. The final "disorder" only depends on the **initial** and **final** states.

**Thermodynamic Analogy ‚ÄĒ "The Energy Spreading Principle":**

- **Heat ($Q$) flowing into a system** at temperature $T$ is like pouring water onto a sponge. The higher the temperature $T$, the more "spread out" each small bit of heat becomes.
- **Entropy measures how dispersed this energy is** among the microscopic degrees of freedom.
- For an **ideal gas**:
  - Increasing **temperature** (T‚ÜĎ) increases molecular kinetic energy ‚Üí more microstates ‚Üí **S increases**.
  - Increasing **volume** (V‚ÜĎ) gives molecules more space to occupy ‚Üí more positional microstates ‚Üí **S increases**.
  - Increasing **pressure** (P‚ÜĎ) compresses the gas ‚Üí fewer positional microstates ‚Üí **S decreases**.

---

## 1.3 Why Ideal Gases Are Special

For an ideal gas, the internal energy $U$ depends **only on temperature** (Joule's Law), and $PV = RT$. These two facts lead to two incredibly powerful and simple expressions for entropy change:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Master Equations for Entropy Change of an Ideal Gas:**
>
> $$\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{C_p}{T}\,dT - R\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$
>
> $$\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{C_v}{T}\,dT + R\ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

These are the **two workhorses** you will use throughout this topic. Every specific process formula is derived from one of these two.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value (SI Units) |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $R$ | Universal Gas Constant | **8.314 J/(mol¬∑K)** |
| $C_p$ | Molar heat capacity at constant P | J/(mol¬∑K) |
| $C_v$ | Molar heat capacity at constant V | J/(mol¬∑K) |
| $C_p - C_v$ | Relation (per mole ideal gas) | **= R** |
| $\gamma$ | Heat capacity ratio $C_p/C_v$ | 1.4 (diatomic), 1.67 (monatomic) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ‚ÄĒ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Logic ‚ÄĒ The "Why" Behind the Equations

To derive the entropy change formulas, we combine the **First Law** ($dU = \delta Q - \delta W$) with the **Second Law** ($\delta Q_{rev} = T\,dS$) and exploit ideal-gas-specific properties.

### Step 1: Combine First and Second Laws (Reversible Path)
For a closed system undergoing a reversible process:
$$dU = \delta Q_{rev} - \delta W_{rev} = T\,dS - P\,dV$$

### Step 2: Apply Joule's Law (Ideal Gas Property)
For an ideal gas, internal energy depends only on temperature:
$$dU = C_v\,dT$$

### Step 3: Equate and Rearrange
$$C_v\,dT = T\,dS - P\,dV$$
$$T\,dS = C_v\,dT + P\,dV$$

For an ideal gas, $PV = RT$, so $P = RT/V$. Substituting:
$$T\,dS = C_v\,dT + R\,T\,\frac{dV}{V}$$
$$dS = \frac{C_v}{T}\,dT + R\,\frac{dV}{V} \quad \text{...(Equation A)}$$

### Step 4: Derive the (T, P) Form Using Enthalpy
A parallel derivation using $dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$ and $dH = C_p\,dT$ gives:
$$dS = \frac{C_p}{T}\,dT - R\,\frac{dP}{P} \quad \text{...(Equation B)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Both Equation A and Equation B are **exact differentials** because $S$ is a state function. The mixed partial derivatives must satisfy Maxwell's relations (this is what guarantees consistency).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet ‚ÄĒ Entropy Change for Common Processes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is your **one-stop revision sheet**. Memorize the boxed integrals and the simplified formulas for each standard process. Assume **constant $C_p$ and $C_v$** unless otherwise stated in the problem.

| # | Process | Constraint | Entropy Change Formula ($\Delta S$) |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | **General (ideal gas)** | $T_1 \to T_2$, $P_1 \to P_2$ | $C_p\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ |
| 2 | **General (ideal gas)** | $T_1 \to T_2$, $V_1 \to V_2$ | $C_v\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R\ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$ |
| 3 | **Isothermal** | $T_1 = T_2$ | $-R\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = R\ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$ |
| 4 | **Isobaric** | $P_1 = P_2$ | $C_p\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$ |
| 5 | **Isochoric (Isovolumetric)** | $V_1 = V_2$ | $C_v\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$ |
| 6 | **Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic)** | $\Delta S = 0$ | $0$ (by definition) |
| 7 | **Polytropic** | $PV^n = \text{const}$ | $C_p\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ (same as general) |
| 8 | **Free Expansion (Irreversible)** | $\Delta U = 0$ | $R\ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$ (use ideal gas path) |

> [!WARNING]
> For **irreversible processes**, you **MUST NOT** try to integrate along the actual path. Instead, construct a reversible path connecting the same initial and final states. Since $S$ is a state function, $\Delta S_{actual} = \Delta S_{reversible\,path}$.

---

## 2.3 When Heat Capacity Varies With Temperature

If $C_p$ is given as a polynomial function of $T$:
$$C_p = a + bT + cT^2 + dT^3$$

Then the entropy change formula becomes:
$$\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2}\frac{(a + bT + cT^2 + dT^3)}{T}\,dT - R\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

$$\Delta S = a\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + b(T_2 - T_1) + \frac{c}{2}(T_2^2 - T_1^2) + \frac{d}{3}(T_2^3 - T_1^3) - R\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

**Mean (Average) Heat Capacity Approach:**
If a mean heat capacity $C_{p,m}$ is provided for the temperature range $T_1$ to $T_2$:
$$\Delta S = C_{p,m}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Why This Calculation Matters |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Gas Turbines & Jet Engines** | Isentropic efficiency calculations require reversible adiabatic entropy change = 0 |
| **Compressors & Expanders** | Polytropic compression/expansion entropy change determines actual work loss |
| **Refrigeration Cycles** | Entropy change across evaporator/condenser is fundamental to COP analysis |
| **LNG/CNG Storage** | Throttling and isothermal compression entropy calculations dictate storage efficiency |
| **Chemical Reactors** | Entropy generation ($\Delta S_{gen} = \Delta S_{system} + \Delta S_{surroundings}$) governs second-law efficiency |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Two Master Equations

### Derivation A: $dS = \dfrac{C_v}{T}dT + R\dfrac{dV}{V}$

**Starting Point ‚ÄĒ Combined First and Second Law (Reversible Closed System):**

$$dU = \delta Q_{rev} - \delta W_{rev}$$

For a reversible process: $\delta Q_{rev} = T\,dS$ and $\delta W_{rev} = P\,dV$.

$$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Apply Joule's Law for Ideal Gas:**

For an ideal gas, internal energy depends only on temperature:
$$dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT = C_v\,dT \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Equate (1) and (2):**

$$C_v\,dT = T\,dS - P\,dV$$

**Isolate the $dS$ term:**

$$T\,dS = C_v\,dT + P\,dV$$

$$dS = \frac{C_v}{T}\,dT + \frac{P}{T}\,dV \quad \text{...(3)}$$

**Substitute Ideal Gas Equation ($PV = RT \Rightarrow P/T = R/V$):**

$$dS = \frac{C_v}{T}\,dT + R\,\frac{dV}{V} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### Derivation B: $dS = \dfrac{C_p}{T}dT - R\dfrac{dP}{P}$

**Starting Point ‚ÄĒ Definition of Enthalpy:**
$$H = U + PV$$

Differential form:
$$dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP \quad \text{...(4)}$$

**Substitute Equation (1) into (4):**
$$dH = (T\,dS - P\,dV) + P\,dV + V\,dP$$
$$dH = T\,dS + V\,dP \quad \text{...(5)}$$

**Apply the Ideal-Gas Property for Enthalpy:**
For an ideal gas, enthalpy depends only on temperature:
$$dH = C_p\,dT \quad \text{...(6)}$$

**Equate (5) and (6):**
$$C_p\,dT = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

**Isolate $dS$:**
$$T\,dS = C_p\,dT - V\,dP$$

$$dS = \frac{C_p}{T}\,dT - \frac{V}{T}\,dP \quad \text{...(7)}$$

**Substitute Ideal Gas Equation ($V/T = R/P$):**

$$dS = \frac{C_p}{T}\,dT - R\,\frac{dP}{P} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### Derivation C: Integrated Form with Constant $C_p$

Starting from Derivation B:
$$dS = \frac{C_p}{T}\,dT - R\,\frac{dP}{P}$$

Integrating from state 1 to state 2 (assuming $C_p$ is constant):
$$\int_{S_1}^{S_2} dS = C_p\int_{T_1}^{T_2}\frac{dT}{T} - R\int_{P_1}^{P_2}\frac{dP}{P}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S = C_p\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)} \quad \text{...(General T-P Formula)}$$

---

## 3.2 Special Case Derivations

### Case 1: Isothermal Process ($T_1 = T_2 = T$)

Since $T_2/T_1 = 1$, the logarithm becomes zero:
$$\Delta S_{isothermal} = C_p\ln(1) - R\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -R\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

Using $P_1V_1 = P_2V_2$ (isothermal ideal gas): $P_2/P_1 = V_1/V_2$

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{isothermal} = R\ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = -R\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)}$$

---

### Case 2: Isobaric Process ($P_1 = P_2 = P$)

Since $P_2/P_1 = 1$, the second term vanishes:
$$\Delta S_{isobaric} = C_p\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$$

---

### Case 3: Isochoric Process ($V_1 = V_2 = V$)

Using the (T,V) form of the integrated equation:
$$\Delta S_{isochoric} = C_v\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R\ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

Since $V_2/V_1 = 1$:
$$\boxed{\Delta S_{isochoric} = C_v\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)}$$

---

### Case 4: Polytropic Process ($PV^n = \text{constant}$)

For a polytropic process with index $n$:
$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{n-1}$$

The entropy change still uses the general formula:
$$\Delta S_{polytropic} = C_p\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

Substituting the polytropic relation:
$$\Delta S_{polytropic} = C_p \cdot \frac{n-1}{n}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) - R\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{polytropic} = \left[C_p\cdot\frac{n-1}{n} - R\right]\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an **isentropic** (reversible adiabatic) process, $\Delta S = 0$. Setting the above to zero gives $C_p(n-1)/n = R$, which simplifies to $n = \gamma = C_p/C_v$. **This is the derivation of the isentropic relation $PV^\gamma = \text{constant}$.**

---

### Case 5: Free Expansion of an Ideal Gas (Irreversible)

A gas expands into a vacuum ($P_{ext} = 0$). Since $Q = 0$ and $W = 0$, we have $\Delta U = 0$.

For an ideal gas, $\Delta U = 0 \Rightarrow \Delta T = 0$. So $T_2 = T_1$.

> [!WARNING]
> Although the actual process is irreversible, we compute $\Delta S$ along a **reversible isothermal path** connecting the same initial and final volumes:
>
> $$\Delta S_{free\,exp} = R\ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

This is **positive** (since $V_2 > V_1$), demonstrating that **entropy of an isolated system increases** in an irreversible process ‚ÄĒ a direct consequence of the Second Law.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Numerical Verification

**Problem:** One mole of an ideal gas at 300 K and 1 bar is compressed reversibly and isothermally to 5 bar. Calculate $\Delta S$.

**Given:**
- $n = 1$ mol, $T_1 = T_2 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 1$ bar, $P_2 = 5$ bar
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K)

**Solution:**

Since the process is isothermal, use the simplified formula:
$$\Delta S = -R\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

$$\Delta S = -8.314 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{5}{1}\right)$$

$$\Delta S = -8.314 \times 1.6094$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S = -13.38 \text{ J/K}}$$

The **negative sign** indicates entropy decrease, which is physically consistent: compression reduces the available volume for molecules, decreasing microstates.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 ‚ÄĒ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Entropy Change of an Ideal Gas ‚ÄĒ Two Reference Frames

This schematic shows the **two equivalent paths** (T-P frame vs T-V frame) for computing entropy change between the same two thermodynamic states. This visualizes the **state-function property**: both paths must yield the **same $\Delta S$**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[State 1: T1 P1 V1] -->|Path A: T-P frame| S2[State 2: T2 P2 V2]
    S1 -->|Path B: T-V frame| S2

    S1 -.Step 1.-> SA1["Hold T constant, change P to P2"]
    SA1 -.Step 2.-> SA2["At constant P2, change T to T2"]
    SA2 -.-> S2

    S1 -.Step 1.-> SB1["Hold T constant, change V to V2"]
    SB1 -.Step 2.-> SB2["At constant V2, change T to T2"]
    SB2 -.-> S2

    SA1 --> R1["őĒS1 = -R ln P2/P1"]
    SA2 --> R2["őĒS2 = Cp ln T2/T1"]
    SB1 --> R3["őĒS3 = R ln V2/V1"]
    SB2 --> R4["őĒS4 = Cv ln T2/T1"]

    R2 --> SUM1
    R3 --> SUM2["őĒS Total Path B = Cv ln T2/T1 + R ln V2/V1"]
    R4 --> SUM2

    SUM1 --> EQUIV[Both equal because S is a state function]
    SUM2 --> EQUIV

    style S2 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
```

# SECTION 5 ‚ÄĒ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]
**"Show that for an ideal gas undergoing an isothermal process, the entropy change is given by $\Delta S = R\ln(V_2/V_1)$."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

For an ideal gas, the general entropy change expression in (T, V) form is:

$$\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2}\frac{C_v}{T}dT + R\ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

For an **isothermal process**, $T_1 = T_2$. Hence the temperature ratio inside the logarithm equals 1:

$$\int_{T_1}^{T_2}\frac{C_v}{T}dT = C_v\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) = C_v\ln(1) = 0$$

Therefore:
$$\Delta S_{isothermal} = 0 + R\ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = R\ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S = R\ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2024]
**"State whether entropy change for an irreversible process can be calculated using the actual process path. Justify your answer."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **No.** Entropy change for an irreversible process cannot be calculated by integrating along the actual irreversible path.

**Justification:**
- Entropy $S$ is a **state function** (point function), so $\Delta S$ depends **only on the initial and final states**, not on the path.
- For an irreversible process, $\delta Q_{rev}/T$ is **not** equal to $dS$ along the actual path because the process is not in equilibrium.
- Therefore, to compute $\Delta S$ for an irreversible process, we **construct a reversible path** connecting the same initial and final states and apply:
$$\Delta S_{actual} = \Delta S_{reversible\,path} = \int_{rev}\frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

**Example:** In free expansion of an ideal gas into vacuum ($Q = 0$, $W = 0$, $\Delta U = 0$), we calculate $\Delta S$ via a reversible isothermal path: $\Delta S = R\ln(V_2/V_1)$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.2 PART B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, **Part B questions carry 14 marks** and offer **internal choice**. Students must answer **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each sub-part typically carries **7 marks**.

---

### ūüďė Question B-A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the expressions for entropy change of an ideal gas in terms of **(i) Temperature and Pressure**, and **(ii) Temperature and Volume**. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two moles of an ideal gas with $C_p = 29.1$ J/(mol¬∑K) are compressed isothermally and reversibly from 1 bar to 10 bar at 400 K. Calculate the entropy change of the gas. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ‚úÖ Model Solution for B-A

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Assumption:** The gas behaves ideally, so $U = U(T)$, $H = H(T)$, and $PV = RT$.

**Part (i) ‚ÄĒ In terms of (T, P):**

Starting from the combined first and second law:
$$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$$

Since $H = U + PV$, we have $dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP$. Substituting $dU$:
$$dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

For an ideal gas, $dH = C_p\,dT$:
$$C_p\,dT = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

Using $V/T = R/P$ (from ideal gas law):
$$dS = \frac{C_p}{T}\,dT - R\,\frac{dP}{P}$$

Integrating:
$$\boxed{\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2}\frac{C_p}{T}\,dT - R\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)}$$

**Part (ii) ‚ÄĒ In terms of (T, V):**

From $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$ and $dU = C_v\,dT$:
$$C_v\,dT = T\,dS - P\,dV$$

Using $P/T = R/V$ (from ideal gas law):
$$dS = \frac{C_v}{T}\,dT + R\,\frac{dV}{V}$$

Integrating:
$$\boxed{\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2}\frac{C_v}{T}\,dT + R\ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $n = 2$ mol, $C_p = 29.1$ J/(mol¬∑K), $T = 400$ K (isothermal)
- $P_1 = 1$ bar, $P_2 = 10$ bar, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K)

**Step 1:** Since the process is isothermal, $T_1 = T_2$, so the temperature term vanishes:
$$\Delta S = -nR\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:
$$\Delta S = -(2)(8.314)\ln\!\left(\frac{10}{1}\right)$$

$$\Delta S = -16.628 \times 2.3026$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S = -38.29 \text{ J/K}}$$

The **negative sign** indicates entropy decrease due to compression. $\blacksquare$

---

### ūüďó Question B-B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2023]

**(a)** One mole of an ideal gas with $C_p = 29.1$ J/(mol¬∑K) is heated at **constant volume** from 300 K to 600 K. Calculate the entropy change of the gas. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An ideal gas undergoes a **polytropic process** $PV^{1.3} = \text{constant}$ from an initial state of 1 bar and 300 K to a final pressure of 5 bar. If $C_p = 29.1$ J/(mol¬∑K) and $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K), calculate the entropy change per mole. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ‚úÖ Model Solution for B-B

**(a) Constant Volume Heating (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $n = 1$ mol, $C_p = 29.1$ J/(mol¬∑K), $V_1 = V_2$, $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_2 = 600$ K

**Step 1:** Calculate $C_v$ using $C_p - C_v = R$:
$$C_v = C_p - R = 29.1 - 8.314 = 20.786 \text{ J/(mol¬∑K)}$$

**Step 2:** For constant volume process:
$$\Delta S = nC_v\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:
$$\Delta S = (1)(20.786)\ln\!\left(\frac{600}{300}\right)$$

$$\Delta S = 20.786 \times \ln(2) = 20.786 \times 0.6931$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S = 14.41 \text{ J/K}} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

**(b) Polytropic Process (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $n = 1$ mol, polytropic index $= 1.3$, $P_1 = 1$ bar, $T_1 = 300$ K
- $P_2 = 5$ bar, $C_p = 29.1$ J/(mol¬∑K), $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K)

**Step 1:** Find $T_2$ using the polytropic relation:
$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$$

$$\frac{T_2}{300} = (5)^{\frac{1.3 - 1}{1.3}} = (5)^{0.2308}$$

$$\frac{T_2}{300} = e^{0.2308 \times \ln 5} = e^{0.2308 \times 1.6094} = e^{0.3714}$$

$$\frac{T_2}{300} = 1.4498$$

$$T_2 = 300 \times 1.4498 = 434.94 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the general entropy change formula:
$$\Delta S = C_p\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

$$\Delta S = 29.1 \times \ln(1.4498) - 8.314 \times \ln(5)$$

$$\Delta S = 29.1 \times 0.3714 - 8.314 \times 1.6094$$

$$\Delta S = 10.808 - 13.380$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S = -2.57 \text{ J/K}} \quad \blacksquare$$

The **negative sign** indicates that compression dominates over heating in this polytropic process, leading to net entropy decrease.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Students often forget to multiply by **number of moles ($n$)** in problems involving multiple moles. Always check if the given $C_p$ is per mole or total. *(Loses 1‚Äď2 marks)*
>
> 2. **Sign Errors in Pressure Ratio:** For isothermal compression, students write $\Delta S = R\ln(P_2/P_1)$ instead of $\Delta S = -R\ln(P_2/P_1)$. Remember the formula is $\Delta S = -R\ln(P_2/P_1)$ or equivalently $R\ln(P_1/P_2)$. *(Loses 1 mark)*
>
> 3. **Using $C_v$ Instead of $C_p$ in Isobaric Process:** Isobaric processes use $C_p$, NOT $C_v$. Isochoric uses $C_v$. Many students swap these. *(Loses 2 marks)*
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Polytropic Relation:** For polytropic processes, students must first find $T_2/T_1$ from $(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n}$ before applying the entropy formula. Skipping this step gives wrong answers. *(Loses 3 marks)*
>
> 5. **Integrating Along Irreversible Path:** This is a **conceptual** mistake worth losing **3‚Äď4 marks**. Always construct a reversible path for irreversible processes.
>
> 6. **Not Stating Assumptions:** When deriving, KTU examiners explicitly look for the assumption "ideal gas so $U = U(T)$". Omitting this loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for Exam Hall:**

- ‚≠ź **Two Master Equations** for ideal-gas entropy change:
  - $\Delta S = C_p\ln(T_2/T_1) - R\ln(P_2/P_1)$
  - $\Delta S = C_v\ln(T_2/T_1) + R\ln(V_2/V_1)$

- ‚≠ź **State Function Property:** $\Delta S$ depends **only on states 1 and 2**, never on the path. Always construct a reversible path for irreversible processes.

- ‚≠ź **Constant Volume Heating** uses $C_v$; **Constant Pressure Heating** uses $C_p$.

- ‚≠ź **Isothermal Process:** $\Delta S = R\ln(V_2/V_1) = -R\ln(P_2/P_1)$.

- ‚≠ź **Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic):** $\Delta S = 0$; relation $PV^\gamma = \text{constant}$ where $\gamma = C_p/C_v$.

- ‚≠ź **Free Expansion of Ideal Gas:** $\Delta U = 0 \Rightarrow \Delta T = 0$; calculate via reversible isothermal path: $\Delta S = R\ln(V_2/V_1) > 0$.

- ‚≠ź **Relation to Remember:** $C_p - C_v = R$ (per mole, ideal gas).

- ‚≠ź **Polytropic Process:** $T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n} = (V_1/V_2)^{n-1}$.

- ‚≠ź **For Variable $C_p$:** Use polynomial integration or mean heat capacity $C_{p,m}$.

- ‚≠ź **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K) ‚ÄĒ use SI units consistently.

- ‚≠ź **Sign Convention:** Compression + Heating ‚Üí check both terms carefully; signs depend on direction.

- ‚≠ź **Always State Assumptions:** Ideal gas, reversible/irreversible path, constant $C_p$ or variable $C_p$.

- ‚≠ź **Units Check:** $\Delta S$ is in **J/K** (not J/kg¬∑K) when using per-mole quantities.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
