# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2024]`

**Q: State and explain any two Maxwell's relations. How are they useful in thermodynamic analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Maxwell's relations are derived from the four fundamental property relations using the exact-differential reciprocity theorem. Two commonly invoked forms are:

**Maxwell-3:**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T
$$

This relates the change of pressure with temperature at constant volume to the change of entropy with volume at constant temperature.

**Maxwell-4:**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T
$$

This connects the measurable thermal-expansion coefficient with an unmeasurable entropy derivative.

**Use:** Maxwell relations replace derivatives involving entropy (a quantity difficult to measure directly) with derivatives involving $P$, $V$, $T$ (easily measurable). They form the basis of **residual property** calculations, **departure functions**, and **fugacity** determination in process simulation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the Clausius‚ÄďClapeyron equation and list the assumptions made in its derivation.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Clausius‚ÄďClapeyron equation** gives the temperature dependence of the saturation vapor pressure of a pure substance:

$$
\ln \frac{P_2^{\text{sat}}}{P_1^{\text{sat}}} = -\frac{\Delta \hat{H}^{\text{vap}}}{R}\left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)
$$

**Assumptions:**

1. The Clapeyron equation is valid at both saturation states.
2. The molar volume of the liquid phase is negligible compared to that of the vapor, i.e., $\hat{V}^{\text{liq}} \ll \hat{V}^{\text{vap}}$.
3. The vapor phase behaves as an **ideal gas**, so $\hat{V}^{\text{vap}} = RT/P^{\text{sat}}$.
4. The molar enthalpy of vaporization $\Delta \hat{H}^{\text{vap}}$ is **constant** over the temperature range of interest.

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students lose marks by **forgetting the constant-of-differentiation variable** in partial derivatives. Always write $(\partial P/\partial T)_V$, never $\partial P/\partial T$. Also, never drop the **negative sign** in Maxwell-1 or Maxwell-4 ‚ÄĒ it costs 1 mark.

### Question A `(a)` ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2024]`

**(a) Starting from the fundamental property relation, derive the four Maxwell's relations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Fundamental Relations:**

The four fundamental property relations derived from the First and Second Laws are:

$$
dU = T\,dS - P\,dV \quad (1)
$$

$$
dH = T\,dS + V\,dP \quad (2)
$$

$$
dA = -P\,dV - S\,dT \quad (3)
$$

$$
dG = V\,dP - S\,dT \quad (4)
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Identifying partial derivatives:**

Because each of $U$, $H$, $A$, $G$ is a state function, the differential is exact. From (1):

$$
T = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_V, \quad -P = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_S
$$

From (2):

$$
T = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial S}\right)_P, \quad V = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_S
$$

From (3):

$$
-P = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial V}\right)_T, \quad -S = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_V
$$

From (4):

$$
V = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T, \quad -S = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Apply Euler reciprocity $\partial^2 z / \partial x \partial y = \partial^2 z / \partial y \partial x$:**

For $U(S,V)$: $\partial^2 U/\partial V \partial S = \partial^2 U/\partial S \partial V$ gives $(\partial T/\partial V)_S = -(\partial P/\partial S)_V$.

For $H(S,P)$: gives $(\partial T/\partial P)_S = (\partial V/\partial S)_P$.

For $A(T,V)$: gives $(\partial P/\partial T)_V = (\partial S/\partial V)_T$.

For $G(T,P)$: gives $(\partial V/\partial T)_P = -(\partial S/\partial P)_T$.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ The four Maxwell relations:**

$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V}
$$

$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P}
$$

$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T}
$$

$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T}
$$

---

### Question A `(b)` ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2024]`

**(b) The normal boiling point of benzene is 353.25 K, and its enthalpy of vaporization is 30.8 kJ/mol. Estimate the boiling point of benzene on a day when the atmospheric pressure is 0.92 bar. Use the Clausius‚ÄďClapeyron equation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 353.25 \text{ K}$
- $P_1^{\text{sat}} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$ (normal atmospheric pressure at standard conditions)
- $P_2^{\text{sat}} = 0.92 \text{ bar}$
- $\Delta \hat{H}^{\text{vap}} = 30.8 \text{ kJ/mol} = 30{,}800 \text{ J/mol}$
- $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol¬∑K)}$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Write the Clausius‚ÄďClapeyron equation:**

$$
\ln \frac{P_2^{\text{sat}}}{P_1^{\text{sat}}} = -\frac{\Delta \hat{H}^{\text{vap}}}{R}\left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Substitute known values:**

$$
\ln \frac{0.92}{1.01325} = -\frac{30{,}800}{8.314}\left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{353.25} \right)
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute LHS:**

$$
\ln(0.9080) = -0.0965
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Solve for $1/T_2$:**

$$
-0.0965 = -3704.6 \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - 2.8309 \times 10^{-3} \right)
$$

$$
\frac{1}{T_2} - 2.8309 \times 10^{-3} = \frac{0.0965}{3704.6} = 2.6045 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{T_2} = 2.8309 \times 10^{-3} + 2.6045 \times 10^{-5} = 2.8570 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Invert:**

$$
T_2 = \frac{1}{2.8570 \times 10^{-3}} = 350.0 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Convert to ¬įC:**

$$
T_2 = 350.0 - 273.15 = 76.85 \text{ ¬įC}
$$

**Result:** Benzene boils at approximately **350.0 K (76.85 ¬įC)** at 0.92 bar. Physically reasonable ‚ÄĒ lower pressure ‚Üí lower boiling point.

---

### Question B `(a)` ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the Clapeyron equation from the equality of Gibbs free energies of two coexisting phases.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ At two-phase coexistence (e.g., $\alpha =$ liquid, $\beta =$ vapor), the temperatures, pressures, and Gibbs energies must be equal:**

$$
T^{\alpha} = T^{\beta} = T, \quad P^{\alpha} = P^{\beta} = P, \quad G^{\alpha}(T,P) = G^{\beta}(T,P)
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Differentiate the equality along the saturation curve:**

$$
dG^{\alpha} = dG^{\beta}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Apply the fundamental relation for $G$ to each phase:**

$$
V^{\alpha}\, dP - S^{\alpha}\, dT = V^{\beta}\, dP - S^{\beta}\, dT
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Group terms:**

$$
\left( V^{\beta} - V^{\alpha} \right) dP = \left( S^{\beta} - S^{\alpha} \right) dT
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Define changes:**

$$
\Delta V = V^{\beta} - V^{\alpha}, \quad \Delta S = S^{\beta} - S^{\alpha}
$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ At equilibrium, $\Delta S = \Delta H / T$ (definition of reversible latent heat):**

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta S}{\Delta V} = \frac{\Delta H}{T\, \Delta V}
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{dP^{\text{sat}}}{dT} = \frac{\Delta \hat{H}^{\text{trans}}}{T\, \Delta \hat{V}}} \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

### Question B `(b)` ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]`

**(b) For a substance, $T_c = 512.5 \text{ K}$, $P_c = 81 \text{ bar}$. Calculate the van der Waals constants $a$ and $b$. Estimate the molar volume at the critical point and compare with the experimental value $V_c = 0.163 \text{ L/mol}$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $T_c = 512.5 \text{ K}$, $P_c = 81 \text{ bar} = 81 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, $R = 83.14 \text{ cm}^3 \text{¬∑bar/(mol¬∑K)}$ (using convenient units for $V_c$ in cm¬≥/mol).

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Formula for $a$:**

$$
a = \frac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c}
$$

$$
a = \frac{27 \times (83.14)^2 \times (512.5)^2}{64 \times 81}
$$

Compute $(83.14)^2 = 6912.3$; $(512.5)^2 = 262{,}656.25$.

$$
a = \frac{27 \times 6912.3 \times 262{,}656.25}{5184} = \frac{4.9 \times 10^{10}}{5184}
$$

Numerator: $27 \times 6912.3 = 186{,}632.1$; $\times 262{,}656.25 = 4.9 \times 10^{10}$.

$$
a = 9.45 \times 10^{6} \text{ cm}^6 \text{¬∑bar/mol}^2
$$

(Equivalent to $\approx 0.945 \text{ Pa¬∑m}^6/\text{mol}^2$.)

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Formula for $b$:**

$$
b = \frac{R T_c}{8 P_c} = \frac{83.14 \times 512.5}{8 \times 81} = \frac{42{,}609.25}{648} = 65.76 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Predicted molar volume at critical point, $V_c^{\text{vdW}} = 3b$:**

$$
V_c^{\text{vdW}} = 3 \times 65.76 = 197.28 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol} = 0.1973 \text{ L/mol}
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Comparison with experiment:**

$$
\text{Error} = \frac{0.1973 - 0.163}{0.163} \times 100\% = +21\%
$$

**Discussion:** The vdW EOS over-predicts $V_c$ by about **21%**. This is a known limitation of cubic EOS ‚ÄĒ the *qualitative* prediction of condensation is correct, but quantitative critical constants are off. SRK and PR are calibrated to give exactly $Z_c = 1/3$ (or $0.307$ for PR), reducing this error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2:**

- ‚ėź A **state property** depends only on the state, not the path; $Q$ and $W$ are NOT properties.
- ‚ėź The four **fundamental property relations** are: $dU$, $dH$, $dA$, $dG$ ‚ÄĒ memorize the *exact algebraic form* with signs.
- ‚ėź **Maxwell's relations** come from Euler reciprocity applied to exact differentials.
- ‚ėź The **most-used** Maxwell relation in chemical engineering is **Maxwell-4**: $(\partial V/\partial T)_P = -(\partial S/\partial P)_T$ ‚ÄĒ used to derive the **Joule‚ÄďThomson coefficient** and **residual entropy**.
- ‚ėź **Clapeyron equation**: $dP^{\text{sat}}/dT = \Delta \hat{H} / (T \Delta \hat{V})$ ‚ÄĒ exact, no assumptions.
- ‚ėź **Clausius‚ÄďClapeyron** requires three assumptions: (i) liquid volume negligible, (ii) ideal-gas vapor, (iii) constant $\Delta \hat{H}^{\text{vap}}$.
- ‚ėź **Equations of state**: order of increasing accuracy ‚ÄĒ Ideal < Virial < vdW < RK < SRK < PR.
- ‚ėź **vdW constants**: $a = 27 R^2 T_c^2 / (64 P_c)$, $b = R T_c / (8 P_c)$, $V_c^{\text{vdW}} = 3b$ ‚Üí $Z_c = 3/8 = 0.375$.
- ‚ėź The **Antoine equation** $\ln P^{\text{sat}} = A - B/(T + C)$ is an empirical improvement over Clausius‚ÄďClapeyron ‚ÄĒ use it when given Antoine constants in problem data.
- ‚ėź **Residual / departure** properties: $M^R = M^{\text{real}} - M^{\text{ig}}$; calculated using EOS to find real-gas $\Delta H$, $\Delta S$ for equipment design.
- ‚ėź Always quote **temperature in Kelvin** and **pressure in consistent SI / bar units** before substitution.
- ‚ėź Watch the **sign conventions** ‚ÄĒ especially the negative sign in $(\partial V/\partial T)_P = -(\partial S/\partial P)_T$.
- ‚ėź **Critical constants** $T_c$, $P_c$, and the **acentric factor $\omega$** characterize a substance for EOS calculations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In numerical problems, always carry **5‚Äď6 significant figures** in intermediate steps to avoid rounding errors that compound in the final answer. KTU examiners award **partial marks** for correctly setting up an equation even if arithmetic fails ‚ÄĒ so write down the formula first!

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **PURE SUBSTANCE (KTU Board Definition):**
> A **pure substance** is defined as a homogeneous material whose chemical composition is fixed and invariant throughout its mass, even though it may exist in **more than one phase simultaneously** in mutual equilibrium. It is *not* restricted to a single chemical element or compound ‚ÄĒ mixtures of multiple phases of the same chemical species (e.g., liquid water + water vapor) are also classified as pure substances.

A pure substance is the foundational object of study in classical chemical engineering thermodynamics because its thermodynamic state can be completely specified by **two independent intensive properties** (Phase Rule: $F = C - P + 2 \Rightarrow F = 1 - 2 + 2 = 1$ for a two-phase pure system, meaning one intensive property fixes the entire equilibrium state).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Boiling Pot" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy (Boiling Pressure Cooker):**
> Imagine a sealed pressure cooker on a stove. As you heat it:
> 1. Initially, water is **liquid only** (compressed/subcooled region).
> 2. The moment the first bubble of steam forms, you have entered the **saturation region** ‚ÄĒ water and vapor coexist.
> 3. Once the last drop evaporates, only **superheated vapor** remains.
> 4. Crank the stove higher: vapor temperature rises, pressure builds ‚ÄĒ you are deep in the **superheated region**.
>
> The **P-V diagram** is like plotting a *movie* of this whole process on a "Pressure vs. Volume" chart, while the **P-T diagram** is a *map* showing the *boundaries* between the phase regions (where you are in the kitchen, where the water turns to ice, where it boils).

## 1.3 Why These Diagrams Matter in Chemical Engineering

- **Boiler & Condenser Design (KTU Module 2 High-Yield):** The P-V dome allows sizing two-phase expansion/compression equipment (e.g., turbines, refrigeration evaporators).
- **Distillation Columns:** P-T phase envelopes determine operating pressure of separation units.
- **Cryogenics & LNG Storage:** Triple-point behavior and sublimation curves dictate safe handling of light hydrocarbons.
- **Refrigeration Cycles (Module 3 link-in):** Saturation properties are used to compute enthalpies of vaporization across evaporator and condenser.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in This Module:**
> - Pressure: **bar** (1 bar = 10‚ĀĶ Pa) or **MPa** (preferred in SI).
> - Temperature: **¬įC** or **K**.
> - Specific Volume: **m¬≥/kg**.
> - Critical Constants (for water as the reference fluid): $T_c = 373.946\,¬įC$, $P_c = 22.064\,MPa$, $v_c = 0.003106\,m^3/kg$.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phase-Change Vocabulary (KTU Board Terminology)

| Term | KTU Definition | Physical Meaning |
|------|----------------|------------------|
| **Saturation Temperature ($T_{sat}$)** | The temperature at which a pure substance boils/condenses at a given pressure. | $T_{sat} = f(P)$ uniquely. |
| **Saturation Pressure ($P_{sat}$)** | The pressure at which boiling/condensation occurs at a given temperature. | $P_{sat} = f(T)$ uniquely. |
| **Saturated Liquid** | Liquid on the verge of vaporization. | State on the **left branch** of the vapor dome. |
| **Saturated Vapor** | Vapor on the verge of condensation. | State on the **right branch** of the vapor dome. |
| **Quality ($x$)** | Mass fraction of vapor in a liquid‚Äďvapor mixture. | $0 \le x \le 1$. |
| **Subcooled / Compressed Liquid** | Liquid below its saturation temperature at a given P. | Region to the **left** of the dome. |
| **Superheated Vapor** | Vapor above its saturation temperature at a given P. | Region to the **right** of the dome. |
| **Critical Point** | The inflection point of the dome where saturated liquid and vapor properties become identical. | $P_c, T_c, v_c$ ‚ÄĒ single point. |
| **Triple Point** | State where solid, liquid, and vapor coexist in equilibrium. | $F = 0$ (invariant). |

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Phase Diagrams

### 2.2.1 Why does a vapor dome exist?
For a pure substance, when liquid and vapor coexist in equilibrium, the **Gibbs free energies** of the two phases must be equal:

$$G_l(T,P) = G_v(T,P)$$

This equality defines a **one-parameter family** of equilibrium states ‚ÄĒ a curve in the P‚Äďv plane. The shape of this curve is asymmetric: the saturated liquid line is nearly vertical (low compressibility of liquids), while the saturated vapor line drops hyperbolically (high compressibility of gases). These two branches meet at the **critical point**, where the latent heat of vaporization vanishes:

$$h_{fg} \to 0 \quad \text{as} \quad (T,P) \to (T_c, P_c)$$

### 2.2.2 How is the saturation curve mathematically described?
The slope of the P‚ÄďT saturation curve is governed by the **Clausius‚ÄďClapeyron equation**, which is a KTU Module 2 and Module 3 high-yield identity:

$$\frac{dP_{sat}}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}$$

For an ideal-gas vapor and a negligible liquid volume ($v_v \gg v_l$):

$$\frac{dP_{sat}}{dT} \approx \frac{P \cdot h_{fg}}{R \cdot T^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln P_{sat} = -\frac{h_{fg}}{R T} + C$$

This explains the **exponentially rising** nature of the vaporization curve on the P‚ÄďT diagram.

### 2.2.3 Why does the P‚ÄďT diagram have only three regions?
Because a pure substance has only **three phases** (solid, liquid, gas), and each pair of phases has a unique phase-boundary line of coexistence, we get:
- **Sublimation curve** (solid‚Äďvapor): low-P, low-T.
- **Vaporization curve** (liquid‚Äďvapor): terminates at the **critical point**.
- **Fusion / Melting curve** (solid‚Äďliquid): nearly vertical for most substances (anomalous for water).

All three curves meet at the **triple point** (a unique invariant state).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ | Specific volume in two-phase region | $m^3/kg$ |
| 2 | $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$ | Volume change on vaporization | $m^3/kg$ |
| 3 | $u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$ | Internal energy in two-phase region | $kJ/kg$ |
| 4 | $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | Enthalpy in two-phase region | $kJ/kg$ |
| 5 | $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ | Entropy in two-phase region | $kJ/(kg \cdot K)$ |
| 6 | $x = \dfrac{v - v_f}{v_g - v_f}$ | Quality from specific volume | dimensionless |
| 7 | $x = \dfrac{h - h_f}{h_g - h_f}$ | Quality from enthalpy (most used) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dfrac{dP_{sat}}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T v_{fg}}$ | Clausius‚ÄďClapeyron (exact) | $Pa/K$ |
| 9 | $\ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = -\dfrac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Clausius‚ÄďClapeyron (ideal-gas, constant $h_{fg}$) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $F = C - P + 2$ | Gibbs Phase Rule | ‚ÄĒ |
| 11 | $v_c v_g v_f \approx 0.375$ (for many substances) | Law of Rectilinear Diameters (approximate) | ‚ÄĒ |
| 12 | $P_{R} = P/P_c$, $T_R = T/T_c$ | Reduced (dimensionless) properties | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked for *any* two-phase property of a pure substance (u, h, s, v), you **must** include the **quality $x$** in your answer. A numerical answer without a stated quality almost always loses 2 marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rankine Cycle (Power Plant):** Steam enters the turbine as a superheated vapor (right of the dome) and exits as a wet vapor (inside the dome). The P‚Äďv diagram is the *backbone* of cycle plotting.
- **Liquefaction of Gases (Cengel, Boles style problem):** Multi-stage compression with intercooling followed by expansion ‚ÄĒ needs critical data and dome geometry.
- **Pipeline Hydraulics (Two-Phase Flow):** Quality $x$ and mixture specific volume determine pressure drop in oil‚Äďgas pipelines.
- **Food Processing / Sterilization:** P‚ÄďT diagram of water dictates safe autoclave operating conditions relative to the saturation curve.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Clausius‚ÄďClapeyron Equation (KTU High-Yield Derivation)

This derivation is **frequently asked for 7 marks** in KTU ESE Module 2.

**Starting Point:** Two phases $\alpha$ and $\beta$ of a pure substance coexist in equilibrium at $(T, P)$. By definition:

$$\mu_\alpha(T, P) = \mu_\beta(T, P)$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $T$ along the saturation curve (Le Chatelier's principle):

$$d\mu_\alpha = d\mu_\beta$$

Using the fundamental relation for a single component: $d\mu = -s\,dT + v\,dP$:

$$-s_\alpha\,dT + v_\alpha\,dP = -s_\beta\,dT + v_\beta\,dP$$

Rearranging:

$$(v_\beta - v_\alpha)\,dP = (s_\beta - s_\alpha)\,dT$$

Let $\Delta v = v_\beta - v_\alpha$ and $\Delta s = s_\beta - s_\alpha$:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta v}$$

For a phase change at constant $T$ and $P$, the entropy change is related to the latent heat:

$$\Delta s = \frac{h_{fg}}{T} \quad \text{(from } ds = \frac{\delta q_{rev}}{T} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T} \text{)}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{dP_{sat}}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}\;}$$

### 3.1.1 Ideal-Gas / Constant-Latent-Heat Simplification

Assume vapor behaves as an ideal gas and $v_l \ll v_v$, so $v_{fg} \approx v_v = RT/P$:

$$\frac{dP_{sat}}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg} \cdot P}{R \cdot T^2}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dP_{sat}}{P} = \frac{h_{fg}}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

Integrating between states 1 and 2 (assuming $h_{fg} \approx \text{const}$):

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{h_{fg}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

$$\ln \frac{P_2}{P_1} = -\frac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

This is the **integrated form** used in KTU numerical problems.

## 3.2 Derivation: Property Variation Across the Two-Phase Region (Lever Rule)

Consider a liquid‚Äďvapor mixture of total mass $m$ and total volume $V$. Let the mass of vapor be $m_g$ and mass of liquid be $m_f$.

$$m = m_f + m_g, \quad V = m_f v_f + m_g v_g$$

Dividing by $m$:

$$v = (1-x) v_f + x v_g = v_f + x(v_g - v_f) = v_f + x v_{fg}$$

Solving for $x$:

$$\boxed{\;x = \frac{v - v_f}{v_g - v_f}\;}$$

This is the **lever rule** and applies identically to $h$, $u$, and $s$ because these are *linear* in $x$ (any extensive specific property is linear in the phase masses).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

> **[Example ‚ÄĒ KTU Model Pattern]**
> Water at 200 ¬įC has a quality of 0.85. Find: (a) specific volume, (b) specific enthalpy, (c) specific internal energy. *(Data: at 200 ¬įC, $P_{sat} = 1554.3\,kPa$, $v_f = 0.001157\,m^3/kg$, $v_g = 0.12721\,m^3/kg$, $h_f = 849.47\,kJ/kg$, $h_{fg} = 1939.8\,kJ/kg$, $u_f = 849.24\,kJ/kg$, $u_{fg} = 1743.7\,kJ/kg$.)*

### Step-by-step Solution:

**Part (a): Specific Volume**

$$v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg} = 0.001157 + 0.85 \cdot (0.12721 - 0.001157)$$

$$v_{fg} = 0.12721 - 0.001157 = 0.126053 \; m^3/kg$$

$$v = 0.001157 + 0.85 \times 0.126053 = 0.001157 + 0.107145$$

$$\boxed{\;v = 0.10830 \; m^3/kg\;}$$

**Part (b): Specific Enthalpy**

$$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} = 849.47 + 0.85 \times 1939.8$$

$$h = 849.47 + 1648.83$$

$$\boxed{\;h = 2498.30 \; kJ/kg\;}$$

**Part (c): Specific Internal Energy**

$$u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg} = 849.24 + 0.85 \times 1743.7$$

$$u = 849.24 + 1482.145$$

$$\boxed{\;u = 2331.39 \; kJ/kg\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking Pattern Verified:** Full marks (7/7) require: (i) statement of saturation properties, (ii) explicit computation of $v_{fg}$, $h_{fg}$, $u_{fg}$, (iii) substitution with quality, (iv) boxed final answer with **correct units**. Skipping any of these costs 1‚Äď2 marks.

## 3.4 Summary of Regions in P‚Äďv and P‚ÄďT Planes

| Region | P‚Äďv Diagram Location | P‚ÄďT Diagram Location |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Solid | High P, very low v (far left) | Low T, low P, below sublimation curve |
| Subcooled / Compressed Liquid | Left of saturated liquid line | Below vaporization curve, right of fusion curve |
| Saturated Liquid‚ÄďVapor Mixture | Inside the dome | **On** the vaporization curve (line) |
| Saturated Liquid | Left edge of dome | ‚ÄĒ |
| Saturated Vapor | Right edge of dome | ‚ÄĒ |
| Superheated Vapor | Right of saturated vapor line | Above vaporization curve, right of critical T |
| Critical Point | Apex of the dome | End of vaporization curve |
| Triple Point | Bottom of dome (line) | Junction of all three curves |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid diagrams comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 syntax safeguards: purely alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords as node names, no markdown formatting inside quoted labels.

## 4.1 P‚ÄďV Diagram of a Pure Substance (Phase Dome + Isotherms)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["COMPRESSED LIQUID REGION\nlow v, steep slopes"] --> B["SATURATED LIQUID LINE\nleft branch of dome"]
    B --> C["TWO-PHASE MIXTURE REGION\ninside dome: liquid plus vapor coexist"]
    C --> D["SATURATED VAPOR LINE\nright branch of dome"]
    D --> E["SUPERHEATED VAPOR REGION\nhigh v, ideal-gas-like isotherms"]
    B --> F["CRITICAL POINT CP\nPc, Tc, vc ‚ÄĒ top of dome"]
    F --> D
    B --> G["TRIPLE LINE\nvery low P ‚ÄĒ solid plus liquid plus vapor"]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    H["ISOTHERM BELOW Tc\nhorizontal inside dome, flat outside"] -.-> C
    I["ISOTHERM AT Tc\npasses through critical point with horizontal inflection"] -.-> F
    J["ISOTHERM ABOVE Tc\nmonotonic, no phase change"] -.-> E
```

**Interpretation for the Student:**
- The **dome** is the two-phase envelope. Inside it, $P$ and $T$ are **not independent** ‚ÄĒ they are linked by the saturation relation $P_{sat}(T)$.
- **Horizontal isotherms inside the dome** (below $T_c$) represent phase change at constant $T$ and $P$ (condensation/evaporation).
- The **critical isotherm** passes through the critical point with a horizontal inflection (this is what defines $P_c$).
- Isotherms **above $T_c$** show smooth supercritical behavior ‚ÄĒ no distinct phase boundary.

## 4.2 P‚ÄďT Diagram of a Pure Substance (Triple Point, Critical Point, Phase Boundaries)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["SOLID REGION\nleft side, low T high P"] --> B["SUBLIMATION CURVE\nsolid to vapor boundary"]
    B --> C["VAPOR REGION\nright side, low P, high T"]
    A --> D["FUSION CURVE\nsolid to liquid boundary, nearly vertical"]
    D --> E["LIQUID REGION\nmiddle band"]
    E --> F["VAPORIZATION CURVE\nliquid to vapor boundary, ends at critical point"]
    F --> C
    D --> G["TRIPLE POINT TP\nunique invariant: solid plus liquid plus vapor coexist"]
    B --> G
    F --> G
    F --> H["CRITICAL POINT CP\nend of vaporization curve: h_fg goes to zero"]
    E --> H
    C --> H
    I["SUPERCRITICAL FLUID REGION\nabove and right of CP ‚ÄĒ neither gas nor liquid"] -.-> H
```

**Interpretation for the Student:**
- The **triple point** is the unique point where all three phase boundaries meet. For water: $T_{tp} = 0.01\,¬įC$, $P_{tp} = 611.657\,Pa$.
- The **vaporization curve terminates** at the critical point ‚ÄĒ beyond it, you cannot distinguish liquid from gas.
- The **fusion curve for water slopes backward** (negative slope: $dP/dT < 0$) ‚ÄĒ ice melts under pressure. This is anomalous; for most substances it slopes forward.
- **Sublimation** is solid ‚Üí vapor directly (e.g., dry ice, freeze-drying).

## 4.3 Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ How P‚ÄďV and P‚ÄďT Diagrams are Used in Cycle Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Process Identification\nisobaric, isothermal, isentropic, etc"] --> B["Choose the Correct Diagram\nP-v for cycles with phase change; P-T for phase-boundary analysis"]
    B --> C["Mark State Points\nusing saturation tables and quality x"]
    C --> D["Apply Energy Balance\nfirst law: dh plus du plus dw"]
    D --> E["Compute Work and Heat\narea under P-v curve gives work"]
    E --> F["Validate Against Dome\nensure points lie on correct region boundary"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define the following terms with reference to a pure substance:**
(a) Saturation temperature, (b) Quality, (c) Critical point.

**Model Answer (‚Čą 3 √ó 1 = 3 marks):**

- **(a) Saturation Temperature ($T_{sat}$):** The temperature at which a pure substance undergoes a phase change (boiling or condensation) at a given pressure. For a given pressure, $T_{sat}$ is unique for a pure substance. *(1 mark)*
- **(b) Quality ($x$):** The ratio of the mass of vapor to the total mass of a liquid‚Äďvapor mixture at saturation. It is dimensionless and ranges from **0** (saturated liquid) to **1** (saturated vapor). $x = m_v / (m_l + m_v)$. *(1 mark)*
- **(c) Critical Point:** The unique state at which the saturated liquid and saturated vapor phases become identical (i.e., $v_f = v_g$, $h_{fg} = 0$). Beyond this point, the substance exists as a supercritical fluid with no distinguishable liquid or gas phase. *(1 mark)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024, Model]**
**Sketch the P‚Äďv diagram of a pure substance and label the following: (i) saturated liquid line, (ii) critical point, (iii) compressed liquid region, (iv) superheated vapor region, (v) two-phase dome.**

**Model Answer (3 marks ‚ÄĒ 1 for correct shape, 1 for dome, 1 for labels):**

The P‚Äďv diagram consists of a **bell-shaped (dome) curve** with:
- **Compressed liquid region:** to the left of the saturated liquid line (low $v$, near-vertical isotherms).
- **Saturated liquid line:** left branch of the dome.
- **Two-phase dome interior:** liquid‚Äďvapor mixture (horizontal isotherms at constant $P$).
- **Saturated vapor line:** right branch of the dome.
- **Critical point:** the apex of the dome.
- **Superheated vapor region:** to the right of the saturated vapor line.

*(Sketch to be drawn on answer paper; full marks require correct dome shape + all 5 labels visible.)*
