## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚ÄĒ Module Internal Choice)

### ‚≠ź Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the Clausius‚ÄďClapeyron equation for a single-component, two-phase equilibrium. State clearly all assumptions used. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Water at 1.4 bar has a quality of 0.7. Determine:
1. The temperature of the mixture.
2. The specific volume, specific enthalpy, and specific entropy of the mixture.
*(At 1.4 bar: $T_{sat} = 109.3\,¬įC$, $v_f = 0.001048\,m^3/kg$, $v_g = 1.2369\,m^3/kg$, $h_f = 459.6\,kJ/kg$, $h_{fg} = 2233.1\,kJ/kg$, $s_f = 1.4009\,kJ/kgK$, $s_{fg} = 5.9493\,kJ/kgK$.)* *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Question A:**

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Two phases $\alpha$ and $\beta$ of a pure substance coexist in equilibrium when their chemical potentials (or molar Gibbs energies) are equal:

$$\mu_\alpha(T, P) = \mu_\beta(T, P)$$

*Step 2 (1 mark):* Differentiating along the saturation curve:

$$d\mu_\alpha = d\mu_\beta$$

*Step 3 (1.5 marks):* Using $d\mu = -s\,dT + v\,dP$ (fundamental relation for single component):

$$-s_\alpha\,dT + v_\alpha\,dP = -s_\beta\,dT + v_\beta\,dP$$

*Step 4 (1 mark):* Rearranging:

$$(v_\beta - v_\alpha)\,dP = (s_\beta - s_\alpha)\,dT$$

*Step 5 (1.5 marks):* For phase change at constant $T$ and $P$: $\Delta s = h_{fg}/T$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{dP_{sat}}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}\;}$$

*Assumptions (1 mark):* (i) Single-component system, (ii) Phases in thermal, mechanical, and chemical equilibrium, (iii) Reversible phase transition, (iv) For ideal-gas form: vapor behaves as ideal gas and $v_l \ll v_v$.

---

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical (7 marks):**

*Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Temperature (1 mark):*
At $P = 1.4\,bar$, the saturation temperature is read directly from the steam table:
$$T = T_{sat} = 109.3\,¬įC$$

*Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Specific Volume (2 marks):*
$$v_{fg} = v_g - v_f = 1.2369 - 0.001048 = 1.23585 \; m^3/kg$$
$$v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg} = 0.001048 + 0.7 \times 1.23585 = 0.001048 + 0.86510$$
$$\boxed{\;v = 0.8661 \; m^3/kg\;}$$

*Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Specific Enthalpy (2 marks):*
$$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} = 459.6 + 0.7 \times 2233.1 = 459.6 + 1563.17$$
$$\boxed{\;h = 2022.77 \; kJ/kg\;}$$

*Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Specific Entropy (2 marks):*
$$s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg} = 1.4009 + 0.7 \times 5.9493 = 1.4009 + 4.1645$$
$$\boxed{\;s = 5.5654 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)\;}$$

---

### ‚≠ź Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat P‚ÄďT diagram, explain the various regions and curves for a pure substance. What is the significance of the triple point and critical point? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Saturated steam at 250 ¬įC is throttled to a pressure of 100 kPa. Find the final temperature and quality of the resulting mixture. *(At 250 ¬įC: $h_g = 2716.9\,kJ/kg$, $s_g = 6.7247\,kJ/kgK$. At 100 kPa: $T_{sat} = 99.63\,¬įC$, $h_f = 417.51\,kJ/kg$, $h_{fg} = 2257.5\,kJ/kg$, $s_f = 1.3027\,kJ/kgK$, $s_{fg} = 6.0568\,kJ/kgK$.)* *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Question B:**

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ P‚ÄďT Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* The P‚ÄďT diagram of a pure substance consists of three regions (**solid**, **liquid**, **vapor**) separated by three phase-boundary curves.

*Step 2 (2 marks):* The three curves are:
- **Sublimation curve:** boundary between solid and vapor (low P, low T).
- **Vaporization curve:** boundary between liquid and vapor ‚ÄĒ terminates at the critical point.
- **Fusion curve:** boundary between solid and liquid ‚ÄĒ nearly vertical.

*Step 3 (2 marks):* **Triple Point Significance:** The unique state where solid, liquid, and vapor coexist in equilibrium ($F = C - P + 2 = 1 - 3 + 2 = 0$, invariant ‚ÄĒ fixed by nature). For water: $T_{tp} = 0.01\,¬įC$, $P_{tp} = 611.657\,Pa$.

*Step 4 (2 marks):* **Critical Point Significance:** End of the vaporization curve. Above this point, no distinction between liquid and gas exists (supercritical fluid). The latent heat of vaporization $h_{fg} \to 0$. For water: $T_c = 373.946\,¬įC$, $P_c = 22.064\,MPa$.

*Sketch requirements (within the 7 marks):* A clean diagram showing all three regions, three curves, triple point, critical point, and the supercritical fluid region.

---

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Throttling Calculation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify Process (1 mark):*
Throttling is an **isenthalpic** process (no work, no heat, negligible kinetic/potential energy change): $h_1 = h_2$.

*Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Initial State (1 mark):*
Saturated steam at 250 ¬įC means $x_1 = 1$, so:
$$h_1 = h_g(250\,¬įC) = 2716.9 \; kJ/kg$$

*Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Final State Identification (1 mark):*
Final pressure = 100 kPa. Saturation temperature at 100 kPa is 99.63 ¬įC. Since $h_1 = 2716.9\,kJ/kg$ is **greater** than $h_g(100\,kPa) = 417.51 + 2257.5 = 2675.01\,kJ/kg$, the final state lies in the **superheated region**.

*Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Final Temperature (2 marks):*
From superheated steam tables at $P = 100\,kPa$:
- At 150 ¬įC: $h = 2776.6\,kJ/kg$
- At 125 ¬įC: $h = 2725.5\,kJ/kg$

Interpolating for $h = 2716.9\,kJ/kg$:
$$T_2 = 125 + \frac{2776.6 - 2716.9}{2776.6 - 2725.5} \times (150 - 125)$$
$$T_2 = 125 + \frac{59.7}{51.1} \times 25 = 125 + 29.2 = 154.2\,¬įC$$

$$\boxed{\;T_2 \approx 154\,¬įC\;}$$

*Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Quality (2 marks):*
Since the final state is **superheated** ($T_2 > T_{sat}$ at 100 kPa), the quality is:

$$\boxed{\;x_2 = \text{undefined (superheated vapor, not a two-phase mixture)}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚ÄĒ Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "saturation" with "sublimation":** Sublimation is solid ‚Üí vapor, NOT liquid ‚Üí vapor. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting to state the quality $x$ in two-phase property calculations:** Even if all numerical work is correct, missing $x$ in the formula costs marks. (-2 marks)
> 3. **Wrong use of $v_{fg}$:** Writing $v_g - v_l$ as $v_g$ alone (omitting subtraction of $v_f$). This is a frequent error. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Throttling problems: assuming the final state is two-phase** without checking $h_1$ vs $h_g$ at the final pressure. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Drawing the P‚ÄďT diagram without labeling the critical point and triple point explicitly:** Marks are reserved for labels. (-1 mark)
> 6. **Writing units as $J$ instead of $kJ$** for enthalpy: A common 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **pure substance** can have multiple phases of the same chemical species in equilibrium.
- [x] **Phase Rule:** $F = C - P + 2$. For a pure substance in two phases: $F = 1$ (one property fixes the state).
- [x] **Saturation curve** on P‚Äďv is a *dome*; on P‚ÄďT it is a *line* ending at the critical point.
- [x] **Critical Point:** top of dome, end of vaporization curve, $h_{fg} = 0$, no phase distinction.
- [x] **Triple Point:** three phases coexist, $F = 0$, fixed by nature.
- [x] **Quality $x$** ranges from 0 (saturated liquid) to 1 (saturated vapor).
- [x] All two-phase specific properties follow the **lever rule**: $\phi = \phi_f + x \phi_{fg}$.
- [x] **Clausius‚ÄďClapeyron:** $\dfrac{dP_{sat}}{dT} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}$ (exact form).
- [x] **Integrated form (ideal gas):** $\ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = -\dfrac{h_{fg}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$.
- [x] **P‚Äďv isotherms** are horizontal inside the dome and smooth outside.
- [x] **P‚ÄďT diagram** has three regions (solid, liquid, vapor) and three curves (sublimation, vaporization, fusion).
- [x] **Supercritical fluid:** region above $T_c$ and $P_c$ ‚ÄĒ no clear phase boundary.
- [x] **Throttling = isenthalpic process:** $h_1 = h_2$ (used for state-change calculations).
- [x] Always **state units explicitly** in final answers ($kJ/kg$, $m^3/kg$, $kJ/(kg \cdot K)$).
- [x] **Anomaly of water:** fusion curve has *negative* slope (ice melts under pressure). For most substances, slope is positive.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 Notes ‚ÄĒ P-V and P-T Diagrams of Pure Substances.**
> Students should practice drawing both diagrams from memory and solving at least three numerical problems on quality and Clausius‚ÄďClapeyron before the ESE. These are the **most frequently repeated 7-mark and 14-mark patterns** in KTU 2024 Scheme assessments.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equations of State for Real Gases: van der Waal's, Redlich-Kwong, Peng-Robinson, and Virial Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Equations of State for Real Gases: van der Waal's, Redlich-Kwong, Peng-Robinson, and Virial Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ‚ÄĒ Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Equation of State (EOS)** is a thermodynamic constitutive relation that mathematically interlinks the three primary intensive state variables ‚ÄĒ **Pressure (P)**, **Temperature (T)**, and **Molar Volume (V_m)** ‚ÄĒ of a pure substance or a defined mixture at thermodynamic equilibrium. While the **Ideal Gas Equation of State** $PV_m = RT$ assumes point-mass molecules with no intermolecular forces, real fluids exhibit **non-ideal behaviour** arising from two dominant physical phenomena: **finite molecular volume (repulsive forces)** and **intermolecular attractive forces (van der Waals forces, dipole interactions, hydrogen bonding, etc.)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Core Concept:** Real-gas EOS are empirical or semi-empirical extensions to the ideal gas law, calibrated to reproduce the experimentally observed **P-V-T** surface of fluids across sub-critical, critical, and super-critical regions. The **Compressibility Factor** $Z = \frac{PV_m}{RT}$ is the master yardstick for non-ideality.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Crowded Elevator" Model

Imagine an **elevator (the container)** packed with **people (the molecules)** going to different floors:

| Scenario | Behaviour | Thermodynamic Analogue |
|----------|-----------|------------------------|
| **Empty Elevator** | People walk freely, no interaction | Ideal Gas ($Z = 1$) |
| **Half-Full Elevator** | People bump into each other occasionally | Real Gas at **moderate P** |
| **Packed Elevator** | People take up real space, push back | Real Gas at **high P** (repulsive term dominant) |
| **Crowded Hallway** | People feel a "stickiness" slowing them | Real Gas at **low P** (attractive term dominant) |

- The **finite size** of each person is like the **co-volume constant `b`** in van der Waals.
- The **attraction** between people slowing the crowd is like the **attractive parameter `a`** in cubic EOS.
- The **ideal gas law fails** the moment you add even one person to an empty elevator ‚ÄĒ and the failure grows as the crowd thickens.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Every cubic EOS (vdW, RK, PR, SRK) is essentially a clever "elevator rulebook" ‚ÄĒ a mathematical translation of these repulsive and attractive effects into a closed-form equation that engineers can solve analytically for V, P, or T.

## 1.3 The Compressibility Factor ‚ÄĒ The Master Diagnostic

$$Z = \frac{PV_m}{RT} = \frac{V_{m,\text{real}}}{V_{m,\text{ideal}}}$$

| Condition | Z Value | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|---------|------------------|
| Ideal gas | $Z = 1$ | No deviation |
| Attractive forces dominate (low P) | $Z < 1$ | Molecules pulled closer |
| Repulsive forces dominate (high P) | $Z > 1$ | Molecules pushed apart |
| Boyle Temperature | $Z = 1$ | Coincidental ideal behaviour |
| Critical Point | $Z_c \approx 0.27$ to $0.31$ | All fluids converge to this range |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ‚ÄĒ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Historical & Theoretical Foundation

Real-gas EOS are best understood through their **chronological evolution** ‚ÄĒ each new equation was developed to overcome a specific limitation of its predecessor.

### 2.1.1 van der Waals Equation (1873) ‚ÄĒ The Pioneer

**Conceptual Basis:** Johannes Diderik van der Waals introduced the first EOS that captured *both* repulsive and attractive effects with **two empirical constants (a and b)**, each having clear physical meaning.

**P-V Form:**

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{V_m^2}\right)(V_m - b) = RT$$

**Expanded (Cubic) Form:**

$$V_m^3 - \left(b + \frac{RT}{P}\right)V_m^2 + \frac{a}{P}V_m - \frac{ab}{P} = 0$$

- **Repulsive term** $\rightarrow b$ (excluded volume, no two molecules can occupy the same space)
- **Attractive term** $\rightarrow a/V_m^2$ (internal pressure correction; attractive forces reduce wall-collisions, hence the *positive* addition inside the bracket)

**Critique:** Quantitative accuracy is poor near the critical point; predicts a *flat* critical isotherm when it should be *inflectional*.

### 2.1.2 Redlich-Kwong Equation (1949) ‚ÄĒ The Improvement

**Conceptual Basis:** Otto Redlich and Joseph Kwong argued that the attractive term in vdW should be **temperature-dependent** because kinetic energy competes with cohesive forces.

**Standard Form:**

$$P = \frac{RT}{V_m - b} - \frac{a}{T^{0.5}\,V_m(V_m + b)}$$

**Trick for quick conversion to cubic form:**

$$Z = \frac{PV_m}{RT}$$

Substituting and defining reduced parameters $A = \frac{aP}{R^2T^{2.5}}$ and $B = \frac{bP}{RT}$, $h = \frac{B}{Z}$:

$$Z = \frac{1}{1-h} - \frac{A}{B}\cdot\frac{h}{1+h}$$

- Better vapour-phase P-V-T predictions than vdW.
- Still fails quantitatively for the liquid phase.

### 2.1.3 Peng-Robinson Equation (1976) ‚ÄĒ The Industrial Workhorse

**Conceptual Basis:** Ding-Yu Peng and Donald Robinson re-engineered the attractive term to yield **excellent liquid-density predictions** ‚ÄĒ critical for refinery and petrochemical design.

**Standard Form:**

$$P = \frac{RT}{V_m - b} - \frac{a\alpha}{V_m(V_m + b) + b(V_m - b)}$$

**Key features:**
- The denominator in the attractive term $V_m(V_m + b) + b(V_m - b)$ is engineered to match the **experimental critical compressibility** $Z_c \approx 0.307$ (vs. RK's $0.333$).
- The **alpha function** $\alpha(T)$ introduces temperature dependence through the **acentric factor** $\omega$.

### 2.1.4 Virial Equation of State ‚ÄĒ The Statistical-Mechanics Backbone

**Conceptual Basis:** Derived rigorously from **statistical mechanics** (cluster expansion of the partition function by Ursell and Mayer, 1937). The virial EOS is an **infinite power series** in either density ($\rho = 1/V_m$) or pressure.

**Density (Leiden) Form:**

$$Z = \frac{PV_m}{RT} = 1 + B(T)\rho + C(T)\rho^2 + D(T)\rho^3 + \cdots$$

**Pressure (Berlin) Form:**

$$Z = 1 + B'(T)P + C'(T)P^2 + D'(T)P^3 + \cdots$$

Where:
- $B(T)$ ‚Üí **2nd virial coefficient** (pair interactions)
- $C(T)$ ‚Üí **3rd virial coefficient** (triplet interactions)
- Higher coefficients ‚Üí many-body interactions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mayer's Relations** (converting between density and pressure forms):
> $B' = B/RT$, $C' = (C - B^2)/(RT)^2$, $D' = (D - 3BC + 2B^3)/(RT)^3$

The virial EOS is **theoretically exact** when truncated at sufficient order, but its convergence depends strongly on the density regime.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula you must memorize** for KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. Constants are expressed in terms of **critical properties** ($T_c$, $P_c$) and the **acentric factor** ($\omega$). All molar-volume-based equations assume SI units (Pa, m¬≥/mol, J/mol¬∑K).

| EOS | Equation Form | Constant `a` | Constant `b` | Additional Terms |
|-----|---------------|--------------|--------------|------------------|
| **Ideal Gas** | $PV_m = RT$ | None | None | Exact only at $\lim P \to 0$ |
| **van der Waals** | $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V_m^2}\right)(V_m - b) = RT$ | $\dfrac{27R^2T_c^2}{64P_c}$ | $\dfrac{RT_c}{8P_c}$ | $Z_c = 0.375$ (predicted) |
| **Redlich-Kwong** | $P = \dfrac{RT}{V_m - b} - \dfrac{a}{T^{0.5}V_m(V_m+b)}$ | $0.42748\dfrac{R^2T_c^{2.5}}{P_c}$ | $0.08664\dfrac{RT_c}{P_c}$ | $Z_c = 0.333$ (predicted) |
| **Peng-Robinson** | $P = \dfrac{RT}{V_m-b} - \dfrac{a\alpha}{V_m(V_m+b)+b(V_m-b)}$ | $0.45724\dfrac{R^2T_c^2}{P_c}$ | $0.07780\dfrac{RT_c}{P_c}$ | $\alpha = (1+\kappa(1-\sqrt{T_r}))^2$ |
| **Virial (Density)** | $Z = 1 + B\rho + C\rho^2 + D\rho^3$ | $B(T)$ ‚ÄĒ 2nd coeff. | $C(T)$ ‚ÄĒ 3rd coeff. | $\rho = 1/V_m$ |
| **Virial (Pressure)** | $Z = 1 + B'P + C'P^2 + D'P^3$ | $B'(T)$ ‚ÄĒ 2nd coeff. | $C'(T)$ ‚ÄĒ 3rd coeff. | Mayer's relation: $B' = B/RT$ |

**Peng-Robinson Auxiliary Definitions:**

$$\kappa = 0.37464 + 1.54226\omega - 0.26992\omega^2$$

$$T_r = \frac{T}{T_c}, \quad P_r = \frac{P}{P_c}, \quad V_r = \frac{P_c V_m}{RT_c}$$

## 2.3 Engineering Utility ‚ÄĒ Why This Topic Earns 14 Marks

| Industry Sector | Preferred EOS | Why |
|-----------------|---------------|-----|
| **Petrochemical / Refinery** | Peng-Robinson (PR) | Excellent for hydrocarbons, predicts liquid density within ~1% |
| **Natural Gas Processing** | Redlich-Kwong (RK) / Soave-RK (SRK) | Good for non-polar light gases, simple to compute |
| **Vapour-Liquid Equilibrium (VLE)** | PR / SRK | Built-in capability to model phase envelopes |
| **Cryogenics (H‚āā, He, N‚āā)** | Virial (2nd coefficient) | Convergence fast at low-to-moderate densities |
| **Educational/Historical** | van der Waals | Conceptual clarity, exact analytical solvability |
| **Supercritical Fluid Apps** | PR / Benedict-Webb-Rubin | Handles supercritical CO‚āā for extraction, drug delivery |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Heuristic:** When a numerical problem gives you $T_c$, $P_c$, and a state (P, T), the **default expectation** is that you use **Peng-Robinson** (as it is the modern industrial standard) unless explicitly directed to use another EOS.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚ÄĒ van der Waals Constants from Critical Point Conditions

**Starting Premise:** At the **critical point**, the isotherm on a P-V diagram has an **inflection point** with zero slope. This yields two simultaneous conditions:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V_m}\right)_{T_c} = 0 \quad \text{(zero slope, extremum condition)}$$

$$\left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V_m^2}\right)_{T_c} = 0 \quad \text{(inflection point condition)}$$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Rearrange vdW equation to solve for P:**

$$P = \frac{RT}{V_m - b} - \frac{a}{V_m^2}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute the first derivative $\partial P / \partial V_m$:**

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial V_m} = -\frac{RT}{(V_m - b)^2} + \frac{2a}{V_m^3}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute the second derivative $\partial^2 P / \partial V_m^2$:**

$$\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V_m^2} = \frac{2RT}{(V_m - b)^3} - \frac{6a}{V_m^4}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Apply Condition 1 at the critical point $(T_c, V_{m,c})$:**

$$-\frac{RT_c}{(V_{m,c} - b)^2} + \frac{2a}{V_{m,c}^3} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{RT_c}{(V_{m,c} - b)^2} = \frac{2a}{V_{m,c}^3}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Apply Condition 2 at the critical point:**

$$\frac{2RT_c}{(V_{m,c} - b)^3} - \frac{6a}{V_{m,c}^4} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{2RT_c}{(V_{m,c} - b)^3} = \frac{6a}{V_{m,c}^4}$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Divide Step 5 by Step 4 to eliminate $T_c$ and $a$:**

$$\frac{\dfrac{2RT_c}{(V_{m,c} - b)^3}}{\dfrac{RT_c}{(V_{m,c} - b)^2}} = \frac{\dfrac{6a}{V_{m,c}^4}}{\dfrac{2a}{V_{m,c}^3}}$$

$$\frac{2}{V_{m,c} - b} = \frac{3}{V_{m,c}}$$

**Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Solve the resulting algebraic relation for $V_{m,c}$:**

$$2 V_{m,c} = 3(V_{m,c} - b)$$

$$2V_{m,c} = 3V_{m,c} - 3b$$

$$V_{m,c} = 3b$$

This is the famous vdW prediction: the critical molar volume equals three times the excluded-volume constant.

**Step 8 ‚ÄĒ Substitute $V_{m,c} = 3b$ back into Step 4:**

$$\frac{RT_c}{(3b - b)^2} = \frac{2a}{(3b)^3}$$

$$\frac{RT_c}{4b^2} = \frac{2a}{27b^3}$$

**Step 9 ‚ÄĒ Solve for `a` in terms of $T_c$ and $b$:**

$$RT_c \cdot 27b^3 = 2a \cdot 4b^2$$

$$27 RT_c b = 8a$$

$$a = \frac{27 R T_c b}{8}$$

**Step 10 ‚ÄĒ Substitute back into Step 8 to obtain `b`:**

$$RT_c = \frac{2a \cdot 4b^2}{27b^3} = \frac{8a}{27b}$$

Combining with $a = 27RT_cb/8$:

$$b = \frac{RT_c}{8P_c}, \quad a = \frac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c}$$

**Step 11 ‚ÄĒ Critical Compressibility Factor (sanity check):**

$$Z_c = \frac{P_c V_{m,c}}{RT_c} = \frac{P_c \cdot 3b}{RT_c} = \frac{3 P_c}{RT_c} \cdot \frac{RT_c}{8P_c} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$$

> [!NOTE]
> Real fluids have $Z_c$ ranging from **0.23 to 0.31**, so vdW's prediction of 0.375 is its most glaring quantitative flaw ‚ÄĒ directly motivating the Redlich-Kwong and Peng-Robinson refinements.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚ÄĒ Redlich-Kwong Constants

**Starting Premise:** Apply the same critical-point conditions to the RK equation:

$$P = \frac{RT}{V_m - b} - \frac{a}{T^{0.5} V_m(V_m + b)}$$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ First derivative with respect to $V_m$ at $T = T_c$:**

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial V_m} = -\frac{RT_c}{(V_{m,c} - b)^2} + \frac{a}{T_c^{0.5}}\left[\frac{2V_m + b}{V_m^2(V_m + b)^2}\right]_{V_m = V_{m,c}} = 0$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Solve the system algebraically (omitting identical process to vdW for brevity).** The result is:

$$V_{m,c} = 3b, \quad a = 0.42748 \frac{R^2 T_c^{2.5}}{P_c}, \quad b = 0.08664 \frac{RT_c}{P_c}$$

$$Z_c = 0.333$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> RK improves the critical compressibility from 0.375 to 0.333 ‚ÄĒ closer to real values but still high. The **temperature exponent of 0.5** is empirical; it lacks theoretical justification.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚ÄĒ Peng-Robinson Constants

**Starting Premise:** Generalize the attractive-term denominator to a flexible function of $V_m$:

$$P = \frac{RT}{V_m - b} - \frac{a\alpha}{V_m^2 + 2bV_m - b^2}$$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Apply critical conditions to the generalized cubic:**

The system yields the same critical volume $V_{m,c} = 3b$ (by design), and the following constants are obtained:

$$a = 0.45724 \frac{R^2 T_c^2}{P_c}, \quad b = 0.07780 \frac{RT_c}{P_c}$$

$$Z_c = 0.307$$

> [!NOTE]
> Peng-Robinson's $Z_c = 0.307$ matches the **experimental average** of hydrocarbons (~0.307) almost perfectly, which is why it is the gold standard for oil & gas simulation packages like **Aspen HYSYS**, **PRO/II**, and **OLGA**.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Derivation of the alpha function (heuristic motivation):**

The acentric factor $\omega$ is defined at $T_r = 0.7$:

$$\omega = -\log_{10}\left(P^{sat}/P_c\right)\big|_{T_r=0.7} - 1$$

The alpha function must satisfy:
- $\alpha(T_c) = 1$ (so that at $T = T_c$, PR reduces to a classical cubic)
- $\alpha(0.7 T_c) = f(\omega)$ (matches vapour pressure at $T_r = 0.7$)

Solving these constraints leads to:

$$\alpha = \left[1 + \kappa\left(1 - \sqrt{T_r}\right)\right]^2$$

with

$$\kappa = 0.37464 + 1.54226\omega - 0.26992\omega^2$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚ÄĒ Virial Equation from Statistical Mechanics (Mayer Cluster Expansion)

**Starting Premise:** The canonical partition function for $N$ molecules interacting via pair potential $u(r)$ can be written as:

$$Z_N = \frac{1}{N!}\left(\frac{2\pi mkT}{h^2}\right)^{3N/2} Q_N$$

where $Q_N$ is the **configurational integral**:

$$Q_N = \int \cdots \int \exp\left(-\frac{U}{kT}\right) d\mathbf{r}_1 \cdots d\mathbf{r}_N$$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Introduce the Mayer f-function:**

$$f_{ij} = \exp\left(-\frac{u(r_{ij})}{kT}\right) - 1$$

This is a "switching function" that equals -1 when molecules are close (strong repulsion) and 0 when far apart.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Expand the Boltzmann factor as a cluster sum:**

$$\exp\left(-\frac{U}{kT}\right) = \prod_{i<j}(1 + f_{ij}) = 1 + \sum_{i<j} f_{ij} + \sum_{i<j<k} f_{ij}f_{jk}f_{ki} + \cdots$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Substitute into $Q_N$ and identify irreducible cluster integrals:**

The **2nd virial coefficient** is the integral of the Mayer f-function over pair interactions:

$$B(T) = -\frac{1}{2} \int f_{12}\, d\mathbf{r}_{12} = -2\pi \int_0^{\infty} \left[\exp\left(-\frac{u(r)}{kT}\right) - 1\right] r^2 dr$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ The 3rd virial coefficient involves triplet integrals:**

$$C(T) = -\frac{1}{3} \iint f_{12} f_{13} f_{23}\, d\mathbf{r}_{12}\, d\mathbf{r}_{13} + \cdots$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Truncate and assemble into the pressure equation:**

$$\frac{P}{kT} = \rho + B(T)\rho^2 + C(T)\rho^3 + \cdots$$

Multiplying by $V_m = 1/\rho$ and dividing by $RT$:

$$Z = 1 + B(T)\rho + C(T)\rho^2 + \cdots \quad \text{(Density form)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The virial EOS is the **only EOS with rigorous statistical-mechanical foundations**. All cubic EOS (vdW, RK, PR) are empirical approximations whose truncation of the virial series at a particular order yields a closed-form equation.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix ‚ÄĒ Choosing the Right EOS

| Feature | Ideal Gas | vdW | RK | PR | Virial (Truncated) |
|---------|-----------|-----|----|----|-------------------|
| **# of Constants** | 0 | 2 | 2 | 2 + $\alpha$ | Infinitely many |
| **Theoretical Basis** | Kinetic theory | Empirical | Empirical | Empirical | Statistical mechanics |
| **Critical $Z_c$** | N/A | 0.375 | 0.333 | 0.307 | Matches data |
| **Vapour P-V-T** | Poor | Fair | Good | Excellent | Excellent (truncated) |
| **Liquid Density** | Useless | Poor | Fair | Excellent | Poor (converges slowly) |
| **Mixtures** | $Z = 1$ | Mixing rules | Mixing rules | Mixing rules | $B_{mix}$ formula |
| **Computational Cost** | Trivial | Cubic solve | Cubic solve | Cubic solve | Series evaluation |
| **Convergence** | Always | Always (1 or 3 real roots) | Always | Always | Depends on density |
| **KTU Frequency** | Low | High | Medium | **Highest** | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 ‚ÄĒ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 ‚ÄĒ Decision Logic for Selecting an EOS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given P, V, T and substance] --> B{Is Z close to 1?}
    B -->|Yes, Z within 5 percent of 1| C[Use Ideal Gas EOS: PV = RT]
    B -->|No, significant deviation| D{Is system near critical point?}
    D -->|No, moderate conditions| E{Is polar substance?}
    D -->|Yes, near T_c| F[Use Peng-Robinson EOS]
    E -->|No, non-polar gas| G{Is pressure less than 10 bar?}
    E -->|Yes, polar| H[Use virial truncated to 2nd coefficient or PR with modified alpha]
    G -->|Yes, low pressure| I[Use 2nd virial coefficient form: Z = 1 + BP/RT]
    G -->|No, moderate to high P| J[Use RK or SRK EOS]
    F --> K[Compute alpha, kappa using acentric factor omega]
    J --> L[Solve cubic for V_m, select physical root]
    I --> L
    H --> L
    C --> M[End: Report P, V, T result]
    L --> M
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 ‚ÄĒ P-V Isotherm Behaviour Across EOS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IDeal_Gas[IDEAL GAS ISOTHERM]
    I1[P decreases smoothly as V increases] --> I2[Hyperbolic PV equals constant curve]
    I2 --> I3[No phase transition, no critical point]
    end

    subgraph VdW_Isotherm[van der Waals ISOTHERM at T less than T_c]
    V1[High P region: P drops steeply] --> V2[Oscillating region: 3 real roots]
    V2 --> V3[Spinodal points and inflection]
    V3 --> V4[Low P tail approaches ideal behaviour]
    end

    subgraph PR_Isotherm[Peng-Robinson ISOTHERM at T less than T_c]
    P1[Liquid branch: high density, low V] --> P2[Vapour branch: low density, high V]
    P2 --> P3[Coexistence envelope: Maxwell construction]
    P3 --> P1
    end

    subgraph Critical_Region[CRITICAL POINT REGION]
    C1[At T equals T_c, the isotherm has an inflection] --> C2[Slope and curvature vanish simultaneously]
    C2 --> C3[All 3 real roots merge into one]
    end

    VdW_Isotherm --> Critical_Region
    PR_Isotherm --> Critical_Region
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 ‚ÄĒ Block-Level Architecture of Cubic EOS Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Input: P, T, T_c, P_c, omega] --> Attract[Compute attractive parameter a]
    Input --> Repulse[Compute repulsive parameter b]
    Input --> Alpha[Compute alpha function T_dependent]
    Input --> Mix[Apply mixing rules if mixture]

    Attract --> Add1[Term 1: RT divided by V minus b]
    Repulse --> Add1
    Add1 --> Term1[Repulsive Pressure Contribution]
    Add1 --> Term2[Attractive Pressure Contribution]
    Alpha --> Term2
    Mix --> Term2

    Term1 --> Sum[Total P equals Repulsive minus Attractive]
    Term2 --> Sum
    Sum --> Cubic[Rearrange to cubic in V_m or Z]
    Cubic --> Solve[Analytical or iterative solver]
    Solve --> Roots[Select real positive root with physical meaning]
    Roots --> Output[Output: V_m and Z]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 ‚ÄĒ Virial Coefficient Hierarchy (Mayer Cluster Levels)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cluster Expansion Partition Function] --> B1[Monomer Cluster 1-body integral]
    A --> B2[Pair Cluster 2-body integral = 2nd virial B]
    A --> B3[Triplet Cluster 3-body integral = 3rd virial C]
    A --> B4[Quadruplet Cluster = 4th virial D]
    A --> B5[Higher order N-body = higher virials]

    B1 --> Z1[Contributes constant 1 to Z]
    B2 --> Z2[Contributes B rho to Z]
    B3 --> Z3[Contributes C rho squared to Z]
    B4 --> Z4[Contributes D rho cubed to Z]
    B5 --> Z5[Contributes higher order terms to Z]

    Z1 --> Series[Z = 1 + B rho + C rho squared + D rho cubed + ...]
    Z2 --> Series
    Z3 --> Series
    Z4 --> Series
    Z5 --> Series
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Mayer cluster expansion** is the only theoretically grounded path to virial coefficients. For spherical molecules (e.g., Argon, Methane), the pair potential $u(r)$ is well approximated by the **Lennard-Jones 6-12 potential**, allowing direct evaluation of B(T).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 ‚ÄĒ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]

> **"State the van der Waals equation of state and explain the physical significance of the constants `a` and `b`."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The van der Waals equation of state is:

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{V_m^2}\right)(V_m - b) = RT$$

**Physical Significance of Constants:**

- **Constant `a`** ‚ÄĒ Represents the magnitude of **attractive (cohesive) forces** between molecules. The term $a/V_m^2$ is the *internal pressure* correction; it accounts for the reduction in the rate of molecular collisions with the container walls due to intermolecular attraction. It has units of **Pa¬∑m‚Ā∂/mol¬≤**.

- **Constant `b`** ‚ÄĒ Represents the **effective excluded volume** per mole of molecules; i.e., the finite physical space occupied by the molecules themselves, which is *not* available for free movement. It is related to the molecular diameter $\sigma$ as $b = \frac{2}{3}\pi N_A \sigma^3$. It has units of **m¬≥/mol**.

| Constant | Physical Meaning | Sign in EOS | Units |
|----------|------------------|-------------|-------|
| a | Intermolecular attraction | Adds to P (positive term) | Pa¬∑m‚Ā∂/mol¬≤ |
| b | Molecular excluded volume | Subtracts from V (exclusion) | m¬≥/mol |

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]

> **"What is the virial equation of state? Why is it considered theoretically superior to cubic equations of state?"** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **virial equation of state** expresses the compressibility factor $Z$ as an infinite power series in either density $\rho$ or pressure P:

$$Z = 1 + B(T)\rho + C(T)\rho^2 + D(T)\rho^3 + \cdots \quad \text{(Density form)}$$

$$Z = 1 + B'(T)P + C'(T)P^2 + D'(T)P^3 + \cdots \quad \text{(Pressure form)}$$

**Why it is theoretically superior:**

1. **Rigorous statistical-mechanical derivation** ‚ÄĒ Unlike cubic EOS (which are empirical), the virial coefficients $B(T)$, $C(T)$, $D(T)$ arise directly from the **Mayer cluster expansion** of the canonical partition function.

2. **Systematic improvement** ‚ÄĒ Truncating at the 2nd, 3rd, or 4th virial coefficient provides a **systematic hierarchy of accuracy** as more terms are included.

3. **Asymptotic correctness** ‚ÄĒ At low densities, the series converges rapidly and the truncated form is exact to the order of truncation.

**Limitation:** Convergence deteriorates at high densities (especially near the critical point and in the liquid region), where many terms are needed.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚ÄĒ KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]

> **"For a real gas, derive the expressions for the van der Waals constants `a` and `b` in terms of the critical constants $T_c$ and $P_c$. A gas has $T_c = 126.2$ K and $P_c = 3.39$ MPa. Calculate the pressure exerted by 1 mole of this gas at T = 200 K confined to a volume of $0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m¬≥ using the van der Waals equation."** (14 Marks)

**Sub-part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation of vdW constants (7 Marks)**

*(Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete 11-step derivation.)*

**Key Result:**

$$a = \frac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c}, \quad b = \frac{R T_c}{8 P_c}$$

**Sub-part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Computation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Calculate constants `a` and `b`:**

$$b = \frac{R T_c}{8 P_c} = \frac{8.314 \times 126.2}{8 \times 3.39 \times 10^6}$$

$$b = \frac{1049.23}{27.12 \times 10^6} = 3.868 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$$

$$a = \frac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c} = \frac{27 \times (8.314)^2 \times (126.2)^2}{64 \times 3.39 \times 10^6}$$

$$a = \frac{27 \times 69.12 \times 15926.44}{216.96 \times 10^6} = \frac{29.73 \times 10^6}{216.96 \times 10^6} = 0.1370 \text{ Pa¬∑m}^6/\text{mol}^2$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Substitute into vdW equation:**

$$P = \frac{RT}{V_m - b} - \frac{a}{V_m^2}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Plug in numerical values:**

$$P = \frac{8.314 \times 200}{0.0005 - 3.868 \times 10^{-5}} - \frac{0.1370}{(0.0005)^2}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Evaluate each term:**

$$P = \frac{1662.8}{4.613 \times 10^{-4}} - \frac{0.1370}{2.5 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$P = 3.604 \times 10^6 - 5.48 \times 10^5$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{P = 3.056 \text{ MPa} = 30.56 \text{ bar}}$$

**Verification:** Ideal gas would give $P_{\text{ideal}} = RT/V = 8.314 \times 200 / 0.0005 = 3.326$ MPa. The real gas pressure is lower, consistent with attractive forces dominating at this moderate density ($Z \approx 0.92$).

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice]

> **"For n-butane at T = 500 K and P = 2 MPa, compare the predictions of the compressibility factor $Z$ using (i) the ideal gas law, (ii) van der Waals EOS, and (iii) the Redlich-Kwong EOS. Given: $T_c = 425.12$ K, $P_c = 3.796$ MPa, $\omega = 0.200$."** (14 Marks)

**Sub-part (a) ‚ÄĒ Setup and Ideal Gas Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Ideal gas prediction:**

$$Z_{\text{ideal}} = 1.000$$

For reference, $T_r = 500/425.12 = 1.176$, $P_r = 2/3.796 = 0.527$.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ van der Waals constants:**

$$b = \frac{R T_c}{8 P_c} = \frac{8.314 \times 425.12}{8 \times 3.796 \times 10^6} = 1.164 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$$

$$a = \frac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c} = \frac{27 \times 69.12 \times 180727.0}{64 \times 3.796 \times 10^6} = 1.389 \text{ Pa¬∑m}^6/\text{mol}^2$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Convert to cubic in Z:**

$$Z^3 - \left(\frac{P b}{RT} + 1\right)Z^2 + \frac{a P}{(RT)^2} Z - \frac{ab P^2}{(RT)^3} = 0$$

Computing dimensionless parameters:

$$\frac{Pb}{RT} = \frac{2 \times 10^6 \times 1.164 \times 10^{-4}}{8.314 \times 500} = 0.0560$$

$$\frac{aP}{(RT)^2} = \frac{1.389 \times 2 \times 10^6}{(8.314 \times 500)^2} = 0.0804$$

$$\frac{abP^2}{(RT)^3} = 0.0560 \times 0.0804 \times 0.0560 \times 2 \times 10^6 = 5.04 \times 10^{-4}$$

(The latter term is negligible; can be dropped for fast approximation.)

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Solve the cubic (truncated):**

$$Z^3 - 1.056 Z^2 + 0.0804 Z - 5.04 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0$$

Trial $Z = 0.95$:

$$0.857 - 0.953 + 0.0764 - 0.0005 = -0.0201$$

Refining: $Z \approx 0.93$:

$$0.804 - 0.913 + 0.0748 - 0.0005 = -0.0347$$

Newton-Raphson converges to:

$$Z_{\text{vdW}} \approx 0.905$$

**Sub-part (b) ‚ÄĒ Redlich-Kwong Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ RK constants:**

$$b = 0.08664 \frac{R T_c}{P_c} = 0.08664 \times \frac{8.314 \times 425.12}{3.796 \times 10^6}$$

$$b = 0.08664 \times 9.312 \times 10^{-4} = 8.07 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$$

$$a = 0.42748 \frac{R^2 T_c^{2.5}}{P_c} = 0.42748 \times \frac{69.12 \times (425.12)^{2.5}}{3.796 \times 10^6}$$

$$(425.12)^{2.5} = 425.12^2 \times \sqrt{425.12} = 180727 \times 20.618 = 3.726 \times 10^6$$

$$a = 0.42748 \times \frac{69.12 \times 3.726 \times 10^6}{3.796 \times 10^6} = 0.42748 \times 67.84 = 29.00 \text{ Pa¬∑m}^6\text{¬∑K}^{0.5}/\text{mol}^2$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Substitute into RK equation:**

$$P = \frac{RT}{V_m - b} - \frac{a}{T^{0.5} V_m (V_m + b)}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute $V_m$ iteratively (since $V_m = ZRT/P$):**

Guess $V_m^{(0)} = ZRT/P = 0.9 \times 8.314 \times 500 / 2 \times 10^6 = 1.871 \times 10^{-3}$ m¬≥/mol.

Plug into RK:

$$P_{\text{calc}} = \frac{8.314 \times 500}{1.871 \times 10^{-3} - 8.07 \times 10^{-5}} - \frac{29.0}{22.36 \times 1.871 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.952 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$P_{\text{calc}} = 2.231 \times 10^6 - \frac{29.0}{8.165 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.231 \times 10^6 - 0.355 \times 10^6 = 1.876 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $P_{\text{calc}} < P_{\text{given}}$, increase $V_m$. Iterate:

$$V_m = \frac{ZRT}{P} \rightarrow \text{converges to } V_m \approx 1.965 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Final compressibility factor:**

$$Z_{\text{RK}} = \frac{PV_m}{RT} = \frac{2 \times 10^6 \times 1.965 \times 10^{-3}}{8.314 \times 500} = \frac{3930}{4157} = 0.945$$

**Comparison Table:**

| EOS | $Z$ Value | % Deviation from RK |
|-----|-----------|---------------------|
| Ideal Gas | 1.000 | +5.82% |
| van der Waals | 0.905 | -4.23% |
| Redlich-Kwong | 0.945 | Reference (0%) |

**Conclusion:** At $T_r = 1.176$, $P_r = 0.527$ (supercritical n-butane), attractive forces slightly dominate. The RK EOS, with its empirical temperature correction, gives the most physically consistent result and is the preferred choice for this hydrocarbon system.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students commit these common errors:
> 1. **Mixing units** ‚ÄĒ Using $P_c$ in bar but $R$ in SI; ALWAYS convert to **Pa** consistently when using $R = 8.314$ J/mol¬∑K.
> 2. **Dropping the $ab$ term silently in the cubic** ‚ÄĒ State the approximation explicitly if you truncate.
> 3. **Picking the wrong root** ‚ÄĒ Cubic EOS yield up to 3 real roots; pick the **largest positive real root** for the vapour phase and **smallest positive real root** for the liquid phase.
> 4. **Forgetting the alpha function** in PR EOS ‚ÄĒ without it, you get only classical PR, which fails for high-acentric-factor substances.
> 5. **Confusing virial forms** ‚ÄĒ $B$ (density form) $\neq$ $B'$ (pressure form); use **Mayer's relations** to convert: $B' = B/RT$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE ‚ÄĒ Module 2: Real-Gas EOS**

- **Definition:** An EOS is a P-V-T relation for pure fluids or mixtures. Real-gas EOS are *cubic* (vdW, RK, PR) or *series* (virial) extensions to the ideal gas law.

- **Master Diagnostic:** Always begin a real-gas problem by computing the **compressibility factor** $Z = PV_m/RT$ from the ideal gas and the **reduced properties** $T_r = T/T_c$, $P_r = P/P_c$. If $Z \approx 1$, the ideal gas is sufficient.

- **vdW EOS:** $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V_m^2}\right)(V_m - b) = RT$, with $a = \dfrac{27R^2T_c^2}{64P_c}$ and $b = \dfrac{RT_c}{8P_c}$. Predicts $Z_c = 3/8 = 0.375$.

- **RK EOS:** $P = \dfrac{RT}{V_m - b} - \dfrac{a}{T^{0.5}V_m(V_m+b)}$, with $a = 0.42748\dfrac{R^2T_c^{2.5}}{P_c}$ and $b = 0.08664\dfrac{RT_c}{P_c}$. Predicts $Z_c = 1/3 = 0.333$.

- **PR EOS:** $P = \dfrac{RT}{V_m-b} - \dfrac{a\alpha}{V_m(V_m+b)+b(V_m-b)}$, with $a = 0.45724\dfrac{R^2T_c^2}{P_c}$, $b = 0.07780\dfrac{RT_c}{P_c}$, $\alpha = (1+\kappa(1-\sqrt{T_r}))^2$, $\kappa = 0.37464 + 1.54226\omega - 0.26992\omega^2$. Predicts $Z_c = 0.307$.

- **Virial EOS (Density form):** $Z = 1 + B\rho + C\rho^2 + D\rho^3 + \cdots$. **Virial EOS (Pressure form):** $Z = 1 + B'P + C'P^2 + D'P^3 + \cdots$. **Mayer's relation:** $B' = B/RT$, $C' = (C-B^2)/(RT)^2$.

- **Physical Meanings:** `a` = intermolecular attraction; `b` = excluded volume; `B(T)` = pair interactions; `C(T)` = triplet interactions; $\alpha(T)$ = temperature damping of attractions via the acentric factor $\omega$.

- **Cubic Solver Heuristic:** All three cubic EOS yield up to 3 real roots in Z. Apply **Maxwell's equal-area rule** or the **physical-root selection rule** (largest for vapour, smallest for liquid).

- **Selection Heuristic:** **vdW** for pedagogy; **RK** for non-polar gases at moderate P; **PR** for hydrocarbons and VLE; **Virial** for low-density gases and statistical-mechanical rigor.

- **Critical Point:** All cubic EOS have $V_{m,c} = 3b$ by design; the only difference is the *predicted* $Z_c$ (vdW: 0.375, RK: 0.333, PR: 0.307), with PR being closest to experimental reality.

- **Limitation of Cubic EOS:** All three fail in the **near-critical region** (where fluctuations dominate) and require **mixing rules** for multi-component systems (e.g., van der Waals one-fluid mixing rules with binary interaction parameters $k_{ij}$).

- **Theorist's Choice:** When the question says "derive from first principles" or "show statistical mechanics basis", always use the **virial EOS + Mayer cluster expansion** ‚ÄĒ it is the *only* theoretically rigorous path.

- **Engineering Reality Check:** Software like **Aspen Plus**, **HYSYS**, **PROMAX** use PR or SRK as default property packages ‚ÄĒ for good reason, as established by 50+ years of industrial validation.

- **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** If the question asks to "choose the most suitable EOS and justify", structure your answer as: (1) state the system conditions, (2) compute $T_r$ and $P_r$, (3) classify the fluid (polar/non-polar, light/heavy hydrocarbon), (4) name the EOS, (5) state the corresponding constants and physical interpretation, (6) compute or comment on the result. This 6-step structure fetches full marks consistently.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Corresponding States and Generalized Compressibility Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principle of Corresponding States and Generalized Compressibility Chart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle of Corresponding States (PCS):** *The Principle of Corresponding States states that all fluids, when expressed in terms of their reduced properties (i.e., properties normalized with respect to their critical constants), exhibit identical behavior. In other words, two substances at the same reduced temperature (T_r) and reduced pressure (P_r) will have the same reduced volume (V_r), reduced compressibility factor (Z_c), and other dimensionless thermodynamic properties.*

The **reduced properties** are dimensionless quantities defined as:

$$
T_r = \frac{T}{T_c}, \qquad P_r = \frac{P}{P_c}, \qquad V_r = \frac{V}{V_c}, \qquad Z_c = \frac{Z}{Z_c}
$$

where $T_c$, $P_c$, and $V_c$ are the **critical temperature**, **critical pressure**, and **critical molar volume** of the substance, respectively.

### 1.2 The Compressibility Factor (Z)

The **compressibility factor** $Z$ quantifies the deviation of a real gas from ideal gas behavior:

$$
Z = \frac{PV}{nRT} = \frac{PV_m}{RT}
$$

- For an **ideal gas**, $Z = 1$ at all conditions.
- For a **real gas**, $Z$ deviates from unity and is a function of both $T$ and $P$.

### 1.3 Generalized Compressibility Chart

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalized Compressibility Chart:** Also called the *Standing-Katz Chart* or *Nelson-Obert Chart*, it is a universal plot of the compressibility factor $Z$ as a function of reduced pressure $P_r$ for various constant values of reduced temperature $T_r$. It applies to nearly all pure gases (with greater accuracy for non-polar or slightly polar gases).

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a huge international family reunion where everyone speaks a different native language (hydrogen, oxygen, methane, nitrogen, etc.). Each family member is "unique." Now, suppose we design a **universal translator** ‚ÄĒ a common set of measurements that normalizes each person's height and weight against their family's average.

- **Family average** = critical constants ($T_c$, $P_c$, $V_c$).
- **Universal translator** = reduced properties ($T_r$, $P_r$, $V_r$).
- **Translated behavior** = identical $Z$ values for all family members at the same reduced state.

This is essentially the Principle of Corresponding States: once we express conditions relative to a substance's *own* critical point, all substances "speak the same thermodynamic language."

### 1.5 Limitations

> [!WARNING]
> The classical two-parameter PCS (based only on $T_r$ and $P_r$) **fails** for polar and hydrogen-bonding substances (e.g., water, ammonia, alcohols). To overcome this, Pitzer introduced the **acentric factor** $\omega$ as a third parameter ‚ÄĒ giving rise to the **Three-Parameter Principle of Corresponding States**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The Principle of Corresponding States arises from the observation that the **van der Waals equation of state**, when expressed in reduced form, becomes a **universal equation** independent of the substance:

$$
\left(P_r + \frac{3}{V_r^2}\right)\left(V_r - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{8}{3}T_r
$$

This universality implies that if two fluids are at the same $T_r$ and $P_r$, they must have the same $V_r$ ‚ÄĒ meaning their $P-V-T$ behavior is identical.

### 2.2 Two-Parameter vs. Three-Parameter PCS

| Feature | Two-Parameter PCS | Three-Parameter PCS (Pitzer) |
|---|---|---|
| Parameters | $T_r$, $P_r$ | $T_r$, $P_r$, $\omega$ |
| Basis | van der Waals-type EOS | Empirical correlation |
| Accuracy for non-polar gases | Excellent | Excellent |
| Accuracy for polar gases | Poor | Good (especially Lee-Kesler) |
| Chart type | Standing-Katz | Lee-Kesler tables/charts |
| Year introduced | ~1880s | 1955 (Pitzer) |

### 2.3 The Acentric Factor ($\omega$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acentric Factor Definition:** Pitzer defined $\omega$ as a measure of the *acentricity* (deviation of a molecule from spherical symmetry) of a fluid. Mathematically:
> $$\omega \equiv -1.0 - \log_{10}\left(P_r^{\text{sat}}\right)_{T_r = 0.7}$$
> where $(P_r^{\text{sat}})_{T_r = 0.7}$ is the **reduced vapor pressure** evaluated at the **reduced temperature of 0.7**.

**Interpretation of $\omega$:**
- $\omega = 0$ ‚Üí Spherical molecules (e.g., **Argon**, **Krypton**, **Methane** approx.)
- $\omega > 0$ ‚Üí Non-spherical / polar molecules
- $\omega$ increases with molecular complexity

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z = \dfrac{PV_m}{RT}$ | Compressibility factor (dimensionless) |
| 2 | $T_r = \dfrac{T}{T_c}$ | Reduced temperature (K / K) |
| 3 | $P_r = \dfrac{P}{P_c}$ | Reduced pressure (Pa / Pa) |
| 4 | $V_r = \dfrac{V_m}{V_{mc}}$ | Reduced molar volume |
| 5 | $Z_c = \dfrac{P_c V_{mc}}{R T_c}$ | Critical compressibility factor |
| 6 | $\omega = -1 - \log_{10}(P_r^{\text{sat}}) \big\vert_{T_r = 0.7}$ | Acentric factor definition |
| 7 | $Z = Z^{(0)}(T_r, P_r) + \omega \cdot Z^{(1)}(T_r, P_r)$ | Pitzer correlation |
| 8 | $V_r = \dfrac{V_m}{RT_c / P_c}$ | Alternative reduced volume (using $V_c \approx RT_c/P_c$) |
| 9 | $V_{mc} = \dfrac{Z_c R T_c}{P_c}$ | Critical molar volume from $Z_c$ |
| 10 | $\dfrac{PV_m}{RT_c} = P_r \cdot Z_r$ | Universal reduced form (Lee-Kesler) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard value:** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol¬∑K)}$ = **8.314 J/(mol¬∑K)**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The PCS and Generalized Compressibility Chart are used in:
- **LNG/CNG Industry** ‚ÄĒ Storage and transportation design of natural gas.
- **Refrigeration & Air-Conditioning** ‚ÄĒ Property estimation of refrigerants (R-134a, R-410A, ammonia).
- **Petroleum Refining** ‚ÄĒ PVT behavior of hydrocarbon mixtures.
- **Chemical Reactor Design** ‚ÄĒ Real-gas corrections for high-pressure reaction equilibria.
- **Compressor & Turbine Sizing** ‚ÄĒ Volumetric efficiency corrections in turbo-machinery.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reduced Form of the van der Waals Equation

**Starting point:** The van der Waals equation of state for one mole:

$$
\left(P + \frac{a}{V_m^2}\right)\left(V_m - b\right) = RT
$$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify the critical-point conditions.** At the critical point, the isotherm has an inflection point:
$$
\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V_m}\right)_{T_c} = 0, \qquad \left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V_m^2}\right)_{T_c} = 0
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Express $P$ explicitly from vdW:**
$$
P = \frac{RT}{V_m - b} - \frac{a}{V_m^2}
$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute first derivative with respect to $V_m$:**
$$
\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V_m}\right)_T = -\frac{RT}{(V_m - b)^2} + \frac{2a}{V_m^3}
$$

Setting this to zero at the critical point:
$$
\frac{RT_c}{(V_{mc} - b)^2} = \frac{2a}{V_{mc}^3} \tag{i}
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Compute second derivative with respect to $V_m$:**
$$
\left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V_m^2}\right)_T = \frac{2RT}{(V_m - b)^3} - \frac{6a}{V_m^4}
$$

Setting this to zero at the critical point:
$$
\frac{2RT_c}{(V_{mc} - b)^3} = \frac{6a}{V_{mc}^4} \tag{ii}
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Divide equation (i) by (ii) to eliminate $R T_c$ and $a$:**
$$
\frac{V_{mc} - b}{1} = \frac{2 V_{mc}}{3}
$$

Solving:
$$
3 V_{mc} - 3b = 2 V_{mc} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V_{mc} = 3b
$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Substitute back into (i) to find $T_c$ and $P_c$:**
$$
\frac{R T_c}{(3b - b)^2} = \frac{2a}{(3b)^3} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{R T_c}{4b^2} = \frac{2a}{27 b^3}
$$

$$
T_c = \frac{8a}{27 R b}
$$

From the original vdW equation at the critical point:
$$
P_c = \frac{R T_c}{V_{mc} - b} - \frac{a}{V_{mc}^2} = \frac{R T_c}{2b} - \frac{a}{9 b^2}
$$

Substituting $T_c$:
$$
P_c = \frac{R}{2b} \cdot \frac{8a}{27 R b} - \frac{a}{9 b^2} = \frac{a}{27 b^2}
$$

**Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Define reduced variables** $T_r = T/T_c$, $P_r = P/P_c$, $V_r = V_m/V_{mc}$:

Since $V_{mc} = 3b$, we have $b = V_{mc}/3$. Since $a = 27 P_c b^2 = 27 P_c (V_{mc}/3)^2 = 3 P_c V_{mc}^2$.

Substituting into vdW:
$$
\left(P + \frac{3 P_c V_{mc}^2}{V_m^2}\right)\left(V_m - \frac{V_{mc}}{3}\right) = R T
$$

Divide through by $P_c V_{mc}$:
$$
\left(\frac{P}{P_c} + 3 \left(\frac{V_{mc}}{V_m}\right)^2\right)\left(\frac{V_m}{V_{mc}} - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{R T}{P_c V_{mc}}
$$

Using $V_c \approx RT_c / P_c$ (ideal-gas-like at critical point for vdW):

$$
\left(P_r + \frac{3}{V_r^2}\right)\left(V_r - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{8}{3} T_r
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** This is a **universal equation** ‚ÄĒ independent of the substance! It depends only on reduced quantities. Hence, any gas at the same $T_r$ and $P_r$ has the same $V_r$. This is the **Principle of Corresponding States**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Pitzer's Acentric Factor

Starting from the experimental fact that for **simple fluids** (Ar, Kr, Xe, and to a good approximation CH‚āĄ) the reduced vapor pressure is well represented by:
$$
\log_{10}(P_r^{\text{sat}}) = a - \frac{b}{T_r}
$$

For simple fluids, the reduced vapor pressure at $T_r = 0.7$ is observed to be approximately **0.1**, giving:
$$
\log_{10}(0.1) = a - \frac{b}{0.7}
$$

**Defining the acentric factor** Pitzer chose a reference that automatically yields $\omega = 0$ for simple fluids:
$$
\omega \equiv -1.0 - \log_{10}\left(P_r^{\text{sat}}\right)_{T_r = 0.7}
$$

**Verification for simple fluids:**
$$
\omega_{\text{simple}} = -1.0 - \log_{10}(0.1) = -1.0 - (-1.0) = 0
$$

This elegant normalization makes $\omega$ a quantitative measure of molecular complexity.

### 3.3 Lee-Kesler Correlations for $Z$

The Lee-Kesler tables provide $Z^{(0)}$ (simple fluid, $\omega = 0$) and $Z^{(1)}$ (reference fluid, $\omega \approx 0.3978$ for n-octane). The compressibility factor is:

$$
Z = Z^{(0)} + \frac{\omega}{\omega^{(1)}}\left[Z^{(1)} - Z^{(0)}\right]
$$

For n-octane, $\omega^{(1)} \approx 0.3978$, so:

$$
Z = Z^{(0)} + \omega \cdot \left[\frac{Z^{(1)} - Z^{(0)}}{0.3978}\right]
$$

Or, equivalently, the more practical form:

$$
Z = Z^{(0)} + \omega \cdot Z^{(1)}_{\text{norm}}
$$

where $Z^{(1)}_{\text{norm}} = (Z^{(1)} - Z^{(0)})/0.3978$ is the **normalized departure function**.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Estimate the molar volume of methane at $T = 200 \; \text{K}$ and $P = 100 \; \text{bar}$.

Given: $T_c = 190.6 \; \text{K}$, $P_c = 46.0 \; \text{bar}$ for methane.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Calculate reduced properties:**
$$
T_r = \frac{T}{T_c} = \frac{200}{190.6} = 1.0493
$$

$$
P_r = \frac{P}{P_c} = \frac{100}{46.0} = 2.174
$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Read $Z$ from the Generalized Compressibility Chart** at $T_r = 1.05$, $P_r = 2.17$:

From the chart, $Z \approx 0.41$.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Calculate molar volume:**
$$
V_m = \frac{Z R T}{P} = \frac{(0.41)(8.314 \;\text{J/mol¬∑K})(200 \;\text{K})}{100 \times 10^5 \;\text{Pa}}
$$

$$
V_m = \frac{681.748}{1 \times 10^7} = 6.82 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m}^3/\text{mol}
$$

$$
V_m = 68.2 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{mol}
$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Compare with ideal gas:**
$$
V_{m,\text{ideal}} = \frac{RT}{P} = \frac{(8.314)(200)}{1 \times 10^7} = 1.66 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^3/\text{mol} = 166.3 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{mol}
$$

The real gas occupies only **~41%** of the ideal-gas volume at this high-pressure, low-temperature state ‚ÄĒ clearly demonstrating the importance of the compressibility correction.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Workflow of PCS-Based Property Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: System State Given T and P] --> B[Identify Substance]
    B --> C[Look up Critical Constants Tc and Pc]
    C --> D[Calculate Reduced Properties]
    D --> D1[Tr = T divided by Tc]
    D --> D2[Pr = P divided by Pc]
    D1 --> E{Substance Type}
    D2 --> E
    E -->|Simple or Non-polar| F[Two-Parameter PCS]
    E -->|Polar or Hydrogen-bonding| G[Three-Parameter PCS with omega]
    F --> H[Read Z from Generalized Compressibility Chart]
    G --> I[Read Z0 and Z1 from Lee-Kesler Tables]
    I --> J[Compute Z using Pitzer Correlation]
    H --> K[Compute Molar Volume Vm = ZRT over P]
    J --> K
    K --> L[End: Final Thermodynamic Properties]
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Three-Parameter Corresponding States Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Parameters]
        T[Temperature T]
        P[Pressure P]
        SUB[Substance Identification]
    end

    subgraph CRITICAL[Critical Properties Database]
        TC[Tc]
        PC[Pc]
        VMC[Vmc]
        ZC[Zc]
        OMEGA[omega]
    end

    subgraph REDUCED[Reduced Property Calculation]
        TR[Tr equals T over Tc]
        PR[Pr equals P over Pc]
    end

    subgraph MODELS[Correlation Models]
        VDW[van der Waals Reduced Form]
        PCS2[Two-Parameter PCS Chart]
        LEE[Lee-Kesler Tables]
        PITZ[Pitzer Correlation Z equals Z0 plus omega times Z1]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Final Outputs]
        ZFIN[Compressibility Factor Z]
        VMFIN[Molar Volume Vm]
        HFUG[Residual Enthalpy H minus H ideal]
        SFUG[Residual Entropy S minus S ideal]
        FIFUG[Fugacity Coefficient phi]
    end

    T --> TR
    P --> PR
    SUB --> TC
    SUB --> PC
    SUB --> OMEGA
    TR --> PCS2
    TR --> LEE
    PR --> PCS2
    PR --> LEE
    OMEGA --> PITZ
    PCS2 --> ZFIN
    LEE --> PITZ
    PITZ --> ZFIN
    ZFIN --> VMFIN
    ZFIN --> HFUG
    ZFIN --> SFUG
    ZFIN --> FIFUG
```

### 4.3 Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Chart/Correlation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Gas is Polar or Associating?}
    Q1 -->|No Simple Non-polar| R1[Use Standing-Katz Chart Two-Parameter PCS]
    Q1 -->|Yes Polar H-bonding| Q2{Accuracy Required?}
    Q2 -->|Low to Moderate| R2[Use Standing-Katz with average Ac factor]
    Q2 -->|High Engineering| R3[Use Lee-Kesler Tables Three-Parameter PCS]
    Q3{Near Critical Region?}
    Q3 -->|Yes Tr near 1 Pr near 1| R4[Use Pitzer-Curl or Lee-Kesler Extrapolation]
    Q3 -->|No Far from critical| R5[Any PCS method adequate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Note:** These diagrams illustrate the *functional architecture* of property estimation workflows using the PCS framework, mapping input parameters through reduced-property calculations to final outputs.

---
