<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]**
*State the Principle of Corresponding States. Define the terms reduced temperature, reduced pressure, and acentric factor.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Principle of Corresponding States (PCS)** states that all fluids, when expressed in terms of their **reduced properties**, exhibit approximately the same P-V-T behavior. That is, two substances at equal reduced temperature and reduced pressure will have equal reduced molar volume and equal compressibility factor.

**Reduced Temperature:**
$$
T_r = \frac{T}{T_c}
$$

**Reduced Pressure:**
$$
P_r = \frac{P}{P_c}
$$

**Acentric Factor ($\omega$):** Introduced by Pitzer, it is defined as:
$$
\omega = -1 - \log_{10}\left(P_r^{\text{sat}}\right)_{T_r = 0.7}
$$

It measures the deviation of molecular shape from spherical symmetry; $\omega = 0$ for simple fluids (Ar, Kr, CH‚āĄ).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď June 2024]**
*What is a generalized compressibility chart? Mention its practical limitations.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Generalized Compressibility Chart** is a universal plot of the compressibility factor $Z$ as a function of reduced pressure $P_r$ for various constant values of reduced temperature $T_r$. It is also called the **Standing-Katz chart** (or Nelson-Obert chart).

**Practical Limitations:**
1. Inaccurate for **strongly polar** and **hydrogen-bonding** substances (e.g., water, alcohols, ammonia) under the two-parameter form.
2. **Inaccurate near the critical region** where $Z$ is highly sensitive to small property changes.
3. Requires accurate values of $T_c$ and $P_c$, which may be unavailable for novel compounds.
4. The chart cannot be applied to **mixtures** without applying mixing rules for pseudo-critical properties.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** ‚ÄĒ [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the reduced form of the van der Waals equation of state and show that it leads to the Principle of Corresponding States. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For nitrogen at $T = 150 \; \text{K}$ and $P = 30 \; \text{bar}$, determine the molar volume using the generalized compressibility chart. Given: $T_c = 126.2 \; \text{K}$, $P_c = 33.9 \; \text{bar}$. Compare with the ideal gas value. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the van der Waals equation:
$$
\left(P + \frac{a}{V_m^2}\right)\left(V_m - b\right) = RT
$$

Apply critical-point conditions:
$$
\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V_m}\right)_{T_c} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V_m^2}\right)_{T_c} = 0
$$

Solving these two simultaneous equations yields:
$$
V_{mc} = 3b, \quad T_c = \frac{8a}{27 R b}, \quad P_c = \frac{a}{27 b^2}
$$

Therefore:
$$
b = \frac{V_{mc}}{3}, \quad a = 3 P_c V_{mc}^2
$$

Substituting these into vdW and introducing reduced variables $T_r = T/T_c$, $P_r = P/P_c$, $V_r = V_m/V_{mc}$:

$$
\left(P_r + \frac{3}{V_r^2}\right)\left(V_r - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{8}{3} T_r
$$

**Conclusion:** This equation is **universal** ‚ÄĒ independent of $a$, $b$, or the identity of the gas. Therefore, any two substances at the same $T_r$ and $P_r$ must possess the same $V_r$, and hence the same $Z$. This is the **Principle of Corresponding States**. ‚úď (7 marks)

---

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Calculate reduced properties.
$$
T_r = \frac{150}{126.2} = 1.189
$$

$$
P_r = \frac{30}{33.9} = 0.885
$$

**Step 2:** From the Generalized Compressibility Chart at $T_r = 1.19$ and $P_r = 0.88$:
$$
Z \approx 0.83
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate real-gas molar volume:
$$
V_{m,\text{real}} = \frac{ZRT}{P} = \frac{(0.83)(8.314 \times 10^{-2} \;\text{L¬∑bar/mol¬∑K})(150 \;\text{K})}{30 \;\text{bar}}
$$

$$
V_{m,\text{real}} = \frac{(0.83)(12.471)}{30} = \frac{10.351}{30} = 0.345 \;\text{L/mol} = 345 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{mol}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate ideal-gas molar volume:
$$
V_{m,\text{ideal}} = \frac{RT}{P} = \frac{(0.08314)(150)}{30} = 0.416 \;\text{L/mol} = 416 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{mol}
$$

**Step 5:** Percentage deviation:
$$
\% \text{Deviation} = \frac{V_{m,\text{real}} - V_{m,\text{ideal}}}{V_{m,\text{ideal}}} \times 100 = \frac{0.345 - 0.416}{0.416} \times 100 \approx -17.1\%
$$

**Final Answer:** $V_{m,\text{real}} = 345 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$, which is **17.1% lower** than the ideal gas value. The negative deviation indicates attractive intermolecular forces dominate at this state. ‚úď (7 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ‚ÄĒ [KTU University Exam ‚Äď June 2023]

**(a)** Explain Pitzer's acentric factor. How does the three-parameter corresponding states principle improve upon the two-parameter form? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gas obeys Pitzer's correlation. At $T_r = 1.30$ and $P_r = 0.50$, the simple-fluid compressibility factor is $Z^{(0)} = 0.86$ and the departure is $Z^{(1)} = -0.08$. Calculate the compressibility factor for:
   (i) Argon ($\omega = -0.004$)
   (ii) Carbon dioxide ($\omega = 0.225$)
   (iii) n-Decane ($\omega = 0.490$)

Comment on the deviation from ideal gas behavior. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Pitzer's Acentric Factor (7 marks):**

Pitzer introduced the **acentric factor** $\omega$ as a third parameter to extend the Principle of Corresponding States to real (non-spherical, polar, hydrogen-bonding) molecules. It is defined as:
$$
\omega = -1 - \log_{10}(P_r^{\text{sat}})_{T_r = 0.7}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- For simple fluids (Ar, Kr, Xe, CH‚āĄ), the reduced vapor pressure at $T_r = 0.7$ is approximately $0.1$, yielding $\omega = 0$.
- Larger $\omega$ indicates greater **acentricity** (departure from spherical molecular shape).
- $\omega$ correlates strongly with molecular properties such as polarity, shape, and hydrogen-bonding tendency.

**Improvements over Two-Parameter PCS:**
1. **Two-parameter PCS** fails for polar and hydrogen-bonding substances because it assumes all molecules are spherical.
2. **Three-parameter PCS (Pitzer)** introduces $\omega$ to capture molecular non-sphericity.
3. The compressibility factor becomes $Z = Z^{(0)} + \omega \cdot Z^{(1)}$, where $Z^{(0)}$ and $Z^{(1)}$ are tabulated functions of $T_r$ and $P_r$.
4. This **dramatically improves** the accuracy of property predictions for polar substances like water, ammonia, and refrigerants.
5. The **Lee-Kesler extension** further refines this approach and is considered the gold standard for engineering calculations.

---

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Using Pitzer's correlation:
$$
Z = Z^{(0)} + \omega \cdot Z^{(1)}
$$

Given: $Z^{(0)} = 0.86$, $Z^{(1)} = -0.08$.

**(i) Argon ($\omega = -0.004$):**
$$
Z_{\text{Ar}} = 0.86 + (-0.004)(-0.08) = 0.86 + 0.00032 \approx 0.860
$$

**(ii) Carbon dioxide ($\omega = 0.225$):**
$$
Z_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.86 + (0.225)(-0.08) = 0.86 - 0.018 = 0.842
$$

**(iii) n-Decane ($\omega = 0.490$):**
$$
Z_{\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_{22}} = 0.86 + (0.490)(-0.08) = 0.86 - 0.0392 = 0.8208
$$

**Comments:**
- All three gases show $Z < 1$, indicating that **attractive forces dominate** at these reduced conditions ($T_r = 1.30$, $P_r = 0.50$).
- Argon, with $\omega \approx 0$, exhibits $Z$ very close to the simple-fluid value.
- As $\omega$ increases (Ar ‚Üí CO‚āā ‚Üí n-Decane), $Z$ decreases further from 1. This is because **non-spherical and polar molecules** experience stronger attractive intermolecular forces at moderate pressures, leading to greater deviations from ideality.
- The deviation from ideal gas ($Z = 1$) ranges from 14% (Argon) to 18% (n-Decane). ‚úď (7 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Units Confusion (‚Čą2 marks lost):** Always express $R$ in consistent units with $P$ and $V$. For $P$ in Pa and $V$ in m¬≥, use $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K). For $P$ in bar and $V$ in L, use $R = 0.08314$ L¬∑bar/(mol¬∑K).
>
> 2. **Incorrect Definition of $\omega$ (‚Čą2 marks lost):** Many students write "$\omega$ measures polarity." **Correction:** $\omega$ measures **acentricity** (departure from spherical shape), not polarity directly. Polarity affects $\omega$, but the definition is geometric in origin.
>
> 3. **Chart Reading Errors (‚Čą1‚Äď2 marks lost):** When reading from the compressibility chart, always interpolate carefully between $T_r$ isotherms. Drawing a small sketch showing your reading point is good practice and earns partial credit.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Compare with Ideal Gas (‚Čą1 mark lost):** KTU examiners **always expect** a comparison/percentage deviation in numerical problems. Never end the answer with just a single number.
>
> 5. **Mixing Critical and Reduced Properties (‚Čą2 marks lost):** Clearly distinguish $T$ from $T_r$, $P$ from $P_r$. Use subscripts consistently.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Universal vdW Derivation Steps:** The critical-point derivative conditions $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V}\right)_{T_c} = 0$ and $\left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V^2}\right)_{T_c} = 0$ must be **explicitly shown**. Skipping them loses 2‚Äď3 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Preparation:**

- [ ] **PCS** ‚ÄĒ Universal behavior in reduced coordinates: same $T_r$ and $P_r$ ‚Üí same $Z$ and $V_r$.
- [ ] **Reduced Properties** ‚ÄĒ $T_r = T/T_c$, $P_r = P/P_c$, $V_r = V_m/V_{mc}$.
- [ ] **Compressibility Factor** ‚ÄĒ $Z = PV_m/(RT)$; $Z = 1$ for ideal gas.
- [ ] **vdW Reduced Form** ‚ÄĒ $\left(P_r + \dfrac{3}{V_r^2}\right)\left(V_r - \dfrac{1}{3}\right) = \dfrac{8}{3} T_r$ ‚ÄĒ Universal, substance-independent.
- [ ] **Critical Constants from vdW** ‚ÄĒ $V_{mc} = 3b$, $T_c = 8a/(27Rb)$, $P_c = a/(27b^2)$.
- [ ] **Acentric Factor** ‚ÄĒ $\omega = -1 - \log_{10}(P_r^{\text{sat}})\big\vert_{T_r=0.7}$; $\omega = 0$ for simple fluids.
- [ ] **Pitzer Correlation** ‚ÄĒ $Z = Z^{(0)}(T_r, P_r) + \omega \cdot Z^{(1)}(T_r, P_r)$.
- [ ] **Lee-Kesler** ‚ÄĒ Refined three-parameter correlation; uses n-octane as reference.
- [ ] **Chart Types** ‚ÄĒ Standing-Katz (two-parameter), Lee-Kesler (three-parameter).
- [ ] **Limitation** ‚ÄĒ Two-parameter PCS fails for polar substances; use $\omega$ corrections.
- [ ] **Numerical Workflow** ‚ÄĒ (1) Find $T_c$, $P_c$; (2) Compute $T_r$, $P_r$; (3) Read $Z$ from chart/table; (4) Compute $V_m = ZRT/P$; (5) Compare with ideal gas.
- [ ] **Engineering Apps** ‚ÄĒ LNG storage, refrigeration, refinery PVT, compressor design.
- [ ] **Remember:** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol¬∑K)} = 0.08314 \; \text{L¬∑bar/(mol¬∑K)}$.
- [ ] **Examiner Tip:** Always show units, always compare with ideal gas, always sketch reading points on charts.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamental Property Relations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fundamental Property Relations # ūüďė SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fundamental Property Relations** are a set of exact differential equations that combine the **First Law** and **Second Law** of thermodynamics to express the changes in thermodynamic potentials ($U$, $H$, $A$, $G$) in terms of measurable state variables ($P$, $V$, $T$, $S$). They form the **mathematical backbone** from which all other thermodynamic property relations, including the celebrated **Maxwell's relations**, are derived. The four fundamental relations are compactly written as: $$ dU = TdS - PdV $$ $$ dH = TdS + VdP $$ $$ dA = -SdT - PdV $$ $$ dG = -SdT + VdP $$ Where: - $U$ = Internal Energy ($\text{kJ}$) - $H$ = Enthalpy ($\text{kJ}$) - $A$ = Helmholtz Free Energy ($\text{kJ}$) - $G$ = Gibbs Free Energy ($\text{kJ}$) - $S$ = Entropy ($\text{kJ/K}$) - $T$ = Absolute Temperature ($\text{K}$) - $P$ = Pressure ($\text{kPa}$) - $V$ = Volume ($\text{m}^3$) --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Rosetta Stone" of Thermodynamics ūüí° > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Four Pillars of a Cathedral** > Imagine a vast, intricate cathedral of thermodynamics. You cannot directly *see* the hidden rooms (entropy $S$, internal energy $U$), but you can feel the pillars (temperature $T$, pressure $P$, volume $V$) that hold them up. The **Fundamental Property Relations** are the **architectural blueprints** that tell you exactly how the hidden rooms connect to the visible pillars. Without these blueprints, you could never navigate from one room to another. **Maxwell's Relations** are the *magic elevators* ‚ÄĒ they let you take a hidden, unmeasurable quantity (like how entropy changes with pressure) and translate it into a measurable one (like how volume changes with temperature). **Why does this matter to a Chemical Engineer?** In real plant operations, you can stick a thermometer in a reactor (measuring $T$) or read a pressure gauge (measuring $P$), but you can **never** directly measure entropy. Fundamental property relations give you the *mathematical machinery* to calculate these hidden quantities from the data you *can* measure. --- ## 1.3 Syllabus Highlights > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Learning Outcomes (CO Mapping):** > This topic directly maps to **CO2** of PEPOT633: *Apply the laws of thermodynamics to derive property relations for pure substances and ideal gas mixtures.* The expected cognitive level per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy is **Apply / Analyze (Level 3-4)**. # ūüďė SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Logic The fundamental property relations rest on **three foundational pillars**: ### Pillar 1: Exact Differentials A state function $Z(x,y)$ has an exact differential: $$ dZ = Mdx + Ndy \quad \text{where} \quad M = \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial x}\right)_y \quad \text{and} \quad N = \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial y}\right)_x $$ For $dZ$ to be exact, the **Euler reciprocity relation** must hold: $$ \left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** Since $U$, $H$, $A$, and $G$ are all state functions, their differentials are exact. This exactness is what *generates* Maxwell's relations. ### Pillar 2: The Bridge Equations From the differentials above, the natural (measurable) variables of each potential are: | Potential | Natural Variables | Key Partial Derivatives | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | $U$ | $(S, V)$ | $T = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial S}\right)_V$, $\quad P = -\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_S$ | | $H$ | $(S, P)$ | $T = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial S}\right)_P$, $\quad V = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial P}\right)_S$ | | $A$ | $(T, V)$ | $S = -\left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_V$, $\quad P = -\left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial V}\right)_T$ | | $G$ | $(T, P)$ | $S = -\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P$, $\quad V = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T$ | ### Pillar 3: Maxwell's Relations (The "Bridges") Applying Euler reciprocity to each potential gives: $$ \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V $$ $$ \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P $$ $$ \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V $$ $$ \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world utility:** Maxwell's relations are routinely used in **refrigeration cycle design**, **distillation column pressure profiling**, and **Joule-Thomson coefficient calculations** for throttling valves. They transform problems with entropy derivatives (impossible to measure) into derivatives of $P$, $V$, and $T$ (routinely measured in industry). --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet ūüďč | # | Equation | Name | Application | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | $dU = TdS - PdV$ | Fundamental relation (U) | Closed, reversible systems | | 2 | $dH = TdS + VdP$ | Fundamental relation (H) | Open flow systems | | 3 | $dA = -SdT - PdV$ | Helmholtz relation | Constant $T$, $V$ processes | | 4 | $dG = -SdT + VdP$ | Gibbs relation | Constant $T$, $P$ processes | | 5 | $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$ | Maxwell-1 | From $U(S,V)$ | | 6 | $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P$ | Maxwell-2 | From $H(S,P)$ | | 7 | $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$ | Maxwell-3 | From $A(T,V)$ | | 8 | $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | Maxwell-4 | From $G(T,P)$ | | 9 | $C_p - C_v = -T\,\frac{\left(\partial V / \partial T\right)_P^{\,2}}{\left(\partial V / \partial P\right)_T}$ | Heat capacity relation | Relates $C_p$ and $C_v$ | | 10 | $\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{trans}}{T\,\Delta V}$ | Clapeyron Equation | Phase equilibria | | 11 | $\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Clausius-Clapeyron Eq. | Vapor pressure estimation | | 12 | $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_P \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T = -1$ | Cyclic Rule | Triple product identity | # ūüďė SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Four Fundamental Property Relations ### Derivation A: $dU = TdS - PdV$ (Internal Energy) > [!NOTE] > **Starting Point:** Combined statement of the First and Second Laws for a closed, reversible system. **Step 1.** Write the First Law for a closed system: $$ dU = \delta Q + \delta W $$ **Step 2.** Substitute the reversible heat and work expressions ($\delta Q_{rev} = TdS$ and $\delta W_{rev} = -PdV$): $$ dU = TdS + (-PdV) $$ **Step 3.** Final result: $$ \boxed{\,dU = TdS - PdV\,} $$ --- ### Derivation B: $dH = TdS + VdP$ (Enthalpy) **Step 1.** Start from the definition of enthalpy $H = U + PV$. Take the total differential: $$ dH = dU + d(PV) = dU + PdV + VdP $$ **Step 2.** Substitute the result from Derivation A ($dU = TdS - PdV$): $$ dH = (TdS - PdV) + PdV + VdP $$ **Step 3.** Cancel the $\pm PdV$ terms: $$ \boxed{\,dH = TdS + VdP\,} $$ --- ### Derivation C: $dA = -SdT - PdV$ (Helmholtz Energy) **Step 1.** Start from the definition $A = U - TS$. Take the total differential: $$ dA = dU - TdS - SdT $$ **Step 2.** Substitute $dU = TdS - PdV$: $$ dA = (TdS - PdV) - TdS - SdT $$ **Step 3.** Cancel the $\pm TdS$ terms: $$ \boxed{\,dA = -SdT - PdV\,} $$ --- ### Derivation D: $dG = -SdT + VdP$ (Gibbs Energy) **Step 1.** Start from the definition $G = H - TS$. Take the total differential: $$ dG = dH - TdS - SdT $$ **Step 2.** Substitute $dH = TdS + VdP$: $$ dG = (TdS + VdP) - TdS - SdT $$ **Step 3.** Cancel the $\pm TdS$ terms: $$ \boxed{\,dG = -SdT + VdP\,} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of All Four Maxwell's Relations > [!NOTE] > **Key Principle:** If $Z$ is a state function of $(x, y)$, then $dZ = Mdx + Ndy$ is exact, which requires $\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$. ### Maxwell-1 (from $U$): Given $dU = TdS - PdV$, we identify $M = T$ and $N = -P$ with $x = S$, $y = V$: $$ \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial (-P)}{\partial S}\right)_V = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V $$ $$ \boxed{\,\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V\,} $$ ### Maxwell-2 (from $H$): Given $dH = TdS + VdP$, with $x = S$, $y = P$: $$ \boxed{\,\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P\,} $$ ### Maxwell-3 (from $A$): Given $dA = -SdT - PdV$, with $x = T$, $y = V$: $$ \boxed{\,\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V\,} $$ ### Maxwell-4 (from $G$): Given $dG = -SdT + VdP$, with $x = T$, $y = P$: $$ \boxed{\,\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P\,} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation of the $C_p - C_v$ Relation **Step 1.** Write definitions of heat capacities: $$ C_p = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P $$ $$ C_v = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V $$ **Step 2.** Subtract the two: $$ C_p - C_v = T\left[\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P - \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V\right] $$ **Step 3.** Express $S$ as a function of $(T, V)$ and apply the chain rule at constant $P$: $$ \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P $$ **Step 4.** Substitute back: $$ C_p - C_v = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P $$ **Step 5.** Apply Maxwell-3: $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$ $$ C_p - C_v = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P $$ **Step 6.** Use the cyclic rule: $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = -\dfrac{\left(\partial V / \partial T\right)_P}{\left(\partial V / \partial P\right)_T}$ $$ \boxed{\,C_p - C_v = -T\,\frac{\left(\partial V / \partial T\right)_P^{\,2}}{\left(\partial V / \partial P\right)_T}\,} $$ **Verification for an ideal gas** ($PV = RT$): $\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{R}{P}$ and $\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\frac{RT}{P^2} = -\frac{V}{P}$ $$ C_p - C_v = -T \cdot \frac{(R/P)^2}{-V/P} = \frac{TR^2/P^2}{V/P} = \frac{TR^2}{PV} = \frac{TR^2}{RT} = R $$ This recovers the famous result $C_p - C_v = R$ for an ideal gas. ‚úÖ --- ## 3.4 Derivation of the Clapeyron Equation **Step 1.** At phase equilibrium $\alpha \rightleftharpoons \beta$, the molar Gibbs energies are equal: $$ G^\alpha = G^\beta \quad \Rightarrow \quad dG^\alpha = dG^\beta $$ **Step 2.** Apply the Gibbs relation $dG = -SdT + VdP$ to both phases: $$ -S^\alpha dT + V^\alpha dP = -S^\beta dT + V^\beta dP $$ **Step 3.** Rearrange to group $dP$ and $dT$ terms: $$ (V^\beta - V^\alpha) dP = (S^\beta - S^\alpha) dT $$ **Step 4.** Recognize $\Delta V = V^\beta - V^\alpha$ and $\Delta S = S^\beta - S^\alpha$: $$ \frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta S}{\Delta V} $$ **Step 5.** At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$, so $\Delta H = T\Delta S \Rightarrow \Delta S = \dfrac{\Delta H}{T}$: $$ \boxed{\,\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{trans}}{T\,\Delta V}\,} $$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation of the Clausius-Clapeyron Equation **Step 1.** Apply Clapeyron to liquid-vapor equilibrium and assume $\Delta V \approx V_g$ (liquid volume negligible): $$ \frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T V_g} $$ **Step 2.** Substitute the ideal gas law $V_g = \dfrac{RT}{P}$: $$ \frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{P\,\Delta H_{vap}}{R T^2} $$ **Step 3.** Separate variables (assuming $\Delta H_{vap}$ is constant over the temperature range): $$ \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\,\frac{dT}{T^2} $$ **Step 4.** Integrate between states 1 and 2: $$ \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2} $$ **Step 5.** Evaluate both integrals: $$ \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) $$ $$ \boxed{\,\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\,} $$ # ūüďė SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Mayer Thermodynamic Square (Mermaid) The classic **mnemonic square** for remembering Maxwell's relations. Opposite edges of the square represent the four Maxwell relations. ```mermaid graph TD A(["A : HELMHOLTZ FREE ENERGY
Natural variables T and V
dA = -SdT - PdV"]) B(["T Temperature"]) C(["S Entropy"]) D(["G : GIBBS FREE ENERGY
Natural variables T and P
dG = -SdT + VdP"]) E(["P Pressure"]) F(["V Volume"]) G(["U : INTERNAL ENERGY
Natural variables S and V
dU = TdS - PdV"]) H(["H : ENTHALPY
Natural variables S and P
dH = TdS + VdP"]) A --- B B --- C C --- D D --- E E --- F F --- G G --- A A --- D B --- H H --- E G --- H C --- F B --- E C --- G note1["MAXWELL 3: dS/dV at T = dP/dT at V
from A T V side"] note2["MAXWELL 4: dS/dP at T = -dV/dT at P
from G T P side"] note3["MAXWELL 1: dT/dV at S = -dP/dS at V
from U S V side"] note4["MAXWELL 2: dT/dP at S = dV/dS at P
from H S P side"] A -. relates .-> note1 D -. relates .-> note2 G -. relates .-> note3 H -. relates .-> note4 ``` --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Relations This schematic shows how the **First Law + Second Law** flow down to produce all property relations used in chemical engineering practice. ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["FIRST LAW OF THERMODYNAMICS
dU = deltaQ + deltaW"] P2["SECOND LAW OF THERMODYNAMICS
dS = deltaQ rev divided by T"] P3["COMBINED FIRST AND SECOND LAWS
dU = TdS - PdV"] P3 --> Q1["Define H = U plus PV
dH = TdS plus VdP"] P3 --> Q2["Define A = U minus TS
dA = -SdT minus PdV"] P1 --> Q3["Define G = H minus TS
dG = -SdT plus VdP"] Q1 --> R1["Apply Exactness Criterion
to H S P"] Q2 --> R2["Apply Exactness Criterion
to A T V"] Q3 --> R3["Apply Exactness Criterion
to G T P"] P3 --> R4["Apply Exactness Criterion
to U S V"] R1 --> S1["MAXWELL 2
dT/dP at S = dV/dS at P"] R2 --> S2["MAXWELL 3
dS/dV at T = dP/dT at V"] R3 --> S3["MAXWELL 4
dS/dP at T = -dV/dT at P"] R4 --> S4["MAXWELL 1
dT/dV at S = -dP/dS at V"] S2 --> T1["DERIVE Cp minus Cv relation
using Maxwell 3 and chain rule"] S3 --> T2["DERIVE dS and dV relations
for property changes"] T1 --> U1["Clapeyron Equation
dP over dT = Delta H over T Delta V"] U1 --> U2["Clausius Clapeyron Equation
ln P2 over P1 = -Delta H over R times 1 over T2 minus 1 over T1"] U2 --> V1["INDUSTRIAL APPLICATIONS
Distillation column design
Refrigeration cycles
Compressor and pump work
Vapor pressure estimation"] ``` --- ## 4.3 Memory Aid Diagram: Property Triangle ```mermaid graph LR U["INTERNAL ENERGY U
Function of S and V
DU equals TdS minus PdV"] H["ENTHALPY H
Function of S and P
DH equals TdS plus VdP"] A["HELMHOLTZ A
Function of T and V
DA equals minus SdT minus PdV"] G["GIBBS G
Function of T and P
DG equals minus SdT plus VdP"] U -->|"Add PV"| H U -->|"Subtract TS"| A H -->|"Subtract TS"| G A -->|"Add PV"| G ``` # ūüďė SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. State and explain any two Maxwell's relations. Discuss their thermodynamic significance.** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Maxwell's relations** are a set of four equations derived from the equality of mixed second partial derivatives of the thermodynamic potentials $U$, $H$, $A$, and $G$. They relate partial derivatives involving **entropy** (a non-measurable quantity) to partial derivatives involving only the **measurable** state variables $P$, $V$, and $T$. **Two of the four relations:** $$ \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V \quad \text{(Maxwell-3, from } A\text{)} $$ $$ \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P \quad \text{(Maxwell-4, from } G\text{)} $$ **Significance:** - They allow us to replace **impossible-to-measure** quantities (entropy gradients) with **easy-to-measure** quantities ($P$‚Äď$V$‚Äď$T$ gradients). - They form the bridge from **thermodynamic principles** to **practical property estimation** (equations of state, residual properties). - They are extensively used in deriving the $C_p - C_v$ relation, Clapeyron equation, and Joule-Thomson coefficient. --- ### **Q2. Define Helmholtz free energy ($A$) and Gibbs free energy ($G$). Write their fundamental property relations.** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024]` **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Helmholtz Free Energy ($A$):** Defined as $A = U - TS$. It represents the maximum non-expansion work obtainable from a closed system at constant $T$ and $V$. Its fundamental property relation is: > $$dA = -SdT - PdV$$ > [!NOTE] > **Gibbs Free Energy ($G$):** Defined as $G = H - TS = U + PV - TS$. It represents the maximum non-expansion work obtainable from a closed system at constant $T$ and $P$. Its fundamental property relation is: > $$dG = -SdT + VdP$$ These relations show that the natural (canonical) variables of $A$ are $(T, V)$ and of $G$ are $(T, P)$ ‚ÄĒ the conditions under which each potential reaches a minimum at equilibrium. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2022]` #### **(a)** Starting from the combined statement of the First and Second Laws, derive the four fundamental property relations for $U$, $H$, $A$, and $G$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1.** First Law for a closed system: $dU = \delta Q + \delta W$ **Step 2.** For a reversible process: $\delta Q_{rev} = TdS$ and $\delta W_{rev} = -PdV$ **Step 3.** Substituting: $$ dU = TdS - PdV \quad \text{...(i)} $$ **Step 4.** From $H = U + PV$: $dH = dU + PdV + VdP$ Substituting (i): $$ dH = TdS + VdP \quad \text{...(ii)} $$ **Step 5.** From $A = U - TS$: $dA = dU - TdS - SdT$ Substituting (i): $$ dA = -SdT - PdV \quad \text{...(iii)} $$ **Step 6.** From $G = H - TS$: $dG = dH - TdS - SdT$ Substituting (ii): $$ dG = -SdT + VdP \quad \text{...(iv)} $$ Equations (i), (ii), (iii), and (iv) are the four fundamental property relations. ‚úÖ --- #### **(b)** A substance undergoes a solid‚Äďliquid phase transition at $T = 350\,\text{K}$ with molar latent heat $\Delta H = 4000\,\text{J/mol}$. The molar volume change on melting is $\Delta V = 5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$. Calculate the slope $dP/dT$ at this temperature using the Clapeyron equation. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $T = 350\,\text{K}$ - $\Delta H = 4000\,\text{J/mol}$ - $\Delta V = 5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$ **Step 1.** Clapeyron equation: $$ \frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H}{T\,\Delta V} $$ **Step 2.** Substitute numerical values: $$ \frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{4000}{350 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}} $$ **Step 3.** Calculate the denominator: $$ 350 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 1.75 \times 10^{-3} $$ **Step 4.** Final calculation: $$ \frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{4000}{1.75 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.286 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa/K} $$ $$ \boxed{\,\frac{dP}{dT} \approx 2.286\,\text{MPa/K}\,} $$ **Physical interpretation:** A very steep slope ‚ÄĒ characteristic of solid‚Äďliquid transitions where $\Delta V$ is very small (densities of solid and liquid are similar). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2023]` #### **(a)** Derive all four Maxwell's relations from the fundamental property relations. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** > [!NOTE] > **Principle Used:** If $Z(x, y)$ is a state function and $dZ = Mdx + Ndy$ is its exact differential, then $\left(\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\dfrac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$ (Euler reciprocity). **From $dU = TdS - PdV$** ($M = T$, $N = -P$, $x = S$, $y = V$): $$ \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial (-P)}{\partial S}\right)_V $$ $$ \boxed{\,\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V\,} \quad \text{Maxwell-1} $$ **From $dH = TdS + VdP$** ($M = T$, $N = V$, $x = S$, $y = P$): $$ \boxed{\,\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P\,} \quad \text{Maxwell-2} $$ **From $dA = -SdT - PdV$** ($M = -S$, $N = -P$, $x = T$, $y = V$): $$ \boxed{\,\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V\,} \quad \text{Maxwell-3} $$ **From $dG = -SdT + VdP$** ($M = -S$, $N = V$, $x = T$, $y = P$): $$ \boxed{\,\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P\,} \quad \text{Maxwell-4} $$ --- #### **(b)** Derive the Clausius-Clapeyron equation. The vapor pressure of water at $100\,^\circ\text{C}$ is $101.325\,\text{kPa}$ and the latent heat of vaporization is $2257\,\text{kJ/kg}$. Estimate the vapor pressure of water at $110\,^\circ\text{C}$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1.** Clapeyron equation for vaporization: $$ \frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T\,\Delta V} $$ **Step 2.** Assume $\Delta V \approx V_g$ and ideal gas: $V_g = \dfrac{RT}{P}$ (per mole) or $V_g = \dfrac{RT}{PM}$ (per kg, where $M$ is molar mass). For water, $M = 18.015 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg/mol}$: $$ \frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{P\,\Delta H_{vap}}{R T^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\,\frac{dT}{T^2} $$ **Step 3.** Integrate (assuming $\Delta H_{vap}$ is constant): $$ \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) $$ **Step 4.** Convert $\Delta H_{vap}$ to molar basis: $$ \Delta H_{vap} = 2257\,\text{kJ/kg} \times 0.018015\,\text{kg/mol} = 40.66\,\text{kJ/mol} = 40660\,\text{J/mol} $$ **Step 5.** Substitute $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol¬∑K)}$, $T_1 = 373.15\,\text{K}$, $T_2 = 383.15\,\text{K}$: $$ \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{383.15} = 2.680 \times 10^{-3} - 2.610 \times 10^{-3} = 7.0 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{K}^{-1} $$ **Step 6.** Compute the exponent: $$ -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) = \frac{40660 \times 7.0 \times 10^{-5}}{8.314} = 0.3423 $$ **Step 7.** Solve for $P_2$: $$ \frac{P_2}{101.325} = e^{0.3423} = 1.408 $$ $$ P_2 = 101.325 \times 1.408 = 142.7\,\text{kPa} $$ $$ \boxed{\,P_2 \approx 142.7\,\text{kPa at } 110\,^\circ\text{C}\,} $$ (Real steam-table value is $\approx 143.4\,\text{kPa}$, validating our assumption of constant $\Delta H_{vap}$.) --- > [!WARNING] > **ūüĒī KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚ÄĒ Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Sign Error in Maxwell's Relations:** Students frequently miss the **negative sign** in Maxwell-1 and Maxwell-4. **Memory trick:** relations from $U$ and $G$ carry a negative sign (the "**U-G**" rule). > 2. **Variable Confusion:** Mixing up $\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ with $\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_V$ ‚ÄĒ the subscript indicates what's held constant, not the variable being differentiated. > 3. **Cyclic Rule Misapplication:** When using the cyclic rule $\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_P \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T = -1$, forgetting the **$-1$** is a classic 2-mark loss. > 4. **Clausius-Clapeyron Unit Mismatch:** Using $\Delta H_{vap}$ in kJ/kg with $R$ in J/(mol¬∑K) without converting to per-mole basis. Always convert $\Delta H$ to J/mol before substituting. > 5. **In Clapeyron problems:** Writing $dP/dT$ instead of $\Delta P/\Delta T$ ‚ÄĒ for a small finite change, $\Delta P = (dP/dT)\cdot\Delta T$ is the correct approach. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ‚ö° > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Module 2: Fundamental Property Relations** - ‚úÖ The **four fundamental property relations** are obtained by combining the 1st and 2nd laws: $dU$, $dH$, $dA$, $dG$. - ‚úÖ **Natural variables:** $U(S,V)$, $H(S,P)$, $A(T,V)$, $G(T,P)$ ‚ÄĒ the variables on the right-hand side. - ‚úÖ Maxwell's relations come from the **exactness** criterion: $\left(\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\dfrac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$. - ‚úÖ **Sign convention:** Maxwell-1 and Maxwell-4 have a **negative sign**; Maxwell-2 and Maxwell-3 are **positive**. - ‚úÖ The **Mayer square** is the fastest way to recall all four Maxwell relations during exams. - ‚úÖ The relation $C_p - C_v = -T\dfrac{\left(\partial V/\partial T\right)_P^{\,2}}{\left(\partial V/\partial P\right)_T}$ is the **general** formula; for ideal gas it reduces to $C_p - C_v = R$. - ‚úÖ The **Clapeyron equation** $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{T\,\Delta V}$ applies to **all** phase transitions. - ‚úÖ The **Clausius-Clapeyron equation** is a special case of Clapeyron assuming $\Delta V \approx V_g$ and ideal gas behavior, with $\Delta H$ independent of $T$. - ‚úÖ **Units to watch:** $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol¬∑K)}$; $\Delta H$ must be in J/mol; $P$ in Pa; $V$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$. - ‚úÖ Maxwell's relations transform **entropy derivatives** (hard) into **$P$‚Äď$V$‚Äď$T$ derivatives** (easy) ‚ÄĒ this is their central engineering utility. - ‚úÖ The **thermodynamic square / Mayer square** mnemonic: $T$‚Äď$S$ on top, $P$‚Äď$V$ on bottom, with $G$ (natural $T,P$) bottom-center and $A$ (natural $T,V$) top-center.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics**, **Maxwell's Equations** (more rigorously called **Maxwell's Relations**) are a set of thermodynamic identities that establish the equivalence between mixed second-order partial derivatives of fundamental thermodynamic potentials. They arise directly from the **Euler reciprocity relation** (also known as the **equality of mixed partial derivatives**) applied to the exact differentials of the four fundamental thermodynamic property relations:

$$
dU = TdS - PdV
$$

$$
dH = TdS + VdP
$$

$$
dA = -SdT - PdV
$$

$$
dG = -SdT + VdP
$$

where:
- $U$ = Internal Energy, $H$ = Enthalpy, $A$ = Helmholtz Free Energy, $G$ = Gibbs Free Energy
- $T$ = Absolute Temperature, $P$ = Pressure, $V$ = Volume, $S$ = Entropy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Maxwell's Relations are the bridge between **easily measurable properties** $(P, V, T)$ and **difficult-to-measure properties** $(S, U, H, A, G)$. They allow a chemical engineer to compute entropy and energy changes from PVT data alone.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Picture

Imagine you are hiking on a smooth mountain. The amount of work (energy) required to climb from the base camp to the summit is **independent of the path taken** ‚ÄĒ it depends only on the **starting point** and the **ending point**. This is the principle of **path independence of state functions**.

Now, suppose you walk up via Path 1 (east-flank trail) and Path 2 (west-flank trail). Since both must yield the same elevation change, the **rate of change of altitude along one direction must equal a related rate along the perpendicular direction**. Mathematically, if $z = f(x, y)$ is the elevation, then:

$$
\left( \frac{\partial^2 z}{\partial x \, \partial y} \right) = \left( \frac{\partial^2 z}{\partial y \, \partial x} \right)
$$

This exact equality is the **Euler Reciprocity Relation**, and it is the **heart** of all Maxwell's Relations in thermodynamics. Because every fundamental potential ($U$, $H$, $A$, $G$) is an exact state function, we can exploit this symmetry to convert *unmeasurable* partial derivatives (e.g., $\partial S / \partial V$ at constant $T$) into *measurable* ones (e.g., $\partial P / \partial T$ at constant $V$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea:** Maxwell's Relations do **not introduce any new physics**. They are mathematical consequences of the fact that $dU$, $dH$, $dA$, and $dG$ are **exact differentials**. Their power lies in **rearranging** derivatives into experimentally accessible forms.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants Referenced

| Symbol | Quantity | Value / Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $R$ | Universal Gas Constant | $\mathbf{8.314 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}}$ |
| $P_c$ | Critical Pressure | Substance-dependent |
| $T_c$ | Critical Temperature | Substance-dependent |
| $\omega$ | Acentric Factor | Dimensionless shape factor |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Euler Reciprocity Theorem

Let $z = z(x, y)$ be a continuous, single-valued function with continuous second partial derivatives. The total differential is:

$$
dz = M \, dx + N \, dy
$$

where $M = \left(\frac{\partial z}{\partial x}\right)_y$ and $N = \left(\frac{\partial z}{\partial y}\right)_x$.

For $dz$ to be an **exact differential**, the cross-second derivatives must be equal:

$$
\left( \frac{\partial M}{\partial y} \right)_x = \left( \frac{\partial N}{\partial x} \right)_y
$$

This is the **Euler Reciprocity Theorem** (also called **Schwarz's Theorem** on symmetry of second derivatives). Every thermodynamic property relation that satisfies this condition yields a Maxwell Relation.

## 2.2 Why Maxwell's Relations Matter in Chemical Engineering

- **Entropy is not directly measurable.** No instrument directly "reads" entropy. Maxwell's Relations allow us to calculate entropy changes from $P$‚Äď$V$‚Äď$T$ measurements, which are routine in laboratories.
- They enable derivation of **departure functions** for real gases and real fluids.
- They form the mathematical backbone for deriving the **Clausius-Clapeyron Equation**, **Joule-Thomson Coefficient**, and **Internal Pressure** equations.
- They are essential for **process simulation software** (Aspen Plus, HYSYS) which calculates thermodynamic properties from equations of state.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet ‚ÄĒ The Four Maxwell Relations

| # | From Potential | Maxwell Relation | Useful Transformation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **MR-1** | $U(S, V)$ | $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$ | Adiabatic expansion coefficient |
| **MR-2** | $H(S, P)$ | $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P$ | Adiabatic compression heating |
| **MR-3** | $A(T, V)$ | $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$ | Most used: relates $S$ to $PVT$ data |
| **MR-4** | $G(T, P)$ | $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | Most used: gives $\Delta S$ from thermal expansion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** In KTU board exams, **MR-3** and **MR-4** are the most frequently tested because they express entropy in terms of **measurable PVT quantities**. Memorize the sign convention ‚ÄĒ **MR-1** and **MR-4** carry the negative sign; **MR-2** and **MR-3** are positive.

## 2.4 Related High-Yield Identities Derived From Maxwell Relations

| Identity Name | Mathematical Form | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Internal Pressure** | $\left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P$ | Real gas deviation from ideality |
| **Joule-Thomson Coefficient** | $\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H = \dfrac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]$ | Throttling valve design, refrigeration |
| **Clausius-Clapeyron** | $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \Delta V_{vap}}$ | Vapor pressure vs. temperature |
| **Cp ‚Äď Cv Relation** | $C_P - C_V = -T\dfrac{\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P^2}{\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial P}\right)_T}$ | Heat capacity difference for real fluids |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Maxwell Relation 3 ‚ÄĒ From Helmholtz Free Energy

**Starting Equation:** The fundamental property relation for Helmholtz Free Energy $A(T, V)$:

$$
dA = -S \, dT - P \, dV
$$

**Step 1:** Identify the partial derivatives by comparing with the general exact differential form $dz = M \, dx + N \, dy$:

Here, $z = A$, $x = T$, $y = V$, so:

$$
M = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial T}\right)_V = -S
$$

$$
N = \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial V}\right)_T = -P
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Euler Reciprocity Theorem:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y
$$

Substituting $M = -S$ and $N = -P$:

$$
\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial V}(-S)\right]_T = \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}(-P)\right]_V
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate both sides explicitly:

$$
-\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V
$$

**Step 4:** Cancel the negative signs on both sides:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V \quad \boxed{\text{MR-3}}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** This relation tells us that if we know how pressure varies with temperature at constant volume (which is easy to measure in a rigid bomb calorimeter), we can immediately obtain how entropy changes with volume at constant temperature.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Maxwell Relation 4 ‚ÄĒ From Gibbs Free Energy

**Starting Equation:**

$$
dG = -S \, dT + V \, dP
$$

**Step 1:** Identify partial derivatives:

$$
M = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -S
$$

$$
N = \left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial P}\right)_T = V
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Euler Reciprocity:

$$
\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial P}(-S)\right]_T = \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial T}(V)\right]_P
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$
-\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P \quad \boxed{\text{MR-4}}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** $\left(\partial V / \partial T\right)_P$ is the **coefficient of thermal expansion** $\alpha$, so MR-4 reduces to:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\alpha V
$$

This means **increasing pressure at constant temperature decreases entropy**, which is physically intuitive (compression reduces molecular disorder).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Internal Pressure Equation ‚ÄĒ Application of Maxwell Relations

**Objective:** Derive $\left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T$ using MR-3.

**Step 1:** Start with the fundamental relation $dU = TdS - PdV$. Hold $T$ constant and divide by $dV$:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T - P
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute MR-3: $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$

$$
\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P
$$

**Step 3:** Verify for ideal gas using $PV = RT$:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{R}{V}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = T \cdot \frac{R}{V} - P = \frac{RT}{V} - P = P - P = 0
$$

This confirms **Joule's Law** for ideal gases ‚ÄĒ internal energy is independent of volume.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Joule-Thomson Coefficient ‚ÄĒ Application of MR-4

**Objective:** Express $\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H$ in measurable quantities.

**Step 1:** Start from $dH = TdS + VdP$. At constant enthalpy ($dH = 0$):

$$
0 = TdS + VdP \quad \Rightarrow \quad dS = -\frac{V}{T}dP
$$

**Step 2:** Express $dS$ as a function of $T$ and $P$:

$$
dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T dP = \frac{C_P}{T} dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T dP
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute MR-4: $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$

$$
dS = \frac{C_P}{T} dT - \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP
$$

**Step 4:** Equate with Step 1:

$$
-\frac{V}{T}dP = \frac{C_P}{T} dT - \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $dT$:

$$
\frac{C_P}{T} dT = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP - \frac{V}{T} dP
$$

$$
dT = \frac{T}{C_P}\left[\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - \frac{V}{T}\right] dP
$$

**Step 6:** Take the ratio:

$$
\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right] \quad \boxed{\text{Joule-Thomson Coefficient}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign of $\mu_{JT}$:** If $\mu_{JT} > 0$, gas cools upon throttling (used in Linde liquefaction). If $\mu_{JT} < 0$, gas heats up on throttling (hydrogen at room temperature).

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Clausius-Clapeyron Equation

**Objective:** Derive $dP/dT$ for a phase transition using MR-3.

**Step 1:** For a reversible phase change at equilibrium:

$$
dG^{\alpha} = dG^{\beta}
$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are coexisting phases.

**Step 2:** Substitute $dG = -SdT + VdP$:

$$
-S^{\alpha} dT + V^{\alpha} dP = -S^{\beta} dT + V^{\beta} dP
$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange:

$$
(V^{\beta} - V^{\alpha}) dP = (S^{\beta} - S^{\alpha}) dT
$$

**Step 4:** Let $\Delta V_{vap} = V^{\beta} - V^{\alpha}$ and $\Delta S_{vap} = S^{\beta} - S^{\alpha}$:

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta S_{vap}}{\Delta V_{vap}}
$$

**Step 5:** At equilibrium, $\Delta S_{vap} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T}$ (since the transition is reversible and isothermal):

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \Delta V_{vap}} \quad \boxed{\text{Clausius-Clapeyron}}
$$

This is the foundation for **boiling point elevation**, **vapor pressure correlations** (Antoine equation), and **distillation column design**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Thermodynamic Square ‚ÄĒ Mnemonic for Maxwell Relations

The **Thermodynamic Square** is a powerful KTU-favorite mnemonic to remember all four Maxwell relations. The vertices represent the four potentials, and the edges encode the partial derivatives.

```mermaid
graph TD
    U_N["U Internal Energy"]:::top
    G_N["G Gibbs Free Energy"]:::bottom
    H_N["H Enthalpy"]:::right
    A_N["A Helmholtz Free Energy"]:::left
    S_N["S Entropy"]:::inside
    T_N["T Temperature"]:::outside
    V_N["V Volume"]:::outside
    P_N["P Pressure"]:::outside

    U_N --- S_N
    U_N --- V_N
    H_N --- S_N
    H_N --- P_N
    A_N --- T_N
    A_N --- V_N
    G_N --- T_N
    G_N --- P_N

    S_N --- T_N
    S_N --- P_N
    V_N --- T_N
    V_N --- P_N

    classDef top fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bottom fill:#80B0FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef right fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef left fill:#FF9999,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef inside fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef outside fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#555,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

**Reading Rule for the Square:**
- **Horizontal edges** (across the center): the variable along the edge is held constant.
- **Diagonal edges** (corner-to-corner): the potential at the corner is held constant.
- The Maxwell Relation equates the partial derivative reading along one path with the negative of the derivative reading along the perpendicular path.
