# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024]
**"State any two Maxwell's Relations. Derive one of them using the fundamental property relation."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Two Maxwell Relations are:

$$
\text{(i)} \quad \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V
$$

$$
\text{(ii)} \quad \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P
$$

**Derivation of (i):**
From $dA = -S \, dT - P \, dV$, we identify $M = -S$ and $N = -P$. Applying Euler reciprocity:

$$
\left[\frac{\partial (-S)}{\partial V}\right]_T = \left[\frac{\partial (-P)}{\partial T}\right]_V \Rightarrow \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]
**"Using Maxwell's Relation, prove that for an ideal gas, internal energy is independent of volume."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

For an ideal gas, $PV = RT$, so:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{R}{V}
$$

By the internal pressure equation derived using MR-3:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = T \cdot \frac{R}{V} - P = \frac{RT}{V} - \frac{RT}{V} = 0
$$

Hence $U$ depends only on $T$, proving Joule's Law. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ‚ÄĒ Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Missing negative sign** in MR-1 and MR-4 ‚ÄĒ deducts **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify the variable held constant** (subscript in the derivative) ‚ÄĒ deducts **0.5 mark per occurrence**.
> 3. **Not showing the starting fundamental equation** before applying Euler reciprocity ‚ÄĒ deducts **1 mark**.
> 4. **Confusing the sign convention** in Joule-Thomson derivation ‚ÄĒ a single sign error can make the coefficient opposite in sign, costing **2 marks**.

---

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive all four Maxwell's Relations starting from the four fundamental property relations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using Maxwell's Relations, derive the expression for the Joule-Thomson coefficient of a real gas. Show that for an ideal gas, $\mu_{JT} = 0$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Relation 1:** From $dU = TdS - PdV$:
$M = T$, $N = -P$, where $x = S$, $y = V$.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V
$$

**Relation 2:** From $dH = TdS + VdP$:
$M = T$, $N = V$, where $x = S$, $y = P$.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P
$$

**Relation 3:** From $dA = -SdT - PdV$:
$M = -S$, $N = -P$, where $x = T$, $y = V$.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V
$$

**Relation 4:** From $dG = -SdT + VdP$:
$M = -S$, $N = V$, where $x = T$, $y = P$.

$$
\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P
$$

**(b) Joule-Thomson Coefficient Derivation:**

Starting from $dH = TdS + VdP$ at constant $H$:

$$
0 = TdS + VdP \quad \Rightarrow \quad dS = -\frac{V}{T}dP \quad \cdots (i)
$$

Express $S$ as $S(T, P)$:

$$
dS = \frac{C_P}{T}dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T dP \quad \cdots (ii)
$$

By MR-4: $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$. Substitute into (ii):

$$
dS = \frac{C_P}{T}dT - \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP \quad \cdots (iii)
$$

Equating (i) and (iii):

$$
-\frac{V}{T}dP = \frac{C_P}{T}dT - \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP
$$

Rearranging for $dT/dP$:

$$
\frac{C_P}{T} dT = \left[\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - \frac{V}{T}\right] dP
$$

$$
\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]
$$

**For ideal gas:** $PV = RT \Rightarrow V = RT/P$

$$
\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{R}{P} \Rightarrow T \cdot \frac{R}{P} - \frac{RT}{P} = 0
$$

Therefore $\mu_{JT}^{ideal} = 0$, confirming that an ideal gas shows **no temperature change** on throttling. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2023]

**(a)** Derive the Clausius-Clapeyron Equation using Maxwell's Relation and explain its significance in distillation column design. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a gas obeying the van der Waals equation $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = RT$, derive an expression for the Joule-Thomson coefficient. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Clausius-Clapeyron Derivation:**

At phase equilibrium between liquid (L) and vapor (V):

$$
G^L(T, P) = G^V(T, P)
$$

Differentiating:

$$
dG^L = dG^V
$$

Substituting $dG = -SdT + VdP$:

$$
-S^L dT + V^L dP = -S^V dT + V^V dP
$$

Rearranging:

$$
(V^V - V^L) dP = (S^V - S^L) dT
$$

For a reversible phase change: $\Delta S = \Delta H_{vap} / T$

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta S_{vap}}{\Delta V_{vap}} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \Delta V_{vap}}
$$

**Significance in Distillation:** This equation predicts **how boiling point changes with operating pressure**. It is used to size reboilers, choose operating pressures for vacuum distillation of heat-sensitive materials (e.g., crude oil vacuum distillation at 50 mmHg to avoid cracking), and design cryogenic air separation columns. $\blacksquare$

**(b) Joule-Thomson Coefficient for van der Waals Gas:**

Starting from the van der Waals equation solved for $P$:

$$
P = \frac{RT}{V - b} - \frac{a}{V^2}
$$

Differentiate with respect to $T$ at constant $P$:

$$
0 = \frac{R}{V-b}\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial T}\right)_P - \frac{RT}{(V-b)^2}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P + \frac{2a}{V^3}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P
$$

$$
\frac{R}{V-b} = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P \left[\frac{RT}{(V-b)^2} - \frac{2a}{V^3}\right]
$$

$$
\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{\dfrac{R}{V-b}}{\dfrac{RT}{(V-b)^2} - \dfrac{2a}{V^3}} = \frac{RV^3(V-b)}{RTV^3 - 2a(V-b)^2}
$$

Substituting into the Joule-Thomson formula:

$$
\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_P}\left[\frac{RTV^3(V-b)}{RTV^3 - 2a(V-b)^2} - V\right]
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\mu_{JT} = \frac{V}{C_P}\left[\frac{RTV^2(V-b)}{RTV^3 - 2a(V-b)^2} - 1\right]
$$

This is the working equation for predicting **inversion temperatures** for real gases. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap ‚ÄĒ Important Things to Remember

- **Maxwell's Relations are NOT laws of physics** ‚ÄĒ they are **mathematical identities** arising from the exactness of thermodynamic differentials.
- **Negative Sign Rule:** MR-1 and MR-4 carry a negative sign; MR-2 and MR-3 are positive.
- **Always specify the variable held constant** in the subscript ‚ÄĒ this is a **frequent valuation deduction**.
- **MR-3 and MR-4 are the workhorses** ‚ÄĒ they express entropy derivatives in terms of measurable $P$‚Äď$V$‚Äď$T$ properties.
- **Thermodynamic Square mnemonic:** $U$ on top, $G$ on bottom, $H$ on right, $A$ on left, $S$ in center; $T$ and $P$ as horizontal handles, $V$ and $S$ as vertical handles.
- **Internal Pressure Equation:** $\left(\dfrac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V - P$ ‚ÄĒ gives $\mathbf{0}$ for ideal gas.
- **Joule-Thomson Coefficient:** $\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{1}{C_P}\left[T\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]$ ‚ÄĒ **positive** for most gases at room temperature, **negative** for $H_2$ and $He$.
- **Clausius-Clapeyron:** $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \Delta V_{vap}}$ ‚ÄĒ basis of Antoine equation and distillation pressure selection.
- **Euler Reciprocity:** $\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 z}{\partial x \, \partial y}\right) = \left(\dfrac{\partial^2 z}{\partial y \, \partial x}\right)$ ‚ÄĒ the foundational principle.
- **Engineering applications:** Entropy change calculations for real fluids, Joule-Thomson inversion curves, vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) prediction, refrigeration cycle analysis, and cryogenic process design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clausius-Clapeyron Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Clausius-Clapeyron Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Clausius-Clapeyron Equation** is a thermodynamic relation that describes the rate at which the saturation vapor pressure of a pure substance changes with respect to temperature along a phase equilibrium curve (typically the liquid‚Äďvapor or solid‚Äďvapor coexistence line). It is the integrated, industrially-usable form of the more general **Clapeyron Equation**, obtained under the simplifying assumption that the molar volume of the condensed phase (liquid or solid) is negligible compared to the molar volume of the vapor phase, and that the vapor obeys the **ideal gas law**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEPOT633 ‚Äď Module 2):**
> The Clausius-Clapeyron equation is a **CO2 / CO3 mapped topic** under the cognitive levels **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)** of Bloom's Taxonomy. Students are expected to *derive* the equation, *list* its assumptions, and *apply* it to compute vapor pressures, normal boiling points, and latent heats of vaporization from experimental P‚ÄďT data.

Mathematically, the integrated form most frequently used in KTU examinations is:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2}-\frac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

where:
- $P_1, P_2$ = saturation vapor pressures at absolute temperatures $T_1, T_2$ respectively
- $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ = molar enthalpy of vaporization (assumed temperature-independent) in **J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ**
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ**

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are climbing a tall mountain ‚ÄĒ say, **Mount Everest** ‚ÄĒ with a kettle of water. At sea level, water boils at **100 ¬įC (373.15 K)** because atmospheric pressure is **101.325 kPa**. As you ascend, atmospheric pressure drops, and your kettle starts whistling at **70 ¬įC** at the summit. Why? Because the **boiling point is essentially the temperature at which the liquid's vapor pressure equals the surrounding pressure**.

The Clausius-Clapeyron equation tells us *exactly* how sensitively this "boiling temperature" responds to the surrounding pressure, depending on how strongly the molecules are bound together (i.e., how large the latent heat of vaporization is). Substances with strong intermolecular forces (like water, $\Delta H_{\text{vap}} \approx 40.7$ kJ/mol) show steep P‚ÄďT curves, while weakly bound liquids (like diethyl ether, $\Delta H_{\text{vap}} \approx 27$ kJ/mol) have gentler curves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** The Clausius-Clapeyron equation is essentially the "thermodynamic GPS" for tracing the vapor-pressure curve of any pure substance as temperature changes. If you know the pressure at *any* one temperature and the latent heat, you can predict the pressure at *any other* temperature.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------------|
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ** |
| $P$ | Vapor pressure | **Pa** or **kPa** or **bar** |
| $T$ | Absolute temperature | **K** (Kelvin) |
| $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ | Latent heat of vaporization | **J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ** or **kJ¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ** |
| $T_b$ | Normal boiling point | **K** at **101.325 kPa** |

> [!TIP]
> Always convert temperatures to **Kelvin** before substituting into the Clausius-Clapeyron equation. Using ¬įC is a common KTU valuation trap that costs 1‚Äď2 marks per question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin: From Clapeyron to Clausius-Clapeyron

The general **Clapeyron equation** (1834) governs *any* first-order phase transition:

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_{\text{trs}}}{T\,\Delta V_{\text{trs}}}
$$

This equation is **exact** ‚ÄĒ it carries no approximations. However, in raw form it is a differential equation that is rarely directly useful. The Clausius-Clapeyron equation is the *engineering simplification* of this for the **vapor‚Äďliquid** and **solid‚Äďvapor** transitions.

## 2.2 The Three Stacked Assumptions

To move from Clapeyron to Clausius-Clapeyron, we layer three assumptions:

1. **Vapor is an ideal gas:** $V_g = \dfrac{RT}{P}$
2. **Molar volume of the condensed phase is negligible:** $V_{\text{liq}} \ll V_g$  (so $\Delta V \approx V_g$)
3. **Latent heat is approximately constant over the temperature range of interest** (a piecewise-constant assumption; for wider ranges, the Kirchhoff-corrected form must be used).

## 2.3 Engineering Significance

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this equation central to chemical engineering?**
> - Distillation column design requires accurate vapor-pressure curves for every component in the feed.
> - Safety relief-valve sizing for storage vessels depends on the *rate* at which vapor pressure rises with temperature.
> - Drying operations, refrigerant selection, and humidification calculations all rely on this relation.
> - It underpins the empirical **Antoine Equation** used in process simulators (the Antoine equation is essentially a curve-fitted, three-parameter descendant of Clausius-Clapeyron).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbol Key | When to Use |
|---|----------|------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{T\,\Delta V}$ | Clapeyron (exact, any phase change) | Whenever neither approximation is justified |
| 2 | $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{P\,\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R\,T^{2}}$ | Differential Clausius-Clapeyron | Derivation, slope of P‚ÄďT curve |
| 3 | $\ln P = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R\,T} + C$ | Linear (semilog) integrated form | Plotting $\ln P$ vs $1/T$ ‚ÄĒ gives straight line with slope $-\Delta H/R$ |
| 4 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Two-point integrated form | Finding unknown $P$ or $T$ given latent heat |
| 5 | $\ln P = A - \dfrac{B}{T}$ | Empirical linear form | Quick extrapolation of vapor-pressure data |
| 6 | $\ln P = A - \dfrac{B}{T+C}$ | **Antoine Equation** | Wide-range industrial vapor-pressure correlation |
| 7 | $\Delta H_{\text{vap}} = -R \cdot \dfrac{d(\ln P)}{d(1/T)}$ | Slope-based latent heat | Experimental determination from P‚ÄďT data |
| 8 | $\dfrac{d(\ln P)}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{RT^{2}}$ | Arrhenius-style rate form | Comparing with reaction kinetics analogies |

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** In equation (4), always write $\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ with $T_2$ first. Some textbooks use the reverse sign convention ‚ÄĒ losing one mark for the wrong order is a classic KTU pitfall.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Clausius-Clapeyron Equation

### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Start from the Clapeyron Equation

The general Clapeyron equation is obtained by combining the **Maxwell relation** with the **Clausius equality** at phase equilibrium. It applies to *any* first-order phase transition:

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} \;=\; \frac{\Delta S_{\text{trs}}}{\Delta V_{\text{trs}}} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_{\text{trs}}}{T\,\Delta V_{\text{trs}}}
$$

The last equality uses the reversible phase-equilibrium identity $\Delta S_{\text{trs}} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{trs}}}{T_{\text{trs}}}$.

### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply Assumption 1: Vapor Behaves as an Ideal Gas

For the vapor phase, the ideal gas law gives the molar volume:

$$
V_{g} \;=\; \frac{RT}{P}
$$

### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Apply Assumption 2: Neglect the Condensed-Phase Volume

For liquid‚Äďvapor equilibrium, $V_{\text{liq}} \ll V_g$ (typically $V_{\text{liq}}/V_g \sim 10^{-3}$ at the normal boiling point). Hence:

$$
\Delta V \;=\; V_g - V_{\text{liq}} \;\approx\; V_g \;=\; \frac{RT}{P}
$$

### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Substitute Back into the Clapeyron Equation

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{T\,\Delta V} \;\approx\; \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{T \cdot (RT/P)} \;=\; \frac{P\,\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R\,T^{2}}
$$

This is the **differential form** of the Clausius-Clapeyron equation.

### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Separate Variables

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{dP}{P} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R\,T^{2}}\,dT
$$

### Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Integrate Between Two States (1) and (2)

Applying Assumption 3 ($\Delta H_{\text{vap}} = $ constant over the interval):

$$
\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^{2}}
$$

$$
\Big[\ln P\Big]_{P_1}^{P_2} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\Big[-\frac{1}{T}\Big]_{T_1}^{T_2}
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2}-\frac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

This is the **integrated Clausius-Clapeyron equation** ‚ÄĒ the workhorse of vapor-pressure thermodynamics in chemical engineering practice.

### Step 7 ‚ÄĒ The "Slope Form" for Latent-Heat Determination

Rearranging the integrated equation into a straight-line form:

$$
\ln P \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T} \;+\; C
$$

A plot of $\ln P$ (y-axis) versus $1/T$ (x-axis) yields a straight line with:

$$
\text{slope} \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Delta H_{\text{vap}} \;=\; -R \cdot (\text{slope})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** This is exactly how experimental vapor-pressure data are reduced to obtain $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ in laboratory measurements. Students are routinely asked to compute $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ from tabulated $(T, P)$ data using a least-squares fit to this linearized form.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Illustration (KTU-Style 7-Mark Sub-Question)

**Problem:** The vapor pressure of water is **7.381 kPa** at **40 ¬įC** and **101.325 kPa** at **100 ¬įC**. Calculate the molar enthalpy of vaporization of water. Assume water vapor behaves ideally and the molar volume of liquid water is negligible.

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step A ‚ÄĒ Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$
T_1 = 40 + 273.15 = 313.15\ \text{K}
$$

$$
T_2 = 100 + 273.15 = 373.15\ \text{K}
$$

**Step B ‚ÄĒ Substitute into the integrated Clausius-Clapeyron equation:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2}-\frac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

**Step C ‚ÄĒ Compute the left-hand side (pressure ratio):**

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} \;=\; \frac{101.325}{7.381} \;=\; 13.728
$$

$$
\ln(13.728) \;=\; 2.6190
$$

**Step D ‚ÄĒ Compute the right-hand side bracket:**

$$
\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \;=\; \frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{313.15}
$$

$$
= \; 0.0026798 - 0.0031934 \;=\; -0.0005136\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step E ‚ÄĒ Solve for $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$:**

$$
2.6190 \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{8.314}\cdot(-0.0005136)
$$

$$
2.6190 \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}\cdot 0.0005136}{8.314}
$$

$$
\Delta H_{\text{vap}} \;=\; \frac{2.6190 \times 8.314}{0.0005136}
$$

$$
\Delta H_{\text{vap}} \;=\; \frac{21.778}{0.0005136} \;\approx\; 42{,}402\ \text{J¬∑mol}^{-1} \;\approx\; 42.4\ \text{kJ¬∑mol}^{-1}
$$

**Step F ‚ÄĒ Compare with the literature value:**

The accepted value for water at 100 ¬įC is **40.656 kJ¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ**. The discrepancy (~4%) arises because $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ actually decreases with temperature, violating our assumption of constant $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$. The Clausius-Clapeyron equation with constant $\Delta H$ therefore gives an *average* value over the temperature interval.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently forget the **negative sign** in the integrated equation. Sign errors here can yield a physically impossible negative $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$. Always cross-check: $P$ must rise with $T$, so $\ln(P_2/P_1)$ and $(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ should have **opposite signs**, and the equation must be self-consistent.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Derivation Chain ‚ÄĒ Block Diagram

The following block diagram maps the logical flow from the fundamental Clapeyron equation down to the integrated Clausius-Clapeyron form, including each assumption applied along the way.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["General Clapeyron Equation dP over dT equals Delta H over T Delta V"] --> B["Identify Phase Equilibrium V to L or V to S"]
    B --> C{"Is Vapor Phase Dominant?"}
    C -- No --> D["Keep Full Clapeyron Form Solid Solid Transitions"]
    C -- Yes --> E["Apply Assumption 1 Vapor Behaves as Ideal Gas Vg equals RT over P"]
    E --> F["Apply Assumption 2 V liquid negligible vs V gas Delta V approx Vg"]
    F --> G["Differential Clausius Clapeyron dP over dT equals P Delta H over R T squared"]
    G --> H{"Is Delta H approximately Constant?"}
    H -- No --> I["Use Kirchhoff Correction Cp difference included"]
    H -- Yes --> J["Separate Variables dP over P equals Delta H over R T squared dT"]
    J --> K["Integrate Between States 1 and 2"]
    K --> L["Integrated Clausius Clapeyron ln P2 over P1 equals minus Delta H over R times one over T2 minus one over T1"]
    L --> M["Linearized Form ln P equals A minus B over T"]
    M --> N["Applications Distillation Relief Valve Sizing Drying"]
    M --> O["Antoine Equation Empirical Three Parameter Form"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: How the Equation Is Used in Practice

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Data Block"]
        I1["Tabulated P vs T Data"]
        I2["Known Latent Heat Delta H vap"]
        I3["Normal Boiling Point at 101.325 kPa"]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE["Clausius Clapeyron Engine"]
        E1["Select Appropriate Form Differential or Integrated"]
        E2["Convert All Temperatures to Kelvin"]
        E3["Apply Assumption Audit Ideal Gas and Negligible Liquid Volume"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Engineering Outputs"]
        O1["Unknown Vapor Pressure at Given T"]
        O2["Boiling Point at Given P e.g. High Altitude or Vacuum"]
        O3["Average Latent Heat from P T Data Fit"]
        O4["Antoine Constants A B C"]
    end

    I1 --> E1
    I2 --> E1
    I3 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> O1
    E3 --> O2
    E3 --> O3
    E3 --> O4
```

## 4.3 P‚ÄďT Phase Boundary Schematic (Text-Based)

A schematic representation of the typical saturation curve (not to scale), showing the qualitative behaviour captured by the Clausius-Clapeyron equation:

```
        P (vapor pressure)
        ^
        |                              *
        |                          *
        |                      *
   P_c -|- - - - - - - - - - * - - - - - - - - Critical Point
        |                *
        |            *
        |        *
        |    *
        | *
   1atm-*-----------------------------> T (temperature)
        |  T_b
        |________________________________________
                                              T_c
```

- Curve starts at the **triple point** and rises steeply toward the **critical point**.
- The slope $\dfrac{dP}{dT}$ at any point is given by the differential Clausius-Clapeyron form.
- The normal boiling point $T_b$ is the temperature where this curve crosses the **1 atm (101.325 kPa)** horizontal line.

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level schematic is used because a faithful physical drawing of a phase boundary requires numerical axes (substance-specific). The architecture above captures the *information flow* that engineers actually use when applying the Clausius-Clapeyron equation to real design problems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the Clausius-Clapeyron equation and list any two assumptions made in its derivation.**

**Model Answer:**

The integrated Clausius-Clapeyron equation for liquid‚Äďvapor equilibrium is:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2}-\frac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

**Two key assumptions:**
1. The vapor phase obeys the **ideal gas law**, so $V_g = RT/P$.
2. The molar volume of the condensed (liquid) phase is **negligible** compared to that of the vapor, so $\Delta V \approx V_g$.
3. (Optional) The molar enthalpy of vaporization $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ is approximately **constant** over the temperature range considered.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why does the boiling point of water decrease at higher altitudes? Explain using the Clausius-Clapeyron equation.**

**Model Answer:**

The boiling point $T_b$ of a liquid is the temperature at which its saturation vapor pressure $P^{\text{sat}}$ equals the surrounding (ambient) pressure. As altitude increases, ambient pressure drops (e.g., ~70 kPa at 3000 m vs 101.325 kPa at sea level). From the Clausius-Clapeyron equation:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P^{\text{sat}}}{P^{\text{sat}}_1}\right) \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_b}-\frac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

For a fixed $P^{\text{sat}} = P_{\text{ambient}}$ (now reduced), the equation can be solved for $T_b$. A lower ambient pressure mathematically requires a **lower $T_b$** to satisfy the equality. Hence water boils at lower temperatures at high altitudes (e.g., ~90 ¬įC at 3 km elevation).

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[Internal Choice Format ‚ÄĒ KTU ESE Pattern]:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**a)** Starting from the Clapeyron equation, derive the integrated form of the Clausius-Clapeyron equation for a liquid‚Äďvapor system. Clearly state all assumptions used in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**b)** The vapor pressure of acetone is **200 mmHg** at **22.7 ¬įC** and **600 mmHg** at **49.1 ¬įC**. Determine:
- (i) The molar enthalpy of vaporization of acetone over this temperature range.
- (ii) The normal boiling point of acetone (i.e., the temperature at which $P^{\text{sat}} = 760$ mmHg). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation (7 marks):**

The derivation follows the seven-step sequence given in Section 3.1 of these notes. The examiner expects the following key checkpoints for full marks:

| Step | Checkpoint | Marks |
|------|------------|-------|
| 1 | State Clapeyron equation $dP/dT = \Delta H / (T \Delta V)$ | 1 |
| 2 | Apply ideal gas law $V_g = RT/P$ | 1 |
| 3 | State $\Delta V \approx V_g$ with justification | 1 |
| 4 | Substitute to obtain $dP/dT = P\Delta H / (RT^2)$ | 1 |
| 5 | Separate variables $dP/P = (\Delta H / RT^2)dT$ | 1 |
| 6 | Integrate between limits 1 and 2 (with $\Delta H$ constant) | 1 |
| 7 | Final integrated form + assumption of constant $\Delta H$ | 1 |

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Calculation of $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$:**

Convert temperatures to Kelvin:

$$
T_1 = 22.7 + 273.15 = 295.85\ \text{K}
$$

$$
T_2 = 49.1 + 273.15 = 322.25\ \text{K}
$$

Apply the integrated Clausius-Clapeyron equation:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2}-\frac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

LHS:

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} \;=\; \frac{600}{200} \;=\; 3.0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \ln(3.0) \;=\; 1.0986
$$

RHS bracket:

$$
\frac{1}{322.25} - \frac{1}{295.85} \;=\; 0.003103 - 0.003380 \;=\; -0.0002772\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

Solving for $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$:

$$
1.0986 \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{8.314}\cdot(-0.0002772)
$$

$$
\Delta H_{\text{vap}} \;=\; \frac{1.0986 \times 8.314}{0.0002772} \;=\; \frac{9.134}{0.0002772} \;\approx\; 32{,}950\ \text{J¬∑mol}^{-1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta H_{\text{vap}} \;\approx\; 32.95\ \text{kJ¬∑mol}^{-1}}
$$

The accepted literature value is **~31.3 kJ¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ**; the small discrepancy is due to the constant-$\Delta H$ approximation.

**(ii) Calculation of the Normal Boiling Point:**

Now use $P_1 = 200$ mmHg at $T_1 = 295.85$ K as the reference, and solve for $T_2$ when $P_2 = 760$ mmHg:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{760}{200}\right) \;=\; -\frac{32{,}950}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{T_2}-\frac{1}{295.85}\right)
$$

LHS:

$$
\ln(3.8) \;=\; 1.3350
$$

RHS coefficient:

$$
-\frac{32{,}950}{8.314} \;=\; -3963.0
$$

So:

$$
1.3350 \;=\; -3963.0\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{295.85}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{295.85} \;=\; \frac{1.3350}{-3963.0} \;=\; -3.368 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{T_2} \;=\; 0.003380 - 0.0003368 \;=\; 0.003043\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
T_2 \;=\; \frac{1}{0.003043} \;\approx\; 328.5\ \text{K} \;=\; 55.4\ ^\circ\text{C}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_b \;\approx\; 55.4\ ^\circ\text{C}}
$$

(Literature value: 56.0 ¬įC ‚ÄĒ excellent agreement.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**a)** The normal boiling point of benzene is **80.1 ¬įC**, and its molar enthalpy of vaporization at this temperature is **30.8 kJ¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ**. Estimate the vapor pressure of benzene at:
- (i) 50 ¬įC, and
- (ii) 100 ¬įC.

Use the Clausius-Clapeyron equation with the assumption of constant $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A chemical engineer needs to estimate the average latent heat of vaporization of a new refrigerant. The following vapor-pressure data were obtained experimentally:

| Temperature (¬įC) | Vapor Pressure (kPa) |
|------------------|---------------------|
| -10              | 125                 |
| 0                | 250                 |
| 20               | 670                 |

Using the linearized Clausius-Clapeyron form $\ln P = A - B/T$ (with $T$ in Kelvin), determine $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ by:
- (i) Plotting a $\ln P$ vs $1/T$ graph and computing the slope, **or**
- (ii) Solving the equation pairwise and averaging.

Report the average molar enthalpy of vaporization. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Reference state (normal boiling point):

$$
P_1 = 760\ \text{mmHg} = 101.325\ \text{kPa},\quad T_1 = 80.1 + 273.15 = 353.25\ \text{K}
$$

$$
\Delta H_{\text{vap}} = 30.8\ \text{kJ¬∑mol}^{-1} = 30{,}800\ \text{J¬∑mol}^{-1}
$$

**(i) At $T_2 = 50\ ^\circ\text{C} = 323.15$ K:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{101.325}\right) \;=\; -\frac{30{,}800}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{323.15}-\frac{1}{353.25}\right)
$$

Compute the bracket:

$$
\frac{1}{323.15} - \frac{1}{353.25} \;=\; 0.003094 - 0.002831 \;=\; 0.0002638\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

RHS:

$$
-\frac{30{,}800}{8.314}\cdot(0.0002638) \;=\; -3704.6 \times 0.0002638 \;=\; -0.9772
$$

So:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{101.325}\right) \;=\; -0.9772
$$

$$
\frac{P_2}{101.325} \;=\; e^{-0.9772} \;=\; 0.3762
$$

$$
\boxed{P_2 \;\approx\; 38.1\ \text{kPa}}
$$

(Literature: ~36 kPa ‚ÄĒ good agreement.)

**(ii) At $T_2 = 100\ ^\circ\text{C} = 373.15$ K:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{101.325}\right) \;=\; -\frac{30{,}800}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{373.15}-\frac{1}{353.25}\right)
$$

Compute the bracket:

$$
\frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{353.25} \;=\; 0.002680 - 0.002831 \;=\; -0.0001512\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

RHS:

$$
-3704.6 \times (-0.0001512) \;=\; +0.5601
$$

So:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{101.325}\right) \;=\; +0.5601
$$

$$
\frac{P_2}{101.325} \;=\; e^{0.5601} \;=\; 1.751
$$

$$
\boxed{P_2 \;\approx\; 177.4\ \text{kPa}}
$$

(Literature: ~180 kPa ‚ÄĒ excellent agreement.)

---

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Data Analysis Solution (7 marks):**

First, convert all data to $(T \text{ in K},\ \ln P)$:

| $T$ (¬įC) | $T$ (K) | $1/T$ (K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ) | $P$ (kPa) | $\ln P$ |
|-----------|---------|---------------|-----------|---------|
| -10       | 263.15  | $3.800 \times 10^{-3}$ | 125  | 4.8283 |
| 0         | 273.15  | $3.661 \times 10^{-3}$ | 250  | 5.5215 |
| 20        | 293.15  | $3.411 \times 10^{-3}$ | 670  | 6.5073 |

**(i) Slope from $\ln P$ vs $1/T$ plot (least-squares fit):**

Let $x = 1/T$ and $y = \ln P$. Compute the slope:

$$
\text{slope} = \frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}
$$

$\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{3}(3.800 + 3.661 + 3.411)\times 10^{-3} = 3.624 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}$

$\bar{y} = \dfrac{1}{3}(4.8283 + 5.5215 + 6.5073) = 5.6190$

| $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | Product | $(x_i-\bar{x})^2$ |
|------------------|------------------|---------|---------------------|
| $+0.176\times10^{-3}$ | $-0.7907$ | $-1.392\times10^{-4}$ | $3.10\times10^{-8}$ |
| $+0.037\times10^{-3}$ | $-0.0975$ | $-3.61\times10^{-6}$  | $1.37\times10^{-9}$ |
| $-0.213\times10^{-3}$ | $+0.8883$ | $-1.892\times10^{-4}$ | $4.54\times10^{-8}$ |
| **Sum**          |                | $-3.32\times10^{-4}$  | $7.78\times10^{-8}$ |

$$
\text{slope} \;=\; \frac{-3.32 \times 10^{-4}}{7.78 \times 10^{-8}} \;=\; -4267\ \text{K}
$$

Then:

$$
\Delta H_{\text{vap}} \;=\; -R\cdot(\text{slope}) \;=\; -8.314 \times (-4267) \;\approx\; 35{,}475\ \text{J¬∑mol}^{-1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta H_{\text{vap}} \;\approx\; 35.5\ \text{kJ¬∑mol}^{-1}}
$$

**(ii) Pairwise verification:**

Using $P_1 = 125$ kPa, $T_1 = 263.15$ K and $P_2 = 670$ kPa, $T_2 = 293.15$ K:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{670}{125}\right) \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{293.15}-\frac{1}{263.15}\right)
$$

$$
\ln(5.36) = 1.6790
$$

$$
\frac{1}{293.15} - \frac{1}{263.15} = -3.892 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\Delta H_{\text{vap}} = \frac{1.6790 \times 8.314}{3.892 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 35{,}870\ \text{J¬∑mol}^{-1}
$$

Similarly for the (0 ¬įC, 20 ¬įC) pair:

$$
\Delta H_{\text{vap}} \approx 34{,}980\ \text{J¬∑mol}^{-1}
$$

Average: $\Delta H_{\text{vap}} \approx 35.4\ \text{kJ¬∑mol}^{-1}$ ‚ÄĒ consistent with the regression result.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚ÄĒ Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Temperature in ¬įC instead of K:** The single most frequent mistake. This alone costs 2‚Äď3 marks per numerical.
> 2. **Sign convention error:** Forgetting the negative sign in the integrated equation gives a negative $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ ‚ÄĒ physically impossible for vaporization.
> 3. **Pressure unit mixing:** mmHg, bar, kPa, atm are all used in different problems. Use ratios where possible, since $P_2/P_1$ is **dimensionless** and unit-independent.
> 4. **Confusing Clapeyron with Clausius-Clapeyron:** Clapeyron is exact but involves $\Delta V$ directly; Clausius-Clapeyron is the integrated, ideal-gas-assisted form.
> 5. **Skipping the assumption statement:** Even if the derivation is mathematically correct, full marks require an *explicit* list of assumptions. Examiners deduct 1‚Äď2 marks routinely.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Clausius-Clapeyron equation** gives the vapor pressure of a pure substance as a function of temperature along a phase equilibrium curve.
- **The integrated form** is:
  $\ln(P_2/P_1) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$
- **Three assumptions** must always be stated explicitly: (i) ideal gas vapor, (ii) negligible liquid/solid molar volume, (iii) constant $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ over the temperature range.
- **Slope form:** A plot of $\ln P$ vs $1/T$ yields a straight line with slope $-\Delta H_{\text{vap}}/R$ ‚ÄĒ the experimental basis for finding latent heats.
- **Boiling point** = temperature at which $P^{\text{sat}} = P_{\text{ambient}}$; the normal boiling point is specifically at **101.325 kPa**.
- **Temperature must always be in Kelvin** ‚ÄĒ the single most common KTU valuation error.
- **The Antoine equation** $\ln P = A - B/(T+C)$ is the empirical, three-parameter industrial descendant of Clausius-Clapeyron.
- **For wide temperature ranges**, $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ varies with $T$ and the simple integrated form loses accuracy; the Kirchhoff equation $\Delta H(T) = \Delta H(T_0) + \Delta C_p(T - T_0)$ must be incorporated.
- **Engineering applications:** Distillation column design, relief-valve sizing, drying calculations, humidification, refrigeration cycle analysis, and process safety (especially runaway reactions where vapor pressure rises rapidly with temperature).
- **Valuation mnemonic:** *"Ideal gas, tiny liquid, constant heat ‚ÄĒ three assumptions you must repeat."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Entropy-Heat Capacity Relationships: Equations for Entropy, Internal Energy, and Enthalpy in Terms of Measurable Quantities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Entropy-Heat Capacity Relationships: Equations for Entropy, Internal Energy, and Enthalpy in Terms of Measurable Quantities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Entropy-Heat Capacity Relationships** are a set of exact differential equations that express the changes in entropy ($dS$), internal energy ($dU$), and enthalpy ($dH$) of a pure substance as functions of **measurable macroscopic quantities** ‚ÄĒ temperature ($T$), pressure ($P$), volume ($V$), and heat capacities ($C_P$ and $C_V$).

These relationships form the mathematical bridge between the abstract Second Law of Thermodynamics and the experimentally accessible quantities available in a chemical engineering laboratory. They allow a process engineer to quantitatively track property changes along any reversible or irreversible path by integrating along a convenient **reversible path** that connects the initial and final thermodynamic states.

For a **closed system** of a pure substance with a single phase, the fundamental property relations (derived from the combined First and Second Laws) are:

$$dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$$

$$dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

$$dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$$

$$dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV$$

These four equations, together with the **Maxwell Relations**, are the foundation upon which every entropy-heat capacity equation is built.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The Bank Account of Energy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a thermodynamic system as a personal bank account:**
> - **Enthalpy ($H$)** is your total *current balance* (heat content).
> - **Entropy ($S$)** is the *measure of disorder* of your financial records ‚ÄĒ how scattered your transactions are.
> - **Heat Capacity ($C_P$)** is the *rate at which your balance grows* when you deposit money at a constant purchasing power (constant pressure).
> - **Temperature ($T$)** is the *economic activity level* ‚ÄĒ how "hot" the market is.

Just as a banker can predict your final balance by knowing your *deposit rate* and the *time* it was deposited, a chemical engineer can predict the final entropy, internal energy, or enthalpy by knowing the heat capacities and the path ($T$, $P$, $V$) the system follows. The mathematical relationships we derive today are the "rules" of this thermodynamic bank.

### 1.3 Key Measurable Quantities Used

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| $C_P$ | Heat capacity at constant pressure | J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ |
| $C_V$ | Heat capacity at constant volume | J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ |
| $T$ | Absolute temperature | **K (Kelvin)** |
| $P$ | Absolute pressure | **Pa (Pascal)** |
| $V$ | Specific/Molar volume | m¬≥¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant = **8.314 J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ** | J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Logic

The derivation strategy proceeds in three logical layers:

**Layer 1: Express $dS$ in terms of $T$ and a second independent variable.**

Since $S = S(T, P)$ for a closed system, the total differential is:

$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

The first partial derivative is connected to $C_P$ by definition:

$$\delta Q_{rev,P} = C_P\,dT = T\,dS \quad\Rightarrow\quad \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{C_P}{T}$$

The second partial derivative is obtained using the **Maxwell Relation**:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Layer 2: For an ideal gas**, $V = RT/P$, so:

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{R}{P}$$

**Layer 3: Combine** the two layers to obtain the working equations.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Maxwell Relations:** They are mathematical consequences of the fact that $dS$, $dU$, $dH$, $dG$, and $dA$ are *exact differentials*. For any exact differential $dZ = M\,dx + N\,dy$, we must have $\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$. This is the **Euler reciprocity relation** ‚ÄĒ the deep mathematical "why" that makes Maxwell relations true.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **most frequently asked equations** in KTU University Examinations under this topic. Memorize the boxed relations.

| # | Equation | Applicable To | Variables Held Variable |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $dS = \dfrac{C_P}{T}dT - \dfrac{R}{P}dP$ | **Ideal Gas** | ‚ÄĒ |
| 2 | $dS = \dfrac{C_V}{T}dT + \dfrac{R}{V}dV$ | **Ideal Gas** | ‚ÄĒ |
| 3 | $dS = \dfrac{C_P}{T}dT - \alpha\,V\,dP$ | Incompressible / Real Substance | ‚ÄĒ |
| 4 | $\Delta S = \displaystyle\int_{T_1}^{T_2}\dfrac{C_P}{T}dT - R\ln\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | Ideal Gas, $\Delta T \neq 0$, $\Delta P \neq 0$ | ‚ÄĒ |
| 5 | $\Delta H = \displaystyle\int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_P\,dT$ | Any substance (constant $P$ process) | $P$ |
| 6 | $\Delta U = \displaystyle\int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_V\,dT$ | Ideal gas (any path), or constant-$V$ process | $V$ |
| 7 | $C_P - C_V = T\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V\left(\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | Real substances (general) | ‚ÄĒ |
| 8 | $C_P - C_V = R$ (for ideal gas) | Ideal Gas | ‚ÄĒ |
| 9 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial C_P}{\partial P}\right)_T = -T\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial T^2}\right)_P$ | Real substances | $T$ |
| 10 | $C_P = a + bT + cT^2 + dT^{-2}$ | Empirical (gases at moderate $T$) | ‚ÄĒ |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of Compressors and Turbines:** Engineers compute $\Delta S$ and $\Delta H$ across each stage to estimate isentropic efficiency.
- **Reactor Heat Integration:** Knowing $C_P(T)$ for reactants/products allows calculation of heat duties from sensible-heat integrals.
- **Cryogenic Storage & LNG Processing:** Entropy changes during cooling from 300 K to 111 K are evaluated using $\int (C_P/T)\,dT$ for multi-component mixtures.
- **Steam Power Cycles (Rankine, Brayton):** Enthalpy changes across boiler, turbine, condenser, and pump are computed from heat-capacity data.
- **Distillation Column Design:** Entropy generation calculations (Second-Law analysis) require accurate $C_P$ values to estimate thermodynamic losses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: $dS$ in Terms of $C_P$, $T$, and $P$ (Ideal Gas)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important derivation** in Module 2. KTU examiners frequently test it for 7 full marks.

**Starting Point:** $S$ is a state function of $T$ and $P$:

$$S = S(T, P)$$

**Step 1:** Take the total exact differential:

$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

**Step 2:** From the definition of $C_P$:

$$\delta Q_{rev,P} = C_P\,dT = T\,dS \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{C_P}{T}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Maxwell relation derived from $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 4:** Substitute Steps 2 and 3 into Step 1:

$$dS = \frac{C_P}{T}\,dT - \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$$

**Step 5:** For an **ideal gas**, $PV = RT$, hence $V = RT/P$. Differentiating with respect to $T$ at constant $P$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{R}{P}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute this result back:

$$\boxed{\,dS = \frac{C_P}{T}\,dT - \frac{R}{P}\,dP\,}$$

This is the **fundamental entropy-heat capacity relation for an ideal gas in $T$‚Äď$P$ coordinates**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: $dS$ in Terms of $C_V$, $T$, and $V$ (Ideal Gas)

**Step 1:** Express $S = S(T, V)$:

$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$

**Step 2:** From the definition of $C_V$:

$$\delta Q_{rev,V} = C_V\,dT = T\,dS \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{C_V}{T}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Maxwell relation derived from $dA = -S\,dT - P\,dV$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

**Step 4:** For an **ideal gas**, $P = RT/V$, so:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{R}{V}$$

**Step 5:** Combine all results:

$$\boxed{\,dS = \frac{C_V}{T}\,dT + \frac{R}{V}\,dV\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Integrated $\Delta S$ for an Ideal Gas (Constant $C_P$)

**Assumption:** $C_P$ is constant over the temperature range $[T_1, T_2]$.

**Starting equation:**

$$dS = \frac{C_P}{T}\,dT - \frac{R}{P}\,dP$$

**Step 1:** Integrate from State 1 to State 2:

$$\int_{S_1}^{S_2} dS = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{C_P}{T}\,dT - \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{R}{P}\,dP$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate each integral (since $C_P$, $R$ are constant):

$$\Delta S = C_P \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Using logarithm properties $a\ln(x) = \ln(x^a)$ and $\ln(A) - \ln(B) = \ln(A/B)$:

$$\boxed{\,\Delta S = \ln\!\left[\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{C_P} \cdot \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{-R}\right] = \ln\!\left[\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{C_P} \cdot \left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{R}\right]\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** If $C_P$ varies with $T$, replace $C_P\ln(T_2/T_1)$ with $\displaystyle\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{C_P(T)}{T}\,dT$. This integral cannot be evaluated in closed form unless $C_P(T)$ is given as a polynomial (e.g., $C_P = a + bT + cT^2 + dT^{-2}$).

---

### 3.4 Derivation IV: $C_P - C_V$ Relation for a General Substance

**Step 1:** Define $H = U + PV$. Differentiate:

$$dH = dU + P\,dV + V\,dP$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$ (First + Second Law):

$$dH = T\,dS + V\,dP$$

**Step 3:** At constant pressure ($dP = 0$):

$$dH\big|_{P} = T\,dS\big|_{P} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P = C_P$$

**Step 4:** At constant volume ($dV = 0$):

$$dU\big|_{V} = T\,dS\big|_{V} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V = C_V$$

**Step 5:** Use the identity $C_P - C_V = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ (derivable from triple-product rule and Maxwell relations). For an **ideal gas**:

$$C_P - C_V = T \cdot \frac{R}{V} \cdot \frac{R}{P} \cdot \frac{V}{R} = R$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation V: $\Delta H$ Using Empirical $C_P$ Polynomial

**Given:** $C_P = a + bT + cT^2 + dT^{-2}$ (J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ)

**Equation to integrate:**

$$\Delta H = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_P\,dT = \int_{T_1}^{T_2}\left(a + bT + cT^2 + dT^{-2}\right)dT$$

**Term-by-term integration:**

$$\Delta H = a(T_2 - T_1) + \frac{b}{2}(T_2^2 - T_1^2) + \frac{c}{3}(T_2^3 - T_1^3) - d\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

This compact result is the **standard format** expected in KTU numerical problems involving sensible-heat calculations.

---

### 3.6 Derivation VI: $\Delta S$ Using Empirical $C_P$ Polynomial

**Starting point:**

$$\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2}\frac{C_P}{T}\,dT - R\ln\frac{P_2}{P_1}$$

**Step 1:** Expand $C_P/T$:

$$\frac{C_P}{T} = \frac{a}{T} + b + cT + \frac{d}{T^3}$$

**Step 2:** Integrate term by term:

$$\Delta S = a\ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} + b(T_2 - T_1) + \frac{c}{2}(T_2^2 - T_1^2) - \frac{d}{2}\left(\frac{1}{T_2^2} - \frac{1}{T_1^2}\right) - R\ln\frac{P_2}{P_1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note carefully** the difference in the $d$-term between $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$:
> - In $\Delta H$: the term is $-d\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$
> - In $\Delta S$: the term is $-\frac{d}{2}\left(\frac{1}{T_2^2} - \frac{1}{T_1^2}\right)$

This subtle difference is a **classic KTU trap** that students frequently miss.

---

### 3.7 Derivation VII: Incompressible Substance (Liquid/Solid)

**Assumption:** $C_P \approx C_V = C$ and $V \approx$ constant.

**Step 1:** From Derivation II's exact differential but treating $V$ as constant:

$$dS = \frac{C}{T}\,dT \quad\text{(since } dV = 0\text{)}$$

**Step 2:** Integrate:

$$\boxed{\,\Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2}\frac{C}{T}\,dT = C\ln\frac{T_2}{T_1}\,}$$

**Step 3:** The corresponding energy change (with $dV = 0$, so $P\,dV = 0$):

$$dU = C\,dT \quad\Rightarrow\quad \Delta U = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} C\,dT$$

This is the basis for sensible-heat calculations in liquid heating/cooling operations (e.g., reactor preheating, condenser cooling).

---

### 3.8 Analytical Master Matrix ‚ÄĒ "When to Use What"

| Process Condition | $dS$ Expression | $dU$ Expression | $dH$ Expression |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ideal gas, $T$ and $P$ change** | $\frac{C_P}{T}dT - \frac{R}{P}dP$ | $C_V\,dT$ | $C_P\,dT$ |
| **Ideal gas, $T$ and $V$ change** | $\frac{C_V}{T}dT + \frac{R}{V}dV$ | $C_V\,dT$ | $C_P\,dT$ |
| **Isothermal ideal gas** | $-R\ln\frac{P_2}{P_1}$ or $R\ln\frac{V_2}{V_1}$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| **Isobaric ideal gas** | $C_P\ln\frac{T_2}{T_1}$ | $C_V(T_2-T_1)$ | $C_P(T_2-T_1)$ |
| **Isochoric ideal gas** | $C_V\ln\frac{T_2}{T_1}$ | $C_V(T_2-T_1)$ | $C_P(T_2-T_1)$ |
| **Incompressible liquid** | $C\ln\frac{T_2}{T_1}$ | $C(T_2-T_1)$ | $\approx C(T_2-T_1)$ + $V\Delta P$ |
| **Adiabatic reversible (isentropic)** | $0$ | $C_V\,dT$ | $C_P\,dT$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Roadmap ‚ÄĒ From Laws to Working Equations

The following flowchart maps the logical chain from the fundamental postulates of thermodynamics to the final working equations used in engineering practice.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["First Law: dU = dQ - dW"]:::law
    B["Second Law: dS >= dQ_rev / T"]:::law
    C["Combined Law: dU = T dS - P dV"]:::core
    D["Define H = U + PV"]:::core
    E["Define A = U - TS"]:::core
    F["Define G = H - TS"]:::core
    G["dH = T dS + V dP"]:::core
    H["dA = -S dT - P dV"]:::core
    I["dG = -S dT + V dP"]:::core
    J["Euler Reciprocity (exact differentials)"]:::bridge
    K["Four Maxwell Relations"]:::bridge
    L["C_P / T = partial S over partial T at P"]:::result
    M["partial S over partial P at T = - partial V over partial T at P"]:::result
    N["dS = C_P/T dT - R/P dP (Ideal Gas)"]:::result
    O["dS = C_V/T dT + R/V dV (Ideal Gas)"]:::result
    P["Delta S integral and Delta H integral"]:::result

    A --> C
    B --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    C --> F
    D --> G
    E --> H
    F --> I
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K
    K --> L
    K --> M
    L --> N
    M --> N
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> P
    O --> P

    classDef law fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#e5f0ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bridge fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture ‚ÄĒ Property-Change Calculator

The block diagram below illustrates how a chemical engineer conceptually "computes" property changes for an ideal gas moving from State 1 to State 2 across any combination of temperature and pressure changes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1["State 1: T1, P1, V1"]:::in
    IN2["State 2: T2, P2, V2"]:::in
    IN3["Heat Capacity Data: C_P = a + bT + cT2 + dT-2"]:::in
    PATH["Path Selection Module"]:::logic
    TH["Thermal Branch: integrate C_P/T dT"]:::calc
    ME["Mechanical Branch: integrate R/P dP"]:::calc
    SUM["Entropy Combiner: dS thermal minus dS mechanical"]:::calc
    HSENS["Sensible Heat Module: integrate C_P dT"]:::calc
    HPRESS["Pressure Correction Module: V dP term"]:::calc
    HOUT["Delta H Output"]:::out
    SOUT["Delta S Output"]:::out
    UOUT["Delta U Output"]:::out
    UMOD["Internal Energy Module: integrate C_V dT"]:::calc

    IN1 --> PATH
    IN2 --> PATH
    IN3 --> HSENS
    IN3 --> TH
    PATH --> TH
    PATH --> ME
    TH --> SUM
    ME --> SUM
    SUM --> SOUT
    HSENS --> HOUT
    HPRESS --> HOUT
    IN3 --> UMOD
    PATH --> UMOD
    UMOD --> UOUT

    classDef in fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#fff0b3,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#cc3300,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart ‚ÄĒ Choosing the Right Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is the substance an ideal gas?"}:::decision
    Q2{"Are T and P the independent variables?"}:::decision
    Q3{"Are T and V the independent variables?"}:::decision
    Q4{"Is C_P constant over the range?"}:::decision
    R1["Use dS = C_P/T dT - R/P dP"]:::result2
    R2["Use dS = C_V/T dT + R/V dV"]:::result2
    R3["Delta S = C_P ln T2/T1 - R ln P2/P1"]:::result2
    R4["Delta S = integral C_P/T dT (numerical/polynomial)"]:::result2
    R5["Use dS = C/T dT with C approx C_P"]:::result2

    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2
    Q1 -->|No| INCOM["Incompressible: V approx const, C = C_P approx C_V"]:::result2
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q4
    Q2 -->|No| Q3
    Q3 -->|Yes| R2
    Q4 -->|Yes| R3
    Q4 -->|No| R4
    INCOM --> R5

    classDef decision fill:#ffe5b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef result2 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**

> State the Maxwell relation that is used in the derivation of the entropy change expression for an ideal gas in terms of $T$ and $P$. Using this, derive the relation $dS = \dfrac{C_P}{T}\,dT - \dfrac{R}{P}\,dP$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The required Maxwell relation, derived from $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$, is:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Derivation (sketch):** Start with $S = S(T, P)$. Write the total differential, then identify $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P = C_P / T$ from the definition of $C_P$. For an ideal gas, $V = RT/P$, giving $\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = R/P$. Substituting into the total differential yields $dS = (C_P/T)dT - (R/P)dP$. **(Full marks: state the Maxwell relation, write the total differential, and substitute the ideal-gas term.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, Sessional-II PYQ]**

> For an ideal gas, derive the relation $C_P - C_V = R$. Mention the Maxwell relations used.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Starting from the definitions of $C_P$ and $C_V$:

$$C_P = \left(\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right)_P = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P, \quad C_V = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_V$$

Apply the relation $C_P - C_V = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$, which itself uses **two Maxwell relations**. For an ideal gas ($P = RT/V$):

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V = \frac{R}{V}, \quad \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{R}{P}$$

Therefore:

$$C_P - C_V = T \cdot \frac{R}{V} \cdot \frac{R}{P} = T \cdot \frac{R}{V} \cdot \frac{R \cdot V}{RT} = R$$

$$\boxed{C_P - C_V = R}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **frequently lose 2‚Äď3 marks** by forgetting the sign in the Maxwell relation: $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ ‚ÄĒ the **negative sign is mandatory**.
> - Many students confuse the $\Delta H$ polynomial integration with the $\Delta S$ polynomial integration. The $dT^{-2}$ term integrates to $-d/T$ for $\Delta H$ but to $-d/(2T^2)$ for $\Delta S$.
> - Forgetting to **convert $C_P$ units** (e.g., from kJ¬∑kmol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ to J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ) before using $R = 8.314$ J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ is a 1-mark killer.
> - Always specify the **reference state** when using the empirical $C_P$ polynomial ‚ÄĒ polynomials are *not* valid at low temperatures (typically not below 298 K without special handling).
> - Showing the **starting point** of every derivation (e.g., "Since $S$ is a state function of $T$ and $P$...") earns the first mark ‚ÄĒ most students skip this.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the change in entropy of an ideal gas in terms of measurable quantities when both temperature and pressure change. Assume $C_P$ is constant. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two moles of an ideal gas with $C_P = 29.1$ J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ are compressed isothermally at 400 K from 100 kPa to 600 kPa. Calculate $\Delta S$, $\Delta U$, and $\Delta H$ for the process. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Since entropy is a state function, $S = S(T, P)$. Hence:

$$dS = \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** At constant pressure, $C_P = T\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P$, so:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{C_P}{T}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** From $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$ and Euler reciprocity:

$$\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** For an ideal gas, $V = RT/P$, so $\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P = R/P$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substituting:

$$dS = \frac{C_P}{T}dT - \frac{R}{P}dP$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Integrating from State 1 to State 2 (with $C_P$ constant):

$$\boxed{\Delta S = C_P \ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} - R \ln\frac{P_2}{P_1}}$$

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $n = 2$ mol, $C_P = 29.1$ J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ, $T_1 = T_2 = 400$ K, $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $P_2 = 600$ kPa.

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Since the process is **isothermal** for an ideal gas:

$$\Delta U = 0, \quad \Delta H = 0$$

*(Reason: $U$ and $H$ depend only on $T$ for an ideal gas, hence $\Delta T = 0 \Rightarrow \Delta U = \Delta H = 0$.)*

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Calculate $\Delta S$:

$$\Delta S = n\left[C_P \ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} - R \ln\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right]$$

$$\Delta S = 2\left[29.1 \cdot \ln(1) - 8.314 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{600}{100}\right)\right]$$

$$\Delta S = 2\left[0 - 8.314 \cdot \ln(6)\right] = 2 \cdot (-8.314) \cdot 1.7918$$

$$\Delta S = -29.79 \text{ J¬∑K}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S = -29.79 \text{ J¬∑K}^{-1}, \quad \Delta U = 0, \quad \Delta H = 0}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The negative $\Delta S$ indicates the gas has become more ordered (compressed). The energy changes are zero because temperature is constant.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** The molar heat capacity of nitrogen at constant pressure is given by the empirical equation:

$$C_P \text{ (J¬∑mol}^{-1}\text{¬∑K}^{-1}\text{)} = 29.00 + 0.2199 \times 10^{-2}\,T + 0.5723 \times 10^{-5}\,T^2 - \frac{2.871 \times 10^{6}}{T^2}$$

Calculate the change in entropy when 5 mol of N‚āā is heated from 300 K to 1000 K at a constant pressure of 1 atm. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for $\Delta H$ of an ideal gas when $C_P$ is given by $C_P = a + bT + cT^2 + dT^{-2}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** The entropy change for an ideal gas at constant pressure:

$$\Delta S = n\int_{T_1}^{T_2}\frac{C_P(T)}{T}\,dT$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute $C_P(T)/T$:

$$\frac{C_P}{T} = \frac{29.00}{T} + 0.2199 \times 10^{-2} + 0.5723 \times 10^{-5}\,T - \frac{2.871 \times 10^{6}}{T^3}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Integrate term by term:

$$\Delta S = n\left[29.00 \ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} + 0.2199 \times 10^{-2}(T_2 - T_1) + \frac{0.5723 \times 10^{-5}}{2}(T_2^2 - T_1^2) + \frac{2.871 \times 10^{6}}{2}\left(\frac{1}{T_2^2} - \frac{1}{T_1^2}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substitute the numerical values (n = 5, $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_2 = 1000$ K):

- Term 1: $29.00 \cdot \ln(1000/300) = 29.00 \cdot 1.2040 = 34.92$ J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ
- Term 2: $0.2199 \times 10^{-2} \cdot 700 = 1.539$ J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ
- Term 3: $(0.5723 \times 10^{-5}/2) \cdot (10^6 - 9 \times 10^4) = 2.609$ J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ
- Term 4: $(2.871 \times 10^{6}/2) \cdot (10^{-6} - 1.111 \times 10^{-5}) = (1.4355 \times 10^6) \cdot (-1.011 \times 10^{-5}) = -14.51$ J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ

**Sum per mole:** $34.92 + 1.54 + 2.61 - 14.51 = 24.56$ J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ

**Total for 5 mol:** $\boxed{\Delta S = 5 \times 24.56 = 122.8 \text{ J¬∑K}^{-1}}$

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** For an ideal gas, $\Delta H$ depends only on $T$:

$$\Delta H = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_P\,dT$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute $C_P$:

$$\Delta H = \int_{T_1}^{T_2}\left(a + bT + cT^2 + dT^{-2}\right)dT$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Evaluate the integral:

$$\boxed{\Delta H = a(T_2 - T_1) + \frac{b}{2}(T_2^2 - T_1^2) + \frac{c}{3}(T_2^3 - T_1^3) - d\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)}$$

**Note:** For a real ideal gas, the path between $T_1$ and $T_2$ is irrelevant because enthalpy is a state function and depends only on $T$ for an ideal gas.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must memorize for KTU exams):**

- **Core Equations to Memorize:**
  - $dS = (C_P/T)dT - (R/P)dP$ ‚ÄĒ Ideal gas in $T,P$ coordinates
  - $dS = (C_V/T)dT + (R/V)dV$ ‚ÄĒ Ideal gas in $T,V$ coordinates
  - $\Delta S = C_P \ln(T_2/T_1) - R \ln(P_2/P_1)$ ‚ÄĒ Constant $C_P$ ideal gas
  - $\Delta H = \int C_P\,dT$ ‚ÄĒ Any process (ideal gas); for any substance at constant $P$
  - $\Delta U = \int C_V\,dT$ ‚ÄĒ Ideal gas (any path); any substance at constant $V$
  - $C_P - C_V = R$ ‚ÄĒ Ideal gas identity

- **Maxwell Relations (must derive from Euler reciprocity):**
  - From $dG$: $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$ ‚Üź **most used**
  - From $dA$: $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial V}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_V$
  - From $dH$: $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_S = \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial S}\right)_P$
  - From $dU$: $\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial V}\right)_S = -\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial S}\right)_V$

- **Key Empirical Polynomial Form:** $C_P = a + bT + cT^2 + dT^{-2}$
  - $\Delta H$ integration: $a\Delta T + (b/2)\Delta T^2 + (c/3)\Delta T^3 - d\Delta(1/T)$
  - $\Delta S$ integration: $a\Delta \ln T + b\Delta T + (c/2)\Delta T^2 - (d/2)\Delta(1/T^2)$

- **Path Independence Principle:** Since $U$, $H$, $S$ are state functions, choose the *easiest reversible path* (not necessarily the actual process path) to compute changes.

- **Ideal Gas Shortcuts:**
  - $U = U(T)$ only
  - $H = H(T)$ only
  - $\Delta U = 0$ and $\Delta H = 0$ for any isothermal ideal-gas process

- **Incompressible Substance Shortcuts:**
  - $C_P \approx C_V = C$
  - $V \approx$ constant, so $P\,dV \approx 0$
  - $\Delta S = C\ln(T_2/T_1)$ and $\Delta U = \Delta H = C(T_2 - T_1)$

- **Common Unit Conversions to Remember:**
  - $R = 8.314$ J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ = **8.314 √ó 10‚ĀĽ¬≤ kJ¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ** = 1.987 cal¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ = 1.986 Btu¬∑lbmol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑¬įR‚ĀĽ¬Ļ
  - 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar

- **Negative Entropy Change:** Indicates **compression / ordering**. Positive indicates **expansion / disordering**.

- **Valuation Traps:**
  1. Missing the negative sign in $\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial P}\right)_T$ Maxwell relation.
  2. Confusing $-d(1/T)$ with $-d/(2T^2)$ between $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$.
  3. Using $R = 8.314$ when data is in kJ units.
  4. Forgetting to multiply by the number of moles ($n$) in the final answer.
  5. Skipping the justification "**since $S$ is a state function of $T$ and $P$**" at the start of a derivation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation** is one of the most fundamental relations in classical and chemical thermodynamics, named after the American physicist **Josiah Willard Gibbs** and the German physicist **Hermann von Helmholtz**. It quantitatively establishes how the **Gibbs free energy (G)** or the **Helmholtz free energy (A)** of a thermodynamic system varies with temperature at constant pressure (or volume).

In the context of **Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics (PEPOT633)**, the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation is the bridge that connects the *temperature sensitivity* of the equilibrium constant ($K$) of a chemical reaction to the *enthalpy change* ($\Delta H$) of that reaction. Without this equation, we cannot compute how reaction equilibria shift with temperature in chemical reactors, distillation columns, or phase-change equipment.

The general mathematical statements of the equation are:

$$ \left[ \frac{\partial \left( \frac{G}{T} \right)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = -\frac{H}{T^{2}} $$

$$ \left[ \frac{\partial \left( \frac{\Delta G}{T} \right)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^{2}} $$

The second form is specifically the **Reaction Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation**, and it is the version most frequently used in KTU board examinations and industrial reactor design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The Gibbs-Helmholtz equation is the cornerstone for deriving the **Van't Hoff Equation** ($\ln K$ vs $1/T$), which is a compulsory derivation under Module 2 of the PEPOT633 syllabus. Students are expected to derive, integrate, and apply this equation in numerical problems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Mountain Pass" Intuition

Imagine you are a hiker crossing a mountain pass at high altitude. The *height* of the mountain pass represents the **Gibbs free energy of reaction ($\Delta G$)**, and the *slope* of the mountain represents how sensitive that height is to the weather (temperature). The Gibbs-Helmholtz equation tells you:

> *"If you know the steepness of the slope (i.e., the **enthalpy $\Delta H$**) and the current altitude ($\Delta G$), you can predict how the path will rise or fall as the weather changes (temperature)."*

A reaction that **absorbs heat** (endothermic, $\Delta H > 0$) is like a slope that becomes *less steep* as temperature rises ‚ÄĒ meaning products are favored at higher $T$. Conversely, an **exothermic reaction** ($\Delta H < 0$) is like a slope that gets *steeper* with cold weather ‚ÄĒ meaning reactants are favored at higher $T$. This is **Le Chatelier's principle**, mathematically formalized.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants Referenced

The following constants are repeatedly used in Gibbs-Helmholtz based calculations and must be memorized:

- Universal gas constant: **R = 8.314 J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ**
- Standard state temperature: **T¬į = 298.15 K** (or **25 ¬įC**)
- Standard pressure: **P¬į = 1 bar = 10‚ĀĶ Pa** (IUPAC standard since 1982)
- Relation: **1 cal = 4.184 J** (for problems using older thermodynamic tables)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Chemical Engineering:** Whenever you design an ammonia synthesis reactor (Haber-Bosch), an esterification reactor, or a steam-methane reformer, you use the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation to compute the **optimum operating temperature** that maximizes conversion while maintaining acceptable reaction rates.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ‚ÄĒ The Logic Chain

The Gibbs-Helmholtz equation does not emerge from a single axiom; it is a *derived consequence* of the four fundamental thermodynamic relations. The logical chain is:

1. **Start with the Definition of G:** $G = H - TS$
2. **Divide by Temperature:** $\dfrac{G}{T} = \dfrac{H}{T} - S$
3. **Differentiate with respect to T at constant P:** Apply the quotient rule.
4. **Substitute the Maxwell Relation** $\left( \dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T} \right)_{P} = -S$.
5. **Use the Reciprocal Relation** for compactness to obtain the final form.

The core "Why" behind the equation is: **We cannot directly measure $\Delta G$ at high temperatures experimentally**, but we *can* measure $\Delta H$ (via calorimetry) and $\Delta S$ (via heat capacity data). The Gibbs-Helmholtz equation allows us to calculate $\Delta G$ (and hence $K$) at any temperature $T$ from a known reference temperature $T_{ref}$.

## 2.2 The Two Principal Forms

### Form A: For a Single Phase / Pure Substance

$$ \left[ \frac{\partial \left( \frac{G}{T} \right)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = -\frac{H}{T^{2}} $$

### Form B: For a Chemical Reaction (Reaction Gibbs-Helmholtz)

$$ \left[ \frac{\partial \left( \frac{\Delta G}{T} \right)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^{2}} $$

This is the form directly applied to chemical reaction equilibria, where $\Delta G$ and $\Delta H$ refer to the **change per mole of reaction** as defined by the reaction stoichiometry.

## 2.3 The Integrated Form ‚ÄĒ Van't Hoff Equation

For most practical KTU problems, the standard-state enthalpy change ($\Delta H¬į$) is assumed **approximately independent of temperature** over the range of interest. This assumption allows direct integration of Form B:

$$ \ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H¬į}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right) $$

If we set $T_{1} = T_{ref}$ (usually 298.15 K), the equation can be written in the **linear Van't Hoff form**:

$$ \ln K = -\frac{\Delta H¬į}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta S¬į}{R} $$

This is a **straight line** when $\ln K$ is plotted against $1/T$, with:
- **Slope** $= -\dfrac{\Delta H¬į}{R}$
- **Y-intercept** $= \dfrac{\Delta S¬į}{R}$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units / Constants** | **When to Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gibbs-Helmholtz (Single phase) | $\left[ \dfrac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = -\dfrac{H}{T^{2}}$ | $G$ in J, $H$ in J, $T$ in K | Pure substances, individual phases |
| Reaction Gibbs-Helmholtz | $\left[ \dfrac{\partial (\Delta G/T)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{T^{2}}$ | $\Delta G, \Delta H$ in J/mol | Chemical reaction equilibria |
| Van't Hoff (Two-point) | $\ln \dfrac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} = -\dfrac{\Delta H¬į}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}} \right)$ | $R = 8.314$ J/mol¬∑K | Computing $K$ at a new $T$ from known $K_{1}$ |
| Van't Hoff (Linear) | $\ln K = -\dfrac{\Delta H¬į}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + \dfrac{\Delta S¬į}{R}$ | Plot $\ln K$ vs $1/T$ | Experimental determination of $\Delta H¬į$ and $\Delta S¬į$ |
| Temperature-corrected $\Delta G$ | $\dfrac{\Delta G(T)}{T} = \dfrac{\Delta G(T_{ref})}{T_{ref}} - \Delta H \left( \dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_{ref}} \right)$ | K must be in Kelvin | Predicting $\Delta G$ at any $T$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Examiner Tip:** In board exams, you must write the integrated form clearly showing both temperature limits ($T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$). Examiners at KTU consistently deduct marks if students write only the differential form without showing the integration limits explicitly.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reactor Design:** Predicting equilibrium conversion in industrial reactors (e.g., methanol synthesis, sulfuric acid contact process).
- **Phase Equilibrium:** Computing vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) constants for distillation column design.
- **Electrochemistry:** Relating standard electrode potentials to temperature (the Nernst equation analog).
- **Biological Thermodynamics:** Predicting protein-ligand binding affinity variations with temperature in pharmaceutical research.
- **Materials Science:** Determining the stability windows of alloys and ceramics at elevated service temperatures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation (Differential Form)

We will derive Form A from first principles using only the **fundamental property relations** of thermodynamics.

### Step 1: Begin with the definition of Gibbs free energy

By definition, the Gibbs free energy $G$ is related to enthalpy $H$ and entropy $S$ as:

$$ G = H - TS $$

### Step 2: Divide both sides by absolute temperature $T$

To create the quotient $G/T$, we divide the entire expression by $T$:

$$ \frac{G}{T} = \frac{H}{T} - S $$

This step is the **key mathematical insight** of the Gibbs-Helmholtz derivation. We rearrange the expression specifically to exploit a Maxwell relation in the next step.

### Step 3: Differentiate both sides with respect to $T$ at constant $P$

Applying the partial derivative $\left( \dfrac{\partial}{\partial T} \right)_{P}$ to both sides. Using the **quotient rule** on the left side and standard differentiation on the right:

$$ \left[ \frac{\partial}{\partial T} \left( \frac{G}{T} \right) \right]_{P} = \left[ \frac{\partial}{\partial T} \left( \frac{H}{T} \right) \right]_{P} - \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial T} \right)_{P} $$

### Step 4: Apply the quotient rule to each term

The quotient rule states: $\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\dfrac{u}{v}\right) = \dfrac{v(\partial u/\partial x) - u(\partial v/\partial x)}{v^{2}}$.

$$ \frac{T \left( \dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T} \right)_{P} - G \cdot 1}{T^{2}} = \frac{T \left( \dfrac{\partial H}{\partial T} \right)_{P} - H \cdot 1}{T^{2}} - \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial T} \right)_{P} $$

### Step 5: Invoke the Maxwell relation $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -S$

From the fundamental property relation $dG = -SdT + VdP$, holding $P$ constant gives:

$$ \left( \frac{\partial G}{\partial T} \right)_{P} = -S $$

Substituting this into the left-hand side:

$$ \frac{T(-S) - G}{T^{2}} = \frac{T \left( \dfrac{\partial H}{\partial T} \right)_{P} - H}{T^{2}} - \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial T} \right)_{P} $$

### Step 6: Expand and simplify

Multiplying through and simplifying algebraically:

$$ \frac{-TS - G}{T^{2}} = \frac{T \left( \dfrac{\partial H}{\partial T} \right)_{P}}{T^{2}} - \frac{H}{T^{2}} - \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial T} \right)_{P} $$

$$ \frac{-TS - G}{T^{2}} = \frac{1}{T} \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial T} \right)_{P} - \frac{H}{T^{2}} - \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial T} \right)_{P} $$

### Step 7: Substitute the definition $G = H - TS$

Since $G = H - TS$, we can write $-TS - G = -TS - (H - TS) = -H$. This gives:

$$ \frac{-H}{T^{2}} = \frac{1}{T} \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial T} \right)_{P} - \frac{H}{T^{2}} - \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial T} \right)_{P} $$

### Step 8: Cancel identical terms and apply the heat capacity definition

The term $-\dfrac{H}{T^{2}}$ appears on both sides, so they cancel:

$$ 0 = \frac{1}{T} \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial T} \right)_{P} - \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial T} \right)_{P} $$

We recognize that $\left( \dfrac{\partial H}{\partial T} \right)_{P} = C_{P}$ (heat capacity at constant pressure), and therefore:

$$ \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial T} \right)_{P} = \frac{C_{P}}{T} $$

### Step 9: Reverse the derivation to obtain the compact form

Returning to the equation before substitution, and using $G = H - TS$ to eliminate $G$ on the left side of Step 5:

$$ \left[ \frac{\partial \left( \frac{G}{T} \right)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = -\frac{H}{T^{2}} $$

This is the **final Gibbs-Helmholtz equation in its differential form**. Note that the derivation did not require any assumptions about ideal gas behavior or incompressibility ‚ÄĒ it is a **general result** valid for any pure substance or single phase.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Integrated Van't Hoff Form

Starting from the reaction form:

$$ \left[ \frac{\partial \left( \frac{\Delta G}{T} \right)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H¬į}{T^{2}} $$

### Step 1: Separate variables for integration

$$ d \left( \frac{\Delta G}{T} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H¬į}{T^{2}} \, dT $$

### Step 2: Apply integration limits

Integrating from a reference temperature $T_{1}$ to a final temperature $T_{2}$:

$$ \int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} d \left( \frac{\Delta G}{T} \right) = -\int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} \frac{\Delta H¬į}{T^{2}} \, dT $$

### Step 3: Evaluate the integrals

Assuming $\Delta H¬į$ is independent of $T$ (the standard KTU assumption unless stated otherwise):

$$ \frac{\Delta G(T_{2})}{T_{2}} - \frac{\Delta G(T_{1})}{T_{1}} = -\Delta H¬į \left[ -\frac{1}{T} \right]_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} = \Delta H¬į \left( \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right) $$

### Step 4: Apply the relation $\Delta G¬į = -RT \ln K$

Substituting $\Delta G¬į = -RT \ln K$ at both temperature limits:

$$ \frac{-RT_{2} \ln K_{2}}{T_{2}} - \frac{-RT_{1} \ln K_{1}}{T_{1}} = \Delta H¬į \left( \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right) $$

$$ -R \ln K_{2} + R \ln K_{1} = \Delta H¬į \left( \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right) $$

### Step 5: Rearrange into the final Van't Hoff form

$$ \ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H¬į}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right) $$

This is the **Two-Point Van't Hoff Equation**, the most commonly tested formula in KTU board examinations under Module 2.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Variation of K with T

| **Sign of $\Delta H¬į$** | **Type of Reaction** | **Effect of Increasing T on $K$** | **Industrial Example** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\Delta H¬į > 0$ | Endothermic | $K$ increases sharply | Cracking of hydrocarbons |
| $\Delta H¬į < 0$ | Exothermic | $K$ decreases | Ammonia synthesis, Sulfuric acid contact |
| $\Delta H¬į \approx 0$ | Thermoneutral | $K$ nearly constant | Isomerization at low T |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
