# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024]

**State the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation. What is its significance in chemical reaction equilibria?**

**Model Answer:**

The Gibbs-Helmholtz equation relates the temperature dependence of the Gibbs free energy to the enthalpy of a system. For a chemical reaction, it is written as:

$$ \left[ \frac{\partial \left( \frac{\Delta G}{T} \right)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^{2}} $$

**Significance:** It enables the calculation of the **equilibrium constant $K$** at any temperature from a single known value of $K$ and the standard enthalpy change $\Delta H¬į$. This is essential in **reactor design**, where the operating temperature must be optimized to achieve desired product conversion without resorting to exhaustive experimental measurements at every temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Students often confuse the differential and integrated forms. Writing *both* forms and labeling them clearly fetches full marks.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]

**Derive the relationship $\Delta G¬į = \Delta H¬į - T\Delta S¬į$ from the integrated form of the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation.**

**Model Answer:**

Starting from the integrated Gibbs-Helmholtz form:

$$ \frac{\Delta G(T)}{T} = \frac{\Delta G(T_{ref})}{T_{ref}} - \Delta H¬į \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{ref}} \right) $$

Multiplying through by $T$:

$$ \Delta G(T) = \Delta G(T_{ref}) \cdot \frac{T}{T_{ref}} - \Delta H¬į \left( \frac{T}{T} - \frac{T}{T_{ref}} \right) $$

$$ \Delta G(T) = \Delta G(T_{ref}) \cdot \frac{T}{T_{ref}} - \Delta H¬į + \Delta H¬į \cdot \frac{T}{T_{ref}} $$

Rearranging:

$$ \Delta G(T) - \Delta G(T_{ref}) \cdot \frac{T}{T_{ref}} + \Delta H¬į = \Delta H¬į \cdot \frac{T}{T_{ref}} $$

Dividing by $T$:

$$ \frac{\Delta G(T)}{T} = \frac{\Delta H¬į}{T_{ref}} - \frac{\Delta G(T_{ref})}{T_{ref}} $$

Since $\Delta G¬į(T_{ref}) = \Delta H¬į - T_{ref}\Delta S¬į$ at the reference, we get $\dfrac{\Delta G¬į}{T_{ref}} = \dfrac{\Delta H¬į}{T_{ref}} - \Delta S¬į$. Substituting yields the standard relation $\Delta G¬į = \Delta H¬į - T\Delta S¬į$ at any temperature, completing the derivation.

---

## Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Option 1: [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation in its differential form starting from the fundamental relation $G = H - TS$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The standard enthalpy change for the reaction $N_{2}(g) + 3H_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NH_{3}(g)$ is $\Delta H¬į_{298} = -92.4$ kJ/mol. The equilibrium constant at 298 K is $K_{298} = 6.8 \times 10^{5}$. Calculate the equilibrium constant at 500 K, assuming $\Delta H¬į$ is temperature independent. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The complete derivation has been presented in Section 3.1 of these notes. Students must write all nine derivation steps explicitly:

1. Start with $G = H - TS$.
2. Divide by $T$.
3. Differentiate at constant $P$.
4. Apply quotient rule.
5. Substitute Maxwell relation $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -S$.
6. Expand and simplify.
7. Substitute $G = H - TS$.
8. Cancel common terms.
9. Arrive at $\left[\dfrac{\partial(G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} = -\dfrac{H}{T^{2}}$.

**Marks Allocation Hint:** Step 1‚Äď3 ‚Üí 2 marks; Step 4‚Äď6 ‚Üí 3 marks; Step 7‚Äď9 ‚Üí 2 marks.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $\Delta H¬į = -92.4$ kJ/mol $= -92400$ J/mol
- $T_{1} = 298$ K
- $K_{1} = 6.8 \times 10^{5}$
- $T_{2} = 500$ K
- $R = 8.314$ J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ

**Formula (Van't Hoff integrated form):**

$$ \ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H¬į}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right) $$

**Substituting values:**

$$ \ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = -\frac{(-92400)}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{500} - \frac{1}{298} \right) $$

$$ \ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = (11113.78) \times (0.002 - 0.003356) $$

$$ \ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = 11113.78 \times (-0.001356) = -15.07 $$

$$ \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} = e^{-15.07} = 2.91 \times 10^{-7} $$

**Final Answer:**

$$ K_{2} = K_{1} \times 2.91 \times 10^{-7} = 6.8 \times 10^{5} \times 2.91 \times 10^{-7} = 0.198 $$

The equilibrium constant decreases from $6.8 \times 10^{5}$ at 298 K to approximately **0.198 at 500 K**, confirming that ammonia synthesis (an exothermic reaction) is favored at lower temperatures.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Option 2: [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]

**(a)** State and explain the significance of the Van't Hoff equation. Show that it is the integrated form of the Gibbs-Helmholtz equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the decomposition reaction $CaCO_{3}(s) \rightleftharpoons CaO(s) + CO_{2}(g)$, the equilibrium constant at 1000 K is $K_{1} = 0.039$ and at 1100 K is $K_{2} = 0.413$. Calculate: (i) the standard enthalpy of reaction $\Delta H¬į$, and (ii) the standard entropy change $\Delta S¬į$ at 1000 K. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The Van't Hoff equation is:

$$ \ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H¬į}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right) $$

**Significance:** It is the **engineer's primary tool** for predicting how a chemical equilibrium shifts with temperature, without needing to perform expensive equilibrium measurements at multiple temperatures. The negative slope of the $\ln K$ versus $1/T$ plot directly yields $\Delta H¬į$, and the intercept gives $\Delta S¬į$.

The derivation showing it as the integrated form has been presented in Section 3.2. Students should write all five integration steps.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $T_{1} = 1000$ K, $K_{1} = 0.039$
- $T_{2} = 1100$ K, $K_{2} = 0.413$
- $R = 8.314$ J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ

**Part (i) ‚ÄĒ Calculate $\Delta H¬į$:**

Rearranging the Van't Hoff equation:

$$ \Delta H¬į = -\frac{R \cdot \ln(K_{2}/K_{1})}{\left( \dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}} \right)} $$

**Compute the numerator:**

$$ \ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = \ln \left( \frac{0.413}{0.039} \right) = \ln(10.59) = 2.36 $$

$$ R \times \ln(K_{2}/K_{1}) = 8.314 \times 2.36 = 19.62 \text{ J/mol} $$

**Compute the denominator:**

$$ \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} = \frac{1}{1100} - \frac{1}{1000} = 0.000909 - 0.001 = -9.09 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1} $$

**Compute $\Delta H¬į$:**

$$ \Delta H¬į = -\frac{19.62}{-9.09 \times 10^{-5}} = 215,840 \text{ J/mol} \approx 215.8 \text{ kJ/mol} $$

**Part (ii) ‚ÄĒ Calculate $\Delta S¬į$ at 1000 K:**

Using $\Delta G¬į = -RT \ln K = \Delta H¬į - T\Delta S¬į$:

$$ \Delta S¬į = \frac{\Delta H¬į - \Delta G¬į}{T} $$

**Compute $\Delta G¬į$ at 1000 K:**

$$ \Delta G¬į = -8.314 \times 1000 \times \ln(0.039) = -8314 \times (-3.24) = 26,937 \text{ J/mol} $$

**Compute $\Delta S¬į$:**

$$ \Delta S¬į = \frac{215840 - 26937}{1000} = \frac{188903}{1000} = 188.9 \text{ J/(mol¬∑K)} $$

**Final Answers:**
- $\Delta H¬į \approx 215.8$ kJ/mol (endothermic, as expected for decomposition)
- $\Delta S¬į \approx 188.9$ J/(mol¬∑K)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign Convention Trap:** A common mistake is forgetting that the equation requires temperatures in **Kelvin**, not Celsius. Marks are deducted for unit errors.
> - **Energy Unit Trap:** $\Delta H¬į$ must be in **J/mol** if $R = 8.314$ J/mol¬∑K. Mixing kJ with R = 8.314 yields numerical errors of 1000√ó.
> - **Exothermic vs Endothermic:** Students often get confused about whether $K$ increases or decreases with $T$. **Remember:** Endothermic ($\Delta H¬į > 0$) ‚Üí $K$ increases with $T$.
> - **Integration Limits:** Always specify $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ explicitly in the integrated form. Examiners at KTU consistently deduct 1‚Äď2 marks for omitted limits.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ‚úÖ The **Gibbs-Helmholtz equation** is written as $\left[\dfrac{\partial(G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} = -\dfrac{H}{T^{2}}$.
- ‚úÖ The **Reaction form** is $\left[\dfrac{\partial(\Delta G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{T^{2}}$.
- ‚úÖ Its **integrated form** (Van't Hoff) is the most heavily tested formula in KTU Module 2.
- ‚úÖ $\Delta H¬į$ is assumed **temperature-independent** unless $C_{p}$ data is explicitly given.
- ‚úÖ All temperatures **must** be in Kelvin (K), not Celsius (¬įC).
- ‚úÖ $\Delta H¬į$ must be in **J/mol** when $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K).
- ‚úÖ The Van't Hoff plot of $\ln K$ versus $1/T$ is a **straight line** with slope $-\Delta H¬į/R$.
- ‚úÖ **Exothermic reactions** ($\Delta H¬į < 0$) have $K$ decreasing with increasing $T$.
- ‚úÖ **Endothermic reactions** ($\Delta H¬į > 0$) have $K$ increasing with increasing $T$.
- ‚úÖ The equation is derived from $G = H - TS$ and the Maxwell relation $\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = -S$.
- ‚úÖ Industrial applications include **ammonia synthesis, methanol production, sulfuric acid manufacture**, and **calcination reactions**.
- ‚úÖ KTU examiners reward students who write the **integration limits explicitly** and label each variable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fugacity and Activity of Pure Fluids: Selection of Standard State, Effect of Temperature and Pressure
Lewis-Randall Rule
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Lewis-Randall Rule # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **LEWIS-RANDALL RULE (KTU 2024 Canonical Definition):** > The **Lewis-Randall Rule** is a fundamental postulate of classical solution thermodynamics which states that *for an ideal solution, the fugacity of each component `i` is directly proportional to its mole fraction, with the proportionality constant being the pure-component fugacity of `i` evaluated at the same temperature and pressure as the solution.* Mathematically, the rule is expressed as: $$f_i^{is} = x_i \, f_i(T, P)$$ where: - $f_i^{is}$ = fugacity of component $i$ in the **ideal solution (is)** - $x_i$ = mole fraction of component $i$ in the liquid phase - $f_i(T, P)$ = **pure-component fugacity** of $i$ at the solution temperature $T$ and total pressure $P$ The rule is the liquid-phase counterpart of **Amagat's Law** (which applies to ideal gas mixtures) and forms the thermodynamic foundation of **Raoult's Law** for vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Anchor:** This topic falls under Module 3 ‚ÄĒ *Solution Thermodynamics: Ideal and Non-Ideal Solutions*. It is a direct prerequisite for understanding **Raoult's Law, Henry's Law, Excess Properties, and Activity Coefficients** in subsequent modules. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ "The Well-Mixed Juice Bar" Imagine a juice bar where two completely miscible, similar-flavored fruit juices (say, orange and mango) are mixed in any proportion without any chemical reaction. Each juice "behaves itself" ‚ÄĒ it doesn't change its fundamental nature just because it's in the mixture. - The **pure juice's intensity of "flavor"** is analogous to the **pure-component fugacity $f_i$** ‚ÄĒ a measure of the "escaping tendency" or chemical potential of the pure substance. - When you **dilute** the orange juice to a mole fraction $x_{\text{orange}}$ by adding mango juice, its "effective flavor" in the mixture drops *linearly and proportionally* to its concentration. - This **linear proportionality** between concentration and effective escaping tendency is the **Lewis-Randall Rule** in action. The key physical insight: **like-like interactions in the mixture are statistically equivalent to like-like interactions in the pure liquids.** This requires the molecules to be of similar size, shape, and intermolecular forces (e.g., benzene-toluene, $n$-hexane-$n$-heptane, ethanol-methanol). > [!TIP] > **Why "ideal"?** The word "ideal" here does **NOT** mean "perfect gas." It means the molecules experience each other through an **average, uniform mean field**, with no preferential clustering or repulsion. The mixture's Gibbs free energy is a simple weighted sum of the pure-component Gibbs energies. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Reference States | Property | Symbol | Standard Reference State | Units (SI) | |---|---|---|---| | Pure-component fugacity | $f_i$ | Pure liquid or gas at system $T$ and $P$ | **Pa (Pascal)** | | Mole fraction | $x_i$ | Dimensionless (sum = 1) | ‚ÄĒ | | Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ** | J¬∑mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ | | System temperature | $T$ | Absolute scale | **K (Kelvin)** | | System pressure | $P$ | Absolute scale | **Pa (bar, atm also used)** | | Standard-state fugacity | $f_i^\circ$ | Pure component at $T$ and standard $P^\circ$ | Pa | | Henry's constant | $H_i$ | Infinitely dilute solute in solvent | Pa | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **standard state** for the Lewis-Randall rule is the **pure component at the system $T$ and $P$**. This distinguishes it from Henry's Law, whose standard state is the *infinitely dilute solution*. Both rules converge at the pure-component limit ($x_i \to 1$). --- ## 1.4 Why the Lewis-Randall Rule Matters in KTU Examinations The rule is a **high-yield topic** in KTU 2024 scheme assessments because it: 1. Bridges pure-component thermodynamics to mixture thermodynamics. 2. Provides the mathematical bedrock for deriving **Raoult's Law** in VLE problems. 3. Is a common conceptual trap in numerical problems where students confuse it with **Henry's Law**. 4. Appears in **Part A (3-mark)** as a definition, and in **Part B (14-mark)** as part of derivation/numerical chains. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Thermodynamic Postulates Underlying the Rule The Lewis-Randall Rule is **not an empirical correlation** ‚ÄĒ it is derived rigorously from three fundamental postulates of solution thermodynamics. The KTU examiner expects students to know the logical chain. ### Postulate I: Existence of Partial Molar Properties For any extensive property $M$ of a homogeneous solution at fixed $T$ and $P$: $$M = \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i \, \bar{M}_i(T, P, x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{N-1})$$ The **partial molar property** $\bar{M}_i$ is defined as: $$\bar{M}_i \equiv \left( \frac{\partial (nM)}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$$ ### Postulate II: Gibbs-Duhem Equation (the "Hidden Constraint") For any solution, the partial molar properties are not independent: $$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \, d\bar{M}_i = 0 \quad \text{(at constant } T, P\text{)}$$ This means **if you know $N-1$ partial molar properties, the last one is fixed by the Gibbs-Duhem equation.** It also enforces consistency in any VLE data reduction. ### Postulate III: The Ideal-Solution Assumption The **chemical potential** of component $i$ in an ideal solution is given by: $$\mu_i^{is}(T, P, x_i) = \mu_i^\circ(T, P) + RT \ln x_i$$ > [!NOTE] > The pure component chemical potential $\mu_i^\circ(T, P)$ is the chemical potential of pure $i$ at the **same $T$ and $P$** as the solution. This $\mu_i^\circ$ is *not* a function of composition. From the rigorous thermodynamic identity $\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln(f_i / f_i^\circ)$, substitution yields the Lewis-Randall Rule directly. --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Deduction of the Rule **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Starting Identity:** The chemical potential of any component in any phase is: $$\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ(T, P^\circ) + RT \ln \frac{f_i}{f_i^\circ}$$ where $f_i^\circ$ is the standard-state fugacity (conventionally pure component at $T$ and reference pressure $P^\circ$). **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Define the Ideal Solution:** By definition, an ideal solution obeys: $$\mu_i^{is} = \mu_i^\circ(T, P) + RT \ln x_i$$ Here, $\mu_i^\circ(T, P)$ is the **pure-component chemical potential at the system $T$ and $P$** ‚ÄĒ this is the new "standard state" for the ideal-solution framework. **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Equate the Two Expressions:** The first equation can be rewritten for the ideal solution as: $$\mu_i^{is} = \mu_i^\circ(T, P) + RT \ln \frac{f_i^{is}}{f_i(T, P)}$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Match the Logarithmic Terms:** Comparing with the ideal-solution definition: $$RT \ln \frac{f_i^{is}}{f_i(T, P)} = RT \ln x_i$$ $$\Rightarrow \ln \frac{f_i^{is}}{f_i(T, P)} = \ln x_i$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Exponentiate:** The result is the **Lewis-Randall Rule**: $$\boxed{f_i^{is} = x_i \, f_i(T, P)}$$ --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **EXAM GOLD:** Memorize the table below. Every formula here is a potential Part-B derivation step in KTU ESE. | # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Conditions | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $f_i^{is} = x_i \, f_i$ | **Lewis-Randall Rule (core)** | Ideal solution, any $x_i$ | | 2 | $\mu_i^{is} = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln x_i$ | Chemical potential in ideal solution | At system $T, P$ | | 3 | $\bar{G}_i^{is} = G_i + RT \ln x_i$ | Partial molar Gibbs energy | At system $T, P$ | | 4 | $\bar{V}_i^{is} = V_i$ | Partial molar volume | **Volume of mixing = 0** | | 5 | $\bar{H}_i^{is} = H_i$ | Partial molar enthalpy | **Heat of mixing = 0** | | 6 | $\bar{S}_i^{is} = S_i - R \ln x_i$ | Partial molar entropy | Entropy of mixing $\neq 0$ | | 7 | $\Delta V^{mix,is} = 0$ | Volume change on mixing | Ideal solution | | 8 | $\Delta H^{mix,is} = 0$ | Enthalpy change on mixing | Ideal solution | | 9 | $\Delta S^{mix,is} = -R \sum x_i \ln x_i$ | Entropy of mixing (always positive) | Ideal solution/gas | | 10 | $\Delta G^{mix,is} = RT \sum x_i \ln x_i$ | Gibbs energy of mixing (always negative) | Ideal solution | | 11 | $y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}$ | **Raoult's Law** (VLE form of LRR) | At equilibrium, $\phi_i^{V} = 1$ | | 12 | $f_i^{is} = x_i H_i$ | Henry's Law | Solute, $x_i \to 0$ | | 13 | $\phi_i = \text{species fugacity coefficient}$ | Vapor phase non-ideality | $\phi_i^V \neq 1$ in general | | 14 | $\gamma_i \equiv f_i / (x_i f_i)$ | **Activity coefficient** | $\gamma_i = 1$ for ideal solution | | 15 | $a_i \equiv f_i / f_i^\circ$ | Activity of $i$ | $a_i = x_i$ for ideal solution | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU trap:** The Lewis-Randall rule gives $\gamma_i = 1$ for **every** component throughout the composition range. This is *not* the case for Henry's Law, which gives $\gamma_i^\infty = 1$ only at the **infinite-dilution limit**. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The Lewis-Randall Rule is the backbone of: 1. **Distillation column design:** Raoult's Law (a direct consequence of LRR) is used to generate $x$-$y$ equilibrium curves for systems like benzene-toluene. 2. **Refrigeration cycles:** Property estimation of refrigerant/lubricant mixtures. 3. **Petroleum refining:** Dew-point and bubble-point calculations on hydrocarbon mixtures. 4. **Process simulators (Aspen Plus, HYSYS):** Used as the default thermodynamic model for "simple" systems until user specifies a non-ideal model (NRTL, UNIQUAC, etc.). 5. **Polymer solution thermodynamics:** Base model for Flory-Huggins theory generalization. The rule is **strictly valid** only when: - Molecules are of **similar size** (within a factor of ~2). - **Similar chemical nature** (e.g., isotopic mixtures, $n$-alkanes, structural isomers). - **No strong specific interactions** (no H-bonding, no acid-base reactions). When these conditions fail, the rule becomes a *reference baseline* against which **excess properties** and **activity coefficient models** are defined. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Gibbs Energy of Mixing for an Ideal Solution **Goal:** Derive the closed-form expression for $\Delta G^{mix,is}$. ### Starting Point The total Gibbs energy of the ideal solution is: $$G^{is}(T, P, n) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i \, \bar{G}_i^{is}$$ where $\bar{G}_i^{is}$ is the partial molar Gibbs energy (= chemical potential $\mu_i^{is}$). ### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Substitute the Ideal-Solution Chemical Potential $$G^{is}(T, P, n) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i \left[ G_i(T, P) + RT \ln x_i \right]$$ Here, $G_i(T, P)$ is the molar Gibbs energy of pure component $i$ at the system $T$ and $P$. ### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Separate the Pure-Component Sum $$G^{is}(T, P, n) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i \, G_i(T, P) + RT \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i \ln x_i$$ The first term is simply the Gibbs energy of the **unmixed pure components**: $$G^{\text{pure}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i \, G_i(T, P)$$ ### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Define the Gibbs Energy of Mixing By definition: $$\Delta G^{mix} \equiv G^{is} - G^{\text{pure}} = RT \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i \ln x_i$$ ### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Convert to Mole-Fraction Form Divide both sides by total moles $n = \sum n_i$: $$\frac{\Delta G^{mix}}{n} = \Delta g^{mix} = RT \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \ln x_i$$ **Result (boxed):** $$\boxed{\Delta g^{mix,is} = RT \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \ln x_i}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** Since $0 < x_i < 1$, each $\ln x_i < 0$. Therefore $\Delta g^{mix} < 0$ ‚ÄĒ mixing is **always spontaneous** for ideal solutions at constant $T$ and $P$. This is the thermodynamic driving force for mixing. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Volume, Enthalpy, and Entropy of Mixing ### 3.2.1 Volume of Mixing (Start from $\bar{V}_i^{is} = V_i$) For an ideal solution, the partial molar volume equals the pure-component molar volume. Therefore: $$V^{is} = \sum n_i \bar{V}_i^{is} = \sum n_i V_i = V^{\text{pure}}$$ $$\Rightarrow \Delta V^{mix,is} = 0$$ ### 3.2.2 Enthalpy of Mixing Using the Maxwell-type identity $\left(\frac{\partial \mu_i / T}{\partial 1/T}\right)_{P, x} = \bar{H}_i$: $$\bar{H}_i^{is} = \left[ \frac{\partial (\mu_i^{is}/T)}{\partial (1/T)} \right]_{P, x}$$ But $\mu_i^{is}/T = \mu_i^\circ/T + R \ln x_i$, and $R \ln x_i$ is independent of $T$. So: $$\bar{H}_i^{is} = H_i \quad \text{(pure-component enthalpy)}$$ $$\Rightarrow \Delta H^{mix,is} = 0$$ ### 3.2.3 Entropy of Mixing Using the identity $\bar{S}_i = -(\partial \mu_i / \partial T)_{P, x}$: $$\bar{S}_i^{is} = -\left( \frac{\partial \mu_i^\circ}{\partial T} \right)_{P, x} - R \ln x_i = S_i - R \ln x_i$$ Therefore: $$\Delta S^{mix} = \sum n_i \bar{S}_i^{is} - \sum n_i S_i = -R \sum n_i \ln x_i$$ $$\Rightarrow \Delta s^{mix,is} = -R \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \ln x_i \quad (> 0)$$ > [!TIP] > **Consistency check:** Using $\Delta g^{mix} = \Delta h^{mix} - T \Delta s^{mix}$: > - $\Delta h^{mix} = 0$ > - $\Delta s^{mix} = -R \sum x_i \ln x_i > 0$ > - $\Delta g^{mix} = 0 - T(-R \sum x_i \ln x_i) = RT \sum x_i \ln x_i$ ‚úď (matches Derivation 1) --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: From Lewis-Randall to Raoult's Law (VLE) **Goal:** Show that the Lewis-Randall Rule is the parent equation of Raoult's Law. ### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium Condition At equilibrium, the fugacity of each component $i$ in the liquid phase equals that in the vapor phase: $$f_i^L = f_i^V$$ ### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply Lewis-Randall to the Liquid Phase The liquid is assumed ideal: $$f_i^L = x_i \, f_i^{\text{pure liquid}}(T, P)$$ ### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Apply the Lewis-Randall (or Ideal Gas) to the Vapor Phase If the vapor phase is also ideal (low pressure): $$f_i^V = y_i \, P$$ ### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Equate $$x_i \, f_i^{\text{pure liquid}}(T, P) = y_i \, P$$ ### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Use the Pure-Liquid Fugacity Identity The pure-liquid fugacity at low-to-moderate pressure equals the saturation fugacity (vapor pressure), since the liquid and pure vapor coexist at saturation: $$f_i^{\text{pure liquid}}(T, P) \approx P_i^{sat}(T) \quad \text{(if } P_i^{sat} \ll P_c \text{ or Poynting correction is negligible)}$$ ### Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Final Result (Raoult's Law) $$\boxed{y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}(T)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Pearl:** Raoult's Law is the **VLE consequence** of the Lewis-Randall Rule for the liquid phase + ideal gas behavior for the vapor phase. The full modified Raoult's Law is $y_i \phi_i^V P = x_i \gamma_i f_i^{\text{pure L}}$, which reduces to the above when $\phi_i^V = 1$ and $\gamma_i = 1$. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Equivalence of Lewis-Randall and Henry's Law at the Pure-Component Limit **Goal:** Show $f_i \to H_i$ as $x_i \to 1$. ### Henry's Law Form For the solute $i$ in a dilute solution: $$f_i^{H} = x_i H_i \quad (x_i \to 0)$$ ### Lewis-Randall Form $$f_i^{LR} = x_i f_i^{\text{pure}} \quad (\text{any } x_i)$$ ### Continuity at the Pure-Component Limit At the pure component, both rules must give $f_i = f_i^{\text{pure}}$ (since the solution becomes pure $i$). From the dilute side: $$\lim_{x_i \to 1} x_i H_i = H_i \stackrel{!}{=} f_i^{\text{pure}}$$ Therefore $H_i = f_i^{\text{pure}}$ **only if** Henry's Law were extrapolated to the pure component. In reality, Henry's Law is only valid in the dilute regime, and $H_i$ is determined experimentally as: $$H_i = \lim_{x_i \to 0} \frac{f_i}{x_i}$$ which is generally **not** equal to $f_i^{\text{pure}}$ (it is the slope of $f_i$ vs $x_i$ at the origin, not the value at $x_i = 1$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Visual summary:** The $f_i$ vs $x_i$ curve is generally a smooth curve. The Lewis-Randall line is a chord from the origin to the pure-component point, while the Henry's Law line is the **tangent at the origin** (solute in solvent) or **tangent at $x_i = 1$** (solvent in solute). They coincide at one end only for *truly ideal* solutions. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Ideal vs Real Solutions | Property | Ideal Solution (LRR holds) | Real Solution (LRR fails) | KTU Implication | |---|---|---|---| | Fugacity $f_i$ | $x_i f_i^{\text{pure}}$ | $x_i \gamma_i f_i^{\text{pure}}$ | Activity coeff. $\gamma_i$ appears | | Volume of mixing | $\Delta V^{mix} = 0$ | $\Delta V^{mix} \neq 0$ | Sign indicates contraction/expansion | | Enthalpy of mixing | $\Delta H^{mix} = 0$ | $\Delta H^{mix} \neq 0$ | Azeotrope formation possible | | Entropy of mixing | $-R \sum x_i \ln x_i$ | $-R \sum x_i \ln(x_i \gamma_i)$ | Excess entropy $S^E$ defined | | Gibbs energy of mixing | $RT \sum x_i \ln x_i$ | $RT \sum x_i \ln(x_i \gamma_i)$ | Excess Gibbs $G^E = RT \sum x_i \ln \gamma_i$ | | Activity coeff. $\gamma_i$ | $= 1$ for all $i, x$ | $\neq 1$ in general | Models: Margules, van Laar, Wilson, NRTL, UNIQUAC | # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart ‚ÄĒ Position of the Lewis-Randall Rule in Solution Thermodynamics ```mermaid flowchart TD A["PURE COMPONENT STATE T and P"] --> B["MOLECULAR MIXING AT CONSTANT T and P"] B --> C{"ARE MOLECULES

When are they equivalent?** #### Model Answer (3 marks): | Feature | Lewis-Randall Rule | Henry's Law | |---|---|---| | **Standard state** | Pure component at $T, P$ | Hypothetical: $x_i = 1$ obeying Henry slope | | **Mathematical form** | $f_i = x_i f_i^{\text{pure}}$ | $f_i = x_i H_i$ | | **Validity range** | All $x_i$ for ideal solutions | Solute in **dilute** region ($x_i \to 0$) | | **Constant** | $f_i^{\text{pure}}(T, P)$ | $H_i(T, P)$ ‚ÄĒ Henry's constant | **Equivalence:** Both become identical at the **pure-component limit** $x_i \to 1$ only if the solution is truly ideal throughout, in which case $H_i = f_i^{\text{pure}}$. **[1 mark]** > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake (1-mark loss):** Students often claim Henry's Law and Lewis-Randall are "the same." They are **not** the same in general. They differ for real solutions because $H_i \neq f_i^{\text{pure}}$. Mark explicitly: different standard states. --- ## PART B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: Each Part-B question has two sub-parts (typically 7+7 marks). The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model QP, PEP633 Module 3]` **(a)** Derive the Lewis-Randall Rule from the fundamental definition of the chemical potential of a component in an ideal solution. Show clearly that the activity coefficient equals unity for all components at all compositions. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A binary liquid mixture of benzene (1) and toluene (2) at 1 atm total pressure can be modeled as an ideal solution. The vapor pressures of pure benzene and pure toluene at 100 ¬įC are 1350 mmHg and 556 mmHg, respectively. Calculate: - (i) The bubble-point temperature and the composition of the first vapor bubble formed from a liquid of $x_1 = 0.40$. **[3 marks]** - (ii) The dew-point temperature of a vapor of $y_1 = 0.40$. **[4 marks]** #### Model Solution ### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation (7 marks) **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Chemical Potential Identity (1 mark):** For any homogeneous phase at temperature $T$ and pressure $P$, the chemical potential of component $i$ is: $$\mu_i(T, P, x) = \mu_i^\circ(T, P) + RT \ln \frac{f_i}{f_i^\circ}$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Ideal-Solution Definition (1.5 marks):** By definition, an ideal solution is one in which: $$\mu_i^{is}(T, P, x_i) = \mu_i^*(T, P) + RT \ln x_i$$ where $\mu_i^*(T, P)$ is the chemical potential of pure $i$ at the system $T$ and $P$, and $f_i^* \equiv f_i(T, P)$ is the pure-component fugacity. **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Expressing $\mu_i^{is}$ via the Fugacity Identity (1.5 marks):** For component $i$ in the ideal solution: $$\mu_i^{is} = \mu_i^*(T, P) + RT \ln \frac{f_i^{is}}{f_i(T, P)}$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Equate and Compare (1.5 marks):** Setting this equal to the ideal-solution definition: $$\mu_i^*(T, P) + RT \ln \frac{f_i^{is}}{f_i(T, P)} = \mu_i^*(T, P) + RT \ln x_i$$ $$RT \ln \frac{f_i^{is}}{f_i(T, P)} = RT \ln x_i$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Exponentiate (1 mark):** $$\boxed{f_i^{is} = x_i \, f_i(T, P)} \quad \text{‚ÄĒ Lewis-Randall Rule}$$ **Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Activity Coefficient (0.5 marks):** By definition, $\gamma_i \equiv f_i / (x_i f_i)$. For the ideal solution, $f_i = f_i^{is} = x_i f_i^{\text{pure}}$, so: $$\gamma_i = \frac{x_i f_i^{\text{pure}}}{x_i f_i^{\text{pure}}} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$ --- ### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical (7 marks) **Given:** $P = 1$ atm $= 760$ mmHg, $P_1^{sat}(100\,¬įC) = 1350$ mmHg, $P_2^{sat}(100\,¬įC) = 556$ mmHg. For benzene-toluene, assume Raoult's Law (consequence of LRR) holds: $$y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$$ **(i) Bubble-point calculation for $x_1 = 0.40$:** (3 marks) The bubble-point equation is: $$P = \sum x_i P_i^{sat}(T_b)$$ $$P = x_1 P_1^{sat}(T_b) + x_2 P_2^{sat}(T_b) = 0.40 \, P_1^{sat}(T_b) + 0.60 \, P_2^{sat}(T_b)$$ Using Antoine equations for benzene and toluene (assumed given in data tables): $$\ln P_1^{sat} = A_1 - \frac{B_1}{T + C_1} \quad ; \quad \ln P_2^{sat} = A_2 - \frac{B_2}{T + C_2}$$ Solve iteratively or graphically. At $T = 95\,¬įC$: $P_1^{sat} \approx 1175$ mmHg, $P_2^{sat} \approx 480$ mmHg: $$P_{\text{calc}} = 0.40(1175) + 0.60(480) = 470 + 288 = 758 \text{ mmHg} \approx 760 \text{ mmHg} \checkmark$$ **Bubble-point temperature $T_b \approx 95\,¬įC$.** **Vapor composition at bubble point:** $$y_1 = \frac{x_1 P_1^{sat}}{P} = \frac{0.40 \times 1175}{760} = 0.618 \quad ; \quad y_2 = 0.382$$ **(ii) Dew-point calculation for $y_1 = 0.40$:** (4 marks) The dew-point equation is: $$P = \frac{1}{\sum y_i / P_i^{sat}(T_d)}$$ Iterating on $T_d$: At $T = 105\,¬įC$: $P_1^{sat} \approx 1530$ mmHg, $P_2^{sat} \approx 635$ mmHg: $$P_{\text{calc}} = \frac{1}{0.40/1530 + 0.60/635} = \frac{1}{0.000261 + 0.000945} = \frac{1}{0.001206} \approx 829 \text{ mmHg}$$ Try $T_d = 102\,¬įC$: $P_1^{sat} \approx 1430$, $P_2^{sat} \approx 590$ mmHg: $$P_{\text{calc}} = \frac{1}{0.40/1430 + 0.60/590} = \frac{1}{0.000280 + 0.001017} = \frac{1}{0.001297} \approx 771 \text{ mmHg} \approx 760 \checkmark$$ **Dew-point temperature $T_d \approx 102\,¬įC$.** **Liquid composition at dew point:** $$x_1 = \frac{y_1 P}{P_1^{sat}} = \frac{0.40 \times 760}{1430} = 0.213 \quad ; \quad x_2 = 0.787$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing bubble-point vs dew-point equations** ‚ÄĒ bubble-point uses $P = \sum x_i P_i^{sat}$ (linear in $x$), dew-point uses $P^{-1} = \sum y_i / P_i^{sat}$ (harmonic in $y$). Mixing these up costs **2‚Äď3 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting Antoine constants** ‚ÄĒ students often leave the problem in symbolic form. Always state the Antoine equation and substitute numerical values. > 3. **Not iterating** ‚ÄĒ bubble/dew-point requires iteration. Show at least **two iterations** explicitly. > 4. **Rounding errors** ‚ÄĒ keep 4 significant figures in intermediate steps; round only at the final answer. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model QP, Alternative]` **(a)** Starting from the Lewis-Randall Rule, derive expressions for the **Gibbs energy of mixing**, **entropy of mixing**, and **enthalpy of mixing** for a binary ideal solution. Prove that for an ideal solution, mixing is always spontaneous at constant $T$ and $P$. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A non-ideal binary liquid mixture exhibits the following behavior at 350 K: - For component 1: $\ln \gamma_1 = 0.5 \, x_2^2$ - For component 2: $\ln \gamma_2 = 0.5 \, x_1^2$ Show that this is **thermodynamically consistent** using the Gibbs-Duhem equation. Then calculate the excess Gibbs energy $G^E/RT$ at $x_1 = 0.5$ and verify whether the system could be an **azeotrope** at this composition. **[7 marks]** #### Model Solution Outline ### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation of Mixing Properties (7 marks) **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Partial Molar Gibbs Energy from LRR (1.5 marks):** $$\mu_i^{is} = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln x_i = G_i + RT \ln x_i$$ $$\therefore \bar{G}_i^{is} = G_i + RT \ln x_i$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Total Gibbs Energy (1 mark):** $$G^{is} = \sum n_i G_i + RT \sum n_i \ln x_i$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Gibbs Energy of Mixing (1 mark):** $$\Delta G^{mix} = G^{is} - \sum n_i G_i = RT \sum n_i \ln x_i$$ $$\Delta g^{mix} = RT \sum x_i \ln x_i < 0 \quad \text{(since each } \ln x_i < 0\text{)} \Rightarrow \text{spontaneous} \checkmark$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Entropy of Mixing (1.5 marks):** From $\bar{S}_i^{is} = S_i - R \ln x_i$: $$\Delta S^{mix} = -R \sum n_i \ln x_i > 0$$ $$\Delta s^{mix} = -R \sum x_i \ln x_i$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Enthalpy of Mixing (1 mark):** From the Gibbs-Helmholtz relation: $$\Delta H^{mix} = \Delta G^{mix} + T \Delta S^{mix} = RT \sum x_i \ln x_i - RT \sum x_i \ln x_i = 0 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Spontaneity (1 mark):** At constant $T$ and $P$, $\Delta G^{mix} = RT \sum x_i \ln x_i < 0$ for all $x_i \in (0, 1)$. Therefore, mixing of an ideal solution is **always spontaneous**. --- ### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Gibbs-Duhem Consistency Check (7 marks) **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Gibbs-Duhem Equation (1 mark):** $$x_1 \, d\ln\gamma_1 + x_2 \, d\ln\gamma_2 = 0 \quad \text{(at constant } T, P\text{)}$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Differentiate the given expressions (1.5 marks):** $$d\ln\gamma_1 = -x_2 \, dx_2 \quad (\text{since } x_1^2 = (1-x_2)^2, \text{ differentiate})$$ $$d\ln\gamma_2 = -x_1 \, dx_1$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Substitute into Gibbs-Duhem (1.5 marks):** With $dx_1 = -dx_2$: $$x_1(-x_2 \, dx_2) + x_2(-x_1 \, (-dx_2)) = -x_1 x_2 \, dx_2 + x_1 x_2 \, dx_2 = 0 \checkmark$$ The system is **thermodynamically consistent**. **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Excess Gibbs Energy (2 marks):** $$G^E / RT = x_1 \ln \gamma_1 + x_2 \ln \gamma_2 = x_1 (0.5 \, x_2^2) + x_2 (0.5 \, x_1^2)$$ $$= 0.5 \, x_1 x_2^2 + 0.5 \, x_2 x_1^2 = 0.5 \, x_1 x_2 (x_2 + x_1) = 0.5 \, x_1 x_2$$ At $x_1 = 0.5$, $x_2 = 0.5$: $$G^E / RT = 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.125$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Azeotrope Check (1 mark):** For an azeotrope, the vapor and liquid compositions are equal at constant $P$ (and $T$). This requires $\gamma_1 = \gamma_2 = 1$ at the azeotropic point, which would demand $G^E = 0$. Since $G^E / RT = 0.125 > 0$, **no azeotrope exists at $x_1 = 0.5$** for this system. > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks Loss:** > 1. **Gibbs-Duhem inconsistency** ‚ÄĒ many students write the equation but don't verify with the *differentials* of the given $\ln \gamma_i$ expressions. > 2. **Azeotrope condition** ‚ÄĒ students often misstate it as "$\alpha = 1$" rather than "vapor and liquid compositions equal AND $\gamma_1 / \gamma_2 = P_2^{sat} / P_1^{sat}$." > 3. **Excess property formula** ‚ÄĒ students forget that $G^E / RT = \sum x_i \ln \gamma_i$ (not $\sum \gamma_i$). --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Revision ‚ÄĒ Read this 30 minutes before the exam.** ### ūüĒĎ Must-Memorize Equations - [x] **Lewis-Randall Rule:** $f_i^{is} = x_i \, f_i(T, P)$ - [x] **Chemical potential:** $\mu_i^{is} = \mu_i^\circ + RT \ln x_i$ - [x] **Partial molar property:** $\bar{G}_i^{is} = G_i + RT \ln x_i$ - [x] **Gibbs energy of mixing:** $\Delta g^{mix} = RT \sum x_i \ln x_i$ (negative ‚áí spontaneous) - [x] **Entropy of mixing:** $\Delta s^{mix} = -R \sum x_i \ln x_i$ (positive ‚áí irreversible) - [x] **Volume of mixing:** $\Delta V^{mix} = 0$ - [x] **Enthalpy of mixing:** $\Delta H^{mix} = 0$ - [x] **Raoult's Law (VLE consequence):** $y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$ - [x] **Activity coefficient:** $\gamma_i = 1$ for ideal solution (all $x_i$) - [x] **Henry's Law (solute dilute):** $f_i = x_i H_i$, valid as $x_i \to 0$ ### ‚ö†ÔłŹ Common KTU Exam Traps - [ ] Confusing Lewis-Randall with Henry's Law ‚ÄĒ they have **different standard states** - [ ] Forgetting the **Poynting correction** at high pressures: $f_i^{L}(T,P) = P_i^{sat} \phi_i^{sat} \exp\left[\frac{V_i^L (P - P_i^{sat})}{RT}\right]$ - [ ] Using LRR for highly non-ideal systems (e.g., water-ethanol) ‚ÄĒ leads to huge errors - [ ] Mixing up **bubble-point** ($P = \sum x_i P_i^{sat}$) and **dew-point** ($1/P = \sum y_i / P_i^{sat}$) formulas - [ ] Stating $H_i = f_i^{\text{pure}}$ ‚ÄĒ true **only for ideal solutions** ### ūüß† Conceptual Hot-Spots - [ ] **Why is mixing always spontaneous for ideal solutions?** ‚ÄĒ $\Delta G^{mix} < 0$ because $\ln x_i < 0$ for $0 < x_i < 1$ - [ ] **Why is $\Delta H^{mix} = 0$?** ‚ÄĒ Pure-component enthalpies are composition-independent in an ideal solution - [ ] **What makes a solution "ideal"?** ‚ÄĒ Similar molecular size, shape, and intermolecular forces; no specific chemical interactions - [ ] **What is the connection between LRR and Raoult's Law?** ‚ÄĒ Raoult's Law is the **VLE form** of LRR when the vapor phase is also ideal ### ūüďĆ Quick Mnemonic > **"LRR = Linear, Raoult, Random mixing"** > - **L**inear relationship between $f_i$ and $x_i$ > - **R**aoult's Law is its VLE form > - **R**andom mixing (no preferential interactions) ### ūüéĮ KTU Mark Distribution Pattern (Typical) | Question Part | Marks | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) | Key Skill Tested | |---|---|---|---| | Define LRR | 2‚Äď3 | Remember/Understand | Recall formula | | Derive from $\mu_i$ | 5‚Äď7 | Apply/Analyze | Algebraic manipulation | | VLE numerical | 7 | Apply | Antoine equation iteration | | Compare with Henry's Law | 3‚Äď5 | Analyze | Critical comparison | | Gibbs energy of mixing | 5‚Äď7 | Apply | Thermodynamic cycle | | Azeotrope / consistency | 7 | Evaluate | Gibbs-Duhem verification | ### ‚úÖ Final Word The Lewis-Randall Rule is the **Rosetta Stone** of solution thermodynamics in your KTU syllabus. Master its derivation, memorize the four mixing properties ($\Delta G, \Delta H, \Delta S, \Delta V$), and practice at least 3 VLE numericals. You are guaranteed to face this topic in some form in the ESE. **All the best for your KTU 2024 examinations!** ūüéď

topics.title: Raoult's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Raoult's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Raoult's Law (Formal Statement):**
> *"At a given temperature, the partial pressure exerted by a component 'i' in a liquid solution is equal to the product of the mole fraction of that component in the liquid phase ($x_i$) and the vapor pressure of the pure component at that temperature ($P_i^{sat}$)."*

For a binary ideal solution of components **A** and **B**, Raoult's Law is expressed as:

$$P_A = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}(T)$$

$$P_B = x_B \cdot P_B^{sat}(T)$$

Where:
- $P_A$ = Partial pressure of component A in the vapor phase
- $x_A$ = Mole fraction of component A in the **liquid** phase
- $P_A^{sat}(T)$ = Saturation (vapor) pressure of pure component A at system temperature T

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Raoult's Law is the cornerstone of **Vapor‚ÄďLiquid Equilibrium (VLE)** for ideal solutions. It is universally assumed valid when the molecules of the components are chemically and structurally similar (e.g., benzene‚Äďtoluene, hexane‚Äďheptane).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Crowded Bus Stop" Intuition

Imagine a bus stop where people from two different offices ‚ÄĒ **Office A** and **Office B** ‚ÄĒ are waiting. The bus arrives every few minutes (analogous to evaporation). Each batch of people who board the bus represents molecules leaving the liquid into the vapor phase.

Now, the key assumption of Raoult's Law is that **every individual, regardless of their office, has the same *eagerness* to board the bus** when compared *relative to their own pure-office scenario*. So if 70% of the people waiting belong to Office A, then roughly 70% of those who board will be from Office A. Their *partial boarding rate* is just a scaled-down version of the pure-component rate.

- **The "waiting crowd"** = the liquid solution
- **"Boarding the bus"** = evaporation into vapor
- **"Office A's pure eagerness"** = $P_A^{sat}$
- **"Fraction of Office A in crowd"** = $x_A$

This is exactly what Raoult's Law says: $P_A = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}$. The more "Office A people" you pack into the crowd, the more A-type molecules will evaporate, in direct proportion.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Highlighted for Quick Recall)

- **Mole fraction in liquid ($x_i$):** Dimensionless, range $[0, 1]$
- **Mole fraction in vapor ($y_i$):** Dimensionless, range $[0, 1]$
- **Saturation pressure ($P_i^{sat}$):** Pressure units, **typically in kPa, bar, or mmHg**
- **Total system pressure ($P_{total}$):** **Standard metric = 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg**
- **System temperature ($T$):** **In Kelvin (K)** for thermodynamic consistency

> [!TIP]
> For ideal solutions, the **K-value** (vapor‚Äďliquid equilibrium ratio) is defined as $K_i = y_i / x_i = P_i^{sat}(T)/P$. This is a very high-yield formula for KTU problems on flash calculations, bubble/dew points, and relative volatility.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Thermodynamic Foundation

Raoult's Law emerges directly from the equality of **fugacities** at vapor‚Äďliquid equilibrium for an ideal solution. The logical chain is:

- For an **ideal solution**, the activity coefficient $\gamma_i = 1$ for all components.
- The component fugacity in the liquid phase simplifies to: $f_i^{L} = x_i \cdot f_i^{pure,L}(T,P)$
- At low-to-moderate pressures, the pure-component liquid fugacity equals the saturation pressure: $f_i^{pure,L}(T,P) \approx P_i^{sat}(T)$
- The component fugacity in the vapor phase (ideal gas assumption): $f_i^{V} = y_i \cdot P$
- Equating $f_i^{V} = f_i^{L}$ at equilibrium yields **Raoult's Law**.

## 2.2 Key Logical Steps for Solving VLE Problems

1. **Identify the system:** Number of components, system type (binary, ternary), given data ($T$ or $P$).
2. **Apply Raoult's Law** to each component to obtain partial pressures.
3. **Apply Dalton's Law** to convert partial pressures to total pressure or vapor-phase composition.
4. **Use mass balance:** $\sum x_i = 1$ and $\sum y_i = 1$.
5. **Compute K-values** $K_i = y_i/x_i$ and **relative volatility** $\alpha_{ij} = K_i/K_j$ when distillation-like analysis is needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dalton's Law (Companion of Raoult's Law):** $P_{total} = P_A + P_B = x_A P_A^{sat} + x_B P_B^{sat}$. This is the bridge used in almost every KTU numerical.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables / Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Raoult's Law (Component i)** | $P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}(T)$ | $P_i$ = partial pressure; $x_i$ = liquid mole fraction | Direct VLE at known T |
| 2 | **Dalton's Law (Total P)** | $P = \sum x_i P_i^{sat}$ | Binary: $P = x_A P_A^{sat} + (1-x_A) P_B^{sat}$ | Bubble-P pressure at given T, x |
| 3 | **Vapor Composition (binary)** | $y_A = \dfrac{x_A P_A^{sat}}{x_A P_A^{sat} + x_B P_B^{sat}}$ | $y_A$ = vapor mole fraction | Dew-T or Bubble-T computations |
| 4 | **K-Value (Ideal)** | $K_i = \dfrac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$ | Ratio of vapor to liquid mole fraction | Flash, distillation |
| 5 | **Relative Volatility** | $\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{K_A}{K_B} = \dfrac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$ | For ideal binary A‚ÄďB | Distillation feasibility |
| 6 | **Inverse Raoult's** (for y ‚Üí x) | $x_A = \dfrac{y_A P}{P_A^{sat}(T)}$ | Useful in dew-point calculations | Dew-P at known T, y |
| 7 | **Modified Raoult's** (non-ideal) | $P_i = \gamma_i x_i P_i^{sat}$ | $\gamma_i$ = activity coefficient | Non-ideal VLE |
| 8 | **Antoine Equation** | $\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{C+T}$ | A, B, C are component-specific | Find $P^{sat}$ at given T |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Raoult's Law is the backbone of **distillation column design**, **flash drum calculations**, **reboiler/condenser sizing**, and **environmental fate modeling** of volatile organic compounds. Almost every chemical plant simulation (Aspen Plus, HYSYS) uses Raoult's Law as the default ideal-solution VLE model.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Pressure as a Linear Function of Liquid Composition

**Starting Point ‚ÄĒ Raoult's Law for each component:**

$$P_A = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}(T)$$

$$P_B = x_B \cdot P_B^{sat}(T)$$

**Apply Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures** to combine the two:

$$P_{total} = P_A + P_B$$

**Substitute the Raoult expressions into the Dalton expression:**

$$P_{total} = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat} + x_B \cdot P_B^{sat}$$

**Use the mole-fraction closure relation** $x_B = 1 - x_A$ (binary system):

$$P_{total} = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat} + (1 - x_A) \cdot P_B^{sat}$$

**Expand the bracket:**

$$P_{total} = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat} + P_B^{sat} - x_A \cdot P_B^{sat}$$

**Group the $x_A$ terms together:**

$$P_{total} = P_B^{sat} + x_A \cdot (P_A^{sat} - P_B^{sat})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For a binary ideal solution, the **total pressure varies *linearly*** with the liquid mole fraction $x_A$ at constant temperature. This straight line is the **bubble-point curve** on a P‚Äďx‚Äďy diagram.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Vapor-Phase Composition from Liquid Composition

**Starting with Dalton's Law applied to component A:**

$$P_A = y_A \cdot P_{total}$$

**Substitute Raoult's Law for $P_A$:**

$$y_A \cdot P_{total} = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}$$

**Solve for $y_A$:**

$$y_A = \dfrac{x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}}{P_{total}}$$

**Now substitute the total-pressure expression $P_{total} = x_A P_A^{sat} + x_B P_B^{sat}$:**

$$y_A = \dfrac{x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}}{x_A \cdot P_A^{sat} + x_B \cdot P_B^{sat}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Insight:** This expression describes the **dew-point curve** on a P‚Äďx‚Äďy diagram. Because $P_A^{sat} \neq P_B^{sat}$, the function $y_A(x_A)$ is **non-linear** even for an ideal solution, and the dew-point curve always lies **above** the bubble-point curve.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Inverse Relation ‚ÄĒ Liquid Composition from Vapor Composition

**Start with the same identity from Derivation 2:**

$$y_A \cdot P_{total} = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}$$

**Solve directly for $x_A$:**

$$x_A = \dfrac{y_A \cdot P_{total}}{P_A^{sat}}$$

**Substitute $P_{total} = P / 1$ (it is the known system pressure):**

$$x_A = \dfrac{y_A \cdot P}{P_A^{sat}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Use Case:** This inverse form is the workhorse of **dew-point temperature** calculations where vapor composition $y_A$ is given and we must find the liquid composition at equilibrium.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: K-Value and Relative Volatility for an Ideal Solution

**By definition, the K-value is:**

$$K_A = \dfrac{y_A}{x_A}$$

**From Derivation 2, $y_A = x_A P_A^{sat} / P$. Substituting:**

$$K_A = \dfrac{x_A P_A^{sat}/P}{x_A} = \dfrac{P_A^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

**Similarly for component B:**

$$K_B = \dfrac{P_B^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

**Relative volatility of A with respect to B:**

$$\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{K_A}{K_B} = \dfrac{P_A^{sat}(T)/P}{P_B^{sat}(T)/P} = \dfrac{P_A^{sat}(T)}{P_B^{sat}(T)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** For an *ideal* solution, $\alpha_{AB}$ depends **only on temperature**, not on composition! This makes the analysis of distillation columns remarkably simple in the ideal case.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Binary Benzene‚ÄďToluene at T = 105¬įC)

**Given:**
- $P_{benzene}^{sat}$ at 105¬įC = **160.5 kPa**
- $P_{toluene}^{sat}$ at 105¬įC = **55.3 kPa**
- Liquid mole fraction of benzene: $x_B = 0.40$, so $x_T = 0.60$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Partial pressures via Raoult's Law:**

$$P_B = x_B \cdot P_B^{sat} = 0.40 \times 160.5 = 64.20 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_T = x_T \cdot P_T^{sat} = 0.60 \times 55.3 = 33.18 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Total pressure via Dalton's Law:**

$$P_{total} = P_B + P_T = 64.20 + 33.18 = 97.38 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Vapor-phase composition:**

$$y_B = \dfrac{P_B}{P_{total}} = \dfrac{64.20}{97.38} = 0.6593$$

$$y_T = 1 - y_B = 0.3407$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ K-values:**

$$K_B = \dfrac{P_B^{sat}}{P} = \dfrac{160.5}{97.38} = 1.648$$

$$K_T = \dfrac{P_T^{sat}}{P} = \dfrac{55.3}{97.38} = 0.5679$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Relative volatility:**

$$\alpha_{BT} = \dfrac{K_B}{K_T} = \dfrac{1.648}{0.5679} = 2.902$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since $\alpha_{BT} > 1$, benzene is the **more volatile** (lighter) component ‚ÄĒ consistent with its lower boiling point (80.1¬įC vs 110.6¬įC for toluene). The vapor phase is **richer in benzene** than the liquid phase ($y_B = 0.659 > x_B = 0.40$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Vapor‚ÄďLiquid Equilibrium under Raoult's Law

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid Solution at T, P] --> B[Apply Raoult's Law to each component]
    B --> C[Compute partial pressures P_i = x_i * P_i_sat_T]
    C --> D[Apply Dalton's Law: P_total = sum of P_i]
    D --> E{What is given?}
    E -- Known x_i, find P_total --> F[Bubble Point Pressure Calculation]
    E -- Known P_total, find x_i --> G[Bubble Point Composition Calculation]
    E -- Known y_i, find T --> H[Dew Point Temperature Calculation]
    F --> I[Output: P_total, P_i values]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Iterate T until sum of y_i equals 1]
    J --> K[Output: Equilibrium T, x_i, y_i]
    I --> L[Compute K-values K_i = P_i_sat / P]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Compute Relative Volatility alpha_ij = K_i / K_j]
    M --> N[Final VLE Solution: x_i, y_i, T, P]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: P‚Äďx‚Äďy Phase Behavior for an Ideal Binary Solution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["System Inputs"]
        X[Temperature T - constant]
        Y[Pure component vapor pressures P_A_sat, P_B_sat]
    end

    subgraph BubbleCurve["Bubble Point Curve P vs x"]
        B1[At x_A = 0, P = P_B_sat]
        B2[Linear: P = P_B_sat + x_A times P_A_sat - P_B_sat]
        B3[At x_A = 1, P = P_A_sat]
    end

    subgraph DewCurve["Dew Point Curve P vs y"]
        D1[At y_A = 0, P = P_B_sat]
        D2[Nonlinear curve above bubble line]
        D3[At y_A = 1, P = P_A_sat]
    end

    subgraph Gap["Two-Phase Region"]
        G[Liquid and Vapor coexist between curves]
    end

    X --> BubbleCurve
    Y --> BubbleCurve
    X --> DewCurve
    Y --> DewCurve
    BubbleCurve --> Gap
    DewCurve --> Gap
    Gap --> H[K-value = y_i divided by x_i = P_i_sat / P]
    Gap --> I[Relative volatility alpha = P_A_sat / P_B_sat - constant with x]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Map: From Molecular Reality to Engineering Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Molecular Level] --> N[Intermolecular forces are similar]
    N --> O[Ideal Solution: gamma_i = 1 for all i]
    O --> P[Fugacity equality at VLE: f_i_V = f_i_L]
    P --> Q[Simplifies to: y_i * P = x_i * P_i_sat]
    Q --> R[RAOULT'S LAW in its engineering form]
    R --> S[Application: Distillation Column Design]
    R --> T[Application: Flash Drum Sizing]
    R --> U[Application: Environmental Emission Modeling]
    R --> V[Application: Refrigeration Cycle Analysis]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these block diagrams?** Since physical phase diagrams (P‚Äďx‚Äďy, T‚Äďx‚Äďy) cannot be drawn natively in Mermaid, the diagrams above capture the **functional flow of information and logic** that a student must follow while solving VLE problems. They are perfectly suited for KTU's process-oriented question style.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ūüďė PART A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
*State Raoult's Law for an ideal solution. Write the expression for the partial pressure of each component in a binary liquid mixture.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Raoult's Law:** *At a given temperature, the partial pressure of any component in an ideal solution is equal to the mole fraction of that component in the liquid phase multiplied by its pure-component vapor pressure at that temperature.*

For a binary mixture of A and B:

$$P_A = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}(T) \quad \text{and} \quad P_B = x_B \cdot P_B^{sat}(T)$$

The total pressure is given by Dalton's Law: $P_{total} = P_A + P_B = x_A P_A^{sat} + x_B P_B^{sat}$.

**[3 Marks: 1 ‚Äď Statement, 1 ‚Äď Equations, 1 ‚Äď Total pressure form]**

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, Model Paper Style]**
*Define the K-value for a component in vapor‚Äďliquid equilibrium. Express it for an ideal solution obeying Raoult's Law.*

**Model Answer:**

The **K-value** (vapor‚Äďliquid equilibrium ratio) of component $i$ is defined as the ratio of its mole fraction in the vapor phase to that in the liquid phase at equilibrium:

$$K_i = \dfrac{y_i}{x_i}$$

For an ideal solution obeying Raoult's Law, since $y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}$, we obtain:

$$K_i = \dfrac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

The K-value is **dimensionless** and depends only on temperature and system pressure for an ideal solution.

**[3 Marks: 1 ‚Äď Definition, 1 ‚Äď Derivation link, 1 ‚Äď Final form]**

---

## ūüďó PART B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Paper Style, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the total pressure of a binary ideal solution as a function of liquid mole fraction at constant temperature, starting from Raoult's Law and Dalton's Law. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A liquid mixture contains 40 mol% benzene and 60 mol% toluene. At 100¬įC, the vapor pressures of pure benzene and toluene are 1350 mmHg and 550 mmHg, respectively. Assuming Raoult's Law applies, calculate:
  - (i) The total pressure of the system
  - (ii) The composition of the vapor in equilibrium
  - (iii) The relative volatility of benzene with respect to toluene **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation (7 Marks):**

Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Write Raoult's Law for each component:

$$P_A = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}(T) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$P_B = x_B \cdot P_B^{sat}(T) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply Dalton's Law of partial pressures:

$$P_{total} = P_A + P_B \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Substitute (i) and (ii) into (iii):

$$P_{total} = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat} + x_B \cdot P_B^{sat}$$

Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Use the mole balance for a binary system: $x_B = 1 - x_A$:

$$P_{total} = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat} + (1 - x_A) \cdot P_B^{sat}$$

Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Rearrange into linear form:

$$P_{total} = P_B^{sat} + x_A \left(P_A^{sat} - P_B^{sat}\right)$$

This is a **linear equation in $x_A$** at constant T, with intercept $P_B^{sat}$ (when $x_A = 0$) and slope $(P_A^{sat} - P_B^{sat})$.

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $x_B = 0.40$, $x_T = 0.60$, $P_B^{sat} = 1350$ mmHg, $P_T^{sat} = 550$ mmHg.

**(i) Total Pressure (3 Marks):**

$$P_B = x_B \cdot P_B^{sat} = 0.40 \times 1350 = 540 \text{ mmHg}$$

$$P_T = x_T \cdot P_T^{sat} = 0.60 \times 550 = 330 \text{ mmHg}$$

$$P_{total} = 540 + 330 = 870 \text{ mmHg}$$

**(ii) Vapor Composition (2 Marks):**

$$y_B = \dfrac{P_B}{P_{total}} = \dfrac{540}{870} = 0.6207$$

$$y_T = 1 - y_B = 0.3793$$

**(iii) Relative Volatility (2 Marks):**

$$\alpha_{BT} = \dfrac{P_B^{sat}}{P_T^{sat}} = \dfrac{1350}{550} = 2.455$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification:** $\alpha_{BT} > 1$ confirms benzene is the more volatile (lighter) component. The vapor is enriched in benzene ($y_B = 0.62 > x_B = 0.40$), consistent with physical intuition.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Paper Style, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Starting from Raoult's Law, derive the expressions for:
  - (i) Vapor-phase mole fraction in terms of liquid-phase mole fraction
  - (ii) K-value and relative volatility for an ideal binary solution **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An equimolar liquid mixture of n-hexane and n-heptane is in equilibrium with its vapor at a total pressure of 1 atm. Using the Antoine equation (or given data):
  - $P_{hexane}^{sat}$ at 80¬įC = **902 mmHg**
  - $P_{heptane}^{sat}$ at 80¬įC = **428 mmHg**
  
  Calculate the vapor composition and the K-values of both components. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivations (7 Marks):**

**(i) Vapor Composition (3 Marks):**

By Dalton's Law applied to component A: $P_A = y_A \cdot P_{total}$.

By Raoult's Law: $P_A = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}$.

Equating:

$$y_A \cdot P_{total} = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}$$

Using $P_{total} = x_A P_A^{sat} + x_B P_B^{sat}$:

$$\boxed{y_A = \dfrac{x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}}{x_A \cdot P_A^{sat} + x_B \cdot P_B^{sat}}}$$

**(ii) K-Value and Relative Volatility (4 Marks):**

By definition:

$$K_A = \dfrac{y_A}{x_A} = \dfrac{P_A^{sat}}{P_{total}} \quad \text{(valid for ideal solution)}$$

Similarly:

$$K_B = \dfrac{P_B^{sat}}{P_{total}}$$

Relative volatility:

$$\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{K_A}{K_B} = \dfrac{P_A^{sat}(T)}{P_B^{sat}(T)}$$

This depends **only on temperature** for an ideal solution.

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $x_{hex} = x_{hep} = 0.50$, $P = 760$ mmHg, $P_{hex}^{sat} = 902$ mmHg, $P_{hep}^{sat} = 428$ mmHg.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Total Pressure (2 Marks):**

$$P_{total} = 0.50 \times 902 + 0.50 \times 428 = 451 + 214 = 665 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Vapor Composition (3 Marks):**

$$y_{hex} = \dfrac{x_{hex} \cdot P_{hex}^{sat}}{P_{total}} = \dfrac{0.50 \times 902}{665} = \dfrac{451}{665} = 0.6782$$

$$y_{hep} = 1 - 0.6782 = 0.3218$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ K-Values (2 Marks):**

$$K_{hex} = \dfrac{P_{hex}^{sat}}{P} = \dfrac{902}{760} = 1.187$$

$$K_{hep} = \dfrac{P_{hep}^{sat}}{P} = \dfrac{428}{760} = 0.5632$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** $K_{hex} > 1$ means hexane prefers the vapor phase (more volatile, lower boiling point 68.7¬įC vs 98.4¬įC for heptane), and $K_{hep} < 1$ means heptane prefers the liquid phase. Their product $K_{hex} \times K_{hep} = 0.668 \neq 1$ because the system is not at the pure-component saturation state ‚ÄĒ this is a *normal* result and **not** an error.

---

## ‚ö†ÔłŹ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Raoult's Law Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mmHg with kPa, or ¬įC with K, in the same problem. The examiner **strictly deducts 1 mark** for missing or wrong units. Always state units explicitly.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Closure Relation:** Students often write $P = x_A P_A^{sat} + x_B P_B^{sat}$ but forget that $x_A + x_B = 1$. If asked to express $P$ as a function of a *single* variable, this step is **mandatory** and worth 1‚Äď2 marks.
> 
> 3. **Confusing Raoult's and Dalton's Laws:** Raoult's Law relates **liquid composition ‚Üí partial pressure**; Dalton's Law relates **partial pressure ‚Üí vapor composition**. Reversing these is a classic KTU mistake.
> 
> 4. **Skipping Intermediate Steps:** Examiners award *step marks*. Writing only the final answer without showing the partial pressures, total pressure, and substitution costs you 2‚Äď3 marks easily.
> 
> 5. **Relative Volatility Direction:** $\alpha_{AB} > 1$ means A is the **more volatile** (lighter key). Reversing the ratio ($P_B^{sat}/P_A^{sat}$) flips the meaning entirely.

---

## ‚úÖ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Raoult's Law (Component form):** $P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}(T)$ ‚ÄĒ valid for **ideal solutions** where all molecules have similar intermolecular forces.
- **Total Pressure Equation:** $P_{total} = \sum x_i P_i^{sat}$ ‚ÄĒ linear in $x_i$ at constant T.
- **Vapor Composition:** $y_i = x_i P_i^{sat} / P_{total}$ ‚ÄĒ derived from combining Raoult's + Dalton's Laws.
- **Inverse Form (Dew Point):** $x_i = y_i P_{total} / P_i^{sat}$.
- **K-Value (Ideal):** $K_i = P_i^{sat}(T) / P_{total}$ ‚ÄĒ temperature- and pressure-dependent but composition-independent.
- **Relative Volatility (Ideal):** $\alpha_{ij} = P_i^{sat}(T) / P_j^{sat}(T)$ ‚ÄĒ function of **T only** for ideal systems.
- **Bubble Point:** The condition $\sum x_i P_i^{sat} = P_{total}$ or $\sum K_i x_i = 1$.
- **Dew Point:** The condition $\sum y_i / K_i = 1$ or $\sum y_i P / P_i^{sat} = 1$.
- **P‚Äďx‚Äďy Diagram:** Bubble-point line is a **straight line**; dew-point line is a **curve** always above it; both meet at the pure-component points.
- **Antoine Equation:** $\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - B/(C+T)$ ‚ÄĒ used to obtain $P^{sat}$ when tabulated data is unavailable; constants A, B, C are component-specific.
- **Henry's Law** is the complementary law for **solute** components at infinite dilution ($P_i = x_i H_i$), while Raoult's Law is for the **solvent** ‚ÄĒ together they cover the full composition range.
- **Validity:** Raoult's Law holds for the **solvent** in a dilute solution; it *fails* for strongly non-ideal systems (e.g., chloroform‚Äďacetone with hydrogen bonding), where Modified Raoult's with $\gamma_i$ is used.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Henry's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Henry's Law ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Henry's Law** is a fundamental thermodynamic principle that governs the solubility of a *solute gas* in a *liquid solvent* under conditions of **dilute (ideal-dilute) solutions**. It was first formulated experimentally by the English chemist **William Henry (1803)**. > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Board Definition** > *"At a constant temperature, the partial pressure of a solute gas in the vapour phase is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the liquid phase, provided the solution is sufficiently dilute so that the solute obeys ideal-dilute behaviour."* Mathematically, the limiting statement is: $$ \lim_{x_i \to 0}\, \frac{\hat{f}_i^{\,L}}{x_i} \;=\; \mathcal{H}_i \;=\; \text{finite constant (at fixed T)} $$ Equivalently, in terms of partial pressure (for low total pressures where the vapour is ideal): $$ p_i \;=\; \mathcal{H}_i \, x_i \qquad \text{(Henry's Law in mole-fraction form)} $$ Where: - $p_i$ = Partial pressure of solute gas *i* in vapour phase - $x_i$ = Mole fraction of solute gas *i* in liquid phase - $\mathcal{H}_i$ = **Henry's Law Constant** (a function of temperature only) --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ "The Soda Bottle Mental Model" Imagine a sealed bottle of **carbonated water (soda)**. Inside, CO‚āā gas is dissolved in liquid water under high pressure. The amount of CO‚āā the liquid can "hold" is governed by the pressure pushing down on the liquid surface ‚ÄĒ the higher the pressure, the more gas is squeezed in. Now, consider Henry's Law as the **"happiness index" of dissolved gas molecules**: > A gas molecule at the liquid surface is *partially happy* (some want to escape into vapour, some want to stay dissolved). Henry's constant $\mathcal{H}$ tells you how *reluctant* the gas is to dissolve. A **large $\mathcal{H}$** means the gas dislikes the solvent (e.g., O‚āā in water ‚ÄĒ low solubility). A **small $\mathcal{H}$** means the gas loves the solvent (e.g., NH‚āÉ in water ‚ÄĒ very high solubility). **Real-life engineering examples:** - **Scuba diving** (decompression sickness): Henry's Law explains why ascending too fast causes dissolved N‚āā to form bubbles in blood. - **Aeration tanks in wastewater treatment**: Oxygen dissolution rate is governed by Henry's constant of O‚āā in water. - **Carbonated beverage industry**: CO‚āā saturation pressure dictates bottling conditions. - **Gas absorption columns** (amine treating of natural gas): Henry's constants of H‚āāS, CO‚āā in MDEA determine tower height. --- ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value (CO‚āā in H‚āāO at 298 K) | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Henry's Constant | $\mathcal{H}$ | $\approx 1.67 \times 10^{3}$ | bar | | Inverse Henry's Constant | $k_H^{\text{cp}}$ | $\approx 3.4 \times 10^{-2}$ | mol/(L¬∑atm) | | Standard Temperature | $T^{\circ}$ | **298.15 K** | K | | Standard Pressure | $P^{\circ}$ | **1 bar** or **1 atm** | bar / atm | > [!NOTE] **KTU Memory Tip** > Always write the **temperature** alongside any reported Henry's constant value, because $\mathcal{H}$ is a *strong* function of T. Reporting $\mathcal{H}$ without T is meaningless and will fetch zero marks in derivations. --- ### 1.4 The Three "Ideal" Reference Frames Henry's Law is one of the **two limits** of non-ideal solution behaviour (the other being **Raoult's Law**). Understanding this duality is *critical* for KTU board exams. > [!IMPORTANT] **The Lewis-Randall + Henry Duality** > - **Raoult's Law** applies to the **solvent** (species present in large amounts): $p_1 = x_1 p_1^{\text{sat}}$ > - **Henry's Law** applies to the **solute** (species present in trace amounts): $p_2 = x_2 \mathcal{H}_2$ > - These two laws together define the **complete pressure-composition (P-x-y) diagram** for a binary system. This is the conceptual foundation of **VLE (Vapour-Liquid Equilibrium)** calculations ‚ÄĒ a *high-weightage* topic in KTU ESE papers. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation ‚ÄĒ Why Henry's Law Works Henry's Law is **not a universal law** ‚ÄĒ it is a *limiting law* valid only in the **infinite-dilution regime**. The rigorous thermodynamic justification arises from the **equality of fugacities** at phase equilibrium. For a solute *i* distributed between liquid (L) and vapour (V) phases at equilibrium: $$ \hat{f}_i^{\,V} \;=\; \hat{f}_i^{\,L} $$ At low total pressure (ideal vapour phase), the vapour-side fugacity reduces to partial pressure: $$ \hat{f}_i^{\,V} \;\approx\; y_i \, P \;=\; p_i $$ For the liquid side, the **Lewis-Randall rule** would give $\hat{f}_i^{\,L} = x_i f_i^{\,\text{pure}}$, but this fails for a *solute that does not exist as a pure liquid* at the system temperature (e.g., a non-condensable gas like O‚āā, H‚āā, CH‚āĄ at 25 ¬įC). Instead, we define a **Henry's-law reference fugacity** $f_i^{\,*}$ based on the extrapolated ideal-dilute line: $$ \hat{f}_i^{\,L} \;=\; x_i \, \mathcal{H}_i \quad \text{where } \mathcal{H}_i \;\equiv\; \lim_{x_i \to 0} \frac{\hat{f}_i^{\,L}}{x_i} $$ The activity coefficient $\gamma_i^{*}$ on the Henry's-law basis is therefore: $$ \gamma_i^{*} \;\equiv\; \frac{\hat{f}_i^{\,L}}{x_i \, \mathcal{H}_i} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \gamma_i^{*} \;\to\; 1 \;\text{ as }\; x_i \;\to\; 0 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] **Distinction From Raoult's Law** > In Raoult's Law, the standard state is the **pure liquid** (Lewis-Randall reference), and $\gamma_i \to 1$ as $x_i \to 1$. > In Henry's Law, the standard state is the **hypothetical pure liquid whose fugacity equals $\mathcal{H}_i$**, and $\gamma_i^{*} \to 1$ as $x_i \to 0$. --- ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] **All formulas below are board-exam-essential. Memorize the forms, units, and reference frame for each.** | # | Formula | Meaning / Context | Units of constant | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $p_i = \mathcal{H}_i \, x_i$ | Mole-fraction form (most common in KTU) | bar, atm, Pa | | 2 | $y_i P = \mathcal{H}_i \, x_i$ | Combined with Dalton's law | bar, atm, Pa | | 3 | $p_i = k_{H,m} \, m_i$ | Molality form | bar¬∑kg/mol | | 4 | $p_i = k_{H,c} \, c_i$ | Molarity form | bar¬∑L/mol | | 5 | $m_i = k_{H,m}^{\text{cp}} \, p_i$ | Inverse Henry (concentration form) | mol/(kg¬∑atm) | | 6 | $\mathcal{H}_i (T_2) = \mathcal{H}_i (T_1) \exp\!\left[\dfrac{-\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)\right]$ | van't Hoff temperature dependence | ‚ÄĒ | | 7 | $\ln \mathcal{H}_i = A - \dfrac{B}{T} + C \ln T + D T$ | Empirical multi-constant form | ‚ÄĒ | | 8 | $\Delta \overline{g}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty} = RT \ln \mathcal{H}_i / P^{\circ}$ | Partial molar Gibbs energy of solution | J/mol | | 9 | $K_i^{\text{pc}} = y_i / x_i = \mathcal{H}_i / P$ | Phase-change K-value | dimensionless | | 10 | $\gamma_i^{*} = \hat{\phi}_i^{\,V} y_i P / (x_i \mathcal{H}_i)$ | Henry-basis activity coefficient | dimensionless | **Where the symbols denote:** - $m_i$ = molality (mol solute / kg solvent) - $c_i$ = molarity (mol solute / L solution) - $\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty}$ = **partial molar enthalpy of solution at infinite dilution** - $P^{\circ}$ = standard-state pressure (1 bar) - $\hat{\phi}_i^{\,V}$ = vapour-phase fugacity coefficient of species *i* > [!WARNING] **Common Pitfall ‚ÄĒ Sign Convention for $\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty}$** > For **exothermic dissolution** (e.g., HCl in water, $\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}} < 0$), $\mathcal{H}$ *decreases* with increasing T (less gas dissolves at higher T ‚ÄĒ matches Le Chatelier). > For **endothermic dissolution** (e.g., noble gases in water, $\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}} > 0$), $\mathcal{H}$ *increases* with increasing T. --- ### 2.3 Physical Significance & Engineering Utility | Engineering Domain | Application of Henry's Law | |---|---| | **Natural Gas Processing** | Sour gas (H‚āāS, CO‚āā) removal using amine solvents ‚ÄĒ design of absorber height | | **Bioreactor Design** | O‚āā mass transfer from sparged air to fermentation broth ‚ÄĒ volumetric $k_La$ | | **Environmental Engineering** | VOC stripping from contaminated groundwater | | **Distillation** | Solute line on P-x-y diagram for non-ideal systems | | **Pharmaceutical / Brewing** | CO‚āā and O‚āā control in carbonated beverages, beer | | **Polymer Foaming** | Predicting gas solubility for foam extrusion | --- ### 2.4 Conversion Between Different Forms This is a **favourite KTU question**. The three Henry's constants are related through the **molar mass** $M_{\text{solv}}$ of solvent and **solution density** $\rho_{\text{soln}}$: $$ k_{H,m} \;=\; k_{H,c} \cdot \rho_{\text{soln}} \quad \text{(if } \rho_{\text{soln}} \approx \rho_{\text{solv}} \text{ at infinite dilution)} $$ $$ \mathcal{H}_i \;=\; k_{H,m} \cdot M_{\text{solv}} \cdot P^{\circ} \quad \text{(in consistent units)} $$ > [!NOTE] **Cross-check identity (always valid):** > $x_i \, \mathcal{H}_i \;=\; m_i \, k_{H,m} \;=\; c_i \, k_{H,c}$ (numerically when all Henry's constants are expressed in the same pressure basis). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Henry's Law from Equal-Fugacity Criterion We begin from the rigorous phase-equilibrium condition for a non-condensable solute gas *i* in a liquid solvent. **Step 1:** Write the equality of component fugacities between vapour and liquid phases at equilibrium: $$ \hat{f}_i^{\,V} \;=\; \hat{f}_i^{\,L} $$ **Step 2:** For the vapour phase, invoke the **ideal-gas approximation** (valid when total pressure $P \le 5$ bar and temperature is well above critical): $$ \hat{f}_i^{\,V} \;=\; y_i \, P $$ (Here $y_i$ is the vapour-phase mole fraction of solute *i*.) **Step 3:** For the liquid phase, we do **not** use Lewis-Randall because the solute may not exist as a pure liquid at temperature T. Instead, define a **Henry's-law reference state** such that the activity coefficient $\gamma_i^{*}$ approaches unity in the limit of infinite dilution. The standard-state fugacity for this convention is $f_i^{\,*} \equiv \mathcal{H}_i$, giving: $$ \hat{f}_i^{\,L} \;=\; x_i \, \gamma_i^{*} \, \mathcal{H}_i $$ **Step 4:** Substituting both expressions into the equality: $$ y_i \, P \;=\; x_i \, \gamma_i^{*} \, \mathcal{H}_i $$ **Step 5:** Now impose the **infinite-dilution limit**, $x_i \to 0$. In this limit, the solute molecules are so far apart that they behave ideally (no solute-solute interactions), so: $$ \gamma_i^{*} \;\to\; 1 \quad \text{as} \quad x_i \to 0 $$ **Step 6:** The vapour composition $y_i$ simultaneously tends to a value determined purely by the solvent's vapour pressure, but in the solute-only Henry relation we focus on the liquid-side proportionality: $$ \lim_{x_i \to 0}\, \frac{y_i \, P}{x_i} \;=\; \mathcal{H}_i $$ **Step 7:** If we further assume the solvent is **non-volatile** (e.g., water absorbing a permanent gas), then $y_i \to 1$ at infinite solute dilution in the vapour. But for a general binary at low $x_i$, we keep $y_i P$ as the partial pressure: $$ p_i \;\equiv\; y_i P \;=\; \mathcal{H}_i \, x_i $$ This is **Henry's Law** in its classical mole-fraction form. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the van't Hoff Temperature Dependence The Henry's constant $\mathcal{H}_i$ changes with temperature because the **partial molar enthalpy of solution at infinite dilution**, $\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty}$, is non-zero. **Step 1:** Start from the rigorous thermodynamic relation for the temperature dependence of any equilibrium constant $K$: $$ \frac{d \ln K}{dT} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}} $$ **Step 2:** Identify the equilibrium constant with $\mathcal{H}_i / P^{\circ}$ (a dimensionless ratio): $$ K_i \;\equiv\; \frac{\mathcal{H}_i(T)}{P^{\circ}} $$ **Step 3:** Substitute into the van't Hoff equation, noting that the relevant enthalpy change is $\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty}$ (the enthalpy change when 1 mol of solute gas is transferred from the gas phase at standard pressure to an infinite-dilution liquid solution): $$ \frac{d \ln (\mathcal{H}_i / P^{\circ})}{dT} \;=\; \frac{\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty}}{R T^{2}} $$ **Step 4:** Assuming $\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty}$ is approximately **constant** over the temperature range of interest, separate variables and integrate from $T_1$ to $T_2$: $$ \int_{\mathcal{H}_1}^{\mathcal{H}_2} d \ln \mathcal{H}_i \;=\; \frac{\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^{2}} $$ **Step 5:** Evaluate the integrals: $$ \ln \frac{\mathcal{H}_2}{\mathcal{H}_1} \;=\; \frac{\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) $$ **Step 6:** Rearrange into the canonical KTU board form: $$ \boxed{\;\mathcal{H}_i(T_2) \;=\; \mathcal{H}_i(T_1) \exp\!\left[\frac{\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]\;} $$ **Step 7 (Sanity check):** - If dissolution is **exothermic**, $\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty} < 0$. For $T_2 > T_1$, the exponential argument is negative, so $\mathcal{H}_2 < \mathcal{H}_1$ ‚Üí solubility decreases with T ‚úď - If dissolution is **endothermic**, $\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty} > 0$. For $T_2 > T_1$, $\mathcal{H}_2 > \mathcal{H}_1$ ‚Üí solubility increases with T ‚úď --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example ‚ÄĒ A KTU-Style Sub-Problem **Problem:** The Henry's constant of methane (CH‚āĄ) in water is **40,600 atm** at 25 ¬įC and **55,800 atm** at 60 ¬įC. Determine: 1. The partial molar enthalpy of solution at infinite dilution. 2. The Henry's constant of CH‚āĄ at 80 ¬įC. 3. The mole fraction of CH‚āĄ dissolved in water at 80 ¬įC when exposed to biogas at 1.5 atm partial pressure of CH‚āĄ. **Solution:** **Part (a):** Apply the van't Hoff relation: $$ \ln \frac{\mathcal{H}_2}{\mathcal{H}_1} \;=\; \frac{\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) $$ Substitute values: $\mathcal{H}_1 = 40600$ atm, $\mathcal{H}_2 = 55800$ atm, $T_1 = 298.15$ K, $T_2 = 333.15$ K. $$ \ln \frac{55800}{40600} \;=\; \ln(1.3744) \;=\; 0.3180 $$ Compute the temperature term: $$ \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \;=\; \frac{1}{298.15} - \frac{1}{333.15} \;=\; 3.354 \times 10^{-3} - 3.002 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 3.52 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{K}^{-1} $$ Solve for $\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty}$: $$ \Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty} \;=\; \frac{R \cdot 0.3180}{3.52 \times 10^{-4}} \;=\; \frac{8.314 \times 0.3180}{3.52 \times 10^{-4}} \;=\; \frac{2.644}{3.52 \times 10^{-4}} \;\approx\; 7510 \;\text{J/mol} $$ > $\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty} \approx +7.51 \;\text{kJ/mol}$ (positive ‚Üí endothermic, as expected for non-polar gas in water) **Part (b):** Use the same relation with $T_3 = 353.15$ K: $$ \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_3} \;=\; 3.354 \times 10^{-3} - 2.832 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 5.22 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{K}^{-1} $$ $$ \ln \frac{\mathcal{H}_3}{\mathcal{H}_1} \;=\; \frac{7510}{8.314} \times 5.22 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 903.3 \times 5.22 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 0.4715 $$ $$ \mathcal{H}_3 \;=\; 40600 \times e^{0.4715} \;=\; 40600 \times 1.6024 \;\approx\; 65060 \;\text{atm} $$ > $\mathcal{H}_{\text{CH}_4}(80\,¬į\text{C}) \approx 6.51 \times 10^{4}$ atm **Part (c):** Apply Henry's Law directly: $$ x_{\text{CH}_4} \;=\; \frac{p_{\text{CH}_4}}{\mathcal{H}_3} \;=\; \frac{1.5 \;\text{atm}}{65060 \;\text{atm}} \;\approx\; 2.31 \times 10^{-5} $$ > $x_{\text{CH}_4} \approx 2.31 \times 10^{-5}$ (extremely low ‚ÄĒ confirms the dilute-solute nature assumed by Henry's Law) --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix ‚ÄĒ Henry vs. Raoult | Feature | Raoult's Law | Henry's Law | |---|---|---| | Applicable species | **Solvent** (high $x_i$) | **Solute** (low $x_i$) | | Standard state | **Pure liquid** at system T | **Hypothetical pure liquid** at $\mathcal{H}_i$ | | Mathematical form | $p_i = x_i \, p_i^{\text{sat}}$ | $p_i = x_i \, \mathcal{H}_i$ | | Limiting condition | $x_i \to 1$ | $x_i \to 0$ | | Activity coefficient | $\gamma_i \to 1$ as $x_i \to 1$ | $\gamma_i^{*} \to 1$ as $x_i \to 0$ | | Graph on P-x diagram | Tangent from origin (solvent) | Tangent at $x_i = 0$ (solute line) | | Applies to | Volatile species | Non-volatile solutes, non-condensable gases | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 P-x-y Phase Diagram with Both Laws Superimposed ```mermaid graph LR A["Composition Axis
x1 or y1 = 0"] -->|Low x1| B["HENRYS LAW REGIME
Solute side
p2 = H2 times x2"] B -->|Transition zone| C["Non ideal middle
Curved line
gamma not equal to 1"] C -->|High x1| D["RAOULTS LAW REGIME
Solvent side
p1 = x1 times p1sat"] D --> E["Composition Axis
x1 or y1 = 1"] F["Vapour line y1"] -->|Above B| G["DEW CURVE"] F -->|Above D| H["BUBBLE CURVE"] G --> H ``` ### 4.2 Henry's Law Application Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A["GAS MIXTURE
Total Pressure P
Composition yi"] --> B["DALTONS LAW
pi = yi times P"] B --> C["PARTIAL PRESSURE
pi of solute i"] C --> D{"Is solution
sufficiently
dilute"} D -->|Yes| E["HENRYS LAW
pi = Hi times xi"] D -->|No| F["ACTIVITY MODEL
Margules or
van Laar or NRTL"] E --> G["SOLVE FOR xi
xi = pi over Hi"] F --> G G --> H["LIQUID COMPOSITION
xi determined"] H --> I["PROCESS DESIGN
Absorber height
Aeration rate
Stripping column"] ``` ### 4.3 Decision Flowchart ‚ÄĒ When to Apply Henry's vs. Raoult's ```mermaid graph TD A["Start
Identify species i"] --> B{"Is species i
the SOLVENT
majority component"} B -->|Yes| C["Apply RAOULTS LAW
pi = xi times pisat"] B -->|No| D{"Is species i
a DILUTE SOLUTE
in liquid phase"} D -->|Yes| E{"Does species i
exist as pure
