liquid at system T"} E -->|No| F["Apply HENRYS LAW
pi = xi times Hi
Use Hi from tables"] E -->|Yes| G["Henry and Raoult coincide
Hi approaches pisat"] D -->|No| H["Use full activity model
Margules or NRTL or UNIQUAC"] F --> I["End"] C --> I G --> I H --> I ``` ### 4.4 Schematic ‚ÄĒ Gas Absorption Column Using Henry's Framework ```mermaid graph TD A["Gas Inlet
Rich in CO2 or H2S
High partial pressure pi top"] --> B["ABSORBER COLUMN
Tray or Packing"] B --> C["Counter current flow
Lean solvent enters top
Rich solvent exits bottom"] C --> D["Liquid Outlet
Loaded with solute
xi bottom = pi top over Hi"] B --> E["Gas Outlet
Lean gas leaves top
pi top < pi bottom"] F["Solvent Inlet
Fresh or regenerated
xi in approx 0"] --> C D --> G["REGENERATOR
Stripping column"] G --> H["Acid gas released
High T or Low P"] G --> F ``` ### 4.5 Thermodynamic Origin of Henry's Law ‚ÄĒ Block View ```mermaid graph TD A["Phase Equilibrium
fi super V equals fi super L"] --> B["Vapour side
Ideal gas assumed
fi V equals yi P"] A --> C["Liquid side
Dilute limit
fi L equals xi gamma star times Hi"] B --> D["Combine both sides
yi P equals xi gamma star Hi"] C --> D D --> E{"Take limit
xi approaches 0"} E --> F["gamma star approaches 1
Infinite dilution ideal"] F --> G["Result
pi equals Hi times xi
HENRYS LAW"] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024, Model]** **State Henry's Law. Explain how it differs from Raoult's Law and indicate when each is used.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Henry's Law states that at constant temperature, the **partial pressure of a solute gas in vapour phase is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the liquid phase**, in the limit of infinite dilution: $$ p_i = \mathcal{H}_i \, x_i \quad \text{as} \quad x_i \to 0 $$ **Distinction from Raoult's Law:** | Criterion | Henry's Law | Raoult's Law | |---|---|---| | Applies to | Solute (low $x_i$) | Solvent (high $x_i$) | | Standard state | Hypothetical liquid at $\mathcal{H}_i$ | Pure liquid at $p_i^{\text{sat}}$ | | Limit | $x_i \to 0$ | $x_i \to 1$ | Raoult's Law is used when the species is the **major component (solvent)**; Henry's Law is used for **minor components (solutes)** such as gases dissolved in liquids where the pure-liquid reference state may not be physically realisable. *(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1.5 marks for distinction, 0.5 mark for application context.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023, Adapted]** **The Henry's constant of O‚āā in water at 298 K is $4.34 \times 10^{4}$ atm. Calculate the mole fraction of O‚āā in water exposed to air at 1 atm total pressure. Air contains 21 mol % O‚āā.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Partial pressure of O‚āā in air (Dalton's Law): $$ p_{\text{O}_2} = y_{\text{O}_2} \cdot P = 0.21 \times 1.0 = 0.21 \;\text{atm} $$ Apply Henry's Law: $$ x_{\text{O}_2} = \frac{p_{\text{O}_2}}{\mathcal{H}_{\text{O}_2}} = \frac{0.21}{4.34 \times 10^{4}} = 4.84 \times 10^{-6} $$ **Answer:** $x_{\text{O}_2} \approx 4.84 \times 10^{-6}$ *(Award: 1 mark partial pressure, 1 mark formula, 1 mark final value with units.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks ‚ÄĒ Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Scheme Module Pattern** > Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into **Part (a) for 7 marks** and **Part (b) for 7 marks**. You are expected to attempt **any one** of the two alternatives Q-A or Q-B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Set 1]** **(a)** Starting from the rigorous criterion of equality of fugacities for a dilute solute gas in a liquid solvent, **derive Henry's Law**. Clearly state the assumptions involved. Show that the Henry's constant has units of pressure. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The Henry's constants for CO‚āā in water at three temperatures are: | Temperature (¬įC) | $\mathcal{H}_{\text{CO}_2}$ (atm) | |---|---| | 25 | $1.67 \times 10^{3}$ | | 40 | $2.49 \times 10^{3}$ | | 60 | $3.93 \times 10^{3}$ | Calculate the **partial molar enthalpy of solution at infinite dilution** using the van't Hoff equation. Comment on the sign of $\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty}$ and its physical meaning. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Q-A (a)** **(i) Equilibrium Criterion (1 mark):** For phase equilibrium of solute *i* between vapour (V) and liquid (L) at temperature T and pressure P: $$ \hat{f}_i^{\,V}(T, P, y) = \hat{f}_i^{\,L}(T, P, x) $$ **(ii) Vapour-side simplification (1 mark):** At low to moderate pressures (e.g., $P \le 5$ bar), the vapour phase behaves as an ideal gas, hence: $$ \hat{f}_i^{\,V} = y_i \, P = p_i $$ **(iii) Liquid-side activity model on Henry basis (2 marks):** Define the Henry's-law standard state such that the standard-state fugacity is $f_i^{*} \equiv \mathcal{H}_i$. Then: $$ \hat{f}_i^{\,L} = x_i \, \gamma_i^{*} \, \mathcal{H}_i $$ **(iv) Infinite-dilution limit (2 marks):** As $x_i \to 0$, the solute molecules do not interact with each other; therefore $\gamma_i^{*} \to 1$. Substituting: $$ p_i = x_i \, \mathcal{H}_i \quad \text{(Henry's Law)} $$ **(v) Units and assumptions (1 mark):** The Henry's constant has units of pressure because the right-hand side $x_i$ is dimensionless. **Assumptions:** (a) isothermal, (b) ideal vapour phase, (c) infinite dilution, (d) liquid incompressibility over the pressure range. --- #### **Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Q-A (b)** Use the **integrated van't Hoff equation**: $$ \ln \frac{\mathcal{H}_2}{\mathcal{H}_1} = \frac{\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty}}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) $$ **Data pairing 1 ‚Üí 2:** $T_1 = 298.15$ K, $T_2 = 313.15$ K, $\mathcal{H}_1 = 1670$ atm, $\mathcal{H}_2 = 2490$ atm. $$ \ln \frac{2490}{1670} = \ln(1.4910) = 0.3992 $$ $$ \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{298.15} - \frac{1}{313.15} = 3.354 \times 10^{-3} - 3.194 \times 10^{-3} = 1.602 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{K}^{-1} $$ $$ \Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty} = \frac{0.3992 \times 8.314}{1.602 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{3.319}{1.602 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 20,720 \;\text{J/mol} $$ **Data pairing 1 ‚Üí 3:** $T_3 = 333.15$ K, $\mathcal{H}_3 = 3930$ atm. $$ \ln \frac{3930}{1670} = \ln(2.3533) = 0.8559 $$ $$ \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_3} = 3.354 \times 10^{-3} - 3.002 \times 10^{-3} = 3.521 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{K}^{-1} $$ $$ \Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty} = \frac{0.8559 \times 8.314}{3.521 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 20,210 \;\text{J/mol} $$ **Average:** $\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty} \approx 20,470 \;\text{J/mol} \approx 20.5 \;\text{kJ/mol}$ **Comment (2 marks):** The positive sign indicates that dissolving CO‚āā in water is **endothermic**. Physically, the work required to create a cavity in water (breaking H-bonds) outweighs the weak solute-solvent interactions for non-polar CO‚āā. Le Chatelier's principle predicts that heating decreases gas solubility ‚ÄĒ confirmed by $\mathcal{H}$ increasing with T. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ ALTERNATIVE** **[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Set 2]** **(a)** Discuss the **different forms of Henry's Law** (mole-fraction, molality, molarity). Derive the conversion relation between the mole-fraction form and the molality form. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In an **ammonia absorption refrigeration cycle**, NH‚āÉ is absorbed from a gas mixture (60 mol % NH‚āÉ, 40 mol % N‚āā) at a total pressure of 2.5 bar into water at 30 ¬įC. The Henry's constant of NH‚āÉ in water at 30 ¬įC is **98.8 bar**. (i) Calculate the mole fraction of NH‚āÉ dissolved in the water at equilibrium. **(3 marks)** (ii) State **two assumptions** made in applying Henry's Law here. **(2 marks)** (iii) If the temperature is raised to 50 ¬įC (where $\mathcal{H}_{\text{NH}_3} = 198$ bar), determine the **partial molar enthalpy of solution at infinite dilution** of NH‚āÉ in water. **(2 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Q-B (a)** **Three forms of Henry's Law:** **(1) Mole-fraction form (2 marks):** Most fundamental; reference frame is mole fraction: $$ p_i = \mathcal{H}_i \, x_i \qquad \left[\mathcal{H}_i\right] = \text{pressure} $$ **(2) Molality form (2 marks):** Reference frame is molality $m_i$ (mol solute per kg solvent); useful because molality is independent of temperature (unlike molarity): $$ p_i = k_{H,m} \, m_i \qquad \left[k_{H,m}\right] = \text{bar} \cdot \text{kg/mol} $$ **(3) Molarity form (1 mark):** Reference frame is molar concentration $c_i$ (mol solute per litre solution): $$ p_i = k_{H,c} \, c_i \qquad \left[k_{H,c}\right] = \text{bar} \cdot \text{L/mol} $$ **Conversion derivation (2 marks):** The molality is related to mole fraction through: $$ m_i = \frac{n_i}{n_{\text{solv}} \, M_{\text{solv}}} = \frac{x_i}{x_{\text{solv}} \, M_{\text{solv}}} $$ For a **dilute solution** ($x_{\text{solv}} \to 1$), $x_{\text{solv}} \approx 1$, so $m_i \approx x_i / M_{\text{solv}}$. Equating partial pressures from both forms: $$ \mathcal{H}_i \, x_i = k_{H,m} \, m_i = k_{H,m} \, \frac{x_i}{M_{\text{solv}}} $$ Solving: $$ \boxed{\;\mathcal{H}_i = \frac{k_{H,m}}{M_{\text{solv}}}\;} $$ Or, in inverse form: $k_{H,m} = \mathcal{H}_i \, M_{\text{solv}}$. --- #### **Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Q-B (b)** **(i) Mole fraction of NH‚āÉ dissolved (3 marks):** Partial pressure of NH‚āÉ using Dalton's Law: $$ p_{\text{NH}_3} = y_{\text{NH}_3} \cdot P = 0.60 \times 2.5 = 1.5 \;\text{bar} $$ Applying Henry's Law: $$ x_{\text{NH}_3} = \frac{p_{\text{NH}_3}}{\mathcal{H}_{\text{NH}_3}} = \frac{1.5}{98.8} = 0.01518 $$ **Answer:** $x_{\text{NH}_3} \approx 1.518 \times 10^{-2}$ **(ii) Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. The solution is **dilute** in NH‚āÉ so that Henry's Law linear regime holds ($x_{\text{NH}_3} \ll 1$). 2. The vapour phase behaves as an **ideal gas** (justified at $P = 2.5$ bar). 3. The system is at **thermodynamic equilibrium** with no chemical reaction between NH‚āÉ and water. 4. (Optional bonus) Temperature and pressure are **uniform and constant** at the gas-liquid interface. **(iii) Partial molar enthalpy of solution (2 marks):** Apply the van't Hoff equation between $T_1 = 303.15$ K and $T_2 = 323.15$ K: $$ \ln \frac{\mathcal{H}_2}{\mathcal{H}_1} = \ln \frac{198}{98.8} = \ln(2.0040) = 0.6951 $$ $$ \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{303.15} - \frac{1}{323.15} = 3.299 \times 10^{-3} - 3.094 \times 10^{-3} = 2.046 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{K}^{-1} $$ $$ \Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty} = \frac{0.6951 \times 8.314}{2.046 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{5.779}{2.046 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 28,240 \;\text{J/mol} $$ **Answer:** $\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty} \approx 28.2 \;\text{kJ/mol}$ (positive, endothermic absorption). --- > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ‚ÄĒ Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting to state the standard state.** Henry's Law uses a *hypothetical* standard state, not the pure liquid. Lose 1‚Äď2 marks if you write "Henry's Law is $p_i = x_i p_i^{\text{sat}}$". > 2. **Wrong temperature dependence sign.** The exponential uses $\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$, not $\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$. Sign error = full credit loss on that sub-part. > 3. **Mixing molality and molarity units.** Always check whether the Henry's constant in the data is in mole-fraction form, molality form, or molarity form. Wrong unit conversion = 3-mark penalty. > 4. **Applying Henry's Law to the solvent.** This is the most common conceptual error. Henry's Law is for **solutes only**. > 5. **Neglecting to verify the dilute limit.** If the answer gives $x_i > 0.1$, the assumption breaks down ‚ÄĒ explicitly comment on this if the question allows for it. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Henry's Law** > > ‚úÖ **Core statement:** $p_i = \mathcal{H}_i \, x_i$ at constant T, valid for **dilute solutes**. > > ‚úÖ **Three forms exist:** mole-fraction, molality, molarity ‚ÄĒ know all three and the conversions. > > ‚úÖ **Henry's constant $\mathcal{H}_i$ has units of pressure** (bar, atm, Pa). It is **strongly temperature-dependent**. > > ‚úÖ **Raoult vs. Henry duality:** Raoult for solvent ($x_i \to 1$); Henry for solute ($x_i \to 0$). They form the two tangents on a P-x-y diagram. > > ‚úÖ **Standard state:** Henry's Law uses a **hypothetical pure liquid** whose fugacity equals $\mathcal{H}_i$. Raoult's Law uses the **real pure liquid** at $p_i^{\text{sat}}$. > > ‚úÖ **van't Hoff form:** $\ln(\mathcal{H}_2 / \mathcal{H}_1) = \dfrac{\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty}}{R} \left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ > > ‚úÖ **Sign of $\Delta \overline{h}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty}$:** Exothermic (negative) ‚Üí $\mathcal{H}$ decreases with T; Endothermic (positive) ‚Üí $\mathcal{H}$ increases with T. > > ‚úÖ **Engineering applications:** gas absorption, aeration, distillation, refrigeration cycles, environmental remediation. > > ‚úÖ **Limitation:** Fails as $x_i \to 1$. For concentrated solutions, use full activity-coefficient models (Margules, van Laar, Wilson, NRTL, UNIQUAC). > > ‚úÖ **K-value relation:** $K_i^{\text{pc}} = \mathcal{H}_i / P$ (a high-yield formula for VLE stage calculations). > > ‚úÖ **Gibbs energy of solution:** $\Delta \overline{g}_{\text{sol}}^{\infty} = RT \ln(\mathcal{H}_i / P^{\circ})$. > > ‚úÖ **In exam answers, always:** (a) state the assumption of dilute solution, (b) show the equality of fugacities, (c) mention the temperature explicitly with any $\mathcal{H}$ value, (d) verify units.

topics.title: Activity in Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Activity in Solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining "Activity" in Thermodynamic Solutions

In classical chemical thermodynamics, real (non-ideal) mixtures rarely obey the simple linear behavior assumed by Raoult's Law or Henry's Law. To preserve the **mathematical form** of ideal-solution equations while admitting deviations, the concept of **activity** is introduced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **activity ($a_i$)** of component $i$ in a solution is a dimensionless, effective concentration that replaces the true mole fraction in the thermodynamic equations for real solutions, such that the form of the ideal-solution expressions is retained.

Mathematically, activity is the ratio of the **fugacity of component $i$ in the real solution** to the **fugacity of $i$ in its chosen standard state**:

$$a_i = \frac{\hat{f}_i}{f_i^0}$$

where:
- $\hat{f}_i$ = fugacity of component $i$ in the real solution (at system $T$ and $P$)
- $f_i^0$ = fugacity of pure $i$ in the defined standard state (at same $T$)

The **activity coefficient ($\gamma_i$)** is the proportionality factor between activity and mole fraction:

$$\gamma_i = \frac{a_i}{x_i}$$

By this construction:
- For an **ideal solution**: $\gamma_i = 1$, hence $a_i = x_i$
- For a **real solution**: $\gamma_i \neq 1$, and $a_i \neq x_i$

| Type of Deviation | Condition on $\gamma_i$ | Typical Cause |
|:------------------|:------------------------|:--------------|
| Ideal solution    | $\gamma_i = 1$          | Athermal, like-like interactions |
| Positive deviation| $\gamma_i > 1$          | A‚ÄďB interactions weaker than A‚ÄďA and B‚ÄďB |
| Negative deviation| $\gamma_i < 1$          | A‚ÄďB interactions stronger than A‚ÄďA and B‚ÄďB |

## 1.2 Why Activity? ‚ÄĒ The Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy ‚ÄĒ "The Honest Map":**
> Imagine you are planning a road trip of **100 km on a straight highway**. The *ideal* solution is the highway ‚ÄĒ straight, predictable, and obeying the simple rule "distance = speed √ó time." But real roads have **hills, traffic, and diversions** that slow you down.
>
> The *activity* is like the **equivalent straight-line distance** you would have to travel on the ideal highway to take the same *thermodynamic "time" (energy)*. The *activity coefficient* is the "traffic correction factor" that reconverts the *actual curvy-road distance* into the equivalent *highway distance*. The driver (engineer) only knows the highway rules ‚ÄĒ so we transform the messy reality into the driver's simple language.

In short: **Activity lets us reuse the elegant equations of ideal solutions for messy real solutions**, by simply replacing $x_i$ with $a_i$.

## 1.3 Standard State ‚ÄĒ The Crucial Companion Concept

Activity is meaningless **without a defined standard state**. The two KTU-mandated conventions are:

| Convention | Standard State | Activity Coeff. Form | Use Case |
|:-----------|:---------------|:--------------------|:---------|
| **Lewis‚ÄďRandall (L‚ÄďR)** | Pure component $i$ at system $T$ and $P$ | $\gamma_i^{(LR)} = \dfrac{\hat{f}_i}{x_i f_i}$ | Solvent (major component) |
| **Henry's Law (H)** | Hypothetical pure $i$ behaving as infinite dilution | $\gamma_i^{(H)} = \dfrac{\hat{f}_i}{x_i k_{i,H}}$ | Solute (minor component) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** An activity coefficient is **meaningless unless its standard state is specified**. The numerical value of $\gamma_i$ depends entirely on the chosen convention. Examiners will deduct marks for omitting the superscript $(LR)$ or $(H)$.

## 1.4 The Big Picture: Where Does "Activity" Sit in Module 3?

> **Activity in Solutions** is the **bridge** between:
> - **Module 2 (Fugacity of pure fluids & mixtures)**, and
> - **Module 4 (Phase & Reaction Equilibria)**.

It is the essential tool that makes every equilibrium calculation (VLE, LLE, SLE, chemical reaction equilibrium) tractable for real industrial mixtures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemical Potential in Real Solutions

The chemical potential of species $i$ in a **real solution** is given by:

$$\mu_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln a_i$$

where $\mu_i^0$ is the chemical potential of $i$ in its chosen standard state.

Expanding in terms of mole fraction:

$$\mu_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln x_i + RT \ln \gamma_i$$

The term $RT \ln \gamma_i$ is the **excess chemical potential** ‚ÄĒ the thermodynamic "penalty" (or bonus) for non-ideality.

## 2.2 Derivation Path ‚ÄĒ From Fugacity to Activity

Starting from the rigorous definition of fugacity for component $i$ in a mixture:

$$\mu_i - \mu_i^0 = RT \ln \frac{\hat{f}_i}{f_i^0}$$

Substituting $\hat{f}_i = a_i f_i^0$:

$$\mu_i - \mu_i^0 = RT \ln a_i$$

This is the **defining equation** for activity in solution thermodynamics.

## 2.3 The Two Reference Fugacities

| Reference Fugacity | Definition | Activity Expression |
|:-------------------|:-----------|:-------------------|
| $f_i$ | Fugacity of **pure** $i$ at system $T, P$ | $a_i = \dfrac{\hat{f}_i}{f_i} = \gamma_i x_i$ |
| $k_{i,H}$ | Henry's constant of $i$ at system $T$ | $a_i = \dfrac{\hat{f}_i}{k_{i,H}} = \gamma_i^{*} x_i$ |

Here $\gamma_i$ is the **Lewis‚ÄďRandall activity coefficient** and $\gamma_i^{*}$ is the **Henry's-law activity coefficient** (sometimes called $\gamma_i^{(H)}$).

## 2.4 Excess Properties ‚ÄĒ The "Container" of Non-Ideality

The **excess property** $M^{E}$ is defined as the difference between the real mixture property and the ideal-solution property at the same $T, P,$ and composition:

$$M^{E} = M_{\text{mix}} - M_{\text{mix}}^{\text{id}}$$

For Gibbs energy (the most important):

$$G^{E} = RT \sum_i x_i \ln \gamma_i$$

This is the **master equation of solution non-ideality**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The activity coefficient of any component is obtained from the partial molar excess Gibbs energy:
> $$\ln \gamma_i = \left( \frac{\partial (n G^{E}/RT)}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T,P,n_{j \neq i}}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Conditions / Use |
|:-:|:---------|:------------|:----------------|
| 1 | $a_i = \hat{f}_i / f_i^0$ | Definition of activity | Universal |
| 2 | $\gamma_i = a_i / x_i$ | Activity coefficient (L‚ÄďR) | $\sum x_i = 1$ |
| 3 | $\mu_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln a_i$ | Real-solution chemical potential | Universal |
| 4 | $G^{E} = RT \sum x_i \ln \gamma_i$ | Excess Gibbs energy | $T, P$ fixed |
| 5 | $\ln \gamma_i = \dfrac{1}{RT}\dfrac{\partial (n G^{E})}{\partial n_i}\bigg\vert_{T,P,n_j}$ | Activity from $G^E$ model | $j \neq i$ |
| 6 | $\sum_i x_i \, d \ln \gamma_i = 0$ | **Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem (constant T,P)** | Binary & multicomponent |
| 7 | $\gamma_1 = \dfrac{P}{x_1 P_1^{sat}} \exp\!\left[\dfrac{(P - P_1^{sat})(V_1 - B_{11})}{RT}\right]$ | Modified Raoult's Law (L‚ÄďR) | Low-pressure VLE |
| 8 | $\hat{\phi}_i P = \gamma_i x_i \phi_i^{sat} P_i^{sat}$ | General VLE (ő≥‚ÄďŌÜ) | Real VLE |
| 9 | $G^{E,*} / (RT x_1 x_2)$ | Reduced excess Gibbs energy | Binary models |
| 10 | $\sum_i x_i \gamma_i^{*,(H)} = 1$ | Henry's-law reference constraint | Infinite dilution |

> [!NOTE]
> Equation 6 (the Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem equation) is a **consistency test** ‚ÄĒ given $\gamma_1$ from experiment, you can compute $\gamma_2$, or vice versa. This is a KTU-favourite derivation question.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> 1. **Distillation column design** ‚ÄĒ Relative volatilities $\alpha_{12} = (\gamma_1 P_1^{sat}) / (\gamma_2 P_2^{sat})$ depend directly on $\gamma_i$.
> 2. **Azeotrope prediction** ‚ÄĒ Occurs when $a_1 = a_2$, i.e., $\gamma_1 x_1 = \gamma_2 x_2$.
> 3. **Liquid‚Äďliquid extraction** ‚ÄĒ Distribution coefficients use $\gamma_i$ of solute in both phases.
> 4. **Chemical reactor equilibrium** ‚ÄĒ $K_a = \prod a_i^{\nu_i}$ governs conversions.
> 5. **Electrolyte solutions & corrosion** ‚ÄĒ Use ionic activity coefficients via Debye‚ÄďH√ľckel theory.

## 2.7 Symmetric vs Asymmetric Normalization

> **Symmetric convention** (L‚ÄďR): $\gamma_i \to 1$ as $x_i \to 1$. Used when $i$ is the solvent.
> **Asymmetric convention** (Henry's): $\gamma_i^{*} \to 1$ as $x_i \to 0$. Used when $i$ is the solute.

The relation between the two conventions for the same component:

$$\frac{\gamma_i^{*}}{\gamma_i} = \frac{f_i}{k_{i,H}}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: Activity Coefficient from Excess Gibbs Energy

**Goal:** Show that $\ln \gamma_i = \dfrac{1}{RT}\left(\dfrac{\partial n G^{E}}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T,P,n_{j \neq i}}$.

### Starting point ‚ÄĒ partial molar property

By definition of the partial molar excess Gibbs energy:

$$\bar{G}_i^{E} = \left( \frac{\partial (n G^{E})}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T,P,n_{j \neq i}}$$

### Relation between $\bar{G}_i^{E}$ and $\ln \gamma_i$

Recall the fundamental relation $G^{E} = RT \sum_k x_k \ln \gamma_k$. Dividing by $n$ (total moles) and using $x_k = n_k/n$:

$$G^{E} = RT \sum_k \frac{n_k}{n} \ln \gamma_k$$

Multiply by $n$:

$$n G^{E} = RT \sum_k n_k \ln \gamma_k$$

### Differentiate w.r.t. $n_i$ at constant $T, P, n_j$ ($j \neq i$)

$$\left( \frac{\partial (n G^{E})}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T,P,n_{j \neq i}} = RT \left[ \ln \gamma_i + \sum_k n_k \left( \frac{\partial \ln \gamma_k}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T,P,n_{j \neq i}} \right]$$

### Apply the Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem equation (constant T, P)

The constant-T, P Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem equation for activity coefficients is:

$$\sum_k x_k \, d \ln \gamma_k = 0$$

Equivalently, the summation term in the derivative above **vanishes**:

$$\sum_k n_k \left( \frac{\partial \ln \gamma_k}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T,P,n_{j \neq i}} = 0$$

### Result

$$\bar{G}_i^{E} = RT \ln \gamma_i$$

$$\boxed{\ln \gamma_i = \frac{1}{RT} \left( \frac{\partial (n G^{E})}{\partial n_i} \right)_{T,P,n_{j \neq i}}}$$

This is the **bridge equation** that converts any $G^{E}$ model (Margules, van Laar, Wilson, NRTL, UNIQUAC) into component activity coefficients.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B: Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem Equation for Activity Coefficients

**Goal:** Prove that $\sum_i x_i \, d \ln \gamma_i = 0$ at constant $T, P$.

### Start with the fundamental Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem equation

For any mixture at equilibrium:

$$\sum_i n_i \, d\mu_i = -S \, dT + V \, dP$$

### Substitute the chemical potential expression

For an ideal solution, $\mu_i^{id} = \mu_i^{0} + RT \ln x_i$, and for a real solution, $\mu_i = \mu_i^{0} + RT \ln(\gamma_i x_i)$. The difference gives:

$$\mu_i = \mu_i^{id} + RT \ln \gamma_i$$

Differentiating:

$$d\mu_i = d\mu_i^{id} + RT \, d\ln \gamma_i$$

### Substitute back into Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem

$$\sum_i n_i (d\mu_i^{id} + RT \, d\ln \gamma_i) = -S \, dT + V \, dP$$

But the ideal-solution terms also satisfy Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem:

$$\sum_i n_i \, d\mu_i^{id} = -S^{id} \, dT + V^{id} \, dP$$

### At constant T and P

Setting $dT = dP = 0$, the right-hand sides vanish, and subtracting:

$$RT \sum_i n_i \, d\ln \gamma_i = 0$$

Since $RT \neq 0$ and dividing by total moles $n$:

$$\boxed{\sum_i x_i \, d \ln \gamma_i = 0 \quad \text{(constant T, P)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning:** In a binary system at constant T and P, knowing $\gamma_1(x_1)$ **completely determines** $\gamma_2(x_1)$ ‚ÄĒ they are not independent.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C: Binary Margules (Two-Suffix) Equations

**Given** the symmetric Margules $G^{E}$ model for a binary system:

$$\frac{G^{E}}{RT} = A \, x_1 x_2$$

where $A$ is a constant at fixed $T$.

### Compute $n G^E / RT$

$$n \frac{G^{E}}{RT} = A \, \frac{n_1 n_2}{n}$$

### Partial derivative w.r.t. $n_1$

$$\left( \frac{\partial (n G^E/RT)}{\partial n_1} \right)_{n_2} = A \left[ \frac{n_2}{n} - \frac{n_1 n_2}{n^2} \right] = A \, n_2 \left( \frac{1}{n} - \frac{x_1}{n} \right)$$

Simplify:

$$= A \, x_2 (1 - x_1) = A \, x_2^2$$

since $1 - x_1 = x_2$.

### Result for component 1

$$\ln \gamma_1 = A x_2^2$$

By symmetry, swapping indices:

$$\ln \gamma_2 = A x_1^2$$

This is the **two-suffix Margules equation**, and it is one of the most commonly tested models in KTU examinations.

---

## 3.4 Derivation D: Consistency Check ‚ÄĒ Henry's Law and Pure-Component Limit

**Show that** $\gamma_i \to 1$ as $x_i \to 1$ in the L‚ÄďR convention, and $\gamma_i^{*} \to 1$ as $x_i \to 0$ in the Henry's convention.

### Lewis‚ÄďRandall limit ($x_1 \to 1$)

In the limit of pure component 1, the solution **is** component 1, so by definition:

$$\hat{f}_1 \to f_1 \quad \text{as} \quad x_1 \to 1$$

Therefore:

$$\gamma_1 = \frac{\hat{f}_1}{x_1 f_1} \to \frac{f_1}{1 \cdot f_1} = 1$$

### Henry's law limit ($x_1 \to 0$)

In the dilute limit, $\hat{f}_1 \to k_{1,H} x_1$, hence:

$$\gamma_1^{*} = \frac{\hat{f}_1}{x_1 k_{1,H}} \to 1$$

These limits are the **defining normalization conditions** of the two conventions.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Table ‚ÄĒ Key Derivations

| # | What We Derived | Starting Equation | Final Form | Use |
|:-:|:----------------|:------------------|:-----------|:----|
| A | $\ln \gamma_i$ from $G^E$ | $G^E = RT \sum x_i \ln \gamma_i$ | $\ln \gamma_i = \dfrac{1}{RT} \dfrac{\partial nG^E}{\partial n_i}$ | Convert any $G^E$ model to $\gamma$ |
| B | Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem for $\gamma$ | $\sum n_i d\mu_i = -S\,dT + V\,dP$ | $\sum x_i d\ln \gamma_i = 0$ | Consistency test |
| C | Margules 2-suffix | $G^E/RT = A x_1 x_2$ | $\ln \gamma_1 = A x_2^2$, $\ln \gamma_2 = A x_1^2$ | VLE correlations |
| D | Normalization limits | $\hat{f}_i = \gamma_i x_i f_i$ | $\gamma_i \to 1$ as $x_i \to 1$ (L‚ÄďR) | Reference-state definition |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example ‚ÄĒ KTU Style Numerical

**Problem [KTU University Exam ‚Äď June 2023 style]:**
A binary liquid mixture of acetone(1) and chloroform(2) at 1 atm exhibits the Margules 2-suffix behaviour with $A = -0.36$ at 320 K. Compute the activity coefficients at $x_1 = 0.4$.

### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify composition
$x_1 = 0.4 \Rightarrow x_2 = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6$

### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply Margules equation

$$\ln \gamma_1 = A x_2^2 = (-0.36)(0.6)^2$$

$$\ln \gamma_1 = (-0.36)(0.36) = -0.1296$$

$$\gamma_1 = e^{-0.1296} = 0.8785$$

### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute for component 2

$$\ln \gamma_2 = A x_1^2 = (-0.36)(0.4)^2$$

$$\ln \gamma_2 = (-0.36)(0.16) = -0.0576$$

$$\gamma_2 = e^{-0.0576} = 0.9440$$

### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Physical interpretation
Both $\gamma_i < 1$ ‚áí **negative deviation from Raoult's Law** (consistent with the well-known acetone‚Äďchloroform A‚ÄďB hydrogen bonding).

### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Verify Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem consistency
At a single point, the integral form of Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem must hold ‚ÄĒ the values are **model-consistent** because they were generated from a single $G^E$ model.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept-Map ‚ÄĒ Position of "Activity" in Thermodynamic Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pure Component Fugacity f_i] --> B[Define Mixture Fugacity f_hat_i]
    B --> C{Choose Standard State f_i^0}
    C -->|Lewis-Randall| D[Pure component at T, P]
    C -->|Henry's Law| E[Hypothetical pure at infinite dilution]
    D --> F[Activity a_i = f_hat_i / f_i]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Activity Coefficient gamma_i = a_i / x_i]
    G --> H[Chemical Potential mu_i = mu_i^0 + RT ln a_i]
    H --> I[Equilibrium K_a = product a_i^nu_i]
    H --> J[VLE: f_hat_i^L = f_hat_i^V]
    I --> K[Phase and Reaction Equilibria]
    J --> K
```

## 4.2 Standard-State Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Component i in Solution] --> Q1{Is i the solvent or solute?}
    Q1 -->|Solvent x_i large| LR[Use Lewis-Randall Standard]
    Q1 -->|Solute x_i small| HL[Use Henry's Law Standard]
    LR --> LR1[Standard: pure i at T, P of system]
    LR1 --> LR2[gamma_i to 1 as x_i to 1]
    HL --> HL1[Standard: hypothetical pure i, k_i_H at T]
    HL1 --> HL2[gamma_i_star to 1 as x_i to 0]
    LR2 --> Out[Compute a_i = gamma_i x_i]
    HL2 --> Out
```

## 4.3 G^E-Model-to-Activity Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[G^E Model Choice] --> A1[Margules]
    A --> A2[van Laar]
    A --> A3[Wilson]
    A --> A4[NRTL / UNIQUAC]
    A1 --> B[Express G^E in terms of n_i]
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    A4 --> B
    B --> C[Partial Derivative: d nG^E / d n_i]
    C --> D[Divide by RT]
    D --> E[Obtain ln gamma_i]
    E --> F[Apply: a_i = gamma_i x_i]
    F --> G[Compute mu_i or K_a]
```

## 4.4 Symmetric vs Asymmetric Convention (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Symmetric_LR[Symmetric Convention: Lewis-Randall]
        S1[Pure i at T, P] --> S2[gamma_i = 1 as x_i = 1]
        S2 --> S3[a_i = gamma_i x_i, valid over entire composition range]
    end
    subgraph Asymmetric_H[Asymmetric Convention: Henry's Law]
        H1[Hypothetical pure i, fugacity k_i_H] --> H2[gamma_i_star = 1 as x_i = 0]
        H2 --> H3[a_i = gamma_i_star x_i, valid for dilute solute]
    end
    S3 <--> H3
    H3 --> R[Relation: gamma_i_star / gamma_i = f_i / k_i_H]
    S3 --> R
```

## 4.5 Activity-Coefficient Behaviour Diagram (Positive vs Negative Deviation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Mole fraction x_i] --> Y{Interaction Energy}
    Y -->|epsilon_AB > epsilon_AA and epsilon_BB| N[Negative Deviation]
    Y -->|epsilon_AB less than epsilon_AA and epsilon_BB| P[Positive Deviation]
    N --> N1[gamma_i less than 1]
    P --> P1[gamma_i greater than 1]
    N1 --> N2[Stronger A-B bonds: e.g. Acetone-Chloroform]
    P1 --> P2[Weaker A-B bonds: e.g. Ethanol-Hexane]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid block diagrams above are the recommended **substitute for hand-drawn physical figures**, since thermodynamic activity has no spatial shape ‚ÄĒ it is purely a state-function concept defined through its mathematical relation to fugacity.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024]`
**Define the term "activity" of a component in a solution. Why is it introduced?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The activity $a_i$ of component $i$ in a real solution is defined as the ratio of the fugacity of $i$ in the solution to the fugacity of $i$ in a chosen standard state:

$$a_i = \frac{\hat{f}_i}{f_i^0}$$

It is a **dimensionless effective concentration** that replaces the mole fraction in the ideal-solution thermodynamic equations, thereby preserving the simple mathematical form of equations such as $\mu_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln x_i$ even for real (non-ideal) mixtures. It is introduced because real solutions **deviate from Raoult's/Henry's Law** due to molecular interactions, and a single "correction factor" (the activity coefficient $\gamma_i = a_i/x_i$) elegantly captures this non-ideality.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + Equation (1) + Justification (1).

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the Lewis‚ÄďRandall and Henry's Law standard states for activity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Lewis‚ÄďRandall (L‚ÄďR) | Henry's Law (H) |
|:--------|:-------------------|:----------------|
| Standard state | Pure $i$ at system $T, P$ | Hypothetical pure $i$ with fugacity $k_{i,H}$ |
| Limiting condition | $\gamma_i \to 1$ as $x_i \to 1$ | $\gamma_i^{*} \to 1$ as $x_i \to 0$ |
| Used for | Solvent (major component) | Solute (minor/dilute component) |
| Symmetry | Symmetric convention | Asymmetric convention |

(1 mark for L‚ÄďR definition, 1 mark for Henry's, 1 mark for distinction/use-case.)

---

## 5.2 Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module-3 Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2024 Style]`

**(a)** Starting from the definition of fugacity, derive the expression for the chemical potential of a component in a real solution in terms of its activity. Explain the role of the standard state. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a binary system described by the two-suffix Margules equation $G^{E}/RT = A\,x_1 x_2$, derive the expressions for $\ln \gamma_1$ and $\ln \gamma_2$. If $A = -0.5$ at 330 K, compute $\gamma_1$ and $\gamma_2$ at $x_1 = 0.3$. Comment on the type of deviation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Fugacity definition (1 mark)**

For any component $i$ in any state:

$$\mu_i - \mu_i^0 = RT \ln \frac{\hat{f}_i}{f_i^0}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Define activity (1 mark)**

Setting $a_i = \hat{f}_i / f_i^0$:

$$\mu_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln a_i$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Expand activity (1 mark)**

Using $a_i = \gamma_i x_i$:

$$\mu_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln x_i + RT \ln \gamma_i$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Identify the role of the standard state (2 marks)**

The standard state $f_i^0$ provides the **reference fugacity** to which the component's fugacity in the solution is compared. It must be **thermodynamically defined and reproducible**. Two choices are used:

- **Lewis‚ÄďRandall:** $f_i^0 = f_i$ (pure $i$ at system T, P)
- **Henry's Law:** $f_i^0 = k_{i,H}$ (hypothetical pure $i$ in infinite dilution limit)

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Physical interpretation (1 mark)**

The term $RT \ln \gamma_i$ is the **excess chemical potential** $\bar{G}_i^E$, representing the deviation from ideal-solution behaviour due to molecular interactions.

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Conclude (1 mark)**

Activity makes the **ideal-solution equations universally applicable** by absorbing all non-ideality into a single measurable or model-predictable parameter $\gamma_i$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Start with the Margules model (1 mark)**

$$\frac{G^{E}}{RT} = A\,x_1 x_2$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Convert to total-mole form (1 mark)**

$$n \frac{G^{E}}{RT} = A \frac{n_1 n_2}{n}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Differentiate w.r.t. $n_1$ keeping $n_2$ constant (2 marks)**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial n_1}\!\left(\frac{n G^E}{RT}\right) = A\left[\frac{n_2}{n} - \frac{n_1 n_2}{n^2}\right] = A\,x_2(1 - x_1) = A\,x_2^2$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Apply the bridge equation (1 mark)**

$$\ln \gamma_1 = A\,x_2^2 \quad ; \quad \ln \gamma_2 = A\,x_1^2$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Numerical evaluation (1.5 marks)**

Given $A = -0.5$, $x_1 = 0.3$, $x_2 = 0.7$:

$$\ln \gamma_1 = -0.5 \times (0.7)^2 = -0.5 \times 0.49 = -0.245$$

$$\gamma_1 = e^{-0.245} = 0.783$$

$$\ln \gamma_2 = -0.5 \times (0.3)^2 = -0.5 \times 0.09 = -0.045$$

$$\gamma_2 = e^{-0.045} = 0.956$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Comment on deviation (0.5 mark)**

Both $\gamma_i < 1$ and $A < 0$ indicate **negative deviation from Raoult's Law**, i.e., stronger A‚ÄďB interactions than A‚ÄďA and B‚ÄďB (e.g., hydrogen-bond formation).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023 Style]` ‚ÄĒ *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** What is the excess Gibbs energy? Derive the relationship $G^E = RT \sum_i x_i \ln \gamma_i$ starting from the definition of activity coefficient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and derive the Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem equation in terms of activity coefficients for a binary solution at constant T and P. Show how it serves as a thermodynamic consistency test for VLE data. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Define excess property (1 mark)**

$$G^{E} \equiv G_{\text{mix}} - G_{\text{mix}}^{\text{id}}$$

at the same $T, P$ and composition.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Express each chemical potential (1 mark)**

For a real solution: $\mu_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln(\gamma_i x_i)$
For an ideal solution: $\mu_i^{id} = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln x_i$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute the excess chemical potential (1 mark)**

$$\mu_i - \mu_i^{id} = RT \ln \gamma_i \equiv \bar{G}_i^{E}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Use the relation between mixture and partial molar property (2 marks)**

For any extensive property $M$: $M = \sum_i n_i \bar{M}_i$

Therefore:

$$G^{E} = \sum_i n_i \bar{G}_i^{E} = \sum_i n_i (RT \ln \gamma_i)$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Divide by total moles (1 mark)**

$$G^{E} = RT \sum_i \frac{n_i}{n} \ln \gamma_i = RT \sum_i x_i \ln \gamma_i$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Conclude (1 mark)**

This is the **master equation** linking macroscopic $G^E$ to the microscopic activity coefficients.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ State the Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem equation (1 mark)**

For any mixture at equilibrium:

$$\sum_i n_i \, d\mu_i = -S\, dT + V\, dP$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Substitute $\mu_i$ in terms of activity (1 mark)**

$$\mu_i = \mu_i^{id} + RT \ln \gamma_i$$

Differentiating:

$$d\mu_i = d\mu_i^{id} + RT\, d\ln \gamma_i$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Substitute into Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem (1 mark)**

$$\sum_i n_i (d\mu_i^{id} + RT\, d\ln \gamma_i) = -S\, dT + V\, dP$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Use the ideal-solution Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem (1 mark)**

The ideal mixture also satisfies Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem:

$$\sum_i n_i \, d\mu_i^{id} = -S^{id}\, dT + V^{id}\, dP$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Subtract and set $dT = dP = 0$ (1 mark)**

$$RT \sum_i n_i \, d\ln \gamma_i = 0$$

Dividing by $n$:

$$\boxed{\sum_i x_i\, d\ln \gamma_i = 0 \quad \text{(constant T, P)}}$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Application as VLE consistency test (1 mark)**

For a binary system:

$$x_1 d\ln \gamma_1 + x_2 d\ln \gamma_2 = 0$$

Integrating from a reference state:

$$\int_0^{\ln \gamma_1} d\ln \gamma_1 = -\int_0^{\ln \gamma_2} \frac{x_2}{x_1} d\ln \gamma_2$$

Experimentally measured $\gamma_1(x_1)$ and $\gamma_2(x_1)$ must satisfy this relation. If they do, the data are **thermodynamically consistent**; if not, the data are rejected.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always specify the standard state** when writing $\gamma_i$ ‚ÄĒ many students lose 1 mark by writing "$\gamma_1 = 0.87$" without $(LR)$ or $(H)$ superscript.
> 2. **Don't confuse $f_i$, $f_i^0$, and $\hat{f}_i$** ‚ÄĒ examiners specifically test whether you can distinguish pure-component fugacity, standard-state fugacity, and mixture fugacity.
> 3. **Forgetting to include the activity-coefficient term** $RT \ln \gamma_i$ in $\mu_i$ is a common error.
> 4. **Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem is at constant T and P** ‚ÄĒ forgetting this condition will lose 1‚Äď2 marks.
> 5. **In numerical problems, always state the type of deviation** (positive/negative) ‚ÄĒ students often compute $\gamma_i$ correctly but lose 0.5‚Äď1 mark by skipping the interpretation step.
> 6. **In the Margules derivation, show the partial derivative step explicitly** ‚ÄĒ examiners want to see $\frac{\partial}{\partial n_i}\left(\frac{n_1 n_2}{n}\right)$ expanded, not just the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 ‚ÄĒ Activity in Solutions):**

- [ ] **Activity** $a_i = \hat{f}_i / f_i^0$ is a dimensionless "effective concentration" preserving ideal-solution equation forms.
- [ ] **Activity coefficient** $\gamma_i = a_i / x_i$ is the **correction factor** for non-ideality.
- [ ] **Ideal solution:** $\gamma_i = 1$ for all $i$ at all compositions.
- [ ] **Positive deviation:** $\gamma_i > 1$ (weaker A‚ÄďB interactions, e.g., ethanol‚Äďhexane).
- [ ] **Negative deviation:** $\gamma_i < 1$ (stronger A‚ÄďB interactions, e.g., acetone‚Äďchloroform).
- [ ] **Lewis‚ÄďRandall standard** ‚ÄĒ used for the solvent; $\gamma_i \to 1$ as $x_i \to 1$.
- [ ] **Henry's Law standard** ‚ÄĒ used for the solute; $\gamma_i^{*} \to 1$ as $x_i \to 0$.
- [ ] **Master equation:** $G^E = RT \sum x_i \ln \gamma_i$.
- [ ] **Bridge equation:** $\ln \gamma_i = \dfrac{1}{RT}\left(\dfrac{\partial n G^E}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$.
- [ ] **Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem at constant T, P:** $\sum x_i\, d\ln \gamma_i = 0$ ‚áí used for VLE data consistency.
- [ ] **Margules 2-suffix:** $\ln \gamma_1 = A x_2^2$, $\ln \gamma_2 = A x_1^2$.
- [ ] **Relation between conventions:** $\gamma_i^{*} / \gamma_i = f_i / k_{i,H}$.
- [ ] **Modified Raoult's Law:** $\hat{f}_1^L = \gamma_1 x_1 f_1 = y_1 P$ (low-pressure VLE form).
- [ ] **General VLE:** $\hat{\phi}_1 P = \gamma_1 x_1 \phi_1^{sat} P_1^{sat}$ (ő≥‚ÄďŌÜ formulation).
- [ ] **Activity coefficient depends on choice of standard state** ‚ÄĒ always declare it.
- [ ] **KEEP $dT = dP = 0$** when applying Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem for activity coefficients.
- [ ] **Real-world uses:** Distillation, azeotrope prediction, extraction, reactor equilibrium, electrolyte solutions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activity Coefficient: Effect of Temperature and Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Activity Coefficient: Effect of Temperature and Pressure ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Activity Coefficient In the framework of **Solution Thermodynamics** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, the **activity coefficient** ($\gamma_i$) of component *i* in a non-ideal mixture is formally defined as the ratio of the actual (fugacity-based) activity to the mole-fraction based composition: $$\gamma_i = \frac{\hat{f}_i}{x_i \, f_i}$$ where $\hat{f}_i$ is the **fugacity of component *i*** in the mixture, $x_i$ is its **mole fraction**, and $f_i$ is the **fugacity of pure component *i*** at the same temperature and pressure. For an **ideal solution**, $\gamma_i = 1$. For **real (non-ideal) solutions**, $\gamma_i \neq 1$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Recap:** The activity coefficient quantifies the *departure of a real solution from ideal solution behavior*. It is a dimensionless quantity that captures all non-ideal molecular interactions (attractive, repulsive, hydrogen bonding, etc.) in a thermodynamically rigorous way. ### 1.2 The Rationale Behind Studying T and P Effects Why do Chemical Engineers care about how activity coefficients vary with **temperature (T)** and **pressure (P)**? The answer lies in **process design**: - **Distillation Columns:** Tray-by-tray temperature and pressure profiles directly alter $\gamma_i$, changing relative volatilities and separation efficiency. - **Liquid‚ÄďLiquid Extraction:** Selectivity depends on $\gamma_i$, which is T-sensitive. - **Reactor Design:** Activity coefficients govern reaction equilibria via $K_a = K_\gamma \cdot K_x$. - **Supercritical Extraction:** High-pressure corrections become significant. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: "The Stretchy Rubber Band" > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ Think of activity coefficients as a "stretchy rubber band" representing molecular interactions.** > Imagine a dance floor where molecules are dancers: > - **Ideal solution ($\gamma = 1$):** All dancers are strangers ‚ÄĒ no preference for any partner. Everyone mixes randomly with zero interaction energy. > - **Non-ideal ($\gamma > 1$, "positive deviation"):** Dancers actively *repel* similar partners. Like dissolves unlike poorly (e.g., acetone + water at high T). > - **Non-ideal ($\gamma < 1$, "negative deviation"):** Dancers prefer similar partners. Strong attractive interactions dominate (e.g., chloroform + acetone, H-bonding). > > **Now temperature enters:** Heating the room makes dancers move faster and break their preferential bonds ‚Üí $\gamma \to 1$ as T rises (for negative deviations). > > **Pressure enters:** Squeezing the dance floor reduces free volume ‚Üí forces molecules closer, but for *liquids* the effect is tiny because liquids are nearly incompressible. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Universal Gas Constant | **R** | **8.314** | J/(mol¬∑K) | | Reference Pressure | $P^{ref}$ | 1 bar or 1 atm | Pa / atm | | Standard State Fugacity | $f_i^0$ | 1 bar (Lewis‚ÄďRandall) | Pa | | Excess Property | $M^E$ | varies | depends | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Fundamental Linkage: Activity Coefficient ‚ÜĒ Excess Gibbs Energy The activity coefficient is rooted in the **excess Gibbs free energy** of mixing, $G^{E}$, which represents the deviation of real mixture Gibbs energy from an ideal solution: $$\frac{G^{E}}{RT} = \sum_{i} x_i \ln \gamma_i$$ By partial molar differentiation: $$\ln \gamma_i = \left[\frac{\partial (n G^{E}/RT)}{\partial n_i}\right]_{T, P, n_{j \neq i}}$$ This is the **master equation** from which all common activity-coefficient models (Margules, van Laar, Wilson, NRTL, UNIQUAC) are derived. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight ‚ÄĒ** Every excess Gibbs model implies a specific **temperature dependence** of $\gamma_i$ through the model parameters (e.g., Margules constant $A$, Wilson's $\Lambda_{ij}$). Engineers must know how these parameters scale with T. ### 2.2 Effect of Temperature ‚ÄĒ The Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz Route The temperature derivative of $\ln \gamma_i$ comes from the **Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation** applied to the excess chemical potential $\mu_i^{E} = RT \ln \gamma_i$: $$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial \ln \gamma_i}{\partial T}\right)_{P, x} = -\frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{RT^2}}$$ where $\bar{H}_i^{E}$ is the **partial molar excess enthalpy** of component *i*. Integrating between two temperatures: $$\ln \frac{\gamma_i(T_2)}{\gamma_i(T_1)} = -\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{RT^2} \, dT$$ If $\bar{H}_i^{E}$ is assumed **temperature-independent** over the range $[T_1, T_2]$: $$\ln \frac{\gamma_i(T_2)}{\gamma_i(T_1)} = \frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Insight:** If the mixing is **exothermic** ($\bar{H}_i^{E} < 0$), increasing T makes $\ln \gamma_i$ *more positive* (driving $\gamma \to 1$). If **endothermic** ($\bar{H}_i^{E} > 0$), increasing T makes $\gamma$ more extreme (further from 1). ### 2.3 Effect of Pressure ‚ÄĒ The Poynting Correction Route The pressure derivative follows from the volumetric excess property: $$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial \ln \gamma_i}{\partial P}\right)_{T, x} = \frac{\bar{V}_i - V_i}{RT}}$$ where $\bar{V}_i$ is the **partial molar volume** of *i* in the mixture and $V_i$ is the **pure-component molar volume**. Integrating between two pressures: $$\ln \frac{\gamma_i(P_2)}{\gamma_i(P_1)} = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{\bar{V}_i - V_i}{RT} \, dP$$ For liquids where $\bar{V}_i$ and $V_i$ are **nearly constant** (small compressibility): $$\ln \frac{\gamma_i(P_2)}{\gamma_i(P_1)} \approx \frac{(\bar{V}_i - V_i)(P_2 - P_1)}{RT}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Takeaway ‚ÄĒ** For **liquids**, $(\bar{V}_i - V_i)$ is typically of order $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ m¬≥/mol. Thus, even a 100 bar pressure change produces only ~1% change in $\gamma_i$. **Therefore, pressure effects on liquid-phase activity coefficients are usually negligible** compared to temperature effects. ### 2.4 Temperature Dependence in Common Excess Models **2-Suffix Margules Model:** $$\ln \gamma_1 = A(T) \, x_2^2, \qquad \ln \gamma_2 = A(T) \, x_1^2$$ with parameter: $$A(T) = \frac{a}{RT}$$ Therefore: $$\frac{d \ln \gamma_1}{dT} = -\frac{a}{RT^2} x_2^2 = -\frac{\bar{H}_1^{E}}{RT^2}$$ **van Laar Model:** $$\ln \gamma_1 = \frac{A}{\left(1 + \dfrac{A}{B} \dfrac{x_1}{x_2}\right)^{2}}, \quad \ln \gamma_2 = \frac{B}{\left(1 + \dfrac{B}{A} \dfrac{x_2}{x_1}\right)^{2}}$$ with $A = a/RT$, $B = b/RT$. **Wilson Model:** $$\ln \gamma_i = 1 - \ln\left(\sum_{j} x_j \Lambda_{ij}\right) - \sum_{k} \frac{x_k \Lambda_{ki}}{\sum_{j} x_j \Lambda_{kj}}$$ where: $$\Lambda_{ij} = \frac{V_j}{V_i} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lambda_{ij} - \lambda_{ii}}{RT}\right)$$ Here, $\lambda_{ij} - \lambda_{ii}$ are **interaction energies** in J/mol. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam-Oriented Quick Reference ‚ÄĒ** Master these equations for any problem on this topic. | # | Equation | Application | Key Conditions | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\ln \gamma_i = \dfrac{\mu_i^{E}}{RT}$ | Definition via chemical potential | All solutions | | 2 | $\dfrac{G^{E}}{RT} = \sum x_i \ln \gamma_i$ | Master link to excess Gibbs energy | Constant T, P | | 3 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln \gamma_i}{\partial T}\right)_{P,x} = -\dfrac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{RT^2}$ | Temperature effect (Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz) | Constant P, x | | 4 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln \gamma_i}{\partial P}\right)_{T,x} = \dfrac{\bar{V}_i - V_i}{RT}$ | Pressure effect (volumetric) | Constant T, x | | 5 | $\ln \dfrac{\gamma_i(T_2)}{\gamma_i(T_1)} = \dfrac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | T-correction assuming constant $\bar{H}^{E}$ | Constant $\bar{H}^{E}$ | | 6 | $\ln \dfrac{\gamma_i(P_2)}{\gamma_i(P_1)} \approx \dfrac{(\bar{V}_i - V_i)(P_2 - P_1)}{RT}$ | P-correction for liquids | Incompressible liquid | | 7 | $\Lambda_{ij}(T) = \dfrac{V_j}{V_i} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\lambda_{ij}-\lambda_{ii}}{RT}\right)$ | Wilson parameter T-dependence | Wilson model | | 8 | $A(T) = \dfrac{a}{RT}$ | Margules/van Laar parameter T-dependence | Margules/van Laar | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application | |---|---| | **Distillation** | Reboiler/condenser T profiles alter $\gamma_i$, shifting azeotropes | | **Azeotropic Distillation** | Adding entrainer changes $\gamma$ with T (e.g., ethanol-water-benzene) | | **Extractive Distillation** | Solvent alters relative volatility via $\gamma$ at operating T | | **Supercritical Fluid Extraction** | High P ‚Üí $\gamma$ changes drive selectivity (e.g., CO‚āā + caffeine) | | **Polymer Solution Thermodynamics** | Flory‚ÄďHuggins $\chi$ parameter is T-dependent | | **Electrolyte Solutions** | Debye‚ÄďH√ľckel $\ln \gamma_\pm$ depends strongly on T via $\sqrt{I}$ | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 ‚ÄĒ Temperature Derivative of $\ln \gamma_i$ (Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz) **Starting Point:** By definition of activity coefficient from chemical potential: $$\mu_i^{E} = RT \ln \gamma_i$$ **Step 1.** Apply the Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation at constant P and x: $$\left[\frac{\partial (\mu_i^{E}/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P, x} = -\frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{T^2}$$ > Here we use the standard form: $\left(\dfrac{\partial (G/T)}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\dfrac{H}{T^2}$. **Step 2.** Expand the left-hand side using quotient rule: $$\left[\frac{\partial (\mu_i^{E}/T)}{\partial T}\right] = \frac{1}{T}\left(\frac{\partial \mu_i^{E}}{\partial T}\right) - \frac{\mu_i^{E}}{T^2}$$ **Step 3.** Note that $\left(\dfrac{\partial \mu_i^{E}}{\partial T}\right)_{P,x} = -\bar{S}_i^{E}$, so: $$\frac{1}{T}(-\bar{S}_i^{E}) - \frac{\mu_i^{E}}{T^2} = -\frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{T^2}$$ **Step 4.** Substitute $\mu_i^{E} = RT \ln \gamma_i$: $$-\frac{\bar{S}_i^{E}}{T} - \frac{R \ln \gamma_i}{T} = -\frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{T^2}$$ **Step 5.** Rearrange. Since $\bar{H}_i^{E} = \mu_i^{E} + T \bar{S}_i^{E} = RT \ln \gamma_i + T \bar{S}_i^{E}$, we can directly differentiate $\ln \gamma_i$: $$\left(\frac{\partial \ln \gamma_i}{\partial T}\right)_{P, x} = -\frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{RT^2}$$ $$\boxed{\therefore \quad \left(\frac{\partial \ln \gamma_i}{\partial T}\right)_{P, x} = -\frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{RT^2}}$$ This is the **canonical temperature-dependence equation**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 ‚ÄĒ Pressure Derivative of $\ln \gamma_i$ **Starting Point:** From the thermodynamic identity for excess chemical potential: $$d\mu_i^{E} = -\bar{S}_i^{E} dT + \bar{V}_i^{E} dP \quad (T, P, x \text{ varying})$$ where $\bar{V}_i^{E} = \bar{V}_i - V_i$ is the **partial molar excess volume**. **Step 1.** Hold T and x constant, vary only P: $$\left(\frac{\partial \mu_i^{E}}{\partial P}\right)_{T, x} = \bar{V}_i^{E} = \bar{V}_i - V_i$$ **Step 2.** Divide both sides by RT and apply chain rule: $$\left(\frac{\partial (\mu_i^{E}/RT)}{\partial P}\right)_{T, x} = \frac{\bar{V}_i - V_i}{RT}$$ **Step 3.** Since $\mu_i^{E}/RT = \ln \gamma_i$: $$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial \ln \gamma_i}{\partial P}\right)_{T, x} = \frac{\bar{V}_i - V_i}{RT}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 ‚ÄĒ Integrated T-Correction (Constant $\bar{H}_i^{E}$) **Step 1.** Separate variables in the differential equation: $$d(\ln \gamma_i) = -\frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^2}$$ **Step 2.** Integrate from $T_1$ to $T_2$: $$\int_{\ln \gamma_i(T_1)}^{\ln \gamma_i(T_2)} d(\ln \gamma_i) = -\frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$ **Step 3.** Evaluate RHS integral: $$\int_{T_1}^{T_2} T^{-2} \, dT = \left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_1}^{T_2} = -\frac{1}{T_2} + \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}$$ **Step 4.** Combine: $$\ln \gamma_i(T_2) - \ln \gamma_i(T_1) = -\frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = \frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$ $$\boxed{\ln \frac{\gamma_i(T_2)}{\gamma_i(T_1)} = \frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)}$$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 ‚ÄĒ Temperature Dependence of Wilson Parameter **Step 1.** Wilson model parameter: $$\Lambda_{ij} = \frac{V_j}{V_i} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta \lambda_{ij}}{RT}\right), \quad \Delta \lambda_{ij} \equiv \lambda_{ij} - \lambda_{ii}$$ **Step 2.** Differentiate $\ln \Lambda_{ij}$ with respect to T: $$\ln \Lambda_{ij} = \ln\!\left(\frac{V_j}{V_i}\right) - \frac{\Delta \lambda_{ij}}{RT}$$ $$\frac{d \ln \Lambda_{ij}}{dT} = 0 - \Delta \lambda_{ij} \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{RT^2}\right) = \frac{\Delta \lambda_{ij}}{RT^2}$$ **Step 3.** Therefore the temperature sensitivity of $\Lambda_{ij}$: $$\boxed{\frac{d \Lambda_{ij}}{dT} = \Lambda_{ij} \cdot \frac{\Delta \lambda_{ij}}{RT^2}}$$ **Step 4.** Since $\Lambda_{ij}$ appears inside the Wilson $\ln \gamma_i$ expression, this propagates directly into the temperature sensitivity of $\gamma_i$. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix ‚ÄĒ T vs P Effects | Aspect | Temperature (T) Effect | Pressure (P) Effect | |---|---|---| | Governing Equation | $\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln \gamma}{\partial T}\right) = -\dfrac{\bar{H}^E}{RT^2}$ | $\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln \gamma}{\partial P}\right) = \dfrac{\bar{V} - V}{RT}$ | | Driving Property | Partial molar **excess enthalpy** | Partial molar **excess volume** | | Typical Magnitude (Liquid) | **Significant** (factor of 2‚Äď10√ó change) | **Negligible** (< 1% per 10 bar) | | Practical Importance | **High** (distillation, extraction) | **Low** for liquids; **High** for gases/supercritical | | Effect on Azeotropes | **Strong** ‚ÄĒ azeotropes shift/vanish with T | **Weak** ‚ÄĒ almost no shift | | Sign Convention | $\bar{H}^E < 0 \Rightarrow \gamma \to 1$ as T ‚ÜĎ | $\bar{V} > V \Rightarrow \gamma \uparrow$ as P ‚ÜĎ | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Map ‚ÄĒ How T and P Influence Activity Coefficients This Mermaid diagram maps the **cause-effect chain** from molecular interactions to macroscopic process outcomes through T and P variations. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Molecular Interactions
H-bonding, vdW, ionic] --> nodeB[Excess Gibbs Energy GE] nodeB --> nodeC[Activity Coefficient gamma_i] nodeT[Temperature Change dT] --> nodeD[Excess Enthalpy HE_bar] nodeD --> nodeC nodeP[Pressure Change dP] --> nodeE[Excess Volume VE_bar] nodeE --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeF[Phase Equilibrium
K-values, relative volatility] nodeC --> nodeG[Reaction Equilibrium
K_a, K_gamma, K_x] nodeF --> nodeH[Distillation Column Design] nodeF --> nodeI[Liquid-Liquid Extraction] nodeG --> nodeJ[Reactor Yield Optimization] nodeH --> nodeK[Industrial Process Output] nodeI --> nodeK nodeJ --> nodeK ``` --- ### 4.2 Workflow ‚ÄĒ Applying T/P Correction to Activity Coefficients ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart[Step 1: Identify gamma_i at Reference T0, P0] --> nodeStep2[Step 2: Obtain Excess Properties HE_bar, VE_bar] nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3{Choose Target T, P} nodeStep3 --> nodePathA[T-Correction Path] nodeStep3 --> nodePathB[P-Correction Path] nodePathA --> nodeEqT[Apply Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation] nodeEqT --> nodeIntT[Integrate d ln gamma over T] nodeIntT --> nodeOutT[New gamma_i at T_new] nodePathB --> nodeEqP[Apply Poynting-Volumetric Equation] nodeEqP --> nodeIntP[Integrate d ln gamma over P] nodeIntP --> nodeOutP[New gamma_i at P_new] nodeOutT --> nodeFinal[Combine T and P corrections for final gamma_i] nodeOutP --> nodeFinal nodeFinal --> nodeApp[Apply to Phase or Reaction Equilibrium Calculation] ``` --- ### 4.3 Decision Tree ‚ÄĒ When Does Pressure Matter for $\gamma_i$? ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeQ1{What is the phase?} nodeQ1 --> nodeLiq[Liquid Phase] nodeQ1 --> nodeGas[Gas Phase] nodeQ1 --> nodeSC[Supercritical Phase] nodeLiq --> nodeAns1[Pressure Effect NEGLIGIBLE
gamma_i approx constant with P] nodeGas --> nodeAns2[Use FUGACITY COEFFICIENT phi
not activity coefficient] nodeSC --> nodeAns3[Pressure Effect SIGNIFICANT
Apply Poynting correction with VE_bar] nodeAns1 --> nodeNote[Continue with T-correction only] nodeAns2 --> nodeNote2[Switch to equation of state] nodeAns3 --> nodeStep[Integrate over full P-range using EOS] ``` --- ### 4.4 Block Diagram ‚ÄĒ Temperature Effects on Common Activity Coefficient Models ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeM[Margules 2-Suffix
A = a / RT] --> nodeT[Apply d ln gamma / dT = -HE_bar / RT^2] nodeV[Van Laar
A = a/RT, B = b/RT] --> nodeT nodeW[Wilson
Lambda_ij exp -delta_lambda / RT] --> nodeT nodeN[NRTL
tau_ij = a_ij / RT] --> nodeT nodeU[UNIQUAC
tau_ij exp -du_ij / RT] --> nodeT nodeT --> nodeOut[Predicted gamma_i as function of T] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Question 1. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024 Model Question]** *State the Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation that governs the temperature dependence of activity coefficients. Define every term clearly.* **Model Answer:** The temperature dependence of the activity coefficient of component *i* in a liquid mixture at constant pressure and composition is governed by: $$\left(\frac{\partial \ln \gamma_i}{\partial T}\right)_{P, x} = -\frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{RT^2}$$ **Term Definitions:** - $\gamma_i$ ‚ÄĒ Activity coefficient of component *i* (dimensionless). - $T$ ‚ÄĒ Absolute temperature in **Kelvin (K)**. - $\bar{H}_i^{E}$ ‚ÄĒ Partial molar **excess enthalpy** of component *i* in J/mol. This represents the enthalpy change when one mole of *i* is added to a large quantity of mixture at constant T and P, minus the enthalpy of pure *i*. - $R$ ‚ÄĒ Universal gas constant, **8.314 J/(mol¬∑K)**. **Physical meaning:** If mixing is exothermic ($\bar{H}_i^{E} < 0$), increasing T drives $\ln \gamma_i$ toward zero (i.e., $\gamma_i \to 1$, ideal behavior). --- **Question 2. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023 Model Question]** *Explain why the effect of pressure on liquid-phase activity coefficients is generally negligible compared to the temperature effect.* **Model Answer:** The pressure dependence is: $$\left(\frac{\partial \ln \gamma_i}{\partial P}\right)_{T, x} = \frac{\bar{V}_i - V_i}{RT}$$ **Reasoning:** 1. **Tiny excess volume:** For liquids, $(\bar{V}_i - V_i)$ is typically of the order of $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ m¬≥/mol because liquid mixtures are nearly ideal volumetrically. 2. **Low compressibility:** Liquids are nearly incompressible, so $\bar{V}_i$ and $V_i$ are essentially constant over moderate pressure ranges. 3. **Order-of-magnitude estimate:** For $(\bar{V}_i - V_i) \sim 10^{-6}$ m¬≥/mol and $\Delta P = 10^7$ Pa (100 bar), $RT \sim 8.314 \times 298 \approx 2477$ J/mol: $$\Delta \ln \gamma_i \approx \frac{(10^{-6})(10^7)}{2477} \approx 0.004 \Rightarrow \sim 0.4\% \text{ change}$$ 4. **Comparison with T effect:** A 50 K temperature change can easily produce a 50‚Äď200% change in $\gamma_i$ for non-ideal systems. **Conclusion:** Pressure effects on liquid-phase $\gamma_i$ are routinely ignored for engineering design; only temperature corrections are applied. --- ### PART B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ## ūüďė QUESTION A ‚ÄĒ (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ July 2024 Model] **(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the relation between activity coefficient and excess Gibbs energy, derive the Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation: $$\left(\frac{\partial \ln \gamma_i}{\partial T}\right)_{P, x} = -\frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{RT^2}$$ Clearly state all assumptions and define every term. **(b) [7 Marks]** For a binary liquid mixture at 1 atm, the activity coefficient of component 1 at infinite dilution follows: $$\ln \gamma_1^{\infty}(T) = \frac{500}{T} - 0.5$$ where T is in Kelvin. If at $T_1 = 350$ K, $\ln \gamma_1^{\infty} = 0.929$, estimate $\bar{H}_1^{E,\infty}$ in J/mol, and predict $\ln \gamma_1^{\infty}$ at $T_2 = 400$ K. Comment on whether the mixing is exothermic or endothermic. --- ### Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Question A #### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation **Step 1.** By definition, the activity coefficient relates to the excess chemical potential: $$\mu_i^{E} = RT \ln \gamma_i \quad \cdots (1)$$ **Step 2.** Apply the **Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation** to the excess chemical potential (at constant P and x): $$\left[\frac{\partial (\mu_i^{E}/T)}{\partial T}\right]_{P, x} = -\frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{T^2} \quad \cdots (2)$$ **Step 3.** Expand the LHS using the quotient rule: $$\left[\frac{\partial (\mu_i^{E}/T)}{\partial T}\right] = \frac{1}{T}\left(\frac{\partial \mu_i^{E}}{\partial T}\right)_{P,x} - \frac{\mu_i^{E}}{T^2}$$ **Step 4.** From the fundamental relation $d\mu_i^{E} = -\bar{S}_i^{E} dT + \bar{V}_i^{E} dP$ at constant P, x: $$\left(\frac{\partial \mu_i^{E}}{\partial T}\right)_{P,x} = -\bar{S}_i^{E}$$ **Step 5.** Substituting: $$\frac{-\bar{S}_i^{E}}{T} - \frac{\mu_i^{E}}{T^2} = -\frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{T^2}$$ **Step 6.** Replace $\mu_i^{E}$ using equation (1): $$-\frac{\bar{S}_i^{E}}{T} - \frac{R \ln \gamma_i}{T} = -\frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{T^2}$$ **Step 7.** Recognize that $\bar{H}_i^{E} = \mu_i^{E} + T \bar{S}_i^{E}$. After rearrangement (or directly differentiating equation 1), we obtain: $$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial \ln \gamma_i}{\partial T}\right)_{P, x} = -\frac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{RT^2}}$$ **Assumptions:** (i) The liquid mixture has constant composition (x fixed); (ii) Pressure held constant; (iii) Ideal-gas-like reference state for the chemical potential definition. --- #### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Calculation **Step 1. Calculate $\bar{H}_1^{E,\infty}$ from the given $\ln \gamma_1^{\infty}(T)$:** $$\ln \gamma_1^{\infty}(T) = \frac{500}{T} - 0.5$$ **Step 2.** Differentiate with respect to T: $$\frac{d \ln \gamma_1^{\infty}}{dT} = -\frac{500}{T^2}$$ **Step 3.** Compare with the Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation: $$\frac{d \ln \gamma_1^{\infty}}{dT} = -\frac{\bar{H}_1^{E,\infty}}{RT^2}$$ Equating: $$-\frac{500}{T^2} = -\frac{\bar{H}_1^{E,\infty}}{RT^2}$$ $$\therefore \bar{H}_1^{E,\infty} = 500 \, R$$ **Step 4.** Substitute $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K): $$\bar{H}_1^{E,\infty} = 500 \times 8.314 = 4157 \text{ J/mol}$$ **Step 5. Predict $\ln \gamma_1^{\infty}$ at $T_2 = 400$ K:** Using the integrated form with constant $\bar{H}_1^{E,\infty}$: $$\ln \gamma_1^{\infty}(T_2) - \ln \gamma_1^{\infty}(T_1) = \frac{\bar{H}_1^{E,\infty}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$ $$\Delta \ln \gamma_1^{\infty} = \frac{4157}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{400} - \frac{1}{350}\right)$$ $$\Delta \ln \gamma_1^{\infty} = 500 \times (0.0025 - 0.002857) = 500 \times (-0.000357)$$ $$\Delta \ln \gamma_1^{\infty} = -0.1786$$ $$\ln \gamma_1^{\infty}(400) = 0.929 - 0.179 = 0.750$$ **Step 6. Verification using direct substitution:** $$\ln \gamma_1^{\infty}(400) = \frac{500}{400} - 0.5 = 1.25 - 0.5 = 0.750 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 7. Comment on the energetics:** Since $\bar{H}_1^{E,\infty} = +4157$ J/mol > 0, the mixing is **endothermic**. This means energy is absorbed when component 1 mixes into component 2 at infinite dilution. As T increases, $\gamma_1^{\infty}$ *decreases* (from 2.53 at 350 K to 2.12 at 400 K) ‚ÄĒ the system moves toward more ideal behavior at higher T, which is consistent with the **Le Chatelier principle** for endothermic mixing. --- ## ūüďó QUESTION B ‚ÄĒ (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2023 Model] **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the pressure dependence of the activity coefficient: $$\left(\frac{\partial \ln \gamma_i}{\partial P}\right)_{T, x} = \frac{\bar{V}_i - V_i}{RT}$$ Show all steps clearly. Hence, obtain the integrated form assuming incompressible liquid behavior. **(b) [7 Marks]** A liquid mixture contains ethanol (1) and water (2). At $T = 350$ K and $P = 1$ bar, $\gamma_1 = 1.95$. The partial molar volume of ethanol in the mixture is $\bar{V}_1 = 58.2$ cm¬≥/mol, and the pure ethanol molar volume is $V_1 = 58.0$ cm¬≥/mol. Estimate $\gamma_1$ at $P = 50$ bar and $T = 350$ K. Comment on the result. --- ### Model Solution ‚ÄĒ Question B #### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation **Step 1.** Total differential of the excess chemical potential: $$d\mu_i^{E} = -\bar{S}_i^{E} dT + \bar{V}_i^{E} dP \quad \text{(at constant x)}$$ where $\bar{V}_i^{E} = \bar{V}_i - V_i$ is the **partial molar excess volume**. **Step 2.** At constant T and x, the partial derivative reduces to: $$\left(\frac{\partial \mu_i^{E}}{\partial P}\right)_{T, x} = \bar{V}_i - V_i$$ **Step 3.** Divide by RT: $$\frac{1}{RT}\left(\frac{\partial \mu_i^{E}}{\partial P}\right)_{T, x} = \frac{\bar{V}_i - V_i}{RT}$$ **Step 4.** Note that: $$\left(\frac{\partial \mu_i^{E}}{\partial P}\right)_{T,x} = RT \left(\frac{\partial \ln \gamma_i}{\partial P}\right)_{T, x}$$ (This follows from $\mu_i^{E} = RT \ln \gamma_i$, holding T constant.) **Step 5.** Therefore: $$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial \ln \gamma_i}{\partial P}\right)_{T, x} = \frac{\bar{V}_i - V_i}{RT}}$$ **Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Integrated form for incompressible liquids:** If $\bar{V}_i$ and $V_i$ are constant over the pressure range (incompressibility assumption): $$\int_{\ln \gamma_i(P_1)}^{\ln \gamma_i(P_2)} d(\ln \gamma_i) = \frac{\bar{V}_i - V_i}{RT} \int_{P_1}^{P_2} dP$$ $$\boxed{\ln \frac{\gamma_i(P_2)}{\gamma_i(P_1)} = \frac{(\bar{V}_i - V_i)(P_2 - P_1)}{RT}}$$ --- #### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Calculation **Given:** - $T = 350$ K - $P_1 = 1$ bar = $10^5$ Pa - $P_2 = 50$ bar = $50 \times 10^5$ Pa = $5 \times 10^6$ Pa - $\bar{V}_1 = 58.2$ cm¬≥/mol = $58.2 \times 10^{-6}$ m¬≥/mol - $V_1 = 58.0$ cm¬≥/mol = $58.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m¬≥/mol - $\gamma_1(P_1) = 1.95$ **Step 1. Compute the excess volume:** $$\bar{V}_1 - V_1 = 58.2 \times 10^{-6} - 58.0 \times 10^{-6} = 0.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$$ **Step 2. Compute the pressure difference:** $$\Delta P = P_2 - P_1 = 5 \times 10^6 - 1 \times 10^5 = 4.9 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 3. Compute RT:** $$RT = 8.314 \times 350 = 2909.9 \text{ J/mol}$$ **Step 4. Apply the integrated form:** $$\ln \frac{\gamma_1(P_2)}{\gamma_1(P_1)} = \frac{(0.2 \times 10^{-6})(4.9 \times 10^6)}{2909.9}$$ $$= \frac{0.98}{2909.9} = 3.368 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 5. Solve for $\gamma_1(P_2)$:** $$\ln \gamma_1(P_2) = \ln \gamma_1(P_1) + 3.368 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\ln \gamma_1(P_2) = \ln(1.95) + 3.368 \times 10^{-4} = 0.6678 + 0.000337 = 0.6681$$ $$\gamma_1(P_2) = e^{0.6681} = 1.9503$$ **Step 6. Compute percentage change:** $$\% \text{ change} = \frac{1.9503 - 1.95}{1.95} \times 100\% \approx 0.017\%$$ **Comment:** The pressure change from 1 bar to 50 bar produces only a **0.017% change** in $\gamma_1$. This confirms the **negligible pressure dependence** of liquid-phase activity coefficients. The positive change arises because $\bar{V}_1 > V_1$ ‚ÄĒ ethanol's partial molar volume in the mixture is slightly larger than its pure-state volume (due to molecular packing disruption by water). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > 1. **Unit Conversion:** Always convert $\bar{V}_i - V_i$ from cm¬≥/mol to m¬≥/mol before substituting into equations with SI units (Pa, J/mol). Forgetting this conversion gives answers off by a factor of $10^6$. > 2. **Sign Error:** Many students confuse $\bar{V}_i - V_i$ with $V_i - \bar{V}_i$. The correct form is $(\bar{V}_i - V_i)$ ‚ÄĒ partial molar minus pure. > 3. **Ideal vs Real Solutions:** Do not apply Poynting-type pressure corrections to *ideal solutions* ‚ÄĒ $\bar{V}_i = V_i$ for an ideal solution, so the derivative is identically zero. > 4. **Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz Sign:** Forgetting the negative sign is a common error. The correct relation is $\partial \ln \gamma_i / \partial T = -\bar{H}_i^{E}/RT^2$. > 5. **Reference State:** The KTU answer must explicitly state the reference state (usually the Lewis‚ÄďRandall rule: pure liquid at T and P of the system). --- ### ūüďĆ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ‚úÖ **Master equation (T effect):** $\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln \gamma_i}{\partial T}\right)_{P,x} = -\dfrac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{RT^2}$ - ‚úÖ **Master equation (P effect):** $\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln \gamma_i}{\partial P}\right)_{T,x} = \dfrac{\bar{V}_i - V_i}{RT}$ - ‚úÖ **Integrated T form (constant $\bar{H}^{E}$):** $\ln \dfrac{\gamma_i(T_2)}{\gamma_i(T_1)} = \dfrac{\bar{H}_i^{E}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ - ‚úÖ **Integrated P form (incompressible liquid):** $\ln \dfrac{\gamma_i(P_2)}{\gamma_i(P_1)} = \dfrac{(\bar{V}_i - V_i)(P_2 - P_1)}{RT}$ - ‚úÖ **Magnitude rule of thumb:** Temperature effects on $\gamma_i$ are **large**; pressure effects on liquids are **negligible**. - ‚úÖ **Exothermic mixing** ($\bar{H}^{E} < 0$): $\gamma \to 1$ as T rises. - ‚úÖ **Endothermic mixing** ($\bar{H}^{E} > 0$): $\gamma$ moves further from 1 as T rises. - ‚úÖ **Excess volume** $\bar{V}_i^{E} > 0$ means $\gamma$ rises with P. - ‚úÖ **Wilson parameter:** $\Lambda_{ij} = \dfrac{V_j}{V_i}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\lambda_{ij}-\lambda_{ii}}{RT}\right)$ - ‚úÖ **Margules/van Laar parameter:** $A(T) = a/RT$, $B(T) = b/RT$ - ‚úÖ **Key constants:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K); $P^{ref} = 1$ bar (Lewis‚ÄďRandall). - ‚úÖ **Engineering relevance:** Distillation, extraction, reactor design, azeotrope breaking.

topics.title: Gibbs-Duhem Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gibbs-Duhem Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem Equation:** A fundamental thermodynamic identity that relates the simultaneous changes in the chemical potentials ($\mu_i$), temperature ($T$), and pressure ($P$) of a multicomponent, single-phase system at thermodynamic equilibrium. It expresses the fact that the intensive state of a system cannot be specified independently ‚ÄĒ the variables are coupled.

The most general form of the Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem equation is:

$$S\,dT - V\,dP + \sum_{i} n_i\,d\mu_i = 0$$

Dividing the equation by the total number of moles $n = \sum n_i$, the **molar form** is obtained:

$$dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP \quad \text{(Fundamental Property Relation)}$$

When extended to multicomponent systems, the Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem equation in **molar form** becomes:

$$\bar{S}\,dT - \bar{V}\,dP + \sum_{i} x_i\,d\mu_i = 0$$

where:
- $\bar{S}$ = molar entropy (J/mol¬∑K)
- $\bar{V}$ = molar volume (m¬≥/mol)
- $x_i$ = mole fraction of component $i$
- $\mu_i$ = chemical potential of component $i$ (J/mol)
- $n_i$ = number of moles of component $i$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Family Budget Constraint**

Imagine a family managing a **shared monthly budget of ‚āĻ100,000**. There are three siblings ‚ÄĒ A, B, and C ‚ÄĒ who each draw from the pool. If A suddenly demands **‚āĻ10,000 more**, then B and C must collectively sacrifice ‚āĻ10,000 to keep the budget balanced. You cannot freely vary each person's share independently.

The Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem equation plays **exactly this role** in thermodynamics:
- The "family budget" is the **total Gibbs free energy** of the system.
- The "siblings" are the **chemical potentials** $\mu_i$, **temperature**, and **pressure**.
- If the temperature rises (or pressure changes), the chemical potentials of the components **must shift collectively** to maintain equilibrium.

A simpler analogy: imagine placing a **ball** on a stretched rubber sheet. The ball cannot move up without the sheet deforming downward somewhere else ‚ÄĒ the system responds as a whole.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|--------|--------------|------------------|
| Temperature | $T$ | **K (Kelvin)** | Absolute thermal intensity |
| Pressure | $P$ | **Pa (Pascal)** or **bar** | Mechanical intensive variable |
| Chemical Potential | $\mu_i$ | **J/mol** | Partial molar Gibbs energy |
| Mole Fraction | $x_i$ | **dimensionless** | Composition intensive variable |
| Molar Entropy | $\bar{S}$ | **J/(mol¬∑K)** | Molar disorder measure |
| Molar Volume | $\bar{V}$ | **m¬≥/mol** | Molar space occupation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem equation is the cornerstone for **Vapor‚ÄďLiquid Equilibrium (VLE)** calculations, **activity coefficient models**, and consistency tests for experimental VLE data (e.g., the **Duhem‚ÄďMargules equation** and **Redlich‚ÄďKister test**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown ‚ÄĒ The "Why" and "How"

The Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem equation arises from a **subtle but profound** observation: **Gibbs free energy is an extensive property, but its natural variables are intensive**. This creates a mathematical constraint that forces the intensive variables to be coupled.

### Logical Derivation Chain:

- **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Start with the Fundamental Property Relation (FPR):**
  For a multicomponent, single-phase closed system:
  $$d(nG) = -nS\,dT + nV\,dP + \sum_{i} \mu_i\,dn_i$$
  where $\mu_i = \left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T,P,n_{j \ne i}}$ is the **chemical potential**.

- **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply Euler's Theorem for Homogeneous Functions:**
  Because $G$ is a first-order homogeneous function of $\{n_i\}$, integration yields:
  $$nG = \sum_{i} n_i \mu_i$$
  Differentiating:
  $$d(nG) = \sum_{i} \mu_i\,dn_i + \sum_{i} n_i\,d\mu_i$$

- **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Equate the Two Expressions for $d(nG)$:**
  $$-nS\,dT + nV\,dP + \sum_{i} \mu_i\,dn_i = \sum_{i} \mu_i\,dn_i + \sum_{i} n_i\,d\mu_i$$

- **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Cancel Identical Terms:**
  The $\sum_{i} \mu_i\,dn_i$ terms vanish, leaving the **Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem Equation**:
  $$\boxed{S\,dT - V\,dP + \sum_{i} n_i\,d\mu_i = 0}$$

- **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Convert to Molar (Intensive) Form:**
  Divide by total moles $n$:
  $$\boxed{\bar{S}\,dT - \bar{V}\,dP + \sum_{i} x_i\,d\mu_i = 0}$$

- **Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Specialization to Constant T and P:**
  For a binary mixture at constant temperature and pressure:
  $$x_1\,d\mu_1 + x_2\,d\mu_2 = 0$$
  This is the famous **Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem relation for binary systems**.

### Why This Matters ‚ÄĒ Physical Interpretation:

- **Intensive variables are not independent.** Specifying all $T, P$, and $x_i$ over-determines the system.
- In a binary system, knowing $\mu_1(x_1, x_2)$ at constant $T, P$ automatically fixes $\mu_2(x_1, x_2)$.
- This is why we cannot measure **partial molar properties** for each component independently.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Condition / Form | Use Case |
|---|----------|------------------|----------|
| 1 | $S\,dT - V\,dP + \sum n_i\,d\mu_i = 0$ | **General Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem** | Most general form |
| 2 | $\bar{S}\,dT - \bar{V}\,dP + \sum x_i\,d\mu_i = 0$ | **Molar form** | Per-mole analysis |
| 3 | $\sum x_i\,d\mu_i = 0$ | Constant $T$ and $P$ | Isothermal, isobaric |
| 4 | $x_1\,d\mu_1 + x_2\,d\mu_2 = 0$ | Binary, constant $T, P$ | Binary VLE |
| 5 | $x_1\,d\bar{M}_1 + x_2\,d\bar{M}_2 = 0$ | **Generalized Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem** for any partial molar property | Partial molar volume, enthalpy |
| 6 | $x_1\,d\ln\gamma_1 + x_2\,d\ln\gamma_2 = 0$ | Activity coefficient form (ideal gas reference, constant $T, P$) | VLE correlation |
| 7 | $x_1 \left(\dfrac{\partial \ln \gamma_1}{\partial x_1}\right)_{T,P} + x_2 \left(\dfrac{\partial \ln \gamma_2}{\partial x_1}\right)_{T,P} = 0$ | Differential form (binary) | Derivative-based calculations |
| 8 | Duhem‚ÄďMargules: $\dfrac{d\ln\gamma_1}{dx_1} = -\dfrac{x_2}{x_1} \cdot \dfrac{d\ln\gamma_2}{dx_1}$ | Binary system | $\gamma$ correlation |
| 9 | $\mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT\ln\hat{f}_i$ | Component fugacity form | Chemical potential definition |
| 10 | $G = \sum x_i \mu_i$ | Euler's theorem | Foundation for derivation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Note:** Avoid using the `|` symbol for absolute values in your answer scripts. Write $\vert x \vert$ instead. Always state the **conditions** (constant T, P) explicitly for each Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem variant ‚ÄĒ examiners award marks for stating assumptions.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Distillation Column Design:** The Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem equation (via activity coefficient models like **Margules, van Laar, Wilson, NRTL, UNIQUAC**) predicts VLE needed for tray-by-tray design.
- **Consistency Testing of VLE Data:** The **Redlich‚ÄďKister area test** uses integrated Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem to validate experimental data:
  $$\int_{0}^{1} \ln\left(\frac{\gamma_1}{\gamma_2}\right) dx_1 = 0$$
- **Polymer Solution Thermodynamics:** Predicting partial molar properties of solvent in polymer blends.
- **Electrolyte Solutions:** Modified Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem for ionic systems with electro-neutrality constraint.
- **Phase Equilibrium Calculations:** Solid‚Äďliquid, liquid‚Äďliquid equilibria.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Start:** The derivation begins from the **Fundamental Property Relation (FPR)** for a multicomponent closed system.

### Derivation 1: From the FPR to the General Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem Equation

**Step 1:** Write the differential of total Gibbs free energy for a multicomponent, single-phase, closed system:

$$d(nG) = -nS\,dT + nV\,dP + \sum_{i=1}^{C} \mu_i\,dn_i$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that $(nG)$ is a first-order homogeneous function of the mole numbers $\{n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_C\}$ because $G$ itself is extensive. Apply **Euler's Theorem for Homogeneous Functions**:

$$nG = \sum_{i=1}^{C} n_i \mu_i$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate this Euler-integrated form on both sides:

$$d(nG) = \sum_{i=1}^{C} \mu_i\,dn_i + \sum_{i=1}^{C} n_i\,d\mu_i$$

**Step 4:** Equate the two expressions for $d(nG)$ from Steps 1 and 3:
