topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Activity Coefficient Models: Wohl's Equation, Margules Equation, van Laar Equation, Wilson Equation (General Concepts Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Activity Coefficient?

In chemical engineering thermodynamics, the **activity coefficient (ő≥ŠĶĘ)** is a dimensionless factor used in thermodynamics to account for deviations of real solutions from ideal behavior. For a component *i* in a liquid mixture:

$$\mu_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln(\gamma_i x_i)$$

where *őľŠĶĘ* is the chemical potential, *xŠĶĘ* is the mole fraction, and *ő≥ŠĶĘ* quantifies **how non-ideal** the mixture is. When *ő≥ŠĶĘ = 1*, the solution behaves ideally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** All four models discussed (Wohl, Margules, van Laar, Wilson) are **excess Gibbs energy (GŠīĪ) models**. They differ in the assumed molecular interaction structure but share the common goal of correlating/predicting VLE data.

## 1.2 The Need for Activity Coefficient Models

Since vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) calculations for non-ideal mixtures require accurate ő≥ŠĶĘ values at all compositions, we need **semi-empirical models** that:
1. Require only a few experimentally determined binary parameters.
2. Allow interpolation and extrapolation of sparse VLE data.
3. Serve as the foundation for predicting multi-component behavior from binary data (using rules like the **Wohl summation** or **local composition**).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Molecular Social Party"

Imagine a party with two types of guests ‚ÄĒ let us call them **A (water-like, sociable)** and **B (oil-like, antisocial)**.

- **Ideal Solution:** Guests mingle completely randomly ‚ÄĒ A does not care if A or B is the neighbor. (*ő≥ŠĶĘ = 1*)
- **Positive Deviation (ő≥ > 1):** A and B strongly dislike each other (like oil and water). Each molecule tries to escape to the vapor phase, raising partial pressures above Raoult's law. (e.g., ethanol‚Äďwater)
- **Negative Deviation (ő≥ < 1):** A and B attract each other (e.g., chloroform‚Äďacetone via H-bonding). They "hold hands" in the liquid, lowering partial pressures.

The four models in this lesson are different **mathematical lenses** for describing the "social behavior" of these molecules:
- **Wohl's** equation is the **master template** from which Margules and van Laar can be derived.
- **Margules** uses a polynomial expansion in mole fraction (Taylor-series-like).
- **van Laar** uses a rational function (assumes the molecules are hard spheres of different sizes).
- **Wilson** uses a **local composition** concept (each molecule sees a *different* neighborhood than the bulk suggests).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Local Composition" Matters:**
> Around a polar molecule (say, methanol), there will be *more* methanol neighbors than the bulk mole fraction would suggest ‚ÄĒ because methanol "prefers" methanol. This **short-range ordering** is the physical basis of the Wilson equation, and it gives Wilson a major advantage: it can represent **strongly non-ideal** systems that two-suffix Margules cannot.

## 1.4 The Key Players at a Glance

| Model | Year | Number of Parameters (Binary) | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wohl | 1946 | 3 (z, q‚āĀ, q‚āā) | Generalized; gives Margules & van Laar as limits | Rarely used directly today |
| Margules (3-suffix) | 1895 | 2 (A‚āĀ‚āā, A‚āā‚āĀ) | Simple, symmetric form available | Cannot fit strongly skewed systems |
| van Laar | 1910 | 2 (A‚āĀ‚āā, A‚āā‚āĀ) | Good for size-asymmetric molecules | Fails for multi-component extrapolation |
| Wilson | 1964 | 2 (őõ‚āĀ‚āā, őõ‚āā‚āĀ) | Excellent for polar/non-ideal systems | **Cannot** predict **LLE** (liquid-liquid immiscibility) |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wohl's Equation ‚ÄĒ The Master Framework

Wohl (1946) developed a generalized excess Gibbs energy expression by introducing:
- **z** ‚ÄĒ a coordination number (typically set to z = 10 in older literature, often taken as 3 or dropped in modern versions).
- **qŠĶĘ** ‚ÄĒ effective molar volume or cross-section parameter for species *i*.

Wohl's expansion expresses ln ő≥ŠĶĘ in terms of mole fractions raised to a denominator, controlled by the ratio *q‚āĀ/q‚āā*. By choosing different values of this ratio, the **Margules** and **van Laar** equations emerge as special cases.

For a binary system, the **three-suffix Wohl equation** is:

$$\log \gamma_1 = x_2^2 \left[ A_{21} + 2 x_1 (A_{12} - A_{21}) \right]$$

$$\log \gamma_2 = x_1^2 \left[ A_{12} + 2 x_2 (A_{21} - A_{12}) \right]$$

where *A‚āĀ‚āā* and *A‚āā‚āĀ* are the Wohl interaction parameters (functions of z, q‚āĀ, q‚āā, and the pairwise interaction energies).

### Reduction to Two-Suffix (Symmetric) Form
If we set *A‚āĀ‚āā = A‚āā‚āĀ = A*:

$$\log \gamma_1 = A \, x_2^2 \qquad \log \gamma_2 = A \, x_1^2$$

This is exactly the **two-suffix Margules** equation, a perfectly symmetric model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Suffix" Count?**
> The number of suffixes equals the number of interaction parameters. *Two-suffix* uses one constant; *three-suffix* uses two. Higher-suffix versions exist (four, five, six) but are rarely used in KTU-level problems.

## 2.2 Margules Equation

The **Margules equations** are the most widely used activity coefficient models in chemical engineering education. They express the logarithm of activity coefficients as power series in mole fraction.

### Two-Suffix Margules (Symmetric)

$$\log \gamma_1 = A \, x_2^2 \qquad \log \gamma_2 = A \, x_1^2$$

- **One parameter** *A*.
- Symmetric in the sense that both equations have the same *A*.
- Adequate for systems that are only mildly non-ideal or roughly symmetric in their deviations.

### Three-Suffix Margules (Asymmetric)

$$\log \gamma_1 = x_2^2 \left[ A_{21} + 2 x_1 (A_{12} - A_{21}) \right]$$

$$\log \gamma_2 = x_1^2 \left[ A_{12} + 2 x_2 (A_{21} - A_{12}) \right]$$

- **Two parameters** *A‚āĀ‚āā* and *A‚āā‚āĀ*.
- Allows the activity coefficients to peak at different compositions ‚ÄĒ handles **asymmetric** systems.
- Same form as Wohl's three-suffix equation when *q‚āĀ = q‚āā* (van Laar reduces to this when *q‚āĀ = q‚āā* AND we set *A‚āĀ‚āā ‚Č† A‚āā‚āĀ*).

### Limiting Activity Coefficients

At infinite dilution (*x‚āĀ ‚Üí 0*, *x‚āā ‚Üí 1*):

$$\log \gamma_1^{\infty} = A_{21}$$

$$\log \gamma_2^{\infty} = A_{12}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Shortcut:** The Wohl/Margules parameters are often tabulated as **ő≥ŠĶĘ^‚ąě** (infinite-dilution activity coefficients). When a problem gives you "ő≥‚āĀ^‚ąě = 2.5" and "ő≥‚āā^‚ąě = 1.8", you directly read off *A‚āā‚āĀ = log 2.5* and *A‚āĀ‚āā = log 1.8*.

## 2.3 van Laar Equation

The **van Laar** equation (1910) was derived assuming molecules of different sizes have hard-sphere interactions. The two-parameter form is:

$$\log \gamma_1 = \frac{A_{12}}{\left[ 1 + \dfrac{A_{12}\, x_1}{A_{21}\, x_2} \right]^2}$$

$$\log \gamma_2 = \frac{A_{21}}{\left[ 1 + \dfrac{A_{21}\, x_2}{A_{12}\, x_1} \right]^2}$$

### Limiting Activity Coefficients

At infinite dilution:

$$\log \gamma_1^{\infty} = A_{12} \qquad \log \gamma_2^{\infty} = A_{21}$$

So *A‚āĀ‚āā* and *A‚āā‚āĀ* are again the log of infinite-dilution activity coefficients ‚ÄĒ exactly the same physical meaning as in Margules, but with a different *composition dependence*.

### Why Use van Laar?

- It is **exact at the limits** *x‚āĀ ‚Üí 0* and *x‚āā ‚Üí 0* by construction.
- It handles **size-asymmetric** systems (e.g., polymer‚Äďsolvent) better than Margules.
- The denominator structure means ő≥ŠĶĘ stays bounded and rarely misbehaves.

## 2.4 Wilson Equation (Local Composition Model)

The **Wilson equation** (1964) introduced a revolutionary idea: the **local composition** around a central molecule is *not* the same as the bulk composition. The model uses **Boltzmann factors** weighted by interaction energies to compute local mole fractions.

The Wilson parameters are defined as:

$$\Lambda_{12} = \frac{v_2}{v_1} \exp\!\left[ -\frac{(g_{12} - g_{11})}{RT} \right]$$

$$\Lambda_{21} = \frac{v_1}{v_2} \exp\!\left[ -\frac{(g_{21} - g_{22})}{RT} \right]$$

where:
- *vŠĶĘ* = liquid molar volume of pure *i* (cm¬≥/mol).
- *gŠĶĘ‚Īľ* = interaction energy between an *i‚Äďj* pair (J/mol).
- *(gŠĶĘ‚Īľ ‚ąí gŠĶĘŠĶĘ)* = energy difference, often written as **(aŠĶĘ‚Īľ)** in KTU textbooks.
- *R* = 8.314 J/(mol¬∑K), *T* in K.
- **őõ‚āĀ‚āā ‚Č† őõ‚āā‚āĀ** in general (unlike *q‚āĀ/q‚āā*).

The activity coefficients are:

$$\ln \gamma_1 = -\ln(x_1 + \Lambda_{12}\, x_2) + x_2 \left[ \frac{\Lambda_{12}}{x_1 + \Lambda_{12}\, x_2} - \frac{\Lambda_{21}}{x_2 + \Lambda_{21}\, x_1} \right]$$

$$\ln \gamma_2 = -\ln(x_2 + \Lambda_{21}\, x_1) - x_1 \left[ \frac{\Lambda_{12}}{x_1 + \Lambda_{12}\, x_2} - \frac{\Lambda_{21}}{x_2 + \Lambda_{21}\, x_1} \right]$$

### Limiting Activity Coefficients

$$\ln \gamma_1^{\infty} = 1 - \ln \Lambda_{12} - \Lambda_{21} \quad \text{(at } x_1 \to 0\text{)}$$

$$\ln \gamma_2^{\infty} = 1 - \ln \Lambda_{21} - \Lambda_{12} \quad \text{(at } x_2 \to 0\text{)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Major Limitation of Wilson:** Because Wilson always yields ő≥ŠĶĘ > 0, it **cannot predict liquid-liquid immiscibility (LLE)**. For partially miscible systems, use NRTL or UNIQUAC instead (outside the KTU 2024 Module 4 scope).

### Why Wilson Succeeds Where Margules Fails

- Wilson correctly predicts the **shape** of ő≥ŠĶĘ curves for strongly non-ideal systems (e.g., alcohol‚Äďwater).
- With only **two** parameters per binary, Wilson can fit highly skewed VLE data.
- It is **thermodynamically consistent** for VLE in completely miscible systems.

## 2.5 The Hierarchy at a Glance

The four models are **not independent** ‚ÄĒ they form a hierarchy:

- **Wohl** is the **parent**.
- **Margules (3-suffix)** = Wohl with *q‚āĀ = q‚āā* (size symmetry).
- **van Laar** = Wohl with a specific size-ratio limit and rational-function form.
- **Wilson** is **independent** in form, but uses the same two-parameter philosophy: each binary has *A‚āĀ‚āā* and *A‚āā‚āĀ* (or őõ‚āĀ‚āā, őõ‚āā‚āĀ).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Model | ő≥‚āĀ Expression | ő≥‚āā Expression | No. of Parameters | Limiting Values |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Margules (2-suffix) | $\log \gamma_1 = A\, x_2^2$ | $\log \gamma_2 = A\, x_1^2$ | 1 (*A*) | $\log \gamma_1^{\infty} = \log \gamma_2^{\infty} = A$ |
| Margules (3-suffix) | $\log \gamma_1 = x_2^2 [A_{21} + 2x_1(A_{12} - A_{21})]$ | $\log \gamma_2 = x_1^2 [A_{12} + 2x_2(A_{21} - A_{12})]$ | 2 | $\log \gamma_1^{\infty} = A_{21}$, $\log \gamma_2^{\infty} = A_{12}$ |
| van Laar | $\log \gamma_1 = A_{12} / [1 + (A_{12} x_1)/(A_{21} x_2)]^2$ | $\log \gamma_2 = A_{21} / [1 + (A_{21} x_2)/(A_{12} x_1)]^2$ | 2 | $\log \gamma_1^{\infty} = A_{12}$, $\log \gamma_2^{\infty} = A_{21}$ |
| Wilson | $\ln \gamma_1 = -\ln(x_1 + \Lambda_{12} x_2) + x_2[\Lambda_{12}/(x_1+\Lambda_{12}x_2) - \Lambda_{21}/(x_2+\Lambda_{21}x_1)]$ | $\ln \gamma_2 = -\ln(x_2 + \Lambda_{21} x_1) - x_1[\Lambda_{12}/(x_1+\Lambda_{12}x_2) - \Lambda_{21}/(x_2+\Lambda_{21}x_1)]$ | 2 (őõ‚āĀ‚āā, őõ‚āā‚āĀ) | $\ln \gamma_1^{\infty} = 1 - \ln \Lambda_{12} - \Lambda_{21}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The base of the logarithm must be **specified explicitly**:
> - If written as "log" ‚Üí use **base 10** (common in KTU textbooks for Margules/van Laar).
> - If written as "ln" ‚Üí use **natural log** (mandatory for Wilson).
> Mixing them up is the **#1 mistake** KTU students make on this topic.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Distillation column design:** Activity coefficient models are used in RADFRAC / Aspen simulations to predict K-values, hence minimum number of stages, reflux ratio, and tray-by-tray composition.
- **Solvent extraction:** Choosing a solvent for liquid‚Äďliquid extraction requires accurate ő≥ values at infinite dilution (ő≥ŠĶĘ^‚ąě), which these models provide.
- **Azeotrope prediction:** Whether a mixture forms an azeotrope (and at what composition) is determined by the **shape of the ő≥ curves** ‚ÄĒ Wilson is the gold standard here for VLE.
- **Reactor design:** Liquid-phase reaction equilibria (K‚āď) depend on activities, not mole fractions.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: How Margules Emerges from Wohl's Equation

We start from Wohl's three-suffix excess Gibbs energy expression in the form:

$$\frac{G^E}{RT\,(x_1 q_1 + x_2 q_2)} = -\left[ \frac{2 q_1 q_2}{q_1 + q_2} \right] a_{12} x_1 x_2 + \text{higher-order terms}$$

In Wohl's notation, the parameters *A‚āĀ‚āā* and *A‚āā‚āĀ* are related to the pairwise energy terms and the *q* parameters. The full derivation proceeds by defining:

$$A_{12} = \frac{q_1}{2} \left( \frac{z}{2} \ln \Gamma_1 - \ln \Gamma_{12} \right), \quad A_{21} = \frac{q_2}{2} \left( \frac{z}{2} \ln \Gamma_2 - \ln \Gamma_{21} \right)$$

where őďŠĶĘ = exp(‚ÄďőĪŠĶĘ/RT) and őďŠĶĘ‚Īľ = exp(‚ÄďőĪŠĶĘ‚Īľ/RT) are Boltzmann factors. These give the partial excess Gibbs energies:

$$\frac{\bar{G}_1^E}{RT} = \ln \gamma_1 = x_2^2 \left[ A_{21} + 2 x_1 (A_{12} - A_{21}) \right]$$

$$\frac{\bar{G}_2^E}{RT} = \ln \gamma_2 = x_1^2 \left[ A_{12} + 2 x_2 (A_{21} - A_{12}) \right]$$

**Step 1: Set q‚āĀ = q‚āā (size-symmetric limit).**

This means *A‚āĀ‚āā* and *A‚āā‚āĀ* are mathematically independent ‚ÄĒ Wohl's three-suffix form becomes the **three-suffix Margules** equation directly.

**Step 2: Set A‚āĀ‚āā = A‚āā‚āĀ = A (two-suffix limit).**

Then:

$$\ln \gamma_1 = x_2^2 [A + 2 x_1 (A - A)] = A\, x_2^2$$

$$\ln \gamma_2 = x_1^2 [A + 2 x_2 (A - A)] = A\, x_1^2$$

This is the **two-suffix (symmetric) Margules** equation. Q.E.D.

## 3.2 Derivation: How van Laar Emerges from Wohl's Equation

Starting from Wohl's expression with general *q‚āĀ* and *q‚āā* (asymmetric), the partial excess Gibbs energies for a binary system take the form:

$$\ln \gamma_1 = \frac{A_{12}\, x_2^2}{\left[ \dfrac{x_1 q_1 + x_2 q_2}{q_1} \right]^2} = \frac{A_{12}\, x_2^2}{\left( \dfrac{x_1 q_1}{q_1} + \dfrac{x_2 q_2}{q_1} \right)^2} = \frac{A_{12}\, x_2^2}{\left( x_1 + x_2 \dfrac{q_2}{q_1} \right)^2}$$

Let us define *r = q‚āā/q‚āĀ*:

$$\ln \gamma_1 = \frac{A_{12}\, x_2^2}{(x_1 + r\, x_2)^2}$$

Similarly:

$$\ln \gamma_2 = \frac{A_{21}\, x_1^2}{(x_2 + x_1/r)^2}$$

**Step 1: Rewrite the denominator using x‚āĀ = 1 ‚Äď x‚āā:**

$$(x_1 + r x_2)^2 = \left( \frac{x_1 + r x_2}{1} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{x_1 + r x_2}{x_1 + x_2} \right)^2$$

**Step 2: Multiply numerator and denominator by *x‚āĀ* to isolate the (1 + A‚āĀ‚āā x‚āĀ/A‚āā‚āĀ x‚āā) form.**

Note that *A‚āā‚āĀ/A‚āĀ‚āā = r* in Wohl's framework. So:

$$\frac{A_{12}}{x_1 + r x_2} \cdot \frac{1}{x_1 + r x_2} = \frac{A_{12}}{x_1 + r x_2} \cdot \frac{1}{x_1 + r x_2}$$

Let us write the entire expression:

$$\log \gamma_1 = \frac{A_{12}}{\left[ \dfrac{x_1 + (A_{12}/A_{21})\, x_2}{1} \right]^2} = \frac{A_{12}}{\left[ 1 + \dfrac{A_{12}\, x_1}{A_{21}\, x_2} \right]^2}$$

We have used the identity *A‚āā‚āĀ = r ¬∑ A‚āĀ‚āā* to replace *r x‚āā* with *(A‚āĀ‚āā/A‚āā‚āĀ) x‚āā*. The final van Laar form is:

$$\log \gamma_1 = \frac{A_{12}}{\left[ 1 + \dfrac{A_{12}\, x_1}{A_{21}\, x_2} \right]^2}$$

$$\log \gamma_2 = \frac{A_{21}}{\left[ 1 + \dfrac{A_{21}\, x_2}{A_{12}\, x_1} \right]^2}$$

Q.E.D. (van Laar derived from Wohl's master equation.)

## 3.3 Derivation: The Wilson Local Composition Concept

**The Physical Idea:** Consider a fluid mixture of species 1 and 2 with bulk mole fractions *x‚āĀ* and *x‚āā*. Now focus on the immediate neighborhood (the "local cell") of a central molecule of type 1. The probability of finding a molecule of type 2 in that local cell is **not** simply *x‚āā* ‚ÄĒ it is modified by an interaction-energy Boltzmann factor.

**Definition of Local Mole Fractions:**

$$\frac{x_{21}}{x_{11}} = \frac{x_2}{x_1} \cdot \frac{\exp(-g_{21}/RT)}{\exp(-g_{11}/RT)}$$

That is, the ratio of molecules of type 2 to molecules of type 1 *near a central 1* is weighted by their Boltzmann interaction factors.

**Step 1: Simplify the exponent.**

$$\frac{x_{21}}{x_{11}} = \frac{x_2}{x_1} \cdot \exp\!\left[ -\frac{(g_{21} - g_{11})}{RT} \right]$$

Define the parameter:

$$\Lambda_{21} = \frac{v_1}{v_2} \exp\!\left[ -\frac{(g_{21} - g_{22})}{RT} \right]$$

But the local composition ratio commonly uses:

$$\frac{x_{21}}{x_{11}} = \frac{x_2}{x_1} \cdot \frac{\exp(-g_{21}/RT)}{\exp(-g_{11}/RT)} = \frac{x_2}{x_1} \cdot \exp\!\left[ -\frac{(g_{21} - g_{11})}{RT} \right]$$

**Step 2: Apply the normalization x‚āĀ‚āĀ + x‚āā‚āĀ = 1** (the local cell around molecule 1 is fully occupied by 1 and 2):

$$x_{11} = \frac{x_1}{x_1 + x_2\, \Lambda_{12}}, \quad x_{21} = \frac{x_2\, \Lambda_{12}}{x_1 + x_2\, \Lambda_{12}}$$

where the Wilson parameter $\Lambda_{12} = (v_2/v_1)\, \exp[-(g_{12} - g_{11})/RT]$.

**Step 3: Build the GŠīĪ/RT expression from the local compositions.**

The two-fluid theory of molecular interactions leads to:

$$\frac{G^E}{RT} = -x_1 \ln(x_1 + \Lambda_{12}\, x_2) - x_2 \ln(x_2 + \Lambda_{21}\, x_1)$$

**Step 4: Differentiate with respect to nŠĶĘ to obtain ln ő≥ŠĶĘ.**

Using $\ln \gamma_i = \partial(n G^E / RT)/\partial n_i$ at constant T, P, and other n‚Īľ:

$$\ln \gamma_1 = -\ln(x_1 + \Lambda_{12}\, x_2) + x_2 \left[ \frac{\Lambda_{12}}{x_1 + \Lambda_{12}\, x_2} - \frac{\Lambda_{21}}{x_2 + \Lambda_{21}\, x_1} \right]$$

$$\ln \gamma_2 = -\ln(x_2 + \Lambda_{21}\, x_1) - x_1 \left[ \frac{\Lambda_{12}}{x_1 + \Lambda_{12}\, x_2} - \frac{\Lambda_{21}}{x_2 + \Lambda_{21}\, x_1} \right]$$

This is the **Wilson equation** for a binary system. Q.E.D.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Wohl (3-suffix) | Margules (3-suffix) | van Laar | Wilson |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Functional form | Polynomial in *x* | Polynomial in *x* | Rational | Logarithmic |
| Size asymmetry handling | Partial (via *q*) | No | Yes (via *A‚āĀ‚āā/A‚āā‚āĀ* ratio) | Yes (via *v*) |
| Number of binary parameters | 2 (or 3 with z) | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| Temperature dependence | Through *A*'s (empirical) | Through *A*'s (empirical) | Through *A*'s (empirical) | **Built-in** (Boltzmann factor) |
| Can predict LLE? | No | No | No | **No** |
| Fits strongly skewed VLE? | Moderate | Poor | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| Base of log | log‚āĀ‚āÄ or ln | log‚āĀ‚āÄ typical | log‚āĀ‚āÄ typical | ln only |
| Modern usage | Mostly historical | Educational/legacy | Legacy | **Standard industrial** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** Notice that *Wilson* is the only model in this group that includes **temperature dependence** in the parameter definition (via the exponential term). This means once őõ‚āĀ‚āā and őõ‚āā‚āĀ are fitted at one temperature, they can be used across a range of temperatures ‚ÄĒ though in practice, the *(gŠĶĘ‚Īľ ‚Äď gŠĶĘŠĶĘ)* terms are often treated as **temperature-independent constants** for narrow T ranges.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define the Margules Two-Suffix Equation. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **two-suffix (or symmetric) Margules equation** is the simplest form of the Margules activity coefficient model. It assumes the excess Gibbs energy of mixing is symmetric in composition and uses a single interaction parameter *A*.

For a binary mixture of components 1 and 2, it is expressed as:

$$\log \gamma_1 = A\, x_2^2$$

$$\log \gamma_2 = A\, x_1^2$$

where *ő≥ŠĶĘ* is the activity coefficient of component *i*, *xŠĶĘ* is the mole fraction, and *A* is a temperature-dependent empirical constant. The equation satisfies the boundary conditions *ő≥‚āĀ ‚Üí 1* as *x‚āĀ ‚Üí 1* and *ő≥‚āā ‚Üí 1* as *x‚āā ‚Üí 1*. It is suitable only for **mildly non-ideal** systems where both *ő≥ŠĶĘ^‚ąě* values are approximately equal.

---

### Question A2: State any two limitations of the Wilson equation. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Cannot Predict Liquid-Liquid Equilibrium (LLE):** Because the Wilson equation always predicts ő≥ŠĶĘ > 0, it cannot represent the type of phase splitting seen in partially miscible systems (e.g., water‚Äďbenzene). The more advanced NRTL or UNIQUAC equations are required for such cases.

2. **Two-Parameter Constraint:** Although Wilson works excellently for VLE with two parameters per binary pair, it has only two adjustable constants and may fail for extremely complex mixtures where three or more parameters are needed. (Some authors cite the lack of a third parameter as a limitation in highly non-ideal systems.)

3. *(Optional third point ‚ÄĒ for a full 3-mark answer)* The temperature dependence in Wilson comes from the Boltzmann factor *exp(‚ÄďőĒg/RT)*, but the energy parameters *őĒg* are often assumed constant; if the system is studied over a wide temperature range, this assumption may break down.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks ‚ÄĒ Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: van Laar and Wohl's Equation [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024 (Modeled)]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Starting from Wohl's master equation, derive the van Laar equation for a binary liquid mixture. Clearly show the role of the parameters *q‚āĀ* and *q‚āā* (or *r = q‚āā/q‚āĀ*) and state the limiting form of the van Laar equation at infinite dilution.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** The van Laar parameters for the acetone(1)‚Äďchloroform(2) system at **T = 50 ¬įC** are *A‚āĀ‚āā = ‚Äď0.44* and *A‚āā‚āĀ = ‚Äď0.34*. Compute ő≥‚āĀ and ő≥‚āā at *x‚āĀ = 0.30* and verify the Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem consistency by checking that *(x‚āĀ d ln ő≥‚āĀ + x‚āā d ln ő≥‚āā)* = 0 approximately across a small composition step. *(Note: For the exam, you may simplify the verification by computing values at x‚āĀ = 0.25, 0.30, 0.35 and applying finite differences.)*

---

### Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation of van Laar from Wohl

Wohl's three-suffix excess Gibbs energy for a binary mixture of species 1 and 2 is given by:

$$\frac{G^E}{RT} = \left( x_1 q_1 + x_2 q_2 \right) \left[ \frac{2 q_1 q_2}{(q_1 + q_2)^2} \right] (-a_{12}) x_1 x_2 + \text{cubic terms}$$

The key parameters that emerge in the partial molar form are:

$$A_{12} = \frac{2 q_1^2 q_2}{(q_1 + q_2)^2} \cdot a_{12} \quad ; \quad A_{21} = \frac{2 q_1 q_2^2}{(q_1 + q_2)^2} \cdot a_{21}$$

From these, the partial excess Gibbs energies give:

$$\ln \gamma_1 = \frac{A_{12}\, x_2^2}{\left( x_1 + (q_2/q_1)\, x_2 \right)^2}$$

$$\ln \gamma_2 = \frac{A_{21}\, x_1^2}{\left( x_2 + (q_1/q_2)\, x_1 \right)^2}$$

**Substitution of *r = q‚āā/q‚āĀ* and noting that A‚āā‚āĀ/A‚āĀ‚āā = r:**

$$\log \gamma_1 = \frac{A_{12}}{\left[ 1 + \dfrac{A_{12}\, x_1}{A_{21}\, x_2} \right]^2}$$

$$\log \gamma_2 = \frac{A_{21}}{\left[ 1 + \dfrac{A_{21}\, x_2}{A_{12}\, x_1} \right]^2}$$

**Infinite dilution limit (x‚āĀ ‚Üí 0, x‚āā ‚Üí 1):**

$$\log \gamma_1^{\infty} = A_{12} \qquad \log \gamma_2^{\infty} = A_{21}$$

This is the **van Laar equation** in standard textbook form. (7 marks)

#### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Evaluation

**Given:** *A‚āĀ‚āā = ‚Äď0.44*, *A‚āā‚āĀ = ‚Äď0.34*, *x‚āĀ = 0.30*, *x‚āā = 0.70*.

**Step 1: Compute ő≥‚āĀ at x‚āĀ = 0.30.**

Compute the denominator term:

$$1 + \frac{A_{12}\, x_1}{A_{21}\, x_2} = 1 + \frac{(-0.44)(0.30)}{(-0.34)(0.70)} = 1 + \frac{-0.132}{-0.238} = 1 + 0.5546 = 1.5546$$

Then:

$$\log \gamma_1 = \frac{-0.44}{(1.5546)^2} = \frac{-0.44}{2.4168} = -0.1821$$

$$\gamma_1 = 10^{-0.1821} = 0.658$$

**Step 2: Compute ő≥‚āā at x‚āĀ = 0.30.**

$$1 + \frac{A_{21}\, x_2}{A_{12}\, x_1} = 1 + \frac{(-0.34)(0.70)}{(-0.44)(0.30)} = 1 + \frac{-0.238}{-0.132} = 1 + 1.8030 = 2.8030$$

$$\log \gamma_2 = \frac{-0.34}{(2.8030)^2} = \frac{-0.34}{7.8568} = -0.04328$$

$$\gamma_2 = 10^{-0.04328} = 0.904$$

**Step 3: Verify Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem consistency (finite-difference check at x‚āĀ = 0.25 and 0.35).**

At *x‚āĀ = 0.25, x‚āā = 0.75*:

$$\frac{A_{12}\, x_1}{A_{21}\, x_2} = \frac{(-0.44)(0.25)}{(-0.34)(0.75)} = \frac{-0.110}{-0.255} = 0.4314$$

$$\log \gamma_1 = \frac{-0.44}{(1.4314)^2} = \frac{-0.44}{2.0489} = -0.2148 \Rightarrow \gamma_1 = 0.610$$

$$\frac{A_{21}\, x_2}{A_{12}\, x_1} = \frac{-0.255}{-0.110} = 2.318$$

$$\log \gamma_2 = \frac{-0.34}{(3.318)^2} = \frac{-0.34}{11.009} = -0.0309 \Rightarrow \gamma_2 = 0.931$$

At *x‚āĀ = 0.35, x‚āā = 0.65*:

$$\frac{A_{12}\, x_1}{A_{21}\, x_2} = \frac{(-0.44)(0.35)}{(-0.34)(0.65)} = \frac{-0.154}{-0.221} = 0.6968$$

$$\log \gamma_1 = \frac{-0.44}{(1.6968)^2} = \frac{-0.44}{2.8792} = -0.1528 \Rightarrow \gamma_1 = 0.703$$

$$\frac{A_{21}\, x_2}{A_{12}\, x_1} = \frac{-0.221}{-0.154} = 1.435$$

$$\log \gamma_2 = \frac{-0.34}{(2.435)^2} = \frac{-0.34}{5.9292} = -0.0573 \Rightarrow \gamma_2 = 0.876$$

**Compute őĒln ő≥ for each step (őĒx‚āĀ = 0.05):**

At midpoint *x‚āĀ = 0.30*: ln(0.658) = ‚Äď0.4187, ln(0.904) = ‚Äď0.1010

Between *x‚āĀ = 0.25 and 0.30*: őĒ ln ő≥‚āĀ = ‚Äď0.4187 ‚Äď (‚Äď0.4947) = +0.0760, őĒ ln ő≥‚āā = ‚Äď0.1010 ‚Äď (‚Äď0.0715) = ‚Äď0.0295

Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem check: *x‚āĀ d ln ő≥‚āĀ + x‚āā d ln ő≥‚āā* = 0.30 √ó 0.0760 + 0.70 √ó (‚Äď0.0295) = 0.0228 ‚Äď 0.0207 ‚Čą 0.002

This is very close to zero, confirming approximate **Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem consistency** of the fitted van Laar parameters. (7 marks)

---

### Question B: Wilson Equation and Limiting Behavior [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023 (Modeled)]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** With the help of the local composition concept, derive the Wilson equation for a binary liquid mixture. Clearly state the physical meaning of the parameters őõ‚āĀ‚āā and őõ‚āā‚āĀ and explain why the Wilson equation cannot be used to predict liquid‚Äďliquid equilibrium.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** The Wilson parameters for the methanol(1)‚Äďwater(2) system at **T = 60 ¬įC** are *őõ‚āĀ‚āā = 0.4054* and *őõ‚āā‚āĀ = 0.7080*. Compute ő≥‚āĀ and ő≥‚āā at *x‚āĀ = 0.40*. Also, determine the infinite-dilution activity coefficients ő≥‚āĀ^‚ąě and ő≥‚āā^‚ąě for this system.

---

### Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation of Wilson's Equation

**The Local Composition Hypothesis:**
In a real fluid, the composition of the immediate neighborhood of a molecule of type *i* differs from the bulk composition. The probability of finding a *j* molecule next to an *i* molecule depends on the interaction energy *gŠĶĘ‚Īľ* via a Boltzmann factor:

$$\frac{x_{ji}}{x_{ii}} = \frac{x_j}{x_i} \cdot \frac{\exp(-g_{ji}/RT)}{\exp(-g_{ii}/RT)}$$

where *xŠĶĘ‚Īľ* denotes the local mole fraction of *j* around *i*, and *xŠĶĘ* is the bulk mole fraction of *i*.

**Define Wilson Parameters:**

$$\Lambda_{ij} = \frac{v_j}{v_i} \cdot \exp\!\left[ -\frac{(g_{ij} - g_{jj})}{RT} \right]$$

These are dimensionless, and encode both the **size ratio** (*v‚Īľ/vŠĶĘ*) and the **interaction energy difference** (*gŠĶĘ‚Īľ ‚Äď g‚Īľ‚Īľ*).

**Local Compositions via Normalization (x‚āĀ‚āĀ + x‚āā‚āĀ = 1):**

$$x_{11} = \frac{x_1}{x_1 + \Lambda_{12}\, x_2}, \quad x_{21} = \frac{\Lambda_{12}\, x_2}{x_1 + \Lambda_{12}\, x_2}$$

$$x_{22} = \frac{x_2}{x_2 + \Lambda_{21}\, x_1}, \quad x_{12} = \frac{\Lambda_{21}\, x_1}{x_2 + \Lambda_{21}\, x_1}$$

**Excess Gibbs Energy (from two-fluid theory):**

$$\frac{G^E}{RT} = -x_1 \ln(x_1 + \Lambda_{12}\, x_2) - x_2 \ln(x_2 + \Lambda_{21}\, x_1)$$

**Differentiate with respect to nŠĶĘ to obtain ln ő≥ŠĶĘ:**

$$\ln \gamma_1 = -\ln(x_1 + \Lambda_{12}\, x_2) + x_2 \left[ \frac{\Lambda_{12}}{x_1 + \Lambda_{12}\, x_2} - \frac{\Lambda_{21}}{x_2 + \Lambda_{21}\, x_1} \right]$$

$$\ln \gamma_2 = -\ln(x_2 + \Lambda_{21}\, x_1) - x_1 \left[ \frac{\Lambda_{12}}{x_1 + \Lambda_{12}\, x_2} - \frac{\Lambda_{21}}{x_2 + \Lambda_{21}\, x_1} \right]$$

**Why Wilson cannot predict LLE:**
Since *x‚āĀ + őõ‚āĀ‚āā x‚āā > 0* and *x‚āā + őõ‚āā‚āĀ x‚āĀ > 0* for all positive *x‚āĀ, x‚āā, őõ‚āĀ‚āā, őõ‚āā‚āĀ*, the logarithms and the bracketed terms remain finite. The function ln ő≥ŠĶĘ ‚Üí ‚Äď‚ąě only when one of the denominators ‚Üí 0, but this requires őõŠĶĘ‚Īľ to be negative, which is **physically forbidden** (őõŠĶĘ‚Īľ > 0 by the exponential definition). Therefore, the Wilson equation **always yields ő≥ŠĶĘ > 0** and **cannot describe a miscibility gap**. (7 marks)

#### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Evaluation

**Given:** őõ‚āĀ‚āā = 0.4054, őõ‚āā‚āĀ = 0.7080, *x‚āĀ = 0.40*, *x‚āā = 0.60*, *T = 60 ¬įC*.

**Step 1: Compute the two denominator terms.**

$$D_1 = x_1 + \Lambda_{12}\, x_2 = 0.40 + (0.4054)(0.60) = 0.40 + 0.2432 = 0.6432$$

$$D_2 = x_2 + \Lambda_{21}\, x_1 = 0.60 + (0.7080)(0.40) = 0.60 + 0.2832 = 0.8832$$

**Step 2: Compute ln ő≥‚āĀ.**

$$\ln \gamma_1 = -\ln(0.6432) + x_2 \left[ \frac{\Lambda_{12}}{D_1} - \frac{\Lambda_{21}}{D_2} \right]$$

$$= -\ln(0.6432) + 0.60 \left[ \frac{0.4054}{0.6432} - \frac{0.7080}{0.8832} \right]$$

$$= 0.4411 + 0.60 \left[ 0.6302 - 0.8017 \right]$$

$$= 0.4411 + 0.60 \times (-0.1715) = 0.4411 - 0.1029 = 0.3382$$

$$\gamma_1 = e^{0.3382} = 1.402$$

**Step 3: Compute ln ő≥‚āā.**

$$\ln \gamma_2 = -\ln(0.8832) - x_1 \left[ \frac{\Lambda_{12}}{D_1} - \frac{\Lambda_{21}}{D_2} \right]$$

$$= -\ln(0.8832) - 0.40 \times (-0.1715)$$

$$= 0.1243 + 0.0686 = 0.1929$$

$$\gamma_2 = e^{0.1929} = 1.213$$

**Step 4: Infinite-dilution activity coefficients.**

At *x‚āĀ ‚Üí 0, x‚āā ‚Üí 1*:

$$\ln \gamma_1^{\infty} = 1 - \ln \Lambda_{12} - \Lambda_{21} = 1 - \ln(0.4054) - 0.7080$$

$$= 1 - (-0.9027) - 0.7080 = 1 + 0.9027 - 0.7080 = 1.1947$$

$$\gamma_1^{\infty} = e^{1.1947} = 3.302$$

At *x‚āā ‚Üí 0, x‚āĀ ‚Üí 1*:

$$\ln \gamma_2^{\infty} = 1 - \ln \Lambda_{21} - \Lambda_{12} = 1 - \ln(0.7080) - 0.4054$$

$$= 1 - (-0.3453) - 0.4054 = 1 + 0.3453 - 0.4054 = 0.9399$$

$$\gamma_2^{\infty} = e^{0.9399} = 2.560$$

**Interpretation:** Both ő≥‚āĀ and ő≥‚āā are greater than 1, indicating **positive deviations from Raoult's law** in methanol‚Äďwater ‚ÄĒ consistent with the well-known behavior of this strongly non-ideal system. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Logarithm Base Confusion (Loss: 1‚Äď2 marks):** Margules and van Laar typically use **log‚āĀ‚āÄ**; Wilson strictly uses **ln**. If a problem says "compute log ő≥‚āĀ" but the model is Wilson, you must compute ln ő≥‚āĀ and either keep it in ln form or convert: *log‚āĀ‚āÄ x = ln x / 2.303*. Mixing bases is a guaranteed mark-deduction.
>
> 2. **Inverting the Parameters in Margules (Loss: 1‚Äď2 marks):** Many students write $\log \gamma_1 = A_{12}\, x_2^2$ instead of $\log \gamma_1 = A_{21}\, x_2^2$. The convention is that the *first* subscript of *A* corresponds to the *central* species... actually, the safe rule: $\log \gamma_1^{\infty} = A_{21}$ and $\log \gamma_2^{\infty} = A_{12}$. Memorize the cross-relation.
>
> 3. **van Laar Denominator Mismatch (Loss: 2‚Äď3 marks):** The denominator in van Laar is $[1 + A_{12} x_1 / (A_{21} x_2)]^2$. Forgetting to **square** it, or using the wrong ratio (*A‚āā‚āĀ/A‚āĀ‚āā* vs. *A‚āĀ‚āā/A‚āā‚āĀ*), is the most common algebraic error.
>
> 4. **Wilson Denominator Neglect (Loss: 2 marks):** The Wilson log term is $-\ln(x_1 + \Lambda_{12} x_2)$, **not** $-\ln x_1$. Forgetting the őõ-weighted denominator will yield totally wrong ő≥ values when őõ differs significantly from 1.
>
> 5. **Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem Check Errors (Loss: 1‚Äď2 marks):** When verifying consistency using finite differences, the equation is $\sum x_i\, d\ln \gamma_i = 0$, not $\sum d\ln \gamma_i = 0$ or $\sum x_i\, d\gamma_i = 0$. Many students confuse the differential form.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Wohl's equation** is the *master* framework; both Margules (3-suffix) and van Laar reduce to special cases of it.
- [x] **Two-suffix Margules** has *one* parameter *A*; **three-suffix Margules** has *two* parameters *A‚āĀ‚āā* and *A‚āā‚āĀ*.
- [x] **van Laar** uses a rational-function form, with *A‚āĀ‚āā* and *A‚āā‚āĀ* equal to $\log \gamma_1^\infty$ and $\log \gamma_2^\infty$.
- [x] **Wilson** introduces **local composition**: Boltzmann-weighted local mole fractions, encoded in őõ‚āĀ‚āā and őõ‚āā‚āĀ.
- [x] **Wilson is the only model here with built-in temperature dependence** (Boltzmann factor *exp(‚ÄďőĒg/RT)*).
- [x] **Wilson cannot predict LLE** because őõŠĶĘ‚Īľ > 0 always, so ő≥ŠĶĘ > 0 always.
- [x] **At infinite dilution:** Margules/van Laar: $\log \gamma_1^\infty = A_{21}$ (or $A_{12}$ for van Laar, depending on convention); Wilson: $\ln \gamma_1^\infty = 1 - \ln \Lambda_{12} - \Lambda_{21}$.
- [x] **Logarithm base:** log‚āĀ‚āÄ for Margules and van Laar (typically); ln (natural) for Wilson. Always re-read the problem statement.
- [x] **Symmetric vs. Asymmetric:** Two-suffix Margules is fully symmetric; three-suffix Margules, van Laar, and Wilson are all asymmetric in general.
- [x] **KTU exam favorite:** Derivations of Margules and van Laar from Wohl's equation appear almost every cycle; commit them to memory.
- [x] **Limiting activity coefficient** is a **direct measure** of how non-ideal the system is ‚ÄĒ a quick way to assess whether Margules (small ő≥^‚ąě) or Wilson (large ő≥^‚ąě) is needed.
- [x] **Positive deviation** ‚áí *A > 0* in Margules/van Laar; *őõ < 1* in Wilson. **Negative deviation** ‚áí *A < 0*; *őõ > 1*.
- [x] **Gibbs‚ÄďDuhem equation** must be satisfied by consistent model parameters: $x_1\, d\ln\gamma_1 + x_2\, d\ln\gamma_2 = 0$ at constant T, P.
- [x] **All four models** reduce to **Raoult's law** (ő≥ŠĶĘ = 1) for ideal solutions.
- [x] **For multi-component extrapolation,** all four models have known rules (Wohl's summation, Margules' Wohl expansion, van Laar's not recommended, Wilson's direct sum).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equations of VLE at High Pressures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Equations of VLE at High Pressures ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium (VLE) at High Pressures** describes the thermodynamic state at which a liquid and its vapor coexist in equilibrium when the system pressure is comparable to, or exceeds, the critical pressures of the constituent components. Under such conditions, the simplifying assumption of ideal vapor and ideal liquid phases breaks down. Therefore, **modified equations of state (EOS)** combined with **mixing rules**, or **excess Gibbs energy models coupled with fugacity coefficients**, are employed to accurately represent the non-ideal behavior of both phases. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Exact Syllabus Phrasing):** > *"Equations of VLE at high pressures account for non-ideality of both vapor and liquid phases by employing the ŌÜ‚ÄďŌÜ approach (using cubic equations of state) and the modified ő≥‚ÄďŌÜ approach (using activity coefficient models and Poynting correction)."* The fundamental equilibrium condition is: $$\mu_i^L(T,P,x_i) = \mu_i^V(T,P,y_i)$$ which, when recast in terms of **fugacity**, becomes the master VLE equation: $$f_i^L = f_i^V$$ ### 1.2 Two Fundamental Approaches (KTU High-Yield) | Approach | Liquid Phase Representation | Vapor Phase Representation | Typical Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **ő≥‚ÄďŌÜ Approach** | Activity coefficient (ő≥ŠĶĘ) | Fugacity coefficient (ŌÜŐāŠĶĘ) | Low to moderate pressures | | **ŌÜ‚ÄďŌÜ Approach** | Fugacity coefficient (ŌÜŠĶĘ) | Fugacity coefficient (ŌÜŐāŠĶĘ) | **High pressures** | The Master Equation for VLE in the ŌÜ‚ÄďŌÜ approach is: $$\phi_i^L \, x_i \, P = \phi_i^V \, y_i \, P \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi_i^L x_i = \phi_i^V y_i$$ ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The "Crowded Dance Floor"** > > Imagine a packed dance club (high-pressure vapor). People (molecules) bump into each other constantly ‚ÄĒ the crowd is so dense that dancers can no longer move freely. The simple "rules" that worked when the club was nearly empty (ideal gas law, Raoult's Law) fail completely when the room is packed. We need **new behavioral rules** for the crowd ‚ÄĒ these new rules are the **equations of state with mixing rules** (e.g., Peng-Robinson, Soave-Redlich-Kwong). The club bouncer (mixing rule) tells you how to "blend" the behaviors of two different groups of dancers (e.g., tall and short) so the overall crowd behavior is predicted accurately. ### 1.4 Why Ideal Methods Fail at High Pressures 1. **Liquid phase non-ideality:** At high pressures, the liquid is highly compressed, and the molar volume changes with pressure. This necessitates the **Poynting Correction Factor**. 2. **Vapor phase non-ideality:** At high pressures, the vapor deviates significantly from ideal gas behavior due to strong intermolecular forces. 3. **Critical region proximity:** As pressure approaches the critical point, both liquid and vapor phases become indistinguishable, requiring a unified equation of state (EOS) for both. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight ‚ÄĒ Standard Constants to Memorize:** > - Universal Gas Constant: **R = 8.314 J/(mol¬∑K)** > - Critical compressibility for many hydrocarbons: **Z_c ‚Čą 0.27‚Äď0.29** > - Atmospheric reference pressure: **P¬į = 101.325 kPa = 1 atm** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The ŌÜ‚ÄďŌÜ Approach: Cubic Equations of State (CEOS) A **Cubic Equation of State** is an empirical, pressure-explicit relation of the form **P = f(T, V)** that captures both vapor and liquid phase behavior using the same mathematical form. The four KTU-relevant cubic EOS are: #### (a) van der Waals Equation (1873) $$P = \frac{RT}{V-b} - \frac{a}{V^2}$$ with parameters: $$a = \frac{27R^2T_c^2}{64P_c}, \qquad b = \frac{RT_c}{8P_c}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Limitation (KTU frequently asked):** The van der Waals equation gives qualitatively correct phase behavior but quantitatively poor predictions of vapor pressure and liquid density. #### (b) Redlich-Kwong (RK) Equation (1949) $$P = \frac{RT}{V-b} - \frac{a}{T^{0.5}V(V+b)}$$ with parameters: $$a = \frac{0.42748 R^2 T_c^{2.5}}{P_c \sqrt{T}}, \qquad b = \frac{0.08664 RT_c}{P_c}$$ #### (c) Soave-Redlich-Kwong (SRK) Equation (1972) ‚ÄĒ KTU Most Tested $$P = \frac{RT}{V-b} - \frac{a(T)}{V(V+b)}$$ with temperature-dependent 'a' parameter: $$a(T) = 0.42748 \frac{R^2T_c^2}{P_c}\left[1 + m\left(1-\sqrt{T_r}\right)\right]^2$$ $$m = 0.480 + 1.574\omega - 0.176\omega^2$$ $$b = 0.08664 \frac{RT_c}{P_c}$$ #### (d) Peng-Robinson (PR) Equation (1976) ‚ÄĒ KTU High-Priority $$P = \frac{RT}{V-b} - \frac{a(T)}{V(V+b) + b(V-b)}$$ with: $$a(T) = 0.45724 \frac{R^2T_c^2}{P_c}\left[1 + \kappa\left(1-\sqrt{T_r}\right)\right]^2$$ $$\kappa = 0.37464 + 1.54226\omega - 0.26992\omega^2$$ $$b = 0.07780 \frac{RT_c}{P_c}$$ ### 2.2 Mixing Rules (van der Waals One-Fluid) For mixtures, parameters 'a' and 'b' are combined using **mixing rules**: $$a_{mix} = \sum_i \sum_j y_i y_j \, a_{ij}, \qquad a_{ij} = (a_i a_j)^{0.5}(1 - k_{ij})$$ $$b_{mix} = \sum_i y_i b_i$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Binary Interaction Parameter (kŠĶĘ‚Īľ):** A fitted constant accounting for unlike-pair interactions. **kŠĶĘŠĶĘ = k‚Īľ‚Īľ = 0**. Typical values: **‚ąí0.5 to +0.5**, often determined from experimental binary VLE data. ### 2.3 Activity Coefficient Models (ő≥‚ÄďŌÜ Approach with Poynting Correction) The modified Raoult's Law with Poynting correction: $$y_i P \phi_i^V = x_i \gamma_i f_i \, \exp\left[\frac{V_i^L (P - P_i^{sat})}{RT}\right]$$ | Model | Key Parameters | Strengths / Weaknesses | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Margules (1-const, 2-suffix)** | A | Symmetric systems | | **Margules (2-const, 3-suffix)** | A‚āĀ‚āā, A‚āā‚āĀ | Asymmetric systems | | **van Laar** | A‚āĀ‚āā, A‚āā‚āĀ | Handles large positive deviations | | **Wilson (1964)** | őõ‚āĀ‚āā, őõ‚āā‚āĀ | **Cannot handle liquid-liquid immiscibility** | | **NRTL (Renon, 1968)** | ŌĄ‚āĀ‚āā, ŌĄ‚āā‚āĀ, őĪ‚āĀ‚āā | Handles partial miscibility (őĪ = 0.2‚Äď0.47) | | **UNIQUAC (1975)** | ŌĄ‚āĀ‚āā, ŌĄ‚āā‚āĀ, q, r | Two-parameter; combinatorial + residual | | **UNIFAC (1975)** | Group interaction a‚āė‚āô | Predictive; no binary data required | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $P = \frac{RT}{V-b} - \frac{a}{V^2}$ | van der Waals EOS | | 2 | $P = \frac{RT}{V-b} - \frac{a}{T^{0.5}V(V+b)}$ | Redlich-Kwong EOS | | 3 | $a(T) = 0.42748 \frac{R^2T_c^2}{P_c}\left[1+m\left(1-\sqrt{T_r}\right)\right]^2$ | SRK a-parameter | | 4 | $m = 0.480 + 1.574\omega - 0.176\omega^2$ | SRK omega correction | | 5 | $a(T) = 0.45724 \frac{R^2T_c^2}{P_c}\left[1+\kappa\left(1-\sqrt{T_r}\right)\right]^2$ | PR a-parameter | | 6 | $\kappa = 0.37464 + 1.54226\omega - 0.26992\omega^2$ | PR omega correction | | 7 | $a_{mix} = \sum_i \sum_j y_i y_j a_{ij}$ | Quadratic mixing rule for a | | 8 | $b_{mix} = \sum_i y_i b_i$ | Linear mixing rule for b | | 9 | $a_{ij} = \sqrt{a_i a_j}(1-k_{ij})$ | Cross-parameter with BIP | | 10 | $\phi_i^L x_i = \phi_i^V y_i$ | ŌÜ‚ÄďŌÜ VLE criterion | | 11 | $y_iP\phi_i^V = x_i\gamma_i f_i \exp\!\left[\frac{V_i^L(P-P_i^{sat})}{RT}\right]$ | Modified Raoult's Law | | 12 | $K_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i} = \frac{\phi_i^L}{\phi_i^V}$ | K-value (ŌÜ‚ÄďŌÜ) | | 13 | $K_i = \frac{\gamma_i f_i}{P \phi_i^V} \exp\!\left[\frac{V_i^L(P-P_i^{sat})}{RT}\right]$ | K-value (ő≥‚ÄďŌÜ with Poynting) | | 14 | $\ln\gamma_1 = x_2^2\left[A_{12} + 2(A_{21}-A_{12})x_1\right]$ | Margules 2-const | | 15 | $\ln\gamma_1 = \frac{A_{12}}{\left[1+\frac{A_{12}x_1}{A_{21}x_2}\right]^2}$ | van Laar | | 16 | $\frac{G^E}{RT} = -x_1\ln(x_1+\Lambda_{12}x_2) - x_2\ln(x_2+\Lambda_{21}x_1)$ | Wilson model | | 17 | $\frac{G^E}{RT} = x_1 x_2\left(\frac{\tau_{21}G_{21}}{x_1+x_2 G_{21}}+\frac{\tau_{12}G_{12}}{x_2+x_1 G_{12}}\right)$ | NRTL model | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Note on Pipes in Tables:** Absolute value $\vert x \vert$ and conditional $\mid$ are written as `\vert` and `\mid` to prevent markdown table parser issues. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Petroleum Refining:** Deethanizer, debutanizer columns operating above 20 bar require SRK/PR. - **Supercritical Fluid Extraction:** SC-CO‚āā extraction of essential oils (P > 73.8 bar). - **LNG Production:** Methane‚Äďethane‚Äďpropane systems at cryogenic high pressures. - **Polymerization Reactors:** Ethylene polymerization at 1000‚Äď3000 bar requires EOS approach. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I: From Chemical Potential to the ŌÜ‚ÄďŌÜ Master Equation **Starting Point:** At VLE, the chemical potential equality holds: $$\mu_i^{L,ig}(T,P) + RT \ln \hat{f}_i^L / f_i^{L,ig} = \mu_i^{V,ig}(T,P) + RT \ln \hat{f}_i^V / f_i^{V,ig}$$ For the same pure component at the same T and P, the standard-state chemical potentials are equal ($\mu_i^{L,ig} = \mu_i^{V,ig}$). Hence: $$\hat{f}_i^L = \hat{f}_i^V$$ **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Expand the liquid fugacity:** By definition of fugacity coefficient: $$\hat{f}_i^L = \hat{\phi}_i^L \, x_i \, P$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Expand the vapor fugacity:** $$\hat{f}_i^V = \hat{\phi}_i^V \, y_i \, P$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Equate both expressions:** $$\hat{\phi}_i^L \, x_i \, P = \hat{\phi}_i^V \, y_i \, P$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Simplify (P cancels):** $$\boxed{\hat{\phi}_i^L \, x_i = \hat{\phi}_i^V \, y_i}$$ This is the **KTU Master VLE Equation for the ŌÜ‚ÄďŌÜ approach**, valid at all pressures. ### 3.2 Derivation II: Fugacity Coefficient from a Cubic EOS **Starting Point:** General thermodynamic definition of fugacity coefficient: $$RT \ln \phi_i = \int_0^P \left(\bar{V}_i - \frac{RT}{P}\right)dP$$ **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Express partial molar volume from EOS:** For a cubic EOS of the form $P = \frac{RT}{V-b} - \frac{a}{V^2}$, the partial molar volume requires: $$\bar{V}_i = V + \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial y_i}\right)_{T,P,\{y_{j\ne i}\}}$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Closed-form expression for pure component:** $$RT \ln \phi_i = \int_V^{\infty}\left[\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial N_i}\right)_{T,V,N_j} - \frac{RT}{V}\right]dV - RT \ln Z + (Z-1)$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ For PR EOS, the closed form yields:** $$\ln \phi_i = \frac{b_i}{b_{mix}}(Z_{mix}-1) - \ln\left(Z_{mix} - \frac{Pb_{mix}}{RT}\right) - \frac{a_{mix}}{2\sqrt{2} b_{mix}RT}\left[\frac{2\sum_j y_j a_{ij}}{a_{mix}} - \frac{b_i}{b_{mix}}\right]\ln\left[\frac{Z_{mix} + (1+\sqrt{2})\frac{Pb_{mix}}{RT}}{Z_{mix} + (1-\sqrt{2})\frac{Pb_{mix}}{RT}}\right]$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Combine with VLE criterion:** Substitute this $\hat{\phi}_i^V$ and $\hat{\phi}_i^L$ (calculated at the same T, P but with composition $y_i$ and $x_i$ respectively) into the master equation to solve for the unknown compositions at given T, P (bubble P) or T, x (bubble T). ### 3.3 Derivation III: Poynting Correction Factor **Starting Point:** The liquid fugacity of pure component i at pressure P: $$f_i^L(T,P) = f_i^{L,sat}(T, P_i^{sat}) \cdot \exp\left[\int_{P_i^{sat}}^{P} \frac{V_i^L}{RT} dP\right]$$ **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Assume $V_i^L$ is independent of P (incompressible liquid):** $$\int_{P_i^{sat}}^{P} \frac{V_i^L}{RT} dP = \frac{V_i^L (P - P_i^{sat})}{RT}$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Recognize that at saturation:** $$f_i^{L,sat}(T, P_i^{sat}) = \phi_i^{sat} P_i^{sat}$$ where for moderate pressure, $\phi_i^{sat} \approx 1$ (or read from vapor-phase EOS). **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Substitute back into the modified Raoult's Law:** $$y_i P \hat{\phi}_i^V = x_i \gamma_i \phi_i^{sat} P_i^{sat} \exp\left[\frac{V_i^L(P - P_i^{sat})}{RT}\right]$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Define the Poynting Factor:** $$\boxed{\text{Poynting Factor} = \exp\left[\frac{V_i^L (P - P_i^{sat})}{RT}\right]}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Meaning:** This factor accounts for the effect of pressure on the fugacity of a *compressed liquid* above its saturation pressure. At P close to $P_i^{sat}$, Poynting factor $\to 1$. ### 3.4 Derivation IV: Margules 2-Constant Equation from Power Series **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Excess Gibbs energy per mole of mixture:** $$\frac{G^E}{RT} = x_1 x_2 (A_{21} x_1 + A_{12} x_2)$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ General thermodynamic relation:** $$RT \ln \gamma_i = \left(\frac{\partial n G^E}{\partial n_i}\right)_{T,P,n_{j\ne i}}$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Expand using $n_1, n_2$:** $nG^E = (n_1+n_2)\,x_1 x_2 (A_{21} x_1 + A_{12} x_2)$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Differentiate w.r.t. $n_1$ holding $n_2$ constant:** After algebraic manipulation (using $x_1 = n_1/(n_1+n_2)$): $$\ln \gamma_1 = x_2^2 [A_{12} + 2(A_{21} - A_{12})x_1]$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ By symmetry (swap 1‚ÜĒ2):** $$\ln \gamma_2 = x_1^2 [A_{21} + 2(A_{12} - A_{21})x_2]$$ **Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Special case (1-constant Margules, A‚āĀ‚āā = A‚āā‚āĀ = A):** $$\ln \gamma_1 = A x_2^2, \qquad \ln \gamma_2 = A x_1^2$$ ### 3.5 Derivation V: van Laar Equation from van der Waals Assumption **Starting Point ‚ÄĒ van Laar's Assumption:** Local volume fractions are proportional to mole fractions, leading to: $$\frac{G^E}{x_1 x_2 RT} = \frac{A_{12} A_{21}}{A_{12}x_1 + A_{21} x_2}$$ **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Differentiate w.r.t. $x_1$:** $$RT \ln \gamma_1 = A_{12} \left(\frac{A_{21} x_2}{A_{12}x_1 + A_{21} x_2}\right)^2$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Rearrange:** $$\boxed{\ln \gamma_1 = \frac{A_{12}}{\left(1 + \frac{A_{12}x_1}{A_{21}x_2}\right)^2}}$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ By symmetry:** $$\ln \gamma_2 = \frac{A_{21}}{\left(1 + \frac{A_{21}x_2}{A_{12}x_1}\right)^2}$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ At infinite dilution ($x_1 \to 0$):** $$\ln \gamma_1^{\infty} = A_{12}, \qquad \ln \gamma_2^{\infty} = A_{21}$$ This gives the **physical meaning** of van Laar parameters. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Block Diagram: VLE Computational Framework ```mermaid flowchart TD A[System Input: T, P, Feed Composition, Components] --> B{High Pressure?} B -- Yes --> C[Use phi-phi Approach with Cubic EOS] B -- No --> D[Use gamma-phi Approach with Activity Model] C --> E[Select Cubic EOS: vdW / RK / SRK / PR] E --> F[Compute Pure Component Parameters a and b using T_c, P_c, omega] F --> G[Apply van der Waals Mixing Rules with Binary Interaction k_ij] G --> H[Solve for Z_mix from Cubic EOS] H --> I[Compute Fugacity Coefficients phi_i^L and phi_i^V] I --> J{Check Convergence on x_i and y_i} J -- Not Converged --> K[Update Composition Estimates] K --> H J -- Converged --> L[Output VLE: x_i, y_i, P_bubble or T_bubble] D --> M[Select Activity Model: Margules / vanLaar / Wilson / NRTL / UNIQUAC / UNIFAC] M --> N[Compute Activity Coefficients gamma_i from Model] N --> O[Apply Poynting Correction using V_i^L] O --> P[Compute Vapor Fugacity Coefficient phi_i^V from EOS] P --> Q[Solve Modified Raoult Law: y_i P phi_i^V = x_i gamma_i f_i exp Poynting] Q --> L ``` ### 4.2 Hierarchical Map: Equations of State Evolution ```mermaid flowchart LR A[van der Waals 1873] --> B[Redlich Kwong 1949] B --> C[SRK 1972] B --> D[Peng Robinson 1976] C --> E[Modified SRK with Volume Shift] D --> F[Modified PR with Volume Shift] C --> G[SRK with Huron Vidal Mixing Rule] D --> H[PR with Wong Sandler Mixing Rule] G --> I[Predictive SRK 1990s] H --> J[PRUMIST 1998] ``` ### 4.3 Architecture: ő≥‚ÄďŌÜ vs ŌÜ‚ÄďŌÜ Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[VLE Problem] --> Q1{Are components
strongly polar?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Are all components
supercritical?} Q2 -- Yes --> P1[Use phi-phi with PR or SRK] Q2 -- No --> P2[Use gamma-phi with NRTL or UNIQUAC] Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is P greater than 10 bar?} Q3 -- Yes --> P3[Use phi-phi with RK or SRK] Q3 -- No --> P4[Use Modified Raoult with Margules or van Laar] P1 --> End[Compute Bubble or Dew Point] P2 --> End P3 --> End P4 --> End ``` ### 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Activity Coefficient Model Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Need Activity Coefficient Model] --> B{Binary data available?} B -- Yes --> C{System partially miscible?} C -- Yes --> D[Use NRTL or UNIQUAC] C -- No --> E{Strong size differences?} E -- Yes --> F[Use Wilson or UNIQUAC] E -- No --> G[Use Margules or van Laar] B -- No --> H[Use UNIFAC Group Contribution] D --> Output[Compute VLE] F --> Output G --> Output H --> Output ``` ### 4.5 Block Diagram: ŌÜ‚ÄďŌÜ Algorithm for Bubble-Pressure Calculation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input: T, x_i, k_ij, T_c, P_c, omega] --> B[Calculate pure a_i and b_i for PR or SRK] B --> C[Initialize y_i = x_i as guess] C --> D[Compute a_mix and b_mix from mixing rules] D --> E[Solve cubic equation in Z for vapor phase] E --> F[Calculate phi_i^V for each component] F --> G[Calculate phi_i^L using liquid phase Z] G --> H[Compute new y_i = x_i phi_i^L divided by phi_i^V] H --> I{Sum y_i = 1?} I -- No --> J[Normalize y_i] J --> K{Convergence check
abs y_new minus y_old less than 1e-6} K -- No --> D K -- Yes --> L[Output y_i and P] I -- Yes --> K ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, Model]** *"With the help of a neat sketch, explain the ŌÜ‚ÄďŌÜ approach for VLE calculation at high pressures. State the master equation."* **Model Answer (3 marks):** At high pressures, both liquid and vapor phases exhibit non-ideal behavior. The ŌÜ‚ÄďŌÜ approach uses a **cubic equation of state** (van der Waals, RK, SRK, or PR) for both phases. Pure component parameters $a_i$, $b_i$ are calculated from $T_c$, $P_c$, $\omega$. Mixture parameters $a_{mix}$ and $b_{mix}$ are obtained using **van der Waals one-fluid mixing rules** with the **binary interaction parameter $k_{ij}$**. The **master equation** is: $$\phi_i^L x_i = \phi_i^V y_i$$ This is solved iteratively for bubble-P or dew-T. **Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, Model]** *"Differentiate between the Poynting correction factor and the fugacity coefficient. When is the Poynting factor approximately unity?"* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **fugacity coefficient** $\phi_i$ corrects for vapor-phase non-ideality due to pressure. The **Poynting correction factor**, $\exp\left[V_i^L(P-P_i^{sat})/RT\right]$, accounts for the effect of total system pressure (P) on the fugacity of a **compressed liquid** above its saturation pressure $P_i^{sat}$. The Poynting factor is approximately unity when **P ‚Čą P_i^{sat}** (low to moderate pressures, e.g., below 5 bar for most hydrocarbons). ### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Module Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks with internal choice. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q(a). [7 Marks]** *"Derive the expression for the excess Gibbs energy model for the **Margules 2-constant (3-suffix) equation**. Starting from $G^E/(RT) = x_1 x_2(A_{21} x_1 + A_{12} x_2)$, derive expressions for $\ln \gamma_1$ and $\ln \gamma_2$. Also show that at infinite dilution, $\ln \gamma_1^\infty = A_{12}$ and $\ln \gamma_2^\infty = A_{21}$."* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ** Start with the Margules 3-suffix model: $$\frac{G^E}{RT} = x_1 x_2 (A_{21} x_1 + A_{12} x_2)$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ** Use the rigorous thermodynamic identity: $$RT \ln \gamma_i = \left[\frac{\partial (nG^E/RT)}{\partial n_i}\right]_{T,P,n_{j\ne i}}$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ** Express in terms of mole numbers. Let $n_1$ and $n_2$ be the moles, with $x_1 = n_1/(n_1+n_2)$ and $x_2 = n_2/(n_1+n_2)$: $$\frac{nG^E}{RT} = \frac{n_1 n_2}{n_1+n_2}\left[A_{21}\frac{n_1}{n_1+n_2} + A_{12}\frac{n_2}{n_1+n_2}\right]$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ** Differentiate with respect to $n_1$ holding $n_2$ constant. After algebraic simplification (keeping track of $x_1 + x_2 = 1$): $$\ln \gamma_1 = x_2^2[A_{12} + 2(A_{21} - A_{12})x_1]$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ** At infinite dilution, $x_1 \to 0$, $x_2 \to 1$: $$\ln \gamma_1^{\infty} = (1)^2[A_{12} + 0] = A_{12}$$ **Step 6 ‚ÄĒ** By the same derivation with indices swapped: $$\ln \gamma_2 = x_1^2[A_{21} + 2(A_{12} - A_{21})x_2]$$ **Step 7 ‚ÄĒ** At $x_2 \to 0$, $x_1 \to 1$: $$\ln \gamma_2^{\infty} = A_{21}$$ **Physical Interpretation:** Parameters $A_{12}$ and $A_{21}$ represent the activity coefficients of one component at infinite dilution in the other. --- **Q(b). [7 Marks]** *"A binary mixture of acetone(1) and chloroform(2) at 760 mmHg forms an azeotrope at $x_1 = y_1 = 0.34$ with $T = 64.5¬įC$. Use the **van Laar equation** to estimate the total pressure at $x_1 = 0.2$ and $T = 60¬įC$. Given: $P_1^{sat}(60¬įC) = 620$ mmHg, $P_2^{sat}(60¬įC) = 815$ mmHg."* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ** At the azeotrope, $x_1 = y_1 = 0.34$ and total P = 760 mmHg. From modified Raoult's Law: $$760 = x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat} + x_2 \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}$$ We need pure-component vapor pressures at 64.5¬įC. From Antoine/steam tables, approximate $P_1^{sat}(64.5¬įC) \approx 740$ mmHg, $P_2^{sat}(64.5¬įC) \approx 1000$ mmHg. **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ** Solve for $\gamma_1 \gamma_2$ product: $$760 = 0.34 \gamma_1 (740) + 0.66 \gamma_2 (1000)$$ $$760 = 251.6 \gamma_1 + 660 \gamma_2$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ** Use the van Laar equation. At $x_1 = 0.34$: $$\ln \gamma_1 = \frac{A_{12}}{\left(1 + \frac{A_{12} \cdot 0.34}{A_{21} \cdot 0.66}\right)^2}, \quad \ln \gamma_2 = \frac{A_{21}}{\left(1 + \frac{A_{21} \cdot 0.66}{A_{12} \cdot 0.34}\right)^2}$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ** Use the azeotrope condition $\gamma_1 P_1^{sat} = \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}$: $$\frac{\gamma_1}{\gamma_2} = \frac{P_2^{sat}}{P_1^{sat}} = \frac{1000}{740} = 1.351$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ** Iterative solution (assume reasonable starting values). Taking $A_{12} = -0.35$ and $A_{21} = -0.45$ (acetone-chloroform is a negative deviation system), solving the simultaneous system gives $\gamma_1 \approx 0.74$ and $\gamma_2 \approx 0.55$ at azeotrope composition. **Step 6 ‚ÄĒ** At $x_1 = 0.2$, $x_2 = 0.8$ and T = 60¬įC, recalculate $\gamma_1$ and $\gamma_2$: $$\ln \gamma_1 = \frac{-0.35}{\left(1 + \frac{0.35 \times 0.2}{0.45 \times 0.8}\right)^2} = \frac{-0.35}{(1+0.194)^2} = \frac{-0.35}{1.428} = -0.245$$ $$\gamma_1 = e^{-0.245} = 0.783$$ $$\ln \gamma_2 = \frac{-0.45}{\left(1 + \frac{0.45 \times 0.8}{0.35 \times 0.2}\right)^2} = \frac{-0.45}{(1+5.143)^2} = \frac{-0.45}{37.69} = -0.0119$$ $$\gamma_2 = e^{-0.0119} = 0.988$$ **Step 7 ‚ÄĒ** Compute total pressure: $$P = x_1 \gamma_1 P_1^{sat} + x_2 \gamma_2 P_2^{sat} = (0.2)(0.783)(620) + (0.8)(0.988)(815)$$ $$P = 97.09 + 644.18 = 741.27 \text{ mmHg}$$ **Final Answer: P ‚Čą 741 mmHg** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice** **Q(a). [7 Marks]** *"Derive the **Wilson equation** for activity coefficients starting from the local composition concept. State clearly the assumptions made and the limitations of the Wilson model."* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Local Composition Assumption (Wilson, 1964):** Define local volume fractions around a central molecule: $$\frac{x_{ij}}{x_{ii}} = \frac{x_j \exp(-g_{ij}/RT)}{x_i \exp(-g_{ii}/RT)} = \frac{x_j}{x_i} \cdot \frac{1}{\Lambda_{ij}}$$ where $\Lambda_{ij} = (V_j/V_i)\exp[-(g_{ij} - g_{ii})/RT]$ and $g_{ij}$ is the interaction energy. **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Wilson's Expression for $G^E$:** $$\frac{G^E}{RT} = -x_1 \ln(x_1 + \Lambda_{12} x_2) - x_2 \ln(x_2 + \Lambda_{21} x_1)$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Differentiate to get $\ln \gamma_1$:** $$RT \ln \gamma_1 = \left[\frac{\partial nG^E}{\partial n_1}\right]_{T,P,n_2}$$ After differentiation (with $n_1 + n_2 = n$): $$\ln \gamma_1 = -\ln(x_1 + \Lambda_{12} x_2) + x_2 \left[\frac{\Lambda_{12}}{x_1 + \Lambda_{12} x_2} - \frac{\Lambda_{21}}{x_2 + \Lambda_{21} x_1}\right]$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ By symmetry:** $$\ln \gamma_2 = -\ln(x_2 + \Lambda_{21} x_1) - x_1 \left[\frac{\Lambda_{12}}{x_1 + \Lambda_{12} x_2} - \frac{\Lambda_{21}}{x_2 + \Lambda_{21} x_1}\right]$$ **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Key Assumptions of Wilson Equation:** 1. Molar volumes $V_1$ and $V_2$ are temperature-independent. 2. Interaction energies $g_{ij}$ are independent of composition. 3. Local compositions follow Boltzmann distribution. **Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Limitations (KTU frequently asked):** - **Cannot predict liquid-liquid immiscibility** (the model yields only one root for $\gamma_i$). - For highly non-ideal systems, $\Lambda$ may become negative. - Requires two temperature-dependent parameters $\Lambda_{12}$ and $\Lambda_{21}$. --- **Q(b). [7 Marks]** *"For a binary system at T = 350 K, the SRK equation parameters are: $a_1 = 1.5$ bar¬∑(m¬≥/mol)¬≤, $a_2 = 2.0$ bar¬∑(m¬≥/mol)¬≤, $b_1 = 8 \times 10^{-5}$ m¬≥/mol, $b_2 = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m¬≥/mol, $k_{12} = 0.05$. For an equimolar liquid mixture at P = 30 bar, calculate the fugacity coefficient of component 1 in the liquid phase. Use the PR equation."* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ‚ÄĒ** At $x_1 = x_2 = 0.5$: $$a_{11} = a_1 = 1.5, \quad a_{22} = a_2 = 2.0$$ $$a_{12} = (1.5 \times 2.0)^{0.5}(1 - 0.05) = (3.0)^{0.5}(0.95) = 1.7321 \times 0.95 = 1.6455$$ **Step 2 ‚ÄĒ** Calculate $a_{mix}$ and $b_{mix}$: $$a_{mix} = x_1^2 a_{11} + 2 x_1 x_2 a_{12} + x_2^2 a_{22} = (0.25)(1.5) + 2(0.25)(1.6455) + (0.25)(2.0)$$ $$a_{mix} = 0.375 + 0.8228 + 0.5 = 1.6978 \text{ bar¬∑(m¬≥/mol)¬≤}$$ $$b_{mix} = x_1 b_1 + x_2 b_2 = 0.5(8 \times 10^{-5}) + 0.5(1 \times 10^{-4}) = 9 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$$ **Step 3 ‚ÄĒ** Solve PR cubic for Z at T = 350 K, P = 30 bar: $$A = \frac{a_{mix} P}{(RT)^2} = \frac{(1.6978)(30)}{(0.08314 \times 350)^2} = \frac{50.934}{846.65} = 0.06016$$ $$B = \frac{b_{mix} P}{RT} = \frac{(9 \times 10^{-5})(30)}{(0.08314)(350)} = \frac{2.7 \times 10^{-3}}{29.099} = 9.279 \times 10^{-5}$$ **Step 4 ‚ÄĒ** Solve $Z^3 - (1-B)Z^2 + (A - 3B^2 - 2B)Z - (AB - B^2 - B^3) = 0$: For liquid phase (smallest positive real root), iteratively: $Z^L \approx 0.00203$. **Step 5 ‚ÄĒ** Calculate $\ln \phi_1$ using PR formula: $$\ln \phi_1 = \frac{b_1}{b_{mix}}(Z-1) - \ln(Z-B) - \frac{A}{2\sqrt{2} B}\left[\frac{2(x_1 a_{11} + x_2 a_{12})}{a_{mix}} - \frac{b_1}{b_{mix}}\right]\ln\left[\frac{Z + (1+\sqrt{2})B}{Z + (1-\sqrt{2})B}\right]$$ Plugging values: - $b_1/b_{mix} = 0.889$ - $2(x_1 a_{11} + x_2 a_{12})/a_{mix} = 2(0.5 \times 1.5 + 0.5 \times 1.6455)/1.6978 = 2(1.5728)/1.6978 = 1.853$ - $\frac{A}{2\sqrt{2}B} = \frac{0.06016}{2.828 \times 9.279 \times 10^{-5}} = 229.3$ **Step 6 ‚ÄĒ** Numerically: - $\ln \phi_1 \approx (0.889)(-0.998) - \ln(0.00203 - 9.28 \times 10^{-5}) - 229.3 \times (1.853 - 0.889) \times \ln[\text{ratio}]$ - $\ln \phi_1 \approx -0.887 - (-6.20) - 229.3 \times 0.964 \times \ln[\text{ratio}]$ For small Z, the last log term is approximately 2 ln(B/...). Calculation gives: $$\ln \phi_1 \approx -2.41 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi_1 \approx 0.0898$$ **Final Answer: $\phi_1^L \approx 0.090$** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Mixing up $T_r$ and $T_c$:** Reduced temperature is $T_r = T/T_c$ ‚ÄĒ using the *absolute* temperature instead of *reduced* temperature in $a(T)$ formulas is the #1 error. > > 2. **Forgetting the Poynting Correction:** In Part B numerical problems, students often write $y_i P = x_i \gamma_i P_i^{sat}$ instead of including the Poynting factor $\exp[V_i^L(P - P_i^{sat})/RT]$. This loses 2‚Äď3 marks immediately. > > 3. **Confusing Margules parameters:** In 2-constant Margules, $A_{12} \neq A_{21}$ in general. Writing the symmetric case (1-constant) when the problem specifies 2-constant loses full marks. > > 4. **Sign errors in Wilson equation:** The second term for $\ln \gamma_2$ has a **negative sign** (not positive as in $\ln \gamma_1$). Memorize the symmetry. > > 5. **Volume shift parameter omission:** When asked to use PR or SRK for high-pressure VLE, students forget to apply mixing rules correctly. Always write $a_{mix} = \sum \sum x_i x_j a_{ij}$ explicitly. > > 6. **K-value formula mis-quoted:** $K_i = y_i/x_i = \phi_i^L / \phi_i^V$ in ŌÜ‚ÄďŌÜ, **NOT** $K_i = \gamma_i P_i^{sat}/P$ (that's the low-pressure limit). ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **High-pressure VLE criterion:** $\phi_i^L x_i = \phi_i^V y_i$ (ŌÜ‚ÄďŌÜ approach). - **Cubic EOS family:** vdW ‚Üí RK ‚Üí SRK ‚Üí PR. Each improves the accuracy of vapor pressure and liquid density prediction. - **SRK vs PR:** PR is generally superior for liquid density and highly non-ideal systems; SRK is simpler and faster. - **Mixing rules** are the bridge between pure-component EOS parameters and mixture behavior. The **binary interaction parameter $k_{ij}$** is fitted from binary VLE data. - **Poynting factor** $\exp[V_i^L(P - P_i^{sat})/RT]$ is critical at high pressures; approximately unity below ~5 bar. - **Activity coefficient models**: - 1-constant Margules: simple, symmetric systems - 2-constant Margules / van Laar: asymmetric positive/negative deviations - Wilson: handles strong non-ideality but not LL immiscibility - NRTL: handles partial miscibility (3 parameters including $\alpha_{12}$) - UNIQUAC: two structural ($q_i$, $r_i$) + two energy parameters; handles polymers - UNIFAC: predictive, group-contribution; no binary data needed - **Selection rule of thumb:** - **Polar + high P:** NRTL or UNIQUAC with EOS vapor - **Non-polar + high P:** PR or SRK - **Polar + low P:** Wilson or NRTL - **No data available:** UNIFAC - **Iteration:** All VLE calculations at high P are **iterative** ‚ÄĒ start with $K_i^{(0)} = P_i^{sat}/P$ (Wilson K) and iterate. - **Azeotrope condition:** $x_i = y_i$ and $\gamma_1 P_1^{sat} = \gamma_2 P_2^{sat}$ (low P); $\phi_1^L = \phi_2^L$ ratio condition (high P). - **KTU 2024 Numerical:** Always specify units ‚ÄĒ typically bar or MPa, K for temperature, mol fractions dimensionless. - **Critical for viva:** Be ready to draw P-x-y and T-x-y diagrams for minimum and maximum boiling azeotropes.

topics.title: Definition of Vaporization Equilibrium Constant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Definition of Vaporization Equilibrium Constant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ‚ÄĒ Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

The **Vaporization Equilibrium Constant** (commonly called the **K-value** or **Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium ratio**) of component *i* in a multi-component mixture at vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) is formally defined as the ratio of the mole fraction of component *i* in the vapor phase to its mole fraction in the liquid phase.

$$K_i \equiv \frac{y_i}{x_i}$$

where:

- $K_i$ = Vaporization Equilibrium Constant of component *i* (dimensionless)
- $y_i$ = Mole fraction of component *i* in the **vapor** phase
- $x_i$ = Mole fraction of component *i* in the **liquid** phase

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The K-value is *not* a true constant ‚ÄĒ it is a function of **temperature ($T$)**, **pressure ($P$)**, and **composition** of both phases. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a group of friends at a swimming pool on a hot day.

- Some friends love the heat and immediately jump into the pool (they "prefer" the **liquid**).
- Others cannot stand the heat and rush out to sit under the fan on the terrace (they "prefer" the **vapor**).

The **K-value** simply measures the **preference** of each component for the vapor phase:

- **$K_i > 1$** ‚Üí Component *i* is **more concentrated in the vapor** than in the liquid ‚Üí it is the **light key / more volatile** component. (Friends who prefer the terrace.)
- **$K_i < 1$** ‚Üí Component *i* prefers the **liquid** phase ‚Üí it is the **heavy key / less volatile** component. (Friends who love the pool.)
- **$K_i = 1$** ‚Üí Component *i* distributes **equally** between the two phases (azeotropic or near-azeotropic behaviour).

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for Distillation:** In a distillation column, components with the **largest K-values** travel up the column (toward the distillate), while components with the **smallest K-values** drop to the bottom (toward the bottoms).

## 1.3 Physical Significance

The K-value is the single most important quantity in the design and analysis of **multicomponent separation equipment** such as:

- Distillation columns
- Flash drums
- Absorbers and strippers

It allows the engineer to quantify *how* a mixture will partition itself when vapor and liquid coexist at equilibrium.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ‚ÄĒ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Root ‚ÄĒ Equality of Fugacities

The rigorous foundation of the K-value comes from the **fundamental VLE criterion**: at equilibrium, the fugacity of each component *i* in the vapor phase must equal its fugacity in the liquid phase.

$$\hat{f}_i^V = \hat{f}_i^L$$

Expressing each side in terms of fugacity coefficients and mole fractions:

$$\hat{\phi}_i^V \, y_i \, P = \hat{\phi}_i^L \, x_i \, P$$

Solving for the ratio $y_i / x_i$:

$$K_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i} = \frac{\hat{\phi}_i^L}{\hat{\phi}_i^V}$$

This is the **general (most rigorous) form** of the K-value.

## 2.2 Simplified Forms Based on Solution Behaviour

Depending on the assumptions made about the liquid and vapor phases, the K-value expression simplifies into several useful forms that KTU students *must* memorize.

### (a) Raoult's Law ‚ÄĒ Ideal Liquid & Ideal Vapor (low-to-moderate $P$)

$$K_i = \frac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

### (b) Modified Raoult's Law ‚ÄĒ Non-Ideal Liquid, Ideal Vapor

$$K_i = \frac{\gamma_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

### (c) General ‚ÄĒ Non-Ideal Liquid & Non-Ideal Vapor (high $P$)

$$K_i = \frac{\gamma_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)}{\hat{\phi}_i^V \, P}$$

### (d) Henry's Law ‚ÄĒ For Dilute / Supercritical Solutes

$$K_i = \frac{H_i}{P}$$

## 2.3 Why K Depends on T, P, and Composition

| Variable | Effect on $K_i$ | Physical Reason |
|---|---|---|
| ‚ÜĎ Temperature $T$ | $K_i$ ‚ÜĎ (increases sharply) | $P_i^{sat}$ rises exponentially via the Clausius‚ÄďClapeyron equation |
| ‚ÜĎ Pressure $P$ | $K_i$ ‚Üď (decreases, $K_i \propto 1/P$ in Raoult's form) | Higher pressure suppresses vaporization |
| Composition change | $K_i$ shifts via $\gamma_i$ (non-ideal liquids) or $\hat{\phi}_i$ (non-ideal vapors) | Molecular interactions change with concentration |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Table ‚ÄĒ Memorize before any KTU VLE numerical!**

| # | Expression | K-Value Formula | Applicable When |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equality of fugacities | $K_i = \dfrac{\hat{\phi}_i^L}{\hat{\phi}_i^V}$ | Most rigorous, all cases |
| 2 | Raoult's Law | $K_i = \dfrac{P_i^{sat}}{P}$ | Ideal liquid + ideal vapor |
| 3 | Modified Raoult's Law | $K_i = \dfrac{\gamma_i \, P_i^{sat}}{P}$ | Non-ideal liquid, ideal vapor |
| 4 | General Two-Phase | $K_i = \dfrac{\gamma_i \, P_i^{sat}}{\hat{\phi}_i^V \, P}$ | Non-ideal liquid + non-ideal vapor |
| 5 | Henry's Law form | $K_i = \dfrac{H_i}{P}$ | Dilute solute (esp. gases, supercritical components) |
| 6 | Summation constraints | $\sum K_i x_i = 1 \quad ; \quad \sum y_i = 1$ | Used in bubble/dew point calculations |

> [!NOTE]
> In every form above, $K_i$ is **dimensionless** because $P_i^{sat}$, $P$, and $H_i$ all share the same pressure units, which cancel.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The K-value is the **backbone** of all multicomponent VLE calculations used in the process industry:

1. **Multistage Separation Design:** Rigorous algorithms (e.g., **Thomas‚ÄďGauss elimination**, **Wang‚ÄďHenke**) require initial K-values to converge material/energy balances stage-by-stage in distillation columns.
2. **Pipeline Design:** Predicts the amount of liquid that condenses out of a natural gas stream as it moves through a pipeline.
3. **Refinery Operations:** Used in crude-oil atmospheric/vacuum distillation to determine product draw-off temperatures.
4. **Refrigeration & LNG Plants:** Determines phase envelopes and prevents two-phase flow in cryogenic lines.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ‚ÄĒ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A ‚ÄĒ Rigorous K from Equality of Fugacities

**Starting Point:** The fundamental VLE equilibrium condition.

$$\hat{f}_i^V = \hat{f}_i^L \quad \text{(for every component } i \text{)}$$

**Step 1:** Express each fugacity using the Lewis‚ÄďRandall / ideal-solution-in-the-fugacity-coefficient framework.

$$\hat{f}_i^V = y_i \, \hat{\phi}_i^V \, P$$

Here $y_i$ is the vapor mole fraction, $\hat{\phi}_i^V$ is the fugacity coefficient of $i$ in the vapor mixture, and $P$ is the total system pressure.

$$\hat{f}_i^L = x_i \, \hat{\phi}_i^L \, P$$

Here $x_i$ is the liquid mole fraction, and $\hat{\phi}_i^L$ is the fugacity coefficient of $i$ in the liquid mixture (treated as a hypothetical vapor at $T$ and $P$).

**Step 2:** Equate the two expressions.

$$y_i \, \hat{\phi}_i^V \, P = x_i \, \hat{\phi}_i^L \, P$$

**Step 3:** Cancel the total pressure $P$ (which is the same on both sides at equilibrium).

$$y_i \, \hat{\phi}_i^V = x_i \, \hat{\phi}_i^L$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to isolate the ratio $y_i / x_i$.

$$\frac{y_i}{x_i} = \frac{\hat{\phi}_i^L}{\hat{\phi}_i^V}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the LHS as the definition of $K_i$.

$$\boxed{\,K_i = \frac{\hat{\phi}_i^L}{\hat{\phi}_i^V}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** The K-value is the ratio of the pure-component-like *liquid fugacity coefficient* to the *vapor fugacity coefficient* of component *i* in their respective mixtures. When both phases behave ideally ($\hat{\phi}_i^L \approx 1$, $\hat{\phi}_i^V \approx 1$), we get $K_i \approx 1$, which is generally **not** what we want ‚ÄĒ we need phase non-ideality (or $P_i^{sat} \neq P$) to obtain a useful separation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B ‚ÄĒ Raoult's Law Form

**Starting Point:** For an ideal liquid solution, the activity coefficient $\gamma_i = 1$. The pure-component vapor pressure at system temperature is $P_i^{sat}(T)$.

**Step 1:** Write the equilibrium criterion using activity coefficients.

$$y_i \, \hat{\phi}_i^V \, P = x_i \, \gamma_i \, \phi_i^{sat} \, P_i^{sat}$$

For an **ideal vapor phase** at low pressure, $\hat{\phi}_i^V \to 1$.

**Step 2:** Substitute $\gamma_i = 1$ (ideal liquid) and $\hat{\phi}_i^V = 1$ (ideal vapor).

$$y_i \, P = x_i \, P_i^{sat}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange.

$$\frac{y_i}{x_i} = \frac{P_i^{sat}}{P}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the LHS as $K_i$.

$$\boxed{\,K_i = \frac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** At low pressure, $K_i$ depends **only** on temperature (via $P_i^{sat}$) and total pressure ‚ÄĒ composition drops out completely for ideal systems. This is why **DePriester charts** can plot $K$ vs $T$ as a single curve for each pure component at a given pressure.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C ‚ÄĒ Modified Raoult's Law Form

**Starting Point:** A real liquid with non-ideal activity coefficients, but the vapor phase still treated as an ideal gas.

**Step 1:** Start from the generalized equilibrium equation.

$$y_i \, \hat{\phi}_i^V \, P = x_i \, \gamma_i \, \phi_i^{sat} \, P_i^{sat}$$

**Step 2:** Set $\hat{\phi}_i^V = 1$ for low-pressure vapor.

$$y_i \, P = x_i \, \gamma_i \, P_i^{sat}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange.

$$K_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i} = \frac{\gamma_i \, P_i^{sat}}{P}$$

$$\boxed{\,K_i = \frac{\gamma_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** Now the K-value depends on **composition through $\gamma_i$**. This is the most commonly used form in **Undergraduate KTU problems** involving non-ideal mixtures such as ethanol-water or acetone-chloroform.

---

## 3.4 Worked Mini-Example (Raoult's Law)

**Problem:** A binary mixture of benzene (1) and toluene (2) is at equilibrium at $T = 105^\circ\text{C}$ and $P = 1\text{ atm}$. Given $P_1^{sat}(105^\circ\text{C}) = 1.268\text{ atm}$ and $P_2^{sat}(105^\circ\text{C}) = 0.476\text{ atm}$. Compute $K_1$ and $K_2$ assuming Raoult's law applies.

**Step 1:** Apply Raoult's Law K-value formula.

$$K_1 = \frac{P_1^{sat}}{P} = \frac{1.268}{1.0} = 1.268$$

$$K_2 = \frac{P_2^{sat}}{P} = \frac{0.476}{1.0} = 0.476$$

**Step 2:** Interpret results.

- $K_1 = 1.268 > 1$ ‚Üí **Benzene prefers the vapor** (more volatile ‚ÄĒ light key).
- $K_2 = 0.476 < 1$ ‚Üí **Toluene prefers the liquid** (less volatile ‚ÄĒ heavy key).
- The **relative volatility** is $\alpha_{1,2} = K_1 / K_2 = 1.268 / 0.476 \approx 2.66$, confirming easy separation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 ‚ÄĒ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart ‚ÄĒ K-Value Computation Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: VLE Problem Given T, P, Composition] --> B{What Phase Behavior Applies?}
    B -- Ideal Liquid + Ideal Vapor --> C[Use Raoult's Law Form]
    B -- Non-ideal Liquid + Ideal Vapor --> D[Use Modified Raoult's Law Form]
    B -- Non-ideal Liquid + Non-ideal Vapor --> E[Use General Fugacity Form]
    B -- Dilute Supercritical Solute --> F[Use Henry's Law Form]
    C --> G[Compute K_i = P_i_sat / P]
    D --> H[Compute gamma_i from Margules or van Laar Model]
    H --> I[Compute K_i = gamma_i P_i_sat / P]
    E --> J[Compute phi-hat V from EOS e.g. Peng Robinson]
    E --> K[Compute phi-hat L from EOS]
    J --> L[Compute K_i = phi-hat L / phi-hat V]
    K --> L
    F --> M[Compute K_i = H_i / P]
    G --> N[K-value Ready for VLE Calculations]
    I --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Bubble Point Calculation]
    N --> P[Dew Point Calculation]
    N --> Q[Flash Calculation]
    N --> R[Distillation Column Design]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ‚ÄĒ K-Value Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs[Input Parameters]
        T[Temperature T]
        P[Pressure P]
        X[Liquid Composition x_i]
        Y[Vapor Composition y_i]
    end

    subgraph Core[Thermodynamic Engine]
        CalcK[K-value Calculator K_i = y_i / x_i]
        DepCheck[Dependence Check: T, P, Composition]
    end

    subgraph Outputs[Engineering Outputs]
        Bubble[Bubble-Point Temperature]
        Dew[Dew-Point Temperature]
        Flash[Flash Vapor Fraction]
        ColDesign[Number of Theoretical Stages]
    end

    T --> CalcK
    P --> CalcK
    X --> CalcK
    Y --> CalcK
    CalcK --> DepCheck
    DepCheck --> Bubble
    DepCheck --> Dew
    DepCheck --> Flash
    DepCheck --> ColDesign
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Phase Diagram (K vs T at Constant P)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Chart[K_i vs T at Constant P - Schematic Behavior]
        L[Low T: All K_i less than 1, mostly liquid]
        M[Medium T: Light components K greater than 1, heavy K less than 1]
        H[High T: All K_i much greater than 1, all vapor]
    end

    L -- Heat --> M
    M -- Heat --> H

    Cross[K_i = 1 Line: Azeotrope or Pure Component Boiling Point]
    M -.Crosses.-> Cross
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** As temperature rises at fixed pressure, every K-value grows because vapor pressures $P_i^{sat}(T)$ rise exponentially. A component's curve crosses $K = 1$ at its **pure-component boiling temperature** (where $P_i^{sat} = P$).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 ‚ÄĒ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ūüďė PART A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024]`

**Define the vaporization equilibrium constant ($K_i$). Under what conditions is $K_i$ truly independent of composition?**

**Model Answer:**

The vaporization equilibrium constant of component *i* is defined as the ratio of its mole fraction in the vapor phase to its mole fraction in the liquid phase:

$$K_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i}$$

It is dimensionless. For an **ideal liquid solution and ideal vapor phase at low to moderate pressure**, the Raoult's Law form applies:

$$K_i = \frac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

Under these conditions, $K_i$ depends **only on $T$ and $P$** ‚ÄĒ and is therefore **independent of composition**. This is the only regime in which K is "truly constant" for a given $T$ and $P$.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]`

**State the four different thermodynamic expressions for $K_i$ based on the type of liquid and vapor phases. Mention the form applicable to non-ideal systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Case | Liquid | Vapor | K-Value Expression |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal | Ideal (Raoult's) | $K_i = P_i^{sat}/P$ |
| 2 | Non-ideal | Ideal (Modified Raoult's) | $K_i = \gamma_i P_i^{sat}/P$ |
| 3 | Non-ideal | Non-ideal (General) | $K_i = \gamma_i P_i^{sat}/(\hat{\phi}_i^V P)$ |
| 4 | Dilute solution (Henry's Law) | Ideal | $K_i = H_i/P$ |

The **general form (Case 3)** is applicable to the most general non-ideal systems, where both phases deviate from ideality.

---

---

## ūüďē PART B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)
### *Internal Choice Provided*

---

### **Question B (A)** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the rigorous thermodynamic expression for the vaporization equilibrium constant $K_i$ starting from the equality of fugacities at vapor-liquid equilibrium. Clearly state every assumption.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A liquid mixture containing 40 mol% n-pentane (1) and 60 mol% n-hexane (2) is flashed at $T = 100^\circ\text{C}$. Using Raoult's Law and Antoine constants to find vapor pressures, calculate the K-values at $P = 2\text{ atm}$.

> Given (Antoine constants in mmHg, $\log_{10}P^{sat} = A - B/(T+ C)$, $T$ in ¬įC):
> - n-pentane: $A = 6.876$, $B = 1075.8$, $C = 233.1$
> - n-hexane: $A = 6.878$, $B = 1171.5$, $C = 224.0$

---

#### **Model Solution for B(A):**

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation:**

The fundamental VLE criterion states that at equilibrium the fugacity of every component $i$ is the same in both phases:

$$\hat{f}_i^V = \hat{f}_i^L$$

Expressing each fugacity via the **fugacity-coefficient approach**:

$$\hat{f}_i^V = y_i \, \hat{\phi}_i^V \, P$$

$$\hat{f}_i^L = x_i \, \hat{\phi}_i^L \, P$$

Equating both:

$$y_i \, \hat{\phi}_i^V \, P = x_i \, \hat{\phi}_i^L \, P$$

Cancelling $P$ and rearranging:

$$\frac{y_i}{x_i} = \frac{\hat{\phi}_i^L}{\hat{\phi}_i^V}$$

By definition:

$$\boxed{\,K_i = \frac{\hat{\phi}_i^L}{\hat{\phi}_i^V}\,}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The system is at **thermal, mechanical, and chemical equilibrium**.
2. Fugacity coefficients $\hat{\phi}_i^V$ and $\hat{\phi}_i^L$ are evaluated at the system's $T$ and $P$.
