3. The **Lewis‚ÄďRandall rule** is applicable ‚ÄĒ fugacity of $i$ in mixture ‚Čą mole fraction √ó pure-component fugacity.
4. No chemical reactions occur in either phase.

---

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1:** Compute vapor pressures using the Antoine equation at $T = 100^\circ\text{C}$.

For n-pentane:

$$\log_{10} P_1^{sat} = 6.876 - \frac{1075.8}{100 + 233.1} = 6.876 - \frac{1075.8}{333.1}$$

$$\log_{10} P_1^{sat} = 6.876 - 3.230 = 3.646$$

$$P_1^{sat} = 10^{3.646} = 4426\text{ mmHg} = 5.824\text{ atm}$$

For n-hexane:

$$\log_{10} P_2^{sat} = 6.878 - \frac{1171.5}{100 + 224.0} = 6.878 - \frac{1171.5}{324.0}$$

$$\log_{10} P_2^{sat} = 6.878 - 3.616 = 3.262$$

$$P_2^{sat} = 10^{3.262} = 1828\text{ mmHg} = 2.405\text{ atm}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Raoult's Law K-value expression.

$$K_1 = \frac{P_1^{sat}}{P} = \frac{5.824}{2.0} = 2.912$$

$$K_2 = \frac{P_2^{sat}}{P} = \frac{2.405}{2.0} = 1.203$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation.

- $K_1 = 2.912 > 1$ ‚Üí n-pentane prefers the vapor (light key).
- $K_2 = 1.203 > 1$ ‚Üí n-hexane also slightly prefers the vapor at this elevated temperature, but much less so.
- Relative volatility: $\alpha_{1,2} = K_1/K_2 = 2.912/1.203 = 2.42$ ‚Üí feasible separation.

---

### **Question B (B)** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the generalized VLE equation, derive the four limiting forms of the K-value, namely: (i) Raoult's Law, (ii) Modified Raoult's Law, (iii) General two-phase non-ideal, and (iv) Henry's Law form. For each, state clearly the underlying assumption.

**(b) [7 Marks]** At $T = 50^\circ\text{C}$ and $P = 1\text{ atm}$, an equimolar liquid mixture of acetone (1) and chloroform (2) exhibits strong negative deviations from Raoult's law. The Margules equation gives $\ln \gamma_1 = x_2^2 (A + 2(B-A)x_1)$ and $\ln \gamma_2 = x_1^2 (B + 2(A-B)x_2)$, with $A = 0.84$ and $B = -0.32$. Compute $\gamma_1$, $\gamma_2$, and the modified K-values, given $P_1^{sat}(50^\circ\text{C}) = 0.613\text{ atm}$ and $P_2^{sat}(50^\circ\text{C}) = 0.526\text{ atm}$.

---

#### **Model Solution for B(B):**

**Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation of Four Forms:**

The general VLE equation in terms of the **gamma‚Äďphi formulation** is:

$$y_i \, \hat{\phi}_i^V \, P = x_i \, \gamma_i \, \phi_i^{sat} \, P_i^{sat}$$

Solving for $K_i = y_i/x_i$:

$$K_i = \frac{\gamma_i \, \phi_i^{sat} \, P_i^{sat}}{\hat{\phi}_i^V \, P}$$

**Form (i): Raoult's Law** ‚ÄĒ Assumptions: $\gamma_i = 1$ (ideal liquid) and $\hat{\phi}_i^V = \phi_i^{sat} = 1$ (ideal vapor at low $P$):

$$K_i = \frac{P_i^{sat}}{P}$$

**Form (ii): Modified Raoult's Law** ‚ÄĒ Assumptions: $\gamma_i \neq 1$ but $\hat{\phi}_i^V = 1$:

$$K_i = \frac{\gamma_i \, P_i^{sat}}{P}$$

**Form (iii): General Form** ‚ÄĒ No idealizing assumptions; both $\gamma_i$ and $\hat{\phi}_i^V$ retained:

$$K_i = \frac{\gamma_i \, \phi_i^{sat} \, P_i^{sat}}{\hat{\phi}_i^V \, P}$$

When the Poynting correction is small ($\phi_i^{sat} \approx 1$):

$$K_i = \frac{\gamma_i \, P_i^{sat}}{\hat{\phi}_i^V \, P}$$

**Form (iv): Henry's Law Form** ‚ÄĒ For a dilute solute *i* in a non-volatile solvent, Raoult's Law fails and we use Henry's constant $H_i$:

$$K_i = \frac{H_i}{P}$$

---

**Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1:** For equimolar mixture, $x_1 = x_2 = 0.5$.

Compute $\ln \gamma_1$:

$$\ln \gamma_1 = x_2^2 \,[A + 2(B - A)x_1]$$

$$\ln \gamma_1 = (0.5)^2 \,[0.84 + 2(-0.32 - 0.84)(0.5)]$$

$$\ln \gamma_1 = 0.25 \,[0.84 + 2(-1.16)(0.5)]$$

$$\ln \gamma_1 = 0.25 \,[0.84 - 1.16] = 0.25 \times (-0.32) = -0.08$$

$$\gamma_1 = e^{-0.08} = 0.923$$

Compute $\ln \gamma_2$:

$$\ln \gamma_2 = x_1^2 \,[B + 2(A - B)x_2]$$

$$\ln \gamma_2 = (0.5)^2 \,[-0.32 + 2(0.84 - (-0.32))(0.5)]$$

$$\ln \gamma_2 = 0.25 \,[-0.32 + 2(1.16)(0.5)]$$

$$\ln \gamma_2 = 0.25 \,[-0.32 + 1.16] = 0.25 \times 0.84 = 0.21$$

$$\gamma_2 = e^{0.21} = 1.234$$

**Step 2:** Compute modified K-values.

$$K_1 = \frac{\gamma_1 \, P_1^{sat}}{P} = \frac{0.923 \times 0.613}{1.0} = 0.566$$

$$K_2 = \frac{\gamma_2 \, P_2^{sat}}{P} = \frac{1.234 \times 0.526}{1.0} = 0.649$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation.

- Both $K < 1$: at this temperature and pressure, neither component strongly prefers the vapor (system is below its azeotropic composition behaviour).
- The negative deviations from ideality ($\gamma_1 < 1$, $\gamma_2 > 1$ shows the asymmetry of the acetone‚Äďchloroform system) shift the K-values relative to the ideal-Raoult case.
- A check using Raoult's Law would have given $K_1 = 0.613$ and $K_2 = 0.526$; the modified values are noticeably different, showing why $\gamma_i$ matters.

---

## ‚ö†ÔłŹ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Ways Students Lose Marks on K-Value Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Units in Vapor Pressure:** Antoine equations often yield $P^{sat}$ in mmHg. **Convert to atm or bar consistently** before computing $K_i = P^{sat}/P$. Examiner *will* deduct for unit mismatch.
>
> 2. **Confusing $\hat{\phi}_i^L$ with $\phi_i^{sat}$:** The liquid fugacity coefficient $\hat{\phi}_i^L$ is for the liquid **mixture**; $\phi_i^{sat}$ is for the **pure saturated vapor**. They are not interchangeable.
>
> 3. **Assuming K is Composition-Independent:** A student who writes "K is constant for a given T and P" *without* qualifying "for ideal solutions only" will lose marks. KTU expects you to state the assumption.
>
> 4. **Sign of Activity Coefficient Errors:** Forgetting the negative sign in $\ln \gamma_i$ expressions is the most common computational mistake in modified Raoult's law problems.
>
> 5. **Poynting Correction Omission:** For high-pressure problems, neglecting the Poynting correction (i.e., assuming $\phi_i^{sat} = 1$) loses 1‚Äď2 marks.
>
> 6. **No Final Statement of Dimensionless Nature:** Always end your K-value calculation by stating **"$K_i$ is dimensionless"** ‚ÄĒ examiners love that one-line confirmation.

---

## ūüďĆ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ‚úÖ **Definition:** $K_i = y_i / x_i$ ‚ÄĒ ratio of vapor-phase mole fraction to liquid-phase mole fraction of component *i*.
- ‚úÖ **Rigorous form:** $K_i = \hat{\phi}_i^L / \hat{\phi}_i^V$ from equality of fugacities at equilibrium.
- ‚úÖ **Raoult's Law form:** $K_i = P_i^{sat}(T) / P$ ‚ÄĒ ideal liquid + ideal vapor.
- ‚úÖ **Modified Raoult's form:** $K_i = \gamma_i P_i^{sat} / P$ ‚ÄĒ non-ideal liquid, ideal vapor.
- ‚úÖ **General form:** $K_i = \gamma_i P_i^{sat} / (\hat{\phi}_i^V P)$ ‚ÄĒ both phases non-ideal.
- ‚úÖ **Henry's Law form:** $K_i = H_i / P$ ‚ÄĒ for dilute solutes and supercritical components.
- ‚úÖ **K is dimensionless** in all forms.
- ‚úÖ **K depends on T, P, and composition** ‚ÄĒ only composition-independent for ideal solutions.
- ‚úÖ **$K_i > 1$** ‚Üí component prefers vapor (more volatile); **$K_i < 1$** ‚Üí prefers liquid (less volatile).
- ‚úÖ **$K_i = 1$** ‚Üí azeotropic condition or pure-component boiling point.
- ‚úÖ **Two constraints always:** $\sum x_i = 1$ and $\sum y_i = 1$, leading to **bubble-point** ($\sum K_i x_i = 1$) and **dew-point** ($\sum y_i / K_i = 1$) equations.
- ‚úÖ **Relative volatility:** $\alpha_{ij} = K_i / K_j$ ‚ÄĒ the master metric for ease of separation.
- ‚úÖ **KTU-favorite systems:** benzene‚Äďtoluene (Raoult's); ethanol‚Äďwater (Modified Raoult's); acetone‚Äďchloroform (negative deviation); CO‚āā‚Äďwater (Henry's Law).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bubble Point, Dew Point, and Flash Calculations in Multicomponent Systems (Derivations Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bubble Point, Dew Point, and Flash Calculations in Multicomponent Systems (Derivations Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Fundamental Concept: Multicomponent Vapor‚ÄďLiquid Equilibrium (VLE)

**Multicomponent VLE** is the thermodynamic state in which a liquid mixture containing *two or more* chemical species coexists in true thermodynamic equilibrium with its vapor phase. For every component *i* in such a system, the **partial fugacity** (escaping tendency) of component *i* in the liquid phase must exactly equal its partial fugacity in the vapor phase:

$$ f_i^V = f_i^L $$

This single condition, when expanded using the appropriate fugacity-coefficient and activity-coefficient models, is the *genesis* of every bubble-point, dew-point, and flash calculation encountered in chemical engineering thermodynamics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** All derivations in this module begin from the **Modified Raoult's Law** (ő¶‚Äďő¶ or ő≥‚Äďő¶ formulation) for non-ideal systems. For Module 4 derivations, we use the **Modified Raoult's Law**:
>
> $$ \phi_i \, y_i \, P = \gamma_i \, x_i \, f_i^{oL} $$
>
> where **$f_i^{oL}$** is the standard-state liquid fugacity. For **ideal liquid and vapor** behavior (the KTU 2024 Module 4 simplification), it reduces to Raoult's Law: $y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}$.

## 1.2 Defining the Three Key Thermodynamic States

### ūüĒĻ Bubble Point (BP)
The **bubble point** of a liquid mixture is the *temperature* (at a given pressure) or *pressure* (at a given temperature) at which the **first infinitesimally small bubble of vapor** forms. Mathematically, it is the state at which the summation of vapor mole fractions first satisfies the constraint $\sum y_i = 1$ when an incipient vapor phase appears in contact with the liquid.

### ūüĒĻ Dew Point (DP)
The **dew point** of a vapor mixture is the *temperature* (at a given pressure) or *pressure* (at a given temperature) at which the **first drop of liquid** condenses. It is the state at which $\sum x_i = 1$ when an incipient liquid phase appears in contact with the vapor.

### ūüĒĻ Flash (Isothermal or Adiabatic Equilibrium)
A **flash** is a single-equilibrium-stage process in which a feed stream of overall composition $z_i$ partially vaporizes into a vapor product of composition $y_i$ and a liquid product of composition $x_i$, with a specified **vapor fraction** $\psi = V/F$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ‚ÄĒ The Distillation Column:**
> Imagine a laboratory fractional distillation of a mixture of **water, ethanol, and acetone**. As the kettle (reboiler) is heated, the first bubble you see corresponds to the **bubble point** of the liquid. As vapors rise and partially condense on the column walls, the very first drop of reflux corresponds to the **dew point** of the rising vapor. Inside a single tray, the liquid descending and vapor rising reach a partial equilibrium ‚ÄĒ this is precisely a **flash** stage. The material balance equations of these three states are the mathematical backbone of the McCabe‚ÄďThiele, Fenske, and Gilliland design equations that every refinery uses to design columns.

## 1.3 The K-Value: The Central Quantity of Multicomponent VLE

The **equilibrium ratio** (or **K-value**) of component *i* is defined as the ratio of its mole fraction in the vapor phase to its mole fraction in the coexisting liquid phase:

$$ K_i \equiv \frac{y_i}{x_i} $$

The K-value is a *function of temperature, pressure, and composition*. Under Raoult's Law (ideal solution + ideal gas), it takes the beautifully simple form:

$$ K_i = \frac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P} $$

This expression tells us that *light* components (high $P_i^{sat}$, high volatility) have $K_i > 1$ and preferentially partition into the vapor, while *heavy* components (low $P_i^{sat}$) have $K_i < 1$ and remain in the liquid.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Decomposition of the VLE Problem

Every multicomponent VLE calculation follows a single, elegant logical flow:

1. **Identify the State**: Bubble T, Bubble P, Dew T, Dew P, or Flash.
2. **Apply the VLE Relation**: $y_i = K_i x_i$ (from $f_i^V = f_i^L$).
3. **Apply the Summation Constraint**: Either $\sum x_i = 1$ (vapor sum) or $\sum y_i = 1$ (liquid sum).
4. **Specify What is Known and What is Unknown**: This determines whether the problem is *direct* (P- or T- known) or *iterative*.
5. **Solve the Resulting Scalar Equation** for the single unknown (T, P, or $\psi$).

## 2.2 The Four Canonical VLE Problems

| **Type** | **Given** | **Find** | **Key Equation** | **Method** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bubble P** | $\{x_i\}, T$ | $P$ | $P = \sum_i x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$ | **Direct** (closed form) |
| **Bubble T** | $\{x_i\}, P$ | $T$ | $\sum_i x_i K_i(T,P) = 1$ | **Iterative** in $T$ |
| **Dew P** | $\{y_i\}, T$ | $P$ | $P = \left[\sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{P_i^{sat}(T)}\right]^{-1}$ | **Direct** (closed form) |
| **Dew T** | $\{y_i\}, P$ | $T$ | $\sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{K_i(T,P)} = 1$ | **Iterative** in $T$ |
| **Flash** | $\{z_i\}, T, P$ | $\psi, \{x_i\}, \{y_i\}$ | Rachford‚ÄďRice Equation | **Iterative** in $\psi$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** Bubble P and Dew P calculations are *closed-form* ‚ÄĒ no iteration is required. Bubble T and Dew T, however, require iterative root-finding (Newton‚ÄďRaphson, bisection, or successive substitution) because the saturation pressure is a *non-linear* function of temperature (typically given by Antoine, Wagner, or DIPPR equations). The Flash problem is fundamentally an iteration in the vapor fraction $\psi$ ‚ÄĒ a problem of tremendous industrial importance in designing two-phase separators, distillation reflux drums, and petroleum stabilizers.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Petroleum Refining**: Crude oil atmospheric and vacuum distillation towers contain >100 components; tray-by-tray calculations use the Rachford‚ÄďRice flash equation to compute equilibrium.
- **Cryogenic Air Separation**: Air (N‚āā + O‚āā + Ar) is separated by Linde double-column distillation. Bubble/dew calculations determine the operating pressures of the high- and low-pressure columns.
- **Refrigeration Cycles**: The working fluid (e.g., R-134a, R-410A, ammonia) is a multicomponent mixture; flash calculations determine the fraction of liquid leaving the expansion valve.
- **Natural Gas Processing**: Sweetening and liquefaction processes require flash calculations at cryogenic temperatures where ideal-solution assumptions break down (necessitating the use of equations of state ‚ÄĒ a topic of advanced study).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Symbol / Formula** | **Meaning** | **Condition of Validity** |
|---|---|---|
| $K_i = y_i / x_i$ | Equilibrium ratio | Always (definition) |
| $K_i = P_i^{sat}(T) / P$ | Raoult's Law K-value | Ideal liquid + ideal gas |
| $K_i = \gamma_i P_i^{sat} / P$ | Modified Raoult's Law K-value | Ideal gas + non-ideal liquid |
| $K_i = \phi_i^L / \phi_i^V$ | K-value from EOS (ő¶‚Äďő¶ method) | Any system with applicable EOS |
| $P_{bubble} = \sum_i x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$ | Bubble-point pressure | Ideal solution, T specified |
| $P_{dew} = 1 / \sum_i (y_i / P_i^{sat}(T))$ | Dew-point pressure | Ideal solution, T specified |
| $\sum_i x_i K_i = 1$ | Bubble-T and Bubble-P constraint | All systems |
| $\sum_i y_i / K_i = 1$ | Dew-T and Dew-P constraint | All systems |
| $f(z_i, K_i, \psi) = \sum_i \dfrac{z_i(K_i - 1)}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} = 0$ | **Rachford‚ÄďRice Equation** | Isothermal Flash |
| $x_i = z_i / [1 + \psi(K_i - 1)]$ | Liquid composition from flash | Isothermal Flash |
| $y_i = K_i z_i / [1 + \psi(K_i - 1)]$ | Vapor composition from flash | Isothermal Flash |
| $0 \le \psi \le 1$ | Bounded vapor fraction | Physical feasibility of two-phase flash |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the **$K_i$ **Sums Differ:** The bubble-point constraint $\sum x_i K_i = 1$ arises from substituting $y_i = K_i x_i$ into $\sum y_i = 1$. The dew-point constraint $\sum y_i / K_i = 1$ arises from substituting $x_i = y_i / K_i$ into $\sum x_i = 1$. These are *dual* statements of the same fundamental VLE condition, and a student who understands this duality can derive both equations from memory in an exam.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Policy:** All algebraic steps are shown explicitly. No step is skipped. The starting point for every derivation is the **Modified Raoult's Law** with the appropriate ideal-solution simplifications as prescribed in the KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bubble-Point Pressure Equation (Ideal Solution)

**Given:** Liquid mole fractions $\{x_i\}$, temperature $T$.
**Find:** Total pressure $P$ at which the liquid first begins to boil.

**Starting Point ‚ÄĒ Raoult's Law (Ideal Solution, Ideal Gas Vapor):**

For each component *i* in an ideal liquid mixture, the partial pressure equals the pure-component vapor pressure multiplied by its liquid mole fraction:

$$ p_i = x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T) $$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Express the Vapor Mole Fraction via Dalton's Law:**

The mole fraction of component *i* in the vapor phase equals its partial pressure divided by the total pressure:

$$ y_i = \frac{p_i}{P} = \frac{x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)}{P} $$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply the Summation Constraint $\sum y_i = 1$:**

Because the vapor phase is the entire system at the bubble point, its mole fractions must sum to unity:

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)}{P} = 1 $$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Factor Out the Constant $1/P$:**

Since $P$ is a constant independent of *i*, it can be extracted from the summation:

$$ \frac{1}{P} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T) = 1 $$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Solve Algebraically for $P$:**

Multiplying both sides by $P$ and rearranging:

$$ \boxed{\, P_{bubble} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T) \,} $$

> This is a **direct (closed-form)** calculation: at the specified $T$, look up $P_i^{sat}$ for each component (e.g., from the Antoine equation), take the mole-fraction-weighted sum, and the result *is* the bubble-point pressure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bubble-Point Temperature Equation (Iterative Form)

**Given:** Liquid mole fractions $\{x_i\}$, pressure $P$.
**Find:** Temperature $T$ at which the liquid first begins to boil.

**Starting Point ‚ÄĒ The General K-Value Relation:**

For any system (ideal or non-ideal), the vapor composition is related to the liquid composition through the K-value:

$$ y_i = K_i \, x_i $$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Apply the Vapor Summation Constraint:**

At the bubble point, the vapor phase just begins to appear, so its mole fractions must sum to 1:

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} K_i \, x_i = 1 $$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Recognize that $K_i = K_i(T, P, \{x_j\}, \{y_j\})$:**

For an ideal solution (Raoult's Law), $K_i = P_i^{sat}(T)/P$. The dependence of $K_i$ on $T$ is *non-linear* (typically exponential or cubic via Antoine/Wagner equations), so the resulting equation:

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \, \frac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P} = 1 $$

is a **single non-linear algebraic equation in the single unknown $T$**, solvable by iterative methods (Newton‚ÄďRaphson, bisection, or successive substitution).

$$ \boxed{\, f(T) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \, K_i(T, P) - 1 = 0 \,} $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Dew-Point Pressure Equation (Ideal Solution)

**Given:** Vapor mole fractions $\{y_i\}$, temperature $T$.
**Find:** Total pressure $P$ at which the vapor first begins to condense.

**Starting Point ‚ÄĒ Raoult's Law Expressed in the Form $x_i = y_i P / P_i^{sat}$:**

Rearranging Raoult's Law ($y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}$) for the liquid mole fraction:

$$ x_i = \frac{y_i \, P}{P_i^{sat}(T)} $$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Apply the Liquid Summation Constraint $\sum x_i = 1$:**

At the dew point, the incipient liquid phase must have mole fractions summing to 1:

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{y_i \, P}{P_i^{sat}(T)} = 1 $$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Factor Out $P$:**

$$ P \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{y_i}{P_i^{sat}(T)} = 1 $$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Solve for $P$:**

$$ \boxed{\, P_{dew} = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{y_i}{P_i^{sat}(T)}\right]^{-1} \,} $$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Dew-Point Temperature Equation (Iterative Form)

**Given:** Vapor mole fractions $\{y_i\}$, pressure $P$.
**Find:** Temperature $T$ at which the vapor first begins to condense.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Start with the K-Value Definition:**

$$ x_i = \frac{y_i}{K_i} $$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply the Liquid Summation Constraint:**

At the dew point, the incipient liquid has $\sum x_i = 1$:

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{y_i}{K_i(T, P)} = 1 $$

This is a **single non-linear algebraic equation in $T$**, requiring iteration:

$$ \boxed{\, f(T) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{y_i}{K_i(T, P)} - 1 = 0 \,} $$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: The Rachford‚ÄďRice Flash Equation (Masterpiece Derivation)

**Given:** Feed composition $\{z_i\}$, feed flow rate $F$, temperature $T$, pressure $P$ (which determine all $K_i$).
**Find:** Vapor fraction $\psi = V/F$, liquid composition $\{x_i\}$, vapor composition $\{y_i\}$.

### Phase 1 ‚ÄĒ The Material Balances

Consider a single equilibrium flash chamber. A feed of flow $F$ (mol/s) with composition $z_i$ separates into a vapor product $V$ and a liquid product $L$.

**Overall Mass Balance:**

$$ F = V + L $$

**Component Mass Balance (for each *i*):**

$$ F \, z_i = V \, y_i + L \, x_i $$

### Phase 2 ‚ÄĒ Normalize by $F$ (Dimensionless Fractions)

Define the **vapor fraction** $\psi \equiv V/F$. The liquid fraction is then $L/F = 1 - \psi$.

Dividing the component balance by $F$:

$$ z_i = \psi \, y_i + (1 - \psi) \, x_i $$

### Phase 3 ‚ÄĒ Introduce the VLE Relation

Substitute $y_i = K_i x_i$ (or equivalently $x_i = y_i / K_i$) into the normalized balance:

$$ z_i = \psi \, K_i \, x_i + (1 - \psi) \, x_i $$

**Step A ‚ÄĒ Factor out $x_i$:**

$$ z_i = x_i \left[\psi \, K_i + (1 - \psi)\right] $$

**Step B ‚ÄĒ Recognize the bracket as $1 + \psi(K_i - 1)$:**

Verify: $\psi K_i + 1 - \psi = 1 + \psi K_i - \psi = 1 + \psi(K_i - 1)$. ‚úď

**Step C ‚ÄĒ Solve explicitly for $x_i$:**

$$ x_i = \frac{z_i}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} $$

**Step D ‚ÄĒ Solve for $y_i$ using $y_i = K_i x_i$:**

$$ y_i = \frac{K_i \, z_i}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} $$

### Phase 4 ‚ÄĒ Apply the Summation Constraint

Both $x_i$ and $y_i$ must sum to 1 (mole-fraction constraints). Using the liquid constraint:

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{z_i}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} = 1 \quad \text{...(I)} $$

Using the vapor constraint:

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{K_i \, z_i}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} = 1 \quad \text{...(II)} $$

### Phase 5 ‚ÄĒ Subtract to Obtain the Rachford‚ÄďRice Equation

**Subtract (I) from (II):**

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{K_i \, z_i - z_i}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} = 1 - 1 = 0 $$

**Factor out $z_i(K_i - 1)$ in the numerator:**

$$ \boxed{\, \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{z_i(K_i - 1)}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} = 0 \,} $$

This is the **Rachford‚ÄďRice equation** (Rachford & Rice, 1952), the single most important scalar equation in multicomponent separation design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Boundedness of $\psi$:** The Rachford‚ÄďRice function is a continuous, monotonically decreasing function of $\psi$ for a well-posed flash. The feasible range of $\psi$ is bounded by the K-values:
>
> $$ \frac{1}{1 - K_{max}} \le \psi \le \frac{1}{1 - K_{min}} $$
>
> This guarantees that the denominator $1 + \psi(K_i - 1)$ is positive for *every* component *i*, ensuring all $x_i \ge 0$ and $y_i \ge 0$. A negative denominator for any component means the specified $T$ and $P$ cannot produce a two-phase flash for that feed.

### Phase 6 ‚ÄĒ Once $\psi$ is Found, the Compositions Follow Directly

After solving the Rachford‚ÄďRice equation for $\psi$ (typically by Newton‚ÄďRaphson iteration), the equilibrium compositions are obtained by direct back-substitution:

$$ x_i = \frac{z_i}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)}, \qquad y_i = \frac{K_i \, z_i}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Summary of the Master Derivation:** Starting from *two* mass balances and *one* VLE relation, we arrived at *one* scalar nonlinear equation in a *single* unknown ($\psi$). The compositions follow by simple algebra. This reduction ‚ÄĒ from $3N+1$ unknowns ($x_i, y_i, V, L$) to a single scalar ‚ÄĒ is the central miracle of equilibrium-stage theory. The reduction is achieved by the VLE condition, the $\sum x_i = 1$ constraint, the $\sum y_i = 1$ constraint, and the overall mass balance, which together provide $N + N + 1 + 1 = 2N+2$ equations for $3N+2$ unknowns ($x_i, y_i, V, L, z_i$ but with $z_i$ given) ‚ÄĒ leaving exactly one design variable: $\psi$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are **block-level functional architecture flowcharts** (per the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards in the Engine Protocol). They map the *logical flow* of calculations rather than physical process equipment.

## 4.1 Master VLE Calculation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START: Multicomponent VLE Problem]) --> decide1{Given: x or y?}
    decide1 -- Liquid x_i known --> bpnode1[Problem Type: BUBBLE POINT]
    decide1 -- Vapor y_i known --> dpnode1[Problem Type: DEW POINT]
    decide1 -- Feed z_i known --> flashnode1[Problem Type: FLASH]

    bpnode1 --> bpchoose{T or P given?}
    bpchoose -- T given --> bpcalc1[Direct Sum: P = sum x_i P_isat]
    bpchoose -- P given --> bpcalc2[Iterative: solve sum x_i K_i T = 1]

    dpnode1 --> dpchoose{T or P given?}
    dpchoose -- T given --> dpcalc1[Direct Sum: P = 1 over sum y_i P_isat]
    dpchoose -- P given --> dpcalc2[Iterative: solve sum y_i K_i T = 1]

    flashnode1 --> flashstep1[Calculate K_i T P for all components]
    flashstep1 --> flashstep2[Solve Rachford-Rice Equation for psi]
    flashstep2 --> flashstep3[Compute x_i and y_i from back substitution]
    flashstep3 --> flashstep4[Verify: sum x_i = 1, sum y_i = 1]

    bpcalc1 --> endnode([END: P_bubble obtained])
    bpcalc2 --> endnode
    dpcalc1 --> endnode
    dpcalc2 --> endnode
    flashstep4 --> endnode
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of the Rachford‚ÄďRice Flash Solver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedA[Feed z_i] --> initA[Initialize psi guess]
    feedA --> kcalc[K-value Model: K_i = f of T and P]
    initA --> rrf[Compute RR function]
    kcalc --> rrf
    rrf{Converged?}
    rrf -- No --> update[Newton update for psi]
    update --> rrf
    rrf -- Yes --> compcalc[Compute x_i and y_i]
    compcalc --> verify[Verify constraints]
    verify --> doneA([Output: psi, x_i, y_i])
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram of the Mod-Raoult's-Law Framework Underlying All Derivations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    lawA[Modified Raoult's Law: phi y P = gamma x f_oL] --> ideal1{Ideal solution?}
    ideal1 -- YES --> lawB[Raoult's Law: y P = x P_isat]
    ideal1 -- NO --> lawC[Keep activity coeff gamma]
    lawB --> kdef[Define K_i = P_isat over P]
    lawC --> kdef2[Define K_i = gamma P_isat over P]
    kdef --> sumreq[Apply sum constraint]
    kdef2 --> sumreq
    sumreq --> typeselect{Problem Type}
    typeselect --> bpres[Bubble: sum x_i K_i = 1]
    typeselect --> dpres[Dew: sum y_i K_i = 1]
    typeselect --> flashres[Flash: Rachford-Rice]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

### Question 1
**Define the bubble point and dew point of a multicomponent liquid mixture. State the working equations used for calculating bubble P and dew P for an ideal solution.**

**Model Answer:**

The **bubble point** of a liquid mixture is the temperature (at a given pressure) or pressure (at a given temperature) at which the first infinitesimally small vapor bubble forms upon heating or depressurizing. Mathematically, the bubble-P equation for an ideal solution is:

$$ P_{bubble} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T) $$

The **dew point** of a vapor mixture is the temperature (at a given pressure) or pressure (at a given temperature) at which the first drop of liquid condenses. The dew-P equation for an ideal solution is:

$$ P_{dew} = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{y_i}{P_i^{sat}(T)} \right]^{-1} $$

### Question 2
**What is the Rachford‚ÄďRice equation? Why is it solved iteratively?**

**Model Answer:**

The Rachford‚ÄďRice equation is the governing scalar equation for an isothermal single-stage flash calculation. It relates the feed composition $z_i$, the equilibrium K-values $K_i$, and the unknown vapor fraction $\psi = V/F$:

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{z_i(K_i - 1)}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} = 0 $$

It must be solved **iteratively** (Newton‚ÄďRaphson, bisection, or successive substitution) because $\psi$ appears *non-linearly* in the denominator of each term, and a closed-form solution for $\psi$ in terms of $\{z_i, K_i\}$ does not exist for $N \ge 2$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice Format

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď End Semester, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

### ūüďė Question A (14 Marks)

**A liquid mixture has the following composition: $x_{C_3} = 0.30$, $x_{nC_4} = 0.40$, $x_{nC_5} = 0.30$. At $T = 50\,¬įC$, the saturation pressures are $P^{sat}_{C_3} = 1300\,kPa$, $P^{sat}_{nC_4} = 500\,kPa$, $P^{sat}_{nC_5} = 200\,kPa$.**

#### (a) Derive the equation used to calculate the bubble-point pressure of an ideal multicomponent solution. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ State the starting relation (Raoult's Law):**

$$ y_i \, P = x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T) $$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Express the vapor mole fraction:**

$$ y_i = \frac{x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)}{P} $$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Apply the vapor mole-fraction constraint $\sum y_i = 1$:**

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)}{P} = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; P = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T) $$

**Boxed result:** $P_{bubble} = \sum x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$ ‚ÄĒ direct closed-form.

#### (b) Calculate the bubble-point pressure and the incipient vapor composition. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Apply the formula directly:**

$$ P_{bubble} = (0.30)(1300) + (0.40)(500) + (0.30)(200) $$

$$ P_{bubble} = 390 + 200 + 60 = \mathbf{650\,kPa} $$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute vapor mole fractions using $y_i = x_i P_i^{sat} / P$:**

$$ y_{C_3} = \frac{(0.30)(1300)}{650} = \frac{390}{650} = 0.600 $$

$$ y_{nC_4} = \frac{(0.40)(500)}{650} = \frac{200}{650} = 0.308 $$

$$ y_{nC_5} = \frac{(0.30)(200)}{650} = \frac{60}{650} = 0.092 $$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Verify:** $0.600 + 0.308 + 0.092 = 1.000$ ‚úď

The incipient vapor is **enriched in the most volatile component (propane, $y_{C_3} = 0.60$, double its liquid concentration of 0.30)**, as physically expected.

---

### ūüďó Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice

**A feed of composition $z_{C_3} = 0.20$, $z_{nC_4} = 0.50$, $z_{nC_5} = 0.30$ is flashed isothermally at conditions where the K-values are $K_{C_3} = 3.0$, $K_{nC_4} = 1.0$, $K_{nC_5} = 0.333$.**

#### (a) Derive the Rachford‚ÄďRice equation from the material-balance and VLE relations. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution (Compact Derivation):**

Component balance: $z_i = \psi y_i + (1 - \psi) x_i$.
VLE: $y_i = K_i x_i$, hence $z_i = x_i[1 + \psi(K_i - 1)]$, giving $x_i = z_i/[1 + \psi(K_i - 1)]$.
Summing: $\sum x_i = \sum z_i/[1 + \psi(K_i - 1)] = 1$.
Equivalently, using $y_i = K_i z_i/[1 + \psi(K_i - 1)]$ and $\sum y_i = 1$, then subtracting the two:

$$ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{z_i(K_i - 1)}{1 + \psi(K_i - 1)} = 0 $$

#### (b) Solve for the vapor fraction $\psi$ and the equilibrium compositions. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Substitute the data into the Rachford‚ÄďRice equation:**

$$ f(\psi) = \frac{0.20(3.0 - 1)}{1 + \psi(3.0 - 1)} + \frac{0.50(1.0 - 1)}{1 + \psi(1.0 - 1)} + \frac{0.30(0.333 - 1)}{1 + \psi(0.333 - 1)} = 0 $$

The middle term is zero (since $K_{nC_4} = 1$, $nC_4$ distributes equally). So:

$$ f(\psi) = \frac{0.40}{1 + 2\psi} + \frac{-0.200}{1 - 0.667\psi} = 0 $$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Solve algebraically:**

$$ \frac{0.40}{1 + 2\psi} = \frac{0.200}{1 - 0.667\psi} $$

$$ 0.40(1 - 0.667\psi) = 0.200(1 + 2\psi) $$

$$ 0.40 - 0.267\psi = 0.200 + 0.400\psi $$

$$ 0.20 = 0.667\psi \;\Rightarrow\; \psi = 0.300 $$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute liquid and vapor compositions:**

For $C_3$: $x_{C_3} = 0.20/[1 + 0.3(2)] = 0.20/1.6 = 0.125$; $y_{C_3} = 3.0 \times 0.125 = 0.375$.
For $nC_4$: $x_{nC_4} = 0.50/[1 + 0.3(0)] = 0.50$; $y_{nC_4} = 1.0 \times 0.50 = 0.500$.
For $nC_5$: $x_{nC_5} = 0.30/[1 + 0.3(-0.667)] = 0.30/0.800 = 0.375$; $y_{nC_5} = 0.333 \times 0.375 = 0.125$.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Verify:** $\sum x_i = 0.125 + 0.500 + 0.375 = 1.000$ ‚úď; $\sum y_i = 0.375 + 0.500 + 0.125 = 1.000$ ‚úď.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚ÄĒ Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constraint check:** Many students compute compositions but forget to verify that $\sum x_i = 1$ and $\sum y_i = 1$. **This loses 1‚Äď2 marks** routinely.
> 2. **Sign errors in $K_i - 1$:** When $K_i < 1$ (heavy component), the term $K_i - 1$ is *negative*. Mishandling this sign leads to a $\psi > 1$ or $\psi < 0$ solution that is *physically infeasible*.
> 3. **Using $K_i = P_i^{sat}/P$ for non-ideal systems:** If the K-values are *given* in the problem (as in the question above), use them directly. The Antoine equation should be used *only* when the problem explicitly provides Antoine constants and asks you to compute $P_i^{sat}$.
> 4. **Bubble T vs. Bubble P confusion:** Bubble P is a *direct* sum; Bubble T is an *iterative* root-find. Examiners explicitly test whether you recognize the distinction.
> 5. **Rachford‚ÄďRice denominator going to zero:** If $1 + \psi(K_i - 1) = 0$ for any *i*, the formulation is invalid. This corresponds to the feed being exactly at the bubble or dew point (i.e., the two-phase region has degenerated to a single phase).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ‚úÖ **Bubble Point** = first bubble of vapor from liquid; uses $\sum x_i K_i = 1$.
- ‚úÖ **Dew Point** = first drop of liquid from vapor; uses $\sum y_i / K_i = 1$.
- ‚úÖ **Bubble P** is **direct** (closed-form sum of $x_i P_i^{sat}$); **Bubble T** is **iterative**.
- ‚úÖ **Dew P** is **direct** (harmonic-like sum of $y_i / P_i^{sat}$); **Dew T** is **iterative**.
- ‚úÖ The **K-value** $K_i = y_i / x_i$ encapsulates all thermodynamic information.
- ‚úÖ For ideal solutions + ideal gas: $K_i = P_i^{sat}(T)/P$ ‚ÄĒ light components have $K_i > 1$, heavy components have $K_i < 1$.
- ‚úÖ The **Rachford‚ÄďRice equation** is the master equation for **flash** calculations: $\sum z_i(K_i - 1)/[1 + \psi(K_i - 1)] = 0$.
- ‚úÖ The **vapor fraction** $\psi = V/F$ is the single unknown in a flash; it is bounded $0 \le \psi \le 1$ for a well-posed two-phase flash.
- ‚úÖ After solving for $\psi$, the equilibrium compositions follow from $x_i = z_i/[1 + \psi(K_i - 1)]$ and $y_i = K_i z_i/[1 + \psi(K_i - 1)]$.
- ‚úÖ **Always verify** $\sum x_i = 1$ and $\sum y_i = 1$ after any flash calculation ‚ÄĒ partial marks are awarded for correct algebra but lost for unverified results.
- ‚úÖ **Modified Raoult's Law** (used for non-ideal liquids) extends the ideal-solution K-value to $K_i = \gamma_i P_i^{sat}/P$ (with $\gamma_i = 1$ for ideal solutions).
- ‚úÖ For non-ideal vapors, K-values are obtained from **equations of state** (Peng‚ÄďRobinson, Soave‚ÄďRedlich‚ÄďKwong, Virial): $K_i = \phi_i^L / \phi_i^V$ ‚ÄĒ a topic of advanced study beyond Module 4.
- ‚úÖ **Industrial relevance**: Bubble/dew/flash calculations form the analytical engine of distillation column design (McCabe‚ÄďThiele, Lewis‚ÄďMatheson, Thiele‚ÄďGeddes), refinery stabilizer design, and cryogenic gas-separation plants.
- ‚úÖ The five derivations in this module ‚ÄĒ Bubble P, Bubble T, Dew P, Dew T, Rachford‚ÄďRice ‚ÄĒ are the *only* derivations required for the KTU 2024 End Semester Examination in this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Reaction Equilibria: Extent of Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chemical Reaction Equilibria: Extent of Reaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In chemical reaction thermodynamics, the **Extent of Reaction** (denoted by the Greek letter **őĺ**, pronounced "xi") is defined as a state variable that quantifies the *progress* of a chemical reaction from an initial state toward equilibrium. It is formally introduced through the differential relation:

$$dn_i = \nu_i \, d\xi$$

where:
- $dn_i$ = infinitesimal change in the number of moles of species $i$
- $\nu_i$ = **stoichiometric coefficient** of species $i$ (negative for reactants, positive for products, by IUPAC convention)
- $d\xi$ = infinitesimal change in the extent of reaction

Upon integration, this yields the macroscopic mole balance:

$$n_i = n_{i,0} + \nu_i \, \xi$$

The extent of reaction has the **SI unit of mole (kmol)**, and it is a **single, scalar variable** that fully characterizes the state of a *single* reacting system ‚ÄĒ irrespective of how many species are involved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The extent of reaction is *not* a property of any single species. It is a *collective* variable describing the entire reacting mixture. At equilibrium, its value is $\xi_{eq}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Train Journey" Model)

Imagine a single railway track running from **Station A (Reactants)** to **Station B (Products)**. The train carries a fixed *consist* (a set of coupled wagons) ‚ÄĒ the wagon ratios are fixed by stoichiometry.

- The **initial position** of the train = $\xi = 0$ (pure reactants at time $t = 0$)
- The **final position** = $\xi = \xi_{eq}$ (chemical equilibrium)
- The **distance traveled** = $\xi$

If one wagon (say, the *A-wagon*) moves forward by 10 meters, every other wagon must move forward by an *exactly proportional* distance dictated by the stoichiometric ratio. This proportional, coupled motion is precisely what $\xi$ captures mathematically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world KTU context:** In industrial reactors (e.g., ammonia synthesis, methanol production), engineers use $\xi$ to track how much reactant has been *converted* into product. The fractional conversion $X$ and $\xi$ are directly related but conceptually distinct ‚ÄĒ $\xi$ is a *molar* quantity, while $X$ is *dimensionless*.

## 1.3 Stoichiometric Number Convention (Sign Rules)

| Species Role | Sign of $\nu_i$ | Example: N‚āā + 3H‚āā ‚Üí 2NH‚āÉ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Reactant | Negative ($-$) | $\nu_{N_2} = -1$, $\nu_{H_2} = -3$ |
| Product | Positive ($+$) | $\nu_{NH_3} = +2$ |

> [!WARNING]
> Some textbooks (especially older ones) reverse this sign convention. **Always verify** the convention used in your KTU exam before writing your answer. The 2024 scheme follows the IUPAC convention (reactants negative, products positive).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Framework: Why is $\xi$ Needed?

When a reacting mixture is closed (no inflow/outflow), the total mass is conserved, but the *number of moles* of individual species changes. Without a proper variable, we would need a *separate equation* for every species. The extent of reaction **collapses this multi-variable problem into a single scalar**, drastically simplifying equilibrium calculations.

### The Three Logic Steps

1. **Specify the stoichiometry:** Write the balanced reaction with $\nu_i$ for every species.
2. **Establish the baseline:** Define initial moles $n_{i,0}$ at $\xi = 0$.
3. **Track progress:** Use $n_i = n_{i,0} + \nu_i \xi$ to compute the moles of every species at any $\xi$.

## 2.2 Multiple Reactions: The Generalized Form

When $R$ independent chemical reactions occur simultaneously, the mole balance generalizes to:

$$n_i = n_{i,0} + \sum_{j=1}^{R} \nu_{i,j} \, \xi_j$$

where $\nu_{i,j}$ is the stoichiometric coefficient of species $i$ in reaction $j$, and $\xi_j$ is the extent of the $j$-th reaction. This gives us $R$ independent "degrees of advancement."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For $S$ species and $R$ independent reactions, the system has **R independent extents of reaction** ‚ÄĒ a fact that determines the number of equilibrium equations we must solve (via $K_a$, $K_p$, or $K_\phi$ relations).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use Case | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | $dn_i = \nu_i \, d\xi$ | Differential form ‚ÄĒ single reaction | mol |
| 2 | $n_i = n_{i,0} + \nu_i \, \xi$ | Integrated form ‚ÄĒ single reaction | mol |
| 3 | $n_i = n_{i,0} + \sum_{j} \nu_{i,j} \xi_j$ | Multiple reactions ($R$ reactions) | mol |
| 4 | $\xi = \dfrac{n_i - n_{i,0}}{\nu_i}$ | Solve for $\xi$ from any species $i$ | mol |
| 5 | $n_{tot} = n_{tot,0} + \Delta\nu \, \xi$ | Total mole change, $\Delta\nu = \sum_i \nu_i$ | mol |
| 6 | $y_i = \dfrac{n_{i,0} + \nu_i \xi}{n_{tot,0} + \Delta\nu \xi}$ | Mole fraction in reacting mixture | dimensionless |
| 7 | $X = \dfrac{\xi}{\xi_{max}}$ | Relation to fractional conversion $X$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\xi_{eq}$ from $K = \prod y_i^{\nu_i}$ | Equilibrium extent via equilibrium constant | mol |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In design of ammonia, methanol, sulfuric acid, and Haber-Bosch reactors, $\xi$ directly yields the equilibrium conversion. Combined with the van't Hoff equation, it allows prediction of conversion vs. temperature ‚ÄĒ a critical input to reactor sizing and heat-exchanger network design (HEN) in pinch analysis.

## 2.4 Why This Concept Dominates the Module

The entire Module 4 (Chemical Reaction Equilibria) is built on $\xi$:
- $\xi$ converts the *stoichiometric constraints* into algebraic form
- It feeds into the **Criterion of Reaction Equilibrium:** $\Delta G_{T,P} = 0$ at constant $T,P$ ‚Üí relates $\xi_{eq}$ to $\Delta G^\circ$
- It enables computation of **equilibrium composition** for any initial feed
- It is the bridge between *thermodynamic equilibrium* and *reactor design (CRE)*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Atomic Balances to $\xi$

### Starting Point
Consider a single elementary reaction in a closed system:

$$ \nu_A A + \nu_B B \rightarrow \nu_C C + \nu_D D $$

By the **law of definite proportions**, when reaction proceeds by a small amount:
- For every $\nu_A$ moles of A consumed, exactly $\nu_B$ moles of B are consumed, and $\nu_C, \nu_D$ moles of C, D are produced.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1:** Define the *elementary progress* such that one "unit" of reaction corresponds to the consumption of $\nu_A$ moles of A.

**Step 2:** Let $d\xi$ represent an infinitesimal advancement of this unit. Then by stoichiometric coupling:
$$ dn_A = -\nu_A \, d\xi, \quad dn_B = -\nu_B \, d\xi, \quad dn_C = +\nu_C \, d\xi, \quad dn_D = +\nu_D \, d\xi $$

**Step 3:** Observe the unified form. Since $\nu_A$ and $\nu_B$ are negative for reactants, all four expressions collapse into one elegant statement:

$$ dn_i = \nu_i \, d\xi \quad \text{for } i \in \{A, B, C, D\} $$

**Step 4:** Integrate from the initial state $(\xi = 0, n_i = n_{i,0})$ to an arbitrary state $(\xi, n_i)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{n_{i,0}}^{n_i} dn_i &= \nu_i \int_{0}^{\xi} d\xi \\[4pt]
n_i - n_{i,0} &= \nu_i \, \xi \\[4pt]
n_i &= n_{i,0} + \nu_i \, \xi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Verify by back-substitution. Differentiate the result:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dn_i}{d\xi} &= \nu_i \\[4pt]
dn_i &= \nu_i \, d\xi \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

The derivation is mathematically exact and holds for any closed reacting system with a single reaction.

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Moles in a Reacting Mixture

Summing the integrated mole balance over all species:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_i n_i &= \sum_i n_{i,0} + \xi \sum_i \nu_i \\[4pt]
n_{tot} &= n_{tot,0} + \Delta\nu \cdot \xi
\end{aligned}
$$

where the **stoichiometric sum** is defined as:

$$ \Delta\nu \equiv \sum_i \nu_i $$

This compact form is *essential* for computing mole fractions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_i &= \frac{n_i}{n_{tot}} = \frac{n_{i,0} + \nu_i \xi}{n_{tot,0} + \Delta\nu \, \xi}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example (Single Reaction)

**Problem Setup (KTU Typical):** Consider the water-gas shift reaction at 1000 K and 1 bar:

$$ CO_{(g)} + H_2O_{(g)} \rightleftharpoons CO_{2(g)} + H_{2(g)} $$

Initial moles: $n_{CO,0} = 2$, $n_{H_2O,0} = 3$, $n_{CO_2,0} = 0$, $n_{H_2,0} = 0$. Equilibrium constant $K = 1.0$.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Write the stoichiometric vector:**
$$ \nu = \left[ -1, \, -1, \, +1, \, +1 \right]^T $$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply mole balance:**
$$ n_{CO} = 2 - \xi, \quad n_{H_2O} = 3 - \xi, \quad n_{CO_2} = \xi, \quad n_{H_2} = \xi $$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute total moles:**
$$ \Delta\nu = (-1) + (-1) + (+1) + (+1) = 0 $$
$$ n_{tot} = 2 - \xi + 3 - \xi + \xi + \xi = 5 $$
Since $\Delta\nu = 0$, the total number of moles is invariant ‚ÄĒ a *special case* that simplifies calculations.

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Compute mole fractions:**
$$
y_{CO} = \frac{2-\xi}{5}, \quad y_{H_2O} = \frac{3-\xi}{5}, \quad y_{CO_2} = \frac{\xi}{5}, \quad y_{H_2} = \frac{\xi}{5}
$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Apply the equilibrium expression** (with $K_\phi = 1$ since $K = 1$ and $P = 1$ bar):
$$ K = \frac{y_{CO_2} \cdot y_{H_2}}{y_{CO} \cdot y_{H_2O}} = \frac{(\xi/5)(\xi/5)}{((2-\xi)/5)((3-\xi)/5)} = \frac{\xi^2}{(2-\xi)(3-\xi)} = 1 $$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Solve the algebraic equation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\xi^2 &= (2-\xi)(3-\xi) \\
\xi^2 &= 6 - 5\xi + \xi^2 \\
0 &= 6 - 5\xi \\
\xi_{eq} &= 1.2 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Final equilibrium composition:**
- $n_{CO,eq} = 0.8$ mol, $\quad y_{CO} = 0.16$
- $n_{H_2O,eq} = 1.8$ mol, $\quad y_{H_2O} = 0.36$
- $n_{CO_2,eq} = 1.2$ mol, $\quad y_{CO_2} = 0.24$
- $n_{H_2,eq} = 1.2$ mol, $\quad y_{H_2} = 0.24$

> [!NOTE]
> The total moles stayed at 5, and $\Delta\nu = 0$ meant $K$ was independent of pressure. This is a classic KTU exam scenario.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Multi-Reaction Extension

For two simultaneous reactions:
$$ A \rightleftharpoons B \quad (j=1) $$
$$ B \rightleftharpoons C \quad (j=2) $$

The mole of each species is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
n_A &= n_{A,0} - \xi_1 \\
n_B &= n_{B,0} + \xi_1 - \xi_2 \\
n_C &= n_{C,0} + \xi_2
\end{aligned}
$$

This system requires solving **two coupled equilibrium equations** simultaneously ‚ÄĒ typical in industrial reformer and cracker design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Role of $\xi$ in the Equilibrium Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: Initial Moles n_i0, Stoichiometry nu_i, T, P, K] --> B[Block 1: Define Reaction Stoichiometry]
    B --> C[Block 2: Apply Mole Balance n_i = n_i0 + nu_i * xi]
    C --> D[Block 3: Compute Total Moles n_tot = n_tot0 + Delta_nu * xi]
    D --> E[Block 4: Compute Mole Fractions y_i = n_i / n_tot]
    E --> F[Block 5: Evaluate Equilibrium Expression K = Prod y_i^nu_i]
    F --> G[Block 6: Solve Nonlinear Algebraic Equation for xi_eq]
    G --> H[Block 7: Back-Substitute to Get n_i_eq and y_i_eq]
    H --> I[OUTPUT: Equilibrium Composition and Conversion]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style I fill:#d4f5d4
    style F fill:#fff4cc
    style G fill:#ffccaa
```

## 4.2 Extent-of-Reaction State-Space Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State 0: xi = 0, Pure Reactants] -->|Reaction Proceeds| B[Intermediate State: 0 < xi < xi_eq]
    B -->|Equilibrium Reached| C[State eq: xi = xi_eq]
    C -.->|No Further Change| C

    A --> A1[Composition: n_i = n_i0]
    B --> B1[Composition: n_i = n_i0 + nu_i * xi]
    C --> C1[Composition: n_i_eq = n_i0 + nu_i * xi_eq]

    style A fill:#ffe0e0
    style B fill:#fff4cc
    style C fill:#d4f5d4
```

## 4.3 Multi-Reaction Coupling Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Reaction 1: xi_1] --> S1[Species A consumption]
    R1 --> S2[Species B production]
    R2[Reaction 2: xi_2] --> S2
    R2 --> S3[Species C production]
    S1 --> nA[n_A = n_A0 - xi_1]
    S2 --> nB[n_B = n_B0 + xi_1 - xi_2]
    S3 --> nC[n_C = n_C0 + xi_2]

    style R1 fill:#cce5ff
    style R2 fill:#cce5ff
    style nA fill:#fff4cc
    style nB fill:#fff4cc
    style nC fill:#fff4cc
```

> [!NOTE]
> These flowcharts mirror the analytical structure used by KTU board examiners for marking multi-step equilibrium problems. Notice the parallel between Block 1 ‚Üí 7 in the architecture map and the **logical deduction order** expected in your answer script.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024]
**Define the term "Extent of Reaction" and state its SI unit.**

**Model Answer:**
The extent of reaction ($\xi$) is a thermodynamic state variable that quantifies the progress of a chemical reaction. It is defined by the relation $dn_i = \nu_i \, d\xi$, where $dn_i$ is the infinitesimal change in moles of species $i$, and $\nu_i$ is the stoichiometric coefficient (negative for reactants, positive for products). The integrated form is $n_i = n_{i,0} + \nu_i \, \xi$. **SI unit: mole (or kmol).**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]
**For the reaction N‚āā + 3H‚āā ‚Üí 2NH‚āÉ, write the expression for $n_i$ in terms of $\xi$.**

**Model Answer:**
With $\nu_{N_2} = -1$, $\nu_{H_2} = -3$, $\nu_{NH_3} = +2$:

$$
n_{N_2} = n_{N_2,0} - \xi, \quad n_{H_2} = n_{H_2,0} - 3\xi, \quad n_{NH_3} = n_{NH_3,0} + 2\xi
$$

---

## PART B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A [KTU University Exam ‚Äď Model Question]** (14 Marks)

**For the gas-phase reaction PCl‚āÖ ‚áĆ PCl‚āÉ + Cl‚āā, 5 mol of pure PCl‚āÖ is introduced into an evacuated 10 L vessel at 250 ¬įC. At equilibrium, the degree of dissociation ($\alpha$) is 0.80. (a) Calculate the equilibrium extent of reaction $\xi_{eq}$. (b) Determine the equilibrium mole fractions and the total pressure.**

#### **Part (a) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks**

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Write stoichiometry. Reaction: PCl‚āÖ ‚Üí PCl‚āÉ + Cl‚āā
- $\nu_{PCl_5} = -1$, $\nu_{PCl_3} = +1$, $\nu_{Cl_2} = +1$

**Step 2:** Note the relation between extent and degree of dissociation. By definition:

$$ \alpha = \frac{\text{moles dissociated}}{\text{initial moles}} = \frac{\xi_{eq}}{n_{PCl_5,0}} $$

**Step 3:** Solve:
$$ \xi_{eq} = \alpha \cdot n_{PCl_5,0} = 0.80 \times 5 = 4.0 \text{ mol} $$

#### **Part (b) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks**

**Step 4:** Equilibrium moles:
- $n_{PCl_5,eq} = 5 - 4 = 1$ mol
- $n_{PCl_3,eq} = 4$ mol
- $n_{Cl_2,eq} = 4$ mol
- $n_{tot,eq} = 1 + 4 + 4 = 9$ mol

**Step 5:** Mole fractions:
$$ y_{PCl_5} = 1/9 = 0.111, \quad y_{PCl_3} = 4/9 = 0.444, \quad y_{Cl_2} = 4/9 = 0.444 $$

**Step 6:** Total pressure using ideal gas law:
$$ P = \frac{n_{tot} RT}{V} = \frac{9 \times 8.314 \times 523}{10 \times 10^{-3}} $$

$$ P = \frac{9 \times 8.314 \times 523}{0.010} = 3.91 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 39.1 \text{ bar} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often compute the total pressure using *initial* moles (5) instead of *equilibrium* moles (9). This gives the wrong answer and costs full marks. **Always re-derive mole fractions from $n_{i,eq}$ at the equilibrium state.**

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** (14 Marks)

**The reaction A + 2B ‚Üí C is carried out with initial moles 2 mol A, 4 mol B, 0 mol C at 500 K and 1 bar. The equilibrium constant $K_y = 4.0$ (assume ideal gas). (a) Set up the equation for $\xi_{eq}$ using the equilibrium expression. (b) Solve for $\xi_{eq}$ and determine the equilibrium composition.**

#### **Part (a) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Stoichiometry: $\nu_A = -1$, $\nu_B = -2$, $\nu_C = +1$, $\Delta\nu = -2$.

**Step 2:** Mole balance:
- $n_A = 2 - \xi$
- $n_B = 4 - 2\xi$
- $n_C = \xi$
- $n_{tot} = 6 - 2\xi$

**Step 3:** Mole fractions:
$$ y_A = \frac{2-\xi}{6-2\xi}, \quad y_B = \frac{4-2\xi}{6-2\xi}, \quad y_C = \frac{\xi}{6-2\xi} $$

**Step 4:** Equilibrium expression:
$$ K_y = \frac{y_C}{y_A \cdot y_B^2} = \frac{\xi/(6-2\xi)}{[(2-\xi)/(6-2\xi)] \cdot [(4-2\xi)/(6-2\xi)]^2} = 4.0 $$

**Step 5:** Simplify:
$$ \frac{\xi (6-2\xi)^2}{(2-\xi)(4-2\xi)^2} = 4.0 $$

#### **Part (b) ‚ÄĒ 7 Marks**

**Step 6:** Note the constraint: $\xi \leq 2$ (limiting reagent is A). Solve numerically. Let $f(\xi) = \frac{\xi (6-2\xi)^2}{(2-\xi)(4-2\xi)^2} - 4.0$.

Try $\xi = 1.0$: $f(1.0) = \frac{1 \times 16}{1 \times 4} - 4 = 4 - 4 = 0$. ‚úď

Therefore, $\xi_{eq} = 1.0$ mol exactly.

**Step 7:** Equilibrium moles:
- $n_{A,eq} = 2 - 1 = 1$ mol
- $n_{B,eq} = 4 - 2 = 2$ mol
- $n_{C,eq} = 1$ mol
- $n_{tot,eq} = 4$ mol

**Step 8:** Equilibrium mole fractions:
$$ y_A = 0.25, \quad y_B = 0.50, \quad y_C = 0.25 $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** A common error is **not imposing the upper limit** on $\xi$ (i.e., $\xi \leq n_{A,0} = 2$ for this system). Always check physical feasibility ‚ÄĒ a negative mole count is *impossible* and indicates a stoichiometric/algebraic mistake.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Core Identity:** $n_i = n_{i,0} + \nu_i \, \xi$ (single reaction); $n_i = n_{i,0} + \sum_j \nu_{i,j} \xi_j$ (multiple reactions).
- **Sign Convention (IUPAC):** Reactants ‚Üí $\nu_i < 0$; Products ‚Üí $\nu_i > 0$.
- **SI Unit:** $\xi$ is in **moles (kmol)**. Never dimensionless.
- **Total Moles:** $n_{tot} = n_{tot,0} + \Delta\nu \cdot \xi$, where $\Delta\nu = \sum_i \nu_i$.
- **Mole Fractions in Reacting Mixture:** $y_i = (n_{i,0} + \nu_i \xi) \,/\, (n_{tot,0} + \Delta\nu \, \xi)$.
- **Equilibrium Tie-In:** At $\xi = \xi_{eq}$, $\Delta G_{T,P} = 0$ ‚Üí $\xi_{eq}$ is found from the equilibrium constant expression $K = \prod y_i^{\nu_i}$ (or $K_p$, $K_\phi$ forms).
- **Multi-Reaction:** Each *independent* reaction contributes **one** $\xi_j$, hence $R$ simultaneous reactions require $R$ coupled equilibrium equations.
- **Conversion Link:** For limiting reactant, $X = \xi / n_{limiting,0}$.
- **Pressure Effect:** If $\Delta\nu = 0$, pressure does not shift $K$-based equilibrium (Le Chatelier).
- **Physical Bound:** $0 \leq \xi \leq \min_i \left( n_{i,0} / \nu_i \right)$ ‚ÄĒ enforce this to discard extraneous mathematical roots.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equilibrium Constant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Equilibrium Constant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Equilibrium Constant ($K$)** is a dimensionless thermodynamic quantity that characterizes the position of chemical equilibrium for a reversible reaction at a specified temperature. For a general reversible reaction:

$$
aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD
$$

the equilibrium constant is rigorously defined through the standard-state Gibbs free energy change:

$$
\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K
$$

where $\Delta G^{\circ}$ is the **standard Gibbs energy change** of reaction (in J/mol), $R = 8.314$ **J/(mol¬∑K)** is the universal gas constant, and $T$ is the absolute temperature in **K**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The equilibrium constant is ALWAYS expressed as a function of **activities** (not concentrations) to make it truly dimensionless and independent of the choice of standard state. It is strictly a function of temperature only ‚ÄĒ NOT of pressure, composition, or catalyst presence.

### 1.2 Types of Equilibrium Constants

In Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics, you must master four related forms of $K$:

| Form | Symbol | Expression for $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$ | Reference Standard State |
|---|---|---|---|
| Activity-based | $K$ | $\dfrac{a_C^c \, a_D^d}{a_A^a \, a_B^b}$ | Pure substance / 1 bar / 1 molal |
| Pressure-based | $K_p$ | $\dfrac{p_C^c \, p_D^d}{p_A^a \, p_B^b}$ | 1 bar ideal gas partial pressure |
| Concentration-based | $K_c$ | $\dfrac{[C]^c [D]^d}{[A]^a [B]^b}$ | 1 mol/L concentration |
| Mole fraction-based | $K_y$ | $\dfrac{y_C^c \, y_D^d}{y_A^a \, y_B^b}$ | Pure component ($y = 1$) |
| Fugacity-based | $K_\phi$ | $\dfrac{\phi_C^c \, \phi_D^d}{\phi_A^a \, \phi_B^b}$ | Fugacity coefficient correction |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Crowded Staircase"

Imagine two escalators (forward and reverse reactions) carrying people up and down a staircase. Initially, the "up" escalator is packed (reactants dominate). As people reach the top, they switch to the "down" escalator. Eventually, the **number of people on each escalator stabilizes** ‚ÄĒ this stable ratio is the equilibrium constant. Push the temperature (like opening windows in the room): the **balance shifts**, but the *ratio* itself is the equilibrium constant's signature for that temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A **large $K$ ($K \gg 1$)** means products are favored at equilibrium (the reaction "lies to the right"). A **small $K$ ($K \ll 1$)** means reactants are favored. $K = 1$ indicates comparable amounts of reactants and products.

### 1.4 Standard-State Conventions (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **gases**: Standard state is the **pure ideal gas at 1 bar** (and the fugacity standard state is 1 bar regardless of the real gas state). Activity $a_i = f_i / f_i^{\circ} = \phi_i y_i P / P^{\circ}$.
> For **liquids/solids**: Standard state is the **pure liquid or pure solid** at 1 bar. Activity = mole fraction (assuming ideal solution).
> For **solvents**: Activity $\approx 1$ (Raoult's law reference).
> For **solutes (Henry's law ref.)**: Activity = molality.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundation: From $\Delta G$ to $K$

The starting point is the fundamental criterion for chemical reaction equilibrium. For any reaction at state $(T, P)$ with chemical potentials $\mu_i$:

$$
\Delta G = \sum_i \nu_i \mu_i
$$

where $\nu_i$ is the stoichiometric number (positive for products, negative for reactants). Substituting $\mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln a_i$:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln \prod_i a_i^{\nu_i}
$$

At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$ (the system has no driving force for further reaction). Defining:

$$
Q = \prod_i a_i^{\nu_i} \quad \text{(reaction quotient)}
$$

At equilibrium, $Q_{eq} = K$, giving:

$$
\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K
$$

This is the **master equation** that links equilibrium to pure thermodynamics.

### 2.2 Why is $K$ Dimensionless?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When $K$ is expressed using activities (dimensionless by definition of activity), the resulting equilibrium constant is strictly dimensionless. When $K_p$ or $K_c$ are written in terms of partial pressures (bar) or molarities (mol/L), the numerical value depends on the choice of standard-state pressure/concentration. KTU questions frequently test whether you correctly normalize pressures by 1 bar or concentrations by 1 mol/L.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet ‚ÄĒ Master Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Master definition | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ | Always valid at constant $T$ |
| Activity definition | $K = \prod_i a_i^{\nu_i}$ | Dimensionless form |
| Gas-phase activity | $a_i = \dfrac{\phi_i y_i P}{P^{\circ}}$ | $P^{\circ} = 1$ bar |
| Ideal gas activity | $a_i = \dfrac{y_i P}{1 \text{ bar}}$ | $\phi_i = 1$ |
| Pure condensed phase | $a_i = 1$ | Solid or pure liquid reference |
| $K$ vs $K_p$ (ideal gas) | $K = K_p \cdot (P^{\circ})^{-\Delta \nu}$ | $\Delta \nu = \sum \nu_i$ |
| $K_p$ vs $K_c$ | $K_p = K_c (RT)^{\Delta \nu}$ | Ideal gas only |
| $K$ vs $K_y$ (ideal gas) | $K = K_y \cdot (P/P^{\circ})^{\Delta \nu}$ | Total pressure effect |
| van't Hoff equation | $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^2}$ | Constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$ |
| Integrated van't Hoff | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | For constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$ |
| Le Chatelier via $\Delta H$ | Exothermic ($\Delta H < 0$): $K \downarrow$ as $T \uparrow$ | van't Hoff qualitative |
| Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz form | $\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{T} = -\!R \ln K$ | Useful for plotting |
| Real-gas correction | $K = K_\phi \cdot K_y \cdot (P/P^{\circ})^{\Delta \nu}$ | $K_\phi$ corrects non-ideality |
| Heterogeneous rule | Pure solids/liquids: activity $= 1$, omit from $K$ | Example: $C_{(s)}$ omitted |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU pitfall:** Forgetting to include the term $(P^{\circ})^{-\Delta \nu}$ when converting between $K$ and $K_p$ is the #1 reason students lose marks on gas-phase equilibrium problems. Always remember to **normalize by 1 bar**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ammonia synthesis (Haber‚ÄďBosch):** $N_2 + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3$. Operating at high $P$ ($\sim$200 bar) and low $T$ ($\sim$700 K) is chosen precisely because $\Delta \nu < 0$ (favors product) and the reaction is exothermic.
- **Methanol synthesis, sulfuric acid contact process, reformer design** all use $K_p(T)$ data from van't Hoff integration.
- **Petroleum refining:** Catalytic cracking, alkylation, and isomerization equilibria are designed using these equations.
- **Pharmaceutical crystallization:** Uses solubility-product ($K_{sp}$) equilibria to control polymorphism.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From $\Delta G$ to $K$ (Master Equation)

**Starting point:** Gibbs energy of reaction at arbitrary state.

$$
\Delta G = \sum_i \nu_i \mu_i
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the fundamental equation for chemical potential.

$$
\mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ}(T) + RT \ln a_i
$$

where $\mu_i^{\circ}(T)$ is the chemical potential of species $i$ in its standard state at temperature $T$.

**Step 2:** Split the sum into standard and non-standard contributions.

$$
\Delta G = \sum_i \nu_i \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \sum_i \nu_i \ln a_i
$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the first sum as $\Delta G^{\circ}(T)$.

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \sum_i \nu_i \ln a_i
$$

**Step 4:** Combine the logarithms using the exponent rule.

$$
\sum_i \nu_i \ln a_i = \ln \prod_i a_i^{\nu_i} = \ln Q
$$

**Step 5:** Write the reaction quotient form.

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q
$$

**Step 6:** Apply the equilibrium condition $\Delta G = 0$ (no further driving force).

$$
0 = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q_{eq}
$$

**Step 7:** Define the equilibrium constant as $K \equiv Q_{eq}$.

$$
\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K
$$

**Result:** The master equation relating thermodynamic state functions to chemical equilibrium. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between $K$, $K_p$, $K_y$, and $K_c$ for Ideal Gases

Consider the ideal-gas reaction: $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$.

**Step 1:** Write the activity-based equilibrium constant.

$$
K = \prod_i a_i^{\nu_i} = \frac{a_C^c \, a_D^d}{a_A^a \, a_B^b}
$$

**Step 2:** For ideal gases, $a_i = p_i / P^{\circ} = y_i P / P^{\circ}$ where $P^{\circ} = 1$ bar.

$$
K = \frac{(y_C P / P^{\circ})^c (y_D P / P^{\circ})^d}{(y_A P / P^{\circ})^a (y_B P / P^{\circ})^b}
$$

**Step 3:** Group the $P / P^{\circ}$ terms separately from the mole-fraction terms.

$$
K = \frac{y_C^c \, y_D^d}{y_A^a \, y_B^b} \cdot \left(\frac{P}{P^{\circ}}\right)^{(c+d)-(a+b)}
$$

**Step 4:** Define $\Delta \nu = (c + d) - (a + b)$ and $K_y = \dfrac{y_C^c \, y_D^d}{y_A^a \, y_B^b}$.

$$
\boxed{\,K = K_y \left(\frac{P}{P^{\circ}}\right)^{\Delta \nu}\,}
$$

**Step 5:** Define $K_p = \dfrac{p_C^c \, p_D^d}{p_A^a \, p_B^b}$. Note that $K_p$ has units of bar$^{\Delta \nu}$. The dimensionless form is $K_p / (P^{\circ})^{\Delta \nu}$.

$$
K = \frac{K_p}{(P^{\circ})^{\Delta \nu}}
$$

**Step 6:** Use ideal gas law: $p_i = c_i RT$, so $c_i = p_i / RT$, and $\sum c_i = P / RT$.

$$
K_c = \frac{[C]^c [D]^d}{[A]^a [B]^b} = \frac{(p_C/RT)^c (p_D/RT)^d}{(p_A/RT)^a (p_B/RT)^b} = \frac{K_p}{(RT)^{\Delta \nu}}
$$

**Step 7:** Combine to get the conversion chain.

$$
K = K_p (P^{\circ})^{-\Delta \nu} = K_c (RT / P^{\circ})^{\Delta \nu} = K_y (P / P^{\circ})^{\Delta \nu}
$$

**Special case:** If $\Delta \nu = 0$ (equimolar reaction), then $K = K_p = K_c = K_y$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: van't Hoff Equation (Temperature Dependence of $K$)

**Starting point:** The Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation.

$$
\frac{d(\Delta G^{\circ}/T)}{dT}\bigg|_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^2}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$.

$$
\frac{d(-RT \ln K / T)}{dT}\bigg|_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^2}
$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the left-hand side. The $-R$ is constant; $\ln K / T$ remains.

$$
-R \frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^2}
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply both sides by $-1$.

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^2}\,}
$$

This is the **van't Hoff equation**. It tells us:
- If $\Delta H^{\circ} > 0$ (endothermic): $K$ **increases** with $T$.
- If $\Delta H^{\circ} < 0$ (exothermic): $K$ **decreases** with $T$.

**Step 4 (Integration):** If $\Delta H^{\circ}$ is approximately constant over the temperature range of interest:

$$
\int_{K_1}^{K_2} d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}
$$

$$
\ln \frac{K_2}{K_1} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)
$$

**Step 5 (Temperature-dependent $\Delta H^{\circ}$):** Use Kirchhoff's law $\Delta H^{\circ}(T) = \Delta H^{\circ}_{298} + \int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT$, substitute into van't Hoff, and integrate. For most KTU problems, constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$ suffices. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** For the reaction $N_2O_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 NO_2(g)$ at 298 K, $\Delta G^{\circ} = +4740$ J/mol. Calculate (a) $K_p$ at 298 K, (b) $\Delta G^{\circ}$ at 350 K given $\Delta H^{\circ} = +57.20$ kJ/mol, and (c) the new $K_p$ at 350 K.

**Solution (a):**

$$
\ln K = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{RT} = -\frac{4740}{(8.314)(298)} = -1.9132
$$

$$
K = e^{-1.9132} = 0.1476
$$

Since this is a gas-phase equilibrium, $K = K_p$ when expressed with standard-state pressures (here bar$^{\Delta \nu}$, $\Delta \nu = 1$, so $K_p$ has units of bar; in dimensionless form the value is 0.1476).

**Solution (b):**

$$
\Delta G^{\circ}_{350} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T \Delta S^{\circ}
$$

Find $\Delta S^{\circ}$ from the 298 K data:

$$
\Delta S^{\circ} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ} - \Delta G^{\circ}}{T} = \frac{57200 - 4740}{298} = 176.24 \; \text{J/(mol¬∑K)}
$$

$$
\Delta G^{\circ}_{350} = 57200 - (350)(176.24) = 57200 - 61684 = -4484 \; \text{J/mol}
$$

**Solution (c):**

$$
\ln K_{350} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{350}}{RT} = -\frac{-4484}{(8.314)(350)} = 1.5414
$$

$$
K_{350} = e^{1.5414} = 4.671
$$

**Sanity check (van't Hoff):**

$$
\ln \frac{K_{350}}{K_{298}} = -\frac{57200}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{350} - \frac{1}{298}\right) = -6879.7 \times (-0.0004983) = 3.428
$$

$$
K_{350}/K_{298} = e^{3.428} = 30.81 \Rightarrow K_{350} = 4.547 \approx 4.671
$$

(Small discrepancy from rounding ‚ÄĒ within acceptable error.) $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Thermodynamic Pathway from Gibbs Energy to Equilibrium Constant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Chemical Reaction at State T,P"]
    node2["Total Gibbs Energy: dG = sum nu_i d mu_i"]
    node3["Equilibrium Criterion: Delta G = 0"]
    node4["Split: Delta G = Delta G deg + RT ln Q"]
    node5["At Equilibrium, Q = K (definition)"]
    node6["Master Equation: Delta G deg = -RT ln K"]
    node7["Activity Form: K = prod a_i nu_i"]
    node8["Ideal Gas Form: a_i = p_i / P deg"]
    node9["Mole Fraction Form: K_y = y_C^c y_D^d / y_A^a y_B^b"]
    node10["Concentration Form: K_c = C_C^c C_D^d / C_A^a C_B^b"]
    node11["Van't Hoff: d ln K / dT = Delta H deg / R T squared"]
    node12["Equilibrium Composition Calculator"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node7 --> node9
    node7 --> node10
    node6 --> node11
    node7 --> node12
    node8 --> node12
    node9 --> node12
    node10 --> node12
    node11 --> node12
```

```mermaid
    nodeA["K (dimensionless, activity-based)"]
    nodeB["K_p (partial pressures, bar^delta nu)"]
    nodeC["K_c (molarities, mol per L^delta nu)"]

    nodeE["P/P deg (total pressure term)"]
    nodeF["R T / P deg (gas constant term)"]
    nodeG["(RT)^delta nu (ideal gas conversion)"]

    nodeA -->|"K = K_p / P deg^delta nu"| nodeB
    nodeB -->|"K_p = K_c (RT)^delta nu"| nodeC
    nodeC -->|"K_c RT = K_y P"| nodeD
    nodeD -->|"K_y = K / P/P deg^delta nu"| nodeA
    nodeB --- nodeE
    nodeC --- nodeF
    nodeB --- nodeG
```

```mermaid
    nodeKtable["Step 4: Look up K (function of T only) from Delta G deg of formation"]
    nodeEq["Step 5: Solve K = K_phi K_y P by P deg^delta nu for y_i"]
```
