## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]** Define the equilibrium constant $K$ for a chemical reaction. Why is it always written in terms of activities and not concentrations?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The equilibrium constant $K$ for the reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$ is defined as the value of the reaction quotient $Q$ at the equilibrium state:
$$
K \equiv \prod_i a_i^{\nu_i} = \frac{a_C^c \, a_D^d}{a_A^a \, a_B^b}
$$
It is defined in terms of **activities** rather than concentrations because activities are dimensionless and reduce to the proper thermodynamic reference for any phase. The chemical potential $\mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \ln a_i$ requires the activity for the equilibrium condition $\Delta G = 0$ to be valid universally across gases, liquids, and solids. Using concentrations would make $K$ depend on the choice of standard state and limit its thermodynamic rigor. **(3 marks: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for rationale.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ June 2024]** State the van't Hoff equation and explain qualitatively how the equilibrium constant changes with temperature for (i) an exothermic reaction and (ii) an endothermic reaction.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The van't Hoff equation is:
$$
\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^2}
$$
**(i)** For an **exothermic** reaction ($\Delta H^{\circ} < 0$), the right-hand side is negative, so $\ln K$ decreases as $T$ increases. Hence, $K$ **decreases** with temperature (Le Chatelier: heat is a "product" and adding heat shifts equilibrium backward).
**(ii)** For an **endothermic** reaction ($\Delta H^{\circ} > 0$), the right-hand side is positive, so $\ln K$ increases with $T$. Hence, $K$ **increases** with temperature (heat is a "reactant"). **(3 marks: 1 for equation + 1 each for parts i and ii.)**

---

### Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Module Rule:** "Choice is permitted ONLY between sub-parts (a) or (b); both sub-parts cover the SAME module." For full 14-mark questions, both sub-parts are answered.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024]*

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the equilibrium constant $K$ and the standard Gibbs free energy change $\Delta G^{\circ}$. Show that $K$ is a function of temperature only. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the gas-phase reaction $2SO_2(g) + O_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2SO_3(g)$, the standard Gibbs energy change at 1000 K is $\Delta G^{\circ}_{1000} = -89.6$ kJ/mol. Assuming ideal-gas behaviour, calculate:
  (i) The equilibrium constant $K$ at 1000 K. **(2 marks)**
  (ii) The equilibrium constant $K_p$ in bar units at 1000 K. **(2 marks)**
  (iii) The composition at equilibrium if initially 2 mol $SO_2$ and 1 mol $O_2$ are present at a total pressure of 1 bar. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Start from the Gibbs energy change for any reaction:

$$
\Delta G = \sum_i \nu_i \mu_i
$$

Substitute $\mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ}(T) + RT \ln a_i$:

$$
\Delta G = \sum_i \nu_i \mu_i^{\circ} + RT \sum_i \nu_i \ln a_i = \Delta G^{\circ}(T) + RT \ln \prod_i a_i^{\nu_i}
$$

Define the reaction quotient $Q = \prod_i a_i^{\nu_i}$. At equilibrium, $\Delta G_{rxn} = 0$:

$$
0 = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln K \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K}
$$

Since $\Delta G^{\circ}$ depends only on $T$ (and reference pressure, which is fixed), $K$ is a **function of temperature alone**. It does NOT depend on pressure, total composition, or catalysts. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

For the reaction $2SO_2 + O_2 \rightleftharpoons 2SO_3$: $\Delta \nu = 2 - (2 + 1) = -1$.

**(i) $K$ at 1000 K (2 marks):**

$$
\ln K = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{RT} = -\frac{-89600}{(8.314)(1000)} = 10.778
$$

$$
K = e^{10.778} = 47926 \approx 4.79 \times 10^{4}
$$

**(ii) $K_p$ in bar units (2 marks):**

For ideal gas, $K = K_p \cdot (P^{\circ})^{-\Delta \nu} = K_p \cdot (1 \text{ bar})^{-(-1)} = K_p \cdot (1 \text{ bar})^{1}$ (since $\Delta \nu = -1$).

So $K_p = K / (1 \text{ bar})^{\Delta \nu} = 47926 \times (1 \text{ bar})^{1} = 4.79 \times 10^{4}$ bar.

Equivalently, $K_p$ has units of bar$^{-1}$ (because $\Delta \nu = -1$). Numerically, $K_p = 4.79 \times 10^{4}$ bar$^{-1}$. **(2 marks)**

**(iii) Equilibrium composition at $P = 1$ bar (3 marks):**

Initial moles: $n_{SO_2,0} = 2$, $n_{O_2,0} = 1$, $n_{SO_3,0} = 0$.

Let $\xi$ = extent of reaction. At equilibrium:
- $n_{SO_2} = 2 - 2\xi$
- $n_{O_2} = 1 - \xi$
- $n_{SO_3} = 2\xi$
- $n_{total} = 3 - \xi$

Mole fractions: $y_i = n_i / n_{total}$. Partial pressures: $p_i = y_i P = y_i$ (bar, since $P = 1$ bar).

Substitute into $K_p$ expression:

$$
K_p = \frac{p_{SO_3}^2}{p_{SO_2}^2 \, p_{O_2}} = \frac{(2\xi/(3-\xi))^2}{((2-2\xi)/(3-\xi))^2 \, ((1-\xi)/(3-\xi))}
$$

$$
K_p = \frac{4\xi^2 (3-\xi)}{4(1-\xi)^2 (1-\xi)} = \frac{\xi^2 (3-\xi)}{(1-\xi)^3}
$$

Setting $K_p = 4.79 \times 10^{4}$ bar$^{-1}$:

$$
\frac{\xi^2 (3-\xi)}{(1-\xi)^3} = 4.79 \times 10^{4}
$$

Solving iteratively (note $K$ is large, so $\xi \to 1$): Try $\xi = 0.99$:
$$
\frac{(0.99)^2 (2.01)}{(0.01)^3} = \frac{1.970}{10^{-6}} = 1.97 \times 10^{6} \quad \text{(too high)}
$$

Try $\xi = 0.95$:
$$
\frac{(0.95)^2 (2.05)}{(0.05)^3} = \frac{1.850}{1.25 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.48 \times 10^{4} \quad \text{(too low)}
$$

Try $\xi = 0.97$:
$$
\frac{(0.97)^2 (2.03)}{(0.03)^3} = \frac{1.910}{2.7 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.07 \times 10^{4} \quad \text{(slightly high)}
$$

Try $\xi = 0.965$:
$$
\frac{(0.965)^2 (2.035)}{(0.035)^3} = \frac{1.895}{4.29 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.42 \times 10^{4} \quad \text{(close)}
$$

Try $\xi = 0.966$:
$$
\frac{(0.966)^2 (2.034)}{(0.034)^3} = \frac{1.897}{3.93 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.83 \times 10^{4} \quad \text{(match)}
$$

So $\xi \approx 0.966$.

Equilibrium moles:
- $n_{SO_2} = 2 - 2(0.966) = 0.068$ mol
- $n_{O_2} = 1 - 0.966 = 0.034$ mol
- $n_{SO_3} = 2(0.966) = 1.932$ mol

Conversion of $SO_2 = 0.966/1 = 96.6\%$. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Derive the van't Hoff equation starting from the Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation. Using it, show that for an exothermic reaction $K$ decreases with increasing temperature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the water-gas shift reaction $CO(g) + H_2O(g) \rightleftharpoons CO_2(g) + H_2(g)$, $K_p = 4.12$ at 1000 K. A stoichiometric mixture (1 mol CO + 1 mol H‚āāO) is brought to equilibrium at 1 bar total pressure.
  (i) Calculate the equilibrium mole fractions of all species. **(4 marks)**
  (ii) If the total pressure is increased to 10 bar, what will be the new equilibrium composition? Explain using Le Chatelier's principle. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the **Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation** applied to the reaction:

$$
\frac{d(\Delta G^{\circ}/T)}{dT}\bigg|_P = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^2}
$$

Substitute $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$:

$$
\frac{d(-R \ln K)}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^2}
$$

Since $R$ is constant:

$$
-R \frac{d \ln K}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^2}\,}
$$

For an **exothermic reaction**, $\Delta H^{\circ} < 0$, so $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} < 0$. As $T$ increases, $\ln K$ decreases, hence $K$ **decreases** with increasing temperature. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Note: This is an **equimolar reaction** ($\Delta \nu = 0$), so $K_p$ is independent of pressure (dimensionless, $K = K_p$).

**(i) Equilibrium at 1 bar (4 marks):**

Let $\xi$ = extent. Initially $n_{CO} = 1$, $n_{H_2O} = 1$, $n_{CO_2} = 0$, $n_{H_2} = 0$.
At equilibrium: $n_{CO} = 1 - \xi$, $n_{H_2O} = 1 - \xi$, $n_{CO_2} = \xi$, $n_{H_2} = \xi$.
Total moles $n_{tot} = 2$ (unchanged because $\Delta \nu = 0$).

Since $P = 1$ bar, partial pressure $p_i = y_i \cdot 1$ bar $= y_i$ (in bar, with $P^{\circ} = 1$ bar).

Mole fractions: $y_{CO} = y_{H_2O} = (1 - \xi)/2$; $y_{CO_2} = y_{H_2} = \xi / 2$.

$$
K_p = \frac{(p_{CO_2})(p_{H_2})}{(p_{CO})(p_{H_2O})} = \frac{y_{CO_2} \, y_{H_2}}{y_{CO} \, y_{H_2O}}
$$

(The bar units cancel because $K_p$ is dimensionless when $\Delta \nu = 0$.)

$$
K_p = \frac{(\xi/2)(\xi/2)}{((1-\xi)/2)((1-\xi)/2)} = \frac{\xi^2}{(1-\xi)^2} = 4.12
$$

Taking the square root:

$$
\frac{\xi}{1 - \xi} = \sqrt{4.12} = 2.0298
$$

$$
\xi = 2.0298(1 - \xi) = 2.0298 - 2.0298\xi
$$

$$
\xi(1 + 2.0298) = 2.0298 \Rightarrow \xi = \frac{2.0298}{3.0298} = 0.6700
$$

Equilibrium mole fractions:
- $y_{CO} = y_{H_2O} = (1 - 0.67)/2 = 0.165$
- $y_{CO_2} = y_{H_2} = 0.67/2 = 0.335$

Check: $0.165 + 0.165 + 0.335 + 0.335 = 1.000$ ‚úď **(4 marks)**

**(ii) Effect of increasing pressure to 10 bar (3 marks):**

Since $\Delta \nu = 0$ for this reaction, the equilibrium constant expressed in terms of mole fractions satisfies:

$$
K_y = K_p \cdot \left(\frac{P^{\circ}}{P}\right)^{\Delta \nu} = K_p \cdot (1)^{0} = K_p = 4.12
$$

So **the equilibrium composition is unchanged at any pressure**. This is because the total number of moles does not change during the reaction ($\Delta \nu = 0$). Le Chatelier's principle predicts no shift in equilibrium when $\Delta \nu = 0$, regardless of pressure change. So $\xi = 0.67$ and mole fractions remain the same. **(3 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ‚ÄĒ Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize** partial pressures by $P^{\circ} = 1$ bar ‚ÄĒ losing up to 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Mixing up $\Delta \nu$ sign** in $K = K_y (P/P^{\circ})^{\Delta \nu}$. Always check: $2SO_2 + O_2 \to 2SO_3$ has $\Delta \nu = -1$, so $K = K_y / (P/P^{\circ})$.
> 3. **Including pure solids** (e.g., $C_{(s)}$ in Boudouard reaction) in the equilibrium constant expression. Pure solids and pure liquids have activity = 1 and MUST be omitted.
> 4. **Using $\Delta G$ instead of $\Delta G^{\circ}$** when applying $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$. The master equation uses ONLY standard-state Gibbs energy.
> 5. **Wrong sign** in van't Hoff: $\ln(K_2/K_1) = -(\Delta H^{\circ}/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$. Always verify with Le Chatelier.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Master equation:** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ ‚ÄĒ the cornerstone of all equilibrium calculations.
- **Activity definition:** $K = \prod a_i^{\nu_i}$ ‚ÄĒ always dimensionless; this is the only truly rigorous form.
- **For ideal gases:** $a_i = y_i P / P^{\circ}$ where $P^{\circ} = 1$ bar.
- **Conversion chain:** $K = K_p (P^{\circ})^{-\Delta \nu} = K_c (RT/P^{\circ})^{\Delta \nu} = K_y (P/P^{\circ})^{\Delta \nu}$.
- **$\Delta \nu = 0$ case:** $K = K_p = K_c = K_y$ ‚ÄĒ pressure has NO effect on equilibrium.
- **van't Hoff:** $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{RT^2}$ ‚ÄĒ sign of $\Delta H^{\circ}$ dictates trend with $T$.
- **Integrated form:** $\ln(K_2/K_1) = -(\Delta H^{\circ}/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ ‚ÄĒ valid for constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$.
- **Heterogeneous equilibria:** Omit pure solids and pure liquids from the $K$ expression.
- **Temperature-only dependence:** $K$ does NOT depend on pressure, initial composition, or catalysts.
- **Large $K$ ($K \gg 1$):** Products favored at equilibrium. **Small $K$ ($K \ll 1$):** Reactants favored.
- **Standard-state pressure:** Always use **1 bar** as $P^{\circ}$ (modern IUPAC convention).
- **Real-gas correction:** $K = K_\phi \cdot K_y \cdot (P/P^{\circ})^{\Delta \nu}$ where $K_\phi = \prod \phi_i^{\nu_i}$.
- **Industrial relevance:** Haber‚ÄďBosch, contact process, water-gas shift, methanol synthesis ‚ÄĒ all designed using $K_p(T)$ and Le Chatelier reasoning.
- **KTU trick questions:** Watch out for (a) $\Delta \nu$ sign errors, (b) including/excluding pure phases, (c) confusing $K$ with $K_p$, (d) forgetting standard-state normalization.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard Free Energy Change
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Standard Free Energy Change

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Free Energy Change (őĒG¬į)** is defined as the change in Gibbs free energy of a chemical reaction when all the reactants and products are in their **standard states** (pure substances at **1 bar** pressure for gases, **1 molal** ideal solution for solutes, and pure liquids/solids for condensed phases) at a **specified temperature**, conventionally reported at **298.15 K** unless otherwise stated.

Mathematically, for a general chemical reaction:

$$aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$$

The standard free energy change is expressed as:

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{rxn} = c \cdot \bar{G}^{\circ}_{C} + d \cdot \bar{G}^{\circ}_{D} - a \cdot \bar{G}^{\circ}_{A} - b \cdot \bar{G}^{\circ}_{B}$$

where $\bar{G}^{\circ}_{i}$ represents the **standard-state molar Gibbs free energy** (also called the **standard chemical potential**, $\mu_i^{\circ}$) of species *i*.

**Key Parameters:**
- **Standard pressure (P¬į):** **1 bar** = **100 kPa** (IUPAC, post-1982 convention)
- **Standard temperature (T):** **298.15 K** (25 ¬įC) ‚ÄĒ though őĒG¬į is a strong function of T
- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol¬∑K)**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Energy Currency" of Reactions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of **Standard Free Energy Change (őĒG¬į)** as the **"spontaneous reaction potential"** ‚ÄĒ just like **voltage** is to electricity, őĒG¬į is to chemistry.

**Real-world Analogy ‚ÄĒ A Ball on a Hill:**
Imagine a ball sitting on a hill. The **height of the hill** represents the **Gibbs free energy** of the system.
- A ball at the **top of a high hill** has **high potential energy** (unstable, like high-energy reactants).
- A ball at the **bottom** has **low potential energy** (stable, like low-energy products).
- The **difference in height** between the top and bottom is the **standard free energy change (őĒG¬į)**.

If őĒG¬į < 0 (ball rolls down): the reaction is **spontaneous** under standard conditions.
If őĒG¬į > 0 (ball would have to be pushed up): the reaction is **non-spontaneous**.
If őĒG¬į = 0 (ball on a flat plateau): the system is at **equilibrium**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Standard free energy change is the **gateway quantity** that links **thermodynamics** (őĒG¬į) with **equilibrium** (K). It tells engineers *whether* a reaction *can* proceed and *how far* it will go before reaching equilibrium.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Standard Free Energy

The standard free energy change unifies two other key thermodynamic quantities via the **Gibbs fundamental equation**:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T \Delta S^{\circ}$$

This single equation ‚ÄĒ sometimes called the **"thermodynamic holy trinity"** ‚ÄĒ connects:
- **Enthalpy change (őĒH¬į)** ‚ÄĒ the *heat* released or absorbed
- **Entropy change (őĒS¬į)** ‚ÄĒ the *disorder* created or destroyed
- **Temperature (T)** ‚ÄĒ the *amplifier* of the entropy term

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** őĒH¬į is the *what* (energy input/output), őĒS¬į is the *why* (disorder), and T¬∑őĒS¬į is the *how strongly* entropy drives the reaction.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Standard Free Energy of Formation (őĒG¬įf)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Standard Free Energy of Formation (őĒG¬įf)** of a compound is the change in Gibbs free energy when **1 mole** of the compound is formed from its constituent elements in their **most stable form** (reference states) at standard conditions.

By convention: **őĒG¬įf of every element in its standard state = 0** (similar to őĒH¬įf).

The standard free energy change of any reaction is computed as:

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{rxn} = \sum_{i} \nu_i \cdot \Delta G^{\circ}_{f,i} \text{(products)} - \sum_{j} \nu_j \cdot \Delta G^{\circ}_{f,j} \text{(reactants)}$$

where $\nu_i$ are the **stoichiometric coefficients** (positive for products, negative for reactants).

## 2.2 The Master Link ‚ÄĒ őĒG¬į and the Equilibrium Constant

The most powerful result in chemical thermodynamics ‚ÄĒ and the one **most heavily tested in KTU exams** ‚ÄĒ is the relation:

$$\boxed{\Delta G^{\circ} = -R T \ln K}$$

Equivalently:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -2.303 \, R T \log_{10} K$$

**Interpretation Matrix:**

| Condition | őĒG¬į | K | Spontaneity |
|-----------|-----|---|-------------|
| Strongly exergonic | Large negative | K >> 1 | Spontaneous, products favored |
| Mildly exergonic | Small negative | K > 1 (modest) | Spontaneous at standard state |
| Equilibrium | őĒG¬į = 0 | K = 1 | No net reaction |
| Mildly endergonic | Small positive | K < 1 (modest) | Non-spontaneous at standard state |
| Strongly endergonic | Large positive | K << 1 | Non-spontaneous, reactants favored |

## 2.3 Temperature Dependence ‚ÄĒ The Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz Equation

The variation of őĒG¬į/T with temperature is given by the **Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation**:

$$\frac{d(\Delta G^{\circ}/T)}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}$$

Integrating (assuming őĒH¬į is constant over the temperature range):

$$\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_2}{T_2} - \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_1}{T_1} = \Delta H^{\circ} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)$$

This is the **engineer's "lever"** for shifting reaction feasibility by changing temperature.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table** ‚ÄĒ these are the **7 most-tested equations** for Standard Free Energy Change problems in KTU ESE.

| # | Equation | Use Case | Units |
|---|----------|----------|-------|
| 1 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -R T \ln K$ | KTU Board favorite: link őĒG¬į to K | J/mol, R in J/(mol¬∑K) |
| 2 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -2.303 R T \log_{10} K$ | Base-10 version of (1) | J/mol |
| 3 | $\Delta G^{\circ}_{rxn} = \sum \nu_i \Delta G^{\circ}_{f,i} (\text{products}) - \sum \nu_i \Delta G^{\circ}_{f,i} (\text{reactants})$ | Calculate őĒG¬įrxn from tables | kJ/mol |
| 4 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T \Delta S^{\circ}$ | Compute őĒG¬į at any T (if őĒH¬į, őĒS¬į given) | kJ/mol |
| 5 | $\Delta G^{\circ}_2 / T_2 - \Delta G^{\circ}_1 / T_1 = \Delta H^{\circ}(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ | őĒG¬į at different T (Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz) | kJ/mol |
| 6 | $\ln(K_2/K_1) = -(\Delta H^{\circ}/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ | Van't Hoff equation (K at different T) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + R T \ln Q$ | Non-standard conditions (reaction quotient Q) | J/mol |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These equations are the *backbone* of **chemical reactor design**, **ammonia synthesis (Haber process)**, **methanol synthesis**, **petroleum refining (FCC)**, and **biomass gasification**. Industrial chemists use them daily to pick the **operating temperature and pressure** for maximum product yield.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: őĒG¬į = ‚ÄďRT ln K (The Foundation Equation)

**Starting Point:** The fundamental equation of chemical equilibrium at any condition:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + R T \ln Q$$

**Row 1 (Textual logic):** At **chemical equilibrium**, the **net change in Gibbs free energy is zero** (the system has reached its minimum free energy state), and the **reaction quotient Q equals the equilibrium constant K**.

**Row 2 (Mathematical substitution):** Setting $\Delta G = 0$ (equilibrium) and $Q = K$:

$$0 = \Delta G^{\circ} + R T \ln K$$

**Row 3 (Algebraic rearrangement):** Isolating őĒG¬į on one side:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -R T \ln K$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G^{\circ} = -R T \ln K \equiv -2.303 \, R T \log_{10} K}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Standard Free Energy from Formation Values

**Row 1 (Textual logic):** The standard Gibbs free energy of reaction is a **state function** ‚ÄĒ it depends only on the initial and final states, not the path. Hence, it can be computed as a difference of formation values, weighted by stoichiometric coefficients.

**Row 2 (Formal statement):** For the reaction $aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$:

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{rxn} = c \cdot \bar{G}^{\circ}_{C} + d \cdot \bar{G}^{\circ}_{D} - a \cdot \bar{G}^{\circ}_{A} - b \cdot \bar{G}^{\circ}_{B}$$

**Row 3 (Relating to formation energies):** Since the standard molar Gibbs energy of a compound is the sum of its constituent elemental formation energies, this becomes:

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{rxn} = \sum_i \nu_i \cdot \Delta G^{\circ}_{f,i}$$

where $\nu_i$ is **positive for products** and **negative for reactants**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz Equation (őĒG¬į vs T)

**Row 1 (Textual logic):** The Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation gives the **temperature dependence of őĒG¬į** and is essential when the KTU problem gives you őĒG¬į at one temperature and asks for it at another.

**Row 2 (Starting point ‚ÄĒ the differential form):**

$$\frac{d(\Delta G^{\circ}/T)}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}$$

**Row 3 (Integrate from $T_1$ to $T_2$):**

$$\int_{T_1}^{T_2} d\left(\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{T}\right) = -\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}} dT$$

**Row 4 (Assume őĒH¬į is constant over [T‚āĀ, T‚āā]):**

$$\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_2}{T_2} - \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_1}{T_1} = -\Delta H^{\circ} \left[ -\frac{1}{T} \right]_{T_1}^{T_2}$$

**Row 5 (Final integrated form):**

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_2}{T_2} - \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_1}{T_1} = \Delta H^{\circ} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Van't Hoff Equation (K vs T)

**Row 1 (Textual logic):** Combining the Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation with $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$, we can derive how the **equilibrium constant K changes with temperature**.

**Row 2 (Substitute $\Delta G^{\circ}_i = -R T_i \ln K_i$ into Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz):**

$$\frac{-R T_2 \ln K_2}{T_2} - \frac{-R T_1 \ln K_1}{T_1} = \Delta H^{\circ} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)$$

**Row 3 (Simplify the left side ‚ÄĒ T cancels):**

$$-R \ln K_2 + R \ln K_1 = \Delta H^{\circ} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)$$

**Row 4 (Final form ‚ÄĒ the famous Van't Hoff equation):**

$$\boxed{\ln \left( \frac{K_2}{K_1} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "**Hot reactions** favor **small K for exothermic**" ‚ÄĒ if $\Delta H¬į < 0$ (exothermic), increasing T *decreases* K (Le Chatelier's principle confirmed mathematically).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** For the reaction $N_2(g) + 3H_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3(g)$, given:
- $\Delta G^{\circ}_{f}(NH_3) = -16.45$ kJ/mol
- $\Delta G^{\circ}_{f}(N_2) = 0$, $\Delta G^{\circ}_{f}(H_2) = 0$
- T = 298.15 K, R = 8.314 J/(mol¬∑K)

**Find:** őĒG¬į and K at 298 K.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Compute őĒG¬įrxn:**

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{rxn} = 2 \cdot \Delta G^{\circ}_{f}(NH_3) - [1 \cdot \Delta G^{\circ}_{f}(N_2) + 3 \cdot \Delta G^{\circ}_{f}(H_2)]$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{rxn} = 2(-16.45) - [0 + 0] = -32.90 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute K using őĒG¬į = ‚ÄďRT ln K:**

Convert őĒG¬į to J/mol: $\Delta G^{\circ} = -32900$ J/mol

$$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{R T} = -\frac{(-32900)}{(8.314)(298.15)}$$

$$\ln K = \frac{32900}{2478.8} = 13.27$$

$$K = e^{13.27} \approx 5.84 \times 10^{5}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Even though K is large at 298 K, the **rate** of ammonia synthesis is negligibly slow without a catalyst (this is why industrial Haber process runs at 400‚Äď500 ¬įC with an Fe catalyst and high pressure ‚ÄĒ a classic *thermodynamics vs. kinetics* trade-off).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map ‚ÄĒ Standard Free Energy Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Chemical Reaction: Reactants to Products] --> B[Thermodynamic State Function: G]
    B --> C{Reference State: 1 bar, 298.15 K}
    C --> D[Standard State Gibbs Energy: G deg]
    D --> E[Standard Free Energy Change: dG deg rxn]
    E --> F1[Compute from formation values: dG deg f]
    E --> F2[Compute from enthalpy and entropy: dH deg and dS deg]
    E --> F3[Link to equilibrium constant: K]
    F1 --> G[Numerical dG deg]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H{Spontaneity Decision}
    H -->|dG deg less than 0| I1[Spontaneous, K greater than 1]
    H -->|dG deg equals 0| I2[At Equilibrium, K equals 1]
    H -->|dG deg greater than 0| I3[Non Spontaneous, K less than 1]
    I1 --> J[Design Reactor: Maximize Conversion]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    end

    end

    Q2 --> Q3
    P3 --> Q4

```

## 4.3 Decision Flow ‚ÄĒ Spontaneity Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Calculate dG deg]) --> Calc[Compute dG deg rxn at T]
    Calc --> Q{dG deg value}
    Q -->|Negative Large| Sp[Spontaneous, Products Favored]
    Q -->|Negative Small| Sp2[Spontaneous but Modest]
    Q -->|Zero| Eq[At Equilibrium]
    Q -->|Positive Small| Nsp[Non Spontaneous, Reactants Favored]
    Q -->|Positive Large| Nsp2[Strongly Non Spontaneous]

    Eq --> End
    Nsp --> End
    Nsp2 --> End
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024 Model]**
> Define **standard free energy change** and state its **SI units**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Standard free energy change (őĒG¬į) of a reaction is the change in Gibbs free energy when the reaction proceeds with **all reactants and products in their standard states** (1 bar pressure, pure substances, 298.15 K) at a specified temperature.
- **SI units (1 mark):** **J/mol** (or kJ/mol in tabulated form).

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023 Model]**
> State the relation between standard free energy change and the **equilibrium constant**. What does a **negative őĒG¬į** imply about K?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Relation (2 marks):** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -R T \ln K = -2.303 R T \log_{10} K$
- **Negative őĒG¬į implication (1 mark):** $\Delta G^{\circ} < 0 \Rightarrow \ln K > 0 \Rightarrow K > 1$ ‚Üí the **forward reaction is spontaneous** at standard conditions, and **products are thermodynamically favored** at equilibrium.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ‚ÄĒ Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) ‚ÄĒ 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023 Module 4 Model]**
> **(a)** Derive the **Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation** relating the standard free energy change to temperature. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For the reaction $2SO_2(g) + O_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2SO_3(g)$ at 298 K, given:
> - $\Delta G^{\circ}_{f}(SO_2) = -300.4$ kJ/mol
> - $\Delta G^{\circ}_{f}(SO_3) = -370.5$ kJ/mol
> - $\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = -198.0$ kJ/mol
>
> Calculate **(i)** őĒG¬į at 298 K, **(ii)** the equilibrium constant K at 298 K, and **(iii)** őĒG¬į at 1000 K using the Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation of Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz Equation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Start from the definition of Gibbs free energy:
$$G = H - TS$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the standard-state change for a reaction at constant pressure:
$$\Delta G^{\circ} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T \Delta S^{\circ}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Divide both sides by T:
$$\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{T} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T} - \Delta S^{\circ}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Differentiate both sides with respect to T (treating őĒH¬į and őĒS¬į as functions of T, but assuming $\Delta C_p \approx 0$ for simplicity):
$$\frac{d}{dT}\left(\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{T}\right) = \frac{1}{T} \frac{d(\Delta H^{\circ})}{dT} - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}} - \frac{d(\Delta S^{\circ})}{dT}$$

Using the relations $\frac{d(\Delta H^{\circ})}{dT} = \Delta C_p$ and $\frac{d(\Delta S^{\circ})}{dT} = \frac{\Delta C_p}{T}$:

$$\frac{d}{dT}\left(\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{T}\right) = \frac{\Delta C_p}{T} - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}} - \frac{\Delta C_p}{T} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Final form and integration (assuming őĒH¬į is constant over T‚āĀ to T‚āā):
$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_2}{T_2} - \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_1}{T_1} = \Delta H^{\circ} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)}$$

---

#### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**(i) Calculate őĒG¬į at 298 K (2 marks):**

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = 2 \cdot \Delta G^{\circ}_{f}(SO_3) - \left[ 2 \cdot \Delta G^{\circ}_{f}(SO_2) + 1 \cdot \Delta G^{\circ}_{f}(O_2) \right]$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = 2(-370.5) - [2(-300.4) + 0]$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -741.0 - (-600.8) = -140.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**(ii) Calculate K at 298 K (2 marks):**

Using $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$, with R = 8.314 J/(mol¬∑K), T = 298 K, őĒG¬į = -140200 J/mol:

$$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{RT} = -\frac{(-140200)}{(8.314)(298)} = \frac{140200}{2477.6} = 56.58$$

$$K = e^{56.58} \approx 3.7 \times 10^{24}$$

**(iii) Calculate őĒG¬į at 1000 K (3 marks):**

Using the Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation:
$$\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_2}{T_2} - \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_1}{T_1} = \Delta H^{\circ} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)$$

$$\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{1000}}{1000} - \frac{-140200}{298} = -198000 \left( \frac{1}{1000} - \frac{1}{298} \right)$$

$$\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{1000}}{1000} + 470.47 = -198000 \left( 0.001 - 0.003356 \right)$$

$$\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{1000}}{1000} + 470.47 = -198000 \times (-0.002356)$$

$$\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{1000}}{1000} + 470.47 = 466.49$$

$$\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{1000}}{1000} = 466.49 - 470.47 = -3.98$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G^{\circ}_{1000} = -3.98 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** As T increases, őĒG¬į becomes **less negative** (from -140.2 to -3.98 kJ/mol), meaning the reaction is still spontaneous but **less product-favored** at 1000 K. This explains why SO‚āÉ production in contact process uses **moderate temperatures** (~700 K) ‚ÄĒ a balance between thermodynamics (favoring low T) and kinetics (favoring high T).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) ‚ÄĒ 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2023 Module 4 Model]**
> **(a)** Derive the relation $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ starting from the fundamental equation $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$. Explain the **physical significance** of the standard free energy change. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For the water-gas shift reaction $CO(g) + H_2O(g) \rightleftharpoons CO_2(g) + H_2(g)$ at 298 K, given:
> - $\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} = -41.2$ kJ/mol
> - $\Delta S^{\circ}_{rxn} = -42.4$ J/(mol¬∑K)
>
> Calculate: **(i)** őĒG¬į at 298 K, **(ii)** the equilibrium constant K, and **(iii)** the temperature at which $\Delta G^{\circ} = 0$ (the **crossover temperature**). **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation of őĒG¬į = ‚ÄďRT ln K (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Starting equation ‚ÄĒ the free energy change under non-standard conditions:
$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + R T \ln Q$$

where $Q = \dfrac{(P_{C}/P^{\circ})^c (P_{D}/P^{\circ})^d}{(P_{A}/P^{\circ})^a (P_{B}/P^{\circ})^b}$ is the reaction quotient (using activities/fugacities in general form).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** At **chemical equilibrium**, the system's Gibbs free energy is at its **minimum**, so the **net change is zero**:
$$\Delta G = 0$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Also, at equilibrium, the reaction quotient equals the **equilibrium constant**:
$$Q_{eq} = K$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substituting:
$$0 = \Delta G^{\circ} + R T \ln K$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G^{\circ} = -R T \ln K}$$

**Physical Significance (intertwined above):** őĒG¬į represents the **maximum non-expansion work** obtainable from a reaction under standard conditions, and its sign **predicts the direction of spontaneity** at standard state.

---

#### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**(i) Calculate őĒG¬į at 298 K (2 marks):**

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T \Delta S^{\circ}$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -41.2 \text{ kJ/mol} - (298 \text{ K})(-42.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kJ/(mol¬∑K)})$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -41.2 + 12.635 = -28.57 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**(ii) Calculate K at 298 K (2 marks):**

$$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{RT} = -\frac{(-28570)}{(8.314)(298)} = \frac{28570}{2477.6} = 11.53$$

$$K = e^{11.53} \approx 1.03 \times 10^{5}$$

**(iii) Calculate the crossover temperature (3 marks):**

At the crossover, $\Delta G^{\circ} = 0$:

$$0 = \Delta H^{\circ} - T_{cross} \cdot \Delta S^{\circ}$$

$$T_{cross} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{\Delta S^{\circ}} = \frac{-41.2 \text{ kJ/mol}}{-0.0424 \text{ kJ/(mol¬∑K)}}$$

$$\boxed{T_{cross} = 971.7 \text{ K} \approx 972 \text{ K}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Below 972 K, őĒG¬į < 0 (spontaneous); above 972 K, őĒG¬į > 0 (non-spontaneous). The industrial water-gas shift reactor is operated at **~700 K** with an Fe-Cr catalyst ‚ÄĒ below the crossover, ensuring thermodynamic favorability, while high enough for acceptable kinetics.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls ‚ÄĒ Read Before You Write!**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Disaster (loses 1‚Äď2 marks):** Forgetting to convert **kJ ‚Üí J** when using the equation $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$. R = 8.314 **J/(mol¬∑K)**, so őĒG¬į must be in **J/mol** for this formula. If you use kJ, you must use R = 8.314 √ó 10‚ĀĽ¬≥ kJ/(mol¬∑K).
>
> 2. **Sign Error in Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz (loses 2‚Äď3 marks):** The equation is $\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}_2}{T_2} - \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}_1}{T_1} = \Delta H^{\circ} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1} \right)$. Students frequently flip the bracket sign or swap T‚āĀ and T‚āā inconsistently.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Standard-State Convention (loses 1 mark):** őĒG¬į is defined at **1 bar**, not 1 atm (IUPAC post-1982). If a problem gives data in atm, convert: 1 atm = 1.01325 bar.
>
> 4. **Van't Hoff vs. Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz Confusion (loses 2 marks):** Van't Hoff uses **K** (equilibrium constant), Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz uses **őĒG¬į**. Mixing them = full mark loss on that part.
>
> 5. **őĒG¬įf of Elements:** Always **zero** for elements in their stable form at 298 K (N‚āā, O‚āā, H‚āā, C-graphite, S-rhombic, etc.). Forgetting this is a silent 1-mark killer.
>
> 6. **Temperature Units:** Always use **Kelvin** in $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$. Converting 25 ¬įC = 298.15 K (not 298 K exactly, but accepted).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist** ‚ÄĒ read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.

- **Definition of őĒG¬į:** Change in Gibbs free energy when reactants and products are at **1 bar, 298.15 K** (standard states).
- **Master Equation (1):** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ ‚ÄĒ links őĒG¬į to equilibrium constant.
- **Master Equation (2):** $\Delta G^{\circ} = \sum \nu_i \Delta G^{\circ}_f (\text{products}) - \sum \nu_i \Delta G^{\circ}_f (\text{reactants})$.
- **Master Equation (3):** $\Delta G^{\circ} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T \Delta S^{\circ}$.
- **Sign Convention:**
  - $\Delta G^{\circ} < 0$: reaction **spontaneous** in forward direction (K > 1).
  - $\Delta G^{\circ} > 0$: reaction **non-spontaneous** in forward direction (K < 1).
  - $\Delta G^{\circ} = 0$: **at equilibrium** (K = 1).
- **őĒG¬įf of elements in standard state = 0** (analogous to őĒH¬įf).
- **Temperature dependence:** Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation: $\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}_2}{T_2} - \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}_1}{T_1} = \Delta H^{\circ} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1} \right)$.
- **Van't Hoff equation:** $\ln(K_2/K_1) = -(\Delta H^{\circ}/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$.
- **Crossover temperature:** $T_{cross} = \Delta H^{\circ} / \Delta S^{\circ}$ (where őĒG¬į = 0).
- **Standard pressure (IUPAC):** **1 bar = 100 kPa** (NOT 1 atm, unless problem specifies).
- **Universal gas constant R = 8.314 J/(mol¬∑K) = 8.314 √ó 10‚ĀĽ¬≥ kJ/(mol¬∑K)**.
- **Two ways to compute őĒG¬į:** (a) From őĒG¬įf tables, (b) From őĒH¬į and őĒS¬į via $\Delta G^{\circ} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T\Delta S^{\circ}$.
- **Industrial applications:** Haber process (NH‚āÉ), Contact process (H‚āāSO‚āĄ), Water-gas shift reactor, Methanol synthesis, Steam reforming ‚ÄĒ all use these equations to **optimize T and P**.
- **For exam:** Always state the **sign of őĒG¬į** and the **direction of spontaneity** explicitly in your conclusion to grab the final "interpretation" mark.

topics.title: Feasibility of Reaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Feasibility of Reaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics**, the **Feasibility of a Chemical Reaction** refers to the thermodynamic assessment of whether a given chemical reaction can spontaneously proceed in a specified direction (forward or reverse) under a defined set of operating conditions (Temperature **T**, Pressure **P**, and Composition). The universal thermodynamic criterion for reaction feasibility at constant **T** and **P** is governed by the **Gibbs Free Energy Change ($\Delta G$)** of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT633 ‚Äď Module 4):**
> A reaction is **thermodynamically feasible (spontaneous)** in the forward direction if and only if $\Delta G_{T,P} < 0$. The reaction is at **equilibrium** when $\Delta G_{T,P} = 0$, and **non-spontaneous (feasible in the reverse direction)** when $\Delta G_{T,P} > 0$.

Mathematically, for a general reaction:
$$\sum_{i} \nu_i A_i \rightarrow \sum_{j} \nu_j B_j$$

the change in Gibbs free energy is expressed as:
$$\Delta G_{rxn} = \sum_{j} \nu_j \mu_j^{products} - \sum_{i} \nu_i \mu_i^{reactants}$$

where $\nu$ represents the **stoichiometric coefficient** and $\mu$ represents the **chemical potential** of each species.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are standing at the top of a hill with a heavy ball. The ball **naturally rolls downhill** because that motion releases energy (lowering potential energy). You never see the ball roll uphill on its own ‚ÄĒ that would require external energy input. The Gibbs free energy works exactly like this "hill" for chemical reactions.

- **$\Delta G < 0$** (Downhill): The reaction proceeds forward **spontaneously** ‚ÄĒ like the ball rolling down. The system releases useful work.
- **$\Delta G = 0$** (Flat ground): The reaction is at **equilibrium** ‚ÄĒ the ball has stopped; no net reaction occurs in either direction.
- **$\Delta G > 0$** (Uphill): The reaction **cannot** proceed forward on its own ‚ÄĒ you would need to push the ball (supply energy). The reverse reaction is feasible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering context:** In an ammonia synthesis reactor ($\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3$), chemical engineers must evaluate $\Delta G$ at the reactor's T and P to decide whether operating at 450 ¬įC and 200 bar will favor ammonia formation. Thermodynamic feasibility dictates the *theoretical maximum conversion*, while kinetics dictates the *rate*.

## 1.3 The Two Reference States: $\Delta G^\circ$ vs. $\Delta G$

It is essential to distinguish between two key quantities:

| Quantity | Definition | Reference State |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| $\Delta G^\circ$ (Standard Gibbs energy change) | Evaluated when **all reactants and products are at standard states** (1 bar, unit activity, specified T) | Hypothetical reference |
| $\Delta G$ (Actual Gibbs energy change) | Evaluated at the **actual** T, P, and composition of the reacting mixture | Real process state |

The relationship between them is given by:
$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient** based on activities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** A reaction with $\Delta G^\circ > 0$ at 298 K can still be feasible in practice if the actual $Q$ is small enough (e.g., by manipulating reactant concentrations). This is why **both** $\Delta G^\circ$ and $Q$ must be evaluated.

## 1.4 Fundamental Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and standard metrics govern reaction feasibility analysis:

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K)
- **Standard Pressure:** $P^\circ = 1$ bar $= 10^5$ Pa
- **Standard Temperature:** $T^\circ = 298.15$ K (25 ¬įC)
- **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96485$ C/mol (used in electrochemical feasibility)
- **Activity of pure solids/liquids:** $a_i = 1$
- **Activity of gases:** $a_i = f_i / f_i^\circ$ (fugacity-based)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always state the **sign convention** clearly. KTU follows the IUPAC convention: a *negative* $\Delta G$ means the forward reaction is feasible.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Feasibility Criteria

The feasibility of a chemical reaction is governed by a hierarchical set of conditions. Below is the structured logical sequence used in KTU-level problem solving:

### Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Identify the System and Reaction
- Write the balanced stoichiometric equation.
- Identify phases of each species (gas, liquid, solid, aqueous).
- Note operating **T**, **P**, and **composition**.

### Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute the Standard Gibbs Energy Change ($\Delta G^\circ$)
- **Method A (Formation data):**
$$\Delta G^\circ_{rxn} = \sum_{j} \nu_j \Delta G_f^\circ(products) - \sum_{i} \nu_i \Delta G_f^\circ(reactants)$$

- **Method B (From equilibrium constant):**
$$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$$

- **Method C (From enthalpy and entropy):**
$$\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$$

### Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute the Reaction Quotient ($Q$)
For a reaction $\sum \nu_i A_i = 0$:
$$Q = \prod_{i} a_i^{\nu_i}$$

where activities are computed as:
- **Gases:** $a_i = \hat{f}_i / f_i^\circ \approx y_i P / P^\circ$ (ideal gas)
- **Liquids/Solids:** $a_i = 1$ (pure phase)
- **Solutions:** $a_i = x_i \gamma_i$ (mole fraction √ó activity coefficient)

### Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Apply the Feasibility Inequality
$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

- If $\Delta G < 0$ ‚Üí **Feasible forward**
- If $\Delta G = 0$ ‚Üí **Equilibrium**
- If $\Delta G > 0$ ‚Üí **Not feasible forward; reverse is feasible**

### Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Temperature Dependence (Van't Hoff Analysis)
The feasibility may shift with temperature if $\Delta H^\circ$ and $\Delta S^\circ$ have opposite effects:
$$\frac{\partial(\Delta G^\circ / T)}{\partial T}\bigg|_P = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2}$$

Integrating (assuming $\Delta H^\circ$ is constant):
$$\ln\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

This is the **Van't Hoff Equation** ‚ÄĒ heavily tested in KTU Module 4.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations ‚ÄĒ They Appear in 90% of KTU Numerical Questions on Reaction Feasibility**

| # | Equation | Application / When to Use | Units |
|---|----------|--------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$ | Master feasibility equation | J/mol |
| 2 | $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ | When equilibrium constant $K$ is known | J/mol |
| 3 | $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$ | When thermal data ($\Delta H^\circ$, $\Delta S^\circ$) given | J/mol |
| 4 | $\Delta G^\circ_{rxn} = \sum \nu_i \Delta G_f^\circ (products) - \sum \nu_i \Delta G_f^\circ (reactants)$ | From tabulated standard free energies of formation | J/mol |
| 5 | $Q = \prod a_i^{\nu_i}$ | Reaction quotient at actual state | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\ln(K_2 / K_1) = -(\Delta H^\circ / R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ | Effect of temperature on equilibrium (Van't Hoff) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\left[\frac{\partial \ln K}{\partial T}\right]_P = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$ | Differential form of Van't Hoff | K$^{-1}$ |
| 8 | $\sum \nu_i \mu_i = 0$ (at equilibrium) | Chemical potential equilibrium condition | J/mol |
| 9 | $\Delta S_{univ} = -\Delta G_{sys} / T$ | Link to second law of thermodynamics | J/(mol¬∑K) |
| 10 | $K_p = K_c (RT)^{\Delta n}$ | Interconversion between $K_p$ and $K_c$ | Depends |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Reminder (KTU):** $a_i^{\nu_i}$ means products raised to positive power, reactants raised to negative power (or written with a minus sign explicitly). $\Delta n$ = (moles of gaseous products) ‚ąí (moles of gaseous reactants).

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Reaction Feasibility Analysis

The feasibility framework is the foundation of **process design** in the chemical industry:

- **Reactor Design:** Engineers use $\Delta G$ vs. T plots (often called **Ellingham-type diagrams** for gas-phase reactions) to select operating temperatures that maximize conversion.
- **Equilibrium-limited processes:** Reactions like methanol synthesis ($\text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{OH}$) and Haber-Bosch ammonia synthesis are strongly equilibrium-limited, so feasibility analysis dictates whether recycle loops are needed.
- **Electrochemical systems:** Feasibility of cell reactions is determined by $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$, linking thermodynamics to electrical work output.
- **Membrane and separation selection:** Reaction feasibility combined with conversion targets drives the choice between reactive distillation, PFR, CSTR, or equilibrium reactors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Whenever you see "feasibility," "spontaneity," or "direction of reaction" in a KTU question, the *first* step is always to compute $\Delta G$ (not just $\Delta G^\circ$).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relation Between $\Delta G^\circ$ and Equilibrium Constant $K$

We start from the fundamental definition of chemical potential for an ideal gas mixture. For species $i$ in an ideal gas mixture:
$$\mu_i = \mu_i^\circ(T) + RT \ln\left(\frac{\hat{f}_i}{f_i^\circ}\right) = \mu_i^\circ(T) + RT \ln a_i$$

For a general reaction written as:
$$\sum_{i} \nu_i A_i = 0$$

(the convention: $\nu_i > 0$ for products, $\nu_i < 0$ for reactants), the change in Gibbs free energy for an infinitesimal extent of reaction $d\xi$ is:
$$dG = \sum_i \mu_i dn_i = \sum_i \mu_i \nu_i d\xi$$

Therefore, the molar Gibbs energy change of reaction is:
$$\Delta G_{rxn} = \sum_i \nu_i \mu_i = \sum_i \nu_i \mu_i^\circ + RT \sum_i \nu_i \ln a_i$$

We define:
- $\sum_i \nu_i \mu_i^\circ = \Delta G^\circ$ (standard Gibbs energy change of reaction)
- $\sum_i \nu_i \ln a_i = \ln \prod_i a_i^{\nu_i} = \ln Q$

Hence:
$$\Delta G_{rxn} = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

At **equilibrium**, the system satisfies two conditions:
1. $\Delta G_{rxn} = 0$ (no driving force for net reaction)
2. $Q = K$ (the reaction quotient equals the equilibrium constant)

Substituting these conditions:
$$0 = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln K$$

Solving for $\Delta G^\circ$:
$$\boxed{\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** This is one of the most fundamental equations in chemical thermodynamics. It directly links the *thermodynamic* quantity $\Delta G^\circ$ to the *compositional* quantity $K$. A large positive $\Delta G^\circ$ corresponds to a vanishingly small $K$ (little product formation), while a large negative $\Delta G^\circ$ corresponds to a large $K$ (near-complete conversion).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Temperature Dependence ‚ÄĒ The Van't Hoff Equation

We start from the Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation applied to the standard reaction:
$$\left[\frac{\partial(\Delta G^\circ / T)}{\partial T}\right]_P = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2}$$

We also know:
$$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$$

Substituting this into the Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation:
$$\left[\frac{\partial (-RT \ln K / T)}{\partial T}\right]_P = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2}$$

Simplifying the left side:
$$\left[\frac{\partial (-R \ln K)}{\partial T}\right]_P = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2}$$

Rearranging:
$$\boxed{\left[\frac{\partial \ln K}{\partial T}\right]_P = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}}$$

**Integration (assuming $\Delta H^\circ$ is constant over the temperature range of interest):**

We separate variables and integrate from state 1 to state 2:
$$\int_{K_1}^{K_2} d(\ln K) = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}\, dT$$

$$\ln K \,\Big|_{K_1}^{K_2} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} T^{-2}\, dT$$

$$\ln K_2 - \ln K_1 = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_1}^{T_2}$$

$$\boxed{\ln\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** For an **exothermic reaction** ($\Delta H^\circ < 0$), increasing temperature *decreases* $K$ (Le Chatelier's principle). This is why the Haber-Bosch process uses a *compromise* temperature (~450 ¬įC) ‚ÄĒ high enough for acceptable kinetics, low enough to keep $K$ reasonably favorable.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz Equation (Foundation of Van't Hoff)

We start from the basic thermodynamic identity:
$$G = H - TS$$

Dividing by $T$:
$$\frac{G}{T} = \frac{H}{T} - S$$

Differentiating with respect to $T$ at constant $P$:
$$\left[\frac{\partial(G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_P = \frac{1}{T}\left[\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right]_P - \frac{G}{T^2} + \frac{1}{T}\left[\frac{\partial H}{\partial T}\right]_P - \left[\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right]_P$$

Using Maxwell relations and the fact that $\left[\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right]_P = -S$ and $H = G + TS$:
$$\left[\frac{\partial(G/T)}{\partial T}\right]_P = -\frac{H}{T^2}$$

For a reaction:
$$\boxed{\left[\frac{\partial(\Delta G^\circ / T)}{\partial T}\right]_P = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2}}$$

This is the **Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation** for reactions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Feasibility Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix to systematically solve any KTU reaction-feasibility problem.**

| Given Data | First Step | Second Step | Final Step |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|------------|
| $\Delta G_f^\circ$ for all species | Compute $\Delta G^\circ_{rxn}$ | Compute $K = e^{-\Delta G^\circ / RT}$ | Decide feasibility from sign of $\Delta G^\circ$ |
| $K$ at one T, $\Delta H^\circ$ | Compute $\Delta G^\circ = -RT\ln K$ | Apply Van't Hoff for new T | Compute new $K$ and feasibility |
| $\Delta H^\circ$ and $\Delta S^\circ$ | Compute $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$ | Apply $\Delta G^\circ = -RT\ln K$ | Find temperature where $\Delta G^\circ = 0$ (inversion T) |
| Actual $Q$ and $\Delta G^\circ$ | Compute $\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT\ln Q$ | Apply sign criterion | Final feasibility verdict |
| $\Delta H^\circ$, $K$ at $T_1$, new $T_2$ | Use Van't Hoff directly | Solve for $K_2$ | Compute $\Delta G^\circ$ at $T_2$ |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** For the reaction $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}_2(g)$ at 298 K, $\Delta G^\circ = 4.73$ kJ/mol. Determine (a) the equilibrium constant $K_p$, and (b) whether the forward reaction is feasible at 298 K when partial pressures are $P_{\text{N}_2\text{O}_4} = 0.5$ bar and $P_{\text{NO}_2} = 0.1$ bar.

### Part (a): Compute $K_p$

Using $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_p$:
$$4730 = -(8.314)(298) \ln K_p$$

$$\ln K_p = -\frac{4730}{8.314 \times 298} = -\frac{4730}{2477.6} = -1.909$$

$$K_p = e^{-1.909} = 0.148$$

### Part (b): Feasibility at actual conditions

Reaction quotient:
$$Q_p = \frac{(P_{\text{NO}_2})^2}{P_{\text{N}_2\text{O}_4}} = \frac{(0.1)^2}{0.5} = \frac{0.01}{0.5} = 0.02$$

Since $Q_p = 0.02 < K_p = 0.148$, the system is **not at equilibrium** and will shift to the right (forward) to reach equilibrium. Alternatively, compute $\Delta G$:
$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q_p = 4730 + (8.314)(298)\ln(0.02)$$

$$\Delta G = 4730 + 2477.6 \times (-3.912) = 4730 - 9694.4 = -4964.4 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\Delta G = -4.96 \text{ kJ/mol} < 0$$

Therefore, **the forward reaction is feasible** at the given conditions. ‚úď

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Takeaway:** This problem illustrates a critical point ‚ÄĒ even though $\Delta G^\circ > 0$ (suggesting the standard state disfavors products), the *actual* $\Delta G < 0$ because the low product partial pressure shifts the reaction forward. **Always compute $\Delta G$, not just $\Delta G^\circ$.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Reaction Feasibility Decision Flow

This diagram maps the analytical sequence a KTU student should follow when solving any reaction feasibility problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Problem Statement] --> B[Identify Reaction Stoichiometry and Phases]
    B --> C{What Data is Given?}
    C -->|Delta Gf data| D[Compute Delta G standard via formation method]
    C -->|K at some T| E[Compute Delta G standard from -RT ln K]
    C -->|Delta H and Delta S| F[Compute Delta G standard = Delta H minus T Delta S]
    D --> G[Compute Equilibrium Constant K]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Need Temperature Effect?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Apply Van t Hoff Equation]
    H -->|No| J[Proceed to Actual State Analysis]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute Reaction Quotient Q from actual state]
    K --> L[Compute Delta G actual = Delta G standard + RT ln Q]
    L --> M{Delta G actual sign?}
    M -->|Negative| N[Feasible Forward: Reaction Proceeds Right]
    M -->|Zero| O[At Equilibrium: No Net Reaction]
    M -->|Positive| P[Not Feasible Forward: Reverse is Feasible]
    N --> Q[End: Report Conclusion with Justification]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Effect of Temperature on Feasibility

This diagram illustrates how the sign of $\Delta G$ shifts with temperature, depending on the signs of $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Case1[Case A: Exothermic and Entropy Decreasing]
        A1[Delta H negative, Delta S negative] --> A2[Feasible at Low T only]
        A2 --> A3[Inversion Temperature: Tinv = Delta H divided by Delta S]
    end
    subgraph Case2[Case B: Endothermic and Entropy Increasing]
        B1[Delta H positive, Delta S positive] --> B2[Feasible at High T only]
        B2 --> B3[Inversion Temperature: Tinv = Delta H divided by Delta S]
    end
    subgraph Case3[Case C: Exothermic and Entropy Increasing]
        C1[Delta H negative, Delta S positive] --> C2[Always Feasible at All T]
    end
    subgraph Case4[Case D: Endothermic and Entropy Decreasing]
        D1[Delta H positive, Delta S negative] --> D2[Never Feasible at Any T]
    end
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture: Relation Between Thermodynamic Potentials

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System at T and P] --> B[Compute Enthalpy Change Delta H]
    A --> C[Compute Entropy Change Delta S]
    B --> D[Combine via Gibbs-Helmholtz Equation]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Compute Delta G standard = Delta H minus T Delta S]
    E --> F[Apply Delta G standard = minus RT ln K]
    F --> G[Compute Equilibrium Constant K]
    G --> H[Compare Q with K to Determine Feasibility]
    H --> I[Final Feasibility Verdict]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a *calculation step*, not a physical unit operation. The arrows indicate the *logical flow of analysis*. Always start from the top of each diagram and follow the branches based on the data you are given in the problem statement.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions are direct, definition-based, or short derivation steps. Each carries 3 marks. Model answers are concise and formula-driven.

---

### **Question A1:** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]`

**State and explain the thermodynamic criteria for the feasibility of a chemical reaction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

At constant temperature **T** and pressure **P**, a chemical reaction is feasible in the forward direction if and only if the change in Gibbs free energy is negative:

$$\Delta G_{T,P} < 0 \quad \text{(Spontaneous forward)}$$

The criteria are:
- **$\Delta G_{T,P} < 0$:** Reaction is **feasible in the forward direction**; the system can do useful work.
- **$\Delta G_{T,P} = 0$:** System is at **equilibrium**; no net reaction in either direction.
- **$\Delta G_{T,P} > 0$:** Reaction is **non-spontaneous forward**; the reverse reaction is feasible.

Mathematically:
$$\Delta G = \sum_i \nu_i \mu_i = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

This criterion arises from the **second law of thermodynamics** applied to reacting systems at constant T and P, where the equilibrium state corresponds to the *minimum* Gibbs free energy.

---

### **Question A2:** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024]`

**Derive the relation between standard Gibbs free energy change and the equilibrium constant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a general reaction at equilibrium, two conditions hold:
1. $\Delta G = 0$
2. $Q = K$

From the fundamental relation:
$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

Substituting equilibrium conditions:
$$0 = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln K$$

Rearranging:
$$\boxed{\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K}$$

This shows that $\Delta G^\circ$ and $K$ are inversely related through a logarithmic function. A more negative $\Delta G^\circ$ corresponds to a larger $K$, indicating greater product formation at equilibrium.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern (ESE):** Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each question typically has two parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Full derivations and numerical work are required.

---

### **Question B1 (Choice A):** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Van't Hoff equation** for the temperature dependence of the equilibrium constant. State all assumptions clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the reaction $\text{CO}(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2(g) + \text{H}_2(g)$ (water-gas shift reaction), the equilibrium constant $K_p = 1.0$ at 1100 K. Given $\Delta H^\circ = -41.2$ kJ/mol, calculate $K_p$ at 1300 K. Assume $\Delta H^\circ$ is constant over this temperature range. Comment on the feasibility of the forward reaction at 1300 K.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B1 (Choice A):**

#### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumption:** $\Delta H^\circ$ is constant over the temperature range of interest (i.e., $\Delta C_p \approx 0$).

**Starting Point:** The Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation:
$$\left[\frac{\partial(\Delta G^\circ / T)}{\partial T}\right]_P = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$:
$$\left[\frac{\partial(-R\ln K)}{\partial T}\right]_P = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{T^2}$$

**Step 2:** Simplify (since $R$ is constant):
$$\frac{d \ln K}{d T} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$$

**Step 3:** Separate variables:
$$d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate from $(T_1, K_1)$ to $(T_2, K_2)$:
$$\int_{K_1}^{K_2} d\ln K = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} T^{-2}\, dT$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate integrals:
$$\ln K_2 - \ln K_1 = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_1}^{T_2} = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\ln\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)}$$

This is the **integrated form of the Van't Hoff equation**. (7 marks)

#### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $K_1 = 1.0$ at $T_1 = 1100$ K
- $\Delta H^\circ = -41.2$ kJ/mol $= -41200$ J/mol
- $T_2 = 1300$ K
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol¬∑K)

**Step 1:** Apply Van't Hoff equation:
$$\ln\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the temperature term:
$$\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{1300} - \frac{1}{1100} = \frac{1100 - 1300}{1300 \times 1100} = \frac{-200}{1,430,000} = -1.3986 \times 10^{-4}\, \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:
$$\ln\left(\frac{K_2}{1.0}\right) = -\frac{(-41200)}{8.314} \times (-1.3986 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$\ln K_2 = -(4955.5) \times (-1.3986 \times 10^{-4}) = 0.6930$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $K_2$:
$$K_2 = e^{0.6930} = 2.0$$

**Step 5:** Compute $\Delta G^\circ$ at 1300 K:
$$\Delta G^\circ_{1300} = -RT_2 \ln K_2 = -(8.314)(1300)\ln(2.0) = -(10808.2)(0.693) = -7489.6 \text{ J/mol} \approx -7.49 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 6:** Comment on feasibility:
Since $\Delta G^\circ_{1300} = -7.49$ kJ/mol $< 0$, the forward reaction is **thermodynamically feasible** at 1300 K. However, $K_p$ has *increased* from 1.0 to 2.0 ‚ÄĒ wait, this contradicts Le Chatelier's principle for an exothermic reaction. Let me recheck.

**Correction:** For exothermic $\Delta H^\circ < 0$, increasing T should *decrease* K. Let me re-evaluate the sign:

$$\ln\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{(-41200)}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{1300} - \frac{1}{1100}\right)$$

The bracket $\left(\frac{1}{1300} - \frac{1}{1100}\right) = -1.3986 \times 10^{-4}$ (negative).

So:
$$\ln\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -(-4955.5)\times(-1.3986 \times 10^{-4}) = 4955.5 \times (-1.3986 \times 10^{-4}) = -0.6930$$

Therefore:
$$K_2 = K_1 \times e^{-0.6930} = 1.0 \times 0.5 = 0.5$$

This is consistent: **for an exothermic reaction, increasing T decreases K from 1.0 to 0.5.** ‚úď

**Revised Step 5:**
$$\Delta G^\circ_{1300} = -RT_2 \ln K_2 = -(8.314)(1300)\ln(0.5) = -(10808.2)(-0.693) = +7489.6 \text{ J/mol} = +7.49 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Final Comment:** Since $\Delta G^\circ_{1300} = +7.49$ kJ/mol $> 0$, the forward reaction is **NOT thermodynamically feasible** at standard state at 1300 K. The equilibrium constant has decreased from 1.0 to 0.5, meaning the reverse reaction (or lower temperatures) is favored. This is consistent with Le Chatelier's principle: heat is a "product" in an exothermic reaction, so adding heat (raising T) shifts equilibrium *backward*.

---

### **Question B2 (Choice B):** `[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **effect of temperature on reaction feasibility** with reference to the four possible sign combinations of $\Delta H^\circ$ and $\Delta S^\circ$. Support your answer with appropriate equations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a hypothetical reaction, $\Delta H^\circ = -85$ kJ/mol and $\Delta S^\circ = -100$ J/(mol¬∑K) at 298 K. (i) Calculate the standard Gibbs free energy change at 298 K. (ii) Determine the temperature at which the reaction just becomes feasible (the **inversion temperature**). (iii) Will the reaction be feasible at 1000 K? Justify.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B2 (Choice B):**

#### Part (a) ‚ÄĒ Theoretical Analysis (7 marks)

The feasibility of a reaction at any temperature depends on the sign of:
$$\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$$

**Four cases arise:**

| Case | $\Delta H^\circ$ | $\Delta S^\circ$ | Feasibility Behavior | Inversion Temp |
|------|----------|----------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Negative (exothermic) | Negative (entropy loss) | Feasible at low T only | $T_{inv} = \Delta H^\circ / \Delta S^\circ$ |
| 2 | Positive (endothermic) | Positive (entropy gain) | Feasible at high T only | $T_{inv} = \Delta H^\circ / \Delta S^\circ$ |
| 3 | Negative (exothermic) | Positive (entropy gain) | Feasible at all T | No inversion |
| 4 | Positive (endothermic) | Negative (entropy loss) | Not feasible at any T | No inversion |

**Derivation of Inversion Temperature:**
Setting $\Delta G^\circ = 0$:
$$0 = \Delta H^\circ - T_{inv} \Delta S^\circ$$

$$\boxed{T_{inv} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{\Delta S^\circ}}$$

This is the temperature at which the reaction transitions between feasible and non-feasible (at standard state). Above $T_{inv}$ (for cases 1 and 2), the sign of $\Delta G^\circ$ reverses.

**Examples:**
- Case 1: $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$ (favored below ~465 K at 1 bar)
- Case 2: $\text{N}_2\text{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}_2(g)$ (favored above ~322 K)

#### Part (b) ‚ÄĒ Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $\Delta H^\circ = -85$ kJ/mol $= -85{,}000$ J/mol
- $\Delta S^\circ = -100$ J/(mol¬∑K)
- $T_1 = 298$ K

#### (i) $\Delta G^\circ$ at 298 K

$$\Delta G^\circ_{298} = \Delta H^\circ - T_1 \Delta S^\circ = -85{,}000 - (298)(-100)$$

$$\Delta G^\circ_{298} = -85{,}000 + 29{,}800 = -55{,}200 \text{ J/mol} = -55.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

Since $\Delta G^\circ_{298} < 0$, the reaction is **feasible at 298 K at standard state**.

#### (ii) Inversion Temperature

$$T_{inv} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{\Delta S^\circ} = \frac{-85{,}000}{-100} = 850 \text{ K}$$

The reaction is feasible *below* 850 K (since $\Delta H^\circ < 0$ and $\Delta S^\circ < 0$, Case 1).

#### (iii) Feasibility at 1000 K

$$\Delta G^\circ_{1000} = -85{,}000 - (1000)(-100) = -85{,}000 + 100{,}000 = +15{,}000 \text{ J/mol} = +15 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

Since $\Delta G^\circ_{1000} > 0$, the reaction is **NOT feasible** at 1000 K at standard state. The forward reaction becomes non-spontaneous because $T > T_{inv} = 850$ K.

**Engineering Interpretation:** This is the thermodynamic reason why *exothermic* reactions with *negative* entropy change (like many polymerization or condensation reactions) must be carried out at *low temperatures* to maintain feasibility, even though kinetics may demand higher temperatures ‚ÄĒ a classic *thermodynamic‚Äďkinetic compromise* in reactor design.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Exams (Module 4 ‚Äď Feasibility):**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$:** Students often write $\Delta G^\circ = +RT \ln K$. This sign error propagates throughout the problem. **Always remember: negative $\Delta G^\circ$ ‚Üí large $K$.**
>
> 2. **Using $T$ in ¬įC instead of K:** The Van't Hoff equation requires absolute temperature in **Kelvin**. Inserting 1300 ¬įC instead of 1573 K is a guaranteed zero.
>
> 3. **Confusing $Q$ with $K$:** $Q$ is the reaction quotient at the *actual* state; $K$ is the value at *equilibrium*. Substituting $K$ in place of $Q$ (or vice versa) leads to incorrect feasibility conclusions.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Sign of $\nu$ in $Q$:** In $Q = \prod a_i^{\nu_i}$, products go in the numerator and reactants in the denominator. A common error is to invert this.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Phase Specification:** For reactions involving liquids or solids, students forget that $a_{solid} = a_{liquid} = 1$ (pure phase), which changes the expression for $Q$ significantly.
>
> 6. **Van't Hoff Sign Error:** When $\Delta H^\circ < 0$ (exothermic) and $T_2 > T_1$, the student must conclude $K_2 < K_1$. Errors in double-negative arithmetic lead to wrong monotonicity.
>
> 7. **Mixing $\Delta G$ and $\Delta G^\circ$:** A reaction with $\Delta G^\circ > 0$ can have $\Delta G < 0$ at actual conditions. Both quantities must be computed and clearly distinguished in the answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ‚ÄĒ Module 4: Feasibility of Reaction**
>
> ‚úÖ **Master Equation:** $\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$
>
> ‚úÖ **Standard Criterion:** Reaction is **feasible forward** iff $\Delta G_{T,P} < 0$
>
> ‚úÖ **Three Feasibility Cases:**
>    - $\Delta G < 0$ ‚Üí Feasible forward
>    - $\Delta G = 0$ ‚Üí Equilibrium
>    - $\Delta G > 0$ ‚Üí Feasible in reverse
>
> ‚úÖ **Equilibrium Link:** $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$
>
> ‚úÖ **Temperature Effect (Van't Hoff):**
>    $$\ln\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$
>
> ‚úÖ **Four Sign Combinations of $(\Delta H^\circ, \Delta S^\circ)$:** Determine feasibility behavior with temperature.
>
> ‚úÖ **Inversion Temperature:** $T_{inv} = \Delta H^\circ / \Delta S^\circ$
>
> ‚úÖ **Activity Rules:**
>    - Pure solids/liquids: $a_i = 1$
>    - Ideal gases: $a_i = y_i P / P^\circ$
>    - Solutions: $a_i = x_i \gamma_i$
>
> ‚úÖ **Standard Reference State:** 1 bar, unit activity, $T$ = specified
>
> ‚úÖ **Equilibrium Condition:** $\sum \nu_i \mu_i = 0$
>
> ‚úÖ **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For exothermic reactions, low T favors products; for endothermic reactions, high T favors products. The *compromise* temperature balances thermodynamics and kinetics.
>
> ‚úÖ **Examiner Mantra:** Always specify the reference state (standard or actual) when reporting $\Delta G$.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes ‚ÄĒ Feasibility of Reaction. Best of luck with your KTU ESE preparation!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Temperature on Equilibrium Constant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effect of Temperature on Equilibrium Constant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **van't Hoff Equation** (sometimes called the van't Hoff isochore) is the fundamental thermodynamic relation that quantitatively describes how the **equilibrium constant** $K$ of a chemical reaction varies with absolute temperature $T$. For a reaction proceeding at standard-state conditions, the differential form is:

$$\frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}$$

where $\Delta H^{\circ}$ is the **standard enthalpy of reaction** (in $\text{J/mol}$) and $R = \mathbf{8.314 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}}$ is the universal gas constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must clearly distinguish between the differential form (used for instantaneous rate of change) and the integrated form (used for finite temperature jumps in numerical/ESE problems).

### Conceptual Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Stretched Rubber Band" Model

Imagine two children pulling on opposite ends of a rubber band:
- The **rubber band** = the equilibrium state of a chemical reaction.
- The **pulling force** = the temperature $T$ applied to the system.
- The **stretch** = the resulting change in $K$.

If the reaction is **endothermic** (absorbs heat, like one child pulling harder as heat is added), the rubber band "wants" to stretch further ‚ÄĒ i.e., $K$ **increases** with $T$. Conversely, an **exothermic** reaction behaves like a rubber band that recoils when heated ‚ÄĒ $K$ **decreases** with $T$. The van't Hoff equation is essentially the mathematical fingerprint of this stretching or recoiling tendency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The sign of $\Delta H^{\circ}$ is the **master switch** that decides whether $K$ increases or decreases with temperature:
> - If $\Delta H^{\circ} > 0$ (endothermic): $K$ **rises** as $T$ rises.
> - If $\Delta H^{\circ} < 0$ (exothermic): $K$ **falls** as $T$ rises.
> - If $\Delta H^{\circ} = 0$: $K$ is **independent** of $T$ (rare, only for "athermal" reactions).

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$
- **Standard-State Reference Temperature:** $T^{\circ} = 298.15 \; \text{K}$ ($25^{\circ}\text{C}$)
- **Equilibrium Constant Types:** $K_p$, $K_c$, $K_x$, $K_a$ ‚ÄĒ all obey the same temperature-dependence law; only the numerical value differs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Structured Stepwise Logic

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Recognize the underlying relation:** The equilibrium constant is linked to the standard Gibbs free energy change through the master equation $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$. Therefore, **anything that affects $\Delta G^{\circ}$ also affects $K$**.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply the Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation:** The Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation,

$$\left[ \frac{\partial (\Delta G^{\circ}/T)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}$$

is the engine that drives the van't Hoff derivation. It tells us how the *ratio* $\Delta G^{\circ}/T$ changes with temperature.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Differentiate the master equation with respect to $T$:**

$$\frac{d(\Delta G^{\circ})}{dT} = -R \ln K - RT \frac{d(\ln K)}{dT}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Eliminate $\Delta G^{\circ}$ and isolate $d(\ln K)/dT$:** Equating the two paths yields the differential form of the van't Hoff equation (see Section 3 for full derivation).

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Integrate to obtain finite-temperature changes:** When $\Delta H^{\circ}$ is treated as independent of $T$ over the temperature range of interest, the integrated form becomes:

$$\ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right) = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{1}} - \frac{1}{T_{2}} \right)$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Account for temperature-varying $\Delta H^{\circ}$:** If the temperature interval is large, $\Delta H^{\circ}$ is no longer constant. Use **Kirchhoff's Law**:

$$\Delta H^{\circ}(T_{2}) = \Delta H^{\circ}(T_{1}) + \int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} \Delta C_{p} \, dT$$

where $\Delta C_{p} = \sum \nu_{i} C_{p,i}^{\circ}$ (products minus reactants).

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Typical Use in ESE |
|---|---|---|
| $\dfrac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}$ | Differential (instantaneous) form | Conceptual/derivation questions |
| $\ln K = -\dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + C$ | Linear (intercept) form | Graphical analysis (slope $= -\Delta H^{\circ}/R$) |
| $\ln \!\left( \dfrac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \!\left( \dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}} \right)$ | Integrated form (constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$) | Numerical ESE problems |
| $\Delta H^{\circ}(T_{2}) = \Delta H^{\circ}(T_{1}) + \displaystyle\int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} \Delta C_{p}\,dT$ | Kirchhoff's equation | Wide-range $\Delta H^{\circ}$ correction |
| $K_{p} = K_{c} (R T)^{\Delta n}$ | $K_{p} \leftrightarrow K_{c}$ conversion | Gas-phase reactions |
| $\Delta G^{\circ} = -R T \ln K$ | Master link to $\Delta G^{\circ}$ | Free-energy based questions |

> [!TIP]
> **Graph Reading Hack:** A plot of $\ln K$ (y-axis) versus $1/T$ (x-axis) is a **straight line**. The slope is $-\Delta H^{\circ}/R$ and the intercept is $\Delta S^{\circ}/R$. This is one of the most-tested topics in ESE graphical questions.

### Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

- **Reactor Design:** Predicting how a reactor's equilibrium yield shifts when operating temperature is changed ‚ÄĒ critical for **ammonia synthesis (Haber process)**, **methanol synthesis**, and **Sulfur dioxide oxidation**.
- **Catalyst Screening:** Helps engineers estimate whether a reaction is kinetically limited vs equilibrium-limited at operating temperature.
- **Process Safety:** Used in equilibrium-limited decompositions (e.g., ethylene cracking) to predict runaway limits.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation I ‚ÄĒ Differential Form of the van't Hoff Equation

**Starting Point ‚ÄĒ Master Equation:**

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Write the Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz equation at constant pressure:**

$$\left[ \frac{\partial (\Delta G^{\circ}/T)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply the quotient rule to expand the left-hand side:**

$$\left[ \frac{T \cdot \dfrac{d(\Delta G^{\circ})}{dT} - \Delta G^{\circ} \cdot 1}{T^{2}} \right] = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Multiply both sides by $T^{2}$:**

$$T \cdot \frac{d(\Delta G^{\circ})}{dT} - \Delta G^{\circ} = -\Delta H^{\circ}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Solve for $\dfrac{d(\Delta G^{\circ})}{dT}$:**

$$\frac{d(\Delta G^{\circ})}{dT} = \frac{\Delta G^{\circ} - \Delta H^{\circ}}{T}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Substitute $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ into the right-hand side:**

$$\frac{d(\Delta G^{\circ})}{dT} = \frac{-RT \ln K - \Delta H^{\circ}}{T}$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Independently differentiate the master equation $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ with respect to $T$ (using the product rule):**

$$\frac{d(\Delta G^{\circ})}{dT} = -R \ln K - RT \frac{d(\ln K)}{dT}$$

**Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Equate the two expressions for $\dfrac{d(\Delta G^{\circ})}{dT}$ obtained in Steps 5 and 6:**

$$-R \ln K - RT \frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = -R \ln K - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T}$$

**Step 8 ‚ÄĒ Cancel $-R \ln K$ from both sides:**

$$-RT \frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T}$$

**Step 9 ‚ÄĒ Divide both sides by $-RT$ and simplify:**

$$\frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}$$

This is the **van't Hoff Equation (differential form)**. $\blacksquare$

---

### Derivation II ‚ÄĒ Integrated Form (Constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Start with the differential form:**

$$\frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Rearrange for integration:**

$$d(\ln K) = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Integrate between two states $(T_{1}, K_{1})$ and $(T_{2}, K_{2})$, assuming $\Delta H^{\circ}$ is constant:**

$$\int_{K_{1}}^{K_{2}} d(\ln K) = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} T^{-2}\, dT$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Evaluate the left integral:**

$$\ln K \,\bigg\vert_{K_{1}}^{K_{2}} = \ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right)$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Evaluate the right integral using $\int T^{-2}\,dT = -1/T$:**

$$\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left[ -\frac{1}{T} \right]_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right)$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Combine the two sides:**

$$\ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right) = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{1}} - \frac{1}{T_{2}} \right)$$

This is the **van't Hoff Equation (integrated form, constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$)**. $\blacksquare$

---

### Derivation III ‚ÄĒ Linear Form (Useful for Graphical Problems)

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Re-arrange the integrated form to the $y = mx + c$ format:**

$$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + C$$

where $C = \dfrac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{R}$ (since $\Delta G^{\circ} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T \Delta S^{\circ}$ and $-RT \ln K = \Delta H^{\circ} - T \Delta S^{\circ}$ imply $\ln K = -\Delta H^{\circ}/RT + \Delta S^{\circ}/R$).

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Identify slope and intercept:**

- **Slope:** $-\Delta H^{\circ}/R$
- **Intercept:** $\Delta S^{\circ}/R$

A plot of $\ln K$ (y) vs $1/T$ (x) yields a straight line whose slope directly gives $-\Delta H^{\circ}/R$ ‚ÄĒ a method frequently used to extract thermodynamic data from laboratory $K$ vs $T$ measurements. $\blacksquare$

---

### Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Decision Tree for ESE Problems

| Given Data | Strategy | Equation to Apply |
|---|---|---|
| $K_{1}, K_{2}, T_{1}, T_{2}$ ‚ÄĒ find $\Delta H^{\circ}$ | Direct substitution into integrated form | $\Delta H^{\circ} = \dfrac{R \ln(K_{2}/K_{1})}{(1/T_{1} - 1/T_{2})}$ |
| $\Delta H^{\circ}, T_{1}, K_{1}, T_{2}$ ‚ÄĒ find $K_{2}$ | Substitute and solve for $K_{2}$ | $K_{2} = K_{1} \exp\!\left[ -\Delta H^{\circ}/R\,(1/T_{2} - 1/T_{1}) \right]$ |
| $\Delta H^{\circ}(T_{1}), \Delta C_{p}, T_{1}, T_{2}$ | First correct $\Delta H^{\circ}$ to $T_{2}$ using Kirchhoff, then apply van't Hoff | Kirchhoff + integrated form |
| Experimental $(\ln K, 1/T)$ data pairs | Linear regression; slope $= -\Delta H^{\circ}/R$ | Linear plot |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram A ‚ÄĒ Causal Architecture of Temperature Effect on Equilibrium Constant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
