```mermaid
    TABS["ABSOLUTE TEMPERATURE T"] --> VHEQ["VAN T HOFF EQUATION"]
    DH0["STANDARD ENTHALPY DH0"] --> VHEQ
    RGAS["GAS CONSTANT R"] --> VHEQ
    VHEQ --> KEQ["EQUILIBRIUM CONSTANT K"]
    DH0 --> BRANCH{DH0 SIGN DECIDES TREND}
    BRANCH --> ENDO["DH0 GREATER THAN ZERO ENDOTHERMIC"]
    BRANCH --> EXO["DH0 LESS THAN ZERO EXOTHERMIC"]
    ENDO --> INCRES["K INCREASES WITH T"]
    EXO --> DECRES["K DECREASES WITH T"]
    VHEQ --> LINPLOT["LINEAR PLOT LN K VS 1 BY T"]
    LINPLOT --> SLOPE["SLOPE EQUALS MINUS DH0 BY R"]
    KEQ --> REACTOR["REACTOR DESIGN YIELD PREDICTION"]
    KEQ --> PROCESS["PROCESS SAFETY LIMIT ESTIMATION"]
```

### Diagram B ‚ÄĒ Derivation Logic Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["DELTA G0 EQUALS MINUS RT LN K"] --> B["APPLY GIBBS HELMHOLTZ"]
    C["D PARTIAL DELTA G0 BY T PARTIAL T EQUALS MINUS DH0 BY T SQUARED"] --> B
    B --> D["EXPAND USING QUOTIENT RULE"]
    D --> E["DIFFERENTIATE MASTER EQN WITH RESPECT TO T"]
    E --> F["EQUATE BOTH EXPRESSIONS FOR D DELTA G0 BY D T"]
    F --> G["CANCEL COMMON LN K TERMS"]
    G --> H["VAN T HOFF DIFFERENTIAL FORM"]
    H --> I["INTEGRATE WITH CONSTANT DH0"]
    I --> J["VAN T HOFF INTEGRATED FORM"]
    H --> K["REARRANGE TO LINEAR FORM"]
    K --> L["LN K VS 1 BY T LINEAR PLOT"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Diagram A is a **causal map** ‚ÄĒ it traces how temperature, enthalpy, and the gas constant feed into the van't Hoff equation to determine $K$ and downstream engineering decisions. Diagram B is a **logic chain** showing the exact derivation path used in Section 3.

### Block-Level Functional Architecture ‚ÄĒ Temperature Compensation in Industrial Reactors

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        T1["T1 INLET TEMPERATURE"]
        K1["K1 INLET EQUILIBRIUM CONSTANT"]
        DH0T["DH0 STANDARD ENTHALPY"]
    end

    subgraph MODULE["VAN T HOFF ENGINE"]
        INTEG["INTEGRATED VAN T HOFF EQN"]
        KIRCH["KIRCHHOFF CORRECTION BLOCK"]
        SOLVE["NUMERICAL SOLVER"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT PARAMETERS"]
        T2["T2 OUTLET TEMPERATURE"]
        K2["K2 PREDICTED EQUILIBRIUM CONSTANT"]
        CONV["CONVERSION EFFICIENCY"]
    end

    T1 --> INTEG
    K1 --> INTEG
    DH0T --> KIRCH
    KIRCH --> INTEG
    INTEG --> SOLVE
    T2 --> SOLVE
    SOLVE --> K2
    K2 --> CONV
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ May 2024]**
**State the van't Hoff equation. Explain qualitatively how the equilibrium constant varies with temperature for (i) an endothermic reaction and (ii) an exothermic reaction.**

**Model Answer:**

The van't Hoff equation (differential form) is:

$$\frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}$$

**(i) Endothermic reaction ($\Delta H^{\circ} > 0$):** The right-hand side is positive. Therefore $d(\ln K)/dT > 0$, meaning $\ln K$ ‚ÄĒ and hence $K$ ‚ÄĒ **increases** with temperature. Qualitatively, heat acts as a "reactant" for endothermic reactions, so adding heat shifts equilibrium toward products, increasing $K$.

**(ii) Exothermic reaction ($\Delta H^{\circ} < 0$):** The right-hand side is negative. Therefore $d(\ln K)/dT < 0$, meaning $K$ **decreases** with rising temperature. Heat acts as a "product", so adding heat shifts equilibrium toward reactants, decreasing $K$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Dec 2023]**
**For the reaction $A \rightleftharpoons B$, $K_{p}$ at $400 \, \text{K}$ is $0.05$ and at $500 \, \text{K}$ is $0.20$. Calculate the standard enthalpy of reaction $\Delta H^{\circ}$.** [$R = 8.314 \; \text{J mol}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$]

**Model Answer:**

Given:
- $T_{1} = 400 \; \text{K}, \quad K_{1} = 0.05$
- $T_{2} = 500 \; \text{K}, \quad K_{2} = 0.20$

Applying the integrated van't Hoff equation:

$$\ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right)$$

Substituting values:

$$\ln \left( \frac{0.20}{0.05} \right) = \ln(4) = 1.3863$$

$$\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} = \frac{1}{500} - \frac{1}{400} = \frac{4 - 5}{2000} = -\frac{1}{2000} = -5 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\Delta H^{\circ} = -\frac{R \ln(K_{2}/K_{1})}{(1/T_{2} - 1/T_{1})} = -\frac{8.314 \times 1.3863}{-5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H^{\circ} \approx 23{,}055 \; \text{J/mol} \approx 23.06 \; \text{kJ/mol (endothermic)}}$$

---

### Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**Q7. (a) [KTU University Exam ‚ÄĒ Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)(i)** Derive the van't Hoff equation relating the temperature dependence of the equilibrium constant to the standard enthalpy of reaction. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The derivation proceeds through the following rigorous steps:

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Master Equation.** The equilibrium constant is linked to the standard Gibbs free energy change:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz Equation.** At constant pressure:

$$\left[ \frac{\partial (\Delta G^{\circ}/T)}{\partial T} \right]_{P} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Expand the LHS using the quotient rule:**

$$\frac{T \cdot (d\Delta G^{\circ}/dT) - \Delta G^{\circ}}{T^{2}} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T^{2}}$$

Multiplying both sides by $T^{2}$:

$$T \cdot \frac{d(\Delta G^{\circ})}{dT} - \Delta G^{\circ} = -\Delta H^{\circ} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Differentiate the master equation (i) with respect to $T$:**

$$\frac{d(\Delta G^{\circ})}{dT} = -R \ln K - RT \frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ From (iii), solve for $d(\Delta G^{\circ})/dT$ and substitute $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$:**

$$\frac{d(\Delta G^{\circ})}{dT} = \frac{\Delta G^{\circ} - \Delta H^{\circ}}{T} = \frac{-RT \ln K - \Delta H^{\circ}}{T} = -R \ln K - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T} \quad \text{...(v)}$$

**Step 6 ‚ÄĒ Equate (iv) and (v):**

$$-R \ln K - RT \frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = -R \ln K - \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T}$$

**Step 7 ‚ÄĒ Cancel $-R \ln K$:**

$$-RT \frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{T}$$

**Step 8 ‚ÄĒ Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\frac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}}$$

---

**(a)(ii)** For the reaction $\text{N}_{2}\text{O}_{4}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NO}_{2}(g)$, the equilibrium constant $K_{p}$ at $298 \, \text{K}$ is $0.148$. Calculate $K_{p}$ at $350 \, \text{K}$ given that $\Delta H^{\circ} = 57.32 \; \text{kJ/mol}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $T_{1} = 298 \; \text{K}$, $K_{1} = 0.148$
- $T_{2} = 350 \; \text{K}$, $\Delta H^{\circ} = 57{,}320 \; \text{J/mol}$
- $R = 8.314 \; \text{J mol}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Apply the integrated van't Hoff equation:**

$$\ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right)$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute the bracket term:**

$$\frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} = \frac{1}{350} - \frac{1}{298} = \frac{298 - 350}{350 \times 298} = \frac{-52}{104{,}300} = -4.9856 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute the right-hand side:**

$$-\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right) = -\frac{57{,}320}{8.314} \times (-4.9856 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$= -6894.4 \times (-4.9856 \times 10^{-4}) = +3.4374$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Solve for $K_{2}$:**

$$\ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{0.148} \right) = 3.4374$$

$$\frac{K_{2}}{0.148} = e^{3.4374} = 31.10$$

$$\boxed{K_{2} = 0.148 \times 31.10 \approx 4.60}$$

Since $\Delta H^{\circ} > 0$ (endothermic), $K$ increased with $T$ ‚ÄĒ consistent with theory. ‚úď

---

**Q7. (b) ‚ÄĒ Alternative Internal Choice**

**(b)(i)** Discuss the integrated form of the van't Hoff equation under the assumption that $\Delta H^{\circ}$ is independent of temperature. Show that a plot of $\ln K$ versus $1/T$ is linear, and explain how $\Delta H^{\circ}$ and $\Delta S^{\circ}$ can be obtained from such a plot. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Integration with constant $\Delta H^{\circ}$:**

Starting from $d(\ln K)/dT = \Delta H^{\circ}/(R T^{2})$ and rearranging to $d(\ln K) = (\Delta H^{\circ}/R) T^{-2}\, dT$, integrate between limits:

$$\ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right)$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Convert to linear form.** Setting $T_{1} = T_{\text{ref}}$ and treating the reference $K_{1}$ as a constant, indefinite integration yields:

$$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + C$$

where $C$ is the constant of integration.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Identify $y$, $x$, slope, and intercept.** Comparing with $y = mx + c$:

- $y = \ln K$
- $x = 1/T$
- $m = -\Delta H^{\circ}/R$ ‚Üí slope
- $c = C = \Delta S^{\circ}/R$ ‚Üí intercept

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Determine thermodynamic parameters from the plot:**

$$\Delta H^{\circ} = -m R \quad ; \quad \Delta S^{\circ} = c R$$

**Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Discussion of validity:**
- The plot is linear **only** if $\Delta H^{\circ}$ is approximately constant over the temperature range.
- For wider ranges, the slope gradually changes, reflecting the $T$-dependence of $\Delta H^{\circ}$ through $\Delta C_{p}$.
- This method is widely used experimentally to extract $\Delta H^{\circ}$ and $\Delta S^{\circ}$ from measured $K$ vs $T$ data.

---

**(b)(ii)** The equilibrium constant for a reaction doubles when the temperature is raised from $300 \; \text{K}$ to $310 \; \text{K}$. Calculate the standard enthalpy of reaction. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $T_{1} = 300 \; \text{K}$, $K_{1} = K$
- $T_{2} = 310 \; \text{K}$, $K_{2} = 2K$ (doubles)
- $R = 8.314 \; \text{J mol}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Apply integrated van't Hoff:**

$$\ln \left( \frac{K_{2}}{K_{1}} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_{2}} - \frac{1}{T_{1}} \right)$$

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Compute $K_{2}/K_{1}$:**

$$\ln \left( \frac{2K}{K} \right) = \ln(2) = 0.6931$$

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Compute $1/T_{2} - 1/T_{1}$:**

$$\frac{1}{310} - \frac{1}{300} = \frac{300 - 310}{310 \times 300} = \frac{-10}{93{,}000} = -1.0753 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Solve for $\Delta H^{\circ}$:**

$$\Delta H^{\circ} = -\frac{R \ln(K_{2}/K_{1})}{(1/T_{2} - 1/T_{1})} = -\frac{8.314 \times 0.6931}{-1.0753 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\Delta H^{\circ} = \frac{5.762}{1.0753 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 53{,}587 \; \text{J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H^{\circ} \approx 53.59 \; \text{kJ/mol (endothermic)}}$$

Positive value is consistent ‚ÄĒ heating caused $K$ to increase. ‚úď

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Error in $\Delta H^{\circ}$:** Students frequently drop the negative sign in the integrated van't Hoff equation. Always cross-check using the qualitative rule: "If $K$ increases with $T$, then $\Delta H^{\circ}$ must be positive."
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** $\Delta H^{\circ}$ must be in **J/mol** (not kJ/mol) when $R$ is in $\text{J mol}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$. This is the **#1 reason** for numerical errors in ESE.
> 3. **Forgetting the negative sign inside the bracket:** $\dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}}$ vs $\dfrac{1}{T_{1}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{2}}$ ‚ÄĒ pick one and be consistent.
> 4. **Validity of constant-$\Delta H^{\circ}$ assumption:** Always mention it; examiners reward the *statement* of the assumption as a separate point.
> 5. **Confusing $K_{p}$ and $K_{c}$:** For gas-phase reactions, conversion $K_{p} = K_{c}(RT)^{\Delta n}$ is required if data is given in mixed forms.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Master Equation:** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$ ‚ÄĒ the gateway to all equilibrium thermodynamics.
- **van't Hoff Differential Form:** $\dfrac{d(\ln K)}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}$ ‚ÄĒ sign of $\Delta H^{\circ}$ dictates $K$ vs $T$ trend.
- **van't Hoff Integrated Form:** $\ln(K_{2}/K_{1}) = -(\Delta H^{\circ}/R)(1/T_{2} - 1/T_{1})$ ‚ÄĒ for finite $T$ jumps when $\Delta H^{\circ} \approx \text{constant}$.
- **Linear Plot Rule:** Slope of $\ln K$ vs $1/T$ is $-\Delta H^{\circ}/R$; intercept is $\Delta S^{\circ}/R$.
- **Endothermic ($\Delta H^{\circ} > 0$):** $K$ **increases** with $T$.
- **Exothermic ($\Delta H^{\circ} < 0$):** $K$ **decreases** with $T$.
- **Athermal ($\Delta H^{\circ} = 0$):** $K$ is **independent** of $T$.
- **Wide $T$-range correction:** Use **Kirchhoff's Law** to update $\Delta H^{\circ}$ before applying van't Hoff.
- **Gas Constant:** Always use $R = 8.314 \; \text{J mol}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$ with $\Delta H^{\circ}$ in J/mol.
- **Le Chatelier's Principle (Quantitative):** van't Hoff equation is the *mathematical embodiment* of Le Chatelier's qualitative rule.
- **Engineering Use:** Reactor yield prediction, catalyst selection, process safety analysis.
- **Always State the Assumption:** "$\Delta H^{\circ}$ is approximately constant over the temperature interval" ‚ÄĒ examiners expect this one-liner.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation of Equilibrium Constant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT633 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Evaluation of Equilibrium Constant # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In chemical thermodynamics, the **equilibrium constant** ($K$) is a dimensionless quantity that quantitatively expresses the extent to which a reversible chemical reaction proceeds at a given temperature. It is rigorously defined through the relationship between the **standard Gibbs free energy change** ($\Delta G^\circ$) and the temperature ($T$), using the universal gas constant ($R = 8.314 \text{ J/mol¬∑K}$): $$\Delta G^\circ = -R T \ln K$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT633 ‚Äď Module 4):** > The equilibrium constant is **NOT** evaluated from kinetics; it is a **purely thermodynamic** property derived from the difference in Gibbs energies of products and reactants in their standard states. The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes the relationship $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ and the **van't Hoff equation** for temperature dependence. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ‚ÄĒ The "Reactor Hill" Model Imagine two mountaineers, **Forward Rex** and **Reverse Rina**, pushing a boulder up and down a hill. The hill represents the Gibbs energy barrier. The boulder represents a reacting mixture. - **Standard Gibbs energy** ($\Delta G^\circ$) is the **net height** the boulder rolls at the start. - If $\Delta G^\circ < 0$, the boulder rolls **downhill toward products** ‚Üí $K > 1$ (products favored). - If $\Delta G^\circ > 0$, the boulder rolls **uphill** ‚Üí $K < 1$ (reactants favored). - The **equilibrium constant** $K$ is simply the *ratio of how strongly each side "holds" the boulder* at the bottom of the hill. > [!NOTE] > **Units Reminder for KTU Board Exams:** > Although $K$ is **thermodynamically dimensionless**, in practice students will encounter: > - $K_p$ : equilibrium constant based on partial pressures (bar or atm) > - $K_c$ : equilibrium constant based on concentrations (mol/L) > - $K_x$ : equilibrium constant based on mole fractions (dimensionless) > - $K_a$ : equilibrium constant based on activities (dimensionless) The conversion relationships (for ideal gases) are: $$K_p = K_c (R T)^{\Delta n}$$ $$K_p = K_x P^{\Delta n}$$ where $\Delta n = \sum \nu_i^{\text{products}} - \sum \nu_i^{\text{reactants}}$ represents the change in the number of moles of gas. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Foundation ‚ÄĒ Why $K$ Exists At equilibrium, the **net Gibbs free energy change vanishes**: $$\Delta G = 0$$ The general Gibbs energy change expression for any reaction mixture (not at equilibrium) is: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + R T \ln Q$$ where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient**. At equilibrium, $Q \rightarrow K$, so: $$0 = \Delta G^\circ + R T \ln K$$ Rearranging gives the fundamental relationship: $$\boxed{\Delta G^\circ = -R T \ln K}$$ This single equation is the **gateway** to evaluating $K$. ## 2.2 The "How" ‚ÄĒ Two Routes to Evaluate $K$ ### Route A: From Standard Gibbs Energy of Formation ($\Delta G_f^\circ$) The standard Gibbs energy change of a reaction is calculated as the difference between products and reactants: $$\Delta G^\circ = \sum_i \nu_i \Delta G_{f,i}^\circ \bigg\vert_{\text{products}} - \sum_j \nu_j \Delta G_{f,j}^\circ \bigg\vert_{\text{reactants}}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always tabulate $\Delta G_f^\circ$ values for each species, multiply by stoichiometric coefficients $\nu_i$, sum the products column, sum the reactants column, and subtract. Careless summation is the #1 cause of mark loss. Once $\Delta G^\circ$ is obtained: $$K = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{R T}\right)$$ ### Route B: From Enthalpy and Entropy Data Using the Gibbs‚ÄďHelmholtz identity, $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$: $$-R T \ln K = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$$ Solving for $\ln K$: $$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}$$ > This equation shows that $K$ depends **exponentially** on $1/T$. **Endothermic** reactions ($\Delta H^\circ > 0$) have $K$ that *increases* with temperature; **exothermic** reactions ($\Delta H^\circ < 0$) have $K$ that *decreases* with temperature. ## 2.3 Temperature Dependence ‚ÄĒ The van't Hoff Equation Differentiating $\ln K = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T} + \dfrac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}$ with respect to $T$ at constant pressure (assuming $\Delta H^\circ$ is approximately independent of $T$): $$\boxed{\frac{d \ln K}{d T} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^{2}}}$$ This is the **differential form of the van't Hoff equation**. **Integrated form** (between temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$, assuming $\Delta H^\circ =$ constant): $$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$ ## 2.4 Accounting for Heat Capacity ($C_p$) Variation When the temperature range is large, $\Delta C_p \neq 0$, and the **complete van't Hoff equation** must be used: $$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ_{298}}{R T} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ_{298}}{R} + \frac{\Delta C_p}{R}\!\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{T}{298}\right) + \frac{298}{T} - 1\right]$$ where $\Delta C_p = \sum_i \nu_i C_{p,i}^{\circ}$. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table before attempting any numerical in the End Semester Examination (ESE).** | # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Conditions | |---|--------------|----------|--------------------| | 1 | Fundamental K‚ÄďőĒG¬į Link | $\Delta G^\circ = -R T \ln K$ | $K$ dimensionless, $T$ in K | | 2 | K from őĒG¬į | $K = \exp\!\left(-\Delta G^\circ / R T\right)$ | $R = 8.314$ J/mol¬∑K | | 3 | őĒG¬į from Formation Data | $\Delta G^\circ = \sum \nu_i \Delta G_{f,i}^\circ$ | Values in kJ/mol | | 4 | K from őĒH¬į and őĒS¬į | $\ln K = -\Delta H^\circ / R T + \Delta S^\circ / R$ | Requires both data | | 5 | van't Hoff (Differential) | $d \ln K / d T = \Delta H^\circ / R T^{2}$ | Constant őĒH¬į assumed | | 6 | van't Hoff (Integrated) | $\ln(K_2 / K_1) = (\Delta H^\circ / R) (1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$ | Two-temperature problem | | 7 | Complete van't Hoff | $\ln K = -\Delta H^\circ_{298} / R T + \Delta S^\circ_{298} / R + (\Delta C_p / R) [\ln(T/298) + 298/T - 1]$ | Wide T range | | 8 | Kp‚ÄďKc Link (ideal gas) | $K_p = K_c (R T)^{\Delta n}$ | $\Delta n$ = mole change | | 9 | Kp‚ÄďKx Link (ideal gas) | $K_p = K_x P^{\Delta n}$ | Total pressure $P$ in bar/atm | | 10 | Kp from Fugacity | $K = \prod (f_i / f_i^\circ)^{\nu_i}$ | Real gas correction | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** In chemical process industries (ammonia synthesis, methanol production, sulfuric acid contact process), engineers evaluate $K$ at the operating temperature and pressure to design reactors, predict equilibrium conversion, and decide whether to use excess reactant, remove products, or adjust operating conditions. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of $\Delta G^\circ = -R T \ln K$ We start with the fundamental definition of the Gibbs energy change for any reaction mixture: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + R T \ln Q$$ **Step 1:** At equilibrium, the system has no further tendency to change, so the **net Gibbs energy change is zero**: $$\Delta G_{\text{eq}} = 0$$ **Step 2:** Substituting this into the equation above: $$0 = \Delta G^\circ + R T \ln Q_{\text{eq}}$$ **Step 3:** Recognizing that the reaction quotient at equilibrium is by definition the equilibrium constant: $$Q_{\text{eq}} \equiv K$$ **Step 4:** Substituting $K$ for $Q_{\text{eq}}$: $$0 = \Delta G^\circ + R T \ln K$$ **Step 5:** Solving for $\Delta G^\circ$: $$\boxed{\Delta G^\circ = -R T \ln K}$$ > **Interpretation:** $\Delta G^\circ$ is the Gibbs energy change when all reactants and products are in their **standard states** (1 bar, ideal behavior for gases, 1 molal for solutes). It is *this* quantity, not the actual $\Delta G$, that determines $K$. ## 3.2 Derivation of the van't Hoff Equation (Differential Form) Starting from the combined relation: $$-R T \ln K = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$$ Dividing both sides by $-R T$: $$\ln K = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}$$ **Step 1:** Differentiate both sides with respect to $T$ at constant pressure. Note that $\Delta H^\circ$ and $\Delta S^\circ$ are first assumed to be **weakly dependent on $T$** (valid for narrow temperature ranges): $$\frac{d \ln K}{d T} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^{2}} - \frac{1}{R T}\frac{d \Delta H^\circ}{d T} + \frac{1}{R}\frac{d \Delta S^\circ}{d T}$$ **Step 2:** Apply Kirchhoff's relation, $\dfrac{d \Delta H^\circ}{d T} = \Delta C_p$, and the thermodynamic identity $\dfrac{d \Delta S^\circ}{d T} = \dfrac{\Delta C_p}{T}$. Substituting: $$\frac{d \ln K}{d T} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^{2}} - \frac{\Delta C_p}{R T} + \frac{\Delta C_p}{R T}$$ The last two terms cancel exactly: $$\boxed{\frac{d \ln K}{d T} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^{2}}}$$ > **Crucial Insight:** The $\Delta C_p$ terms cancel **regardless** of whether $\Delta C_p$ is zero. Thus, the van't Hoff equation is **exact** as long as we use the *temperature-dependent* $\Delta H^\circ(T)$. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Integrated van't Hoff Equation **Step 1:** Separate variables in the differential form: $$d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^{2}} \, d T$$ **Step 2:** Assume $\Delta H^\circ \approx$ constant (valid for narrow $T$ ranges): $$d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \cdot \frac{d T}{T^{2}}$$ **Step 3:** Integrate from state 1 ($T_1, K_1$) to state 2 ($T_2, K_2$): $$\int_{K_1}^{K_2} d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} T^{-2} \, d T$$ **Step 4:** Evaluate both integrals: $$\ln K \bigg\vert_{K_1}^{K_2} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_1}^{T_2}$$ **Step 5:** Apply the limits: $$\ln K_2 - \ln K_1 = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(-\frac{1}{T_2} + \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$ **Step 6:** Simplify: $$\boxed{\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ‚ÄĒ Worked Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Standard Problem in KTU ESE (Dec 2023, May 2024 patterns):** **Problem:** For the reaction $\text{N}_2(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3(g)$ at 700 K, evaluate the equilibrium constant $K_p$ given the following standard thermodynamic data at 298 K: | Species | $\Delta G_f^\circ$ (kJ/mol) | $\Delta H_f^\circ$ (kJ/mol) | |---------|------------------------------|------------------------------| | N‚āā(g) | 0 | 0 | | H‚āā(g) | 0 | 0 | | NH‚āÉ(g) | ‚ąí16.45 | ‚ąí46.11 | ### Solution **Step 1:** Calculate $\Delta G^\circ_{298}$ for the reaction: $$\Delta G^\circ_{298} = \sum \nu_i \Delta G_{f,i}^\circ \bigg\vert_{\text{prod}} - \sum \nu_j \Delta G_{f,j}^\circ \bigg\vert_{\text{react}}$$ $$\Delta G^\circ_{298} = [2 \times (-16.45)] - [1 \times 0 + 3 \times 0] = -32.90 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate $\Delta H^\circ_{298}$: $$\Delta H^\circ_{298} = [2 \times (-46.11)] - [0] = -92.22 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate $\Delta S^\circ_{298}$ using $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$: $$\Delta S^\circ_{298} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ_{298} - \Delta G^\circ_{298}}{298} = \frac{(-92.22) - (-32.90)}{298}$$ $$\Delta S^\circ_{298} = \frac{-59.32 \times 10^{3} \text{ J/mol}}{298 \text{ K}}$$ $$\Delta S^\circ_{298} = -199.06 \text{ J/mol¬∑K}$$ **Step 4:** Assume $\Delta H^\circ$ and $\Delta S^\circ$ are approximately constant. Calculate $\Delta G^\circ_{700}$: $$\Delta G^\circ_{700} = \Delta H^\circ_{298} - T \Delta S^\circ_{298}$$ $$\Delta G^\circ_{700} = -92.22 \times 10^{3} - 700 \times (-199.06)$$ $$\Delta G^\circ_{700} = -92{,}220 + 139{,}342 = +47{,}122 \text{ J/mol}$$ **Step 5:** Calculate $K_p$ at 700 K: $$K_p = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta G^\circ_{700}}{R T}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{47{,}122}{8.314 \times 700}\right)$$ $$K_p = \exp(-8.097) = 3.04 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 6:** Physical interpretation: > Since $K_p \ll 1$ at 700 K, the equilibrium lies toward the **reactant side** at this temperature. Industrial ammonia synthesis compensates by using very high pressure (~150‚Äď300 bar), the **Le Chatelier effect**, and lower temperatures combined with an iron catalyst to achieve acceptable conversion. ### Cross-Check Using van't Hoff (Should Match) $$\ln K_{700} = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R} = -\frac{-92{,}220}{8.314 \times 700} + \frac{-199.06}{8.314}$$ $$\ln K_{700} = 15.85 - 23.94 = -8.09 \implies K_{700} = 3.04 \times 10^{-4} \checkmark$$ Both methods yield identical results, confirming the thermodynamic consistency. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ‚ÄĒ Evaluation of Equilibrium Constant The following schematic maps the **decision-flow** a KTU student must follow when evaluating $K$ for any chemical reaction: ```mermaid flowchart TD StartA([Start: Reaction Given]) --> IdA[Identify Species and Stoichiometry nu_i] IdA --> Q1{Data Available?} Q1 -->|Delta Gf only| PathA[Path A: Route via Delta G standard] Q1 -->|Delta Hf and S standard| PathB[Path B: Route via Enthalpy and Entropy] Q1 -->|Kp at T1, find Kp at T2| PathC[Path C: Use van't Hoff Equation] PathA --> CalcA1[Sum products minus reactants of nu_i times Delta Gf] CalcA1 --> CalcA2[Compute Delta G standard at Temperature T] CalcA2 --> CalcA3[Apply K equals exp of minus Delta G standard over R T] CalcA3 --> OutA((K evaluated)) PathB --> CalcB1[Calculate Delta H standard reaction] CalcB1 --> CalcB2[Calculate Delta S standard reaction] CalcB2 --> CalcB3[Apply ln K equals minus Delta H over R T plus Delta S over R] CalcB3 --> OutB((K evaluated)) PathC --> CalcC1[Assume Delta H constant] CalcC1 --> CalcC2[Compute van't Hoff Integrated Form] CalcC2 --> CalcC3[Substitute K1, T1, K2, T2] CalcC3 --> OutC((K evaluated)) OutA --> Conv{Real Gas?} OutB --> Conv OutC --> Conv Conv -->|Yes| Fug[Use Fugacity Coefficients phi_i] Conv -->|No| Ideal[Assume Ideal Behavior] Fug --> Final((Final Kp Kc Kx Reported)) Ideal --> Final ``` ## 4.2 Conceptual Hierarchy ‚ÄĒ Routes to $K$ ```mermaid flowchart LR ThermoData[Thermodynamic Data
Delta Gf, Delta Hf, S standard] --> Math[Mathematical Operations
Summation and Subtraction] Math --> DeltaG[Compute Delta G standard of Reaction] DeltaG --> RelLink[Apply Delta G equals minus R T ln K] RelLink --> K[Evaluate K at T] Data2[Data: Kp at T1] --> vH[Apply van't Hoff Equation] vH --> K2[Kp at T2] CpData[Heat Capacity Data Cp] --> vH2[Complete van't Hoff with Delta Cp] vH2 --> K3[K over wide T range] ``` ## 4.3 Sign Convention and Physical Behavior Map ```mermaid flowchart TD DeltaHSign{Delta H standard sign?} DeltaHSign -->|Positive Endothermic| End[As T increases, K increases] DeltaHSign -->|Negative Exothermic| Exo[As T increases, K decreases] DeltaGSign{Delta G standard sign?} DeltaGSign -->|Negative| Spont[K greater than 1, Products Favored] DeltaGSign -->|Zero| EquilK[K equals 1] DeltaGSign -->|Positive| NonSpont[K less than 1, Reactants Favored] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always include a **sign analysis** at the end of any problem where you evaluate $K$. Examiners award 1‚Äď2 marks for correctly interpreting the result (whether products or reactants are favored), even if numerical errors occur. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A ‚ÄĒ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024 Style]** **Q:** Define the equilibrium constant $K$ in terms of the standard Gibbs free energy change. Mention the conditions under which the relationship $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ is valid. **Model Answer:** The equilibrium constant $K$ is the value of the reaction quotient $Q$ when the reaction is at chemical equilibrium. It is related to the standard Gibbs free energy change by the fundamental thermodynamic equation: $$\Delta G^\circ = -R T \ln K$$ where $R$ is the universal gas constant ($8.314 \text{ J/mol¬∑K}$), $T$ is the absolute temperature (K), and $\Delta G^\circ$ is the standard Gibbs energy change (J/mol). **Conditions for validity:** 1. The reaction must be at chemical equilibrium. 2. The system must be at constant temperature and pressure. 3. All species must be in their standard states (1 bar for gases, pure substances for condensed phases, 1 molal for solutes). 4. $K$ is a dimensionless quantity, obtained by expressing all activities relative to standard states. From the equation, $K = \exp(-\Delta G^\circ / R T)$. If $\Delta G^\circ < 0$, then $K > 1$ (products favored); if $\Delta G^\circ > 0$, then $K < 1$ (reactants favored); if $\Delta G^\circ = 0$, then $K = 1$ (equal mixture). --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023 Style]** **Q:** State and explain the van't Hoff equation. What does it tell us about the temperature dependence of the equilibrium constant for an exothermic reaction? **Model Answer:** The van't Hoff equation expresses how the equilibrium constant changes with temperature. In its differential form: $$\frac{d \ln K}{d T} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^{2}}$$ The integrated form (assuming $\Delta H^\circ$ is constant over the temperature range) is: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$ **For an exothermic reaction** ($\Delta H^\circ < 0$): - The right-hand side becomes negative when $T_2 > T_1$. - Therefore, $\ln(K_2 / K_1) < 0$, implying $K_2 < K_1$. - **Conclusion:** As temperature increases, the equilibrium constant for an exothermic reaction **decreases**. This is consistent with the **Le Chatelier principle** ‚ÄĒ heat behaves as a "product" in exothermic reactions, so adding heat shifts the equilibrium toward reactants. --- ## Part B ‚ÄĒ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit conversion mistakes** are the #1 cause of mark loss. $\Delta G^\circ$ and $\Delta H^\circ$ in data tables are typically given in **kJ/mol**, but the $R T$ product uses **J/mol**. Always convert kJ ‚Üí J by multiplying by 1000. > 2. **Sign error** when applying $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$ ‚ÄĒ the term is **minus** $T \Delta S^\circ$, not plus. > 3. **Wrong sign on the exponent** when computing $K = \exp(-\Delta G^\circ / R T)$. If $\Delta G^\circ < 0$, the exponent is positive ‚Üí $K > 1$. > 4. **Forgetting to specify the temperature** when reporting the final $K$ value. Always state $K_{700}$ or $K_p$ at 700 K. > 5. In van't Hoff problems, ensure you match $T_1$ with $K_1$ and $T_2$ with $K_2$ in the correct orientation of $(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď May 2024, Modified Pattern]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ starting from the general Gibbs free energy change expression. **(b) [7 Marks]** For the water-gas shift reaction $\text{CO}(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2(g) + \text{H}_2(g)$ at 1000 K, evaluate $K_p$ using the following data at 298 K: | Species | $\Delta G_f^\circ$ (kJ/mol) | $\Delta H_f^\circ$ (kJ/mol) | |---------|------------------------------|------------------------------| | CO(g) | ‚ąí137.17 | ‚ąí110.53 | | H‚āāO(g) | ‚ąí228.57 | ‚ąí241.83 | | CO‚āā(g) | ‚ąí394.36 | ‚ąí393.52 | | H‚āā(g) | 0 | 0 | #### Model Solution for (a): Start with the general expression for Gibbs free energy change for any reaction mixture: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + R T \ln Q$$ At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$ (no driving force remains), and $Q \rightarrow K$ (the reaction quotient equals the equilibrium constant). Substituting: $$0 = \Delta G^\circ + R T \ln K$$ Solving for $\Delta G^\circ$: $$\boxed{\Delta G^\circ = -R T \ln K}$$ This relationship shows that $K$ is uniquely determined by the standard Gibbs energy change at a given temperature. #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1:** Calculate $\Delta G^\circ_{298}$: $$\Delta G^\circ_{298} = [(-394.36) + 0] - [(-137.17) + (-228.57)]$$ $$\Delta G^\circ_{298} = -394.36 - (-365.74) = -28.62 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate $\Delta H^\circ_{298}$: $$\Delta H^\circ_{298} = [(-393.52) + 0] - [(-110.53) + (-241.83)]$$ $$\Delta H^\circ_{298} = -393.52 - (-352.36) = -41.16 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate $\Delta S^\circ_{298}$: $$\Delta S^\circ_{298} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ_{298} - \Delta G^\circ_{298}}{298} = \frac{(-41.16) - (-28.62)}{298} \times 10^{3}$$ $$\Delta S^\circ_{298} = \frac{-12.54 \times 10^{3}}{298} = -42.08 \text{ J/mol¬∑K}$$ **Step 4:** Calculate $\Delta G^\circ_{1000}$ assuming $\Delta H^\circ$, $\Delta S^\circ$ are temperature-independent: $$\Delta G^\circ_{1000} = -41.16 \times 10^{3} - 1000 \times (-42.08)$$ $$\Delta G^\circ_{1000} = -41{,}160 + 42{,}080 = +920 \text{ J/mol}$$ **Step 5:** Evaluate $K_p$: $$K_p = \exp\!\left(-\frac{920}{8.314 \times 1000}\right) = \exp(-0.1107)$$ $$\boxed{K_p = 0.895 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$ **Step 6:** Interpretation: Since $K_p \approx 0.895 < 1$, the equilibrium slightly favors reactants (CO and H‚āāO) at 1000 K. The reaction is mildly exothermic, so increasing temperature slightly disfavors products, consistent with our result. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) ‚ÄĒ Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam ‚Äď Dec 2023 Pattern]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the differential form of the van't Hoff equation, derive the integrated form and clearly state the assumptions. **(b) [7 Marks]** For a reaction, $K_p = 10$ at 400 K. If the standard enthalpy change of the reaction is $\Delta H^\circ = -60$ kJ/mol (assumed constant), calculate $K_p$ at 500 K. #### Model Solution for (a): **Step 1:** Start with the differential form: $$\frac{d \ln K}{d T} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^{2}}$$ **Step 2:** Separate variables: $$d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^{2}} \, d T$$ **Step 3:** Integrate between limits $(T_1, K_1)$ and $(T_2, K_2)$: $$\int_{K_1}^{K_2} d \ln K = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} T^{-2} \, d T$$ **Step 4:** Evaluate: $$\ln K_2 - \ln K_1 = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\left(-\frac{1}{T_2} + \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$ $$\boxed{\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)}$$ **Assumptions:** 1. $\Delta H^\circ$ is independent of temperature (i.e., $\Delta C_p = 0$ over the temperature range). 2. The system is at constant pressure. 3. The ideal-gas law or ideal-solution behavior holds for all species. #### Model Solution for (b): **Given:** - $K_1 = 10$ at $T_1 = 400$ K - $\Delta H^\circ = -60$ kJ/mol $= -60{,}000$ J/mol - $T_2 = 500$ K **Step 1:** Apply the integrated van't Hoff equation: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{K_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Substitute numerical values: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{10}\right) = \frac{-60{,}000}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{1}{400} - \frac{1}{500}\right)$$ **Step 3:** Compute the bracket: $$\frac{1}{400} - \frac{1}{500} = \frac{500 - 400}{400 \times 500} = \frac{100}{200{,}000} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$ **Step 4:** Compute the coefficient: $$\frac{-60{,}000}{8.314} = -7217.7 \text{ K}$$ **Step 5:** Multiply: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_2}{10}\right) = -7217.7 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = -3.609$$ **Step 6:** Solve for $K_2$: $$\frac{K_2}{10} = e^{-3.609} = 0.0270$$ $$\boxed{K_2 \approx 0.270 \text{ at } 500 \text{ K}}$$ **Step 7:** Interpretation: Since the reaction is exothermic ($\Delta H^\circ < 0$), increasing temperature from 400 K to 500 K causes $K_p$ to **decrease** from 10 to 0.270, consistent with Le Chatelier's principle. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PEPOT633 ‚Äď Module 4 (Evaluation of Equilibrium Constant)** - **Master Equation:** $\Delta G^\circ = -R T \ln K$ is the foundation of *all* equilibrium calculations in this module. - **Two Routes to $K$:** - **Route 1 (Direct):** Use tabulated $\Delta G_f^\circ$ values; compute $\Delta G^\circ$ reaction; then $K = \exp(-\Delta G^\circ / R T)$. - **Route 2 (Indirect):** Use tabulated $\Delta H_f^\circ$ and $S^\circ$; compute $\Delta H^\circ$ and $\Delta S^\circ$; then $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$. - **van't Hoff Equation (Differential):** $\dfrac{d \ln K}{d T} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^{2}}$ ‚ÄĒ **exact** if temperature-dependent $\Delta H^\circ(T)$ is used. - **van't Hoff Equation (Integrated):** $\ln(K_2 / K_1) = (\Delta H^\circ / R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$ ‚ÄĒ assumes $\Delta H^\circ \approx$ constant. - **Sign Convention Mastery:** - Endothermic ($\Delta H^\circ > 0$): $K$ **increases** with $T$. - Exothermic ($\Delta H^\circ < 0$): $K$ **decreases** with $T$. - **Unit Vigilance:** $\Delta G^\circ$, $\Delta H^\circ$ typically in kJ/mol ‚Üí **multiply by 1000** before using with $R = 8.314$ J/mol¬∑K. - **$\Delta n$ Matters:** $K_p = K_c (R T)^{\Delta n}$ ‚ÄĒ only for ideal gases; $\Delta n$ is the net change in gaseous moles. - **Physical Interpretation is Mandatory:** Always conclude a problem with: "Since $K$ is greater than 1 / less than 1, products/reactants are favored." - **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Expect at least **one 14-mark problem** combining Route 2 (calculation of $\Delta G^\circ$ from $\Delta H^\circ$ and $\Delta S^\circ$) with a van't Hoff follow-up; one **3-mark conceptual** on signs and the meaning of $K$. - **Real Gas Extension:** When the problem states "non-ideal," replace partial pressures with fugacities: $K = \prod (f_i / f_i^\circ)^{\nu_i}$, using fugacity coefficients $\phi_i$ from generalized charts, the Redlich‚ÄďKwong EOS, or the Peng‚ÄďRobinson EOS. - **Complete van't Hoff (with $\Delta C_p$):** $\ln K = -\Delta H^\circ_{298} / R T + \Delta S^\circ_{298} / R + (\Delta C_p / R)[\ln(T/298) + 298/T - 1]$ ‚ÄĒ use when temperature range exceeds ~100 K. > **End of Module 4 Notes ‚ÄĒ Evaluation of Equilibrium Constant**
